meta.overview: The Creation account — the foundational chapter of the entire Bible. In six days, God creates the heavens and the earth, bringing order out of chaos and filling the void with life. This chapter establishes God as the sovereign, all-powerful Creator who speaks all things into existence. The progression moves from formlessness to form (Days 1-3) and from emptiness to fullness (Days 4-6), culminating in the creation of humanity in God's own image.
meta.theme: Creation & the Sovereignty of God
meta.keyWordMeaning: To create out of nothing — used exclusively for divine creative activity
outline: Day 1 (vv.1-5): Light separated from Darkness
Day 2 (vv.6-8): Waters above separated from waters below (Firmament)
Day 3 (vv.9-13): Dry land and vegetation
Day 4 (vv.14-19): Sun, Moon, and Stars
Day 5 (vv.20-23): Sea creatures and Birds
Day 6 (vv.24-31): Land animals and Humanity

verses.kjv_text: In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth.
verses.original_text: בְּרֵאשִׁית בָּרָא אֱלֹהִים אֵת הַשָּׁמַיִם וְאֵת הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Bereshit bara Elohim et hashamayim ve'et ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The opening verse of the Bible establishes the most fundamental truths in Scripture: (1) There was a beginning to the material universe, (2) God existed before and independent of that creation, (3) God alone is the Creator. The Hebrew word 'Elohim' is grammatically plural used with a singular verb 'bara' — this unity-in-plurality hints at the Trinitarian nature of God more fully revealed in the New Testament. The phrase 'the heaven and the earth' is a merism — a Hebrew literary device using two opposites to encompass everything between them, meaning God created absolutely everything.
verses.doctrinal_note: This single verse refutes: atheism (God exists), agnosticism (God is knowable through His works), polytheism (one God created), pantheism (God is separate from creation), materialism (matter had a beginning), and humanism (God, not man, is the ultimate reality).

verses.kjv_text: And the earth was without form, and void; and darkness was upon the face of the deep. And the Spirit of God moved upon the face of the waters.
verses.original_text: וְהָאָרֶץ הָיְתָה תֹהוּ וָבֹהוּ וְחֹשֶׁךְ עַל־פְּנֵי תְהוֹם וְרוּחַ אֱלֹהִים מְרַחֶפֶת עַל־פְּנֵי הַמָּיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Veha'aretz hayetah tohu vavohu vechoshekh al-penei tehom veruach Elohim merachefet al-penei hamayim.
verses.study_note: This verse describes the raw, unformed state of creation before God shaped it. 'Tohu vavohu' describes two deficiencies: the earth was unformed (no structure) and unfilled (no inhabitants). Days 1-3 address the formlessness by creating realms, while Days 4-6 address the emptiness by filling them. The Spirit 'hovering' (merachefet) uses the same word as a mother eagle hovering over her young (Deut 32:11) — suggesting protective, nurturing, creative power.

verses.kjv_text: And God said, Let there be light: and there was light.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים יְהִי אוֹר וַיְהִי־אוֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Elohim yehi or vayehi-or.
verses.study_note: God creates by speaking — His word carries creative, life-giving power. The brevity of the Hebrew (just 6 words) underscores the effortlessness and immediacy of divine creation. Light was created before the sun (Day 4), indicating that God Himself is the ultimate source of light. Paul connects this to spiritual illumination in 2 Corinthians 4:6.

verses.kjv_text: And God saw the light, that it was good: and God divided the light from the darkness.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא אֱלֹהִים אֶת־הָאוֹר כִּי־טוֹב וַיַּבְדֵּל אֱלֹהִים בֵּין הָאוֹר וּבֵין הַחֹשֶׁךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayar Elohim et-ha'or ki-tov vayavdel Elohim bein ha'or uvein hachoshekh.
verses.study_note: Two significant acts: God evaluates ('saw... good') and separates ('divided'). The pattern of evaluation and separation runs through the entire creation account. God's work of creating involves making distinctions — between light and darkness, waters above and below, sea and land. This principle of holy distinction becomes foundational to the Law and to the Gospel.

verses.kjv_text: And God called the light Day, and the darkness he called Night. And the evening and the morning were the first day.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא אֱלֹהִים לָאוֹר יוֹם וְלַחֹשֶׁךְ קָרָא לָיְלָה וַיְהִי־עֶרֶב וַיְהִי־בֹקֶר יוֹם אֶחָד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayikra Elohim la'or yom velachoshekh kara laylah vayehi-erev vayehi-voker yom echad.
verses.study_note: God's naming act demonstrates His absolute sovereignty. To name something in Hebrew culture is to define it and have authority over it. The formula 'evening and morning' establishes the Hebrew day beginning at sunset. The word 'echad' (one) rather than 'rishon' (first) is the same word used in the Shema (Deut 6:4) to describe God's oneness — a word expressing composite unity.

verses.kjv_text: And God said, Let there be a firmament in the midst of the waters, and let it divide the waters from the waters.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים יְהִי רָקִיעַ בְּתוֹךְ הַמָּיִם וִיהִי מַבְדִּיל בֵּין מַיִם לָמָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Elohim yehi raqia betokh hamayim vihi mavdil bein mayim lamayim.
verses.study_note: Day 2 begins. The 'raqia' (firmament/expanse) is the atmospheric expanse — the sky. God creates a separation between the waters on earth's surface and the moisture in the atmosphere. This is the only day where God does not pronounce His work 'good' — possibly because the separation is completed on Day 3.

verses.kjv_text: And God made the firmament, and divided the waters which were under the firmament from the waters which were above the firmament: and it was so.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַשׂ אֱלֹהִים אֶת־הָרָקִיעַ וַיַּבְדֵּל בֵּין הַמַּיִם אֲשֶׁר מִתַּחַת לָרָקִיעַ וּבֵין הַמַּיִם אֲשֶׁר מֵעַל לָרָקִיעַ וַיְהִי־כֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Vaya'as Elohim et-haraqia vayavdel bein hamayim asher mitachat laraqia uvein hamayim asher me'al laraqia vayehi-khen.
verses.study_note: Note the shift from 'bara' (create from nothing) to 'asah' (make/fashion from existing material). God takes the formless creation and shapes it. 'And it was so' (vayehi-khen) appears 6 times in Genesis 1, affirming the absolute efficacy of God's word.

verses.kjv_text: And God called the firmament Heaven. And the evening and the morning were the second day.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא אֱלֹהִים לָרָקִיעַ שָׁמָיִם וַיְהִי־עֶרֶב וַיְהִי־בֹקֶר יוֹם שֵׁנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayikra Elohim laraqia shamayim vayehi-erev vayehi-voker yom sheni.
verses.study_note: God names the expanse 'Heaven' (shamayim). Scripture recognizes multiple heavens: (1) atmospheric, (2) celestial/outer space, (3) God's dwelling place. This is the only day without 'it was good,' as the work continues into Day 3.

verses.kjv_text: And God said, Let the waters under the heaven be gathered together unto one place, and let the dry land appear: and it was so.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים יִקָּווּ הַמַּיִם מִתַּחַת הַשָּׁמַיִם אֶל־מָקוֹם אֶחָד וְתֵרָאֶה הַיַּבָּשָׁה וַיְהִי־כֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Elohim yikkavu hamayim mitachat hashamayim el-maqom echad vetera'eh hayabashah vayehi-khen.
verses.study_note: Day 3 begins with God gathering the waters into seas and causing dry land to emerge. God's command over the waters demonstrates absolute dominion over nature. Job 38:8-11 describes God setting boundaries for the sea.

verses.kjv_text: And God called the dry land Earth; and the gathering together of the waters called he Seas: and God saw that it was good.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא אֱלֹהִים לַיַּבָּשָׁה אֶרֶץ וּלְמִקְוֵה הַמַּיִם קָרָא יַמִּים וַיַּרְא אֱלֹהִים כִּי־טוֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayikra Elohim layabashah eretz ulemikveh hamayim kara yamim vayar Elohim ki-tov.
verses.study_note: The first 'good' of Day 3. God names the dry ground 'Earth' and the waters 'Seas,' exercising sovereign authority. He names the realms before filling them with inhabitants.

verses.kjv_text: And God said, Let the earth bring forth grass, the herb yielding seed, and the fruit tree yielding fruit after his kind, whose seed is in itself, upon the earth: and it was so.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים תַּדְשֵׁא הָאָרֶץ דֶּשֶׁא עֵשֶׂב מַזְרִיעַ זֶרַע עֵץ פְּרִי עֹשֶׂה פְּרִי לְמִינוֹ אֲשֶׁר זַרְעוֹ־בוֹ עַל־הָאָרֶץ וַיְהִי־כֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Elohim tadsheh ha'aretz deshe esev mazria zera etz peri oseh peri lemino asher zar'o-vo al-ha'aretz vayehi-khen.
verses.study_note: Three categories of vegetation: grass (simple ground cover), herbs (seed-bearing plants), and fruit trees. 'After his kind' (lemino) appears 10 times in Genesis 1, establishing reproduction within defined types. The word 'seed' (zera) becomes theologically crucial — the seed of the woman (Gen 3:15), the seed of Abraham, and ultimately Christ (Galatians 3:16).

verses.kjv_text: And the earth brought forth grass, and herb yielding seed after his kind, and the tree yielding fruit, whose seed was in itself, after his kind: and God saw that it was good.
verses.original_text: וַתּוֹצֵא הָאָרֶץ דֶּשֶׁא עֵשֶׂב מַזְרִיעַ זֶרַע לְמִינֵהוּ וְעֵץ עֹשֶׂה־פְּרִי אֲשֶׁר זַרְעוֹ־בוֹ לְמִינֵהוּ וַיַּרְא אֱלֹהִים כִּי־טוֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Vatotze ha'aretz deshe esev mazria zera leminehu ve'etz oseh-peri asher zar'o-vo leminehu vayar Elohim ki-tov.
verses.study_note: The earth responds obediently to God's command. The second 'good' on Day 3 compensates for Day 2's absence. Vegetation was created before the sun (Day 4) — showing plant life depends ultimately on God, not merely on natural processes.

verses.kjv_text: And the evening and the morning were the third day.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי־עֶרֶב וַיְהִי־בֹקֶר יוֹם שְׁלִישִׁי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi-erev vayehi-voker yom shelishi.
verses.study_note: Day 3 closes with a double blessing. In the New Testament, the third day becomes the day of resurrection (1 Cor 15:4). Days 1-3 have addressed the formlessness — creating realms. Days 4-6 will address the emptiness by filling each realm.

verses.kjv_text: And God said, Let there be lights in the firmament of the heaven to divide the day from the night; and let them be for signs, and for seasons, and for days, and years:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים יְהִי מְאֹרֹת בִּרְקִיעַ הַשָּׁמַיִם לְהַבְדִּיל בֵּין הַיּוֹם וּבֵין הַלָּיְלָה וְהָיוּ לְאֹתֹת וּלְמוֹעֲדִים וּלְיָמִים וְשָׁנִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Elohim yehi me'orot birqia hashamayim lehavdil bein hayom uvein halaylah vehayu le'otot ulemo'adim uleyamim veshanim.
verses.study_note: Day 4: Four purposes for the luminaries: (1) divide day from night, (2) signs — navigational, prophetic markers, (3) seasons (mo'adim) — literally 'appointed times,' linking to God's sacred calendar and feast days, (4) days and years. They are 'me'orot' (light-bearers), not the source of light itself.

verses.kjv_text: And let them be for lights in the firmament of the heaven to give light upon the earth: and it was so.
verses.original_text: וְהָיוּ לִמְאוֹרֹת בִּרְקִיעַ הַשָּׁמַיִם לְהָאִיר עַל־הָאָרֶץ וַיְהִי־כֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayu lime'orot birqia hashamayim leha'ir al-ha'aretz vayehi-khen.
verses.study_note: The practical purpose of the luminaries: to illuminate the earth for its future inhabitants. They are servants, not masters — created to serve God's purposes. This counters pagan worship of celestial bodies.

verses.kjv_text: And God made two great lights; the greater light to rule the day, and the lesser light to rule the night: he made the stars also.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַשׂ אֱלֹהִים אֶת־שְׁנֵי הַמְּאֹרֹת הַגְּדֹלִים אֶת־הַמָּאוֹר הַגָּדֹל לְמֶמְשֶׁלֶת הַיּוֹם וְאֶת־הַמָּאוֹר הַקָּטֹן לְמֶמְשֶׁלֶת הַלַּיְלָה וְאֵת הַכּוֹכָבִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vaya'as Elohim et-shenei hame'orot hagedolim et-hama'or hagadol lememsheleth hayom ve'et-hama'or hakaton lememsheleth halaylah ve'et hakkokhavim.
verses.study_note: The sun and moon are not named — they are simply 'the greater light' and 'the lesser light.' This avoids naming pagan deities (shemesh/yareach). 'He made the stars also' is one of Scripture's greatest understatements — billions upon billions of stars are a casual afterthought to God who 'calleth them all by names' (Psalm 147:4).

verses.kjv_text: And God set them in the firmament of the heaven to give light upon the earth,
verses.original_text: וַיִּתֵּן אֹתָם אֱלֹהִים בִּרְקִיעַ הַשָּׁמָיִם לְהָאִיר עַל־הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayitten otam Elohim birqia hashamayim leha'ir al-ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: God 'set' (placed intentionally) the luminaries. Every celestial body occupies its God-appointed place. Modern astronomy confirms the extraordinary precision of cosmic fine-tuning.

verses.kjv_text: And to rule over the day and over the night, and to divide the light from the darkness: and God saw that it was good.
verses.original_text: וְלִמְשֹׁל בַּיּוֹם וּבַלַּיְלָה וּלְהַבְדִּיל בֵּין הָאוֹר וּבֵין הַחֹשֶׁךְ וַיַּרְא אֱלֹהִים כִּי־טוֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Velimshol bayom uvalaylah ulehavdil bein ha'or uvein hachoshekh vayar Elohim ki-tov.
verses.study_note: The governing function mirrors Day 1's separation of light from darkness, now through appointed instruments. The reliability of the sun and moon becomes a metaphor for God's covenant faithfulness (Jeremiah 33:20-21).

verses.kjv_text: And the evening and the morning were the fourth day.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי־עֶרֶב וַיְהִי־בֹקֶר יוֹם רְבִיעִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi-erev vayehi-voker yom revi'i.
verses.study_note: Day 4 corresponds to Day 1: where Day 1 created light, Day 4 creates the light-bearers. This parallel (Day 1/4, Day 2/5, Day 3/6) reveals God's orderly design — forming realms, then filling them.

verses.kjv_text: And God said, Let the waters bring forth abundantly the moving creature that hath life, and fowl that may fly above the earth in the open firmament of heaven.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים יִשְׁרְצוּ הַמַּיִם שֶׁרֶץ נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה וְעוֹף יְעוֹפֵף עַל־הָאָרֶץ עַל־פְּנֵי רְקִיעַ הַשָּׁמָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Elohim yishretzu hamayim sheretz nefesh chayyah ve'of ye'ofef al-ha'aretz al-penei reqia hashamayim.
verses.study_note: Day 5: God fills the realms created on Day 2 (sky and sea). 'Swarm' (sharats) emphasizes extravagant abundance. 'Nefesh chayyah' (living soul) marks a new level — animal life possesses an animating life-principle that plants do not.

verses.kjv_text: And God created great whales, and every living creature that moveth, which the waters brought forth abundantly, after their kind, and every winged fowl after his kind: and God saw that it was good.
verses.original_text: וַיִּבְרָא אֱלֹהִים אֶת־הַתַּנִּינִם הַגְּדֹלִים וְאֵת כָּל־נֶפֶשׁ הַחַיָּה הָרֹמֶשֶׂת אֲשֶׁר שָׁרְצוּ הַמַּיִם לְמִינֵהֶם וְאֵת כָּל־עוֹף כָּנָף לְמִינֵהוּ וַיַּרְא אֱלֹהִים כִּי־טוֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayivra Elohim et-hatanninim hagedolim ve'et kol-nefesh hachayyah haromeset asher sharetzu hamayim lemineihem ve'et kol-of kanaf leminehu vayar Elohim ki-tov.
verses.study_note: The return of 'bara' (second use after v.1) signals something new: animate life with 'nefesh' (soul). 'Tanninim' (great sea creatures) are merely creatures in God's creation — countering pagan myths of sea monsters as chaos deities.

verses.kjv_text: And God blessed them, saying, Be fruitful, and multiply, and fill the waters in the seas, and let fowl multiply in the earth.
verses.original_text: וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתָם אֱלֹהִים לֵאמֹר פְּרוּ וּרְבוּ וּמִלְאוּ אֶת־הַמַּיִם בַּיַּמִּים וְהָעוֹף יִרֶב בָּאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayevarekh otam Elohim lemor peru urevu umil'u et-hamayim bayamim veha'of yirev ba'aretz.
verses.study_note: The first blessing in Scripture — God endows creatures with the capacity to reproduce. 'Be fruitful and multiply' will be repeated to humanity (v.28) and to Noah (Gen 9:1). This is not merely permission but divine empowerment.

verses.kjv_text: And the evening and the morning were the fifth day.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי־עֶרֶב וַיְהִי־בֹקֶר יוֹם חֲמִישִׁי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi-erev vayehi-voker yom chamishi.
verses.study_note: Day 5 corresponds to Day 2: where Day 2 created sky and sea, Day 5 fills them with inhabitants.

verses.kjv_text: And God said, Let the earth bring forth the living creature after his kind, cattle, and creeping thing, and beast of the earth after his kind: and it was so.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים תּוֹצֵא הָאָרֶץ נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה לְמִינָהּ בְּהֵמָה וָרֶמֶשׂ וְחַיְתוֹ־אֶרֶץ לְמִינָהּ וַיְהִי־כֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Elohim totze ha'aretz nefesh chayyah leminah behemah varemes vechayyeto-eretz leminah vayehi-khen.
verses.study_note: Day 6 begins — the climactic day. Three categories of land animals: livestock (behemah), creeping things (remes), and wild beasts (chayyah). All reproduce 'after their kind.'

verses.kjv_text: And God made the beast of the earth after his kind, and cattle after their kind, and every thing that creepeth upon the earth after his kind: and God saw that it was good.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַשׂ אֱלֹהִים אֶת־חַיַּת הָאָרֶץ לְמִינָהּ וְאֶת־הַבְּהֵמָה לְמִינָהּ וְאֵת כָּל־רֶמֶשׂ הָאֲדָמָה לְמִינֵהוּ וַיַּרְא אֱלֹהִים כִּי־טוֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Vaya'as Elohim et-chayyat ha'aretz leminah ve'et-habehemah leminah ve'et kol-remes ha'adamah leminehu vayar Elohim ki-tov.
verses.study_note: God 'made' (asah) the land animals. The word 'adamah' (ground) appears here, foreshadowing the creation of 'adam' (man) from the same ground (Gen 2:7). God pronounces this 'good,' setting the stage for humanity.

verses.kjv_text: And God said, Let us make man in our image, after our likeness: and let them have dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the air, and over the cattle, and over all the earth, and over every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים נַעֲשֶׂה אָדָם בְּצַלְמֵנוּ כִּדְמוּתֵנוּ וְיִרְדּוּ בִדְגַת הַיָּם וּבְעוֹף הַשָּׁמַיִם וּבַבְּהֵמָה וּבְכָל־הָאָרֶץ וּבְכָל־הָרֶמֶשׂ הָרֹמֵשׂ עַל־הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Elohim na'aseh adam betsalmenu kidmutenu veyirdu vidgat hayam uve'of hashamayim uvabehemah uvekhol-ha'aretz uvekhol-haremes haromes al-ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The most theologically dense verse in Genesis 1. Unique features: (1) Divine deliberation — 'Let us make' introduces a heavenly counsel. (2) The plural 'us/our' — Christians see the Trinity in dialogue. (3) 'Image and likeness' — humanity uniquely reflects God in rationality, morality, creativity, and relational capacity. (4) Dominion — humanity is God's vice-regent, ruling creation on His behalf as stewards.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Imago Dei (Image of God): This doctrine is the foundation of human dignity, equality, ethics, and rights. Every human being bears God's image regardless of age, ability, race, or status.

verses.kjv_text: So God created man in his own image, in the image of God created he him; male and female created he them.
verses.original_text: וַיִּבְרָא אֱלֹהִים אֶת־הָאָדָם בְּצַלְמוֹ בְּצֶלֶם אֱלֹהִים בָּרָא אֹתוֹ זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה בָּרָא אֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayivra Elohim et-ha'adam betsalmo betselem Elohim bara oto zakhar unekevah bara otam.
verses.study_note: 'Bara' appears THREE times in this verse — a poetic triplet emphasizing the supreme significance of human creation. The threefold repetition may echo the Trinity. Both male and female bear God's image equally. Gender distinction is part of God's original design.
verses.doctrinal_note: Both male and female bear God's image equally. Gender distinction is part of God's original creative design, not a result of the Fall.

verses.kjv_text: And God blessed them, and God said unto them, Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth, and subdue it: and have dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the air, and over every living thing that moveth upon the earth.
verses.original_text: וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתָם אֱלֹהִים וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶם אֱלֹהִים פְּרוּ וּרְבוּ וּמִלְאוּ אֶת־הָאָרֶץ וְכִבְשֻׁהָ וּרְדוּ בִּדְגַת הַיָּם וּבְעוֹף הַשָּׁמַיִם וּבְכָל־חַיָּה הָרֹמֶשֶׂת עַל־הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayevarekh otam Elohim vayomer lahem Elohim peru urevu umil'u et-ha'aretz vekhivshuha uredu bidgat hayam uve'of hashamayim uvekhol-chayyah haromeset al-ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The Cultural Mandate: God's first words to humanity — a blessing followed by five commands: (1) Be fruitful, (2) Multiply, (3) Fill the earth, (4) Subdue it, (5) Have dominion. This is stewardship, not exploitation. Work, family, and cultural development are part of God's original 'very good' design.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Cultural Mandate: Human civilization — work, art, science, culture, family — is not secular but sacred. This mandate has never been revoked.

verses.kjv_text: And God said, Behold, I have given you every herb bearing seed, which is upon the face of all the earth, and every tree, in the which is the fruit of a tree yielding seed; to you it shall be for meat.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים הִנֵּה נָתַתִּי לָכֶם אֶת־כָּל־עֵשֶׂב זֹרֵעַ זֶרַע אֲשֶׁר עַל־פְּנֵי כָל־הָאָרֶץ וְאֶת־כָּל־הָעֵץ אֲשֶׁר־בּוֹ פְרִי־עֵץ זֹרֵעַ זָרַע לָכֶם יִהְיֶה לְאָכְלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Elohim hinneh natatti lakhem et-kol-esev zorea zera asher al-penei khol-ha'aretz ve'et-kol-ha'etz asher-bo feri-etz zorea zara lakhem yihyeh le'okhlah.
verses.study_note: God's original provision was a plant-based diet. Meat-eating is permitted only after the Flood (Gen 9:3). This vegetarian beginning points to the ideal state of creation before the Fall, and Isaiah 11:6-9 suggests a return to this harmony.

verses.kjv_text: And to every beast of the earth, and to every fowl of the air, and to every thing that creepeth upon the earth, wherein there is life, I have given every green herb for meat: and it was so.
verses.original_text: וּלְכָל־חַיַּת הָאָרֶץ וּלְכָל־עוֹף הַשָּׁמַיִם וּלְכֹל רוֹמֵשׂ עַל־הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר־בּוֹ נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה אֶת־כָּל־יֶרֶק עֵשֶׂב לְאָכְלָה וַיְהִי־כֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Ulekhol-chayyat ha'aretz ulekhol-of hashamayim ulekhol romes al-ha'aretz asher-bo nefesh chayyah et-kol-yerek esev le'okhlah vayehi-khen.
verses.study_note: All animals were also given plants for food — the original creation was entirely non-predatory. No death in the animal kingdom before the Fall. Isaiah 11:6-7 envisions a future restoration where 'the lion shall eat straw like the ox.'

verses.kjv_text: And God saw every thing that he had made, and, behold, it was very good. And the evening and the morning were the sixth day.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא אֱלֹהִים אֶת־כָּל־אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה וְהִנֵּה־טוֹב מְאֹד וַיְהִי־עֶרֶב וַיְהִי־בֹקֶר יוֹם הַשִּׁשִּׁי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayar Elohim et-kol-asher asah vehineh-tov me'od vayehi-erev vayehi-voker yom hashishi.
verses.study_note: The grand finale. God declares creation 'very good' (tov me'od) — the only superlative used. Everything functions in perfect harmony. Note 'THE sixth day' uniquely has the definite article (ha-shishi), giving this final day special prominence. This is the benchmark against which the Fall (Gen 3) and New Creation (Rev 21-22) must be understood.
verses.doctrinal_note: The original creation was entirely good — free from sin, death, suffering, and disorder. Evil is not part of God's design but an intrusion. The biblical narrative arcs from 'very good' through the Fall, through redemption, to ultimate restoration when God will 'make all things new' (Rev 21:5).

1.meaning: In the beginning — the absolute starting point

2.meaning: Without form — formlessness, chaos, confusion. The earth in its initial unformed state.
Void — emptiness, waste. Together with 'tohu,' it describes total desolation — unformed and unfilled.
The deep — the primordial abyss, the deep waters. Here it is simply water, not a deity as in pagan myths.
Spirit/Wind/Breath — here 'the Spirit of God,' the Holy Spirit. The same word means spirit, wind, and breath.
Moved/Hovered/Brooded — like a bird hovering protectively over its nest (cf. Deut 32:11). Conveys tender, creative energy.

3.meaning: And He said — the first recorded words of God. Creation by divine speech demonstrates absolute authority.
Let there be — jussive (command) form. Not a wish but a sovereign decree.
Light — physical electromagnetic radiation, also symbolizing God's presence, truth, and revelation throughout Scripture.

4.meaning: Good — beautiful, pleasant, excellent, fit for purpose. God's aesthetic and moral evaluation of His work.
Divided/Separated — to make a distinction, to set apart. A key creation verb — God creates order through holy distinctions.

5.meaning: Called/Named — to name is to exercise sovereignty and authority over something.
Day — can refer to a 24-hour period, daylight hours, or an era. With 'evening and morning,' marks a defined period.
Evening — the setting, the mixing of light and dark. The Hebrew day begins at evening.
Morning — the breaking through, the dawn.
One/First — a unified one. Same word used in the Shema to describe God's oneness, expressing composite unity.

6.meaning: Firmament/Expanse — from 'raqa' (to spread out, expand). The vast expanse of the sky/atmosphere.

7.meaning: Made/Fashioned — 'asah' means to make from existing material, distinct from 'bara' (create from nothing).
And it was so — God's commands are immediately and completely fulfilled.

8.meaning: Heaven/Sky — here the atmospheric heaven, as distinct from God's dwelling place (Deut 10:14).

9.meaning: Be gathered — to collect, bind together. The waters converge into defined basins.
Dry land — firm ground, the habitable surface of the earth emerges.

11.meaning: Grass/Vegetation — tender, young plant growth. The simplest category of plant life.
Herb/Plant — seed-bearing plants, including grains and vegetables.
After its kind — according to its species/type. Reproduction within kinds.

14.meaning: Lights/Luminaries — light-bearers or light-holders, not the source of light but vessels that regulate it.
Signs — signals, markers. Celestial bodies serve as God's visible signs (cf. Star of Bethlehem).
Seasons/Appointed times — literally 'appointed meetings.' Later used for Israel's feast days (Leviticus 23).

16.meaning: To rule/govern — delegated authority under God's ultimate sovereignty.
The stars — mentioned almost as an afterthought, yet numbering in the billions. This understatement magnifies God's power.

20.meaning: Bring forth abundantly/Swarm — to teem in great numbers. God creates with lavish abundance.
Living creature/soul — the breath-soul, animating life principle. First use of 'nefesh' distinguishing animal from plant life.
Fowl/Flying creatures — birds and all creatures that fly.

21.meaning: Great whales/Sea monsters — large sea creatures. In pagan myth these were chaos deities

22.meaning: Blessed — the first divine blessing in Scripture. To endow with power for success, fruitfulness, and prosperity.
Be fruitful and multiply — the first command to living creatures. God's blessing enables what His command requires.

24.meaning: Cattle/Livestock — domesticated or large land animals.
Creeping things — small animals that move close to the ground: reptiles, insects, small mammals.
Beast/Wild animal — untamed creatures of the field and forest.

26.meaning: Let us make — first person plural cohortative. A divine deliberation before the supreme creative act.
Man/Humanity — from 'adamah' (ground). The one formed from dust who bears God's image.
Image — a representative figure, reflection of the original. Essential nature and structural likeness.
Likeness — resemblance, similitude. Relational and functional resemblance, not physical identity.
Have dominion — to rule as God's vice-regent, exercising stewardship under divine authority.

27.meaning: Created — appears THREE times, a rare poetic intensification emphasizing humanity's unique, sacred creation.
Male — from a root meaning 'to remember, to be mindful of.'
Female — from a root meaning 'to pierce, to designate.' Both equally bear God's image.

28.meaning: Blessed — God's empowering word enabling humanity to fulfill its mandate.
Be fruitful and multiply — the cultural mandate to populate the earth.
Subdue — bring under control, harness resources. Active cultivation, not passive existence.
Have dominion — rule as God's representative. Stewardship, not exploitation.

31.meaning: And behold! — an exclamation of wonder and delight. God beholds His completed creation with joyful satisfaction.
Very good — exceedingly, supremely good. The highest evaluation in the creation account.
THE sixth — uniquely has the definite article, unlike days 1-5, giving it special emphasis as the climactic day.

meta.overview: Genesis 2 provides a detailed account of Day 7 (God's rest) and then zooms into Day 6 — the creation of man and woman. While Genesis 1 gives the panoramic view, Genesis 2 gives the intimate portrait. God forms Adam from dust, breathes life into him, plants the Garden of Eden, establishes the first commandment (the tree prohibition), and creates Eve from Adam's side as his perfect complement. This chapter establishes marriage, work, and humanity's relationship with God.
meta.theme: Rest, Relationship & the Garden of God
meta.keyWordMeaning: To form, to fashion — like a potter shaping clay. Describes God's intimate, hands-on creation of man.
outline: Day 7 (vv.1-3): God rests — the Sabbath established
The Formation of Man (vv.4-7): Adam formed from dust, receives the breath of life
The Garden of Eden (vv.8-14): Paradise planted, the river and its branches
The First Commandment (vv.15-17): Tend the garden, do not eat of the tree
The Creation of Woman (vv.18-25): Not good to be alone — Eve from Adam's side

verses.kjv_text: Thus the heavens and the earth were finished, and all the host of them.
verses.original_text: וַיְכֻלּוּ הַשָּׁמַיִם וְהָאָרֶץ וְכָל־צְבָאָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayekhullu hashamayim veha'aretz vekhol-tzeva'am.
verses.study_note: 'Finished' (yekhullu) — creation is complete, lacking nothing. 'Host' (tzeva'am) means the vast array — stars, angels, creatures, everything. God's creative work was comprehensive and perfect.

verses.kjv_text: And on the seventh day God ended his work which he had made; and he rested on the seventh day from all his work which he had made.
verses.original_text: וַיְכַל אֱלֹהִים בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי מְלַאכְתּוֹ אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה וַיִּשְׁבֹּת בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי מִכָּל־מְלַאכְתּוֹ אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayekhal Elohim bayom hashevi'i melakhto asher asah vayishbot bayom hashevi'i mikol-melakhto asher asah.
verses.study_note: 'Rested' (shabbat) does not imply fatigue but cessation — God stopped because creation was complete, not because He was tired (Isaiah 40:28). The seventh day has no closing formula ('evening and morning') — God's rest is ongoing.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Sabbath principle: God built rhythms of work and rest into creation itself. The Sabbath points forward to our ultimate rest in Christ (Hebrews 4:9-10).

verses.kjv_text: And God blessed the seventh day, and sanctified it: because that in it he had rested from all his work which God created and made.
verses.original_text: וַיְבָרֶךְ אֱלֹהִים אֶת־יוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי וַיְקַדֵּשׁ אֹתוֹ כִּי בוֹ שָׁבַת מִכָּל־מְלַאכְתּוֹ אֲשֶׁר בָּרָא אֱלֹהִים לַעֲשׂוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayevarekh Elohim et-yom hashevi'i vayekaddesh oto ki vo shavat mikol-melakhto asher bara Elohim la'asot.
verses.study_note: God 'blessed' and 'sanctified' (set apart) the seventh day — the first thing declared holy in Scripture is not a place or person but a day. 'Created and made' (bara...la'asot) combines both words for divine creation, summarizing the entire week.
verses.doctrinal_note: Sanctification means 'set apart for God.' Before the Law, before Israel, God established the principle that time itself belongs to Him.

verses.kjv_text: These are the generations of the heavens and of the earth when they were created, in the day that the LORD God made the earth and the heavens,
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה תוֹלְדוֹת הַשָּׁמַיִם וְהָאָרֶץ בְּהִבָּרְאָם בְּיוֹם עֲשׂוֹת יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים אֶרֶץ וְשָׁמָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Eleh toledot hashamayim veha'aretz behibbar'am beyom asot Yahweh Elohim eretz veshamayim.
verses.study_note: 'Toledot' (generations/account) — this heading appears 11 times in Genesis, structuring the entire book. Here we meet the full divine name 'Yahweh Elohim' (LORD God) for the first time — the covenant-keeping Creator.

verses.kjv_text: And every plant of the field before it was in the earth, and every herb of the field before it grew: for the LORD God had not caused it to rain upon the earth, and there was not a man to till the ground.
verses.original_text: וְכֹל שִׂיחַ הַשָּׂדֶה טֶרֶם יִהְיֶה בָאָרֶץ וְכָל־עֵשֶׂב הַשָּׂדֶה טֶרֶם יִצְמָח כִּי לֹא הִמְטִיר יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים עַל־הָאָרֶץ וְאָדָם אַיִן לַעֲבֹד אֶת־הָאֲדָמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol siach hasadeh terem yihyeh va'aretz vekhol-esev hasadeh terem yitzmach ki lo himtir Yahweh Elohim al-ha'aretz ve'adam ayin la'avod et-ha'adamah.
verses.study_note: Two things were lacking: rain and a gardener. This sets the stage for God's dual provision — water (v.6) and man (v.7). The wordplay between 'adam' (man) and 'adamah' (ground) is foundational.

verses.kjv_text: But there went up a mist from the earth, and watered the whole face of the ground.
verses.original_text: וְאֵד יַעֲלֶה מִן־הָאָרֶץ וְהִשְׁקָה אֶת־כָּל־פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'ed ya'aleh min-ha'aretz vehishkah et-kol-penei ha'adamah.
verses.study_note: 'Ed' (mist/spring) — God provided water from below before rain came from above. The earth was irrigated by underground springs, suggesting a different pre-Flood ecology.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD God formed man of the dust of the ground, and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life; and man became a living soul.
verses.original_text: וַיִּיצֶר יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים אֶת־הָאָדָם עָפָר מִן־הָאֲדָמָה וַיִּפַּח בְּאַפָּיו נִשְׁמַת חַיִּים וַיְהִי הָאָדָם לְנֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitzer Yahweh Elohim et-ha'adam aphar min-ha'adamah vayyippach be'appav nishmat chayyim vayehi ha'adam lenefesh chayyah.
verses.study_note: The most intimate act of creation. 'Formed' (yatsar) — like a potter with clay (cf. Isaiah 64:8). 'Breathed' (naphach) — God gives mouth-to-mouth resuscitation to a clay figure. 'Nishmat chayyim' (breath of life) — the divine spark that makes humans unique. 'Nephesh chayyah' (living soul) — man is not a body with a soul; he became a soul.
verses.doctrinal_note: The dual nature of humanity: dust (humble origin, mortality) + divine breath (dignity, eternal capacity). We are both earthly and heavenly, material and spiritual.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD God planted a garden eastward in Eden; and there he put the man whom he had formed.
verses.original_text: וַיִּטַּע יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים גַּן־בְּעֵדֶן מִקֶּדֶם וַיָּשֶׂם שָׁם אֶת־הָאָדָם אֲשֶׁר יָצָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitta Yahweh Elohim gan-be'Eden mikkedem vayyasem sham et-ha'adam asher yatsar.
verses.study_note: 'Eden' means 'delight/pleasure' — God prepared a place of joy for man. 'Planted' — God Himself is the first gardener. 'Eastward' (mikkedem) can also mean 'from ancient times,' suggesting Eden was prepared from the beginning.

verses.kjv_text: And out of the ground made the LORD God to grow every tree that is pleasant to the sight, and good for food; the tree of life also in the midst of the garden, and the tree of knowledge of good and evil.
verses.original_text: וַיַּצְמַח יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים מִן־הָאֲדָמָה כָּל־עֵץ נֶחְמָד לְמַרְאֶה וְטוֹב לְמַאֲכָל וְעֵץ הַחַיִּים בְּתוֹךְ הַגָּן וְעֵץ הַדַּעַת טוֹב וָרָע׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyatzmach Yahweh Elohim min-ha'adamah kol-etz nechmad lemar'eh vetov lema'akhal ve'etz hachayyim betokh haggan ve'etz hadda'at tov vara.
verses.study_note: Two special trees: (1) The Tree of Life — sustaining eternal life, later guarded by cherubim (3:24), reappearing in Revelation 22:2. (2) The Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil — the test of obedience. 'Pleasant to the sight and good for food' — God appeals to both beauty and sustenance.
verses.doctrinal_note: The two trees represent two paths: life through trust and obedience (Tree of Life), or autonomy apart from God (Tree of Knowledge). This choice echoes throughout all of Scripture.

verses.kjv_text: And a river went out of Eden to water the garden; and from thence it was parted, and became into four heads.
verses.original_text: וְנָהָר יֹצֵא מֵעֵדֶן לְהַשְׁקוֹת אֶת־הַגָּן וּמִשָּׁם יִפָּרֵד וְהָיָה לְאַרְבָּעָה רָאשִׁים׃
verses.transliteration: Venahar yotze me'Eden lehashkot et-haggan umisham yippared vehayah le'arba'ah rashim.
verses.study_note: A single river from Eden divides into four — abundance flows from God's presence. The garden is the source, not the destination, of life-giving water. This imagery reappears in Ezekiel 47 and Revelation 22:1.

verses.kjv_text: The name of the first is Pison: that is it which compasseth the whole land of Havilah, where there is gold;
verses.original_text: שֵׁם הָאֶחָד פִּישׁוֹן הוּא הַסֹּבֵב אֵת כָּל־אֶרֶץ הַחֲוִילָה אֲשֶׁר־שָׁם הַזָּהָב׃
verses.transliteration: Shem ha'echad Pishon hu hassovev et kol-eretz Havilah asher-sham hazzahav.
verses.study_note: Pishon means 'gushing.' Havilah is associated with wealth. The detailed geography emphasizes Eden was a real place, not merely symbolic, though its exact location is debated due to the Flood.

verses.kjv_text: And the gold of that land is good: there is bdellium and the onyx stone.
verses.original_text: וּזֲהַב הָאָרֶץ הַהִוא טוֹב שָׁם הַבְּדֹלַח וְאֶבֶן הַשֹּׁהַם׃
verses.transliteration: Uzahav ha'aretz hahi tov sham habbedolach ve'even hashoham.
verses.study_note: Gold, bdellium (aromatic resin), and onyx — luxury materials later used in the Tabernacle (Exodus 25, 28). Eden's treasures foreshadow worship. God surrounded Adam with beauty and abundance.

verses.kjv_text: And the name of the second river is Gihon: the same is it that compasseth the whole land of Ethiopia.
verses.original_text: וְשֵׁם הַנָּהָר הַשֵּׁנִי גִּיחוֹן הוּא הַסּוֹבֵב אֵת כָּל־אֶרֶץ כּוּשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Veshem hannahar hasheni Gichon hu hassovev et kol-eretz Kush.
verses.study_note: Gihon means 'bursting forth.' Kush (Cush/Ethiopia) — the river system of Eden watered vast regions. A spring named Gihon later supplied Jerusalem (1 Kings 1:33).

verses.kjv_text: And the name of the third river is Hiddekel: that is it which goeth toward the east of Assyria. And the fourth river is Euphrates.
verses.original_text: וְשֵׁם הַנָּהָר הַשְּׁלִישִׁי חִדֶּקֶל הוּא הַהֹלֵךְ קִדְמַת אַשּׁוּר וְהַנָּהָר הָרְבִיעִי הוּא פְרָת׃
verses.transliteration: Veshem hannahar hashlishi Chiddekel hu haholekh kidmat Asshur vehannahar harevi'i hu Ferat.
verses.study_note: Hiddekel is the Tigris; Euphrates (Ferat) is well known. These two rivers still flow today through Iraq. The Tigris and Euphrates bracket Mesopotamia — the 'cradle of civilization' — connecting Eden to known geography.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD God took the man, and put him into the garden of Eden to dress it and to keep it.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים אֶת־הָאָדָם וַיַּנִּחֵהוּ בְגַן־עֵדֶן לְעָבְדָהּ וּלְשָׁמְרָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach Yahweh Elohim et-ha'adam vayyannechehu vegan-Eden le'ovdah uleshomrah.
verses.study_note: 'Dress' (avad = to serve/work) and 'keep' (shamar = to guard/protect). Work existed before the Fall — it is not a curse but a calling. The same two words describe the Levites' duties in the Tabernacle (Numbers 3:7-8), suggesting Adam was a priest-king in God's garden-temple.
verses.doctrinal_note: Work is a divine vocation. Adam was given meaningful purpose before sin entered. The command to 'guard' implies there was something to guard against — foreshadowing the serpent of chapter 3.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD God commanded the man, saying, Of every tree of the garden thou mayest freely eat:
verses.original_text: וַיְצַו יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים עַל־הָאָדָם לֵאמֹר מִכֹּל עֵץ־הַגָּן אָכֹל תֹּאכֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayetzav Yahweh Elohim al-ha'adam lemor mikkol etz-haggan akhol tokhel.
verses.study_note: 'Freely eat' (akhol tokhel — an emphatic doubling) — God's first word to man is generous permission, not restriction. The emphasis is on abundant provision. Every tree is available except one.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's character: lavish generosity with one reasonable boundary. The prohibition that follows must be understood against this backdrop of overwhelming permission.

verses.kjv_text: But of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, thou shalt not eat of it: for in the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die.
verses.original_text: וּמֵעֵץ הַדַּעַת טוֹב וָרָע לֹא תֹאכַל מִמֶּנּוּ כִּי בְּיוֹם אֲכָלְךָ מִמֶּנּוּ מוֹת תָּמוּת׃
verses.transliteration: Ume'etz hadda'at tov vara lo tokhal mimmennu ki beyom akholkha mimmennu mot tamut.
verses.study_note: 'Surely die' (mot tamut — 'dying you shall die') — another emphatic doubling. This is both immediate (spiritual death — separation from God) and eventual (physical death). The prohibition tests whether man will trust God's word or seek autonomous knowledge.
verses.doctrinal_note: The first covenant: God's generosity (freely eat everything) with one condition (not this tree) and a clear consequence (death). This pattern — grace, law, consequence — runs through all of Scripture.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD God said, It is not good that the man should be alone; I will make him an help meet for him.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים לֹא־טוֹב הֱיוֹת הָאָדָם לְבַדּוֹ אֶעֱשֶׂה־לּוֹ עֵזֶר כְּנֶגְדּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Yahweh Elohim lo-tov heyot ha'adam levaddo e'eseh-lo ezer kenegdo.
verses.study_note: The first 'not good' in creation. After six 'goods' and one 'very good,' God declares solitude 'not good.' 'Ezer kenegdo' — 'helper corresponding to him.' 'Ezer' (helper) is used of God Himself 16 times in the OT (Psalm 33:20, 70:5). It implies strength, not inferiority. 'Kenegdo' means 'opposite/corresponding' — equal yet complementary.
verses.doctrinal_note: Human beings are designed for relationship. Aloneness is the first thing God identifies as 'not good.' The woman is not an afterthought but the completion of God's design.

verses.kjv_text: And out of the ground the LORD God formed every beast of the field, and every fowl of the air; and brought them unto Adam to see what he would call them: and whatsoever Adam called every living creature, that was the name thereof.
verses.original_text: וַיִּצֶר יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים מִן־הָאֲדָמָה כָּל־חַיַּת הַשָּׂדֶה וְאֵת כָּל־עוֹף הַשָּׁמַיִם וַיָּבֵא אֶל־הָאָדָם לִרְאוֹת מַה־יִּקְרָא־לוֹ וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר יִקְרָא־לוֹ הָאָדָם נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה הוּא שְׁמוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitzer Yahweh Elohim min-ha'adamah kol-chayyat hasadeh ve'et kol-of hashamayim vayyave el-ha'adam lir'ot mah-yikra-lo vekhol asher yikra-lo ha'adam nefesh chayyah hu shemo.
verses.study_note: God brings the animals to Adam — a parade of creation. 'To see what he would call them' — God gives Adam genuine freedom and authority. Naming demonstrates sovereignty and intelligence. This process also reveals that among all creatures, none is a suitable companion for man.

verses.kjv_text: And Adam gave names to all cattle, and to the fowl of the air, and to every beast of the field; but for Adam there was not found an help meet for him.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא הָאָדָם שֵׁמוֹת לְכָל־הַבְּהֵמָה וּלְעוֹף הַשָּׁמַיִם וּלְכֹל חַיַּת הַשָּׂדֶה וּלְאָדָם לֹא־מָצָא עֵזֶר כְּנֶגְדּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra ha'adam shemot lekhol-habbehemah ule'of hashamayim ulekhol chayyat hasadeh ule'adam lo-matza ezer kenegdo.
verses.study_note: The naming process had a purpose: Adam would discover his own need. No animal — however wonderful — could be his 'ezer kenegdo.' The felt lack prepares him to receive God's gift with gratitude and wonder.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD God caused a deep sleep to fall upon Adam, and he slept: and he took one of his ribs, and closed up the flesh instead thereof;
verses.original_text: וַיַּפֵּל יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים תַּרְדֵּמָה עַל־הָאָדָם וַיִּישָׁן וַיִּקַּח אַחַת מִצַּלְעֹתָיו וַיִּסְגֹּר בָּשָׂר תַּחְתֶּנָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyappel Yahweh Elohim tardemah al-ha'adam vayyishan vayyiqqach achat mittzal'otav vayyisgor basar tachttennah.
verses.study_note: 'Tardemah' (deep sleep) — the same word used when God made covenant with Abraham (Gen 15:12). This is a divine surgery under divine anesthesia. 'Tsela' is traditionally 'rib' but can mean 'side' — suggesting woman came from man's very side, his equal.

verses.kjv_text: And the rib, which the LORD God had taken from man, made he a woman, and brought her unto the man.
verses.original_text: וַיִּבֶן יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים אֶת־הַצֵּלָע אֲשֶׁר־לָקַח מִן־הָאָדָם לְאִשָּׁה וַיְבִאֶהָ אֶל־הָאָדָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiven Yahweh Elohim et-hattzela asher-laqach min-ha'adam le'ishshuh vayyevi'eha el-ha'adam.
verses.study_note: 'Built' (banah) — not 'formed' (yatsar) as with Adam. God 'built' the woman, implying careful, architectural craftsmanship. 'Brought her to the man' — God presents Eve to Adam like a father presenting a bride. The first wedding, with God as the officiant.
verses.doctrinal_note: God is the author of marriage. He designed it, He initiated it, and He presented the bride to the groom. Marriage is not a human invention but a divine institution.

verses.kjv_text: And Adam said, This is now bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh: she shall be called Woman, because she was taken out of Man.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר הָאָדָם זֹאת הַפַּעַם עֶצֶם מֵעֲצָמַי וּבָשָׂר מִבְּשָׂרִי לְזֹאת יִקָּרֵא אִשָּׁה כִּי מֵאִישׁ לֻקְחָה־זֹּאת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer ha'adam zot happa'am etzem me'atzamai uvasar mibbsari lezot yikkare ishah ki me'ish luqqachah-zot.
verses.study_note: Adam's first recorded words — and they are poetry! 'This at last!' (zot happa'am) — joyful recognition after the parade of animals. 'Bone of my bones, flesh of my flesh' — the language of covenant kinship. 'Ishah' (woman) from 'ish' (man) — the Hebrew wordplay shows their essential unity.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife: and they shall be one flesh.
verses.original_text: עַל־כֵּן יַעֲזָב־אִישׁ אֶת־אָבִיו וְאֶת־אִמּוֹ וְדָבַק בְּאִשְׁתּוֹ וְהָיוּ לְבָשָׂר אֶחָד׃
verses.transliteration: Al-ken ya'azov-ish et-aviv ve'et immo vedavak be'ishto vehayu levasar echad.
verses.study_note: The narrator's commentary — the foundational marriage text. Three actions: (1) Leave — a new primary loyalty, (2) Cleave (davak — cling, be glued to) — permanent covenant bond, (3) One flesh — a mysterious spiritual, emotional, and physical union. Jesus quotes this verse directly in Matthew 19:5.
verses.doctrinal_note: Marriage defined: one man, one woman, one lifetime. 'Leave, cleave, one flesh' — this is God's design from the very beginning, before the Fall, before culture, before law. Jesus affirms this as the Creator's original intent (Matt 19:4-6).

verses.kjv_text: And they were both naked, the man and his wife, and were not ashamed.
verses.original_text: וַיִּהְיוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם עֲרוּמִּים הָאָדָם וְאִשְׁתּוֹ וְלֹא יִתְבֹּשָׁשׁוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyihyu sheneihem arummim ha'adam ve'ishto velo yitboshashu.
verses.study_note: 'Naked and not ashamed' — the sign of perfect innocence. No guilt, no fear, no hiding, no barriers between them or between them and God. This stands in stark contrast to 3:7 where they immediately cover themselves. 'Arummim' (naked) is a wordplay with 'arum' (crafty) in 3:1, linking innocence and the coming temptation.
verses.doctrinal_note: The original state of humanity: transparent, vulnerable, unashamed before God and each other. Sin brings shame, covering, and hiding. The gospel restores us to shameless intimacy with God (Romans 8:1).

2.meaning: To cease, to rest — not from fatigue but from completion. The root of 'Sabbath.'
Work, craftsmanship — purposeful, skilled labor. Same word used for Tabernacle construction.

7.meaning: To form, fashion, shape — like a potter with clay. Intimate, hands-on creation.
Dust, dry earth — man's humble origin. 'Dust to dust' (Gen 3:19).
To breathe, blow — God personally gives the breath of life. Intimate and life-giving.
Breath of life — the divine animating force unique to humans. Not given to animals this way.
Living soul/being — the whole person: body + breath = living soul. Man is a unity.

9.meaning: Tree of Life — sustaining eternal life. Reappears in Proverbs 3:18, 11:30 and Revelation 2:7, 22:2.
Knowledge — experiential knowing, not merely intellectual. 'Good and evil' = moral autonomy apart from God.

15.meaning: To work, serve, till — same word for worshipping/serving God. Work is worship.
To keep, guard, protect — the priestly duty. Same word used for keeping God's commandments.

17.meaning: Knowledge of good and evil — the capacity to determine right and wrong independently of God.
Dying you shall die — emphatic: certain, inevitable death. Both spiritual (immediate) and physical (eventual).

18.meaning: Not good — the first negative evaluation in creation. Solitude was the only thing God called 'not good.'
Helper, ally, strength — used 16 times of God Himself (Ps 33:20). Implies power, not weakness.
Corresponding to him, opposite him — equal and complementary. A mirror-match, not a subordinate.

22.meaning: Built — not 'formed' (yatsar) or 'made' (asah). Architectural craftsmanship. God carefully constructed woman.
Rib or side — can mean 'side chamber.' Woman came from man's side: not above to rule, not below to be trampled, but beside as equal.

24.meaning: Leave, forsake — establishing a new primary loyalty. Marriage creates a new family unit.
Cleave, cling, be joined — like glue. Permanent, unbreakable covenant bond. Same word for clinging to God (Deut 10:20).
One flesh — a profound unity: spiritual, emotional, physical. 'Echad' (one) is the same word as in the Shema (Deut 6:4).

verses.kjv_text: Now the serpent was more subtil than any beast of the field which the LORD God had made. And he said unto the woman, Yea, hath God said, Ye shall not eat of every tree of the garden?
verses.original_text: וְהַנָּחָשׁ הָיָה עָרוּם מִכֹּל חַיַּת הַשָּׂדֶה אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל־הָאִשָּׁה אַף כִּי־אָמַר אֱלֹהִים לֹא תֹאכְלוּ מִכֹּל עֵץ הַגָּן׃
verses.transliteration: Vehanachash hayah arum mikkol chayyat hasadeh asher asah Yahweh Elohim vayyomer el-ha'ishah af ki-amar Elohim lo tokhelu mikkol etz haggan.
verses.study_note: 'Arum' (crafty/subtle) — a wordplay with 'arummim' (naked) from 2:25. Innocence meets cunning. The serpent's first tactic: questioning God's word — 'Did God really say?' He also distorts the command, implying God restricted all the trees, when in fact He gave all but one. Satan always makes God seem restrictive.
verses.doctrinal_note: Satan's strategy has three steps that never change: (1) Question God's word — 'Did God really say?' (2) Deny God's judgment — 'You shall not surely die' (3) Promise godhood — 'You shall be as gods.' Every temptation follows this pattern.

verses.kjv_text: And the woman said unto the serpent, We may eat of the fruit of the trees of the garden:
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר הָאִשָּׁה אֶל־הַנָּחָשׁ מִפְּרִי עֵץ־הַגָּן נֹאכֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomer ha'ishah el-hannachash mipperi etz-haggan nokhel.
verses.study_note: Eve correctly states they may eat from the garden trees — but notice she is already in conversation with the serpent, entertaining his question rather than rejecting it outright.

verses.kjv_text: But of the fruit of the tree which is in the midst of the garden, God hath said, Ye shall not eat of it, neither shall ye touch it, lest ye die.
verses.original_text: וּמִפְּרִי הָעֵץ אֲשֶׁר בְּתוֹךְ־הַגָּן אָמַר אֱלֹהִים לֹא תֹאכְלוּ מִמֶּנּוּ וְלֹא תִגְּעוּ בּוֹ פֶּן־תְּמֻתוּן׃
verses.transliteration: Umipperi ha'etz asher betokh-haggan amar Elohim lo tokhelu mimmennu velo tigge'u bo pen-temutun.
verses.study_note: Eve makes three subtle changes to God's word: (1) She omits 'freely' from God's generous permission, (2) She adds 'neither shall ye touch it' — God never said this, (3) She softens 'surely die' to 'lest ye die.' When we mishandle God's word, we become vulnerable to deception.

verses.kjv_text: And the serpent said unto the woman, Ye shall not surely die:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר הַנָּחָשׁ אֶל־הָאִשָּׁה לֹא־מוֹת תְּמֻתוּן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer hannachash el-ha'ishah lo-mot temutun.
verses.study_note: The first lie in recorded history — a direct contradiction of God's word. God said 'dying you shall die' (mot tamut); the serpent says 'not dying you shall die' (lo-mot temutun). Satan simply puts a 'not' in front of God's truth. Jesus calls Satan 'the father of lies' (John 8:44).

verses.kjv_text: For God doth know that in the day ye eat thereof, then your eyes shall be opened, and ye shall be as gods, knowing good and evil.
verses.original_text: כִּי יֹדֵעַ אֱלֹהִים כִּי בְּיוֹם אֲכָלְכֶם מִמֶּנּוּ וְנִפְקְחוּ עֵינֵיכֶם וִהְיִיתֶם כֵּאלֹהִים יֹדְעֵי טוֹב וָרָע׃
verses.transliteration: Ki yodea Elohim ki beyom akholkhem mimmennu venifkechu eineikhem vihyitem kelohim yodei tov vara.
verses.study_note: The serpent impugns God's motives — 'God is keeping something good from you.' Then the ultimate temptation: 'ye shall be as gods' (kelohim). This is the root sin — wanting to be autonomous, to define good and evil for ourselves, to be our own god. Their eyes were indeed 'opened' (v.7), but to shame, not glory.
verses.doctrinal_note: The essence of sin is not primarily a behavior but a disposition: wanting to be God instead of trusting God. Every sin flows from this root — the desire for autonomy over submission, self-will over God's will.

verses.kjv_text: And when the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was pleasant to the eyes, and a tree to be desired to make one wise, she took of the fruit thereof, and did eat, and gave also unto her husband with her; and he did eat.
verses.original_text: וַתֵּרֶא הָאִשָּׁה כִּי טוֹב הָעֵץ לְמַאֲכָל וְכִי תַאֲוָה־הוּא לָעֵינַיִם וְנֶחְמָד הָעֵץ לְהַשְׂכִּיל וַתִּקַּח מִפִּרְיוֹ וַתֹּאכַל וַתִּתֵּן גַּם־לְאִישָׁהּ עִמָּהּ וַיֹּאכַל׃
verses.transliteration: Vattere ha'ishah ki tov ha'etz lema'akhal vekhi ta'avah-hu la'einayim venechmad ha'etz lehaskil vattiqqach mippiryo vattokhel vatitten gam-le'ishah immah vayyokhal.
verses.study_note: Three appeals: (1) 'good for food' — lust of the flesh, (2) 'pleasant to the eyes' — lust of the eyes, (3) 'desired to make one wise' — pride of life. The same three temptations appear in 1 John 2:16 and Satan's temptation of Jesus (Matt 4). 'With her' — Adam was present the entire time, silent when he should have spoken. Five devastating verbs: saw, took, ate, gave, he ate.
verses.doctrinal_note: Adam's sin was arguably greater than Eve's. She was deceived (1 Tim 2:14); he sinned with full knowledge, choosing his wife over God. As representative head of humanity, it is Adam's sin that plunges the race into death (Rom 5:12).

verses.kjv_text: And the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked; and they sewed fig leaves together, and made themselves aprons.
verses.original_text: וַתִּפָּקַחְנָה עֵינֵי שְׁנֵיהֶם וַיֵּדְעוּ כִּי עֵירֻמִּם הֵם וַיִּתְפְּרוּ עֲלֵה תְאֵנָה וַיַּעֲשׂוּ לָהֶם חֲגֹרֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vatippakachnah einei sheneihem vayyedu ki erummim hem vayyitperu aleh te'enah vayyaasu lahem chagorot.
verses.study_note: Their eyes were opened — but not to glory, as promised, but to shame. The immediate instinct: cover themselves. Fig leaves — humanity's first attempt at self-redemption. 'Naked' (erummim) echoes 2:25 — what was once innocent is now shameful. Contrast this with 3:21 where God provides lasting coverings.
verses.doctrinal_note: The fig leaves represent every human attempt to cover sin through our own effort — religion, morality, good works. They are inadequate. Only God can provide true covering (v.21).

verses.kjv_text: And they heard the voice of the LORD God walking in the garden in the cool of the day: and Adam and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the LORD God amongst the trees of the garden.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁמְעוּ אֶת־קוֹל יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים מִתְהַלֵּךְ בַּגָּן לְרוּחַ הַיּוֹם וַיִּתְחַבֵּא הָאָדָם וְאִשְׁתּוֹ מִפְּנֵי יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים בְּתוֹךְ עֵץ הַגָּן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishme'u et-qol Yahweh Elohim mithalekh baggan leruach hayyom vayyitchabbe ha'adam ve'ishto mippenei Yahweh Elohim betokh etz haggan.
verses.study_note: 'Walking in the garden' (mithalekh) — suggests God's regular fellowship with Adam. 'Cool of the day' (ruach hayyom) — literally 'the wind/spirit of the day.' The same God who walked in Eden later 'tabernacled' among us (John 1:14). They hid among the trees — the very trees God gave them. Sin turns gifts into hiding places.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD God called unto Adam, and said unto him, Where art thou?
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים אֶל־הָאָדָם וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ אַיֶּכָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra Yahweh Elohim el-ha'adam vayyomer lo ayyekkah.
verses.study_note: 'Where are you?' (Ayyekkah) — God's first question to fallen humanity. Not because He didn't know, but to draw Adam out, to make him face his condition. This is the voice of grace pursuing sinners. God comes seeking; Adam is hiding. The gospel pattern begins here — God always initiates.
verses.doctrinal_note: The seeking God: Before Adam sought God, God sought Adam. This pattern runs through all of Scripture — God seeking the lost (Luke 15, 19:10). We love because He first loved us (1 John 4:19).

verses.kjv_text: And he said, I heard thy voice in the garden, and I was afraid, because I was naked; and I hid myself.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֶת־קֹלְךָ שָׁמַעְתִּי בַּגָּן וָאִירָא כִּי־עֵירֹם אָנֹכִי וָאֵחָבֵא׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer et-qolekha shamati baggan va'ira ki-erom anokhi va'echave.
verses.study_note: The first fear. The first shame. The first hiding. Three new experiences that did not exist before sin. 'I was afraid' — fear of God replaces fellowship with God. 'I was naked' — awareness of exposure. 'I hid' — the instinct to flee from the One who is our only hope.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Who told thee that thou wast naked? Hast thou eaten of the tree, whereof I commanded thee that thou shouldest not eat?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מִי הִגִּיד לְךָ כִּי עֵירֹם אָתָּה הֲמִן־הָעֵץ אֲשֶׁר צִוִּיתִיךָ לְבִלְתִּי אֲכָל־מִמֶּנּוּ אָכָלְתָּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer mi higgid lekha ki erom attah hamin-ha'etz asher tzivvitikhya levilti akhol-mimmennu akhalta.
verses.study_note: God asks diagnostic questions — not for information but for confession. 'Who told you?' targets the source of false knowledge. 'Have you eaten?' demands honest admission. God gives Adam the opportunity to confess, to own his sin and receive mercy.

verses.kjv_text: And the man said, The woman whom thou gavest to be with me, she gave me of the tree, and I did eat.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר הָאָדָם הָאִשָּׁה אֲשֶׁר נָתַתָּה עִמָּדִי הִוא נָתְנָה־לִּי מִן־הָעֵץ וָאֹכֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer ha'adam ha'ishah asher natattah immadi hi natnah-li min-ha'etz va'okhel.
verses.study_note: The first blame-shifting. Adam blames Eve AND God — 'the woman YOU gave me.' Instead of taking responsibility, he shifts blame upward (to God) and sideways (to Eve). Sin breaks accountability as well as fellowship.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD God said unto the woman, What is this that thou hast done? And the woman said, The serpent beguiled me, and I did eat.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים לָאִשָּׁה מַה־זֹּאת עָשִׂית וַתֹּאמֶר הָאִשָּׁה הַנָּחָשׁ הִשִּׁיאַנִי וָאֹכֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Yahweh Elohim la'ishah mah-zot asit vattomer ha'ishah hannachash hishi'ani va'okhel.
verses.study_note: Eve follows the pattern — she blames the serpent. 'Beguiled' (hishi'ani) means deceived, led astray. While the deception was real (1 Tim 2:14), it does not eliminate responsibility. Note: God does not ask the serpent for an explanation — there is no opportunity for repentance for Satan.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD God said unto the serpent, Because thou hast done this, thou art cursed above all cattle, and above every beast of the field; upon thy belly shalt thou go, and dust shalt thou eat all the days of thy life:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים אֶל־הַנָּחָשׁ כִּי עָשִׂיתָ זֹּאת אָרוּר אַתָּה מִכָּל־הַבְּהֵמָה וּמִכֹּל חַיַּת הַשָּׂדֶה עַל־גְּחֹנְךָ תֵלֵךְ וְעָפָר תֹּאכַל כָּל־יְמֵי חַיֶּיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Yahweh Elohim el-hannachash ki asita zot arur attah mikkol-habbehemah umikkol chayyat hasadeh al-gechonekha telekh ve'aphar tokhal kol-yemei chayyeykha.
verses.study_note: The first curse in Scripture falls on the serpent. 'Cursed above all' — the strongest curse. 'Upon thy belly' — the serpent's posture becomes a permanent symbol of humiliation and defeat. 'Dust shalt thou eat' — perpetual degradation. The physical serpent bears the mark of Satan's use.

verses.kjv_text: And I will put enmity between thee and the woman, and between thy seed and her seed; it shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel.
verses.original_text: וְאֵיבָה אָשִׁית בֵּינְךָ וּבֵין הָאִשָּׁה וּבֵין זַרְעֲךָ וּבֵין זַרְעָהּ הוּא יְשׁוּפְךָ רֹאשׁ וְאַתָּה תְּשׁוּפֶנּוּ עָקֵב׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'eivah ashit beinkha uvein ha'ishah uvein zar'akha uvein zar'ah hu yeshufkha rosh ve'attah teshufennu aqev.
verses.study_note: The PROTOEVANGELIUM — the first gospel proclamation. 'Her seed' — remarkable because seed is always traced through the male line, foreshadowing the virgin birth. 'He shall bruise thy head' — a fatal, crushing blow. 'Thou shalt bruise his heel' — painful but not fatal. Christ on the cross: His heel was bruised (crucifixion), but He crushed Satan's head (victory over sin and death).
verses.doctrinal_note: This single verse contains the entire plan of redemption: (1) Conflict between good and evil throughout history, (2) A male descendant of the woman (Christ, born of a virgin), (3) Temporary suffering of the Redeemer (the cross), (4) Permanent defeat of Satan (the resurrection). Every Bible story from here forward is about this promised Seed.

verses.kjv_text: Unto the woman he said, I will greatly multiply thy sorrow and thy conception; in sorrow thou shalt bring forth children; and thy desire shall be to thy husband, and he shall rule over thee.
verses.original_text: אֶל־הָאִשָּׁה אָמַר הַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה עִצְּבוֹנֵךְ וְהֵרֹנֵךְ בְּעֶצֶב תֵּלְדִי בָנִים וְאֶל־אִישֵׁךְ תְּשׁוּקָתֵךְ וְהוּא יִמְשָׁל־בָּךְ׃
verses.transliteration: El-ha'ishah amar harbah arbeh itzzevonekh veheronekh be'etzev teldi vanim ve'el-ishekh teshuqatekh vehu yimshal-bakh.
verses.study_note: 'Sorrow' (etzev) — the same word used for Adam's toil (v.17). Pain touches both their callings. 'Desire' (teshuqah) — used again in 4:7 where sin's desire is to master Cain. The harmonious relationship of Eden is now marked by tension and power struggle. This is descriptive of the Fall's consequences, not prescriptive of God's ideal.

verses.kjv_text: And unto Adam he said, Because thou hast hearkened unto the voice of thy wife, and hast eaten of the tree, of which I commanded thee, saying, Thou shalt not eat of it: cursed is the ground for thy sake; in sorrow shalt thou eat of it all the days of thy life;
verses.original_text: וּלְאָדָם אָמַר כִּי־שָׁמַעְתָּ לְקוֹל אִשְׁתֶּךָ וַתֹּאכַל מִן־הָעֵץ אֲשֶׁר צִוִּיתִיךָ לֵאמֹר לֹא תֹאכַל מִמֶּנּוּ אֲרוּרָה הָאֲדָמָה בַּעֲבוּרֶךָ בְּעִצָּבוֹן תֹּאכְלֶנָּה כֹּל יְמֵי חַיֶּיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Ule'adam amar ki-shamata leqol ishtekha vattokhel min-ha'etz asher tzivvitikhya lemor lo tokhal mimmennu arurah ha'adamah ba'avurekha be'itztzavon tokhalennah kol yemei chayyeykha.
verses.study_note: Note: God curses the ground, not Adam directly. 'Because you listened to your wife's voice' — not that listening to one's wife is wrong, but that Adam chose her voice over God's command. 'In sorrow' (itztzavon) — the same root as the woman's pain. Work, once joyful, now becomes toilsome.

verses.kjv_text: Thorns also and thistles shall it bring forth to thee; and thou shalt eat the herb of the field;
verses.original_text: וְקוֹץ וְדַרְדַּר תַּצְמִיחַ לָךְ וְאָכַלְתָּ אֶת־עֵשֶׂב הַשָּׂדֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Veqotz vedardar tatzmiyach lakh ve'akhalta et-esev hasadeh.
verses.study_note: Thorns and thistles — the ground itself now resists human effort. Nature, once a partner, becomes an adversary. Thorns reappear symbolically: in Christ's crown of thorns (Matt 27:29), He bore the curse literally on His head.

verses.kjv_text: In the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat bread, till thou return unto the ground; for out of it wast thou taken: for dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return.
verses.original_text: בְּזֵעַת אַפֶּיךָ תֹּאכַל לֶחֶם עַד שׁוּבְךָ אֶל־הָאֲדָמָה כִּי מִמֶּנָּה לֻקָּחְתָּ כִּי־עָפָר אַתָּה וְאֶל־עָפָר תָּשׁוּב׃
verses.transliteration: Beze'at appekha tokhal lechem ad shuvkha el-ha'adamah ki mimmennah luqqachta ki-aphar attah ve'el-aphar tashuv.
verses.study_note: 'Dust to dust' — the sentence of physical death. What God breathed life into (2:7) now returns to dust. This is the ultimate consequence: mortality. Yet even here there is hope — 'return to the ground' is not the final word. The One who breathed life can breathe it again (John 11:25).
verses.doctrinal_note: Physical death entered the world through Adam's sin (Romans 5:12). Every funeral, every grave, every tear traces back to this verse. But Christ, 'the last Adam' (1 Cor 15:45), reverses the curse through resurrection.

verses.kjv_text: And Adam called his wife’s name Eve; because she was the mother of all living.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא הָאָדָם שֵׁם אִשְׁתּוֹ חַוָּה כִּי הִוא הָיְתָה אֵם כָּל־חָי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra ha'adam shem ishto Chavvah ki hi haytah em kol-chai.
verses.study_note: 'Eve' (Chavvah) means 'life' or 'living.' Remarkably, Adam names her this after the death sentence — an act of faith! He believed God's promise in v.15 that the woman's seed would come. Despite death, life will continue through her. This is the first expression of saving faith after the Fall.

verses.kjv_text: Unto Adam also and to his wife did the LORD God make coats of skins, and clothed them.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַשׂ יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים לְאָדָם וּלְאִשְׁתּוֹ כָּתְנוֹת עוֹר וַיַּלְבִּשֵׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaas Yahweh Elohim le'adam ule'ishto kotnot or vayyalbishem.
verses.study_note: 'Coats of skins' — an animal died to cover human shame. This is the first death, the first blood shed, and it was God Himself who performed it. The fig leaves of human effort (v.7) are replaced by God's provision. This foreshadows the entire sacrificial system and ultimately Christ — 'the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world' (John 1:29).
verses.doctrinal_note: The first sacrifice: God provided what man could not provide for himself. An innocent life was taken to cover the guilty. This is the gospel in seed form — substitutionary atonement. God replaces our inadequate self-covering with His perfect provision.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD God said, Behold, the man is become as one of us, to know good and evil: and now, lest he put forth his hand, and take also of the tree of life, and eat, and live for ever:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים הֵן הָאָדָם הָיָה כְּאַחַד מִמֶּנּוּ לָדַעַת טוֹב וָרָע וְעַתָּה פֶּן־יִשְׁלַח יָדוֹ וְלָקַח גַּם מֵעֵץ הַחַיִּים וְאָכַל וָחַי לְעֹלָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Yahweh Elohim hen ha'adam hayah ke'achad mimmennu lada'at tov vara ve'attah pen-yishlach yado velaqach gam me'etz hachayyim ve'akhal vachai le'olam.
verses.study_note: 'As one of us' — the Trinity speaks again (cf. 1:26). Preventing access to the Tree of Life was mercy, not cruelty — to live forever in a fallen, cursed state would be eternal misery without hope of redemption. God blocks the path to immortality so that death, and through death, resurrection and renewal, can accomplish salvation.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's banishment from Eden was an act of mercy. Eternal life in a fallen state would be eternal suffering. God closes one door (the Tree of Life in Eden) to open another (the Tree of Life in the New Jerusalem — Rev 22:2).

verses.kjv_text: Therefore the LORD God sent him forth from the garden of Eden, to till the ground from whence he was taken.
verses.original_text: וַיְשַׁלְּחֵהוּ יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים מִגַּן־עֵדֶן לַעֲבֹד אֶת־הָאֲדָמָה אֲשֶׁר לֻקַּח מִשָּׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayeshallechehu Yahweh Elohim miggan-Eden la'avod et-ha'adamah asher luqqach misham.
verses.study_note: 'Sent forth' — exile from paradise. Man returns to the ground from which he was taken (2:7), but now under the curse. Yet 'sent forth' is not the same as 'abandoned.' God sends Adam out with a promise (v.15), a provision (v.21), and a purpose.

verses.kjv_text: So he drove out the man; and he placed at the east of the garden of Eden Cherubims, and a flaming sword which turned every way, to keep the way of the tree of life.
verses.original_text: וַיְגָרֶשׁ אֶת־הָאָדָם וַיַּשְׁכֵּן מִקֶּדֶם לְגַן־עֵדֶן אֶת־הַכְּרֻבִים וְאֵת לַהַט הַחֶרֶב הַמִּתְהַפֶּכֶת לִשְׁמֹר אֶת־דֶּרֶךְ עֵץ הַחַיִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayegaresh et-ha'adam vayyashken mikkedem legan-Eden et-hakkeruvim ve'et lahat hacherev hammithappekhet lishmor et-derekh etz hachayyim.
verses.study_note: 'Drove out' (garash) — a stronger word than 'sent forth.' Cherubim — angelic guardians who later appear on the Ark of the Covenant (Ex 25:18-22) and in Ezekiel's visions. The flaming sword 'guards the way' — using the same word 'shamar' from 2:15. The way to life is blocked — until Christ declares: 'I am the Way' (John 14:6).
verses.doctrinal_note: The way to the Tree of Life is guarded but not destroyed. God preserves it. Through Christ, the way is reopened: 'To him who overcomes I will give to eat from the tree of life, which is in the midst of the Paradise of God' (Rev 2:7). Eden lost becomes Paradise restored.

meta.overview: Genesis 3 is the hinge of human history — the chapter that explains why the world is broken and why we need a Savior. The serpent deceives Eve, Adam willingly disobeys, and sin enters creation. Immediately everything changes: shame replaces innocence, blame replaces unity, and death replaces life. Yet in the midst of judgment, God speaks the first promise of redemption (v.15) — the 'Protoevangelium' (first gospel). God seeks the sinners, covers their shame, and promises a Deliverer.
meta.theme: The Fall, the Curse & the First Promise of Redemption
meta.keyWordMeaning: Serpent — also related to the verb meaning 'to practice divination' or 'to shine.' The deceiver who brought humanity's downfall.
outline: The Temptation (vv.1-5): The serpent questions, denies, and contradicts God's word
The Fall (vv.6-7): Eve takes, eats, gives to Adam — innocence lost
God Seeks the Sinners (vv.8-13): Where are you? — God's first question to fallen man
The Protoevangelium (v.15): The first gospel — the Seed of the woman will crush the serpent
The Consequences (vv.14-19): Curse on serpent, pain in childbirth, toil in work, death
Grace in Judgment (vv.20-21): Eve named, God provides coverings of skin
Exile from Eden (vv.22-24): Driven out, cherubim guard the way to the Tree of Life

verses.kjv_text: And Adam knew Eve his wife; and she conceived, and bare Cain, and said, I have gotten a man from the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְהָאָדָם יָדַע אֶת־חַוָּה אִשְׁתּוֹ וַתַּהַר וַתֵּלֶד אֶת־קַיִן וַתֹּאמֶר קָנִיתִי אִישׁ אֶת־יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Veha'adam yada et-Chavvah ishto vattehar vateled et-Qayin vatomer qaniti ish et-Yahweh.
verses.study_note: 'Knew' (yada) — the most intimate Hebrew word for conjugal union, implying total personal knowledge. The exile from Eden has not destroyed the blessing of Gen 1:28 — human life continues by God's provision. 'I have gotten a man from the LORD' (qaniti ish et-Yahweh) — Eve's joyful exclamation. Some scholars read this as 'I have gotten a man: the LORD' — a messianic hope that her firstborn might be the Promised Seed of Gen 3:15. She is looking for the Deliverer. 'Qayin' (Cain) sounds like the Hebrew qanah, 'to acquire/get.' Even in exile, God's grace sustains human life.

verses.kjv_text: And she again bare his brother Abel. And Abel was a keeper of sheep, but Cain was a tiller of the ground.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּסֶף לָלֶדֶת אֶת־אָחִיו אֶת־הָבֶל וַיְהִי הֶבֶל רֹעֵה צֹאן וְקַיִן הָיָה עֹבֵד אֲדָמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vattosef laledet et-achiv et-Hevel vayehi Hevel roeh tzon veQayin hayah oved adamah.
verses.study_note: 'Abel' (Hevel) means 'breath' or 'vapor' — the very word Ecclesiastes uses for life's transience ('vanity of vanities'). His name anticipates his brief life. Both occupations are honorable — shepherd and farmer both appear in the patriarchal narratives as dignified callings. There is no moral distinction implied in their work. The difference lies not in their professions but in their hearts when they worship. 'Tiller of the ground' (oved adamah) echoes Adam's mandate in 2:15 and the curse of 3:17-18.

verses.kjv_text: And in process of time it came to pass, that Cain brought of the fruit of the ground an offering unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי מִקֵּץ יָמִים וַיָּבֵא קַיִן מִפְּרִי הָאֲדָמָה מִנְחָה לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi miqqetz yamim vayave Qayin mipperi ha'adamah minchah la-Yahweh.
verses.study_note: 'In process of time' (miqqetz yamim) — literally 'at the end of days,' suggesting a regular appointed time for worship. Both brothers come to present offerings to the LORD — this is spontaneous worship, emerging from human hearts before any formal law commanded it. The instinct to worship is built into human nature (Gen 1:27). Cain brings 'of the fruit of the ground' — notice the vague, unspecified language. Scripture does not say he brought the first or best; simply 'some of the fruit.' The contrast with Abel's offering (v.4) will be telling.

verses.kjv_text: And Abel, he also brought of the firstlings of his flock and of the fat thereof. And the LORD had respect unto Abel and to his offering:
verses.original_text: וְהֶבֶל הֵבִיא גַם־הוּא מִבְּכֹרוֹת צֹאנוֹ וּמֵחֶלְבֵהֶן וַיִּשַׁע יְהוָה אֶל־הֶבֶל וְאֶל־מִנְחָתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehevel hevi gam-hu mibbekhorot tzono umechelvehem vayyisha Yahweh el-Hevel ve'el-minchatoh.
verses.study_note: Three specific details mark Abel's offering as distinctly different: (1) 'Firstlings' (bekhorot) — the firstborn of the flock, the most valuable, given before he knew if others would follow, (2) 'of the fat thereof' — the choicest portion, reserved for God alone (Lev 3:16: 'all the fat is the LORD's'), (3) 'he also' — implying deliberate, willing action. Abel gives what is most precious without being asked. God 'had respect' (sha'ah — to gaze upon with approval) to Abel AND to his offering — the person is accepted before the gift. Hebrews 11:4 tells us Abel offered 'by faith.' Faith is the invisible ingredient that transforms an offering.
verses.doctrinal_note: Why was Abel's offering accepted? Not because he used animals while Cain used grain — grain offerings were later commanded in the Law (Lev 2). The difference was faith (Heb 11:4) and heart attitude (1 John 3:12 — 'his works were righteous'). Abel gave his best and first; Cain gave generically. God always sees the giver behind the gift. 'Man looketh on the outward appearance, but the LORD looketh on the heart' (1 Sam 16:7).

verses.kjv_text: But unto Cain and to his offering he had not respect. And Cain was very wroth, and his countenance fell.
verses.original_text: וְאֶל־קַיִן וְאֶל־מִנְחָתוֹ לֹא שָׁעָה וַיִּחַר לְקַיִן מְאֹד וַיִּפְּלוּ פָּנָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'el-Qayin ve'el-minchatoh lo sha'ah vayichar leQayin me'od vayyippelu fanav.
verses.study_note: The rejection comes first to Cain and then to his offering — mirroring v.4 where Abel was accepted before his gift. This is not arbitrary divine preference; it reflects the state of the worshipper. 'Very wroth' (yichar me'od — literally 'it burned greatly to Cain') — the same root as the 'flaming' sword of 3:24. His anger is incendiary. 'His countenance fell' — the outward face mirrors the inward collapse. God will address this directly in vv.6-7. Rather than examining his heart, Cain directs his anger outward — at his brother and ultimately at God.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Cain, Why art thou wroth? and why is thy countenance fallen?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־קַיִן לָמָּה חָרָה לָךְ וְלָמָּה נָפְלוּ פָנֶיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Yahweh el-Qayin lammah charah lakh velammah naflu fanekha.
verses.study_note: God comes to Cain before the murder, not after — this is divine grace intervening in the window of opportunity. 'Why?' (lammah) — two diagnostic questions, just as God asked 'Where are you?' of Adam (3:9) and 'Where is Abel?' will come (v.9). God is not asking for information but issuing an invitation to self-examination, confession, and repentance. The door of grace is still open. God addresses the warning signs: the visible symptoms (fallen countenance, burning anger) before the fatal act. This is God as the preventive physician of the soul.

verses.kjv_text: If thou doest well, shalt thou not be accepted? and if thou doest not well, sin lieth at the door. And unto thee shall be his desire, and thou shalt rule over him.
verses.original_text: הֲלוֹא אִם־תֵּיטִיב שְׂאֵת וְאִם לֹא תֵיטִיב לַפֶּתַח חַטָּאת רֹבֵץ וְאֵלֶיךָ תְּשׁוּקָתוֹ וְאַתָּה תִּמְשָׁל־בּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Halo im-tetiv se'et ve'im lo tetiv lappetach chattat rovetz ve'eleykhah teshukato ve'attah timshol-bo.
verses.study_note: One of the most profound warnings in Scripture. God gives Cain a clear binary: do well and be accepted, or face what is crouching at his door. 'Sin' (chattat) is personified as a predatory animal 'crouching/lying in wait' (rovetz — used of a lion ready to pounce, Gen 49:9). Sin is not passive; it is aggressive and territorial. 'His desire' (teshuqah) — the identical word used in 3:16 for the woman's desire toward her husband in the context of conflict. Sin's desire is to possess and master Cain. But God says 'thou shalt rule over him' — the capacity to resist was present. The door was not yet crossed. Cain chose not to listen.
verses.doctrinal_note: The doctrine of sin's nature: Sin is portrayed here as a crouching predator, alert and hungry. This verse teaches: (1) Sin is premeditated — it lies in wait, (2) Sin desires to master its host ('his desire is toward you'), (3) Sin can be resisted — 'thou shalt rule over him.' The NT echoes this: 'resist the devil and he will flee' (James 4:7), 'be sober, be vigilant; your adversary the devil, as a roaring lion, walketh about, seeking whom he may devour' (1 Pet 5:8).

verses.kjv_text: And Cain talked with Abel his brother: and it came to pass, when they were in the field, that Cain rose up against Abel his brother, and slew him.
