verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר קַיִן אֶל־הֶבֶל אָחִיו וַיְהִי בִּהְיוֹתָם בַּשָּׂדֶה וַיָּקָם קַיִן אֶל־הֶבֶל אָחִיו וַיַּהַרְגֵהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Qayin el-Hevel achiv vayehi biheyotam bassadeh vayyaqam Qayin el-Hevel achiv vayyahargehu.
verses.study_note: The Hebrew text here is unusual: 'Cain said to Abel his brother' without recording what he said. The Samaritan Pentateuch, LXX, and Peshitta add 'Let us go into the field' — Scripture's omission may be deliberate, silencing Cain's treachery. The word 'brother' (achiv) is repeated emphatically — 'his brother' appears four times in verses 8-11, pounding the magnitude of the betrayal. 'Rose up against' (vayyaqam) — a sudden, decisive act of violence. The same word used of standing up against an enemy. He chose the field — isolated, away from any witness, just as Cain had been warned. The crouching sin had pounced.
verses.doctrinal_note: The world's first murder was fratricide — a brother killing a brother. This is the first death in human history, and it was not natural but violent. The progression from sin to murder: envy (unaccepted offering) → anger (countenance fell) → rejection of God's counsel (vv.6-7) → premeditation ('talked with Abel') → murder. James 1:15: 'sin, when it is finished, bringeth forth death.' And the motive was religious jealousy — Abel's righteousness was Cain's accusation.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Cain, Where is Abel thy brother? And he said, I know not: Am I my brother’s keeper?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־קַיִן אֵי הֶבֶל אָחִיךָ וַיֹּאמֶר לֹא יָדַעְתִּי הֲשֹׁמֵר אָחִי אָנֹכִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Yahweh el-Qayin ei Hevel achikha vayyomer lo yadati hashomer achi anokhi.
verses.study_note: God asks the second great searching question of human history: 'Where is Abel your brother?' — deliberately echoing 'Where are you?' of 3:9. God again seeks the sinner, not for information but for confrontation and potential repentance. Cain's response is the first recorded lie: 'I know not.' He is lying to the omniscient God. Then the brazen deflection: 'Am I my brother's keeper?' (hashomer achi anokhi). The Hebrew word 'shomer' (keeper/guardian) is the same word God used for Adam's duty in the garden (2:15, 'to keep it'). Cain is asking if he had a duty of care — and the answer, which he already knows, is yes.
verses.doctrinal_note: The doctrine of human responsibility: 'Am I my brother's keeper?' is the question every generation must answer. The Law commands it (Lev 19:18), Christ commands it (Matt 22:39), and the Good Samaritan parable (Luke 10:29-37) was Jesus' direct answer to this exact question asked in a different form. We are indeed our brother's keeper.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, What hast thou done? the voice of thy brother’s blood crieth unto me from the ground.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֶה עָשִׂיתָ קוֹל דְּמֵי אָחִיךָ צֹעֲקִים אֵלַי מִן־הָאֲדָמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer meh asita qol demei achikha tzo'aqim elai min-ha'adamah.
verses.study_note: God's declaration is staggering in its poetry: 'the voice of your brother's bloods' (demei — plural) 'is crying out to me from the ground.' The Hebrew uses the plural 'bloods' (demei) — a grammatical intensification suggesting Abel's blood and the blood of all his descendants who would never be born. Blood 'crying out' — the ground that was cursed to resist Adam (3:17) now becomes the witness against Cain. This cry reaches God directly. No crime is truly hidden; all injustice has a voice before God. This will become the basis for the prohibition of murder in Gen 9:5-6.
verses.doctrinal_note: Blood as witness: Hebrews 12:24 draws the ultimate contrast — the blood of Christ 'speaks better things than that of Abel.' Abel's blood cried for justice; Christ's blood cries for mercy. Both are heard by God. No crime is hidden from the God who hears the blood of the innocent. This is the theological foundation for capital justice and divine accountability.

verses.kjv_text: And now art thou cursed from the earth, which hath opened her mouth to receive thy brother’s blood from thy hand;
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה אָרוּר אַתָּה מִן־הָאֲדָמָה אֲשֶׁר פָּצְתָה אֶת־פִּיהָ לָקַחַת אֶת־דְּמֵי אָחִיךָ מִיָּדֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah arur attah min-ha'adamah asher patzetah et-piha laqachat et-demei achikha miyyadekha.
verses.study_note: The curse falls directly on Cain — unlike Adam's curse which fell on the ground (3:17), Cain's curse comes from the ground. The ground which received Abel's blood now rejects Cain. It 'opened its mouth' — a vivid personification of the earth as horrified witness. 'From your hand' — the instrument of the crime becomes the focus of the judgment. The punishment is proportional: a farmer who desecrated the ground is banished from it.

verses.kjv_text: When thou tillest the ground, it shall not henceforth yield unto thee her strength; a fugitive and a vagabond shalt thou be in the earth.
verses.original_text: כִּי תַעֲבֹד אֶת־הָאֲדָמָה לֹא־תֹסֵף תֵּת־כֹּחָהּ לָךְ נָע וָנָד תִּהְיֶה בָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Ki ta'avod et-ha'adamah lo-tosef tet-kochah lakh na venad tihyeh va'aretz.
verses.study_note: The punishment is a double curse: (1) Agricultural failure — the ground, which Cain worked and loved, refuses to yield. His identity and vocation are stripped away. (2) Wandering — 'fugitive and vagabond' (na venad) is an alliterative pair meaning 'one who staggers and wanders.' The same root 'nad' gives us the land of 'Nod' (v.16). Cain is condemned to restlessness — the opposite of the Sabbath rest God intended. No home, no roots, no fruitful soil. This is a portrait of a life without God.

verses.kjv_text: And Cain said unto the LORD, My punishment is greater than I can bear.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר קַיִן אֶל־יְהוָה גָּדוֹל עֲוֹנִי מִנְּשֹׂא׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Qayin el-Yahweh gadol avoni minse.
verses.study_note: 'My punishment is greater than I can bear' — the Hebrew is ambiguous and profound: 'avoni' can mean both 'my iniquity' and 'my punishment' — the same word covers both the sin and its consequence. Cain may be saying 'my sin is too great to be forgiven' OR 'my punishment is too heavy to endure' — or both simultaneously. Remarkably, Cain still speaks to the LORD, acknowledging both God and his own condition. This is not repentance (there is no acknowledgment of wrongdoing, no sorrow for Abel) — but it is not total despair either.

verses.kjv_text: Behold, thou hast driven me out this day from the face of the earth; and from thy face shall I be hid; and I shall be a fugitive and a vagabond in the earth; and it shall come to pass, that every one that findeth me shall slay me.
verses.original_text: הֵן גֵּרַשְׁתָּ אֹתִי הַיּוֹם מֵעַל פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה וּמִפָּנֶיךָ אֶסָּתֵר וְהָיִיתִי נָע וָנָד בָּאָרֶץ וְהָיָה כָל־מֹצְאִי יַהַרְגֵנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Hen gerashta oti hayyom me'al penei ha'adamah umipanekha essater vehayiti na venad ba'aretz vehayah kol-motze'i yahargeini.
verses.study_note: Cain articulates his true terror: not the loss of agricultural productivity, but separation from God's face. 'From Thy face shall I be hid' — this is the deepest consequence of sin: exile from divine presence. This was the essence of the Fall itself (3:8). For all his guilt, Cain grasps that life without God's face is unbearable. There is also a practical fear: without God's protection (the mark, v.15), he is vulnerable to blood vengeance. The question of 'who would find him?' has generated much discussion about the population of the ancient world.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto him, Therefore whosoever slayeth Cain, vengeance shall be taken on him sevenfold. And the LORD set a mark upon Cain, lest any finding him should kill him.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ יְהוָה לָכֵן כָּל־הֹרֵג קַיִן שִׁבְעָתַיִם יֻקָּם וַיָּשֶׂם יְהוָה לְקַיִן אוֹת לְבִלְתִּי הַכּוֹת־אֹתוֹ כָּל־מֹצְאוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer lo Yahweh lakhen kol-horeg Qayin shiv'atayim yuqqam vayyasem Yahweh leQayin ot levilti hakkot-oto kol-motz'o.
verses.study_note: The most startling mercy in the chapter: God protects the murderer. Not condoning the murder — the punishment stands — but preventing a cycle of retaliatory violence. 'Sevenfold vengeance' establishes God's sovereign monopoly on justice (Deut 32:35, Rom 12:19). 'A mark' (ot — the same word as 'signs' in 1:14) — not a mark of shame but a mark of protection. We do not know its nature. The word 'ot' in Genesis always indicates a gracious covenant sign (Gen 9:12-13, 17:11). Even in judgment God extends mercy to the guilty — this is the scandalous grace of the God who pursued Adam and now marks Cain.
verses.doctrinal_note: Common grace: God's preservation of Cain illustrates the doctrine of common grace — God's goodness extended even to the unrepentant. He does not simply destroy the murderer. He preserves him, sets limits on retribution, and allows civilization to continue. This is not approval but governance. The mark of Cain is a mercy mark, not a curse mark.

verses.kjv_text: And Cain went out from the presence of the LORD, and dwelt in the land of Nod, on the east of Eden.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּצֵא קַיִן מִלִּפְנֵי יְהוָה וַיֵּשֶׁב בְּאֶרֶץ נוֹד קִדְמַת עֵדֶן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyetze Qayin millifnei Yahweh vayyeshev be'eretz Nod qidmat Eden.
verses.study_note: 'Went out from the presence of the LORD' — the saddest journey in Genesis after the exile of 3:24. 'Nod' (Nod) comes from the same root as 'wanderer' (nad, v.12) — literally 'the land of wandering.' It is not merely a geographic location but a spiritual state: to live east of Eden (qidmat Eden — same direction as the exile from the garden, 3:24) is to live in the far country of separation from God. Jonah used the same phrase when he fled from God's presence (Jonah 1:3, 10). The city Cain builds in the next verse is an attempt to create security and meaning apart from God.

verses.kjv_text: And Cain knew his wife; and she conceived, and bare Enoch: and he builded a city, and called the name of the city, after the name of his son, Enoch.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּדַע קַיִן אֶת־אִשְׁתּוֹ וַתַּהַר וַתֵּלֶד אֶת־חֲנוֹךְ וַיְהִי בֹּנֶה עִיר וַיִּקְרָא שֵׁם הָעִיר כְּשֵׁם בְּנוֹ חֲנוֹךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyeda Qayin et-ishto vattehar vateled et-Chanokh vayehi boneh ir vayyikra shem ha'ir keshem beno Chanokh.
verses.study_note: Cain builds the first city — named after his son Enoch (Chanokh, 'dedication'). The condemned wanderer immediately begins building a permanent settlement — attempting to create security and legacy through human achievement and urban civilization. This is the 'city of man' — human culture and community organized apart from God. Augustine saw in this the foundation of his 'city of man' vs 'city of God' theological framework. Cities in themselves are not evil; but civilization built on the principle of independence from God is the Babel-pattern. God's wife question leads to another question — where did she come from? She was Adam's daughter, per the implications of Gen 5.

verses.kjv_text: And unto Enoch was born Irad: and Irad begat Mehujael: and Mehujael begat Methusael: and Methusael begat Lamech.
verses.original_text: וַיִּוָּלֵד לַחֲנוֹךְ אֶת־עִירָד וְעִירָד יָלַד אֶת־מְחוּיָאֵל וּמְחִיָּיאֵל יָלַד אֶת־מְתוּשָׁאֵל וּמְתוּשָׁאֵל יָלַד אֶת־לָמֶךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyivaled laChanokh et-Irad ve'Irad yalad et-Mechuyael umechiyyael yalad et-Metushaael uMetushaael yalad et-Lamekh.
verses.study_note: The genealogy of Cain's line through six generations to Lamech. This 'city of man' genealogy contrasts with the 'city of God' genealogy of Seth's line (5:1ff). Both lines share some similar names (Enoch, Lamech, Methusael/Methuselah) — a reminder that common humanity connects both streams. The Cainite line produces cultural advancement (vv.20-22) but also escalating violence (v.23-24). Technology and civilization without God produce both beauty and danger.

verses.kjv_text: And Lamech took unto him two wives: the name of the one was Adah, and the name of the other Zillah.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח לוֹ לֶמֶךְ שְׁתֵּי נָשִׁים שֵׁם הָאַחַת עָדָה וְשֵׁם הַשֵּׁנִית צִלָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach lo Lamekh shetei nashim shem ha'achat Adah veshem hashenit Tzillah.
verses.study_note: The first recorded instance of polygamy — a departure from God's design of one man and one woman (2:24). Lamech, not God, initiates this pattern. Adah means 'ornament' or 'dawn'; Zillah means 'shade' or 'shadow.' Polygamy is recorded here without explicit condemnation in the narrative, but every subsequent instance in Scripture is attended by conflict and sorrow. The fact that it appears first in the violent Cainite line is instructive.

verses.kjv_text: And Adah bare Jabal: he was the father of such as dwell in tents, and of such as have cattle.
verses.original_text: וַתֵּלֶד עָדָה אֶת־יָבָל הוּא הָיָה אֲבִי יֹשֵׁב אֹהֶל וּמִקְנֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vateled Adah et-Yaval hu hayah avi yoshev ohel umikneh.
verses.study_note: Jabal — 'father of those who dwell in tents and raise livestock.' The first nomadic pastoralist, the ancestor of the herding and ranching tradition. 'Father' here means originator or pioneer. The Cainite line, despite its spiritual darkness, makes genuine cultural contributions. Common grace enables those outside the covenant to develop skills and arts that benefit all of humanity. Abel was a keeper of sheep (v.2) — Jabal systematizes and expands this into a way of life.

verses.kjv_text: And his brother’s name was Jubal: he was the father of all such as handle the harp and organ.
verses.original_text: וְשֵׁם אָחִיו יוּבָל הוּא הָיָה אֲבִי כָּל־תֹּפֵשׂ כִּנּוֹר וְעוּגָב׃
verses.transliteration: Veshem achiv Yuval hu hayah avi kol-tofes kinnor ve'ugav.
verses.study_note: Jubal — 'father of all who play the harp and flute.' The inventor of music and musical instruments. 'Kinnor' (harp/lyre) and 'ugav' (pipe/flute) — the first mention of musical instruments in Scripture. Music is a gift of common grace, present even in the Cainite line, pointing to the universal human need for beauty and worship. The human capacity for music reflects the image of God (Gen 1:27), present even in those who do not acknowledge Him. David will sanctify the kinnor for worship; the Levites will fill the Temple with ugav.

verses.kjv_text: And Zillah, she also bare Tubalcain, an instructer of every artificer in brass and iron: and the sister of Tubalcain was Naamah.
verses.original_text: וְצִלָּה גַם־הִוא יָלְדָה אֶת־תּוּבַל קַיִן לֹטֵשׁ כָּל־חֹרֵשׁ נְחֹשֶׁת וּבַרְזֶל וַאֲחוֹת תּוּבַל קַיִן נַעֲמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vetzillah gam-hi yaledah et-Tuval Qayin lotesh kol-choresh nechoshet uvarzel va'achot Tuval Qayin Na'amah.
verses.study_note: Tubal-cain — 'instructor/forger of every cutting instrument in bronze and iron.' The first metallurgist; the father of weapons and tools. His name incorporates 'Cain' (qayin), connecting him to the murderous line. Bronze and iron technology represents both the capacity for civilization (agricultural tools) and destruction (weapons of war). The mention of his sister Naamah ('pleasantness') without explanation is mysterious — some ancient traditions connect her to significant roles, but Scripture simply names her without elaboration.

verses.kjv_text: And Lamech said unto his wives, Adah and Zillah, Hear my voice; ye wives of Lamech, hearken unto my speech: for I have slain a man to my wounding, and a young man to my hurt.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לֶמֶךְ לְנָשָׁיו עָדָה וְצִלָּה שְׁמַעַן קוֹלִי נְשֵׁי לֶמֶךְ הַאֲזֵנָּה אִמְרָתִי כִּי אִישׁ הָרַגְתִּי לְפִצְעִי וְיֶלֶד לְחַבֻּרָתִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Lamekh lenashav Adah veTzillah shema'an qoli neshei Lamekh ha'azinnah imrati ki ish haraqti lefitz'i veyelved lehavurati.
verses.study_note: The 'Song of the Sword' or Lamech's Boast — the first recorded poem in Scripture after Adam's joyful song in 2:23. But where Adam sang of love and union, Lamech sings of killing and vengeance. He boasts to his wives about murdering a man who merely wounded him — disproportionate, brutal vengeance. This song represents the apex of Cainite culture: artistic sophistication (lyric poetry) in the service of glorified violence. The same gift of language God gave for worship and love is here used to celebrate murder.
verses.doctrinal_note: The escalation of sin: from Cain's single unplanned murder (v.8), to Lamech's deliberate, premeditated, proudly celebrated killing seven generations later. This is sin's trajectory when unchecked — each generation more brazen than the last. The 'Song of the Sword' is a cultural monument to depravity. Paul quotes a similar principle: 'the wages of sin is death' (Rom 6:23) — but the Cainite line does not reckon with wages; they celebrate the act.

verses.kjv_text: If Cain shall be avenged sevenfold, truly Lamech seventy and sevenfold.
verses.original_text: כִּי שִׁבְעָתַיִם יֻקַּם קָיִן וְלֶמֶךְ שִׁבְעִים וְשִׁבְעָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ki shiv'atayim yuqqam Qayin veLamekh shiv'im veshiv'ah.
verses.study_note: Lamech inverts the mercy of v.15. God promised Cain sevenfold protection; Lamech claims seventy-sevenfold vengeance for himself. He has usurped divine prerogative — God's protective sevenfold is twisted into human seventy-sevenfold retaliation. This is the Cainite line at its end: violent, boastful, self-sufficient, self-avenging. Jesus will reverse this completely in Matthew 18:22 — not seventy-sevenfold vengeance but seventy-sevenfold forgiveness. The same number, the same structure, the exact opposite spirit.
verses.doctrinal_note: The great reversal: Lamech claims 'seventy times seven' vengeance. Christ commands 'seventy times seven' forgiveness (Matt 18:22). Two uses of the same number: one rooted in pride and self-vindication, the other in grace and mercy. The cross is God's seventy-sevenfold answer to the Lamech principle.

verses.kjv_text: And Adam knew his wife again; and she bare a son, and called his name Seth: For God, said she, hath appointed me another seed instead of Abel, whom Cain slew.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּדַע אָדָם עוֹד אֶת־אִשְׁתּוֹ וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן וַתִּקְרָא אֶת־שְׁמוֹ שֵׁת כִּי שָׁת־לִי אֱלֹהִים זֶרַע אַחֵר תַּחַת הֶבֶל כִּי הֲרָגוֹ קָיִן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyeda Adam od et-ishto vateled ben vatiqra et-shemo Shet ki shat-li Elohim zera acher tachat Hevel ki harago Qayin.
verses.study_note: A pivot of enormous hope. After the darkness of the Cainite genealogy and Lamech's boast, the narrative returns to Adam and Eve with the birth of Seth. 'Seth' (Shet) means 'appointed/granted' — from the word 'shat' (to set, to appoint). 'Another seed instead of Abel' — the precise language of Gen 3:15: the promised Seed who will crush the serpent. Eve understands this birth in redemptive terms. The Messianic line runs through Seth, not Cain — through Enosh, Noah, Abraham, David, and ultimately to Christ (Luke 3:38).
verses.doctrinal_note: The substitution principle: Seth is given 'instead of Abel' (tachat Hevel). This word 'tachat' (instead of, in the place of) is the same word used for the ram 'instead of' Isaac in Gen 22:13. Substitution — one in the place of another — is the heartbeat of biblical redemption. Abel's death is answered by Seth's birth; Isaac's near-death is answered by the ram; our death is answered by Christ.

verses.kjv_text: And to Seth, to him also there was born a son; and he called his name Enos: then began men to call upon the name of the LORD.
verses.original_text: וּלְשֵׁת גַּם־הוּא יֻלַּד־בֵּן וַיִּקְרָא אֶת־שְׁמוֹ אֱנוֹשׁ אָז הוּחַל לִקְרֹא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ulashet gam-hu yullad-ben vayyikra et-shemo Enosh az huchal liqro beshem Yahweh.
verses.study_note: The chapter ends not with Lamech's boast but with prayer. 'Enos' (Enosh) means 'mortal man, frail humanity' — the name itself is a confession of dependence. 'Then began men to call upon the name of the LORD' — this is the first corporate, public worship recorded in Scripture. 'Call upon' (qara beshem Yahweh) — the same phrase used throughout the Psalms, by Elijah on Carmel (1 Kings 18:24), by the prophets (Joel 2:32, 'whosoever shall call on the name of the LORD shall be saved'), and by Paul in Romans 10:13 as the definition of saving faith. While the Cainite line built cities, forged weapons, and composed boastful poetry, the Sethite line called on God.
verses.doctrinal_note: The first corporate prayer: 'Calling on the name of the LORD' becomes the defining mark of the people of God throughout Scripture. Joel 2:32 quotes it as the way of salvation. Romans 10:13 makes it the New Testament gospel call: 'For whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.' What began with Enos reaches its fulfillment in every soul who calls on Christ.

meta.overview: Genesis 4 is the first chapter after the Fall — and it records the first birth, the first worship, the first murder, and the first city. Two brothers bring offerings to God; one is accepted, one rejected. Jealousy festers into rage, and the world witnesses its first killing. Yet even in this darkness, God pursues the murderer with questions, pronounces a measured judgment, and sets a protective mark on Cain. The chapter closes with profound hope: Seth is born as a replacement seed, and his son Enos inaugurates the practice of calling on the name of the LORD — the first corporate worship in Scripture.
meta.theme: Worship, Jealousy, Judgment & the Mercy of God
meta.keyWordMeaning: Offering, gift, tribute — the word at the heart of this chapter, distinguishing acceptable from unacceptable worship.
outline: The Birth of Cain and Abel (vv.1-2): Two brothers, two vocations
The Two Offerings (vv.3-5): Cain's produce and Abel's firstlings — one accepted, one not
God's Warning to Cain (vv.6-7): Sin crouches at the door — master it
The First Murder (v.8): Cain rises against Abel — the world's first death
God Confronts Cain (vv.9-12): Where is Abel? — the curse of the wanderer
The Mark of Cain (vv.13-16): God's protective mercy on the guilty
Cain's Lineage: Culture and Violence (vv.17-24): Cities, arts, and Lamech's boast
The Seed of Hope (vv.25-26): Seth born — calling on the name of the LORD

verses.kjv_text: This is the book of the generations of Adam. In the day that God created man, in the likeness of God made he him;
verses.original_text: זֶה סֵפֶר תּוֹלְדֹת אָדָם בְּיוֹם בְּרֹא אֱלֹהִים אָדָם בִּדְמוּת אֱלֹהִים עָשָׂה אֹתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Zeh sefer toledot adam beyom bero Elohim adam bidmut Elohim asah oto.
verses.study_note: The heading 'sefer toledot' (book of generations) is the first use of the word 'sefer' (book, written record) in Scripture — suggesting this genealogy was a real, written document. The phrase 'toledot adam' structures the entire book of Genesis (cf. 2:4; 6:9; 10:1; 11:10 etc.). Crucially, the chapter opens by returning to the Imago Dei of Gen 1:26-27 — 'in the likeness of God made He him.' This is not accidental. Before recording the long march of death ('and he died'), the narrator anchors every human life in its divine dignity. Even dying men and women bear God's image.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Imago Dei in the genealogy: Every name in this chapter represents a person who bore God's image. The 'and he died' that follows each entry is a tragedy — not God's intention (1:31) but sin's intrusion (3:19). The genealogy is simultaneously a tribute to human dignity and a lament for human mortality.

verses.kjv_text: Male and female created he them; and blessed them, and called their name Adam, in the day when they were created.
verses.original_text: זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה בְּרָאָם וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתָם וַיִּקְרָא אֶת־שְׁמָם אָדָם בְּיוֹם הִבָּרְאָם׃
verses.transliteration: Zakhar uneqevah bera'am vayevarekh otam vayyiqra et-shmam adam beyom hibbare'am.
verses.study_note: Both male and female are together called 'Adam' — a striking statement of equal human dignity and shared identity. The name Adam is both individual (the first man) and collective (humanity). God blessed both, named both, and gave both the same designation. This verse is a summary of Gen 1:27-28, deliberately recalled here at the start of the genealogy to remind the reader what is at stake in every human birth: divine image, divine blessing, divine naming.

verses.kjv_text: And Adam lived an hundred and thirty years, and begat a son in his own likeness, after his image; and called his name Seth:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי אָדָם שְׁלֹשִׁים וּמְאַת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בִּדְמוּתוֹ כְּצַלְמוֹ וַיִּקְרָא אֶת־שְׁמוֹ שֵׁת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Adam sheloshim ume'at shanah vayyoled bidmuto ketzalmo vayyiqra et-shemo Shet.
verses.study_note: A profound theological pivot: God created Adam 'in the likeness of God' (1:26; 5:1), but Adam begat Seth 'in his own likeness, after his image.' The formula is identical but the source has changed. Seth inherits Adam's image — and with it, both the dignity of the Imago Dei AND the corruption of the fallen nature. This is the biblical doctrine of original sin transmitted: every person born in the natural line of Adam inherits his image, which now includes both divine likeness (preserved) and fallen nature (acquired). Adam is 130 at Seth's birth — the chronology of Genesis 5 is intentional and consistent.
verses.doctrinal_note: Original sin and the Imago Dei: Both truths are held together in this verse. Seth is made in Adam's 'likeness and image' (the same words as 1:26) — showing the Imago Dei persists after the Fall. But now the transmitter is fallen Adam, not unfallen God. Every human born of natural generation inherits both God's image (dignity) and Adam's sin (depravity). Only the Second Adam (Christ, 1 Cor 15:45-49) restores the full image by being conceived by the Holy Spirit, not by natural generation.

verses.kjv_text: And the days of Adam after he had begotten Seth were eight hundred years: and he begat sons and daughters:
verses.original_text: וַיִּהְיוּ יְמֵי־אָדָם אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת־שֵׁת שְׁמֹנֶה מֵאֹת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyihyu yemei-Adam acharei holido et-Shet shemoneh meot shanah vayyoled banim uvanot.
verses.study_note: Adam lived 800 years after Seth's birth, fathering 'sons and daughters' — the source of the population that filled the pre-Flood world. The question 'where did Cain find his wife?' (4:17) is answered here and in 5:4: Adam had many daughters (and sons) who populated the early world. Intermarriage among close relatives was not prohibited (and genetically less harmful) in this early period of humanity. The longevity of pre-Flood patriarchs enabled massive population growth.

verses.kjv_text: And all the days that Adam lived were nine hundred and thirty years: and he died.
verses.original_text: וַיִּהְיוּ כָּל־יְמֵי אָדָם אֲשֶׁר־חַי תְּשַׁע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וּשְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה וַיָּמֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyihyu kol-yemei Adam asher-chai tesha meot shanah usheloshim shanah vayyamot.
verses.study_note: 'And he died' — the first 'and he died' of the chapter, and it falls on Adam himself. 930 years of life, and still the sentence of Gen 3:19 was carried out: 'for dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return.' Adam lived long enough to see multiple generations — he was still alive when Noah's father Lamech was born (cross-referencing the chronology). He saw the world populate, saw sin spread, saw death enter every life around him. The solemn refrain 'and he died' will toll like a funeral bell through this entire chapter — nine times before Noah — insisting that death is not natural but an intrusion, not permanent but a consequence that awaits a Conqueror.
verses.doctrinal_note: The wages of sin: 'and he died' — the first four words of God's warning in 2:17 ('thou shalt surely die') are now fulfilled in Adam's epitaph. Romans 5:12: 'death passed upon all men, for that all have sinned.' Yet the genealogy continues — proving that death does not have the final word. Each 'and he died' is followed by a name, a son, a continuation. Life persists through death until the One who said 'I am the resurrection and the life' (John 11:25).

verses.kjv_text: And Seth lived an hundred and five years, and begat Enos:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי שֵׁת חָמֵשׁ שָׁנִים וּמְאַת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת־אֱנוֹשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Shet chamesh shanim ume'at shanah vayyoled et-Enosh.
verses.study_note: Seth, born as 'another seed instead of Abel' (4:25), now perpetuates the redemptive line. Seth is 105 when he begets Enos (Enosh, 'mortal man'). The Sethite genealogy — Seth, Enos, Cainan, Mahalaleel, Jared, Enoch, Methuselah, Lamech, Noah — is the 'holy line' through which the promise of Gen 3:15 is preserved. This line contrasts with the Cainite line of chapter 4: same world, same lifespan, radically different spiritual legacy.

verses.kjv_text: And Seth lived after he begat Enos eight hundred and seven years, and begat sons and daughters:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי שֵׁת אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת־אֱנוֹשׁ שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים וּשְׁמֹנֶה מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Shet acharei holido et-Enosh sheva shanim ushemoneh meot shanah vayyoled banim uvanot.
verses.study_note: Seth's 807 years after Enos — the long lifespan allowed patriarchs to overlap significantly. Seth would still have been alive to know Lamech (Noah's father) if the chronology is taken literally. These overlapping generations meant that the oral tradition of creation, the Fall, and God's promises was transmitted directly person to person with very few links in the chain.

verses.kjv_text: And all the days of Seth were nine hundred and twelve years: and he died.
verses.original_text: וַיִּהְיוּ כָּל־יְמֵי שֵׁת שְׁתֵּים עֶשְׂרֶה שָׁנָה וּתְשַׁע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיָּמֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyihyu kol-yemei Shet shtem esreh shanah utsha meot shanah vayyamot.
verses.study_note: 'And he died' — the refrain falls on Seth, the appointed seed, the hope of Eve. Even the one given 'instead of Abel' could not escape the curse. 912 years, and still death came. The genealogy is relentless: no human being, however faithful, however long-lived, can escape the consequence of Adam's sin through natural means. The solution must come from outside the line — from One born not of Adam's seed (cf. Gen 3:15, 'her seed'; Isaiah 7:14).

verses.kjv_text: And Enos lived ninety years, and begat Cainan:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי אֱנוֹשׁ תִּשְׁעִים שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת־קֵינָן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Enosh tish'im shanah vayyoled et-Qenan.
verses.study_note: Enos (Enosh) — 'frail, mortal man' — is the one in whose generation men 'began to call upon the name of the LORD' (4:26). He begets Cainan (Qenan) at 90. The name Cainan is related to the Cainite 'Cain' (Qayin), showing that similar names existed across both lines — a reminder that both lines share common humanity. The Sethite Cainan carries the name but walks in the light of God's covenant.

verses.kjv_text: And Enos lived after he begat Cainan eight hundred and fifteen years, and begat sons and daughters:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי אֱנוֹשׁ אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת־קֵינָן חֲמֵשׁ עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה וּשְׁמֹנֶה מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Enosh acharei holido et-Qenan chamesh esreh shanah ushmoneh meot shanah vayyoled banim uvanot.
verses.study_note: Enos lives 815 more years after Cainan's birth, fathering many sons and daughters. In those 815 years, Enos would have overlapped with the birth of Jared and possibly lived into Enoch's era. The pre-Flood patriarchs did not simply live long — they lived in community with each other across multiple generations simultaneously. Adam could have personally told Seth, Enos, Cainan, and Mahalaleel about the Garden of Eden.

verses.kjv_text: And all the days of Enos were nine hundred and five years: and he died.
verses.original_text: וַיִּהְיוּ כָּל־יְמֵי אֱנוֹשׁ חָמֵשׁ שָׁנִים וּתְשַׁע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיָּמֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyihyu kol-yemei Enosh chamesh shanim utsha meot shanah vayyamot.
verses.study_note: 905 years — and he died. Enos, whose generation inaugurated calling on God's name, whose very name confessed human frailty, dies like every other. Prayer does not exempt us from physical death — but it connects us to the One who will conquer it. The 'frail man' who called on God points forward to Christ, who in Gethsemane 'offered up prayers and supplications with strong crying and tears unto him that was able to save him from death' (Heb 5:7).

verses.kjv_text: And Cainan lived seventy years, and begat Mahalaleel:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי קֵינָן שִׁבְעִים שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת־מַהֲלַלְאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Qenan shiv'im shanah vayyoled et-Mahalalel.
verses.study_note: Cainan (Qenan — 'possessor, smith') begets Mahalaleel at 70. Mahalaleel means 'praise of God' or 'God is praised' — from 'mahalal' (praise) and 'El' (God). In the midst of the genealogy's march toward death, a father names his son 'Praise of God.' Even in a world under the curse, worship and praise survive. The name is a small flame of doxology in the darkness.

verses.kjv_text: And Cainan lived after he begat Mahalaleel eight hundred and forty years, and begat sons and daughters:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי קֵינָן אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת־מַהֲלַלְאֵל אַרְבָּעִים שָׁנָה וּשְׁמֹנֶה מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Qenan acharei holido et-Mahalalel arba'im shanah ushmoneh meot shanah vayyoled banim uvanot.
verses.study_note: Cainan's 840 remaining years after Mahalaleel's birth — another long life, another multiplication of sons and daughters. The pre-Flood earth was populated by an extraordinarily long-lived and fertile generation. The genealogy's numbers invite us to recognize the scale of pre-Flood civilization — this was not a small tribe but a world of millions when the Flood came.

verses.kjv_text: And all the days of Cainan were nine hundred and ten years: and he died.
verses.original_text: וַיִּהְיוּ כָּל־יְמֵי קֵינָן עֶשֶׂר שָׁנִים וּתְשַׁע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיָּמֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyihyu kol-yemei Qenan eser shanim utsha meot shanah vayyamot.
verses.study_note: 910 years — and he died. The toll of the bell continues. Four patriarchs down, five to go before Enoch breaks the pattern. The reader is meant to feel the weight of repetition — 'and he died... and he died... and he died' — so that when Enoch's entry arrives, the absence of those three words rings like a resurrection trumpet.

verses.kjv_text: And Mahalaleel lived sixty and five years, and begat Jared:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי מַהֲלַלְאֵל חָמֵשׁ שָׁנִים וְשִׁשִּׁים שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת־יֶרֶד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Mahalalel chamesh shanim veshishim shanah vayyoled et-Yered.
verses.study_note: Mahalaleel ('praise of God') begets Jared at 65. 'Jared' (Yered) means 'descent' or 'to come down' — possibly an allusion to the descent of the 'sons of God' in chapter 6, which may have occurred in his time. Jared will live to 962 years — the second longest life in the genealogy. He is the father of Enoch, the most remarkable figure in this entire chapter.

verses.kjv_text: And Mahalaleel lived after he begat Jared eight hundred and thirty years, and begat sons and daughters:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי מַהֲלַלְאֵל אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת־יֶרֶד שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה וּשְׁמֹנֶה מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Mahalalel acharei holido et-Yered sheloshim shanah ushmoneh meot shanah vayyoled banim uvanot.
verses.study_note: Mahalaleel's 'praise of God' is the appropriate backdrop for Jared's life — the father of the man who will walk with God more closely than any other. A parent's worship can shape a child's walk with God across generations. The name's meaning ('praise of God') becomes a prophetic legacy when his grandson Enoch walks with God.

verses.kjv_text: And all the days of Mahalaleel were eight hundred ninety and five years: and he died.
verses.original_text: וַיִּהְיוּ כָּל־יְמֵי מַהֲלַלְאֵל חָמֵשׁ וְתִשְׁעִים שָׁנָה וּשְׁמֹנֶה מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיָּמֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyihyu kol-yemei Mahalalel chamesh vetish'im shanah ushmoneh meot shanah vayyamot.
verses.study_note: 895 years — and he died. Mahalaleel, the praiser of God, joins the march. One wonders what those years of praising God looked like — how they shaped his son Jared, and through Jared, Enoch. Every generation of worship leaves a deposit in those who come after.

verses.kjv_text: And Jared lived an hundred sixty and two years, and he begat Enoch:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי יֶרֶד שְׁתַּיִם וְשִׁשִּׁים שָׁנָה וּמְאַת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת־חֲנוֹךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Yered shettayim veshishim shanah ume'at shanah vayyoled et-Chanokh.
verses.study_note: Jared at 162 begets Enoch — the most celebrated man in this genealogy. 'Enoch' (Chanokh) means 'dedicated, initiated, consecrated.' The same name was given to Cain's son and his city (4:17) — but this Enoch will live out the name's meaning in a way his namesake never did. Jared will live to 962, but his greatest legacy will not be his longevity but his son who walked with God.

verses.kjv_text: And Jared lived after he begat Enoch eight hundred years, and begat sons and daughters:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי יֶרֶד אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת־חֲנוֹךְ שְׁמֹנֶה מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Yered acharei holido et-Chanokh shemoneh meot shanah vayyoled banim uvanot.
verses.study_note: Jared lived 800 years after Enoch was born — long enough to see Enoch taken by God. A father who outlives his son is a tragedy in ordinary life; here it is a testimony to the extraordinary nature of Enoch's translation. Jared would have witnessed what no other parent had: a son who did not die, but was simply 'not, for God took him.'

verses.kjv_text: And all the days of Jared were nine hundred sixty and two years: and he died.
verses.original_text: וַיִּהְיוּ כָּל־יְמֵי יֶרֶד שְׁתַּיִם וְשִׁשִּׁים שָׁנָה וּתְשַׁע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיָּמֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyihyu kol-yemei Yered shettayim veshishim shanah utsha meot shanah vayyamot.
verses.study_note: 962 years — the second longest life in Scripture — and still 'and he died.' Jared saw more of human history than almost any other recorded human being. He lived through the rise of Cainite civilization, the years of Enoch's prophetic ministry, and the beginning of the pre-Flood moral decline. Long life is not automatically a blessing — what matters is what one does with the years. Jared's legacy is Enoch.

verses.kjv_text: And Enoch lived sixty and five years, and begat Methuselah:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי חֲנוֹךְ חָמֵשׁ וְשִׁשִּׁים שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת־מְתוּשָׁלַח׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Chanokh chamesh veshishim shanah vayyoled et-Metushelach.
verses.study_note: At 65 — remarkably young by the standards of this genealogy — Enoch begets Methuselah. There is ancient Jewish tradition that the birth of Methuselah was the moment Enoch's walk with God began (v.22 says 'Enoch walked with God after he begat Methuselah'). The name Methuselah (Metushelach) is interpreted as 'when he dies, it shall come' — a prophecy embedded in his name. And remarkably, the year Methuselah died IS the year of the Flood in the biblical chronology. The longest-lived human carried a name that was a countdown.
verses.doctrinal_note: Methuselah's name as prophecy: If 'metu-shelach' means 'his death shall send [the Flood]' — then God's mercy is visible even in the name: the Flood was delayed as long as Methuselah lived. His 969-year life is the longest in Scripture, and every year of it was a year of God's patience. Peter reflects this: God is 'not willing that any should perish, but that all should come to repentance' (2 Pet 3:9).

verses.kjv_text: And Enoch walked with God after he begat Methuselah three hundred years, and begat sons and daughters:
verses.original_text: וַיִּתְהַלֵּךְ חֲנוֹךְ אֶת־הָאֱלֹהִים אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת־מְתוּשָׁלַח שְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyithhalekh Chanokh et-ha'Elohim acharei holido et-Metushelach shlosh meot shanah vayyoled banim uvanot.
verses.study_note: 'Walked with God' (yithhalekh et-ha-Elohim) — the phrase that transforms this entry. Every other patriarch 'lived' (vayechi) — Enoch 'walked.' The word 'hithallek' (to walk continually, to walk back and forth) is an intensive reflexive form suggesting habitual, devoted, ongoing communion — not a single act but a lifestyle. 'With God' (et-ha-Elohim) — the preposition 'et' implies close companionship, walking alongside. This is the same word used in 3:8 when God 'walked' in the garden. Enoch recovered what Adam lost. And this walk lasted 300 years — a lifetime of intimacy with God.
verses.doctrinal_note: What does it mean to walk with God? It means living in conscious fellowship with the divine presence — in every decision, every relationship, every moment. It is Noah's description too (6:9). It anticipates 'walking in the Spirit' (Gal 5:16), 'walking in the light' (1 John 1:7), and ultimately the restored walk of the New Jerusalem where God will again 'dwell with them' (Rev 21:3). Enoch previews what redemption restores.

verses.kjv_text: And all the days of Enoch were three hundred sixty and five years:
verses.original_text: וַיִּהְיוּ כָּל־יְמֵי חֲנוֹךְ חָמֵשׁ וְשִׁשִּׁים שָׁנָה וּשְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyihyu kol-yemei Chanokh chamesh veshishim shanah ushlosh meot shanah.
verses.study_note: 365 years — the shortest life in the genealogy, and the only one that does not end with 'and he died.' Note that 365 is also the number of days in a solar year — a detail not lost on ancient interpreters who saw Enoch's life as one complete 'year' of devotion, then taken. His years are counted just as the others are, but his entry is about to break every pattern established so far. Notice also that the formula is slightly changed — it says 'all the days of Enoch were 365 years' — and then pauses. The reader who has read the chapter knows what comes next. It does not come.

verses.kjv_text: And Enoch walked with God: and he was not; for God took him.
verses.original_text: וַיִּתְהַלֵּךְ חֲנוֹךְ אֶת־הָאֱלֹהִים וְאֵינֶנּוּ כִּי־לָקַח אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyithhalekh Chanokh et-ha'Elohim ve'einennu ki-laqach oto Elohim.
verses.study_note: The most extraordinary verse in the genealogy — six Hebrew words that detonate like a resurrection. The pattern that had tolled nine times before now simply does not come: no 'and he died.' Instead: 'he was not' (ve'einennu — he was gone, he ceased to be present here) 'for God took him' (ki-laqach oto Elohim — because God took him). The verb 'laqach' (to take) is used in 2 Kings 2:3,5,9-10 for Elijah's translation. God does not allow death to touch Enoch. He is 'taken' — raptured before death. Hebrews 11:5 clarifies: 'he was translated that he should not see death.' He is the Old Testament type of the rapture (1 Thess 4:17).
verses.doctrinal_note: Enoch's translation is the first proof in Scripture that death is not inevitable — that a man so close to God can bypass the curse of Gen 3:19. It foreshadows the resurrection and transformation of believers at Christ's coming (1 Cor 15:51-52; 1 Thess 4:16-17). Enoch and Elijah stand as two witnesses that God has power over death — preparing us for the One who said 'I am the resurrection and the life' (John 11:25). The man who walked with God was simply 'taken home' — the walk continued to its eternal destination.

verses.kjv_text: And Methuselah lived an hundred eighty and seven years, and begat Lamech:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי מְתוּשֶׁלַח שֶׁבַע וּשְׁמֹנִים שָׁנָה וּמְאַת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת־לָמֶךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Metushelach sheva ushmonim shanah ume'at shanah vayyoled et-Lamekh.
verses.study_note: Methuselah at 187 begets Lamech — the father of Noah. Methuselah is the son of the man who walked with God (Enoch) and the grandfather of the man who also walked with God (Noah). He bridges two of the most extraordinary figures in pre-Flood history. His 969 years span from Enoch's walking with God all the way to the brink of the Flood. If the etymology 'when he dies, the Flood comes' is correct, then every year of Methuselah's extraordinary life was a year of grace extended.

verses.kjv_text: And Methuselah lived after he begat Lamech seven hundred eighty and two years, and begat sons and daughters:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי מְתוּשֶׁלַח אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת־לֶמֶךְ שְׁתַּיִם וּשְׁמֹנִים שָׁנָה וּשְׁבַע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Metushelach acharei holido et-Lamekh shettayim ushmonim shanah usheva meot shanah vayyoled banim uvanot.
verses.study_note: Methuselah's 782 years after Lamech's birth take him to the very edge of the Flood. Noah was 600 when the Flood came (7:6); Methuselah was 187 when Lamech was born; Lamech was 182 when Noah was born (5:28-29). This means Methuselah was 187+182+600 = 969 years old when the Flood came — precisely his age at death (v.27). The mathematics confirm: Methuselah died the year of the Flood.

verses.kjv_text: And all the days of Methuselah were nine hundred sixty and nine years: and he died.
verses.original_text: וַיִּהְיוּ כָּל־יְמֵי מְתוּשֶׁלַח תֵּשַׁע וְשִׁשִּׁים שָׁנָה וּתְשַׁע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיָּמֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyihyu kol-yemei Metushelach tesha veshishim shanah utsha meot shanah vayyamot.
verses.study_note: 969 years — the longest human life ever recorded in Scripture — and still, 'and he died.' Even the oldest man who ever lived could not outrun death. No amount of time solves the problem of sin; only an atoning sacrifice can. Methuselah's death — whether from natural causes or in the Flood itself — marks the end of God's extended patience and the beginning of judgment. The longest life in the Bible ends not with triumph but with the same three words as every other: 'and he died.'
verses.doctrinal_note: Methuselah's record lifespan is a monument to divine patience. 2 Peter 3:9 — 'The Lord is not slack concerning his promise, as some men count slackness; but is longsuffering toward us, not willing that any should perish.' Every one of Methuselah's 969 years was a year the door of the ark remained open in principle — a year of grace. When he died, the Flood came. Judgment follows the exhaustion of patience.

verses.kjv_text: And Lamech lived an hundred eighty and two years, and begat a son:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי לֶמֶךְ שְׁתַּיִם וּשְׁמֹנִים שָׁנָה וּמְאַת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בֵּן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Lamekh shettayim ushmonim shanah ume'at shanah vayyoled ben.
verses.study_note: The suspense is built: 'begat a son' — but the son is not named yet. Scripture delays the name to create anticipation. This Lamech is the Sethite Lamech — completely different from the murderous, boastful Cainite Lamech of chapter 4, though they share the same name. They represent two possible destinies for any life: one Lamech celebrated violence (4:23), this one will name his son with a prophecy of comfort and hope.

verses.kjv_text: And he called his name Noah, saying, This same shall comfort us concerning our work and toil of our hands, because of the ground which the LORD hath cursed.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא אֶת־שְׁמוֹ נֹחַ לֵאמֹר זֶה יְנַחֲמֵנוּ מִמַּעֲשֵׂנוּ וּמֵעִצְּבוֹן יָדֵינוּ מִן הָאֲדָמָה אֲשֶׁר אֵרְרָהּ יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqra et-shemo Noach lemor zeh yenachamenu mimma'asenu ume'itzevon yadeinu min ha'adamah asher erarah Yahweh.
verses.study_note: 'Noah' (Noach) from the root 'nacham' (to comfort, to bring rest) and 'nuach' (to rest, settle). Lamech's naming is a prophetic act — he sees in this son the answer to the curse of 3:17: 'the ground which the LORD hath cursed.' He is looking for comfort from the toil and sorrow of fallen work. Lamech does not know the form the comfort will take — the Flood, the new beginning, the covenant, and ultimately the Christ who brings true rest (Matt 11:28-29). Noah is a type of Christ: he does not remove the curse (Gen 8:21 says the curse persists) but he brings the world through judgment to a new beginning.
verses.doctrinal_note: Noah as a type of Christ: Lamech prophecies that Noah will bring 'comfort' (nacham) from the curse. This is fulfilled partially — Noah navigates the judgment and brings humanity to a new beginning. But the ultimate 'rest' (nuach) is found in Christ: 'Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest' (Matt 11:28). The Hebrew root of Noah's name (nuach/nacham) is the same root as the Holy Spirit's title 'Comforter' (Parakletos in Greek translates Hebrew nacham).

verses.kjv_text: And Lamech lived after he begat Noah five hundred ninety and five years, and begat sons and daughters:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי לֶמֶךְ אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת־נֹחַ חָמֵשׁ וְתִשְׁעִים שָׁנָה וַחֲמֵשׁ מֵאֹת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Lamekh acharei holido et-Noach chamesh vetish'im shanah vachamesh meot shanah vayyoled banim uvanot.
verses.study_note: Lamech lives 595 years after Noah's birth — dying just 5 years before the Flood (based on the chronology). He does not live to see his prophetic naming fulfilled in the Flood and the new beginning. But his faith in naming his son 'comfort' was not misplaced — he simply did not live to see it worked out. This is the pattern of faith described in Hebrews 11: many 'died in faith, not having received the promises, but having seen them afar off' (Heb 11:13).

verses.kjv_text: And all the days of Lamech were seven hundred seventy and seven years: and he died.
verses.original_text: וַיִּהְיוּ כָּל־יְמֵי לֶמֶךְ שֶׁבַע וְשִׁבְעִים שָׁנָה וּשְׁבַע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיָּמֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyihyu kol-yemei Lamekh sheva veshiv'im shanah usheva meot shanah vayyamot.
verses.study_note: 777 years — a number saturated with symbolism. Seven is the number of completeness and rest throughout Scripture (Gen 2:2-3, Rev 1:4). 777 is a triple completion — the fullness of fullness. The man who named his son 'rest' lived exactly 777 years and then rested from his own labors in death. Where the Cainite Lamech claimed 'seventy-sevenfold' vengeance (4:24), the Sethite Lamech lived 777 years of hope. One number is a threat; the other is a testimony.

verses.kjv_text: And Noah was five hundred years old: and Noah begat Shem, Ham, and Japheth.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי נֹחַ בֶּן־חֲמֵשׁ מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד נֹחַ אֶת־שֵׁם אֶת־חָם וְאֶת־יָפֶת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi Noach ben-chamesh meot shanah vayyoled Noach et-Shem et-Cham ve'et-Yafet.
verses.study_note: The genealogy ends not with 'and he died' but with the birth of three sons — an opening, not a closing. Noah at 500 fathers Shem, Ham, and Japheth — the three men whose descendants will repopulate the entire earth after the Flood (Genesis 10, the Table of Nations). These three names carry the future of humanity: Shem ('name/renown' — from whom the Semitic peoples, including Israel and the Messiah's lineage), Ham ('hot/dark' — from whom Egypt, Canaan, and Africa), and Japheth ('may he expand/beauty' — from whom the Indo-European peoples of Europe and Asia). The genealogy that opened with Adam's image-bearing dignity ends with three men who will carry that image through the waters of judgment into a new world.
verses.doctrinal_note: The three sons of Noah represent the three streams of post-Flood humanity. Genesis 10 — the Table of Nations — shows 70 nations descending from them, the ancestors of all people alive today. Paul declares to Athens: 'God hath made of one blood all nations of men' (Acts 17:26) — rooted in this very genealogy. Every human being on earth today traces their ancestry to Shem, Ham, or Japheth, and through Noah back to Adam, and through Adam back to the God who made him in His image.

meta.overview: Genesis 5 is the first formal genealogy in Scripture — the 'Book of Generations' (sefer toledot ha-adam). It traces ten generations from Adam to Noah with a solemn, rhythmic formula: born, lived, begat, died. The relentless repetition of 'and he died' is the drumbeat of Gen 3:19, sin's wages echoing through the centuries. But two figures break the pattern: Enoch, who 'walked with God and was not, for God took him' — the first translation in Scripture, a foretaste of resurrection; and Noah, whose name carries the promise of rest and comfort. Read carefully, this genealogy is not merely a list of names but a theological statement: the Imago Dei is transmitted through the line of Seth, sin produces death in every generation, and God preserves a redemptive line from Adam all the way to the Flood and beyond.
meta.theme: The Image of God Transmitted — Life, Death & the Line of Promise
meta.keyWordMeaning: Generations, account, history — the structural heading of Genesis 5, linking each life to the next in God's unfolding redemptive purpose.
outline: The Heading and the Imago Dei (vv.1-2): The book of Adam's generations — male and female
Adam (vv.3-5): 930 years — and he died
Seth (vv.6-8): 912 years — and he died
Enos (vv.9-11): 905 years — and he died
Cainan (vv.12-14): 910 years — and he died
Mahalaleel (vv.15-17): 895 years — and he died
Jared (vv.18-20): 962 years — and he died
Enoch (vv.21-24): Walked with God — and was not, for God took him
Methuselah (vv.25-27): 969 years — the oldest man who ever lived
Lamech (vv.28-31): 777 years — he named his son Noah: rest and comfort
Noah (v.32): Father of Shem, Ham, and Japheth — the Flood generation

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when men began to multiply on the face of the earth, and daughters were born unto them,
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כִּי־הֵחֵל הָאָדָם לָרֹב עַל־פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה וּבָנוֹת יֻלְּדוּ לָהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi ki-hechel ha'adam larov al-penei ha'adamah uvanot yulledu lahem.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens by picking up directly from the end of Genesis 5 — the multiplication of humanity. 'When men began to multiply' — the blessing of Gen 1:28 ('be fruitful and multiply') is being fulfilled, but in a world now saturated with sin. Population growth without godliness produces not a garden but a catastrophe. 'Daughters were born unto them' — this sets the stage for v.2, where the attractiveness of these daughters becomes the occasion for the transgression that follows. The blessing of fruitfulness, given for God's glory, is about to be corrupted.

verses.kjv_text: That the sons of God saw the daughters of men that they were fair; and they took them wives of all which they chose.
verses.original_text: וַיִּרְאוּ בְנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים אֶת־בְּנוֹת הָאָדָם כִּי טֹבֹת הֵנָּה וַיִּקְחוּ לָהֶם נָשִׁים מִכֹּל אֲשֶׁר בָּחָרוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiru benei ha'Elohim et-benot ha'adam ki tovot hennah vayyiqchu lahem nashim mikkol asher bacharu.
verses.study_note: One of the most debated verses in Scripture. 'Sons of God' (benei ha-Elohim) — three main interpretations: (1) Angels — the most ancient view (held by Enoch, Josephus, Dead Sea Scrolls, many church fathers). This reading is supported by the identical phrase in Job 1:6, 2:1, 38:7 where it clearly means divine beings; and by Jude 6-7, 2 Pet 2:4 which speak of angels who 'kept not their first estate.' (2) Sethite men — godly line of Seth intermarrying with Cainite women, breaking spiritual separation. (3) Mighty rulers or divine kings who took women by force. Whatever the interpretation, the result is the same: a catastrophic blurring of divinely established boundaries. 'They took' (vayyiqchu) — the same word used for Eve taking the fruit (3:6). Desire without God's boundaries always takes.
verses.doctrinal_note: The transgression of boundaries: God created distinct categories — heavenly and earthly, holy and common, covenant and world. The mixing of what God separated is always catastrophic. Whether angels or Sethites, the principle is the same: when the people of God intermarry with the godless purely on the basis of outward beauty ('they were fair'), spiritual catastrophe follows. Paul warns the same in 2 Cor 6:14: 'be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers.'

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said, My spirit shall not always strive with man, for that he also is flesh: yet his days shall be an hundred and twenty years.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה לֹא־יָדוֹן רוּחִי בָאָדָם לְעֹלָם בְּשַׁגַּם הוּא בָשָׂר וְהָיוּ יָמָיו מֵאָה וְעֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Yahweh lo-yadon ruchi va'adam le'olam beshagam hu vasar vehayu yamav meah ve'esrim shanah.
verses.study_note: God's response to the transgression — a declaration of divine patience with a deadline. 'My Spirit shall not always strive' (lo-yadon ruchi) — the Holy Spirit has been restraining and striving with sinful humanity (cf. John 16:8), but this cannot continue indefinitely. 'For that he also is flesh' — man has chosen the way of flesh over the way of the Spirit. The 120 years is most naturally read as a grace period before the Flood — God gives humanity 120 more years to repent. During this time Noah preaches (2 Pet 2:5) and builds the ark. God is not hasty in judgment; He gives maximum opportunity for repentance.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Spirit's striving: The Holy Spirit restrains evil and calls humanity to repentance (John 16:8; 2 Thess 2:7). But there is a point at which persistent rejection of the Spirit results in judgment. Jesus warns of the unpardonable sin (Matt 12:31-32). The 120-year grace period is an expression of God's longsuffering — but judgment has been set. There is always a 'last day' of opportunity.

verses.kjv_text: There were giants in the earth in those days; and also after that, when the sons of God came in unto the daughters of men, and they bare children to them, the same became mighty men which were of old, men of renown.
verses.original_text: הַנְּפִילִים הָיוּ בָאָרֶץ בַּיָּמִים הָהֵם וְגַם אַחֲרֵי כֵן אֲשֶׁר יָבֹאוּ בְּנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים אֶל־בְּנוֹת הָאָדָם וְיָלְדוּ לָהֶם הֵמָּה הַגִּבֹּרִים אֲשֶׁר מֵעוֹלָם אַנְשֵׁי הַשֵּׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Hannefilim hayu va'aretz bayyamim hahem vegam acharei ken asher yavo'u benei ha'Elohim el-benot ha'adam veyaldu lahem hemmah haggiborim asher me'olam anshei hashem.
verses.study_note: The Nephilim (from 'naphal' — to fall, or possibly 'the fallen ones') were present before and after the sons of God union. They are called 'giborim' (mighty warriors, heroes) and 'anshei hashem' (men of renown/name). This verse has fueled enormous speculation — the Nephilim appear again in Numbers 13:33 when the spies report giants in Canaan. The key theological point is not their physical stature but their cultural dominance: they were the celebrities, the warriors, the heroes that the ancient world celebrated — power and fame without God. 'Men of renown' — 'shem' (name/renown) is the opposite of what Babel sought (11:4) and what God promises Abram (12:2). True name/renown comes from God, not from violence and domination.

verses.kjv_text: And God saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth, and that every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא יְהוָה כִּי רַבָּה רָעַת הָאָדָם בָּאָרֶץ וְכָל־יֵצֶר מַחְשְׁבֹת לִבּוֹ רַק רַע כָּל־הַיּוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyar Yahweh ki rabbah ra'at ha'adam ba'aretz vekhol-yetzer machshevot libbo raq ra kol-hayyom.
verses.study_note: The most comprehensive biblical statement of human depravity: five terms stack on each other for devastating effect. (1) 'Wickedness' (ra'ah) — moral evil, (2) 'great' (rabbah) — vast, extensive, (3) 'every' (kol) — without exception, (4) 'imagination' (yetzer) — the formed tendency of the mind, (5) 'only evil' (raq ra) — exclusively, entirely evil, (6) 'continually' (kol-hayyom — all the day). There is no qualification, no exception, no partial righteousness. This is total depravity at its biblical maximum. Note that God 'saw' — the same word as His evaluation of creation in Genesis 1 ('and God saw that it was good'). Now God sees, and it is all evil. The creation that was 'very good' has been entirely inverted.
verses.doctrinal_note: Total depravity: This verse is the biblical foundation for the Reformed doctrine of total depravity — not that every person is as evil as they could possibly be, but that every part of human nature (mind, will, imagination, heart) is corrupted by sin. No faculty is untouched. 'Every imagination' — the Hebrew 'yetzer' is the word used in rabbinic tradition for the evil inclination (yetzer hara). Paul quotes this principle in Romans 3:10-18. It is the diagnosis that makes the gospel necessary.

verses.kjv_text: And it repented the LORD that he had made man on the earth, and it grieved him at his heart.
verses.original_text: וַיִּנָּחֶם יְהוָה כִּי־עָשָׂה אֶת־הָאָדָם בָּאָרֶץ וַיִּתְעַצֵּב אֶל־לִבּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyinnachem Yahweh ki-asah et-ha'adam ba'aretz vayyit'atzev el-libbo.
verses.study_note: The most anthropomorphic statement about God in Genesis — 'it repented the LORD' (vayyinnachem Yahweh) and 'it grieved him at his heart.' These are not contradictions of God's omniscience or immutability. They are anthropopathisms — describing God's response in human emotional terms so we can grasp the reality of divine feeling. 'Nacham' (repented) is the same root as Noah's name — a painful comfort, a sorrowful change of direction. Not that God made a mistake, but that His genuine love for His creation means He is genuinely grieved by its corruption. 'Grieved at his heart' — God has a heart, and it can be broken. Jesus wept (John 11:35). 'Grieve not the Holy Spirit' (Eph 4:30).
verses.doctrinal_note: Does God change His mind? The word 'repented' (nacham) here does not mean God made an error or that He is inconsistent. God cannot lie (Num 23:19; 1 Sam 15:29). Rather, nacham describes God's genuine emotional response — grief, sorrow — over the direction of His beloved creation. It is the language of a parent grieving over a child's choices. His purpose does not change; His heart feels the grief of what sin has done. This is the God who is not unmoved — 'in all their affliction he was afflicted' (Isa 63:9).

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said, I will destroy man whom I have created from the face of the earth; both man, and beast, and the creeping thing, and the fowls of the air; for it repenteth me that I have made them.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶמְחֶה אֶת־הָאָדָם אֲשֶׁר־בָּרָאתִי מֵעַל פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה מֵאָדָם עַד־בְּהֵמָה עַד־רֶמֶשׂ וְעַד־עוֹף הַשָּׁמָיִם כִּי נִחַמְתִּי כִּי עֲשִׂיתִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Yahweh emcheh et-ha'adam asher-bareti me'al penei ha'adamah me'adam ad-behemah ad-remes ve'ad-of hashamayim ki nichamti ki asitim.
verses.study_note: 'I will destroy' (emcheh — to wipe out, blot out, like erasing writing from a tablet) — a total reversal of creation. The same categories created in Genesis 1 — man, beast, creeping things, birds — are now to be unmade. The Flood is an un-creation, a return to the watery chaos of 1:2, followed by a new creation. The animals share in the judgment because the earth itself has been corrupted through human sin (cf. Romans 8:20-22: 'the creation was made subject to vanity'). Creation suffers with its fallen steward.

verses.kjv_text: But Noah found grace in the eyes of the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְנֹחַ מָצָא חֵן בְּעֵינֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Venoach matza chen be'einei Yahweh.
verses.study_note: The pivot of the entire chapter — eight Hebrew words that change everything. After six verses of devastating judgment and divine grief, one conjunction transforms the narrative: 'But Noah' (veNoach). The contrast is absolute. 'Found grace' (matza chen) — the first use of the word 'chen' (grace, favor) in all of Scripture. Noah did not earn grace; he found it. Grace is not achieved, acquired, or deserved — it is encountered. 'In the eyes of the LORD' — God's gaze, which had looked on total evil in v.5, now rests with favor on one man. Note the wordplay: Noah (Noach) found grace (chen) — the two words share the same Hebrew consonants (n-ch) in reverse (Noach/chen). His very name is hidden in the word for grace.
verses.doctrinal_note: The first grace: 'Chen' (grace) appears here for the first time in Scripture — and it appears in the context of judgment. Grace is not needed when everything is fine; it shines brightest against the backdrop of deserved judgment. Noah did not deserve to be spared any more than the others (Rom 3:23; Eph 2:8-9 — 'by grace are ye saved through faith... not of works'). The pattern established here runs through all of Scripture: judgment is deserved by all; grace selects and saves. The ark becomes the first great type of Christ — the only means of salvation from divine judgment.

verses.kjv_text: These are the generations of Noah: Noah was a just man and perfect in his generations, and Noah walked with God.
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת נֹחַ נֹחַ אִישׁ צַדִּיק תָּמִים הָיָה בְּדֹרֹתָיו אֶת־הָאֱלֹהִים הִתְהַלֶּךְ נֹחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Eleh toledot Noach Noach ish tzaddiq tamim hayah bedorotav et-ha'Elohim hithalekh Noach.
verses.study_note: A new 'toledot' heading — 'these are the generations of Noah' — marks a new section. Noah is described with three epithets: (1) 'Just' (tzaddiq) — righteous, legally right before God, (2) 'Perfect' (tamim) — complete, wholehearted, undivided in his devotion. Tamim is the same word used for a blameless sacrificial animal (Lev 1:3) — Noah was God's acceptable offering in a corrupt generation. (3) 'Walked with God' (et-ha-Elohim hithalekh) — the identical phrase used of Enoch (5:22-24). Only Enoch and Noah receive this description in Genesis. 'In his generations' — some read this as limiting praise (he was only righteous compared to his corrupt contemporaries); others read it as intensifying praise (even in such corrupt generations, he was righteous). The latter is more consistent with Hebrews 11:7.
verses.doctrinal_note: Noah's righteousness was not the ground of his salvation — it was the fruit of his grace. He 'found grace' first (v.8), then is described as righteous (v.9). The order matters: grace precedes righteousness; righteousness flows from grace. Hebrews 11:7 says he was 'heir of the righteousness which is by faith' — saved by faith, not by personal perfection. The 'perfect' (tamim) here does not mean sinless but wholehearted and undivided in commitment to God.

verses.kjv_text: And Noah begat three sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth.
verses.original_text: וַיּוֹלֶד נֹחַ שְׁלֹשָׁה בָנִים אֶת־שֵׁם אֶת־חָם וְאֶת־יָפֶת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyoled Noach sheloshah vanim et-Shem et-Cham ve'et-Yafet.
verses.study_note: Repeated from 5:32 — now placed in the context of the Flood narrative. Shem, Ham, and Japheth will carry humanity through the Flood. Three sons for a family that will become a world. The number three echoes through Scripture as the number of divine completeness. These three men are the entire future of the human race — every person alive today descends from one of them.

verses.kjv_text: The earth also was corrupt before God, and the earth was filled with violence.
verses.original_text: וַתִּשָּׁחֵת הָאָרֶץ לִפְנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים וַתִּמָּלֵא הָאָרֶץ חָמָס׃
verses.transliteration: Vattishshachet ha'aretz lifnei ha'Elohim vatimmale ha'aretz chamas.
verses.study_note: Two words describe the world before the Flood: 'corrupt' (shachat) and 'violence' (chamas). 'Corrupt' (shachat) means spoiled, ruined, destroyed — the earth has been disfigured from its original 'very good' state. 'Violence' (chamas) — this single word summarizes the social dimension: physical brutality, injustice, oppression, cruelty. 'Chamas' is used throughout the prophets to describe Israel's sin and is the name of a modern terrorist organization — demonstrating the word's meaning has not changed. 'Before God' — the corruption was not hidden; it was openly practiced in the presence of the God who sees all.

verses.kjv_text: And God looked upon the earth, and, behold, it was corrupt; for all flesh had corrupted his way upon the earth.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא אֱלֹהִים אֶת־הָאָרֶץ וְהִנֵּה נִשְׁחָתָה כִּי הִשְׁחִית כָּל־בָּשָׂר אֶת־דַּרְכּוֹ עַל הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyar Elohim et-ha'aretz vehineh nishchatah ki hishchit kol-basar et-darko al ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: 'God looked' (vayyar Elohim) — the divine audit. In Genesis 1, God looked and declared everything 'good.' Now He looks again and beholds corruption. 'And behold' (vehineh) — the same word of exclamation used in 1:31 ('and behold, it was very good'), now reversed into horror. 'All flesh had corrupted his way' — every living creature had abandoned the path God designed. 'His way' (darko) — the singular pronoun implies God's intended way for creation, now abandoned universally. 'Corrupt' appears three times in vv.11-12, pounding the diagnosis.

verses.kjv_text: And God said unto Noah, The end of all flesh is come before me; for the earth is filled with violence through them; and, behold, I will destroy them with the earth.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים לְנֹחַ קֵץ כָּל־בָּשָׂר בָּא לְפָנַי כִּי־מָלְאָה הָאָרֶץ חָמָס מִפְּנֵיהֶם וְהִנְנִי מַשְׁחִיתָם אֶת־הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Elohim leNoach qetz kol-basar ba lefanai ki-mal'ah ha'aretz chamas mippeneihem vehineni mashchitam et-ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: God speaks directly to Noah — the first recorded words God has spoken to any man since warning Adam (2:16-17; 3:9ff). 'The end of all flesh is come before me' — a formal judicial declaration. The decision has been made. 'Qetz' (end) is the same word used in eschatological prophecy (Dan 8:17, 11:35) for the end of the age. The Flood is a miniature eschatology — a type of the final judgment. 'And behold, I will destroy them with the earth' — God announces judgment but immediately calls Noah to prepare. Judgment and salvation are always announced together in Scripture.

verses.kjv_text: Make thee an ark of gopher wood; rooms shalt thou make in the ark, and shalt pitch it within and without with pitch.
verses.original_text: עֲשֵׂה לְךָ תֵּבַת עֲצֵי־גֹפֶר קִנִּים תַּעֲשֶׂה אֶת־הַתֵּבָה וְכָפַרְתָּ אֹתָהּ מִבַּיִת וּמִחוּץ בַּכֹּפֶר׃
verses.transliteration: Aseh lekha tevat atzei-gofer qinnim ta'aseh et-hattevah vekhafarta otah mibbayit umichuts bakkhofer.
verses.study_note: 'Ark' (tevah) — not the word for 'ship' but a box/chest/container. The same word is used for the basket that carried baby Moses (Ex 2:3-5). The ark was not built for speed or navigation but for preservation. 'Gopher wood' — the only use of this word in Scripture; the exact species is unknown. 'Pitch' (kofer — to cover, to seal, to atone) — the most theologically rich word in the verse. The root of 'kofer' is the same as 'kaphar' — to make atonement, to cover sin. The ark was covered (kafar) with pitch (kofer) to keep out the waters of judgment. This is a stunning type: the blood of Christ covers (kaphar) the believer, keeping out the waters of divine wrath.
verses.doctrinal_note: The ark as a type of Christ: (1) The ark was the only means of salvation — no ark, no survival. Christ is the only way (John 14:6). (2) The ark was covered with pitch (kofer) whose root means atonement (kaphar). Christ's atonement covers us from judgment. (3) God shut Noah in (7:16) — God preserves those He saves. (4) The ark carried all who entered safely through judgment to a new world. 'In Christ' (a phrase used 164 times by Paul) corresponds to 'in the ark' — the only safe place in the storm of judgment.

verses.kjv_text: And this is the fashion which thou shalt make it of: The length of the ark shall be three hundred cubits, the breadth of it fifty cubits, and the height of it thirty cubits.
verses.original_text: וְזֶה אֲשֶׁר תַּעֲשֶׂה אֹתָהּ שְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת אַמָּה אֹרֶךְ הַתֵּבָה חֲמִשִּׁים אַמָּה רָחְבָּהּ וּשְׁלֹשִׁים אַמָּה קוֹמָתָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vezeh asher ta'aseh otah shlosh meot ammah orekh hattevah chamishim ammah rochbah ushloshim ammah qomatah.
verses.study_note: The dimensions: 300 cubits long × 50 wide × 30 high. A cubit is approximately 18 inches (45cm), giving: roughly 450 feet long × 75 wide × 45 high — about the size of a modern cargo ship. The proportions (6:1 ratio of length to width) are remarkably consistent with modern shipbuilding principles for maximum stability in rough seas. These are not mythological dimensions but precise engineering specifications. Studies have confirmed that a vessel of these proportions would be exceptionally stable in stormy conditions — the ideal design for surviving a global flood, not for speed or maneuverability.

verses.kjv_text: A window shalt thou make to the ark, and in a cubit shalt thou finish it above; and the door of the ark shalt thou set in the side thereof; with lower, second, and third stories shalt thou make it.
verses.original_text: צֹהַר תַּעֲשֶׂה לַתֵּבָה וְאֶל־אַמָּה תְּכַלֶּנָּה מִלְמַעְלָה וּפֶתַח הַתֵּבָה בְּצִדָּהּ תָּשִׂים תַּחְתִּיִּם שְׁנִיִּם וּשְׁלִשִׁים תַּעֲשֶׂהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Tzohar ta'aseh lattevah ve'el-ammah tekhalennah lemilma'lah upetach hattevah betziddah tasim tachtiyyim sheniyyim ushlishim ta'aseha.
verses.study_note: 'A window' (tzohar) — either a roof opening or a row of windows running the length of the ark one cubit below the roof, providing ventilation and light. 'The door of the ark shalt thou set in the side' — one door. This is profoundly theological: there was only one door into the ark, as there is only one way into salvation. Jesus declares: 'I am the door: by me if any man enter in, he shall be saved' (John 10:9). Three stories provided structural strength and separated different categories of animals and supplies.
verses.doctrinal_note: The single door: One ark, one door — everyone who entered was saved; everyone outside perished. The exclusivity of the ark's door anticipates the exclusivity of Christ: 'Neither is there salvation in any other: for there is none other name under heaven given among men, whereby we must be saved' (Acts 4:12). The narrow door of salvation has always been singular.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, I, even I, do bring a flood of waters upon the earth, to destroy all flesh, wherein is the breath of life, from under heaven; and every thing that is in the earth shall die.
verses.original_text: וַאֲנִי הִנְנִי מֵבִיא אֶת־הַמַּבּוּל מַיִם עַל הָאָרֶץ לְשַׁחֵת כָּל־בָּשָׂר אֲשֶׁר־בּוֹ רוּחַ חַיִּים מִתַּחַת הַשָּׁמָיִם כֹּל אֲשֶׁר בָּאָרֶץ יִגְוָע׃
verses.transliteration: Va'ani hineni mevi et-hammabbul mayim al ha'aretz leshachet kol-basar asher-bo ruach chayyim mittachat hashamayim kol asher ba'aretz yigva.
verses.study_note: 'I, even I' (va'ani hineni) — the double emphasis is God staking His personal authority on this judgment. This is not an accident of nature but a deliberate divine act. 'The flood' (mabbul) — a specific word for this catastrophic event, different from ordinary floods ('nahar' or 'nachal'). The mabbul is the Flood, unique in history. 'Breath of life' (ruach chayyim) — echoing 2:7 when God breathed life into Adam. The same life-breath God gave will now be extinguished by water. This is the theological inversion of creation: God's breath gave life; God's waters will take it.

verses.kjv_text: But with thee will I establish my covenant; and thou shalt come into the ark, thou, and thy sons, and thy wife, and thy sons’ wives with thee.
verses.original_text: וַהֲקִמֹתִי אֶת־בְּרִיתִי אִתָּךְ וּבָאתָ אֶל הַתֵּבָה אַתָּה וּבָנֶיךָ וְאִשְׁתְּךָ וּנְשֵׁי בָנֶיךָ אִתָּךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vahaqimoti et-beriti ittakh uvata el-hattevah attah uvanekha ve'ishtekha uneshei vanekha ittakh.
verses.study_note: The most momentous verse in the chapter: 'I will establish my covenant' — the first use of 'berit' (covenant) in Scripture. God's first covenant word is spoken to Noah, in the context of judgment, as a promise of salvation. A covenant (berit) is a binding, sworn commitment — God pledges Himself to Noah's preservation. 'With thee' (ittakh) — personal, specific, individual. Grace is always particular before it is universal. Noah's family — 8 people — will carry humanity through the Flood. Note the order: God announces judgment (vv.13-17), then immediately announces covenant and salvation (v.18). This is the gospel pattern: law then grace, condemnation then justification.
verses.doctrinal_note: The first covenant: 'Berit' (covenant) appears here for the first time in Scripture — in the context of Noah, the ark, and salvation from judgment. This establishes the covenant as God's primary instrument of grace throughout redemptive history: Noahic, Abrahamic, Mosaic, Davidic, and New (Jer 31:31-34; Luke 22:20). Every covenant is God's sworn commitment to save His people through judgment. The ark is the first covenant institution — the visible, physical means of covenant salvation.

verses.kjv_text: And of every living thing of all flesh, two of every sort shalt thou bring into the ark, to keep them alive with thee; they shall be male and female.
verses.original_text: וּמִכָּל הַחַי מִכָּל בָּשָׂר שְׁנַיִם מִכֹּל תָּבִיא אֶל הַתֵּבָה לְהַחֲיֹת אִתָּךְ זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה יִהְיוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Umikkal hachai mikkol basar shenayim mikkol tavi el-hattevah lehachayot ittakh zakhar unekevah yihyu.
verses.study_note: Two of every kind — male and female — to preserve the created diversity of life. This verse gives the general instruction; chapter 7:2-3 adds the specification of seven pairs of clean animals for sacrificial purposes. God's concern extends beyond humanity to all creatures: He cares about the preservation of animal life. The same God who created every living thing in Genesis 1 now acts to preserve it. This is also a restoration of the original creation pattern — male and female, each after their kind (1:21-25).

verses.kjv_text: Of fowls after their kind, and of cattle after their kind, of every creeping thing of the earth after his kind, two of every sort shall come unto thee, to keep them alive.
verses.original_text: מֵהָעוֹף לְמִינֵהוּ וּמִן הַבְּהֵמָה לְמִינָהּ מִכֹּל רֶמֶשׂ הָאֲדָמָה לְמִינֵהוּ שְׁנַיִם מִכֹּל יָבֹאוּ אֵלֶיךָ לְהַחֲיוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Meha'of leminehu umin-habbehemah leminah mikkol remes ha'adamah leminehu shenayim mikkol yavo'u elekha lehachayot.
verses.study_note: 'After their kind' (leminehu) — the same phrase from creation (1:11-25) reappears. God preserves the created kinds, not every individual species. 'They shall come to you' (yavo'u elekha) — note the passive: Noah does not go hunting. God sends the animals to Noah, just as God brought the animals to Adam for naming (2:19). The same divine sovereignty that assembled creation now assembles its remnant. Noah's role is preparation and reception, not capture.

verses.kjv_text: And take thou unto thee of all food that is eaten, and thou shalt gather it to thee; and it shall be for food for thee, and for them.
verses.original_text: וְאַתָּה קַח לְךָ מִכָּל מַאֲכָל אֲשֶׁר יֵאָכֵל וְאָסַפְתָּ אֵלֶיךָ וְהָיָה לְךָ וְלָהֶם לְאָכְלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah qach lekha mikkol ma'akhal asher ye'akhel ve'asafta elekha vehayah lekha valahem le'okhlah.
verses.study_note: God provides a complete survival plan — not just the ark structure, not just the animals, but the food supply. Nothing is left to chance.
