God's provision for salvation is total: He provides the means of escape (the ark), the vehicle of preservation (the covenant), and the daily sustenance (the food). This anticipates God's wilderness provision for Israel (manna and quail) and ultimately Christ's declaration: 'I am the bread of life' (John 6:35). Salvation includes daily sustenance.

verses.kjv_text: Thus did Noah; according to all that God commanded him, so did he.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַשׂ נֹחַ כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים כֵּן עָשָׂה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaas Noach kekhol asher tzivvah oto Elohim ken asah.
verses.study_note: The most important verse in the chapter, and one of the most important in the Old Testament. 'Thus did Noah; according to all that God commanded him, so did he.' Seven Hebrew words of total obedience. The verse is emphatic to the point of repetition — 'all that God commanded... so did he.' This is the closing bookend to v.8: Noah found grace (v.8) → Noah walked with God (v.9) → Noah obeyed completely (v.22). Grace produces walking, and walking produces obedience. This verse will be repeated almost word for word in 7:5 — Scripture's way of underlining that Noah's obedience was real, sustained, and total. He built a massive, expensive, time-consuming structure in a world that thought him foolish. And he did it all.
verses.doctrinal_note: Faith and obedience: Hebrews 11:7 — 'By faith Noah, being warned of God of things not seen as yet, moved with fear, prepared an ark to the saving of his house.' Noah's total obedience was not legalism but faith. He could not see the Flood coming; he acted on God's word alone. This is the pattern of saving faith: hearing God's word about unseen things, and obeying completely. 'So did he' — the simplest and most powerful statement of faith in action. The same completeness of obedience is attributed to Abraham (22:3) and is ultimately fulfilled by Christ: 'obedient unto death' (Phil 2:8).

meta.overview: Genesis 6 is one of the most dramatic and theologically weighty chapters in all of Scripture. The world that began 'very good' (1:31) has descended into such corruption that God grieves He ever made it. Two remarkable events open the chapter: the mysterious union of the 'sons of God' with the 'daughters of men,' producing the Nephilim; and God's heart-breaking assessment that every imagination of man's heart is 'only evil continually.' Into this darkness, one man stands apart: 'Noah found grace in the eyes of the LORD.' The second half of the chapter records God's detailed instructions for the ark — dimensions, materials, purpose, and the covenant God pledges to keep. Genesis 6 holds the first use of 'grace' (chen) in Scripture, the first use of 'covenant' (berit), and the pattern of judgment and salvation that runs through the entire Bible.
meta.theme: Total Depravity, Sovereign Grace & the Ark of Salvation
meta.keyWordMeaning: Grace, favor — the first occurrence of this word in Scripture: 'Noah found grace in the eyes of the LORD.' Unmerited, unearned divine favor that saves from judgment.
outline: The Sons of God and Daughters of Men (vv.1-4): Mysterious unions, the Nephilim, 120 years
God's Grief over Human Corruption (vv.5-7): Every imagination only evil — the LORD repented
Noah Found Grace (v.8): The pivot of the chapter — one man, unmerited favor
Noah's Character (v.9): Just, perfect, walked with God
The Corruption of All Flesh (vv.10-12): God sees — all flesh had corrupted his way
God Announces the Flood (vv.13-17): The end of all flesh is come
The Covenant Promised (v.18): The first covenant in Scripture
Instructions for the Ark (vv.14-16, 19-21): Gopher wood, three stories, dimensions, animals, food
Noah's Obedience (v.22): He did according to all that God commanded

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Noah, Come thou and all thy house into the ark; for thee have I seen righteous before me in this generation.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה לְנֹחַ בֹּא אַתָּה וְכָל בֵּיתְךָ אֶל הַתֵּבָה כִּי אֹתְךָ רָאִיתִי צַדִּיק לְפָנַי בַּדּוֹר הַזֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Yahweh leNoach bo attah vekhol beitekha el-hattevah ki otkha ra'iti tzaddiq lefanai baddor hazzeh.
verses.study_note: The first word God speaks to Noah in this chapter is an invitation: 'Come' (bo) — not 'go,' but 'come.' God is already in the ark, welcoming Noah in. The Hebrew 'bo' implies movement toward the speaker — God invites Noah to come to where He already is. This is grace: the initiative is entirely divine. 'For thee have I seen righteous' — the basis of Noah's invitation is not his perfection but God's declaration. God has seen (ra'iti) what man has not. 'In this generation' (baddor hazzeh) — Noah's righteousness is measured against the context of total moral collapse around him. To be righteous in an unrighteous generation is itself a remarkable testimony of grace.
verses.doctrinal_note: The divine invitation: The Flood narrative opens with an invitation, not a command. 'Come into the ark' is the same pattern as Christ's invitation: 'Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden' (Matt 11:28). 'Come' appears repeatedly in Isaiah 55:1, Revelation 22:17. Salvation has always been an invitation from a God who has already prepared the place and is calling people into it. The door of the ark was open; God was inviting; the only question was whether people would enter.

verses.kjv_text: Of every clean beast thou shalt take to thee by sevens, the male and his female: and of beasts that are not clean by two, the male and his female.
verses.original_text: מִכֹּל הַבְּהֵמָה הַטְּהוֹרָה תִּקַּח לְךָ שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה אִישׁ וְאִשְׁתּוֹ וּמִן הַבְּהֵמָה אֲשֶׁר לֹא טְהֹרָה הִוא שְׁנַיִם אִישׁ וְאִשְׁתּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Mikkol habbehemah hattehorah tiqqach lekha shivah shivah ish ve'ishto umin-habbehemah asher lo tehorah hi shenayim ish ve'ishto.
verses.study_note: Seven pairs ('by sevens') of clean animals, two pairs of unclean — a specification beyond the general 'two of every kind' in 6:19-20. The extra clean animals served two purposes: (1) sacrificial worship after the Flood (8:20 — Noah offers burnt offerings from every clean animal and bird), and (2) food provision after the Flood (9:3). This verse also reveals that the clean/unclean distinction existed long before the Mosaic Law — it was not invented at Sinai but codified there. Noah already understood the categories, suggesting an earlier divine revelation about holiness that predates Moses.
verses.doctrinal_note: Clean and unclean before Moses: The clean/unclean distinction in Noah's day demonstrates that God's holiness categories are not inventions of the Mosaic covenant but part of the moral fabric of creation. The Levitical laws (Lev 11; Deut 14) did not create these distinctions — they formalized what was already understood. This matters for understanding the nature of God's law: it is not arbitrary but rooted in the created order.

verses.kjv_text: Of fowls also of the air by sevens, the male and the female; to keep seed alive upon the face of all the earth.
verses.original_text: גַּם מֵעוֹף הַשָּׁמַיִם שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה לְחַיּוֹת זֶרַע עַל פְּנֵי כָל הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Gam me'of hashamayim shivah shivah zakhar unekevah lechayot zera al penei khol ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: 'To keep seed alive' (lechayot zera) — the purpose of the entire ark project stated in a single phrase. 'Zera' (seed) is the same word as Gen 3:15 — the promised seed that would crush the serpent's head. Every creature preserved in the ark is preserved because God is preserving the seed of life. The Flood does not eliminate life; it eliminates corruption while preserving the seed for a new beginning. The ark is a greenhouse of hope in the middle of a sea of judgment.

verses.kjv_text: For yet seven days, and I will cause it to rain upon the earth forty days and forty nights; and every living substance that I have made will I destroy from off the face of the earth.
verses.original_text: כִּי לְיָמִים עוֹד שִׁבְעָה אָנֹכִי מַמְטִיר עַל הָאָרֶץ אַרְבָּעִים יוֹם וְאַרְבָּעִים לָיְלָה וּמָחִיתִי אֶת כָּל הַיְקוּם אֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתִי מֵעַל פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ki leyamim od shivah anokhi mamtir al ha'aretz arba'im yom ve'arba'im laylah umachiti et kol hayequm asher asiti me'al penei ha'adamah.
verses.study_note: Seven days of final grace before the Flood — a last week of opportunity. The number seven resonates with creation (Gen 2:2-3) and rest — this final week is a grace period before the un-creation begins. 'Forty days and forty nights' — forty is the biblical number of testing and judgment: Moses on Sinai (Ex 24:18), Israel in the wilderness (Num 14:33), Elijah fleeing Jezebel (1 Kgs 19:8), Jonah's warning to Nineveh (Jon 3:4), Jesus's temptation (Matt 4:2). The Flood's forty days establishes the biblical pattern. 'Every living substance... will I destroy' — the word 'yequm' (substance, standing thing) appears only here and in v.23, framing the total scope of judgment.
verses.doctrinal_note: The last seven days: God gave seven final days after the ark was ready and before the Flood came. This grace period is an agonizing picture of divine patience — the door was open, the animals were in, and still God waited seven more days. The same pattern: Christ's return is announced, the invitation is extended, and still God delays — 'not willing that any should perish' (2 Pet 3:9). When the seven days expired, the moment of grace ended and judgment came.

verses.kjv_text: And Noah did according unto all that the LORD commanded him.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַשׂ נֹחַ כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר צִוָּהוּ יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaas Noach kekhol asher tzivvahu Yahweh.
verses.study_note: Virtually identical to 6:22 — Scripture repeats this verdict deliberately. The repetition is not accidental; it is emphatic. Noah's obedience is so complete and so foundational that it must be stated twice. The first statement (6:22) closed the chapter of preparation; this statement (7:5) opens the chapter of execution. Same obedience, confirmed. In a world where 'every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually' (6:5), one man's complete obedience shines like a lighthouse. This verse is the hinge between God's command and God's action.

verses.kjv_text: And Noah was six hundred years old when the flood of waters was upon the earth.
verses.original_text: וְנֹחַ בֶּן שֵׁשׁ מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וְהַמַּבּוּל הָיָה מַיִם עַל הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Venoach ben shesh meot shanah vehammabbul hayah mayim al ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The precise chronological anchor: Noah was exactly 600 when the Flood came. Cross-referencing with Genesis 5 — Noah was born when Lamech was 182 (5:28-29), and Methuselah was 369 at Noah's birth. So Methuselah was 369 + 600 = 969 when the Flood came — precisely his age at death (5:27). The numbers are designed to be cross-referenced. They are not mythology but a coherent, interlocking chronological system. Noah at 600 was not an old man in his world — he had another 350 years to live (9:28-29). The Flood came at the midpoint of his life.

verses.kjv_text: And Noah went in, and his sons, and his wife, and his sons’ wives with him, into the ark, because of the waters of the flood.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא נֹחַ וּבָנָיו וְאִשְׁתּוֹ וּנְשֵׁי בָנָיו אִתּוֹ אֶל הַתֵּבָה מִפְּנֵי מֵי הַמַּבּוּל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo Noach uvanav ve'ishto uneshei vanav itto el-hattevah mippenei mei hammabbul.
verses.study_note: Eight people entered the ark: Noah, his three sons (Shem, Ham, Japheth), and their four wives. This is confirmed in 1 Peter 3:20: 'eight souls were saved by water.' Eight is the biblical number of new beginnings — circumcision on the eighth day (Lev 12:3), the eighth day in Levitical offerings, the octave in music. Eight people constitute the entire future of humanity. The whole subsequent human race descends from these eight. 'Because of the waters of the flood' — they entered in obedient anticipation of what had not yet come. Faith acts on God's word before the evidence arrives (Heb 11:1).
verses.doctrinal_note: Eight souls: Eight is the number of new beginning in Scripture — one beyond the seven of completion. The resurrection of Christ is described as the 'eighth day' (Sunday, the first day of a new week). Eight souls through the Flood into a new world foreshadows the new creation through Christ's resurrection. 1 Peter 3:20-21 connects the eight souls saved through water to baptism as 'the answer of a good conscience toward God by the resurrection of Jesus Christ.'

verses.kjv_text: Of clean beasts, and of beasts that are not clean, and of fowls, and of every thing that creepeth upon the earth,
verses.original_text: מִן הַבְּהֵמָה הַטְּהוֹרָה וּמִן הַבְּהֵמָה אֲשֶׁר אֵינֶנָּה טְהֹרָה וּמִן הָעוֹף וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר רֹמֵשׂ עַל הָאֲדָמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Min habbehemah hattehorah umin habbehemah asher einennah tehorah umin ha'of vekhol asher romes al ha'adamah.
verses.study_note: The comprehensive inventory of what entered — a deliberate echo of the creation taxonomy of Genesis 1 (clean beasts, unclean beasts, birds, creeping things). Every category of land and air creature that God created is represented. The ark contained a microcosm of creation — not as a zoo but as a seed bank of life. The same God who delighted in the diversity of His creation (1:25 — 'and God saw that it was good') now moves to preserve that diversity through judgment.

verses.kjv_text: There went in two and two unto Noah into the ark, the male and the female, as God had commanded Noah.
verses.original_text: שְׁנַיִם שְׁנַיִם בָּאוּ אֶל נֹחַ אֶל הַתֵּבָה זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֱלֹהִים אֶת נֹחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Shenayim shenayim ba'u el Noach el-hattevah zakhar unekevah ka'asher tzivvah Elohim et Noach.
verses.study_note: 'There went in... unto Noah' — the animals came to Noah, not the reverse. God superintended the gathering (as in 2:19 when God brought the animals to Adam). The phrase 'as God had commanded' closes this verse, linking the animals' entry to divine sovereignty. Even the animal world obeys God's commands. A creation that had 'corrupted its way' (6:12) now, in this small remnant, walks in orderly obedience — male and female, two by two, as God designed.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass after seven days, that the waters of the flood were upon the earth.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי לְשִׁבְעַת הַיָּמִים וּמֵי הַמַּבּוּל הָיוּ עַל הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi leshivat hayyamim umei hammabbul hayu al ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: After the seven days of final grace (v.4), the waters came — exactly as God had said. The seven-day waiting period is significant: it was also the traditional period of mourning for the dead in Israel (cf. Job 2:13, Ezek 3:15). It is as if these seven days were a mourning period — God grieving the world He was about to judge (6:6). Then on the eighth day — the day of new beginning — the waters came and the new creation process began, though first through destruction. The precision of 'after seven days' underlines that God's word is always fulfilled exactly on schedule.

verses.kjv_text: In the six hundredth year of Noah’s life, in the second month, the seventeenth day of the month, the same day were all the fountains of the great deep broken up, and the windows of heaven were opened.
verses.original_text: בִּשְׁנַת שֵׁשׁ מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה לְחַיֵּי נֹחַ בַּחֹדֶשׁ הַשֵּׁנִי בְּשִׁבְעָה עָשָׂר יוֹם לַחֹדֶשׁ בַּיּוֹם הַזֶּה נִבְקְעוּ כָּל מַעְיְנֹת תְּהוֹם רַבָּה וַאֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם נִפְתָּחוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Bishenat shesh meot shanah lechayei Noach bachodesh hasheni beshivah asar yom lacchodesh bayyom hazzeh nivqeu khol ma'yenot tehom rabbah va'arubboth hashamayim niftachu.
verses.study_note: The most precisely dated event in Genesis — year, month, and day. This is not myth but historical record. The date given (2nd month, 17th day) was later recognized as the anniversary of the Flood in Jewish tradition. Two simultaneous, catastrophic water sources: (1) 'All the fountains of the great deep' (kol ma'yenot tehom rabbah) — the subterranean waters of the earth rupturing upward, breaking open the ocean floors and underground reservoirs. (2) 'The windows of heaven' (arubboth hashamayim) — the atmospheric waters above the firmament (1:6-8) released downward. The Flood came from below AND above simultaneously — a cosmic hydraulic event. This is no ordinary rainstorm but a reversal of the separation of waters in Genesis 1:6-9.
verses.doctrinal_note: The cosmic scale of the Flood: The dual mechanism — fountains of the deep and windows of heaven — points to a global, catastrophic event, not a local flood. The language of 'tehom rabbah' (the great deep) connects to Genesis 1:2 (tehom — the deep over which the Spirit hovered). The Flood is a reversal of creation's Day 2 and Day 3 — the separated waters are rejoined. This is why 2 Peter 3:5-6 describes the Flood as the world that 'was overflowed with water, and perished' — it was a complete undoing of the created order.

verses.kjv_text: And the rain was upon the earth forty days and forty nights.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי הַגֶּשֶׁם עַל הָאָרֶץ אַרְבָּעִים יוֹם וְאַרְבָּעִים לָיְלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi haggeshem al ha'aretz arba'im yom ve'arba'im laylah.
verses.study_note: The rain — 'geshem' (heavy, torrential rain) — fell for exactly forty days and nights, as God promised in v.4. The precision confirms divine faithfulness: God said forty, it rained forty. But the forty days of rain was not the full duration of the Flood — the waters continued to prevail for 150 days total (v.24), and it would be over a year before Noah exited the ark (8:13-14). The rain was the trigger and primary mechanism; the total flood was vastly larger and longer.

verses.kjv_text: In the selfsame day entered Noah, and Shem, and Ham, and Japheth, the sons of Noah, and Noah’s wife, and the three wives of his sons with them, into the ark;
verses.original_text: בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה בָּא נֹחַ וְשֵׁם וָחָם וָיֶפֶת בְּנֵי נֹחַ וְאֵשֶׁת נֹחַ וּשְׁלֹשֶׁת נְשֵׁי בָנָיו אִתָּם אֶל הַתֵּבָה׃
verses.transliteration: Be'etzem hayyom hazzeh ba Noach veShem veCham vaYafet benei Noach ve'eshet Noach usheloshet neshei vanav ittam el-hattevah.
verses.study_note: 'In the selfsame day' (be'etzem hayyom hazzeh) — a phrase used in Scripture for momentous, decisive days: the same phrase marks the day of the Exodus (Ex 12:41, 51), the day Moses died (Deut 32:48), and the day Jerusalem fell (Ezek 24:2). 'This very day' — it carries the weight of divine appointment. This was not a gradual, casual process but a single decisive day. The eight names are recorded individually — each person matters to God. The ark received them 'in the selfsame day' — the day of deliverance was also the day judgment began.

verses.kjv_text: They, and every beast after his kind, and all the cattle after their kind, and every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth after his kind, and every fowl after his kind, every bird of every sort.
verses.original_text: הֵמָּה וְכָל הַחַיָּה לְמִינָהּ וְכָל הַבְּהֵמָה לְמִינָהּ וְכָל הָרֶמֶשׂ הָרֹמֵשׂ עַל הָאָרֶץ לְמִינֵהוּ וְכָל הָעוֹף לְמִינֵהוּ כֹּל צִפּוֹר כָּל כָּנָף׃
verses.transliteration: Hemmah vekhol hachayah leminah vekhol habbehemah leminah vekhol haremes haromes al ha'aretz leminehu vekhol ha'of leminehu kol tzippor kol kanaf.
verses.study_note: A second recitation of the creation taxonomy — 'after their kind' (leminehu) appearing four times in one verse. This is not redundancy but theological emphasis: God preserves the created 'kinds' (min). The same God who delighted in categorizing His creation (Gen 1) now recites those same categories as they enter the ark. Creation is being folded into the ark for safekeeping. 'Every bird of every sort' (kol tzippor kol kanaf — every bird of every wing) — even the most specific individual is not forgotten.

verses.kjv_text: And they went in unto Noah into the ark, two and two of all flesh, wherein is the breath of life.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹאוּ אֶל נֹחַ אֶל הַתֵּבָה שְׁנַיִם שְׁנַיִם מִכָּל הַבָּשָׂר אֲשֶׁר בּוֹ רוּחַ חַיִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo'u el Noach el-hattevah shenayim shenayim mikkol habasar asher bo ruach chayyim.
verses.study_note: 'Wherein is the breath of life' (asher bo ruach chayyim) — connecting directly to Gen 2:7 when God breathed the breath of life into Adam. Every creature preserved in the ark carries God's gift of life-breath. The ark preserves not merely bodies but God's life-breath distributed through creation. This life — 'ruach chayyim' — is what the waters will take from those outside and what the ark preserves for those inside. The distinction between in and out is the distinction between life and death.

verses.kjv_text: And they that went in, went in male and female of all flesh, as God had commanded him: and the LORD shut him in.
verses.original_text: וְהַבָּאִים זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה מִכָּל בָּשָׂר בָּאוּ כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים וַיִּסְגֹּר יְהוָה בַּעֲדוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehabba'im zakhar unekevah mikkol basar ba'u ka'asher tzivvah oto Elohim vayyisgur Yahweh ba'ado.
verses.study_note: The most theologically charged sentence in the chapter — six words in Hebrew: 'vayyisgur Yahweh ba'ado' — 'and the LORD shut him in.' God personally closed and sealed the door. Not Noah. Not a servant. The LORD (YHWH — the covenant God of grace) shut the door. This act has two simultaneous meanings: (1) Perfect security for those inside — they are sealed by God's own hand. Nothing can open what God has shut (Rev 3:7: 'he that shutteth and no man openeth'). (2) Final, irreversible judgment for those outside — God has declared the time of grace over. The door is now shut by divine decree. Note also the name shift: 'God' (Elohim) commands the entry; 'the LORD' (YHWH) shuts the door. The covenant God performs the covenant act of preservation.
verses.doctrinal_note: God shuts the door: This verse is the basis of one of the most solemn warnings in Scripture. When God shuts a door, no human power can open it. The ten foolish virgins arrived after the door was shut and heard: 'I know you not' (Matt 25:10-12). The same applies to the ark: once the LORD shut it, no amount of knocking, weeping, or pleading could open it from the outside. The time of grace is determined by God alone. The urgency of the gospel rests on this truth: the door is open now — but God will shut it.

verses.kjv_text: And the flood was forty days upon the earth; and the waters increased, and bare up the ark, and it was lift up above the earth.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי הַמַּבּוּל אַרְבָּעִים יוֹם עַל הָאָרֶץ וַיִּרְבּוּ הַמַּיִם וַיִּשְׂאוּ אֶת הַתֵּבָה וַתָּרָם מֵעַל הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi hammabbul arba'im yom al ha'aretz vayyirbu hammayim vayyis'u et-hattevah vataram me'al ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The waters that threatened to destroy the ark instead lifted it up. 'Bare up the ark' (vayyis'u et-hattevah) — the very same floodwaters that drowned the world carried the ark safely above it. The judgment that destroyed everything else became the mechanism of salvation for those inside. This is the profound typology: the waters of judgment that condemn the unbeliever are the same waters that, through the ark, lift the believer above the catastrophe. Paul uses the same principle: 'the same fire that destroys the chaff purifies the gold' (1 Cor 3:13-15). Judgment and salvation are not opposites but the same event seen from inside or outside the ark.
verses.doctrinal_note: Judgment as salvation's vehicle: The flood-waters that killed the world were the very waters that lifted Noah above it. This is the gospel paradox: the wrath of God poured out on the cross is simultaneously the judgment that condemns sin and the flood that lifts the believer into new life. Christ bore the flood of divine wrath — and that very bearing lifts us above condemnation (Rom 8:1). 'There is therefore now no condemnation to them which are in Christ Jesus' — they are in the ark, lifted above the flood.

verses.kjv_text: And the waters prevailed, and were increased greatly upon the earth; and the ark went upon the face of the waters.
verses.original_text: וַיִּגְבְּרוּ הַמַּיִם וַיִּרְבּוּ מְאֹד עַל הָאָרֶץ וַתֵּלֶךְ הַתֵּבָה עַל פְּנֵי הַמָּיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyigberu hammayim vayyirbu me'od al ha'aretz vattelekh hattevah al penei hammayim.
verses.study_note: 'Prevailed' (vayyigberu — to overpower, to be mighty) — the waters were not merely rising; they were triumphant, overwhelming every earthly height. 'The ark went upon the face of the waters' (vattelekh hattevah al penei hammayim) — the ark moved (halakh — to walk) on the surface of the waters. The ark 'walks' while the world drowns — a striking image. God's vessel of salvation moves purposefully while judgment covers the earth. 'Upon the face of the waters' echoes Gen 1:2 — the Spirit moved upon the face of the waters at creation; now the ark moves upon them at un-creation.

verses.kjv_text: And the waters prevailed exceedingly upon the earth; and all the high hills, that were under the whole heaven, were covered.
verses.original_text: וְהַמַּיִם גָּבְרוּ מְאֹד מְאֹד עַל הָאָרֶץ וַיְכֻסּוּ כָּל הֶהָרִים הַגְּבֹהִים אֲשֶׁר תַּחַת כָּל הַשָּׁמָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehammayim gaveru me'od me'od al ha'aretz vayekhassu khol heharim hagevonim asher tachat kol hashamayim.
verses.study_note: 'Exceedingly, exceedingly' (me'od me'od) — the doubled superlative insists on totality. 'All the high hills... under the whole heaven' — the scope is universal: every elevated landmass under every part of the sky was submerged. This language is deliberately global — not 'the hills of this region' or 'the mountains nearby' but 'all the high hills under the whole heaven.' The text will not permit a local flood interpretation. The theological purpose is also total: if any human outside the ark could have survived on a mountain peak, the judgment would have been partial. The Flood's universality corresponds to the universality of sin (6:5) and the universality of grace available in the ark.
verses.doctrinal_note: Global or local? The language of vv.19-21 is as universal as language can be: 'all the high hills under the whole heaven,' 'all flesh died,' 'all in whose nostrils was the breath of life.' The Hebrew 'kol' (all/every) appears repeatedly and is not qualified. The NT confirms the global reading: Matt 24:38-39 ('the flood came and took them all away'), 2 Pet 3:6 ('the world that then was... perished'). The global scope is theologically essential — sin was universal; judgment was universal; and grace through the ark was available to all.

verses.kjv_text: Fifteen cubits upward did the waters prevail; and the mountains were covered.
verses.original_text: חֲמֵשׁ עֶשְׂרֵה אַמָּה מִלְמַעְלָה גָּבְרוּ הַמַּיִם וַיְכֻסּוּ הֶהָרִים׃
verses.transliteration: Chamesh esreh ammah milma'lah gavru hammayim vayekhassu heharim.
verses.study_note: Fifteen cubits — approximately 22-23 feet above the highest mountains. This specific measurement may have a practical significance: the ark itself drew 15 cubits of water (half its 30-cubit height, 6:15). In other words, the waters were deep enough that even the highest mountains could not snag or ground the ark — it floated freely above every obstacle. The precision of this number is the precision of a God who calculated the necessary depth for His vessel of salvation to navigate safely above all earthly obstacles.

verses.kjv_text: And all flesh died that moved upon the earth, both of fowl, and of cattle, and of beast, and of every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth, and every man:
verses.original_text: וַיִּגְוַע כָּל בָּשָׂר הָרֹמֵשׂ עַל הָאָרֶץ בָּעוֹף וּבַבְּהֵמָה וּבַחַיָּה וּבְכָל הַשֶּׁרֶץ הַשֹּׁרֵץ עַל הָאָרֶץ וְכֹל הָאָדָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyigva kol basar haromes al ha'aretz ba'of uvabbehemah uvachayah uvkhol hasheretz hashoretz al ha'aretz vekhol ha'adam.
verses.study_note: 'Died' (vayyigva) — a specific word for dying by expiration/breathing out one's last. The same taxonomy of Genesis 1 is now listed in reverse, each category dying in turn. 'And every man' (vekhol ha'adam) — the human race, the crown of creation, is not exempted. The Flood did not spare humanity while judging animals; it judged the entire corrupted creation. The silence that follows this verse in the narrative is the silence of a world emptied of all life except for what is in the ark. Creation's song has gone silent — birds, beasts, man — all gone. Only the sound of water remains.

verses.kjv_text: All in whose nostrils was the breath of life, of all that was in the dry land, died.
verses.original_text: כֹּל אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁמַת רוּחַ חַיִּים בְּאַפָּיו מִכֹּל אֲשֶׁר בֶּחָרָבָה מֵתוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Kol asher nishmat ruach chayyim be'appav mikkol asher becharavah metu.
verses.study_note: 'The breath of life' (nishmat ruach chayyim) — the same phrase as Genesis 2:7 when God breathed into Adam's nostrils. Every land creature that carried God's original breath of life died. The word 'nostrils' (appav) is the same as in 2:7 — God breathed into his nostrils; now the breath in those nostrils goes out forever. This is the full reversal of creation: God breathed in (2:7); now the breath goes out (7:22). The Flood undoes the breath-giving of creation day. Only in the new creation — when Christ breathes on His disciples (John 20:22) — is the life-breath restored.

verses.kjv_text: And every living substance was destroyed which was upon the face of the ground, both man, and cattle, and the creeping things, and the fowl of the heaven; and they were destroyed from the earth: and Noah only remained alive, and they that were with him in the ark.
verses.original_text: וַיִּמַח אֶת כָּל הַיְקוּם אֲשֶׁר עַל פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה מֵאָדָם עַד בְּהֵמָה עַד רֶמֶשׂ וְעַד עוֹף הַשָּׁמַיִם וַיִּמָּחוּ מִן הָאָרֶץ וַיִּשָּׁאֶר אַךְ נֹחַ וַאֲשֶׁר אִתּוֹ בַּתֵּבָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyimach et kol hayequm asher al penei ha'adamah me'adam ad behemah ad remes ve'ad of hashamayim vayyimmachehu min ha'aretz vayyisha'er akh Noach va'asher itto battevah.
verses.study_note: The pivot of the chapter in its final verses: 'And every living substance was destroyed' (vayyimach — to wipe out, blot out, exactly as God threatened in 6:7) — and then the turn: 'and Noah only remained' (vayyisha'er akh Noach). 'Only' (akh) — an exclusive particle. The entire world gone; one man remained. The contrast is absolute. 'They that were with him in the ark' — those eight souls became the entire human population of the earth. The ark was not a comfortable addition to life; it was life itself. Everything outside was gone; everything that mattered was inside.
verses.doctrinal_note: Noah only remained: The word 'remained' (sha'ar) is the root of the Hebrew concept of the 'remnant' (she'erit/sha'ar) — the theological concept running through all the prophets of a preserved, faithful remnant through whom God's purposes continue. Isaiah, Jeremiah, Micah, Zephaniah, Paul (Rom 9:27, 11:5) — all build on the pattern established here: judgment is total, but God preserves a remnant. Noah is the first remnant. Israel will be the next. The church is the ultimate remnant — saved not by wood and pitch but by the cross.

verses.kjv_text: And the waters prevailed upon the earth an hundred and fifty days.
verses.original_text: וַיִּגְבְּרוּ הַמַּיִם עַל הָאָרֶץ חֲמִשִּׁים וּמְאַת יוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyigberu hammayim al ha'aretz chamishim ume'at yom.
verses.study_note: 150 days — five months of the waters 'prevailing.' The 40 days of rain (v.12) was the beginning; the total domination of the waters lasted 150 days before they began to recede (8:3). This closing verse bridges to chapter 8 and sets the stage for the turn: the waters that prevailed for 150 days will then begin their retreat as God 'remembers' Noah (8:1). The word 'remembered' (zakar) that opens Genesis 8 is the great reversal of Genesis 7:23 — after the world has been wiped out (mach) and Noah only remains, God 'remembers' him and the waters begin to subside. Memory overcomes erasure; grace overcomes judgment.
verses.doctrinal_note: God remembered Noah: The transition from 7:24 to 8:1 is the theological heart of the Flood narrative. The waters 'prevailed' (gaveru) for 150 days — and then 'God remembered (zakar) Noah.' Divine remembrance is not a recovery from forgetfulness; it is a decisive act of covenant faithfulness. The same word is used of God remembering Abraham when He rescued Lot (19:29), remembering Rachel and opening her womb (30:22), and remembering His covenant with Israel in Egypt (Ex 2:24). Remembrance is the beginning of redemption.

meta.overview: Genesis 7 is the chapter of the Flood itself — the moment the long-announced judgment of God falls upon the earth. After 120 years of grace (6:3), 100 years of ark-building, and Noah's preaching of righteousness (2 Pet 2:5), the door is finally shut and the waters come. The chapter records God's final invitation to Noah (vv.1-4), the obedient entry of Noah's family and the animals (vv.5-9), the precise date the Flood began (v.11), the cosmic scale of the catastrophe — rain from above and fountains from below (v.11) — the moment God Himself shuts the door (v.16), the rising of the waters above every mountain (v.20), and the death of all flesh (vv.21-23). Two verses frame the entire tragedy: 'God shut him in' (v.16) — the security of the saved; and 'every living substance was destroyed' (v.23) — the totality of judgment. Yet Noah remained — and through him, all of us.
meta.theme: Judgment Falls — The Security of the Saved & the Totality of Wrath
meta.keyWordMeaning: And the LORD shut him in — the most tender and the most terrifying sentence in the chapter. God personally sealed Noah inside the ark, guaranteeing his salvation and sealing the fate of those outside.
outline: God's Final Invitation (vv.1-4): Come thou into the ark — righteous before Me in this generation
The Obedient Entry (vv.5-9): Noah did all the LORD commanded — clean and unclean, seven and two
The Day the Flood Began (vv.10-12): Fountains of the deep broken up, windows of heaven opened
All Enter the Ark (vv.13-16): The same day — Noah, his family, every creature
God Shuts the Door (v.16): The LORD shut him in — salvation sealed, judgment sealed
The Waters Rise (vv.17-20): Forty days — fifteen cubits above the mountains
All Flesh Dies (vv.21-23): Every living thing destroyed — only Noah remained
The Duration (v.24): Waters prevailed 150 days

verses.kjv_text: And God remembered Noah, and every living thing, and all the cattle that was with him in the ark: and God made a wind to pass over the earth, and the waters asswaged;
verses.original_text: וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים אֶת נֹחַ וְאֵת כָּל הַחַיָּה וְאֶת כָּל הַבְּהֵמָה אֲשֶׁר אִתּוֹ בַּתֵּבָה וַיַּעֲבֵר אֱלֹהִים רוּחַ עַל הָאָרֶץ וַיָּשֹׁכּוּ הַמָּיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyizkor Elohim et Noach ve'et kol hachayah ve'et kol habbehemah asher itto battevah vayyaaver Elohim ruach al ha'aretz vayyashochu hammayim.
verses.study_note: Three words open the chapter and reverse everything: 'God remembered Noah' (vayyizkor Elohim et Noach). After 150 days of silence — of water prevailing, of a world submerged — God's remembrance acts. 'Zakar' (remember) in Scripture is never mere mental recollection; it is covenant action. When God 'remembers,' He moves. The same word is used in Exodus 2:24 — 'God remembered his covenant with Abraham' and the Exodus began; in Genesis 19:29 — 'God remembered Abraham' and rescued Lot; in Genesis 30:22 — 'God remembered Rachel' and she conceived. Divine remembrance is the trigger of redemption. 'God made a wind' (ruach) — the same word as Spirit (ruach) in Genesis 1:2 when the Spirit hovered over the waters. Now the wind-Spirit moves over the flood-waters to initiate the new creation, exactly as it moved over the original chaos.
verses.doctrinal_note: God remembers — covenant faithfulness: When God 'remembers,' He has not forgotten. He was never absent from Noah's ark. But 'remembering' in Hebrew covenant language means turning His active, redemptive attention toward His people. Psalm 9:12: 'He forgetteth not the cry of the humble.' Psalm 105:8: 'He hath remembered his covenant for ever.' The God who shut Noah in (7:16) is the same God who remembers him and begins the work of restoration. The same God who 'shut him in' now 'opens' the new world. What He closes, He also opens (Rev 3:7).

verses.kjv_text: The fountains also of the deep and the windows of heaven were stopped, and the rain from heaven was restrained;
verses.original_text: וַיִּסָּכְרוּ מַעְיְנֹת תְּהוֹם וַאֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמָיִם וַיִּכָּלֵא הַגֶּשֶׁם מִן הַשָּׁמָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyissakhru ma'yenot tehom va'arubboth hashamayim vayyikkale haggeshem min hashamayim.
verses.study_note: The exact reversal of 7:11 — 'all the fountains of the great deep were broken up, and the windows of heaven were opened.' Now both are shut. The fountains are 'stopped' (sakharu — sealed, stopped up) and the windows are 'restrained' (kala — withheld, restrained). The catastrophic dual mechanism of the Flood is now doubly reversed. This verse-pair (7:11 / 8:2) forms a perfect chiastic bracket around the entire flood event. What God opened, He closes; what He broke open, He seals. The precision of the reversal testifies to divine sovereignty at every stage — both in judgment and in restoration.

verses.kjv_text: And the waters returned from off the earth continually: and after the end of the hundred and fifty days the waters were abated.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשֻׁבוּ הַמַּיִם מֵעַל הָאָרֶץ הָלוֹךְ וָשׁוֹב וַיַּחְסְרוּ הַמַּיִם מִקְצֵה חֲמִשִּׁים וּמְאַת יוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashuvu hammayim me'al ha'aretz halokh vashuv vayyachseru hammayim miqqetzeh chamishim ume'at yom.
verses.study_note: 'Returned... continually' (halokh vashuv — literally 'going and returning') — a Hebrew idiom for a continuous, gradual process. The waters didn't vanish suddenly; they receded progressively. This is the pattern of God's restoration work — often gradual, steady, and sustained rather than instantaneous. After exactly 150 days from the Flood's start, the waters began measurably decreasing (chaser — to diminish, to be lacking). The mathematical precision of 150 days (7:24 and 8:3) confirms these are real chronological records, not literary approximations.

verses.kjv_text: And the ark rested in the seventh month, on the seventeenth day of the month, upon the mountains of Ararat.
verses.original_text: וַתָּנַח הַתֵּבָה בַּחֹדֶשׁ הַשְּׁבִיעִי בְּשִׁבְעָה עָשָׂר יוֹם לַחֹדֶשׁ עַל הָרֵי אֲרָרָט׃
verses.transliteration: Vatanach hattevah bachodesh hashevi'i beshivah asar yom lacchodesh al harei Ararat.
verses.study_note: 'The ark rested' (vatanach) — from the same root as Noah's name (nuach — rest). Noah found rest when the ark found rest. The place of resting: 'the mountains of Ararat' (harei Ararat) — the ancient kingdom of Urartu, in modern-day eastern Turkey near Lake Van. The Hebrew 'harei' is plural — the mountain range, not necessarily the single peak. The date: 7th month, 17th day. In the later Jewish calendar, the 7th month 17th day would fall in the month of Nisan — and Jewish tradition notes that the exact same date (17th Nisan) is the date of the resurrection of Christ. The ark rested on the day that would become resurrection day. The connection between Noah's rest and Christ's resurrection is profound: both mark the beginning of a new world after judgment.
verses.doctrinal_note: The ark's resting as type: 'Vatanach' — the ark rested. This is Noah's name (nuach) fulfilled in geography. The man whose name meant 'rest' found rest when his vessel of salvation rested on solid ground after the Flood. This foreshadows Christ's resurrection rest — 'he rested from all his works' (Heb 4:4,10) — and the believer's ultimate rest in the new creation. The ark's resting on the mountain is the first act of solid-ground new creation.

verses.kjv_text: And the waters decreased continually until the tenth month: in the tenth month, on the first day of the month, were the tops of the mountains seen.
verses.original_text: וְהַמַּיִם הָיוּ הָלוֹךְ וְחָסוֹר עַד הַחֹדֶשׁ הָעֲשִׂירִי בָּעֲשִׂירִי בְּאֶחָד לַחֹדֶשׁ נִרְאוּ רָאשֵׁי הֶהָרִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vehammayim hayu halokh vechasor ad hachodesh ha'asiri ba'asiri be'echad lacchodesh nir'u rashei heharim.
verses.study_note: Two and a half months after the ark rested on Ararat, the mountaintops became visible. The gradual revelation of land is also the gradual revelation of hope — each mountaintop that appeared was a promise that the new world was emerging from beneath the waters. 'Were seen' (nir'u — niphal passive of ra'ah, to see) — the tops appeared, were revealed. The same word is used of God's appearances (theophany) — God 'appears' (nira) to Abraham, Isaac, Jacob. Land appearing from the waters echoes creation Day 3 (1:9-10) when God gathered the waters and 'let the dry land appear' (vateira hayyabashah).

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass at the end of forty days, that Noah opened the window of the ark which he had made:
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי מִקֵּץ אַרְבָּעִים יוֹם וַיִּפְתַּח נֹחַ אֶת חַלּוֹן הַתֵּבָה אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi miqqetz arba'im yom vayyiftach Noach et challon hattevah asher asah.
verses.study_note: After another forty days of waiting — echoing the forty days of rain (7:12) — Noah opens the window (challon — the same word for a latticed window, cf. 2 Kgs 1:2). Noah had waited patiently in the ark: 150 days of flood, then the ark rested, then 40 more days before he opened the window. The total time in the ark will be over a year (see vv.13-14). Noah's patience is a theological virtue — he does not burst out the moment conditions seem better but waits on God's timing. 'Which he had made' — a small note reminding us that this window was part of God's original design (6:16), crafted by Noah's obedient hands, now serving its purpose.

verses.kjv_text: And he sent forth a raven, which went forth to and fro, until the waters were dried up from off the earth.
verses.original_text: וַיְשַׁלַּח אֶת הָעֹרֵב וַיֵּצֵא יָצוֹא וָשׁוֹב עַד יְבֹשֶׁת הַמַּיִם מֵעַל הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayeshalach et ha'orev vayyetze yatzo vashuv ad yevoshet hammayim me'al ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The raven (orev) is sent first. The raven is an unclean bird (Lev 11:15; Deut 14:14), a carrion-feeder — and it finds the Flood's aftermath entirely to its liking. It 'went forth to and fro' (yatzo vashuv — going out and returning, the same idiom as the waters receding in v.3) — but it never definitively returned to the ark. The raven could feed on the floating carcasses of the judgment and had no need to land. It could survive in the world of death. This is often read typologically: the raven represents the old nature, the flesh — which is perfectly comfortable in the realm of death and judgment and never brings back a message of new life. Only the dove, the clean bird, will carry the gospel of the olive leaf.
verses.doctrinal_note: The raven and the dove: The contrast between the raven (unclean, feeds on death, never returns with life) and the dove (clean, searches for life, returns with an olive branch) has been read as a picture of law and grace, flesh and spirit, or judgment and peace. The dove (yonah) is the Hebrew name of the prophet Jonah — who himself was sent on a mission, fled, was cast into the deep, and returned to fulfill his calling. The dove is also the symbol of the Holy Spirit at Jesus' baptism (Matt 3:16) — descending and resting, bringing the message of new creation.

verses.kjv_text: Also he sent forth a dove from him, to see if the waters were abated from off the face of the ground;
verses.original_text: וַיְשַׁלַּח אֶת הַיּוֹנָה מֵאִתּוֹ לִרְאוֹת הֲקַלּוּ הַמַּיִם מֵעַל פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayeshalach et hayyonah me'itto lirot haqqallu hammayim me'al penei ha'adamah.
verses.study_note: The dove (yonah) is sent 'from him' (me'itto — from with him) — a more intimate connection than the raven. The dove was sent with a purpose: 'to see' (lirot) whether the waters had subsided. Noah uses creation's own creatures as his instruments of inquiry. The dove's mission is to discern whether the earth is ready for new life — to bring back intelligence from the new world. This act of sending and waiting is itself a picture of prayer: we release our petitions toward God, wait, and watch for the answer to return.

verses.kjv_text: But the dove found no rest for the sole of her foot, and she returned unto him into the ark, for the waters were on the face of the whole earth: then he put forth his hand, and took her, and pulled her in unto him into the ark.
verses.original_text: וְלֹא מָצְאָה הַיּוֹנָה מָנוֹחַ לְכַף רַגְלָהּ וַתָּשָׁב אֵלָיו אֶל הַתֵּבָה כִּי מַיִם עַל פְּנֵי כָל הָאָרֶץ וַיִּשְׁלַח יָדוֹ וַיִּקָּחֶהָ וַיָּבֵא אֹתָהּ אֵלָיו אֶל הַתֵּבָה׃
verses.transliteration: Velo matze'ah hayyonah manoach lekhaph raglah vatashav elav el-hattevah ki mayim al penei khol ha'aretz vayyishlach yado vayyiqqacheha vayyave otah elav el-hattevah.
verses.study_note: 'Found no rest for the sole of her foot' (lo matz'ah hayyonah manoach) — the same root as Noah (nuach/manoach). The dove found no 'Noah-rest' — no resting place in the still-flooded world. The earth was not yet ready. She returned, and Noah reached out his hand and drew her back in. This tender detail — 'he put forth his hand, and took her, and pulled her in' — is one of the most intimate moments in the narrative. Noah personally received the dove back, sheltering her. It is a picture of the Good Shepherd who, when the world offers no rest, draws His own back to Himself. 'Come unto me... and I will give you rest' (Matt 11:28) — the same word: manoach.
verses.doctrinal_note: No rest in the world of judgment: The dove's inability to find a resting place in the flooded world is the condition of every soul before the new creation is complete. 'There is no peace, saith my God, to the wicked' (Isa 57:20-21). Augustine: 'our heart is restless until it finds rest in Thee.' The dove returns to the ark — to Noah's hand — because the only rest available is in the place of salvation. The world under judgment offers no foothold for the soul; only Christ — the ark — gives true manoach.

verses.kjv_text: And he stayed yet other seven days; and again he sent forth the dove out of the ark;
verses.original_text: וַיָּחֶל עוֹד שִׁבְעַת יָמִים אֲחֵרִים וַיֹּסֶף שַׁלַּח אֶת הַיּוֹנָה מִן הַתֵּבָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyachel od shivat yamim acherim vayyoseph shalach et-hayyonah min-hattevah.
verses.study_note: Another seven days of waiting — Noah's patient rhythm of sevens echoes God's seven-day creation and the seven-day grace period before the Flood (7:4). Noah is not impetuous; he waits seven days between each sending. This patience is the hallmark of faith that operates on God's timetable, not its own urgency. 'He stayed' (yachal — to wait with hope, to tarry in expectation) — the same word as 'I will wait upon the LORD' in the Psalms (Ps 130:5). Waiting with hope is not passive resignation but active anticipation.

verses.kjv_text: And the dove came in to him in the evening; and, lo, in her mouth was an olive leaf pluckt off: so Noah knew that the waters were abated from off the earth.
verses.original_text: וַתָּבֹא אֵלָיו הַיּוֹנָה לְעֵת עֶרֶב וְהִנֵּה עֲלֵה זַיִת טָרָף בְּפִיהָ וַיֵּדַע נֹחַ כִּי קַלּוּ הַמַּיִם מֵעַל הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vatavo elav hayyonah le'et erev vehineh aleh zayit taraf befiha vayyeda Noach ki qallu hammayim me'al ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The most famous image in the Flood narrative: the dove returning at evening with a fresh olive leaf in her beak. Every element carries meaning. 'Evening' (erev) — the end of the day, when hope might be fading, the dove returns with the answer. 'Behold' (vehineh — and look!) — the same exclamation of wonder as in creation (1:31). 'Olive leaf' (aleh zayit) — the olive tree was known for its deep roots and ability to survive submerged; the fact that it was 'freshly plucked' (taraf — newly torn off, still green) confirmed living vegetation had returned to the earth. The olive branch has become the universal symbol of peace precisely because of this moment. 'Noah knew' (vayyeda Noach) — a knowing born of evidence; faith confirmed by sight.
verses.doctrinal_note: The olive branch as peace: The olive leaf from the dove has become the universal symbol of peace — from Noah's ark to the United Nations emblem. It represents the end of hostility between God and creation, the beginning of covenant peace. The olive tree in Scripture is associated with anointing (oil), peace, prosperity, and the presence of God (Ps 52:8 — 'I am like a green olive tree in the house of God'). The dove with the olive branch anticipates the Holy Spirit descending at Jesus' baptism — bringing the message of God's peace with humanity through the anointed One.

verses.kjv_text: And he stayed yet other seven days; and sent forth the dove; which returned not again unto him any more.
verses.original_text: וַיִּיָּחֶל עוֹד שִׁבְעַת יָמִים אֲחֵרִים וַיְשַׁלַּח אֶת הַיּוֹנָה וְלֹא יָסְפָה שׁוּב אֵלָיו עוֹד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiyachel od shivat yamim acherim vayeshalach et-hayyonah velo yasefah shuv elav od.
verses.study_note: The third sending — and the dove does not return. 'She returned not again unto him any more' (lo yasefah shuv elav od) — the new earth could receive her. She had found her resting place outside the ark. This is the completion of the sign: the first sending showed the world was not ready; the second showed life had begun; the third showed the world was fully habitable. The dove's non-return is as significant as her return with the olive leaf. The silence is the answer. When the messenger sent to the new world does not come back, it means the new world is ready to receive all who will come.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass in the six hundredth and first year, in the first month, the first day of the month, the waters were dried up from off the earth: and Noah removed the covering of the ark, and looked, and, behold, the face of the ground was dry.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בְּאַחַת וְשֵׁשׁ מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה בָּרִאשׁוֹן בְּאֶחָד לַחֹדֶשׁ חָרְבוּ הַמַּיִם מֵעַל הָאָרֶץ וַיָּסַר נֹחַ אֶת מִכְסֵה הַתֵּבָה וַיַּרְא וְהִנֵּה חָרְבוּ פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi be'achat veshesh meot shanah barishon be'echad lacchodesh charvu hammayim me'al ha'aretz vayyasar Noach et mikhseh hattevah vayyar vehineh charvu penei ha'adamah.
verses.study_note: The first day of the first month of Noah's 601st year — a new year's day, a new beginning. The waters were 'dried up' (charevu — thoroughly dry). Noah removed the covering (mikhseh) of the ark and 'looked' (vayyar) — the same verb used of God's inspection of creation ('God saw' in Genesis 1). Noah now surveys the new world as a steward. 'And behold' (vehineh) — the same exclamation of wonder as in 1:31 ('and God saw... behold, it was very good'). The new creation is presented to Noah's eyes: the face of the ground (penei ha'adamah) — the same phrase as 2:6, when the streams watered the face of the ground before man was formed. The earth is beginning again.

verses.kjv_text: And in the second month, on the seven and twentieth day of the month, was the earth dried.
verses.original_text: וּבַחֹדֶשׁ הַשֵּׁנִי בְּשִׁבְעָה וְעֶשְׂרִים יוֹם לַחֹדֶשׁ יָבְשָׁה הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Uvacchodesh hasheni beshivah ve'esrim yom lacchodesh yavshah ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The earth was completely dry on the 27th day of the 2nd month — almost two months after the waters first dried up (v.13). 'Yavshah' (dried, firm-dry) — a stronger word than the 'charvu' of v.13. The surface had dried first; now the ground itself was firm. The Flood began on the 17th day of the 2nd month in Noah's 600th year (7:11); it ended on the 27th day of the 2nd month in his 601st year. Total time in the ark: approximately 12 months and 10 days — just over a full year. The entire year was spent in the ark before God brought them out.

verses.kjv_text: And God spake unto Noah, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר אֱלֹהִים אֶל נֹחַ לֵאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber Elohim el Noach lemor.
verses.study_note: A simple, powerful sentence — God speaks. Notice that Noah did not exit the ark on his own initiative, even though the ground was dry. He waited for the divine command. The earth was ready in v.14; the command to exit does not come until v.16. Noah had been told to 'come in' (7:1) and now must be told to 'go forth' (v.16). He neither entered nor exited by his own decision. This is the obedience of faith — not moving without God's word, even when circumstances seem ready. The dry ground alone was not enough; Noah waited for God's voice.
verses.doctrinal_note: Waiting for the word: Noah's patient waiting inside the dry-grounded ark is a picture of the believer who does not move by sight or circumstance alone but by God's word. The earth could receive him; but he waited for God to say so. This is the discipline of guidance — not acting on what seems obvious but waiting for divine instruction. Proverbs 3:5-6: 'lean not unto thine own understanding... and he shall direct thy paths.'

verses.kjv_text: Go forth of the ark, thou, and thy wife, and thy sons, and thy sons’ wives with thee.
verses.original_text: צֵא מִן הַתֵּבָה אַתָּה וְאִשְׁתְּךָ וּבָנֶיךָ וּנְשֵׁי בָנֶיךָ אִתָּךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Tze min-hattevah attah ve'ishttekha uvanekha uneshei vanekha ittakh.
verses.study_note: 'Go forth' (tze — come out, exit) — the command that releases Noah from the ark into the new world. The same God who said 'Come into the ark' (7:1) now says 'Go forth.' The two commands bracket the entire Flood experience: God called in; God calls out. Salvation is not merely rescue from judgment — it is calling out into new creation and new purpose. 'Thou, and thy wife, and thy sons...' — the family unit is preserved and sent out together. The new world begins with family, as the original world began with a family (2:24).

verses.kjv_text: Bring forth with thee every living thing that is with thee, of all flesh, both of fowl, and of cattle, and of every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth; that they may breed abundantly in the earth, and be fruitful, and multiply upon the earth.
verses.original_text: כָּל הַחַיָּה אֲשֶׁר אִתְּךָ מִכָּל בָּשָׂר בָּעוֹף וּבַבְּהֵמָה וּבְכָל הָרֶמֶשׂ הָרֹמֵשׂ עַל הָאָרֶץ הוֹצֵא אִתָּךְ וְשָׁרְצוּ בָאָרֶץ וּפָרוּ וְרָבוּ עַל הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Kol hachayah asher ittekha mikkol basar ba'of uvabbehemah uvkhol haremes haromes al ha'aretz hotze ittekha vesheretu va'aretz ufaru verabu al ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: 'Be fruitful and multiply' (peru urebu) — the exact words of the creation blessing in Genesis 1:22 (to the animals) and 1:28 (to man). The new creation explicitly re-issues the original creation mandate. The Flood did not terminate God's original purpose for His creation; it purified the earth and started the mandate again. 'Breed abundantly' (sharatz — to swarm, to teem) — the word used for the swarming sea creatures on Day 5 (1:20). Creation's Day 5 energy is released again into the new post-Flood world.
verses.doctrinal_note: The creation mandate renewed: God does not issue new commands after the Flood — He renews the original ones: be fruitful, multiply, fill the earth. This demonstrates that the Flood was not an abandonment of creation but a renewal of it. God's purposes for His creation were not changed by the Flood; they were reconfirmed. The same mandate will be reissued to Abraham (17:6), to Israel, and ultimately fulfilled in the new creation (Rev 21-22).

verses.kjv_text: And Noah went forth, and his sons, and his wife, and his sons’ wives with him:
verses.original_text: וַיֵּצֵא נֹחַ וּבָנָיו וְאִשְׁתּוֹ וּנְשֵׁי בָנָיו אִתּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyetze Noach uvanav ve'ishto uneshei vanav itto.
verses.study_note: Noah went forth — immediately, completely, and exactly as commanded. Just as he entered by God's command (7:7), he exits by God's command (8:16). The family exits together, as they entered together. There is a beautiful structural symmetry in the ark narrative: God said 'come in' — Noah went in. God said 'go out' — Noah went out. His entire time in the ark was governed by divine command, not human initiative. This is the pattern of obedient faith from beginning to end.

verses.kjv_text: Every beast, every creeping thing, and every fowl, and whatsoever creepeth upon the earth, after their kinds, went forth out of the ark.
verses.original_text: כָּל הַחַיָּה כָּל הָרֶמֶשׂ וְכָל הָעוֹף כֹּל רוֹמֵשׂ עַל הָאָרֶץ לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתֵיהֶם יָצְאוּ מִן הַתֵּבָה׃
verses.transliteration: Kol hachayah kol haremes vekhol ha'of kol romes al ha'aretz lemishpechoteihem yatze'u min-hattevah.
verses.study_note: 'After their kinds' — but here the word is 'lemishpechoteihem' (according to their families/clans) rather than the 'lemineihem' (according to their kinds) of creation. The emphasis shifts from biological category to relational family — these are not just species but communities emerging into the new world. Every creature exits in its family groupings, ready to fill the earth. Creation Day 5 and 6 are re-enacted: the creatures go forth just as they were brought forth in creation, category by category, family by family.

verses.kjv_text: And Noah builded an altar unto the LORD; and took of every clean beast, and of every clean fowl, and offered burnt offerings on the altar.
verses.original_text: וַיִּבֶן נֹחַ מִזְבֵּחַ לַיהוָה וַיִּקַּח מִכֹּל הַבְּהֵמָה הַטְּהוֹרָה וּמִכֹּל הָעוֹף הַטָּהוֹר וַיַּעַל עֹלֹת בַּמִּזְבֵּחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiven Noach mizbeach laYahweh vayyiqqach mikkol habbehemah hattehorah umikkol ha'of hattaghor vayyaal olot bamizbeach.
verses.study_note: The first act of the new creation is worship. Before building a house, before planting a field, before any human enterprise — Noah builds an altar. This is the right ordering: God first, creation second. 'Mizbeach' (altar) — the first altar in Scripture, built by Noah. The same word will be used for Abraham's altars (12:7-8), Isaac's (26:25), Jacob's (35:7), and ultimately the tabernacle and temple altars. The 'olot' (burnt offerings — whole burnt offerings, entirely consumed by fire) were the most complete expression of devotion: the entire sacrifice goes up to God, nothing held back. Noah uses the extra clean animals that God had specifically preserved for this moment (7:2-3). God's provision for worship was built into the ark from the beginning.
verses.doctrinal_note: The first altar: Noah's altar is the biblical prototype of all worship — coming into God's presence through sacrifice, acknowledging that access to God requires atonement. The burnt offering (olah) represents total consecration — the entire animal consumed, nothing withheld. It anticipates Christ's total self-offering: 'who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without spot to God' (Heb 9:14). Noah's first act in the new world is the same as every believer's first act in the new creation: worship through the blood of the atoning sacrifice.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD smelled a sweet savour; and the LORD said in his heart, I will not again curse the ground any more for man’s sake; for the imagination of man’s heart is evil from his youth; neither will I again smite any more every thing living, as I have done.
verses.original_text: וַיָּרַח יְהוָה אֶת רֵיחַ הַנִּיחֹחַ וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל לִבּוֹ לֹא אֹסִף לְקַלֵּל עוֹד אֶת הָאֲדָמָה בַּעֲבוּר הָאָדָם כִּי יֵצֶר לֵב הָאָדָם רַע מִנְּעֻרָיו וְלֹא אֹסִף עוֹד לְהַכּוֹת אֶת כָּל חַי כַּאֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyarach Yahweh et reach hannichoach vayyomer Yahweh el libbo lo osif leqalel od et ha'adamah ba'avur ha'adam ki yetzer lev ha'adam ra minne'urav velo osif od lehakot et kol chai ka'asher asiti.
verses.study_note: 'The LORD smelled a sweet savour' (reach hannichoach — a soothing aroma) — God accepted Noah's sacrifice. This phrase recurs throughout the Levitical sacrificial system (Lev 1:9, 13, 17; 2:2; 3:5 etc.) — the smell of sacrifice was acceptable to God. Paul uses the same language of Christ's sacrifice: 'a sweet-smelling savour' (Eph 5:2). Then the most remarkable theological pivot: God says He will not curse the ground 'because' the imagination of man's heart is evil from youth. Before the Flood, the same evil heart was the reason for judgment (6:5). Now the same evil heart is the reason for grace. The logic has inverted: not because man has improved, but because God has found a way — through sacrifice — to accept fallen humanity without destroying creation again.
verses.doctrinal_note: Grace, not merit: The reason God will not curse the ground again is not that man has been improved by the Flood (8:21 confirms the heart is still evil). The reason is the sacrificial offering — God accepted the 'sweet savour' of the burnt offering. This is the gospel logic: judgment is not suspended because humanity has improved but because atonement has been made. The same heart condition that provoked the Flood (6:5) is acknowledged in 8:21 as unchanged — yet grace is extended. This is what makes it grace. The answer to human depravity is not human reform but divine atonement.

verses.kjv_text: While the earth remaineth, seedtime and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night shall not cease.
verses.original_text: עֹד כָּל יְמֵי הָאָרֶץ זֶרַע וְקָצִיר וְקֹר וָחֹם וְקַיִץ וָחֹרֶף וְיוֹם וָלַיְלָה לֹא יִשְׁבֹּתוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Od kol yemei ha'aretz zera veqatzir veqor vachom vaqayitz vachoref veyom valalyah lo yishbotu.
verses.study_note: The closing promise of the chapter — six pairs of natural rhythms guaranteed to continue as long as the earth exists. Seedtime and harvest (agriculture), cold and heat (temperature), summer and winter (seasons), day and night (time itself) — all will be uninterrupted. 'Shall not cease' (lo yishbotu — will not stop, will not sabbath, will not rest) — the word 'shavat' (to cease, to sabbath) is used: these rhythms will never take a sabbath rest, never be interrupted. God has built a reliable, stable creation order as the framework for human life and redemption. This verse is the foundation of what theologians call 'common grace' — God's sustaining of the natural order for the benefit of all humanity, believer and unbeliever alike (Matt 5:45).
verses.doctrinal_note: Common grace and creation order: God's promise to maintain seedtime, harvest, seasons, and day and night is the foundation of what we call common grace — God's goodness to all people, not just His covenant people. Paul preaches this in Acts 14:17: 'he left not himself without witness, in that he did good, and gave us rain from heaven, and fruitful seasons.' Jesus echoes it in Matthew 5:45: God 'sendeth rain on the just and on the unjust.' The stability of the natural order is a daily testimony to God's faithfulness — and an act of grace toward a world that does not deserve it.

meta.overview: Genesis 8 is the chapter of return — the great reversal after the great judgment. It opens with the most tender sentence in the Flood narrative: 'God remembered Noah.' From that moment of divine remembrance, everything reverses: the waters recede, the ark rests, the earth dries, and life resumes. The chapter follows Noah's patient waiting as he sends out first a raven, then a dove — three times — testing whether the earth is ready. When the dove returns with an olive leaf, and finally does not return at all, the new world is confirmed. God then commands Noah to exit the ark, and the first act of the new world is worship: Noah builds an altar and offers burnt offerings. God responds with a promise — never again will He curse the ground or destroy every living thing. Genesis 8 is the hinge of the Flood narrative: judgment has ended, new creation has begun, and the covenant relationship between God and redeemed humanity is about to be formally established (Genesis 9).
meta.theme: God Remembers — New Creation After Judgment, Worship Before Covenant
meta.keyWordMeaning: And God remembered — the opening act of redemption after judgment. Divine remembrance is not a recovery from forgetfulness but a decisive covenant act that initiates deliverance. The same word that begins Israel's rescue from Egypt (Ex 2:24).
outline: God Remembers Noah (v.1): The wind, the fountains stopped, the rain restrained
The Waters Recede (vv.2-5): 150 days to Ararat — the ark rests
Noah's Windows Opened (v.6): After 40 days Noah opens the window
The Raven Sent Out (v.7): Goes back and forth — restless judgment bird
The Dove — First Sending (vv.8-9): No rest for the sole of her foot — returns
The Dove — Second Sending (vv.10-11): Returns with an olive leaf — life signal
The Dove — Third Sending (v.12): Does not return — earth is ready
The Earth is Dry (vv.13-14): Noah removes the covering — he sees
God Commands: Go Forth (vv.15-17): Come out — be fruitful and multiply
Noah Worships (v.20): Burnt offerings from every clean animal
God's Promise (vv.21-22): Never again — seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, day and night

verses.kjv_text: And God blessed Noah and his sons, and said unto them, Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth.
verses.original_text: וַיְבָרֶךְ אֱלֹהִים אֶת נֹחַ וְאֶת בָּנָיו וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶם פְּרוּ וּרְבוּ וּמִלְאוּ אֶת הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayevarekh Elohim et Noach ve'et banav vayyomer lahem peru urevu umile'u et ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with the same blessing and mandate as Genesis 1:28 — 'Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth.' The words are verbatim: 'peru urevu umile'u et ha'aretz.' The Flood was not the end of God's creative purpose but a reset — the original commission stands. God blesses before He commands: the blessing (berakhah) precedes and empowers the mandate. Noah and his sons are the new Adam, standing at the threshold of a new world with the same calling as the first man. The difference is that they carry the knowledge of judgment — they have seen what sin produces and what grace costs.
verses.doctrinal_note: New creation — same mandate: The repetition of Genesis 1:28 in 9:1 is theologically deliberate. God does not issue a new purpose after the Flood; He reissues the original one. This establishes that the Flood was restorative, not merely punitive. The creation mandate — to fill and steward the earth — has never been revoked. It is renewed here, renewed at Sinai, renewed in the Great Commission (Matt 28:18-20 — 'Go... make disciples of all nations... fill the earth with worshippers').

verses.kjv_text: And the fear of you and the dread of you shall be upon every beast of the earth, and upon every fowl of the air, upon all that moveth upon the earth, and upon all the fishes of the sea; into your hand are they delivered.
verses.original_text: וּמוֹרַאֲכֶם וְחִתְּכֶם יִהְיֶה עַל כָּל חַיַּת הָאָרֶץ וְעַל כָּל עוֹף הַשָּׁמָיִם בְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר תִּרְמֹשׂ הָאֲדָמָה וּבְכָל דְּגֵי הַיָּם בְּיֶדְכֶם נִתָּנוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Umorakhem vechitkhem yihyeh al kol chayyat ha'aretz ve'al kol of hashamayim bekhol asher tirmos ha'adamah uvkhol degei hayyam beyadkhem nittanu.
verses.study_note: A new element in the post-Flood creation order: the fear and dread of man is placed upon animals. In the original creation, man and animals coexisted without this terror — Adam named the animals without fear (2:19-20). The Fall damaged the harmony; the Flood finalises the new arrangement. 'Fear' (mora) and 'dread' (chitta) — two words for instinctive terror. 'Into your hand are they delivered' (beyadkhem nittanu — given into your hand) — the same language of divine gift used in warfare when God gives enemies into Israel's hand. The animal kingdom is now under human authority in a way that includes natural fear, reflecting the broken relationship between humanity and creation caused by sin.

verses.kjv_text: Every moving thing that liveth shall be meat for you; even as the green herb have I given you all things.
verses.original_text: כָּל רֶמֶשׂ אֲשֶׁר הוּא חַי לָכֶם יִהְיֶה לְאָכְלָה כְּיֶרֶק עֵשֶׂב נָתַתִּי לָכֶם אֶת כֹּל׃
verses.transliteration: Kol remes asher hu chai lakhem yihyeh le'okhlah keyereq esev natatti lakhem et kol.
verses.study_note: A significant expansion of the original diet. In Genesis 1:29-30, God gave plants and fruit for food — no mention of animals. Now, post-Flood, God explicitly grants permission to eat 'every moving thing that liveth.' 'As the green herb' — the same phrase as 1:30, equating meat with the earlier plant-permission. The reasons for this expansion are not stated but may include: the changed post-Flood earth (less fertile initially), the increased human responsibility to manage animal populations, and a pointing toward the sacrificial system where flesh would be consumed in covenant meals. The clean/unclean distinctions will later qualify this permission (Lev 11), and Christ will ultimately declare all foods clean (Mark 7:19).
verses.doctrinal_note: Diet and redemption: The progression of biblical diet — plants only (Gen 1-2) → meat permitted (Gen 9) → clean/unclean distinctions (Lev 11) → all foods declared clean (Mark 7:19; Acts 10) — is not arbitrary but tracks the arc of redemption. The clean/unclean food laws were a sign of covenant separation for Israel; their removal in the NT signals the breaking down of the Jew/Gentile wall (Eph 2:14-15). The original diet will be restored in the new creation (Rev 22:2 — the tree of life for the healing of the nations).

verses.kjv_text: But flesh with the life thereof, which is the blood thereof, shall ye not eat.
verses.original_text: אַךְ בָּשָׂר בְּנַפְשׁוֹ דָמוֹ לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Akh basar benaphsho damo lo tokhelu.
verses.study_note: The one restriction on the newly permitted diet: blood must not be eaten. 'Flesh with the life thereof, which is the blood' — the blood is identified as the seat of life (nephesh — soul, life-principle). 'The blood is the life' (Deut 12:23). This prohibition precedes the Mosaic Law (it is given here to all humanity through Noah) and persists into the New Testament (Acts 15:20, 29 — the Jerusalem Council reaffirmed it for Gentile believers). The reason: blood belongs to God because life belongs to God. Eating blood would be consuming the God-owned life-principle. This also grounds the entire Levitical sacrificial system — the blood of the sacrifice is what makes atonement: 'for it is the blood that maketh an atonement for the soul' (Lev 17:11).
verses.doctrinal_note: The sanctity of blood: The blood prohibition of Gen 9:4 is not merely a dietary rule but a theological statement: life (nephesh) belongs to God, not man. Blood is the physical carrier of life — and since life is God's property, blood cannot be consumed as food. This principle underlies all of the sacrificial theology: when blood is shed in sacrifice, life is given to God in place of the guilty life. The ultimate fulfillment: the blood of Christ shed for the remission of sins — His life given to God as the supreme sacrifice (Heb 9:22).

verses.kjv_text: And surely your blood of your lives will I require; at the hand of every beast will I require it, and at the hand of man; at the hand of every man’s brother will I require the life of man.
verses.original_text: וְאַךְ אֶת דִּמְכֶם לְנַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם אֶדְרֹשׁ מִיַּד כָּל חַיָּה אֶדְרְשֶׁנּוּ וּמִיַּד הָאָדָם מִיַּד אִישׁ אָחִיו אֶדְרֹשׁ אֶת נֶפֶשׁ הָאָדָם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'akh et dimkhem lenaphshoteikhem edrosh miyyad kol chayah edreshennu umiyyad ha'adam miyyad ish achiv edrosh et nephesh ha'adam.
verses.study_note: God Himself will 'require' (darash — seek, demand, hold accountable) the blood of human life — from animals that kill humans, and from humans who kill humans. 'I will require' (edrosh) — three times in this verse, the triple emphasis insisting on divine accountability for human blood. 'At the hand of every man's brother' (miyyad ish achiv) — the word 'brother' (ach) echoes Genesis 4 where Cain shed Abel's blood. God required Abel's blood from Cain (4:10); now He establishes a universal principle: all human bloodshed will be required. Human life has infinite accountability because it bears the divine image.

verses.kjv_text: Whoso sheddeth man’s blood, by man shall his blood be shed: for in the image of God made he man.
verses.original_text: שֹׁפֵךְ דַּם הָאָדָם בָּאָדָם דָּמוֹ יִשָּׁפֵךְ כִּי בְּצֶלֶם אֱלֹהִים עָשָׂה אֶת הָאָדָם׃
verses.transliteration: Shofekh dam ha'adam ba'adam damo yishshaphekh ki betzelem Elohim asah et ha'adam.
verses.study_note: The most carefully crafted sentence in the chapter — a perfect chiasm in Hebrew: 'Shedder / blood / of man / by man / his blood / shall be shed.' The syntax itself mirrors the principle of proportional justice: the sentence is structured in reverse symmetry (A-B-C-C-B-A). The ground for capital justice is explicitly stated: 'for in the image of God made He man' (betzelem Elohim asah et ha'adam). This is the Imago Dei from Genesis 1:26-27, now applied as the foundation of criminal justice. To murder a human being is not merely a social crime — it is an attack on God's image. The death penalty for murder is the most ancient law in Scripture, preceding Moses by over a thousand years, given to all humanity through Noah.
verses.doctrinal_note: Capital justice and the Imago Dei: Genesis 9:6 is the biblical foundation for capital punishment. The logic is irreversible: (1) Man is made in God's image (1:26-27), (2) Therefore, murdering a human is the gravest offense against God, (3) Therefore, it demands the ultimate judicial response. This is not vengeance but proportional justice rooted in human dignity. Note that this law is given to all humanity (not just Israel), through the Noahic covenant — it is a universal moral principle, not merely a Mosaic regulation. It undergirds Paul's statement in Romans 13:4 that the governing authority 'beareth not the sword in vain' — civil government has God-given authority to execute justice on murderers.

verses.kjv_text: And you, be ye fruitful, and multiply; bring forth abundantly in the earth, and multiply therein.
verses.original_text: וְאַתֶּם פְּרוּ וּרְבוּ שִׁרְצוּ בָאָרֶץ וּרְבוּ בָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attem peru urevu shirzu va'aretz urevu vah.
verses.study_note: The creation mandate (v.1) is reaffirmed immediately after the gravity of the Imago Dei and capital justice. The bracketing is intentional: human dignity (vv.5-6) is surrounded by human fruitfulness (vv.1, 7). God's purpose is not merely to punish murder but to fill the earth with image-bearers. 'Bring forth abundantly' (shirzu — to swarm, teem) — the word used of the sea creatures on Day 5 (1:20) and of Israel's fruitfulness in Egypt (Ex 1:7). The new humanity is called to multiply and fill — to populate the new earth with the image of God.

verses.kjv_text: And God spake unto Noah, and to his sons with him, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים אֶל נֹחַ וְאֶל בָּנָיו אִתּוֹ לֵאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Elohim el Noach ve'el banav itto lemor.
verses.study_note: God now formally addresses Noah and his sons together to establish the covenant. The address is inclusive — Noah and his sons, not Noah alone. The covenant that follows will encompass all humanity descended from these men. 'Saying' (lemor) — the word that introduces a formal declaration. What follows is not mere instruction but a covenant pronouncement — God's sworn commitment to creation.

verses.kjv_text: And I, behold, I establish my covenant with you, and with your seed after you;
verses.original_text: וַאֲנִי הִנְנִי מֵקִים אֶת בְּרִיתִי אִתְּכֶם וְאֶת זַרְעֲכֶם אַחֲרֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Va'ani hineni meqim et beriti ittekhem ve'et zar'akhem acharekhem.
verses.study_note: 'I, behold, I' (va'ani hineni) — the emphatic double first-person, exactly as in 6:17 when God announced the Flood. The same emphasis that announced judgment now announces covenant. God's self-emphasis is the guarantee: this covenant rests on His character alone, not on human faithfulness. 'I establish' (meqim — causing to stand, confirming) — the covenant is not proposed but established, set on an unshakeable foundation. 'With you, and with your seed after you' — the covenant is multigenerational, extending from Noah to all his descendants — which means all of humanity.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Noahic Covenant — the most universal covenant: The Noahic Covenant is unique among biblical covenants in its scope — it encompasses all of Noah's descendants (all humanity), all living creatures (v.10), and the earth itself (v.11). It is the only covenant where God explicitly says 'every living creature' (vv.10, 12, 15, 16, 17). It is unconditional — God does not require Noah to do anything to maintain it. It is eternal ('everlasting covenant,' v.16). It is the foundation of all subsequent covenants — the platform of stable creation on which redemptive history is conducted.

verses.kjv_text: And with every living creature that is with you, of the fowl, of the cattle, and of every beast of the earth with you; from all that go out of the ark, to every beast of the earth.
verses.original_text: וְאֵת כָּל נֶפֶשׁ הַחַיָּה אֲשֶׁר אִתְּכֶם בָּעוֹף בַּבְּהֵמָה וּבְכָל חַיַּת הָאָרֶץ אִתְּכֶם מִכֹּל יֹצְאֵי הַתֵּבָה לְכֹל חַיַּת הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et kol nephesh hachayah asher ittekhem ba'of babbehemah uvkhol chayyat ha'aretz ittekhem mikkol yotze'ei hattevah lekhol chayyat ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The covenant partners are explicitly named: not only human beings but 'every living creature' (kol nephesh hachayah). God's covenant commitment extends to the animals — every bird, every domestic animal, every wild creature. This is a remarkable statement: God enters into covenant relationship with non-rational creatures. This does not mean animals are morally responsible covenant parties but that God's committed care encompasses all of creation. Paul's statement in Romans 8:21 that 'creation itself also shall be delivered from the bondage of corruption' is rooted in this Noahic commitment.

verses.kjv_text: And I will establish my covenant with you; neither shall all flesh be cut off any more by the waters of a flood; neither shall there any more be a flood to destroy the earth.
verses.original_text: וַהֲקִמֹתִי אֶת בְּרִיתִי אִתְּכֶם וְלֹא יִכָּרֵת כָּל בָּשָׂר עוֹד מִמֵּי הַמַּבּוּל וְלֹא יִהְיֶה עוֹד מַבּוּל לְשַׁחֵת הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vahaqimoti et beriti ittekhem velo yikharet kol basar od mimmei hammabbul velo yihyeh od mabbul leshachet ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The content of the covenant stated explicitly: 'Never again' (lo... od — not... anymore) — the phrase appears twice, with unmistakable finality. Never again will all flesh be cut off by flood waters. Never again will a flood destroy the earth. This is a unilateral, unconditional divine commitment — God binds Himself without condition. He does not say 'as long as you obey' but simply 'never again.' The Flood was unique and unrepeatable.
