Isaiah builds on this: 'For as I have sworn that the waters of Noah should no more go over the earth; so have I sworn that I would not be wroth with thee' (Isa 54:9) — the Noahic covenant becomes a basis for understanding God's covenant faithfulness to Israel and to us.
verses.doctrinal_note: Never again: The double 'lo... od' (never again) is God's sworn self-commitment. God does not need to repeat the Flood because the cross accomplishes what the Flood pointed toward: the final and complete judgment of sin in the body of Christ (Rom 8:3). The Flood was a type; the cross is the antitype. God can say 'never again' to the Flood because 'it is finished' (John 19:30) — the full weight of divine judgment has been poured out once for all.

verses.kjv_text: And God said, This is the token of the covenant which I make between me and you and every living creature that is with you, for perpetual generations:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים זֹאת אוֹת הַבְּרִית אֲשֶׁר אֲנִי נֹתֵן בֵּינִי וּבֵינֵיכֶם וּבֵין כָּל נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה אֲשֶׁר אִתְּכֶם לְדֹרֹת עוֹלָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Elohim zot ot habberit asher ani noten beini uveneikhem uvein kol nephesh chayah asher ittekhem ledorot olam.
verses.study_note: God announces a 'token' (ot — sign) of the covenant. Every covenant in Scripture has a sign: the Noahic covenant has the rainbow (vv.13-17), the Abrahamic covenant has circumcision (17:11), the Mosaic covenant has the Sabbath (Ex 31:13), the New Covenant has baptism and the Lord's Supper. The sign is not the covenant itself but its visible, tangible reminder — a physical anchor for covenant faith. 'For perpetual generations' (ledorot olam — for generations of eternity) — the sign is not temporary but permanent. Every rainbow throughout all history is a covenant sign.

verses.kjv_text: I do set my bow in the cloud, and it shall be for a token of a covenant between me and the earth.
verses.original_text: אֶת קַשְׁתִּי נָתַתִּי בֶּעָנָן וְהָיְתָה לְאוֹת בְּרִית בֵּינִי וּבֵין הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Et qashthi natatti be'anan vehayetah le'ot berit beini uvein ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: 'My bow' (qashthi — my bow, the possessive is God's) — the Hebrew 'qeshet' is the word for a warrior's bow, the weapon of battle and judgment. God sets His own war-bow in the clouds — the weapon of divine wrath is hung up, pointed skyward, no longer aimed at the earth. This is a stunning image: the God who could unleash cosmic judgment has placed His weapon in a position of peace. The rainbow is not merely a meteorological phenomenon but a disarmed war-bow — the sign that God's wrath toward the earth has been laid aside. Interestingly, in Revelation 4:3 and 10:1, the rainbow appears around the throne of God — the same bow, now a permanent feature of heaven's throne room.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's bow hung up: The warrior's bow laid down is an ancient Near Eastern symbol of peace after victory. God's war-bow hung in the cloud signals that the war against the earth is over. This is the most dramatic act of divine disarmament in Scripture. The bow will appear again in heaven (Rev 4:3; 10:1) — around the throne, encircling the Lamb who was slain — the covenant sign eternally commemorating that God's judgment was poured out on Christ, not on creation. The rainbow around the throne is the Noahic Covenant fulfilled.

verses.kjv_text: And it shall come to pass, when I bring a cloud over the earth, that the bow shall be seen in the cloud:
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה בְּעַנְנִי עָנָן עַל הָאָרֶץ וְנִרְאֲתָה הַקֶּשֶׁת בֶּעָנָן׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah be'anni anan al ha'aretz venire'atah haqeshet be'anan.
verses.study_note: The rainbow appears specifically when clouds gather — when the conditions for rain (and potentially another flood) are present. It is precisely in the moment of potential threat that the covenant sign appears. The rainbow does not appear on clear days but in storm clouds — it is seen against the very backdrop that might provoke fear. This is the genius of the sign: when dark clouds gather and the earth might tremble at the prospect of judgment, the bow appears to reassure. Covenant signs are given for the moments of greatest vulnerability, not the moments of ease.

verses.kjv_text: And I will remember my covenant, which is between me and you and every living creature of all flesh; and the waters shall no more become a flood to destroy all flesh.
verses.original_text: וְזָכַרְתִּי אֶת בְּרִיתִי אֲשֶׁר בֵּינִי וּבֵינֵיכֶם וּבֵין כָּל נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה בְּכָל בָּשָׂר וְלֹא יִהְיֶה עוֹד הַמַּיִם לְמַבּוּל לְשַׁחֵת כָּל בָּשָׂר׃
verses.transliteration: Vezakharthi et beriti asher beini uveneikhem uvein kol nephesh chayah bekhol basar velo yihyeh od hammayim lemabbul leshachet kol basar.
verses.study_note: 'I will remember my covenant' (vezakharthi et beriti) — the same word 'zakar' (remember) that opened chapter 8 ('God remembered Noah'). The rainbow is the trigger of divine remembrance — not that God forgets between rainbows, but that the rainbow is the appointed occasion of God's active covenant attention. When God 'sees' the bow (v.16), He 'remembers' — He actively reaffirms His covenant commitment. The covenant sign is not for God's benefit (He cannot forget) but for humanity's — a visible anchor of assurance. But God chooses to say He will 'remember' when He sees it, taking the covenant sign seriously as His own appointed reminder.

verses.kjv_text: And the bow shall be in the cloud; and I will look upon it, that I may remember the everlasting covenant between God and every living creature of all flesh that is upon the earth.
verses.original_text: וְהָיְתָה הַקֶּשֶׁת בֶּעָנָן וּרְאִיתִיהָ לִזְכֹּר בְּרִית עוֹלָם בֵּין אֱלֹהִים וּבֵין כָּל נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה בְּכָל בָּשָׂר אֲשֶׁר עַל הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayetah haqeshet be'anan ure'itiha lizkor berit olam bein Elohim uvein kol nephesh chayah bekhol basar asher al ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: 'I will look upon it' (ure'itiha — and I will see it) — God sees His own bow and remembers. 'The everlasting covenant' (berit olam) — the first use of 'olam' (everlasting, eternal) with 'berit' (covenant) in Scripture. The Noahic Covenant is explicitly eternal — it does not expire, cannot be broken, and has no termination clause. The same term 'berit olam' will be used of the Abrahamic covenant (17:7, 13, 19), the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 23:5), and the New Covenant (Isa 55:3; Jer 32:40; Heb 13:20). The 'everlasting covenant' is the backbone of all redemptive history.
verses.doctrinal_note: Berit olam — the everlasting covenant: The Noahic Covenant is the first 'berit olam' in Scripture — and it is with all of creation. Every subsequent covenant in redemptive history is built on this foundation of a stable, sustained creation order. The New Covenant is the ultimate 'berit olam' (Heb 13:20 — 'the everlasting covenant' secured by the blood of Christ), which fulfills and surpasses all previous covenants while the Noahic continues to guarantee creation's stability until the final new creation (Rev 21:1).

verses.kjv_text: And God said unto Noah, This is the token of the covenant, which I have established between me and all flesh that is upon the earth.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים אֶל נֹחַ זֹאת אוֹת הַבְּרִית אֲשֶׁר הֲקִמֹתִי בֵּינִי וּבֵין כָּל בָּשָׂר אֲשֶׁר עַל הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Elohim el Noach zot ot habberit asher haqimoti beini uvein kol basar asher al ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The covenant section closes (vv.8-17) with God addressing Noah directly and summarising: 'This is the token of the covenant.' The repetition throughout this passage ('covenant' appears seven times in vv.8-17) is deliberate — seven times, the number of completeness, sealing the completeness of the commitment. The rainbow is confirmed as the permanent sign. Every person who has ever looked at a rainbow has looked at a covenant sign — God's sworn, eternal commitment never to destroy the earth by flood. The world may not know it, but the sign remains.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Noah, that went forth of the ark, were Shem, and Ham, and Japheth: and Ham is the father of Canaan.
verses.original_text: וַיִּהְיוּ בְנֵי נֹחַ הַיֹּצְאִים מִן הַתֵּבָה שֵׁם וְחָם וָיָפֶת וְחָם הוּא אֲבִי כְנָעַן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyihyu venei Noach hayyotze'im min-hattevah Shem veCham vaYafet vecHam hu avi Khena'an.
verses.study_note: The narrative transition from covenant to the Ham episode. The aside 'Ham is the father of Canaan' is deliberately inserted here — before the story is told — as a signal to the reader that what follows will have implications for Canaan specifically. The Canaanites were the people Israel would eventually displace in the Promised Land. The reader of Genesis in Moses' day would immediately understand why this genealogical detail is planted here. The narrative is not only historical but prophetically shaped: what happens in the tent will explain why Canaan is cursed rather than Ham directly.

verses.kjv_text: These are the three sons of Noah: and of them was the whole earth overspread.
verses.original_text: שְׁלֹשָׁה אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי נֹחַ וּמֵאֵלֶּה נָפְצָה כָל הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Sheloshah elleh venei Noach ume'elleh naftzah khol ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: 'Of them was the whole earth overspread' (naftzah — was scattered, spread, dispersed) — every nation on earth traces its ancestry to these three men. This verse is the theological bridge to Genesis 10 (the Table of Nations) and ultimately to Paul's statement in Acts 17:26: 'God hath made of one blood all nations of men.' The unity of humanity is grounded in Noah's three sons. Every ethnic group, every nation, every culture on earth shares a common ancestor in Noah and ultimately in Adam.

verses.kjv_text: And Noah began to be an husbandman, and he planted a vineyard:
verses.original_text: וַיָּחֶל נֹחַ אִישׁ הָאֲדָמָה וַיִּטַּע כָּרֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyachel Noach ish ha'adamah vayyitta karem.
verses.study_note: The post-Flood narrative takes a sobering turn. 'Noah began to be a husbandman' (ish ha'adamah — a man of the ground/soil) — echoing Adam who was taken from the ground (adamah) and put to work it (2:15). Noah plants a vineyard — wine-making is not sinful in itself (Ps 104:15 celebrates wine as God's gift to gladden the heart). The text does not condemn the vineyard. The problem comes in the next verse. This is the first mention of viticulture in Scripture — and it is immediately followed by the first mention of drunkenness.

verses.kjv_text: And he drank of the wine, and was drunken; and he was uncovered within his tent.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּשְׁתְּ מִן הַיַּיִן וַיִּשְׁכָּר וַיִּתְגַּל בְּתוֹךְ אָהֳלֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyesht min hayyayin vayyishkar vayyitgal betokh oholoh.
verses.study_note: The man who survived the Flood, who walked with God, who built the first altar — now stumbles. 'Drunken' (shikkar — intoxicated, drunk) and 'uncovered' (yitgal — stripped bare, exposed). The same Noah who was described as 'righteous, blameless, walked with God' (6:9) is here found drunk and naked in his tent. This is not a contradiction but a sober biblical realism: the greatest saints are still fallen. The same sin-pattern of the garden (nakedness and shame, Gen 3:7) re-emerges in the new world. The Flood purged the earth but not human nature — as 8:21 warned ('the imagination of man's heart is evil from his youth').
verses.doctrinal_note: The failure of the best: Noah's drunkenness is a warning: no human being — however righteous, however used by God — is immune to sin. The best of men are men at best. This is why the biblical narrative does not sanitise its heroes. Abraham lied, Moses struck the rock, David committed adultery and murder, Peter denied Christ. The point is not to excuse sin but to insist that salvation cannot rest on human faithfulness. Only One has walked through this world without stumbling.

verses.kjv_text: And Ham, the father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father, and told his two brethren without.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא חָם אֲבִי כְנַעַן אֵת עֶרְוַת אָבִיו וַיַּגֵּד לִשְׁנֵי אֶחָיו בַּחוּץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyar Cham avi Khena'an et ervat aviv vayyagged lishnei echav bachutz.
verses.study_note: Ham 'saw' (vayyar — looked at, gazed upon) and 'told' (vayyagged — reported, announced). The sin is not merely accidental viewing but a deliberate gaze followed by gleeful reporting. The repeated identification 'Ham, the father of Canaan' signals again the future implications. 'Saw the nakedness of his father' (ervat aviv) — a phrase with possible deeper significance: in Leviticus 18, 'uncovering nakedness' (ervah) is the euphemism for sexual violation within the family. Whether physical or attitudinal, Ham's act was a fundamental violation of filial honour — mocking rather than covering his father's vulnerability. Telling his brothers 'outside' suggests he made it a public matter, exposing his father's shame rather than quietly covering it.
verses.doctrinal_note: Honouring parents: Ham's sin is a violation of the 5th commandment's principle (Ex 20:12) before it was given at Sinai — honour your father and mother. The covenant community's health depends on proper honouring of authority and generational dignity. Ham's mockery of his father's vulnerability is the inversion of what his brothers will do in the next verse. The same situation reveals two completely opposite responses: exposure versus covering.

verses.kjv_text: And Shem and Japheth took a garment, and laid it upon both their shoulders, and went backward, and covered the nakedness of their father; and their faces were backward, and they saw not their father’s nakedness.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח שֵׁם וָיֶפֶת אֶת הַשִּׂמְלָה וַיָּשִׂימוּ עַל שְׁכֶם שְׁנֵיהֶם וַיֵּלְכוּ אֲחֹרַנִּית וַיְכַסּוּ אֵת עֶרְוַת אֲבִיהֶם וּפְנֵיהֶם אֲחֹרַנִּית וְעֶרְוַת אֲבִיהֶם לֹא רָאוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach Shem vaYafet et hassimlah vayyasimu al shekhem sheneihem vayyelechu achoranit vaykhasshu et ervat avihem ufneihem achoranit ve'ervat avihem lo ra'u.
verses.study_note: The most beautiful verse in the episode — a tableau of filial honour. Shem and Japheth took a garment (simlah — an outer robe), laid it on both their shoulders together, walked backward, and covered their father's nakedness — their faces turned away the whole time, never looking. The detail of walking backward and keeping their faces averted is the text's way of emphasising that they saw nothing. Every detail of this verse exudes deliberate, careful, costly honour: (1) they acted together, (2) they bore the garment jointly on both shoulders, (3) they moved backward (awkward, slower, more difficult), (4) they averted their gaze entirely. This is the love that 'covereth all sins' (Prov 10:12) and the grace that 'covereth a multitude of sins' (1 Pet 4:8).
verses.doctrinal_note: Covering as grace: Shem and Japheth's backward walk of covering is a picture of what God did for Adam and Eve (3:21 — 'God made coats of skins and clothed them') and what Christ does for the believer — covering our shame with His righteousness. 'Love covereth a multitude of sins' (1 Pet 4:8). The gospel is fundamentally a covering: Christ bears our shame and clothes us with His righteousness (Isa 61:10 — 'he hath clothed me with the garments of salvation, he hath covered me with the robe of righteousness').

verses.kjv_text: And Noah awoke from his wine, and knew what his younger son had done unto him.
verses.original_text: וַיִּיקֶץ נֹחַ מִיֵּינוֹ וַיֵּדַע אֵת אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה לוֹ בְּנוֹ הַקָּטָן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqetz Noach miyveno vayyeda et asher asah lo beno haqqatan.
verses.study_note: 'His younger son' (beno haqqatan) — Ham is identified as the youngest of Noah's three sons. 'Knew what his younger son had done to him' — how Noah knew is not specified. He may have been told by Shem and Japheth, or perceived it by other means. 'Done to him' (asah lo) — there is a weight to this phrase; something was done to Noah, not merely seen. Whatever the full nature of Ham's act, it was experienced by Noah as a violation that warranted a prophetic response. Noah's awakening from wine is a small picture of the awakening that sin eventually brings — the fog clears, and the consequences must be faced.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Cursed be Canaan; a servant of servants shall he be unto his brethren.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אָרוּר כְּנָעַן עֶבֶד עֲבָדִים יִהְיֶה לְאֶחָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer arur Khena'an eved avadim yihyeh le'echav.
verses.study_note: Noah's curse falls on Canaan, not on Ham. This has puzzled readers throughout history. The most coherent explanation is that the curse falls on the son who most embodies or perpetuates Ham's spirit — and the Canaanites became precisely the most egregiously corrupt people in the ancient Near East (Lev 18:24-25 — their sexual immorality and child sacrifice filled the land with moral filth). 'A servant of servants' (eved avadim) — the superlative in Hebrew: the most abject servant, the lowest of slaves. This was fulfilled when Joshua conquered Canaan and the Gibeonites (Canaanites) became 'hewers of wood and drawers of water' to Israel (Josh 9:21-27). This verse has been grotesquely misused to justify African slavery — a profound misreading, as Canaan's descendants were not Africans but the inhabitants of the land of Canaan (modern Israel/Palestine region).
verses.doctrinal_note: The curse of Canaan: The prophetic oracle of vv.25-27 is not a license for racial oppression but a prediction of Canaan's moral and political future. It was fulfilled historically in Israel's conquest of Canaan under Joshua. The 'servant of servants' language points to political subjugation. This oracle has been wickedly misapplied to justify slavery of African peoples — a historical travesty with no textual basis (Ham's son Cush, not Canaan, is the ancestor of African peoples). Scripture never uses this text to justify slavery and consistently condemns man-stealing (Ex 21:16; 1 Tim 1:10).

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Blessed be the LORD God of Shem; and Canaan shall be his servant.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר בָּרוּךְ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי שֵׁם וִיהִי כְנַעַן עֶבֶד לָמוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer barukh Yahweh Elohei Shem viyhi Khena'an eved lamo.
verses.study_note: 'Blessed be the LORD God of Shem' — remarkably, Noah does not say 'blessed be Shem' but 'blessed be the LORD God of Shem.' The blessing is on God's relationship with Shem, not on Shem personally. This is profoundly significant: Shem is blessed because YHWH (the covenant name!) is his God. The use of the covenant name 'YHWH' here — connected to Shem — is the first indication that the Messianic line runs through Shem. The Semitic peoples (Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Israel, David, Christ) are all descendants of Shem. God being called 'the God of Shem' anticipates 'the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob' — the same covenant God of the Messianic line.
verses.doctrinal_note: Shem and the Messianic line: 'The LORD God of Shem' is the prophetic identification of the Semitic line as the covenant lineage through which YHWH will work in history. From Shem comes: Eber ('Hebrew' — Gen 10:21), Abraham (11:10-26), Isaac, Jacob, the twelve tribes, David, and Jesus of Nazareth (Luke 3:36 — 'which was the son of Shem, which was the son of Noah'). The entire Messianic genealogy flows from this blessing. When Jesus declares 'salvation is of the Jews' (John 4:22), He is the ultimate fulfillment of 'blessed be the LORD God of Shem.'

verses.kjv_text: God shall enlarge Japheth, and he shall dwell in the tents of Shem; and Canaan shall be his servant.
verses.original_text: יַפְתְּ אֱלֹהִים לְיֶפֶת וְיִשְׁכֹּן בְּאָהֳלֵי שֵׁם וִיהִי כְנַעַן עֶבֶד לָמוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Yaft Elohim leYafet veyishkon be'oholei Shem viyhi Khena'an eved lamo.
verses.study_note: A remarkable wordplay: 'God shall enlarge Japheth' — 'yaft' is the verb form of 'Japheth' (yafet). His name means expansion, and his destiny is expansion. The Indo-European peoples (Greeks, Romans, Persians, Europeans) are Japheth's descendants — historically the peoples who have numerically and geographically dominated the world. 'He shall dwell in the tents of Shem' — Japheth will inhabit Shem's spiritual territory, sharing in Shem's covenant blessings. This is widely understood as a prophecy of the Gentile inclusion in the gospel — the nations (Japheth) coming to share in the covenant blessings of Israel (Shem). Paul's entire ministry is the fulfillment of this: 'that the Gentiles should be fellow heirs' (Eph 3:6).
verses.doctrinal_note: Japheth in Shem's tents — the Gentile mission: 'He shall dwell in the tents of Shem' is the first prophecy of Gentile inclusion in covenant blessing. The tents of Shem are the covenant community — Israel, the church, the household of faith. When Gentiles come to faith through the gospel, they enter Shem's tents. Paul in Romans 11:17 uses the metaphor of grafting wild olive branches (Gentiles) into the cultivated olive tree (Israel). Ephesians 2:12-13: Gentiles who were 'strangers from the covenants of promise' are now brought near. The Great Commission (Matt 28:19) is the execution of this oracle: go to all nations (Japheth) and bring them into the tents of Shem.

verses.kjv_text: And Noah lived after the flood three hundred and fifty years.
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי נֹחַ אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל שְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַחֲמִשִּׁים שָׁנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Noach achar hammabbul shlosh meot shanah vachamishim shanah.
verses.study_note: Noah lived 350 years after the Flood — longer after the judgment than most men lived their entire lives. He was 600 when the Flood came (7:6) and 950 at death (v.29). These 350 post-Flood years included the dispersion of nations (Gen 10), the building of Babel (Gen 11), and the early life of Abraham — chronologically Noah was still alive when Abraham was born. The man who survived the Flood lived to see humanity begin again, multiply, and already begin to repeat its pre-Flood patterns in the tower of Babel. He saw the whole cycle begin to turn again.

verses.kjv_text: And all the days of Noah were nine hundred and fifty years: and he died.
verses.original_text: וַיִּהְיוּ כָּל יְמֵי נֹחַ תְּשַׁע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַחֲמִשִּׁים שָׁנָה וַיָּמֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyihyu kol yemei Noach tesha meot shanah vachamishim shanah vayyamot.
verses.study_note: 950 years — and he died. The man whose name meant 'rest' and 'comfort,' who walked with God, who alone found grace in the eyes of the LORD, who survived the Flood, who built the first altar, who received the first rainbow covenant — 'and he died.' The familiar refrain of Genesis 5 returns. Even Noah, the greatest man of his age, could not escape the mortality that sin introduced in Eden. 950 years of life — but still death came. The only One who could abolish death had not yet come. The genealogy's pattern holds until the resurrection morning. 'The last enemy that shall be destroyed is death' (1 Cor 15:26) — not by Noah, but by the Seed of the woman, the Son of Shem, the Lord Jesus Christ.

meta.overview: Genesis 9 is the chapter of covenant and fall — simultaneously the highest point of the post-Flood world and the beginning of its next descent. God formally establishes the Noahic Covenant with Noah, his family, and every living creature — the most universal covenant in Scripture, covering all of creation. God renews the creation mandate (be fruitful, multiply), grants permission to eat meat, establishes the sanctity of human life grounded in the Imago Dei, and sets His bow in the clouds as the sign of the covenant. These verses (1-17) are among the most foundational in all of Scripture for understanding human dignity, capital justice, and God's commitment to the natural order. Then the chapter pivots to a deeply troubling episode: Noah drinks wine, becomes drunk, and lies uncovered in his tent. Ham sees his father's nakedness and tells his brothers. Shem and Japheth cover their father with honour. When Noah awakes and understands what happened, he pronounces a curse on Canaan (Ham's son) and blessings on Shem and Japheth — a prophetic oracle that will shape the entire subsequent narrative of Genesis and the history of Israel.
meta.theme: The Covenant with All Creation — Human Dignity, the Rainbow, and the First Prophecy of Nations
meta.keyWordMeaning: Bow — the rainbow set in the clouds as the sign of the Noahic Covenant. The same word for a warrior's bow — God has hung up His war-bow, a sign that His wrath toward the earth has been laid aside.
outline: The Renewed Mandate (vv.1-3): Be fruitful — meat permitted — fear of man on animals
The Sanctity of Human Life (vv.4-6): Blood forbidden — Imago Dei grounds capital justice
The Noahic Covenant Established (vv.7-11): I establish my covenant — never again
The Rainbow Sign (vv.12-17): My bow in the cloud — token of the covenant
Noah's Vineyard and Drunkenness (vv.18-21): He planted — he drank — he was uncovered
Ham's Dishonour and the Brothers' Honour (vv.22-23): Ham told — Shem and Japheth covered
Noah Awakes and Pronounces (vv.24-27): Cursed be Canaan — blessed be Shem — God enlarge Japheth
Noah's Final Years and Death (vv.28-29): 950 years — and he died

verses.kjv_text: Now these are the generations of the sons of Noah, Shem, Ham, and Japheth: and unto them were sons born after the flood.
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת בְּנֵי־נֹחַ שֵׁם חָם וָיָפֶת וַיִּוָּלְדוּ לָהֶם בָּנִים אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'eleh toledot benei-Noach Shem Cham vaYafet vayyivvaledu lahem banim achar hammabbul.
verses.study_note: The fourth 'toledot' section in Genesis. The sons are listed Shem, Ham, Japheth — but the account proceeds in reverse: Japheth first (briefest), Ham second (most detailed, due to Nimrod and Canaan), Shem last (the covenant line, given the place of honor). 'After the flood' — all nations descend from one family, fulfilling God's renewed mandate (9:1).
verses.doctrinal_note: The unity of the human race: every nation, tribe, and tongue descends from one family. This is the biblical basis for the equality of all peoples (Acts 17:26).

verses.kjv_text: The sons of Japheth; Gomer, and Magog, and Madai, and Javan, and Tubal, and Meshech, and Tiras.
verses.original_text: בְּנֵי יֶפֶת גֹּמֶר וּמָגוֹג וּמָדַי וְיָוָן וְתֻבָל וּמֶשֶׁךְ וְתִירָס׃
verses.transliteration: Benei Yefet Gomer uMagog uMadai veYavan veTuval uMeshekh veTiras.
verses.study_note: Japheth's seven sons became the Indo-European peoples. Gomer = Cimmerians (later Galatians/Celts). Magog = Scythians (Ezekiel 38-39 prophecy). Madai = Medes (later Media-Persia). Javan = Ionians/Greeks. Tubal and Meshech = peoples of Asia Minor (modern Turkey). Tiras = Thracians or possibly the Etruscans. These settled primarily north and west of Israel.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Gomer; Ashkenaz, and Riphath, and Togarmah.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי גֹּמֶר אַשְׁכְּנַז וְרִיפַת וְתֹגַרְמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Gomer Ashkenaz veRifat veTogarmah.
verses.study_note: Gomer's three sons: Ashkenaz = Germanic/Scythian peoples (the term 'Ashkenazi Jews' derives from this name, referring to Jews who settled in Germanic lands). Riphath = possibly Paphlagonians. Togarmah = Armenians or peoples of eastern Asia Minor. Ezekiel 38:6 places Togarmah in the 'far north.'

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Javan; Elishah, and Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי יָוָן אֱלִישָׁה וְתַרְשִׁישׁ כִּתִּים וְדֹדָנִים׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Yavan Elishah veTarshish Kittim veDodanim.
verses.study_note: Javan's four sons — all maritime/Mediterranean peoples. Elishah = Cyprus or Aeolian Greeks (Ezek 27:7). Tarshish = Tartessus in Spain, or Mediterranean seafarers — the farthest west (Jonah fled to Tarshish). Kittim = Cyprus, later used broadly for western Mediterranean peoples (Daniel 11:30). Dodanim = Rhodians (people of Rhodes) — some manuscripts read 'Rodanim' (1 Chr 1:7).

verses.kjv_text: By these were the isles of the Gentiles divided in their lands; every one after his tongue, after their families, in their nations.
verses.original_text: מֵאֵלֶּה נִפְרְדוּ אִיֵּי הַגּוֹיִם בְּאַרְצֹתָם אִישׁ לִלְשֹׁנוֹ לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם בְּגוֹיֵהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Me'eleh niferedu iyyei haggoyim be'artzotam ish lilshono lemishpechotam begoyeihem.
verses.study_note: The Japhethite summary. 'Isles of the Gentiles' (iyyei haggoyim) refers to coastlands and islands — the Mediterranean world. Four categories of division: lands, tongues, families, nations. This anticipates the Babel event (chapter 11) where languages were confused. The spread of Japheth's descendants across Europe and Asia fulfills the prophecy 'God shall enlarge Japheth' (9:27).
verses.doctrinal_note: God sovereignly directs where nations settle (Acts 17:26-27). The division by 'tongues' points ahead to Babel (Gen 11) and forward to Pentecost (Acts 2), where the curse of language confusion is reversed by the Spirit.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Ham; Cush, and Mizraim, and Phut, and Canaan.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי חָם כּוּשׁ וּמִצְרַיִם וּפוּט וּכְנָעַן׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Cham Kush uMitzrayim uFut uKhena'an.
verses.study_note: Ham's four sons represent the peoples south and southwest of Israel. Cush = Ethiopia/Nubia (Upper Nile). Mizraim = Egypt (the dual form may refer to Upper and Lower Egypt). Phut = Libya/North Africa. Canaan = the peoples who occupied the Promised Land. Ham's line receives the most detailed treatment because of Israel's future interactions with these nations — Egypt (bondage), Canaan (conquest), and the Hamitic empires.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Cush; Seba, and Havilah, and Sabtah, and Raamah, and Sabtechah: and the sons of Raamah; Sheba, and Dedan.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי כוּשׁ סְבָא וַחֲוִילָה וְסַבְתָּה וְרַעְמָה וְסַבְתְּכָא וּבְנֵי רַעְמָה שְׁבָא וּדְדָן׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Kush Seva vaHavilah veSavtah veRa'mah veSavtekha uvenei Ra'mah Sheva uDedan.
verses.study_note: Cush's five sons and two grandsons settled in Arabia and East Africa. Seba = in the Horn of Africa or southern Arabia. Havilah = a region in Arabia (cf. Gen 2:11 — possibly different). Raamah = southwestern Arabia. Sheba = the Sabean kingdom (Queen of Sheba, 1 Kings 10). Dedan = a trading people in northwestern Arabia. These became prosperous trading nations along the Red Sea and Indian Ocean routes.

verses.kjv_text: And Cush begat Nimrod: he began to be a mighty one in the earth.
verses.original_text: וְכוּשׁ יָלַד אֶת־נִמְרֹד הוּא הֵחֵל לִהְיוֹת גִּבֹּר בָּאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: VeKhush yalad et-Nimrod hu hechel lihyot gibbor ba'aretz.
verses.study_note: The narrative pauses to focus on Nimrod — the most significant individual in this chapter. 'He began to be' (hechel lihyot) — he was the first to become a 'gibbor' (mighty warrior/tyrant). The same root 'chalal' (began) can mean 'to profane' — Nimrod may have been the first to profane by seeking power over others. His name likely derives from 'marad' (to rebel), though this is debated. Nimrod represents the first organized political power — the prototype of human empire built on conquest rather than divine mandate.
verses.doctrinal_note: Nimrod is the archetype of human autonomy and empire-building apart from God. His kingdoms — Babel, Nineveh — become the great oppressors of God's people throughout Scripture. Human power centralized apart from God leads to tyranny.

verses.kjv_text: He was a mighty hunter before the LORD: wherefore it is said, Even as Nimrod the mighty hunter before the LORD.
verses.original_text: הוּא־הָיָה גִבֹּר־צַיִד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה עַל־כֵּן יֵאָמַר כְּנִמְרֹד גִּבּוֹר צַיִד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Hu-hayah gibbor-tzayid lifnei Yahweh al-ken ye'amar keNimrod gibbor tzayid lifnei Yahweh.
verses.study_note: 'Before the LORD' (lifnei Yahweh) can mean 'in the face of' — either in God's sight (neutral) or 'against/in defiance of' God (hostile). The ancient Jewish traditions and the Septuagint favor the sense of 'against' or 'in opposition to.' 'Mighty hunter' may mean hunter of men as well as animals — a conqueror who hunted people into subjection. His fame became proverbial: 'like Nimrod' was the ancient world's way of saying 'the greatest conqueror.

verses.kjv_text: And the beginning of his kingdom was Babel, and Erech, and Accad, and Calneh, in the land of Shinar.
verses.original_text: וַתְּהִי רֵאשִׁית מַמְלַכְתּוֹ בָּבֶל וְאֶרֶךְ וְאַכַּד וְכַלְנֵה בְּאֶרֶץ שִׁנְעָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vattehi reshit mamlakhto Bavel ve'Erekh ve'Akkad veKhalneh be'eretz Shin'ar.
verses.study_note: Nimrod's first kingdom: four cities in Shinar (Mesopotamia/southern Iraq). Babel (Babylon) — the name means 'gate of God' (Akkadian) but Genesis 11:9 connects it to 'confusion' (balal). Erech = Uruk, one of the oldest cities in the world. Accad = the capital of the Akkadian empire (Sargon the Great). Calneh = possibly Nippur or Kullani. 'The land of Shinar' is the biblical name for Babylonia (cf. Gen 11:2, Dan 1:2).
verses.doctrinal_note: Babel/Babylon becomes the Bible's symbol of human rebellion against God — from this chapter through Revelation 17-18. Every human attempt to build a kingdom apart from God echoes Nimrod's Babel.

verses.kjv_text: Out of that land went forth Asshur, and builded Nineveh, and the city Rehoboth, and Calah,
verses.original_text: מִן־הָאָרֶץ הַהִוא יָצָא אַשּׁוּר וַיִּבֶן אֶת־נִינְוֵה וְאֶת־רְחֹבֹת עִיר וְאֶת־כָּלַח׃
verses.transliteration: Min-ha'aretz hahi yatza Asshur vayyiven et-Nineveh ve'et-Rechovot Ir ve'et-Kalach.
verses.study_note: This verse can be translated two ways: (1) 'Asshur went forth' — an individual (Shem's son, v.22) moved north, or (2) 'He [Nimrod] went forth into Assyria' — Nimrod expanded his empire northward. Many scholars favor the second reading, making Nimrod the founder of both Babylonian and Assyrian civilizations. Nineveh became the capital of the Assyrian empire — the great enemy that destroyed the northern kingdom of Israel (722 BC). Later, God sent Jonah to preach repentance there.

verses.kjv_text: And Resen between Nineveh and Calah: the same is a great city.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־רֶסֶן בֵּין נִינְוֵה וּבֵין כָּלַח הִוא הָעִיר הַגְּדֹלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-Resen bein Nineveh uvein Kalach hi ha'ir hagedolah.
verses.study_note: 'The great city' may refer to the entire metropolitan complex of Nineveh-Calah-Resen, sometimes called 'Greater Nineveh' — an urban sprawl connecting multiple cities. Jonah 3:3 describes Nineveh as a city of 'three days' journey' — likely referring to this metropolitan area. Archaeological excavations have confirmed these cities along the Tigris.

verses.kjv_text: And Mizraim begat Ludim, and Anamim, and Lehabim, and Naphtuhim,
verses.original_text: וּמִצְרַיִם יָלַד אֶת־לוּדִים וְאֶת־עֲנָמִים וְאֶת־לְהָבִים וְאֶת־נַפְתֻּחִים׃
verses.transliteration: UMitzrayim yalad et-Ludim ve'et-Anamim ve'et-Lehavim ve'et-Naftuchim.
verses.study_note: Mizraim (Egypt) 'begat' peoples, not individuals — the plural '-im' suffix indicates ethnic groups. Ludim = Libyans or related North African peoples. Anamim = possibly in the western Delta of Egypt. Lehabim = Libyans (from 'lahav,' flame — possibly light-skinned North Africans). Naphtuhim = people of the Nile Delta ('nof' = Memphis region). These are the ethnic groups that comprised ancient Egypt and its neighbors.

verses.kjv_text: And Pathrusim, and Casluhim, (out of whom came Philistim,) and Caphtorim.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־פַּתְרֻסִים וְאֶת־כַּסְלֻחִים אֲשֶׁר יָצְאוּ מִשָּׁם פְּלִשְׁתִּים וְאֶת־כַּפְתֹּרִים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-Patrusim ve'et-Kasluchim asher yatze'u misham Pelishtim ve'et-Kaftorim.
verses.study_note: Pathrusim = Upper Egypt (Pathros, Isaiah 11:11). Casluhim = uncertain location. The parenthetical note identifies the origin of the Philistines. However, other passages (Amos 9:7, Jer 47:4) say the Philistines came from Caphtor (Crete). The Philistines may have migrated through both regions. Caphtorim = Cretans/Aegean people. The Philistines became Israel's perennial enemy — Goliath was a Philistine.

verses.kjv_text: And Canaan begat Sidon his first born, and Heth,
verses.original_text: וּכְנַעַן יָלַד אֶת־צִידֹן בְּכֹרוֹ וְאֶת־חֵת׃
verses.transliteration: UKhna'an yalad et-Tzidon bekhoro ve'et-Chet.
verses.study_note: The Canaanite genealogy is critical because these are the peoples Israel would encounter in the Promised Land. Sidon = the Phoenician city-state (modern Lebanon), Canaan's firstborn. Heth = the Hittites — Abraham purchased the cave of Machpelah from 'the sons of Heth' (Gen 23). The Hittites became a major empire in Asia Minor.
verses.doctrinal_note: Canaan's descendants occupied the land God promised to Abraham (Gen 15:18-21). Their dispossession was not ethnic but moral — God judged them for their accumulated wickedness (Gen 15:16, Lev 18:24-28).

verses.kjv_text: And the Jebusite, and the Amorite, and the Girgasite,
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־הַיְבוּסִי וְאֶת־הָאֱמֹרִי וְאֵת הַגִּרְגָּשִׁי׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-haYevusi ve'et-ha'Emori ve'et haGirgashi.
verses.study_note: Three more Canaanite peoples: Jebusites = the inhabitants of Jebus (later Jerusalem) until David conquered it (2 Sam 5:6-9). Amorites = 'highlanders' — one of the most prominent Canaanite groups, occupying the hill country. In some texts 'Amorite' is used as a general term for all Canaanites. Girgashites = mentioned frequently in lists of Canaanite nations (Deut 7:1) but otherwise little known.

verses.kjv_text: And the Hivite, and the Arkite, and the Sinite,
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־הַחִוִּי וְאֶת־הָעַרְקִי וְאֶת־הַסִּינִי׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-haChivvi ve'et-ha'Arqi ve'et-haSini.
verses.study_note: Hivites = a Canaanite people, living in the central hill country and Lebanon (Josh 11:3). The Gibeonites who deceived Joshua were Hivites (Josh 9). Arkites = from Arka/Arqa in northern Lebanon (modern Tell Arqa). Sinites = possibly related to a town near Arka; some early traditions connected them to the Chinese (Sin/Sinim, Isaiah 49:12), though this is speculative.

verses.kjv_text: And the Arvadite, and the Zemarite, and the Hamathite: and afterward were the families of the Canaanites spread abroad.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־הָאַרְוָדִי וְאֶת־הַצְּמָרִי וְאֶת־הַחֲמָתִי וְאַחַר נָפֹצוּ מִשְׁפְּחוֹת הַכְּנַעֲנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-ha'Arvadi ve'et-haTzmari ve'et-haChamati ve'achar nafotzu mishpechot haKna'ani.
verses.study_note: The final Canaanite clans: Arvadites = from the island of Arvad/Arwad off the coast of Syria (Ezek 27:8,11). Zemarites = from Sumra/Zemar in coastal Syria. Hamathites = from Hamath (modern Hama, Syria), the northern boundary of the Promised Land (Num 34:8). 'Spread abroad' (nafotzu) — the Canaanites expanded throughout the region God had designated for Abraham's descendants.

verses.kjv_text: And the border of the Canaanites was from Sidon, as thou comest to Gerar, unto Gaza; as thou goest, unto Sodom, and Gomorrah, and Admah, and Zeboim, even unto Lasha.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי גְבוּל הַכְּנַעֲנִי מִצִּידֹן בֹּאֲכָה גְרָרָה עַד־עַזָּה בֹּאֲכָה סְדֹמָה וַעֲמֹרָה וְאַדְמָה וּצְבֹיִם עַד־לָשַׁע׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi gevul haKna'ani miTzidon bo'akhah Gerarah ad-Azzah bo'akhah Sedomah va'Amorah ve'Admah uTzvoyim ad-Lasha.
verses.study_note: The precise borders of Canaan: from Sidon (north) to Gaza (southwest), to the cities of the plain — Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboim (southeast). This territory remarkably overlaps with the Promised Land boundaries. Sodom and Gomorrah are mentioned here before their infamous destruction in Genesis 19 — already occupied by Canaanites under Noah's curse (9:25).

verses.kjv_text: These are the sons of Ham, after their families, after their tongues, in their countries, and in their nations.
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה בְנֵי־חָם לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לִלְשֹׁנֹתָם בְּאַרְצֹתָם בְּגוֹיֵהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Eleh venei-Cham lemishpechotam lilshonotam be'artzotam begoyeihem.
verses.study_note: The Hamite summary uses the same four categories as the Japhethite summary (v.5): families, tongues, countries, nations. Ham's descendants occupy the most strategic and powerful positions in the ancient world — Egypt, Babylon, Assyria, Canaan. Yet their prominence does not nullify the prophetic word of 9:25-27.

verses.kjv_text: Unto Shem also, the father of all the children of Eber, the brother of Japheth the elder, even to him were children born.
verses.original_text: וּלְשֵׁם יֻלַּד גַּם־הוּא אֲבִי כָּל־בְּנֵי־עֵבֶר אֲחִי יֶפֶת הַגָּדוֹל׃
verses.transliteration: UleShem yullad gam-hu avi khol-benei-Ever achi Yefet haggadol.
verses.study_note: Shem's genealogy is introduced with a unique title: 'father of all the children of Eber.' This singles out Eber (from whom 'Hebrew' derives) as Shem's most important descendant — because through Eber comes Peleg, then eventually Abraham. 'The brother of Japheth the elder' clarifies the birth order. Shem is listed last (place of honor) because he carries the covenant line.
verses.doctrinal_note: Shem means 'name' or 'renown' — God would make His 'name' known through Shem's line. From Shem comes Abraham, Israel, and ultimately Jesus Christ. The covenant line is traced not through the powerful (Ham/Nimrod) but through the line God chose.

verses.kjv_text: The children of Shem; Elam, and Asshur, and Arphaxad, and Lud, and Aram.
verses.original_text: בְּנֵי שֵׁם עֵילָם וְאַשּׁוּר וְאַרְפַּכְשַׁד וְלוּד וַאֲרָם׃
verses.transliteration: Benei Shem Eilam ve'Asshur ve'Arpakhshad veLud va'Aram.
verses.study_note: Shem's five sons: Elam = peoples east of Mesopotamia (southwest Iran) — Susa was their capital. Asshur = Assyria (yes, also claimed by Ham via Nimrod — indicating intermingling or dual origins). Arphaxad = the covenant line — father of Shelah, grandfather of Eber, ancestor of Abraham. Lud = Lydians of Asia Minor. Aram = Arameans/Syrians — Aramaic became the lingua franca of the ancient Near East (the language Jesus likely spoke).

verses.kjv_text: And the children of Aram; Uz, and Hul, and Gether, and Mash.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי אֲרָם עוּץ וְחוּל וְגֶתֶר וָמַשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Aram Utz veChul veGeter vaMash.
verses.study_note: Aram's four sons: Uz = the land where Job lived (Job 1:1) — probably in the Syrian desert region east of the Jordan. Hul, Gether, and Mash are less well identified, but represent Aramean tribal groups in Syria and Mesopotamia. The 'land of Uz' becomes significant as the setting for the oldest theological debate in Scripture — the book of Job.

verses.kjv_text: And Arphaxad begat Salah; and Salah begat Eber.
verses.original_text: וְאַרְפַּכְשַׁד יָלַד אֶת־שָׁלַח וְשֶׁלַח יָלַד אֶת־עֵבֶר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'Arpakhshad yalad et-Shelach veShelach yalad et-Ever.
verses.study_note: The covenant line advances: Arphaxad → Shelah → Eber. Shelah ('sent') and Eber ('one who crosses over') carry the line toward Abraham. Luke 3:35-36 adds 'Cainan' between Arphaxad and Shelah (following the Septuagint), while the Hebrew text goes directly. Eber is singled out because 'Hebrew' (Ivri) derives from his name — Abraham is the first person called 'the Hebrew' (Gen 14:13).

verses.kjv_text: And unto Eber were born two sons: the name of one was Peleg; for in his days was the earth divided; and his brother’s name was Joktan.
verses.original_text: וּלְעֵבֶר יֻלַּד שְׁנֵי בָנִים שֵׁם הָאֶחָד פֶּלֶג כִּי בְיָמָיו נִפְלְגָה הָאָרֶץ וְשֵׁם אָחִיו יָקְטָן׃
verses.transliteration: Ule'Ever yullad shenei vanim shem ha'echad Peleg ki veyamav niflegah ha'aretz veshem achiv Yoktan.
verses.study_note: A pivotal verse. Peleg ('division') — 'in his days the earth was divided.' This likely refers to the division at Babel (Gen 11:1-9), when God scattered humanity by confusing languages. Some have speculated this refers to continental drift, but the context and language strongly favor the Babel interpretation. Peleg carries the covenant line (to Abraham), while Joktan's descendants settle in Arabia. The wordplay is deliberate — Peleg's very name memorializes God's judgment at Babel.
verses.doctrinal_note: God divides to prevent unified rebellion (Babel) — the same principle of holy separation seen throughout Genesis 1. Division is not always punishment; it can be protection against consolidated evil.

verses.kjv_text: And Joktan begat Almodad, and Sheleph, and Hazarmaveth, and Jerah,
verses.original_text: וְיָקְטָן יָלַד אֶת־אַלְמוֹדָד וְאֶת־שָׁלֶף וְאֶת־חֲצַרְמָוֶת וְאֶת־יָרַח׃
verses.transliteration: VeYoktan yalad et-Almodad ve'et-Shalef ve'et-Chatzarmavet ve'et-Yarach.
verses.study_note: Joktan's 13 sons (vv.26-29) are the Arabian tribes. Almodad = possibly 'God is a friend.' Sheleph = 'a drawing out.' Hazarmaveth = Hadramaut, a well-known region in southern Arabia (modern Yemen). Jerah = 'moon' — possibly connected to moon-worship common in pre-Islamic Arabia. These names correspond to regions and tribes in the Arabian Peninsula.

verses.kjv_text: And Hadoram, and Uzal, and Diklah,
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־הֲדוֹרָם וְאֶת־אוּזָל וְאֶת־דִּקְלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-Hadoram ve'et-Uzal ve'et-Diqlah.
verses.study_note: More Arabian tribes. Hadoram = possibly connected to Dauram in southern Arabia. Uzal = identified with Sana'a, the ancient capital of Yemen. Diklah = 'palm grove' — associated with a palm-rich region of Arabia. These names survive in Arabian geography, confirming the historical reliability of this table.

verses.kjv_text: And Obal, and Abimael, and Sheba,
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־עוֹבָל וְאֶת־אֲבִימָאֵל וְאֶת־שְׁבָא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-Oval ve'et-Avima'el ve'et-Sheva.
verses.study_note: Obal = called 'Ebal' in 1 Chronicles 1:22 — a southern Arabian tribe. Abimael = 'God is my father' — a theophoric name suggesting awareness of God among early Arabian peoples. Sheba = appears here as a Semitic people through Joktan (distinct from the Hamitic Sheba in v.7 through Cush). The Queen of Sheba who visited Solomon likely came from this Arabian Sheba (1 Kings 10).

verses.kjv_text: And Ophir, and Havilah, and Jobab: all these were the sons of Joktan.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־אוֹפִר וְאֶת־חֲוִילָה וְאֶת־יוֹבָב כָּל־אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי יָקְטָן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-Ofir ve'et-Chavilah ve'et-Yovav kol-eleh benei Yoktan.
verses.study_note: The final three sons complete Joktan's 13. Ophir = famous for its gold (1 Kings 9:28, 10:11). Solomon sent ships to Ophir — its location is debated: southern Arabia, East Africa, or India. Havilah = an Arabian region (distinct from the Cushite Havilah in v.7). Jobab = possibly connected to Job or a region near Edom. Thirteen sons of Joktan populate the Arabian Peninsula — a significant portion of the Table of Nations.

verses.kjv_text: And their dwelling was from Mesha, as thou goest unto Sephar a mount of the east.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי מוֹשָׁבָם מִמֵּשָׁא בֹּאֲכָה סְפָרָה הַר הַקֶּדֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi moshovam miMesha bo'akhah Sefarah har hakkedem.
verses.study_note: The Joktanite territory stretched across the Arabian Peninsula: from Mesha (possibly in northern Arabia near the Persian Gulf) to Sephar (possibly Dhofar in Oman or the Sabaean capital). 'A mount of the east' (har hakkedem) locates them to the east and south of Israel. This geographical precision underscores that Genesis 10 records real peoples in real places.

verses.kjv_text: These are the sons of Shem, after their families, after their tongues, in their lands, after their nations.
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה בְנֵי־שֵׁם לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לִלְשֹׁנֹתָם בְּאַרְצֹתָם לְגוֹיֵהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Eleh venei-Shem lemishpechotam lilshonotam be'artzotam legoyeihem.
verses.study_note: The Shemite summary — the same four categories: families, tongues, lands, nations. Though listed last, Shem's line is the most theologically significant. Through Arphaxad → Eber → Peleg → Abraham, God's covenant promises would flow. The Semitic peoples include the Hebrews, Arameans, Arabs, Assyrians, and Elamites.

verses.kjv_text: These are the families of the sons of Noah, after their generations, in their nations: and by these were the nations divided in the earth after the flood.
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹת בְּנֵי־נֹחַ לְתוֹלְדֹתָם בְּגוֹיֵהֶם וּמֵאֵלֶּה נִפְרְדוּ הַגּוֹיִם בָּאָרֶץ אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל׃
verses.transliteration: Eleh mishpechot benei-Noach letoledotam begoyeihem ume'eleh niferedu haggoyim ba'aretz achar hammabbul.
verses.study_note: The grand summary of the Table of Nations. Seventy nations are traditionally counted in this chapter (matching the number of Jacob's family who went to Egypt, Gen 46:27, and the seventy elders of Israel, Exod 24:1). 'The nations divided' (niferedu haggoyim) points forward to the Babel narrative (chapter 11), which explains how the division happened. This chapter establishes that every nation on earth has a known origin, a divine purpose, and a place in God's sovereign plan.
verses.doctrinal_note: All nations are under God's sovereignty. He determines their times and boundaries (Acts 17:26). The seventy nations of Genesis 10 become the mission field of the gospel. What Babel scattered, Pentecost begins to reunite. God's plan has always been global — 'in thee shall all families of the earth be blessed' (Gen 12:3).

meta.overview: The Table of Nations — the most remarkable ethnographic document in ancient literature. Genesis 10 traces the post-Flood spread of humanity through Noah's three sons: Japheth (Indo-European peoples), Ham (African and Canaanite peoples), and Shem (Semitic peoples leading to Abraham). This is not merely a genealogy but a theological map showing how God fulfilled His command to 'fill the earth' (9:1). Seventy nations are listed — the same number later sent by Jesus on mission (Luke 10:1). The chapter demonstrates that all humanity shares one origin, one blood, and one Creator, while also explaining the geopolitical landscape of the ancient Near East.
meta.theme: The Nations Under God's Sovereignty
meta.keyWordMeaning: Generations, account — the structural keyword of Genesis, tracing God's purposes through family lines.
outline: Introduction (v.1): The sons of Noah after the Flood
The Line of Japheth (vv.2-5): Indo-European peoples — coastland nations
The Line of Ham (vv.6-20): African, Canaanite, and Mesopotamian peoples — including Nimrod
The Line of Shem (vv.21-31): Semitic peoples — the covenant line to Abraham
Summary (v.32): The nations divided after the Flood

verses.kjv_text: And the whole earth was of one language, and of one speech.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כָל־הָאָרֶץ שָׂפָה אֶחָת וּדְבָרִים אֲחָדִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi khol-ha'aretz safah echat udevarim achadim.
verses.study_note: The narrative opens with a picture of total linguistic unity. 'One language' (safah echat — literally 'one lip') and 'one speech' (devarim achadim — 'one set of words'). This unity was not inherently evil — it was God's gift. But fallen humanity would use it to organize rebellion rather than worship. Chronologically, this precedes the scattering described in chapter 10. The two words — safah (lip/language) and devarim (words/matters) — suggest both shared vocabulary and shared purpose.
verses.doctrinal_note: Unity is a powerful force. In the hands of redeemed humanity, it accomplishes God's purposes (Acts 2). In the hands of fallen humanity, it accelerates rebellion. God sometimes divides to restrain evil.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, as they journeyed from the east, that they found a plain in the land of Shinar; and they dwelt there.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בְּנָסְעָם מִקֶּדֶם וַיִּמְצְאוּ בִקְעָה בְּאֶרֶץ שִׁנְעָר וַיֵּשְׁבוּ שָׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi benas'am mikkedem vayyimtze'u viq'ah be'eretz Shin'ar vayyeshvu sham.
verses.study_note: 'From the east' (mikkedem) — or 'eastward.' The same word can mean 'from the ancient/from the beginning,' carrying overtones of moving away from Eden (which was 'eastward,' Gen 2:8). Every movement 'east' in Genesis signals departure from God's presence: Cain went east (4:16), Lot chose eastward (13:11). 'The land of Shinar' = Mesopotamia/Babylonia (cf. 10:10). 'They dwelt there' — instead of spreading out as God commanded (9:1), they settled down in defiance.

verses.kjv_text: And they said one to another, Go to, let us make brick, and burn them thoroughly. And they had brick for stone, and slime had they for morter.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ אִישׁ אֶל־רֵעֵהוּ הָבָה נִלְבְּנָה לְבֵנִים וְנִשְׂרְפָה לִשְׂרֵפָה וַתְּהִי לָהֶם הַלְּבֵנָה לְאָבֶן וְהַחֵמָר הָיָה לָהֶם לַחֹמֶר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru ish el-re'ehu havah nilbenah levenim venisrefah lisrefah vattehi lahem hallevenah le'even vehachemar hayah lahem lachomer.
verses.study_note: The first 'let us' — a human 'let us' that parodies God's divine 'Let us' in 1:26. 'Go to' (havah) is an interjection of mutual encouragement — 'Come on!' They make bricks instead of using stone — Mesopotamia lacked stone but had abundant clay. 'Burn them thoroughly' — kiln-fired bricks were far stronger than sun-dried. 'Slime' (chemar) = bitumen/asphalt, abundant in Mesopotamia. The technology is impressive; the motive is rebellious.

verses.kjv_text: And they said, Go to, let us build us a city and a tower, whose top may reach unto heaven; and let us make us a name, lest we be scattered abroad upon the face of the whole earth.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ הָבָה נִבְנֶה־לָּנוּ עִיר וּמִגְדָּל וְרֹאשׁוֹ בַשָּׁמַיִם וְנַעֲשֶׂה־לָּנוּ שֵׁם פֶּן־נָפוּץ עַל־פְּנֵי כָל־הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru havah nivneh-llanu ir umigdal verosho vashamayim vena'aseh-llanu shem pen-nafutz al-penei khol-ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The heart of the rebellion expressed in three desires: (1) 'Let us build a city and a tower' — permanent settlement defying God's command to fill the earth. (2) 'Whose top may reach unto heaven' — not literally touching heaven but a tower dedicated to heaven (a ziggurat for astral worship). (3) 'Let us make us a name' — self-glorification rather than glorifying God. The phrase 'lest we be scattered' reveals their conscious defiance of God's mandate in 9:1. Three times 'us/we' appears — the language is entirely self-referential. God is completely absent from their plans.
verses.doctrinal_note: The three sins of Babel: (1) Defiance of God's command (refusing to scatter/fill the earth). (2) Idolatry (a tower 'to heaven' — seeking to reach God on their own terms). (3) Pride (making a name for themselves). These same three sins — disobedience, false worship, and pride — recur throughout Scripture as the root of all rebellion. God would later make Abram's name great (12:2) — not through self-exaltation but through faith and obedience.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD came down to see the city and the tower, which the children of men builded.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּרֶד יְהוָה לִרְאֹת אֶת־הָעִיר וְאֶת־הַמִּגְדָּל אֲשֶׁר בָּנוּ בְּנֵי הָאָדָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyered Yahweh lir'ot et-ha'ir ve'et-hammigdal asher banu benei ha'adam.
verses.study_note: One of Scripture's most devastatingly ironic verses. Humanity built a tower 'reaching heaven' — yet God has to 'come down' to even see it. The tower that seemed so impressive from earth was invisible from heaven. 'The LORD came down' is an anthropomorphism — God is omniscient and needs no inspection — but the language emphasizes His sovereign condescension. 'Children of men' (benei ha'adam) — literally 'sons of Adam.' They are still just dust-creatures playing at being gods.
verses.doctrinal_note: The contrast between human ambition and divine perspective: what humanity considered their greatest achievement was so small that God had to 'come down' to see it. All human pride is microscopic in God's sight (Isaiah 40:15-17).

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said, Behold, the people is one, and they have all one language; and this they begin to do: and now nothing will be restrained from them, which they have imagined to do.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה הֵן עַם אֶחָד וְשָׂפָה אַחַת לְכֻלָּם וְזֶה הַחִלָּם לַעֲשׂוֹת וְעַתָּה לֹא־יִבָּצֵר מֵהֶם כֹּל אֲשֶׁר יָזְמוּ לַעֲשׂוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Yahweh hen am echad vesafah achat lekhullam vezeh hachillam la'asot ve'attah lo-yibatzer mehem kol asher yazemu la'asot.
verses.study_note: God's assessment. He acknowledges their unity and its dangerous potential: 'nothing will be restrained from them.' This is not a statement of divine insecurity but a recognition that unified human rebellion would accelerate evil beyond the pre-Flood conditions. 'Imagined' (yazemu) means 'plot, scheme' — their imagination was bent toward evil (cf. 6:5). God intervenes not to crush human potential but to restrain consolidated wickedness. The phrase 'this they begin to do' echoes 'began to be' (10:8) about Nimrod.

verses.kjv_text: Go to, let us go down, and there confound their language, that they may not understand one another’s speech.
verses.original_text: הָבָה נֵרְדָה וְנָבְלָה שָׁם שְׂפָתָם אֲשֶׁר לֹא יִשְׁמְעוּ אִישׁ שְׂפַת רֵעֵהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Havah neredah venavlah sham sefatam asher lo yishme'u ish sefat re'ehu.
verses.study_note: God's response deliberately mirrors the builders' language. They said 'havah' (come on, v.3-4); God says 'havah' (come on). They said 'let us build'; God says 'let us go down.' They planned to reach heaven; God descends to earth. 'Let us' (neredah) — the divine plural again (cf. 1:26, 3:22), consistent with the Trinity in counsel. 'Confound' (navlah, from balal) — the verb that gives Babel its name. The punishment fits the crime: they used unified language for rebellion, so God removes their unity.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's judgment is precise and merciful. He does not destroy them (as in the Flood) but scatters them — accomplishing His original purpose (filling the earth) while restraining consolidated evil. Judgment serves God's redemptive purposes.

verses.kjv_text: So the LORD scattered them abroad from thence upon the face of all the earth: and they left off to build the city.
verses.original_text: וַיָּפֶץ יְהוָה אֹתָם מִשָּׁם עַל־פְּנֵי כָל־הָאָרֶץ וַיַּחְדְּלוּ לִבְנֹת הָעִיר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyafetz Yahweh otam misham al-penei khol-ha'aretz vayyachdelu livnot ha'ir.
verses.study_note: The irony is complete. 'Lest we be scattered' (v.4) — and God scattered them. The very thing they feared became their reality. 'The LORD scattered them' — Yahweh personally accomplished what they refused to do voluntarily. 'They left off' (vayyachdelu) — they ceased, stopped, desisted. The half-finished tower and abandoned city stand as a monument to the futility of opposing God. Archaeology has uncovered numerous abandoned ziggurats in Mesopotamia.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore is the name of it called Babel; because the LORD did there confound the language of all the earth: and from thence did the LORD scatter them abroad upon the face of all the earth.
verses.original_text: עַל־כֵּן קָרָא שְׁמָהּ בָּבֶל כִּי־שָׁם בָּלַל יְהוָה שְׂפַת כָּל־הָאָרֶץ וּמִשָּׁם הֱפִיצָם יְהוָה עַל־פְּנֵי כָּל־הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Al-ken kara shemah Bavel ki-sham balal Yahweh sefat kol-ha'aretz umisham hefitzam Yahweh al-penei kol-ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The etymology: 'Babel' because God 'balal' (confused) languages there. The Babylonians claimed 'Babel' meant 'gate of god' (Bab-ili in Akkadian), but the Hebrew narrator gives the true meaning: confusion. They wanted to make a 'name' (shem, v.4); instead their city's name (shem) became a byword for confusion and judgment. 'From thence' — Babel is the geographic origin point of all the nations listed in chapter 10. This verse closes the primeval history (Gen 1-11) and sets the stage for God's redemptive solution: the call of Abram.
verses.doctrinal_note: Babel/Babylon becomes Scripture's ultimate symbol of human civilization organized against God — from Genesis 11 through Revelation 18. Its fall is certain because it is built on the same foundation as the tower: human pride, self-made religion, and defiance of God's word. But what Babel scattered, Pentecost begins to reunite — in Acts 2, the Spirit reverses the curse by enabling people of every language to hear the gospel. The story of the Bible is the story of God gathering the nations Babel scattered.

verses.kjv_text: These are the generations of Shem: Shem was an hundred years old, and begat Arphaxad two years after the flood:
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת שֵׁם שֵׁם בֶּן־מְאַת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת־אַרְפַּכְשַׁד שְׁנָתַיִם אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל׃
verses.transliteration: Eleh toledot Shem Shem ben-me'at shanah vayyoled et-Arpakhshad shenatayim achar hammabbul.
verses.study_note: The fifth 'toledot' section begins. After the widest lens (all nations at Babel), the narrative narrows to one line: Shem's. This genealogy of ten generations (Shem to Abram) parallels the ten generations from Adam to Noah (Gen 5). The pattern: name, age at fathering, remaining years, 'and he died' (though this last phrase is notably absent — perhaps because the lifespans overlap into the patriarchal period). Shem was 100 when Arphaxad was born, two years after the Flood.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's response to Babel's scattering is not abandonment but election. From all the scattered nations, God chooses one line — Shem's — through which to bring blessing to all nations (Gen 12:3). Grace always follows judgment in Genesis.

verses.kjv_text: And Shem lived after he begat Arphaxad five hundred years, and begat sons and daughters.
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי־שֵׁם אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת־אַרְפַּכְשַׁד חֲמֵשׁ מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi-Shem acharei holido et-Arpakhshad chamesh me'ot shanah vayyoled banim uvanot.
verses.study_note: Shem lived 500 years after Arphaxad — a total of 600 years. This means Shem outlived Abraham by 35 years! He was alive through the entire patriarchal period. The declining lifespans in this chapter are notable: Shem (600), Arphaxad (438), Shelah (433), Eber (464), Peleg (239), Reu (239), Serug (230), Nahor (148), Terah (205). A dramatic decline from the pre-Flood ages of 900+ years, stabilizing around 200 years by Abraham's time.

verses.kjv_text: And Arphaxad lived five and thirty years, and begat Salah:
verses.original_text: וְאַרְפַּכְשַׁד חַי חָמֵשׁ וּשְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת־שָׁלַח׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'Arpakhshad chai chamesh usheloshim shanah vayyoled et-Shelach.
verses.study_note: Arphaxad fathered Shelah at age 35. The Septuagint (Greek OT) and Luke 3:36 insert 'Cainan' between Arphaxad and Shelah. The Hebrew text goes directly from Arphaxad to Shelah. This difference suggests either a scribal addition in the LXX or a compression in the Hebrew text — a reminder that ancient genealogies sometimes skip generations ('begat' can mean 'became ancestor of').

verses.kjv_text: And Arphaxad lived after he begat Salah four hundred and three years, and begat sons and daughters.
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי אַרְפַּכְשַׁד אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת־שֶׁלַח שָׁלֹשׁ שָׁנִים וְאַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Arpakhshad acharei holido et-Shelach shalosh shanim ve'arba me'ot shanah vayyoled banim uvanot.
verses.study_note: Arphaxad's total lifespan: 438 years. Already significantly shorter than the pre-Flood patriarchs (who lived 800-900+ years). 'Sons and daughters' — each generation produced many children, but only the covenant line is traced. The genealogy functions as a theological telescope, narrowing from many to one.

verses.kjv_text: And Salah lived thirty years, and begat Eber:
verses.original_text: וְשֶׁלַח חַי שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת־עֵבֶר׃
verses.transliteration: VeShelach chai sheloshim shanah vayyoled et-Ever.
verses.study_note: Shelah fathered Eber at age 30. Eber is singled out in 10:21 as the ancestor whose name gives us 'Hebrew' (Ivri). The word means 'one who crosses over' — a fitting description of Abraham, who would cross the Euphrates in obedience to God's call, and of his descendants, who would cross the Jordan into the Promised Land.

verses.kjv_text: And Salah lived after he begat Eber four hundred and three years, and begat sons and daughters.
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי־שֶׁלַח אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת־עֵבֶר שָׁלֹשׁ שָׁנִים וְאַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi-Shelach acharei holido et-Ever shalosh shanim ve'arba me'ot shanah vayyoled banim uvanot.
verses.study_note: Shelah's total lifespan: 433 years. The lifespans in this genealogy are declining but still far exceed modern norms. This transition period between the antediluvian world and the patriarchal era shows gradual change, not sudden collapse.

verses.kjv_text: And Eber lived four and thirty years, and begat Peleg:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי־עֵבֶר אַרְבַּע וּשְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת־פָּלֶג׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi-Ever arba usheloshim shanah vayyoled et-Paleg.
verses.study_note: Eber fathered Peleg at age 34. Peleg ('division') — in his days the earth was divided (10:25). If this refers to Babel, the event occurred within Peleg's lifetime, approximately 100-340 years after the Flood. Eber himself lived 464 years — the longest post-Flood lifespan in this genealogy, even longer than Shem's remaining years. This anomaly may signal Eber's special significance as the 'Hebrew' ancestor.

verses.kjv_text: And Eber lived after he begat Peleg four hundred and thirty years, and begat sons and daughters.
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי־עֵבֶר אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת־פֶּלֶג שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה וְאַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi-Ever acharei holido et-Peleg sheloshim shanah ve'arba me'ot shanah vayyoled banim uvanot.
verses.study_note: Eber's total lifespan: 464 years. Remarkably, Eber outlived four generations of his own descendants (Peleg, Reu, Serug, and Nahor). He would have been alive during Abraham's early life. Multiple generations overlapped significantly in this era.

verses.kjv_text: And Peleg lived thirty years, and begat Reu:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי־פֶלֶג שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת־רְעוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi-Peleg sheloshim shanah vayyoled et-Re'u.
verses.study_note: Peleg fathered Reu at 30. With Peleg, the lifespans drop dramatically — from 464 (Eber) to 239 (Peleg). This steep decline may correlate with the Babel event during Peleg's lifetime. Reu's name means 'friend' or 'companion,' possibly reflecting the need for community after the scattering.

verses.kjv_text: And Peleg lived after he begat Reu two hundred and nine years, and begat sons and daughters.
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי־פֶלֶג אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת־רְעוּ תֵּשַׁע שָׁנִים וּמָאתַיִם שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi-Peleg acharei holido et-Re'u tesha shanim umataim shanah vayyoled banim uvanot.
verses.study_note: Peleg's total lifespan: 239 years — less than half of his father Eber's lifespan. This dramatic decline continues through the next generations, with lifespans stabilizing around 200-230 years.

verses.kjv_text: And Reu lived two and thirty years, and begat Serug:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי רְעוּ שְׁנַיִם וּשְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת־שְׂרוּג׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Re'u shenayim usheloshim shanah vayyoled et-Serug.
verses.study_note: Reu fathered Serug at 32. Serug's name may mean 'branch' or 'intertwining.' Ancient tradition places Serug in the region of Haran (northern Mesopotamia), which is where the family line is heading geographically — from southern Mesopotamia (Ur) toward Haran, and ultimately toward Canaan.

verses.kjv_text: And Reu lived after he begat Serug two hundred and seven years, and begat sons and daughters.
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי רְעוּ אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת־שְׂרוּג שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים וּמָאתַיִם שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Re'u acharei holido et-Serug sheva shanim umataim shanah vayyoled banim uvanot.
verses.study_note: Reu's total lifespan: 239 years — identical to Peleg's. The lifespans have stabilized at approximately 230-240 years for several generations before declining further.

verses.kjv_text: And Serug lived thirty years, and begat Nahor:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי שְׂרוּג שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת־נָחוֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Serug sheloshim shanah vayyoled et-Nachor.
verses.study_note: Serug fathered Nahor at 30. This is Nahor I — Abraham's grandfather (distinct from Nahor II, Abraham's brother, v.26). Nahor is an attested ancient name in Mesopotamian records. The city of Nahor near Haran is mentioned in the Mari tablets (18th century BC), confirming the historicity of these names.

verses.kjv_text: And Serug lived after he begat Nahor two hundred years, and begat sons and daughters.
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי שְׂרוּג אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת־נָחוֹר מָאתַיִם שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Serug acharei holido et-Nachor mataim shanah vayyoled banim uvanot.
verses.study_note: Serug's total lifespan: 230 years. The genealogy marches steadily toward Terah and Abram. Ancient Jewish tradition (Joshua 24:2) records that Terah's family served other gods — the religious context was polytheistic Mesopotamia.

verses.kjv_text: And Nahor lived nine and twenty years, and begat Terah:
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי נָחוֹר תֵּשַׁע וְעֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת־תָּרַח׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Nachor tesha ve'esrim shanah vayyoled et-Terach.
verses.study_note: Nahor fathered Terah at age 29. Terah's name may be related to the Hebrew word for 'moon' (yareach) or to the Akkadian word for an ibex. Given that Ur and Haran were both centers of moon-god worship (Sin/Nanna), the connection to lunar worship is suggestive. Joshua 24:2 confirms Terah served 'other gods.'

verses.kjv_text: And Nahor lived after he begat Terah an hundred and nineteen years, and begat sons and daughters.
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי נָחוֹר אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת־תֶּרַח תְּשַׁע־עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה וּמְאַת שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Nachor acharei holido et-Terach tesha-esreh shanah ume'at shanah vayyoled banim uvanot.
verses.study_note: Nahor's total lifespan: 148 years — the shortest in this genealogy. The decline from Shem (600) to Nahor (148) represents a loss of over 75% of lifespan across these generations. By Abraham's time (175 years, Gen 25:7), the ages have nearly stabilized toward what we consider the patriarchal norm.

verses.kjv_text: And Terah lived seventy years, and begat Abram, Nahor, and Haran.
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי־תֶרַח שִׁבְעִים שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת־אַבְרָם אֶת־נָחוֹר וְאֶת־הָרָן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi-Terach shiv'im shanah vayyoled et-Avram et-Nachor ve'et-Haran.
verses.study_note: The genealogy reaches its destination: Terah and his three sons — Abram, Nahor, and Haran. The listing of three sons echoes Noah's three sons (5:32). Abram is listed first (as the most important), but may not have been the firstborn — Haran died before Terah (v.28), and Abram was 75 when he left Haran at age Terah was 205 (suggesting Abram was born when Terah was 130, not 70). The name 'Abram' means 'exalted father' — later changed to 'Abraham' ('father of a multitude,' 17:5).
verses.doctrinal_note: Ten generations from Shem to Abram mirror ten generations from Adam to Noah (Gen 5). Both genealogies end with three sons. Both lead to a man of faith through whom God would preserve and advance His purposes. The pattern of Genesis: widespread sin → divine judgment → grace through one chosen line.

verses.kjv_text: Now these are the generations of Terah: Terah begat Abram, Nahor, and Haran; and Haran begat Lot.
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת תֶּרַח תֶּרַח הוֹלִיד אֶת־אַבְרָם אֶת־נָחוֹר וְאֶת־הָרָן וְהָרָן הוֹלִיד אֶת־לוֹט׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'eleh toledot Terach Terach holid et-Avram et-Nachor ve'et-Haran veHaran holid et-Lot.
verses.study_note: The sixth 'toledot' in Genesis. Terah's 'generations' will follow Abram — the most significant son. Haran's only mentioned descendant is Lot, whose story intertwines with Abraham's throughout Genesis 12-19. Lot's journey represents the contrast between faith (Abraham) and compromise (Lot) — both leave Ur, but they respond differently to God's calling.

verses.kjv_text: And Haran died before his father Terah in the land of his nativity, in Ur of the Chaldees.
verses.original_text: וַיָּמָת הָרָן עַל־פְּנֵי תֶּרַח אָבִיו בְּאֶרֶץ מוֹלַדְתּוֹ בְּאוּר כַּשְׂדִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyamot Haran al-penei Terach aviv be'eretz moladeto be'Ur Kasdim.
verses.study_note: The first recorded death of a son before his father in Scripture. 'Before his father' (al-penei — literally 'in the face of') — Terah witnessed his son's death. 'Ur of the Chaldees' — a major Sumerian city in southern Mesopotamia (modern Tell el-Muqayyar, Iraq). Archaeological excavations by Leonard Woolley (1922-1934) revealed a sophisticated civilization with ziggurats, royal tombs, and advanced culture. This was the world Abram was called to leave — not a primitive backwater but the pinnacle of human civilization.

verses.kjv_text: And Abram and Nahor took them wives: the name of Abram’s wife was Sarai; and the name of Nahor’s wife, Milcah, the daughter of Haran, the father of Milcah, and the father of Iscah.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח אַבְרָם וְנָחוֹר לָהֶם נָשִׁים שֵׁם אֵשֶׁת־אַבְרָם שָׂרָי וְשֵׁם אֵשֶׁת־נָחוֹר מִלְכָּה בַּת־הָרָן אֲבִי־מִלְכָּה וַאֲבִי יִסְכָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach Avram veNachor lahem nashim shem eshet-Avram Sarai veshem eshet-Nachor Milkah bat-Haran avi-Milkah va'avi Yiskah.
verses.study_note: Abram marries Sarai ('my princess' — later renamed Sarah, 'princess,' Gen 17:15). Nahor marries Milcah ('queen'), daughter of their deceased brother Haran — Nahor married his niece. Ancient tradition (Josephus) identifies Iscah with Sarai, making Abram's wife also Haran's daughter and therefore Abram's niece — consistent with Genesis 20:12 where Abram calls her 'the daughter of my father but not the daughter of my mother.' Milcah would become the grandmother of Rebekah (Gen 22:23).

verses.kjv_text: But Sarai was barren; she had no child.
verses.original_text: וַתְּהִי שָׂרַי עֲקָרָה אֵין לָהּ וָלָד׃
verses.transliteration: Vattehi Sarai aqarah ein lah valad.
verses.study_note: One of the most important single sentences in Genesis. 'Barren' (aqarah) — the word echoes through the patriarchal narratives: Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, and Hannah were all barren. The double emphasis — 'barren... no child' — underscores the impossibility. How can the 'exalted father' (Abram) become the father of nations if his wife cannot bear children? This sets up the central tension of the Abraham narrative and the necessity of divine intervention. Every promise to Abram will require a miracle.
verses.doctrinal_note: Barrenness in Genesis is not a curse but a stage for God's power. The pattern of barrenness → divine intervention → miraculous birth runs from Sarah to Hannah to Elizabeth (Luke 1:7). God chooses the impossible to demonstrate that salvation comes from Him alone, not from human ability (1 Cor 1:27-29).

verses.kjv_text: And Terah took Abram his son, and Lot the son of Haran his son’s son, and Sarai his daughter in law, his son Abram’s wife; and they went forth with them from Ur of the Chaldees, to go into the land of Canaan; and they came unto Haran, and dwelt there.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח תֶּרַח אֶת־אַבְרָם בְּנוֹ וְאֶת־לוֹט בֶּן־הָרָן בֶּן־בְּנוֹ וְאֵת שָׂרַי כַּלָּתוֹ אֵשֶׁת אַבְרָם בְּנוֹ וַיֵּצְאוּ אִתָּם מֵאוּר כַּשְׂדִּים לָלֶכֶת אַרְצָה כְּנַעַן וַיָּבֹאוּ עַד־חָרָן וַיֵּשְׁבוּ שָׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach Terach et-Avram beno ve'et-Lot ben-Haran ben-beno ve'et Sarai kallato eshet Avram beno vayyetze'u ittam me'Ur Kasdim lalekhet artzah Kena'an vayyavo'u ad-Charan vayyeshvu sham.
verses.study_note: The journey begins — but incompletely. Terah leads the family out of Ur toward Canaan, but they stop at Haran (a city in northern Mesopotamia, modern Turkey). Acts 7:2-4 reveals that God had already called Abram while still in Ur (before Gen 12:1). The family set out for Canaan but settled in Haran — another center of moon-god worship, like Ur. Partial obedience is not full obedience. 'They dwelt there' — the same phrase used of the Babel builders (v.2). Haran became a comfortable compromise.
verses.doctrinal_note: Partial obedience is a recurring human pattern. God called Abram to Canaan, but the family stopped halfway. It would take Terah's death (v.32) and God's renewed call (12:1) to complete the journey. God is patient with our partial steps of faith, but He does not lower His calling.

verses.kjv_text: And the days of Terah were two hundred and five years: and Terah died in Haran.
verses.original_text: וַיִּהְיוּ יְמֵי־תֶרַח חָמֵשׁ שָׁנִים וּמָאתַיִם שָׁנָה וַיָּמָת תֶּרַח בְּחָרָן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyihyu yemei-Terach chamesh shanim umataim shanah vayyamot Terach beCharan.
verses.study_note: Terah dies at 205 in Haran — he never reached Canaan. His death clears the way for Abram's full obedience in chapter 12. Acts 7:4 specifically notes that Abram left Haran 'after his father died.' Terah's life ends in a city named after his deceased son, worshipping false gods (Josh 24:2), having stopped short of the Promised Land. He is a picture of religious intent without completed faith. The chapter that began with all humanity united in rebellion (Babel) ends with one man poised to step out in faith — from death and idolatry into the promises of the living God.
verses.doctrinal_note: Genesis 11 closes the primeval history with both judgment and hope. Babel shows humanity's worst; Abram's call shows God's grace. The entire narrative arc — creation, fall, flood, Babel — has been narrowing toward this moment: one man, called from paganism, through whom God will bless every nation that Babel scattered. Chapter 12 opens with the most important words since 'In the beginning' — 'Now the LORD had said unto Abram, Get thee out.

meta.overview: Genesis 11 contains two sections of monumental importance. First, the Tower of Babel narrative (vv.1-9) explains how humanity's unified rebellion against God's command to 'fill the earth' resulted in the confusion of languages and the scattering of nations — the event previewed in chapter 10. Second, the genealogy from Shem to Abram (vv.10-32) traces ten generations of the covenant line, narrowing from all nations to one man through whom 'all families of the earth' would be blessed. The chapter moves from the widest lens (all humanity united in rebellion) to the narrowest focus (one man, Abram, in one city, Ur). It is the hinge between primeval history (Gen 1-11) and patriarchal history (Gen 12-50).
meta.theme: Human Rebellion & Divine Election
meta.keyWordMeaning: To confuse, confound, mix — God confused the languages at Babel, scattering humanity across the earth.
outline: The Tower of Babel (vv.1-4): One language, one project — humanity's unified rebellion
God's Judgment (vv.5-9): The LORD comes down, confuses languages, scatters the nations
The Line of Shem (vv.10-26): Ten generations from Shem to Abram — the covenant genealogy
