Abraham's immediate, lavish response reflects both the cultural imperative of hospitality and, potentially, a recognition that these are no ordinary travelers. Hebrews 13:2 alludes to this episode: "some have entertained angels unawares."

verses.kjv_text: And said, My LORD, if now I have found favour in thy sight, pass not away, I pray thee, from thy servant:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמַר אֲדֹנָי אִם־נָא מָצָאתִי חֵן בְּעֵינֶיךָ אַל־נָא תַעֲבֹר מֵעַל עַבְדֶּךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomar Adonai im-na matzati chen be'einekha al-na ta'avor me'al avdekha.
verses.study_note: Abraham addresses the lead visitor as "Adonai" — in the Hebrew text this is written with the special vowel pointing reserved for God (as distinct from "adoni," a respectful address to a human superior). This indicates Abraham recognized, at least on some level, the divine nature of this visitor. His plea — "do not pass by" — reflects the ancient understanding that God's presence is the greatest blessing and its departure the greatest loss. Abraham calls himself "your servant" (avdekha), the posture of covenant relationship.

verses.kjv_text: Let a little water, I pray you, be fetched, and wash your feet, and rest yourselves under the tree:
verses.original_text: יֻקַּח־נָא מְעַט־מַיִם וְרַחֲצוּ רַגְלֵיכֶם וְהִשָּׁעֲנוּ תַּחַת הָעֵץ׃
verses.transliteration: Yukkach-na me'at-mayim verachatzu ragleikhem vehisha'anu tachat ha'etz.
verses.study_note: Foot-washing was the first act of hospitality in the dusty ancient Near East — essential for comfort after travel on unpaved roads in sandals. Abraham offers "a little water" with characteristic understatement, as he is about to prepare a lavish feast. The "tree" (likely a terebinth or oak of Mamre) provided shade in the heat. Jesus would later take the servant's role of washing feet (John 13:5), transforming this cultural practice into a model of divine service.

verses.kjv_text: And I will fetch a morsel of bread, and comfort ye your hearts; after that ye shall pass on: for therefore are ye come to your servant. And they said, So do, as thou hast said.
verses.original_text: וְאֶקְחָה פַת־לֶחֶם וְסַעֲדוּ לִבְּכֶם אַחַר תַּעֲבֹרוּ כִּי־עַל־כֵּן עֲבַרְתֶּם עַל־עַבְדְּכֶם וַיֹּאמְרוּ כֵּן תַּעֲשֶׂה כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְתָּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'ekchah fat-lechem vesa'adu libbkhem achar ta'avoru ki-al-ken avarttem al-avdekhem vayomeru ken ta'aseh ka'asher dibbarta.
verses.study_note: Abraham offers "a morsel of bread" — this classic understatement is followed by an extravagant feast (vv.6-8). This rhetorical modesty was characteristic of Ancient Near Eastern hospitality: promise little, deliver much. "Comfort your hearts" (sa'adu libbkhem) — bread sustains the inner being. The visitors' consent — "So do, as thou hast said" — is remarkably simple. God accepts Abraham's hospitality, a stunning picture of divine condescension.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham hastened into the tent unto Sarah, and said, Make ready quickly three measures of fine meal, knead it, and make cakes upon the hearth.
verses.original_text: וַיְמַהֵר אַבְרָהָם הָאֹהֱלָה אֶל־שָׂרָה וַיֹּאמֶר מַהֲרִי שְׁלֹשׁ סְאִים קֶמַח סֹלֶת לוּשִׁי וַעֲשִׂי עֻגוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayemaher Avraham ha'ohelah el-Sarah vayomer mahari shelosh se'im kemach solet lushi va'asi ugot.
verses.study_note: Three measures (se'ah) of fine flour was an enormous amount — roughly 22 liters or about 6 gallons. This would make bread for a small village, not three travelers. Abraham's urgency is emphasized: he "hastened" (vayemaher) and tells Sarah to act "quickly" (mahari). The entire household is mobilized — Sarah bakes, Abraham selects cattle, a servant prepares it. The finest ingredients — "solet" (fine flour) was the premium grade, used later for temple offerings (Leviticus 2:1).

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham ran unto the herd, and fetcht a calf tender and good, and gave it unto a young man; and he hasted to dress it.
verses.original_text: וְאֶל־הַבָּקָר רָץ אַבְרָהָם וַיִּקַּח בֶּן־בָּקָר רַךְ וָטוֹב וַיִּתֵּן אֶל־הַנַּעַר וַיְמַהֵר לַעֲשׂוֹת אֹתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'el-habakar ratz Avraham vayyikkach ben-bakar rakh vatov vayyitten el-hana'ar vayemaher la'asot oto.
verses.study_note: Abraham runs again — a 99-year-old patriarch repeatedly running is remarkable and deliberate in the narrative. He personally selects the calf — "tender and good" (rakh vatov), the choicest of the herd. A whole calf for three guests is extraordinarily generous. The "young man" (na'ar) who prepares it may be a servant, or possibly Ishmael. Every action word conveys urgency: ran, fetched, hastened.

verses.kjv_text: And he took butter, and milk, and the calf which he had dressed, and set it before them; and he stood by them under the tree, and they did eat.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח חֶמְאָה וְחָלָב וּבֶן־הַבָּקָר אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה וַיִּתֵּן לִפְנֵיהֶם וְהוּא־עֹמֵד עֲלֵיהֶם תַּחַת הָעֵץ וַיֹּאכֵלוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikkach chem'ah vechalav uven-habakar asher asah vayyitten lifneihem vehu-omed aleihem tachat ha'etz vayokhelu.
verses.study_note: The meal is lavish: curds (chem'ah), milk (chalav), and the prepared calf — dairy and meat together, which later Mosaic law would separate (Exodus 23:19). This pre-law feast underscores the patriarchal setting. Abraham stands while they eat — the host serving as a personal attendant was the highest honor. Most astonishing: "they did eat" (vayokhelu) — the LORD and two angels consumed physical food. This anticipates the resurrected Christ eating with His disciples (Luke 24:41-43).
verses.doctrinal_note: The incarnational foreshadowing here is profound: God takes human form, sits under a tree, and eats a meal prepared by human hands. This is not metaphorical — the text states plainly "they did eat." The God who needs nothing graciously receives from His creature.

verses.kjv_text: And they said unto him, Where is Sarah thy wife? And he said, Behold, in the tent.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֵלָיו אַיֵּה שָׂרָה אִשְׁתֶּךָ וַיֹּאמֶר הִנֵּה בָאֹהֶל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomeru elav ayyeh Sarah ishtekha vayomer hinneh va'ohel.
verses.study_note: The visitors ask for Sarah by her new name (changed from Sarai in 17:15) — they already know it, though this is their first encounter. The question is not for information but to direct attention to the coming announcement. That the visitors know Sarah's name reveals their supernatural knowledge. Sarah is "in the tent" — culturally appropriate for a woman during a male guest visit, but also positioning her to overhear the divine promise.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, I will certainly return unto thee according to the time of life; and, lo, Sarah thy wife shall have a son. And Sarah heard it in the tent door, which was behind him.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר שׁוֹב אָשׁוּב אֵלֶיךָ כָּעֵת חַיָּה וְהִנֵּה־בֵן לְשָׂרָה אִשְׁתֶּךָ וְשָׂרָה שֹׁמַעַת פֶּתַח הָאֹהֶל וְהוּא אַחֲרָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer shov ashuv elekha ka'et chayyah vehineh-ven leSarah ishtekha veSarah shoma'at petach ha'ohel vehu acharav.
verses.study_note: "I will certainly return" (shov ashuv) — the emphatic doubling guarantees fulfillment. "According to the time of life" (ka'et chayyah) means approximately a year from now — the natural gestation period. The promise is specific: Sarah — not a concubine, not Hagar — "shall have a son." Sarah is listening at the tent door, positioned behind the speaker. The narrative carefully notes her eavesdropping because her response will become the central dialogue.
verses.doctrinal_note: The specificity of divine promise: God names the mother (Sarah), the timeframe (within a year), and the result (a son). God's promises are not vague hopes but precise declarations. This is the third and most specific announcement of Isaac's birth (see also 15:4 and 17:19).

verses.kjv_text: Now Abraham and Sarah were old and well stricken in age; and it ceased to be with Sarah after the manner of women.
verses.original_text: וְאַבְרָהָם וְשָׂרָה זְקֵנִים בָּאִים בַּיָּמִים חָדַל לִהְיוֹת לְשָׂרָה אֹרַח כַּנָּשִׁים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'Avraham veSarah zekenim ba'im bayyamim chadal lihyot leSarah orach kannashim.
verses.study_note: The narrator provides the biological reality: both were elderly, and Sarah was past menopause. "It ceased to be with Sarah after the manner of women" is a delicate euphemism for the end of her reproductive capacity. This statement makes clear that Isaac's conception will be miraculous — a supernatural act of God, not a natural occurrence. The parallel to the virgin birth is evident: God specializes in bringing life where nature says it is impossible.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore Sarah laughed within herself, saying, After I am waxed old shall I have pleasure, my lord being old also?
verses.original_text: וַתִּצְחַק שָׂרָה בְּקִרְבָּהּ לֵאמֹר אַחֲרֵי בְלֹתִי הָיְתָה־לִּי עֶדְנָה וַאדֹנִי זָקֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Vatitschak Sarah bekirbah lemor acharei veloti hayetah-li ednah va'adoni zaken.
verses.study_note: Sarah's laugh (tsachaq) becomes the very name of her promised son — Isaac (Yitzhak, "he laughs"). Her laugh is internal ("within herself"), yet God perceives it. "After I am waxed old" (acharei veloti) — she acknowledges her physical deterioration. "Shall I have pleasure" (ednah, related to "Eden") — a frank acknowledgment that conception requires physical capacity she no longer has. She calls Abraham "my lord" (adoni) — a term of respect that Peter later commends (1 Peter 3:6). The honesty of her doubt is not condemned but addressed with a question, not a rebuke.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Abraham, Wherefore did Sarah laugh, saying, Shall I of a surety bear a child, which am old?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־אַבְרָהָם לָמָּה זֶּה צָחֲקָה שָׂרָה לֵאמֹר הַאַף אֻמְנָם אֵלֵד וַאֲנִי זָקַנְתִּי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHWH el-Avraham lammah zeh tzachakah Sarah lemor ha'af umnam eled va'ani zakanti.
verses.study_note: The LORD speaks directly — confirming His identity. He perceived Sarah's internal, silent laugh. God's question is not for information but for conviction. Note the divine sensitivity: the LORD addresses Abraham, not Sarah directly, maintaining her dignity. God also slightly softens Sarah's words — she had mentioned Abraham's age too ("my lord being old"), but God only quotes her reference to her own age. This gentle editing protects Abraham's dignity and keeps the focus on faith, not marital dynamics.

verses.kjv_text: Is any thing too hard for the LORD? At the time appointed I will return unto thee, according to the time of life, and Sarah shall have a son.
verses.original_text: הֲיִפָּלֵא מֵיְהוָה דָּבָר לַמּוֹעֵד אָשׁוּב אֵלֶיךָ כָּעֵת חַיָּה וּלְשָׂרָה בֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Hayippale meYHWH davar lamo'ed ashuv elekha ka'et chayyah uleSarah ven.
verses.study_note: One of the most quoted verses in the Old Testament. "Is any thing too hard (yippale) for the LORD?" — the word "pala" means to be extraordinary, wonderful, beyond human capacity. It is the same root as "wonderful" in Isaiah 9:6 describing the Messiah. The question expects the answer "No!" — nothing is beyond God's power. The angel Gabriel echoes this exact principle to Mary: "For with God nothing shall be impossible" (Luke 1:37). This verse is the hinge of the chapter — it answers Sarah's doubt, confirms the promise, and establishes a foundational truth about God's nature.
verses.doctrinal_note: The omnipotence of God: This question — "Is any thing too hard for the LORD?" — is the theological backbone of the entire Bible. It undergirds creation (Gen 1), the Exodus, the virgin birth, the resurrection, and the new creation. Jeremiah echoes it in his prayer: "there is nothing too hard for thee" (Jer 32:17), and God responds by quoting Genesis 18:14 back to him: "Is there any thing too hard for me?" (Jer 32:27).

verses.kjv_text: Then Sarah denied, saying, I laughed not; for she was afraid. And he said, Nay; but thou didst laugh.
verses.original_text: וַתְּכַחֵשׁ שָׂרָה לֵאמֹר לֹא צָחַקְתִּי כִּי יָרֵאָה וַיֹּאמֶר לֹא כִּי צָחָקְתְּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vatekachesh Sarah lemor lo tzachakti ki yare'ah vayomer lo ki tzachaqt.
verses.study_note: Sarah's fear-driven denial is met with God's gentle but firm correction: "Nay; but thou didst laugh" (lo ki tzachaqt). God does not thunderously condemn — He simply states the truth. This is grace-filled confrontation. The Hebrew is strikingly brief and direct. Sarah's denial ("I laughed not") parallels Peter's denial ("I know not the man") — fear causes God's people to deny what is true. Yet God does not reject Sarah; within the year, she will hold Isaac and her laughter will be transformed from doubt to delight (Gen 21:6).

verses.kjv_text: And the men rose up from thence, and looked toward Sodom: and Abraham went with them to bring them on the way.
verses.original_text: וַיָּקֻמוּ מִשָּׁם הָאֲנָשִׁים וַיַּשְׁקִפוּ עַל־פְּנֵי סְדֹם וְאַבְרָהָם הֹלֵךְ עִמָּם לְשַׁלְּחָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyakumu misham ha'anashim vayyashkifu al-penei Sedom ve'Avraham holekh immam leshallecham.
verses.study_note: A dramatic transition. The visitors rise and "look toward Sodom" — the Hebrew word "vayashkifu" (looked down upon) implies they were on elevated ground, gazing toward the Jordan Valley where Sodom lay. The verb carries overtones of divine surveillance and coming judgment. Abraham accompanies them — "to bring them on the way" (leshallecham) was the final act of hospitality: escorting guests part of their journey. This walk becomes the setting for one of Scripture's most intimate divine-human conversations.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said, Shall I hide from Abraham that thing which I do;
verses.original_text: וַיהוָה אָמָר הַמְכַסֶּה אֲנִי מֵאַבְרָהָם אֲשֶׁר אֲנִי עֹשֶׂה׃
verses.transliteration: VaYHWH amar hamekhasseh ani me'Avraham asher ani oseh.
verses.study_note: A stunning moment — God deliberates aloud about whether to share His plans with Abraham. The rhetorical question implies "No, I will not hide this." God treats Abraham as a confidant, a friend (cf. Isaiah 41:8, James 2:23). This divine "thinking aloud" reveals that God desires relationship, not merely obedience. The pattern of God revealing His plans to His servants before acting becomes a prophetic principle: "Surely the Lord GOD will do nothing, but he revealeth his secret unto his servants the prophets" (Amos 3:7).
verses.doctrinal_note: God's transparency with Abraham establishes the prophetic principle: God does not act in judgment without first warning through His servants. This is the basis of all prophetic ministry and demonstrates that God's justice is never arbitrary or capricious.

verses.kjv_text: Seeing that Abraham shall surely become a great and mighty nation, and all the nations of the earth shall be blessed in him?
verses.original_text: וְאַבְרָהָם הָיוֹ יִהְיֶה לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל וְעָצוּם וְנִבְרְכוּ בוֹ כֹּל גּוֹיֵי הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'Avraham hayo yihyeh legoy gadol ve'atsum venivrekhu vo kol goyei ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: God recalls the Abrahamic promise (Gen 12:2-3) as the reason for transparency. Abraham's role is global — "all the nations of the earth shall be blessed in him." This universal scope is crucial: God's judgment on Sodom is not vindictive but part of His redemptive plan for all nations. Paul cites this very verse as the "gospel preached beforehand to Abraham" (Galatians 3:8). The promise of universal blessing through Abraham ultimately finds its fulfillment in Christ.

verses.kjv_text: For I know him, that he will command his children and his household after him, and they shall keep the way of the LORD, to do justice and judgment; that the LORD may bring upon Abraham that which he hath spoken of him.
verses.original_text: כִּי יְדַעְתִּיו לְמַעַן אֲשֶׁר יְצַוֶּה אֶת־בָּנָיו וְאֶת־בֵּיתוֹ אַחֲרָיו וְשָׁמְרוּ דֶּרֶךְ יְהוָה לַעֲשׂוֹת צְדָקָה וּמִשְׁפָּט לְמַעַן הָבִיא יְהוָה עַל־אַבְרָהָם אֵת אֲשֶׁר־דִּבֶּר עָלָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Ki yedativ lema'an asher yetsavveh et-banav ve'et-beito acharav veshameru derekh YHWH la'asot tsedaqah umishpat lema'an havi YHWH al-Avraham et asher-dibber alav.
verses.study_note: "I know him" (yedativ) — the Hebrew "yada" denotes intimate relational knowledge, not merely intellectual awareness. God has chosen Abraham, and this choice comes with purpose: Abraham will "command his children and his household" — the mandate to transmit faith generationally. The content of this transmission: "the way of the LORD" (derekh YHWH), "justice" (tsedaqah), and "judgment" (mishpat). This verse is the foundation of Deuteronomy 6:4-9 and all biblical teaching on family discipleship. The ironic context: God is about to judge Sodom for injustice, while Abraham is called to model justice.
verses.doctrinal_note: Generational faithfulness: God's covenant purpose includes the formation of godly households and communities. Abraham's calling is not merely personal but familial and ultimately global. Faith is meant to be transmitted — "commanded" — from generation to generation. The way of the LORD is defined as doing justice (tsedaqah) and judgment (mishpat) — the ethical fruit of covenant relationship.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said, Because the cry of Sodom and Gomorrah is great, and because their sin is very grievous;
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה זַעֲקַת סְדֹם וַעֲמֹרָה כִּי־רָבָּה וְחַטָּאתָם כִּי כָבְדָה מְאֹד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHWH za'aqat Sedom va'Amorah ki-rabbah vechattatam ki khavedah me'od.
verses.study_note: The "cry" (za'aqat) of Sodom — this same word describes the cry of the oppressed in Egypt (Exodus 3:7) and Abel's blood crying from the ground (Gen 4:10). Sin produces a cry that ascends to God. The term implies the outcry of victims, not merely the noise of wickedness. "Very grievous" (khavedah me'od) — literally "exceedingly heavy." God's judgment is not capricious but responsive to accumulated, persistent, extreme evil.

verses.kjv_text: I will go down now, and see whether they have done altogether according to the cry of it, which is come unto me; and if not, I will know.
verses.original_text: אֵרֲדָה־נָּא וְאֶרְאֶה הַכְּצַעֲקָתָהּ הַבָּאָה אֵלַי עָשׂוּ כָּלָה וְאִם־לֹא אֵדָעָה׃
verses.transliteration: Eredah-na ve'er'eh hakketsa'aqatah habba'ah elai asu khalah ve'im-lo eda'ah.
verses.study_note: "I will go down and see" — anthropomorphic language portraying God as a just Judge who investigates before judging. God does not condemn on rumor but verifies firsthand. This models due process and is echoed in Genesis 11:5 (Tower of Babel). The statement is for Abraham's benefit — God already knows the truth. It demonstrates that divine judgment is always informed, fair, and never hasty. "Khalah" (altogether/completely) — God investigates whether the corruption is total.

verses.kjv_text: And the men turned their faces from thence, and went toward Sodom: but Abraham stood yet before the LORD.
verses.original_text: וַיִּפְנוּ מִשָּׁם הָאֲנָשִׁים וַיֵּלְכוּ סְדֹמָה וְאַבְרָהָם עוֹדֶנּוּ עֹמֵד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyifnu misham ha'anashim vayyelechu Sedomah ve'Avraham odenu omed lifnei YHWH.
verses.study_note: The two angels depart toward Sodom (they arrive in 19:1), while Abraham remains standing before the LORD. "Standing before" (omed lifnei) is the posture of a servant before a king, or a priest ministering before God. An ancient scribal tradition (one of the "Tiqqune Sopherim") notes that the original may have read "the LORD stood yet before Abraham" — suggesting God lingered, inviting Abraham to intercede. Whether or not that is the case, this moment becomes the setting for one of the Bible's greatest prayers.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham drew near, and said, Wilt thou also destroy the righteous with the wicked?
verses.original_text: וַיִּגַּשׁ אַבְרָהָם וַיֹּאמַר הַאַף תִּסְפֶּה צַדִּיק עִם־רָשָׁע׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiggash Avraham vayomar ha'af tispeh tsaddiq im-rasha.
verses.study_note: Abraham "draws near" (vayyiggash) — a bold act of approaching God as an intercessor. His question strikes at the heart of theodicy: will God destroy the righteous alongside the wicked? This is not irreverence but covenant boldness — Abraham appeals to God's own character. The question assumes that righteous people may exist within Sodom (Abraham is thinking of Lot). This verse inaugurates the great intercession that will negotiate from fifty righteous down to ten.
verses.doctrinal_note: The foundation of intercessory prayer: Abraham approaches God not on the basis of his own merit but on the basis of God's character. All true intercession appeals to who God is, not to who we are. Abraham models what Paul commands: "I exhort therefore, that supplications, prayers, intercessions, and giving of thanks, be made for all men" (1 Timothy 2:1).

verses.kjv_text: Peradventure there be fifty righteous within the city: wilt thou also destroy and not spare the place for the fifty righteous that are therein?
verses.original_text: אוּלַי יֵשׁ חֲמִשִּׁים צַדִּיקִם בְּתוֹךְ הָעִיר הַאַף תִּסְפֶּה וְלֹא־תִשָּׂא לַמָּקוֹם לְמַעַן חֲמִשִּׁים הַצַּדִּיקִם אֲשֶׁר בְּקִרְבָּהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ulai yesh chamishim tsaddiqim betokh ha'ir ha'af tispeh velo-tissa lamaqom lema'an chamishim hatsaddiqim asher bekirbah.
verses.study_note: Abraham begins with fifty — a generous estimate that gives God maximum room. His argument: the presence of righteous people should protect the entire city. This is the principle of corporate preservation through a righteous remnant. "Spare the place" (tissa lamaqom) — literally "lift up" or "forgive the place." Abraham asks not merely that the righteous be evacuated but that the whole city be forgiven for their sake.

verses.kjv_text: That be far from thee to do after this manner, to slay the righteous with the wicked: and that the righteous should be as the wicked, that be far from thee: Shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?
verses.original_text: חָלִלָה לְּךָ מֵעֲשֹׂת כַּדָּבָר הַזֶּה לְהָמִית צַדִּיק עִם־רָשָׁע וְהָיָה כַצַּדִּיק כָּרָשָׁע חָלִלָה לָּךְ הֲשֹׁפֵט כָּל־הָאָרֶץ לֹא יַעֲשֶׂה מִשְׁפָּט׃
verses.transliteration: Chalilah lekha me'asot kaddavar hazzeh lehamit tsaddiq im-rasha vehayah katsaddiq karasha chalilah lakh hashofet kol-ha'aretz lo ya'aseh mishpat.
verses.study_note: The theological climax of Abraham's intercession. "Chalilah" (far be it from thee) — an expression of moral horror, appearing twice for emphasis. Abraham's argument is not sentimental but theological: God's nature requires justice. "Shall not the Judge (shofet) of all the earth do right (mishpat)?" — this question does not doubt God but appeals to His own character. Abraham holds God to God's own standard. This is the first explicit identification of God as universal Judge. The logic: if God treats the righteous and wicked identically, there is no justice — and that would be contrary to God's nature.
verses.doctrinal_note: The justice of God: Abraham's argument establishes that God's actions must be consistent with His character. God cannot treat the righteous and wicked identically without violating His own nature. This principle undergirds all biblical theology: God is both merciful AND just. The ultimate answer to Abraham's question is the Cross — where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Romans 3:25-26).

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said, If I find in Sodom fifty righteous within the city, then I will spare all the place for their sakes.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אִם־אֶמְצָא בִסְדֹם חֲמִשִּׁים צַדִּיקִם בְּתוֹךְ הָעִיר וְנָשָׂאתִי לְכָל־הַמָּקוֹם בַּעֲבוּרָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHWH im-emtsa viSdom chamishim tsaddiqim betokh ha'ir venasati lekhol-hamaqom ba'avuram.
verses.study_note: God agrees immediately — He does not resist or argue. Fifty righteous would be enough to spare the entire city. "For their sakes" (ba'avuram) — the righteous serve as a shield for the wicked. This principle — the many preserved by the few — reaches its ultimate expression in the one Righteous Man, Christ, whose righteousness covers all who are in Him (Romans 5:19). God's willingness here reveals His desire to show mercy rather than judgment.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham answered and said, Behold now, I have taken upon me to speak unto the LORD, which am but dust and ashes:
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַן אַבְרָהָם וַיֹּאמַר הִנֵּה־נָא הוֹאַלְתִּי לְדַבֵּר אֶל־אֲדֹנָי וְאָנֹכִי עָפָר וָאֵפֶר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'an Avraham vayomar hinneh-na ho'alti ledabber el-Adonai ve'anokhi afar va'efer.
verses.study_note: Abraham's humility intensifies as his boldness increases. "Dust and ashes" (afar va'efer) — dust, the material of human creation (Gen 2:7), and ashes, the residue of destruction. Abraham holds both realities simultaneously: he is nothing before God, yet dares to speak. This is the paradox of true prayer — utter humility combined with bold access. Jesus teaches this same tension: pray to the Father in heaven (transcendence) yet call Him "Abba" (intimacy).

verses.kjv_text: Peradventure there shall lack five of the fifty righteous: wilt thou destroy all the city for lack of five? And he said, If I find there forty and five, I will not destroy it.
verses.original_text: אוּלַי יַחְסְרוּן חֲמִשִּׁים הַצַּדִּיקִם חֲמִשָּׁה הֲתַשְׁחִית בַּחֲמִשָּׁה אֶת־כָּל־הָעִיר וַיֹּאמֶר לֹא אַשְׁחִית אִם־אֶמְצָא שָׁם אַרְבָּעִים וַחֲמִשָּׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Ulai yachserun chamishim hatsaddiqim chamishah hataschit bachamishah et-kol-ha'ir vayomer lo aschit im-emtsa sham arba'im vachamishah.
verses.study_note: Abraham's negotiation strategy: a modest reduction of only five. He does not leap from fifty to ten but inches downward respectfully. His argument subtly shifts — "for lack of five" implies it would be unjust to destroy a city over so small a difference. God agrees without hesitation. The pattern of immediate divine consent reveals that God is more inclined to mercy than Abraham even dares to hope.

verses.kjv_text: And he spake unto him yet again, and said, Peradventure there shall be forty found there. And he said, I will not do it for forty’s sake.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּסֶף עוֹד לְדַבֵּר אֵלָיו וַיֹּאמַר אוּלַי יִמָּצְאוּן שָׁם אַרְבָּעִים וַיֹּאמֶר לֹא אֶעֱשֶׂה בַּעֲבוּר הָאַרְבָּעִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyosef od ledabber elav vayomar ulai yimmatse'un sham arba'im vayomer lo e'eseh ba'avur ha'arba'im.
verses.study_note: Forty righteous. Abraham continues his descent, now dropping by ten. Each time, the answer comes swiftly and positively. The repetitive structure builds dramatic tension — how far will Abraham dare to go? How far will God's mercy extend? The literary pattern emphasizes God's patience: He never shows irritation, never cuts Abraham off, never rebukes his persistence.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto him, Oh let not the LORD be angry, and I will speak: Peradventure there shall thirty be found there. And he said, I will not do it, if I find thirty there.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אַל־נָא יִחַר לַאדֹנָי וַאֲדַבֵּרָה אוּלַי יִמָּצְאוּן שָׁם שְׁלֹשִׁים וַיֹּאמֶר לֹא אֶעֱשֶׂה אִם־אֶמְצָא שָׁם שְׁלֹשִׁים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer al-na yichar laAdonai va'adabberah ulai yimmatse'un sham sheloshim vayomer lo e'eseh im-emtsa sham sheloshim.
verses.study_note: "Oh let not the Lord be angry" (al-na yichar) — Abraham acknowledges that his persistence risks provoking divine displeasure, yet presses on anyway. This is the courage of intercession — persisting even when it feels presumptuous. Thirty righteous. God agrees again. Each concession reveals more of God's merciful character and encourages Abraham to continue.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Behold now, I have taken upon me to speak unto the LORD: Peradventure there shall be twenty found there. And he said, I will not destroy it for twenty’s sake.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר הִנֵּה־נָא הוֹאַלְתִּי לְדַבֵּר אֶל־אֲדֹנָי אוּלַי יִמָּצְאוּן שָׁם עֶשְׂרִים וַיֹּאמֶר לֹא אַשְׁחִית בַּעֲבוּר הָעֶשְׂרִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer hinneh-na ho'alti ledabber el-Adonai ulai yimmatse'un sham esrim vayomer lo aschit ba'avur ha'esrim.
verses.study_note: Twenty righteous. Abraham repeats his self-humbling phrase from v.27: "I have taken upon me to speak." Each repetition both acknowledges his audacity and steels his courage for the next request. The numbers are falling rapidly now: 50 → 45 → 40 → 30 → 20. God's patience has not wavered once.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Oh let not the LORD be angry, and I will speak yet but this once: Peradventure ten shall be found there. And he said, I will not destroy it for ten’s sake.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אַל־נָא יִחַר לַאדֹנָי וַאֲדַבְּרָה אַךְ־הַפַּעַם אוּלַי יִמָּצְאוּן שָׁם עֲשָׂרָה וַיֹּאמֶר לֹא אַשְׁחִית בַּעֲבוּר הָעֲשָׂרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer al-na yichar laAdonai va'adabberah akh-happa'am ulai yimmatse'un sham asarah vayomer lo aschit ba'avur ha'asarah.
verses.study_note: "Yet but this once" (akh-happa'am) — Abraham signals this is his final request. He stops at ten. Why not five? Why not one? Perhaps because ten was the minimum for a Jewish congregation (minyan) in later tradition. Or perhaps Abraham assumed that Lot's family — Lot, his wife, their daughters, and sons-in-law — would constitute at least ten righteous. Tragically, when the angels arrive, not even ten can be found. God agrees: ten righteous would save the entire city. The negotiation reveals that God's mercy exceeds even Abraham's boldest imagination.
verses.doctrinal_note: The intercession reveals a profound theology: (1) God is approachable. (2) God welcomes persistent prayer. (3) God is more merciful than we dare ask. (4) The righteous serve as a preserving presence for the wicked. (5) Even so, there is a threshold beyond which judgment must fall. The great tragedy of Sodom is not that God judged, but that not even ten righteous could be found in an entire city.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD went his way, as soon as he had left communing with Abraham: and Abraham returned unto his place.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּלֶךְ יְהוָה כַּאֲשֶׁר כִּלָּה לְדַבֵּר אֶל־אַבְרָהָם וְאַבְרָהָם שָׁב לִמְקֹמוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyelekh YHWH ka'asher killah ledabber el-Avraham ve'Avraham shav limkomo.
verses.study_note: The LORD departs when He has "finished speaking" (killah ledabber) — God, not Abraham, determines when the conversation ends. Abraham "returned to his place" (shav limkomo) — back to Mamre, back to waiting, back to trusting. The narrative creates suspense: Will ten righteous be found? Chapter 19 will provide the devastating answer. Abraham has done all he can. The intercessor has spoken; now the Judge will act. The chapter closes as quietly as it opened — yet everything has changed. God has reaffirmed His promise, revealed His justice, and shown that He hears the prayers of His friend.

meta.overview: Genesis 18 is one of the most dramatic chapters in Scripture, containing a theophany (appearance of God in human form), the confirmation of Isaac's birth, and one of the Bible's greatest examples of intercessory prayer. The LORD appears to Abraham at Mamre as one of three visitors, receives his lavish hospitality, and reaffirms that Sarah will bear a son within a year. When Sarah laughs in disbelief, God responds with the foundational question: "Is any thing too hard for the LORD?" The chapter climaxes with Abraham boldly interceding for Sodom, negotiating from fifty righteous down to ten — a profound revelation of God's justice, mercy, and willingness to hear prayer.
meta.theme: Theophany, Promise & Intercession
meta.keyWordMeaning: Is anything too hard/wonderful? — From "pala" meaning to be extraordinary, beyond human capacity. The rhetorical question that defines the chapter (v.14).
outline: The LORD Appears at Mamre (vv.1-5): Three visitors arrive; Abraham's lavish hospitality
The Meal & Promise Renewed (vv.6-8): Abraham serves; the LORD confirms Isaac
Sarah Laughs — Is Anything Too Hard? (vv.9-15): Doubt meets divine omniscience
The LORD Reveals His Plan for Sodom (vv.16-21): God's deliberation and Abraham's calling
Abraham Intercedes for Sodom (vv.22-33): The great negotiation — from fifty to ten

verses.kjv_text: And there came two angels to Sodom at even; and Lot sat in the gate of Sodom: and Lot seeing them rose up to meet them; and he bowed himself with his face toward the ground;
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹאוּ שְׁנֵי הַמַּלְאָכִים סְדֹמָה בָּעֶרֶב וְלוֹט יֹשֵׁב בְּשַׁעַר־סְדֹם וַיַּרְא־לוֹט וַיָּקָם לִקְרָאתָם וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ אַפַּיִם אָרְצָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayavo'u shenei hammal'akhim Sedomah ba'erev veLot yoshev besha'ar-Sedom vayar-Lot vayyakam likratam vayishtachu appayim artzah.
verses.study_note: The two visitors who left Abraham (18:22) arrive at Sodom in the evening. They are now explicitly called 'angels' (mal'akhim — messengers). Lot sits 'in the gate' — the place of civic authority and legal proceedings. This shows Lot has risen to prominence in Sodom's governance, a troubling detail given the city's character. Lot's hospitality mirrors Abraham's (ch.18) but with critical differences: Abraham ran, Lot merely rises; Abraham was at his tent (separate), Lot is at the city gate (integrated).

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Behold now, my lords, turn in, I pray you, into your servant’s house, and tarry all night, and wash your feet, and ye shall rise up early, and go on your ways. And they said, Nay; but we will abide in the street all night.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר הִנֶּה נָּא־אֲדֹנַי סוּרוּ נָא אֶל־בֵּית עַבְדְּכֶם וְלִינוּ וְרַחֲצוּ רַגְלֵיכֶם וְהִשְׁכַּמְתֶּם וַהֲלַכְתֶּם לְדַרְכְּכֶם וַיֹּאמְרוּ לֹּא כִּי בָרְחוֹב נָלִין׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer hinneh na-adonai suru na el-beit avdekhem velinu verachatzu ragleikhem vehishkamtem vahalakhtem ledarkkekhem vayomeru lo ki varechov nalin.
verses.study_note: Lot offers hospitality — foot-washing, lodging, an early departure. But the angels initially refuse: 'we will abide in the street' (varechov nalin). This refusal may test Lot's sincerity, or it may serve to expose Sodom's danger. Lot addresses them as 'adonai' (my lords) — the human plural form, unlike Abraham's divine singular. Lot knows the streets of Sodom are unsafe at night — his urgency reflects this awareness.

verses.kjv_text: And he pressed upon them greatly; and they turned in unto him, and entered into his house; and he made them a feast, and did bake unleavened bread, and they did eat.
verses.original_text: וַיִּפְצַר־בָּם מְאֹד וַיָּסֻרוּ אֵלָיו וַיָּבֹאוּ אֶל־בֵּיתוֹ וַיַּעַשׂ לָהֶם מִשְׁתֶּה וּמַצּוֹת אָפָה וַיֹּאכֵלוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiftsar-bam me'od vayyasuru elav vayavo'u el-beto vayya'as lahem mishteh umatzot afah vayokhelu.
verses.study_note: Lot 'pressed upon them greatly' (vayyiftsar) — insistent urgency born from knowing Sodom's dangers. His meal is simpler than Abraham's feast: 'unleavened bread' (matzot) — quick flat bread without time for leavening. This is the first mention of matzot in Scripture, which later becomes central to the Passover (Exodus 12). The contrast with Abraham's elaborate feast (18:6-8) subtly reflects Lot's diminished circumstances despite his civic position.

verses.kjv_text: But before they lay down, the men of the city, even the men of Sodom, compassed the house round, both old and young, all the people from every quarter:
verses.original_text: טֶרֶם יִשְׁכָּבוּ וְאַנְשֵׁי הָעִיר אַנְשֵׁי סְדֹם נָסַבּוּ עַל־הַבַּיִת מִנַּעַר וְעַד־זָקֵן כָּל־הָעָם מִקָּצֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Terem yishkavu ve'anshei ha'ir anshei Sedom nasabbu al-habbayit minna'ar ve'ad-zaken kol-ha'am mikkatzeh.
verses.study_note: The narrator emphasizes the totality of Sodom's corruption: 'both old and young, ALL the people from every quarter.' No demographic is exempt. This detail answers Abraham's intercession in chapter 18 — there were not even ten righteous in the city. The phrase 'mikkatzeh' (from every quarter/from one end to the other) underscores that corruption has saturated the entire population. This is why God's investigation (18:21) concluded in judgment.

verses.kjv_text: And they called unto Lot, and said unto him, Where are the men which came in to thee this night? bring them out unto us, that we may know them.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרְאוּ אֶל־לוֹט וַיֹּאמְרוּ לוֹ אַיֵּה הָאֲנָשִׁים אֲשֶׁר־בָּאוּ אֵלֶיךָ הַלָּיְלָה הוֹצִיאֵם אֵלֵינוּ וְנֵדְעָה אֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikre'u el-Lot vayyomeru lo ayyeh ha'anashim asher-ba'u eleikha hallaylah hotzi'em eleinu venede'ah otam.
verses.study_note: The mob demands the visitors be brought out 'that we may know them' (venede'ah otam). 'Yada' (to know) here unmistakably carries its sexual meaning (as in Gen 4:1). The demand is for gang rape of the visitors — a supreme violation of hospitality and human dignity. Ezekiel 16:49-50 later identifies Sodom's sins as pride, fullness of bread, idleness, neglecting the poor, and 'abomination' — this scene is the culmination, not the totality, of their wickedness.

verses.kjv_text: And Lot went out at the door unto them, and shut the door after him,
verses.original_text: וַיֵּצֵא אֲלֵהֶם לוֹט הַפֶּתְחָה וְהַדֶּלֶת סָגַר אַחֲרָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyetze alehem Lot happetchah vehaddeleth sagar acharav.
verses.study_note: Lot goes out to face the mob alone, closing the door behind him to protect his guests. This shows genuine courage — he places himself between danger and his visitors. Despite his compromised life in Sodom, Lot retains enough moral conviction to defend the stranger, even at great personal risk. Peter describes him as 'righteous Lot, vexed with the filthy conversation of the wicked' (2 Peter 2:7).

verses.kjv_text: And said, I pray you, brethren, do not so wickedly.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמַר אַל־נָא אַחַי תָּרֵעוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomar al-na achai tare'u.
verses.study_note: Lot calls the mob 'brethren' (achai) — he has become part of their community. His plea 'do not so wickedly' (tare'u — from 'ra,' evil) is brief and ineffective. He recognizes the moral evil but has lost the authority to confront it, having chosen to live among them. The word 'tare'u' is the same root as the name 'Sodom' is associated with in prophetic literature — ultimate wickedness.

verses.kjv_text: Behold now, I have two daughters which have not known man; let me, I pray you, bring them out unto you, and do ye to them as is good in your eyes: only unto these men do nothing; for therefore came they under the shadow of my roof.
verses.original_text: הִנֵּה־נָא לִי שְׁתֵּי בָנוֹת אֲשֶׁר לֹא־יָדְעוּ אִישׁ אוֹצִיאָה־נָּא אֶתְהֶן אֲלֵיכֶם וַעֲשׂוּ לָהֶן כַּטּוֹב בְּעֵינֵיכֶם רַק לָאֲנָשִׁים הָאֵל אַל־תַּעֲשׂוּ דָבָר כִּי־עַל־כֵּן בָּאוּ בְּצֵל קֹרָתִי׃
verses.transliteration: Hinneh-na li shetei vanot asher lo-yade'u ish otzi'ah-na ethen aleikhem va'asu lahen kattov be'eineikhem rak la'anashim ha'el al-ta'asu davar ki-al-ken ba'u vetzel korati.
verses.study_note: One of the most disturbing proposals in Scripture. Lot offers his virgin daughters to the mob to protect his guests. The hospitality code in the ancient Near East placed guests under the host's absolute protection — but Lot's 'solution' reveals how deeply Sodom's moral corruption has warped his own judgment. The text records this without approval; the narrative presents it as evidence of the devastating effect of prolonged moral compromise. 'The shadow of my roof' (vetzel korati) invokes the sacred obligation of shelter.

verses.kjv_text: And they said, Stand back. And they said again, This one fellow came in to sojourn, and he will needs be a judge: now will we deal worse with thee, than with them. And they pressed sore upon the man, even Lot, and came near to break the door.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ גֶּשׁ־הָלְאָה וַיֹּאמְרוּ הָאֶחָד בָּא־לָגוּר וַיִּשְׁפֹּט שָׁפוֹט נִלְחֲצָה־בָאִישׁ בְּלוֹט מְאֹד וַיִּגְּשׁוּ לִשְׁבֹּר הַדָּלֶת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru gesh-hal'ah vayyomeru ha'echad ba-lagur vayyishpot shafot nilchatzah-va'ish beLot me'od vayyigshu lishbor haddalet.
verses.study_note: The mob's response exposes Sodom's attitude toward righteousness: 'This one came to sojourn, and he will needs be a judge' (vayyishpot shafot). They reject any moral authority from an outsider. Lot's attempt to judge is met with contempt — the very activity God commended in Abraham (18:19, 'to do justice and judgment') is despised in Sodom. They threaten Lot with worse treatment than the visitors and try to break down the door. Lot's moral authority has been completely eroded by his compromised position.

verses.kjv_text: But the men put forth their hand, and pulled Lot into the house to them, and shut to the door.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁלְחוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים אֶת־יָדָם וַיָּבִיאוּ אֶת־לוֹט אֲלֵיהֶם הַבָּיְתָה וְאֶת־הַדֶּלֶת סָגָרוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishlechu ha'anashim et-yadam vayyavi'u et-Lot aleihem habbayetah ve'et-haddeleth sagaru.
verses.study_note: The angels intervene — pulling Lot inside and shutting the door. Grace rescues what human effort cannot. Lot went out to protect his guests; his guests end up protecting him. This reversal underscores a key theological truth: we do not save God; God saves us. The angels' power to shut the door against the mob foreshadows the supernatural protection that marks the entire rescue.

verses.kjv_text: And they smote the men that were at the door of the house with blindness, both small and great: so that they wearied themselves to find the door.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־הָאֲנָשִׁים אֲשֶׁר־פֶּתַח הַבַּיִת הִכּוּ בַּסַּנְוֵרִים מִקָּטֹן וְעַד־גָּדוֹל וַיִּלְאוּ לִמְצֹא הַפָּתַח׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-ha'anashim asher-petach habbayit hikku bassanverim mikkaton ve'ad-gadol vayyil'u limtzo happetach.
verses.study_note: The angels strike the mob with 'blindness' (sanverim) — a rare word used only here and in 2 Kings 6:18 (when Elisha strikes the Aramean army). This is not ordinary blindness but a disorienting confusion. Remarkably, even after being blinded, the men of Sodom 'wearied themselves to find the door' — they persist in their evil intent. This detail powerfully illustrates the hardened, relentless nature of their wickedness — judgment itself does not produce repentance.

verses.kjv_text: And the men said unto Lot, Hast thou here any besides? son in law, and thy sons, and thy daughters, and whatsoever thou hast in the city, bring them out of this place:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים אֶל־לוֹט עֹד מִי־לְךָ פֹה חָתָן וּבָנֶיךָ וּבְנֹתֶיךָ וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר־לְךָ בָּעִיר הוֹצֵא מִן־הַמָּקוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru ha'anashim el-Lot od mi-lekha foh chatan uvanekha uvenotekha vekhol asher-lekha ba'ir hotze min-hammakom.
verses.study_note: The angels reveal their mission and extend mercy beyond Lot to his entire household — sons-in-law, sons, daughters, 'whatsoever thou hast.' This echoes the household principle of Genesis 17 and 18:19. God's rescue reaches Lot 'for Abraham's sake' (v.29). The urgency of 'bring them out' (hotze) signals imminent destruction.

verses.kjv_text: For we will destroy this place, because the cry of them is waxen great before the face of the LORD; and the LORD hath sent us to destroy it.
verses.original_text: כִּי־מַשְׁחִתִים אֲנַחְנוּ אֶת־הַמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה כִּי־גָדְלָה צַעֲקָתָם אֶת־פְּנֵי יְהוָה וַיְשַׁלְּחֵנוּ יְהוָה לְשַׁחֲתָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ki-mashchitim anachnu et-hammakom hazzeh ki-gadelah tza'akatam et-penei YHVH vayeshallechenu YHVH leshachatah.
verses.study_note: The angels plainly state their mission: 'we will destroy this place.' The 'cry' (tza'akatam) from 18:20-21 has been verified — Sodom's corruption is complete. 'The LORD hath sent us' — they act under divine commission, not independently. The verb 'mashchitim' (we are destroying) is a Hiphil participle indicating imminent action already in process.
verses.doctrinal_note: Angels are instruments of divine judgment. They do not act autonomously but under God's direct commission. This establishes the principle that God's judgment, while delegated through agents, remains God's own act of justice.

verses.kjv_text: And Lot went out, and spake unto his sons in law, which married his daughters, and said, Up, get you out of this place; for the LORD will destroy this city. But he seemed as one that mocked unto his sons in law.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּצֵא לוֹט וַיְדַבֵּר אֶל־חֲתָנָיו לֹקְחֵי בְנֹתָיו וַיֹּאמֶר קוּמוּ צְאוּ מִן־הַמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה כִּי־מַשְׁחִית יְהוָה אֶת־הָעִיר וַיְהִי כִמְצַחֵק בְּעֵינֵי חֲתָנָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyetze Lot vaydabber el-chatanav lokechei venotav vayomer kumu tze'u min-hammakom hazzeh ki-mashchit YHVH et-ha'ir vayehi khimtzachek be'einei chatanav.
verses.study_note: Lot warns his sons-in-law, but 'he seemed as one that mocked' (khimtzachek — from 'tzachak,' to laugh, the same root as Isaac's name). The tragic irony: a man who has compromised with Sodom for years now sounds like a joker when he speaks truth. His long accommodation to sin has destroyed his credibility. This is one of the most poignant illustrations in Scripture of the cost of spiritual compromise — when the moment comes to speak the truth, no one believes you.

verses.kjv_text: And when the morning arose, then the angels hastened Lot, saying, Arise, take thy wife, and thy two daughters, which are here; lest thou be consumed in the iniquity of the city.
verses.original_text: וּכְמוֹ הַשַּׁחַר עָלָה וַיָּאִיצוּ הַמַּלְאָכִים בְּלוֹט לֵאמֹר קוּם קַח אֶת־אִשְׁתְּךָ וְאֶת־שְׁתֵּי בְנֹתֶיךָ הַנִּמְצָאֹת פֶּן־תִּסָּפֶה בַּעֲוֺן הָעִיר׃
verses.transliteration: Ukhemo hashachar alah vayya'itzu hammal'akhim beLot lemor kum kach et-ishtekha ve'et-shetei venotekha hannimtza'ot pen-tissafeh ba'avon ha'ir.
verses.study_note: At dawn, the angels urge Lot with extreme urgency. 'Hastened' (ya'itzu) — pressed, pushed. 'Lest thou be consumed' (pen-tissafeh) — literally 'swept away' in the punishment (avon — iniquity/punishment for iniquity). The Hebrew 'avon' can mean both the sin and its consequences — sin and judgment are inseparable. Only the family members present (hannimtza'ot — 'which are found/here') will be saved; the sons-in-law who mocked are left behind.

verses.kjv_text: And while he lingered, the men laid hold upon his hand, and upon the hand of his wife, and upon the hand of his two daughters; the LORD being merciful unto him: and they brought him forth, and set him without the city.
verses.original_text: וַיִּתְמַהְמָהּ וַיַּחֲזִקוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים בְּיָדוֹ וּבְיַד אִשְׁתּוֹ וּבְיַד שְׁתֵּי בְנֹתָיו בְּחֶמְלַת יְהוָה עָלָיו וַיֹּצִאֻהוּ וַיַּנִּחֻהוּ מִחוּץ לָעִיר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitmamah vayyachaziku ha'anashim beyado uveyad ishto uveyad shetei venotav bechemlat YHVH alav vayyotzi'uhu vayyannichhu michhutz la'ir.
verses.study_note: One of the most theologically loaded verses in the chapter. Lot 'lingered' (vayyitmamah) — he hesitated, delayed. Despite the urgency, he cannot let go of Sodom. So the angels physically 'laid hold' (vayyachaziku — seized, grasped firmly) of his hand, his wife's hand, and his daughters' hands, and literally dragged them out. The narrator gives the reason: 'the LORD being merciful unto him' (bechemlat YHVH — in the compassion/pity of the LORD). Salvation here is entirely by grace — Lot is rescued not because he ran but because he was dragged.
verses.doctrinal_note: Salvation by grace: Lot is the supreme Old Testament illustration of rescue despite reluctance. He did not flee eagerly — he was pulled out. 'The LORD being merciful' (bechemlat YHVH) makes clear that the rescue is rooted in God's compassion, not Lot's merit. This foreshadows the New Testament truth: 'While we were yet sinners, Christ died for us' (Rom 5:8).

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when they had brought them forth abroad, that he said, Escape for thy life; look not behind thee, neither stay thou in all the plain; escape to the mountain, lest thou be consumed.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כְהוֹצִיאָם אֹתָם הַחוּצָה וַיֹּאמֶר הִמָּלֵט עַל־נַפְשֶׁךָ אַל־תַּבִּיט אַחֲרֶיךָ וְאַל־תַּעֲמֹד בְּכָל־הַכִּכָּר הָהָרָה הִמָּלֵט פֶּן־תִּסָּפֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi kehotzi'am otam hachutzah vayomer himmalet al-nafshekha al-tabbit acharekha ve'al-ta'amod bekhol-hakkikkar haharah himmalet pen-tissafeh.
verses.study_note: Three commands: (1) Escape for thy life, (2) Look not behind thee, (3) Do not stay in the plain. 'Look not behind thee' (al-tabbit acharekha) — not merely a travel instruction but a spiritual command: do not long for what God has judged. Looking back represents attachment to the condemned world. Jesus references this in Luke 17:32: 'Remember Lot's wife.' The mountain represents safety above the judgment.

verses.kjv_text: And Lot said unto them, Oh, not so, my LORD:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹט אֲלֵהֶם אַל־נָא אֲדֹנָי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Lot alehem al-na Adonai.
verses.study_note: Even in the midst of divine rescue, Lot negotiates. 'Oh, not so' (al-na) — he objects to the escape plan. The contrast with Abraham is stark: Abraham interceded for others; Lot bargains for his own comfort. He addresses the angel as 'Adonai' (my Lord), acknowledging divine authority while simultaneously resisting divine instructions.

verses.kjv_text: Behold now, thy servant hath found grace in thy sight, and thou hast magnified thy mercy, which thou hast shewed unto me in saving my life; and I cannot escape to the mountain, lest some evil take me, and I die:
verses.original_text: הִנֵּה־נָא מָצָא עַבְדְּךָ חֵן בְּעֵינֶיךָ וַתַּגְדֵּל חַסְדְּךָ אֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתָ עִמָּדִי לְהַחֲיוֹת אֶת־נַפְשִׁי וְאָנֹכִי לֹא אוּכַל לְהִמָּלֵט הָהָרָה פֶּן־תִּדְבָּקַנִי הָרָעָה וָמַתִּי׃
verses.transliteration: Hinneh-na matza avdekha chen be'einekha vattagdel chasdekha asher asita immadi lehachayot et-nafshi ve'anokhi lo ukhal lehimmalet haharah pen-tidbekani hara'ah vamatti.
verses.study_note: Lot acknowledges grace (chen) and mercy (chesed) but immediately shifts to fear and self-preservation. 'I cannot escape to the mountain' — he fears the journey, though God has promised safety. The contrast is notable: Abraham trusted God's word about a son at 99; Lot cannot trust God's word about a mountain. 'Lest some evil take me' — Lot sees danger everywhere, even in God's prescribed refuge.

verses.kjv_text: Behold now, this city is near to flee unto, and it is a little one: Oh, let me escape thither, (is it not a little one?) and my soul shall live.
verses.original_text: הִנֵּה־נָא הָעִיר הַזֹּאת קְרֹבָה לָנוּס שָׁמָּה וְהִיא מִצְעָר אַל־נָא אִמָּלְטָה שָׁמָּה הֲלֹא מִצְעָר הִוא וּתְחִי נַפְשִׁי׃
verses.transliteration: Hinneh-na ha'ir hazzo't kerovah lanus shammah vehi mitz'ar al-na immaletah shammah halo mitz'ar hi utechi nafshi.
verses.study_note: Lot asks to flee to a nearby small city instead. 'Is it not a little one?' (mitz'ar) — he minimizes the compromise, asking for a small exception. The repeated 'mitz'ar' (little/small) becomes the basis for the city's name: Zoar (v.22, from the root 'small'). Even in fleeing judgment, Lot seeks the minimum distance from his old life.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto him, See, I have accepted thee concerning this thing also, that I will not overthrow this city, for the which thou hast spoken.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו הִנֵּה נָשָׂאתִי פָנֶיךָ גַּם לַדָּבָר הַזֶּה לְבִלְתִּי הָפְכִּי אֶת־הָעִיר אֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְתָּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer elav hinneh nasati fanekha gam laddavar hazzeh levilti hofki et-ha'ir asher dibbarta.
verses.study_note: The angel grants Lot's request — a remarkable display of divine accommodation. 'I have accepted thee' (nasati fanekha — literally 'I have lifted your face') is the idiom for showing favor. God bends even His judgment plan to accommodate this fearful man. 'Not overthrow' (hofki — from 'haphakh') — the key verb of the chapter, meaning total destruction/reversal.

verses.kjv_text: Haste thee, escape thither; for I cannot do anything till thou be come thither. Therefore the name of the city was called Zoar.
verses.original_text: מַהֵר הִמָּלֵט שָׁמָּה כִּי לֹא אוּכַל לַעֲשׂוֹת דָּבָר עַד־בֹּאֲךָ שָׁמָּה עַל־כֵּן קָרָא שֵׁם־הָעִיר צוֹעַר׃
verses.transliteration: Maher himmalet shammah ki lo ukhal la'asot davar ad-bo'akha shammah al-ken kara shem-ha'ir Tzo'ar.
verses.study_note: A stunning declaration: 'I cannot do any thing till thou be come thither' — God restrains His judgment until the righteous are safe. This principle echoes throughout Scripture: God delayed the Flood until Noah was in the ark (Gen 7:16), held back the plagues until Israel was protected (Exod 8-12), and will gather His elect before final judgment (Matt 24:31). Zoar (Tzo'ar — 'smallness') is named for Lot's request.

verses.kjv_text: The sun was risen upon the earth when Lot entered into Zoar.
verses.original_text: הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ יָצָא עַל־הָאָרֶץ וְלוֹט בָּא צֹעֲרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Hashemesh yatza al-ha'aretz veLot ba Tzo'arah.
verses.study_note: A brief, powerful narrative pause. The sun rises — normally a symbol of hope and new beginning — but this dawn brings destruction. The timing is precise: Lot reaches safety at sunrise, and then judgment falls. The detail underscores God's careful protection of the righteous, even to the exact moment.

verses.kjv_text: Then the LORD rained upon Sodom and upon Gomorrah brimstone and fire from the LORD out of heaven;
verses.original_text: וַיהוָה הִמְטִיר עַל־סְדֹם וְעַל־עֲמֹרָה גָּפְרִית וָאֵשׁ מֵאֵת יְהוָה מִן־הַשָּׁמָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: VaYHVH himtir al-Sedom ve'al-Amorah gafrit va'esh me'et YHVH min-hashamayim.
verses.study_note: The destruction: 'the LORD rained... brimstone and fire from the LORD out of heaven.' The phrase 'the LORD... from the LORD' (YHVH... me'et YHVH) is striking — some see here a plurality within the Godhead: YHVH on earth (the visitor) acting with YHVH in heaven. 'Brimstone' (gafrit — sulfur) combined with fire produces a catastrophic destruction. Archaeological evidence at sites near the Dead Sea shows layers of ash and sulfur consistent with a cataclysmic event.
verses.doctrinal_note: Divine judgment is real, historical, and catastrophic. The destruction of Sodom becomes the paradigm for God's judgment throughout Scripture — referenced over 20 times in the Bible. Jesus uses it as a warning for future judgment (Luke 17:28-30). Jude connects it to 'eternal fire' (Jude 1:7). The 'LORD... from the LORD' formulation hints at the plurality within the Godhead.

verses.kjv_text: And he overthrew those cities, and all the plain, and all the inhabitants of the cities, and that which grew upon the ground.
verses.original_text: וַיַּהֲפֹךְ אֶת־הֶעָרִים הָאֵל וְאֵת כָּל־הַכִּכָּר וְאֵת כָּל־יֹשְׁבֵי הֶעָרִים וְצֶמַח הָאֲדָמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyahafokh et-he'arim ha'el ve'et kol-hakkikkar ve'et kol-yoshevei he'arim vetzemach ha'adamah.
verses.study_note: Total destruction: cities, plains, inhabitants, and vegetation — everything 'overthrown' (vayyahafokh). The word 'haphakh' means complete reversal — what God had made, He un-makes. The fertile Jordan plain that Lot chose for its abundance (Gen 13:10) is now desolation. What once resembled 'the garden of the LORD' becomes a lifeless wasteland. The reversal is devastating and total.

verses.kjv_text: But his wife looked back from behind him, and she became a pillar of salt.
verses.original_text: וַתַּבֵּט אִשְׁתּוֹ מֵאַחֲרָיו וַתְּהִי נְצִיב מֶלַח׃
verses.transliteration: Vattabbet ishto me'acharav vattehi netziv melach.
verses.study_note: One of the most haunting verses in Scripture. 'She looked back' (vattabbet) — not a casual glance but an intense, longing gaze (from 'nabat,' to look with attention). She violated the explicit command of v.17: 'Look not behind thee.' She became a 'pillar of salt' (netziv melach) — the Dead Sea region is rich in salt formations. Jesus makes her a permanent warning: 'Remember Lot's wife' (Luke 17:32). She was rescued from Sodom but her heart never left it.
verses.doctrinal_note: Lot's wife represents those who are delivered from judgment but whose hearts remain attached to the condemned world. Physical departure without spiritual separation is fatal. She stands as a permanent warning: proximity to salvation is not salvation itself. One can be dragged out of Sodom and still perish.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham gat up early in the morning to the place where he stood before the LORD:
verses.original_text: וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אַבְרָהָם בַּבֹּקֶר אֶל־הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר־עָמַד שָׁם אֶת־פְּנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashkem Avraham babboker el-hammakom asher-amad sham et-penei YHVH.
verses.study_note: The scene shifts back to Abraham. He rises early and returns to the exact spot where he had interceded before the LORD (18:22-33). 'The place where he stood before the LORD' — the place of prayer has become sacred. Abraham comes to see the outcome of his intercession. The early rising suggests anxious concern for Lot and the cities.

verses.kjv_text: And he looked toward Sodom and Gomorrah, and toward all the land of the plain, and beheld, and, lo, the smoke of the country went up as the smoke of a furnace.
verses.original_text: וַיַּשְׁקֵף עַל־פְּנֵי סְדֹם וַעֲמֹרָה וְעַל־כָּל־פְּנֵי אֶרֶץ הַכִּכָּר וַיַּרְא וְהִנֵּה עָלָה קִיטֹר הָאָרֶץ כְּקִיטֹר הַכִּבְשָׁן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashkef al-penei Sedom va'Amorah ve'al-kol-penei eretz hakkikkar vayyar vehineh alah kitor ha'aretz kekitor hakkivshan.
verses.study_note: Abraham sees the devastating answer to his prayer. The 'smoke of the country went up as the smoke of a furnace' (kitor hakkivshan) — the same word used for a smelting furnace. The fertile plain that resembled the garden of the LORD (Gen 13:10) now resembles an industrial wasteland. Abraham had prayed for fifty, then ten — not even ten were found. The image of furnace smoke connects to God's covenant vision in Genesis 15:17 where a 'smoking furnace' passed between the pieces.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when God destroyed the cities of the plain, that God remembered Abraham, and sent Lot out of the midst of the overthrow, when he overthrew the cities in the which Lot dwelt.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בְּשַׁחֵת אֱלֹהִים אֶת־עָרֵי הַכִּכָּר וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים אֶת־אַבְרָהָם וַיְשַׁלַּח אֶת־לוֹט מִתּוֹךְ הַהֲפֵכָה בַּהֲפֹךְ אֶת־הֶעָרִים אֲשֶׁר־יָשַׁב בָּהֵן לוֹט׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi beshachet Elohim et-arei hakkikkar vayyizkor Elohim et-Avraham vayeshallach et-Lot mittokh hahafekah bahafokh et-he'arim asher-yashav bahen Lot.
verses.study_note: The key verse of the chapter: 'God remembered Abraham, and sent Lot out.' Lot was not saved for his own merit but 'for Abraham's sake' — because of the intercessor's prayer and God's covenant. 'Remembered' (vayyizkor) — the same word used of God remembering Noah (Gen 8:1) and later Israel in Egypt (Exod 2:24). Divine 'remembering' means God acts on His covenant promises.
verses.doctrinal_note: Intercessory prayer has power: Lot was rescued not because of his own righteousness but because God remembered Abraham. This is the Old Testament pattern fulfilled in Christ — we are saved not by our own merit but through the intercession of Another. Christ 'ever liveth to make intercession' (Hebrews 7:25) and is the ultimate Abraham, securing the rescue of those who are His.

verses.kjv_text: And Lot went up out of Zoar, and dwelt in the mountain, and his two daughters with him; for he feared to dwell in Zoar: and he dwelt in a cave, he and his two daughters.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַל לוֹט מִצּוֹעַר וַיֵּשֶׁב בָּהָר וּשְׁתֵּי בְנֹתָיו עִמּוֹ כִּי יָרֵא לָשֶׁבֶת בְּצוֹעַר וַיֵּשֶׁב בַּמְּעָרָה הוּא וּשְׁתֵּי בְנֹתָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'al Lot mitTzo'ar vayyeshev bahar ushetei venotav immo ki yare lashevet beTzo'ar vayyeshev bamme'arah hu ushetei venotav.
verses.study_note: The irony deepens. Lot finally flees to the very mountain the angels originally commanded (v.17) — the place he feared. His bargaining for Zoar was pointless; he ends up exactly where God directed. Now he lives in a cave — a dramatic fall from his gate-sitting prominence in Sodom. Fear continues to dominate: 'he feared to dwell in Zoar.' From wealthy patriarch to cave-dweller, Lot's trajectory illustrates the wages of compromise.

verses.kjv_text: And the firstborn said unto the younger, Our father is old, and there is not a man in the earth to come in unto us after the manner of all the earth:
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר הַבְּכִירָה אֶל־הַצְּעִירָה אָבִינוּ זָקֵן וְאִישׁ אֵין בָּאָרֶץ לָבוֹא עָלֵינוּ כְּדֶרֶךְ כָּל־הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomer habekhirah el-hatze'irah avinu zaken ve'ish ein ba'aretz lavo aleinu kederekh kol-ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The elder daughter reasons that 'there is not a man in the earth' — she may believe the destruction was universal (like the Flood) or that no suitable men remain. Living in an isolated cave with no news, this is understandable but wrong. The phrase 'after the manner of all the earth' (kederekh kol-ha'aretz) means the normal way of procreation. Their plan reflects desperation, Sodomite moral corruption, and the absence of faith in God's provision.

verses.kjv_text: Come, let us make our father drink wine, and we will lie with him, that we may preserve seed of our father.
verses.original_text: לְכָה נַשְׁקֶה אֶת־אָבִינוּ יַיִן וְנִשְׁכְּבָה עִמּוֹ וּנְחַיֶּה מֵאָבִינוּ זָרַע׃
verses.transliteration: Lekhah nashkeh et-avinu yayin venishkevah immo unechayyeh me'avinu zara.
verses.study_note: The plan: intoxicate their father and 'preserve seed' (nechayyeh... zara — keep alive his seed/offspring). The word 'zera' (seed) recalls Genesis 3:15 and the covenant promises — but here the means is sinful. Wine becomes the instrument of moral failure, as it will later with Noah (Gen 9:21). The daughters learned their moral reasoning in Sodom — survival by any means, without reference to God. Not once do they pray or seek God's guidance.

verses.kjv_text: And they made their father drink wine that night: and the firstborn went in, and lay with her father; and he perceived not when she lay down, nor when she arose.
verses.original_text: וַתַּשְׁקֶיןָ אֶת־אֲבִיהֶן יַיִן בַּלַּיְלָה הוּא וַתָּבֹא הַבְּכִירָה וַתִּשְׁכַּב אֶת־אָבִיהָ וְלֹא־יָדַע בְּשִׁכְבָהּ וּבְקוּמָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattashkena et-avihen yayin ballaylah hu vattavo habekhirah vattishkav et-aviha velo-yada beshikhvah uvekumah.
verses.study_note: The text records the act with clinical brevity. Lot 'perceived not' (lo-yada) — again 'yada,' the chapter's recurring word (vv.5,8,33,35), now meaning lack of awareness. The text neither condones nor excuses — it simply reports, allowing the reader to see the devastating end of Lot's compromised life. He who chose Sodom (Gen 13:11) now suffers its consequences in his own family.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass on the morrow, that the firstborn said unto the younger, Behold, I lay yesternight with my father: let us make him drink wine this night also; and go thou in, and lie with him, that we may preserve seed of our father.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי מִמָּחֳרָת וַתֹּאמֶר הַבְּכִירָה אֶל־הַצְּעִירָה הֵן־שָׁכַבְתִּי אֶמֶשׁ אֶת־אָבִי נַשְׁקֶנּוּ יַיִן גַּם־הַלַּיְלָה וּבֹאִי שִׁכְבִי עִמּוֹ וּנְחַיֶּה מֵאָבִינוּ זָרַע׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi mimmocharot vattomer habekhirah el-hatze'irah hen-shakhavti emesh et-avi nashkenu yayin gam-hallaylah uvo'i shikhvi immo unechayyeh me'avinu zara.
verses.study_note: The plan is repeated the next night with the younger daughter. The matter-of-fact repetition highlights the calculated nature of the scheme. The elder daughter instructs and takes the lead — the same initiative, the same intoxication, the same outcome. The narrative gives no indication that either daughter feels remorse or moral conflict.

verses.kjv_text: And they made their father drink wine that night also: and the younger arose, and lay with him; and he perceived not when she lay down, nor when she arose.
verses.original_text: וַתַּשְׁקֶיןָ גַּם בַּלַּיְלָה הַהוּא אֶת־אֲבִיהֶן יַיִן וַתָּקָם הַצְּעִירָה וַתִּשְׁכַּב עִמּוֹ וְלֹא־יָדַע בְּשִׁכְבָהּ וּבְקֻמָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattashkena gam ballaylah hahu et-avihen yayin vattakam hatze'irah vattishkav immo velo-yada beshikhvah uvekumah.
verses.study_note: The identical formula is repeated: wine, incest, Lot's obliviousness. The repetition emphasizes the deliberate, premeditated nature of the act. 'He perceived not' — Lot's physical state mirrors his spiritual condition throughout the chapter: unaware, passive, dragged along by events and by others' decisions.

verses.kjv_text: Thus were both the daughters of Lot with child by their father.
verses.original_text: וַתַּהֲרֶיןָ שְׁתֵּי בְנוֹת־לוֹט מֵאֲבִיהֶן׃
verses.transliteration: Vattaharena shetei venot-Lot me'avihen.
verses.study_note: A stark, one-verse summary. No commentary, no condemnation from the narrator — just the fact. The brevity is devastating. Both daughters conceive from their father. This scene shows the long-term fruit of Lot's choices: he chose Sodom (13:11), rose in Sodom (19:1), internalized Sodom's values (19:8), and now reaps the harvest even after Sodom's destruction. The corruption of Sodom outlived the city.

verses.kjv_text: And the first born bare a son, and called his name Moab: the same is the father of the Moabites unto this day.
verses.original_text: וַתֵּלֶד הַבְּכִירָה בֵּן וַתִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ מוֹאָב הוּא אֲבִי־מוֹאָב עַד־הַיּוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vatteled habekhirah ven vattikra shemo Mo'av hu avi-Mo'av ad-hayyom.
verses.study_note: The firstborn names her son 'Moab' — which can mean 'from [my] father' (me-av). The name preserves the shameful origin. The Moabites become a significant nation east of the Dead Sea, often hostile to Israel. Yet remarkably, Ruth the Moabitess becomes an ancestor of David and ultimately of Christ (Ruth 4:17, Matt 1:5) — demonstrating that God's grace can bring redemption from the most broken origins.

verses.kjv_text: And the younger, she also bare a son, and called his name Benammi: the same is the father of the children of Ammon unto this day.
verses.original_text: וְהַצְּעִירָה גַם־הִוא יָלְדָה בֵּן וַתִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ בֶּן־עַמִּי הוּא אֲבִי בְנֵי־עַמּוֹן עַד־הַיּוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehatze'irah gam-hi yaledah ven vattikra shemo Ben-Ammi hu avi venei-Ammon ad-hayyom.
verses.study_note: The younger daughter names her son 'Ben-Ammi' — 'son of my people/kinsman.' Less explicit than 'Moab' but still recording the incestuous origin. The Ammonites settle northeast of the Dead Sea and become persistent enemies of Israel (Judges 10-11, 1 Sam 11). The chapter that began with angels at Sodom's gate ends with the birth of two nations from a cave — nations that will trouble Israel for centuries. Yet the narrative reveals God's sovereignty: even from sin and catastrophe, God weaves the threads of redemptive history.

meta.overview: Genesis 19 is one of the most dramatic and sobering chapters in the Bible. It begins with two angels arriving in Sodom and Lot offering them hospitality — a pale reflection of Abraham's lavish welcome in chapter 18. The depravity of Sodom is graphically exposed when the men of the city surround Lot's house demanding the visitors. The angels strike the mob with blindness, urge Lot to flee, and literally drag his hesitating family out of the doomed city. Fire and brimstone rain from heaven, destroying the cities of the plain. Lot's wife looks back and becomes a pillar of salt. The chapter closes with Lot's daughters, believing civilization has ended, committing incest — producing Moab and Ammon. Throughout, the narrative contrasts Abraham's faithful intercession (ch.18) with Lot's compromised life, and reveals both the severity and mercy of God's judgment.
meta.theme: Divine Judgment, Mercy in Rescue & the Cost of Compromise
meta.keyWordMeaning: To overthrow, overturn — the word used for God's total destruction of Sodom, reversing what He had built. Used as a paradigm of divine judgment throughout Scripture.
outline: The Angels Arrive in Sodom (vv.1-3): Lot's hospitality contrasted with Abraham's
The Wickedness of Sodom Exposed (vv.4-11): The men of the city and the angelic rescue
Lot Warned to Flee (vv.12-14): Sons-in-law mock the warning
The Rescue — Dragged Out by Grace (vv.15-22): Lot lingers, the angels seize his hand
Fire and Brimstone — Sodom Destroyed (vv.23-29): Lot's wife looks back and becomes salt
Lot's Daughters and the Aftermath (vv.30-38): The birth of Moab and Ammon

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham journeyed from thence toward the south country, and dwelled between Kadesh and Shur, and sojourned in Gerar.
verses.original_text: וַיִּסַּע מִשָּׁם אַבְרָהָם אַרְצָה הַנֶּגֶב וַיֵּשֶׁב בֵּין־קָדֵשׁ וּבֵין שׁוּר וַיָּגָר בִּגְרָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyissa misham Avraham artzah hannegev vayyeshev bein-Qadesh uvein Shur vayyagar biGerar.
verses.study_note: Abraham moves southward from Hebron after the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah (Gen 19). 'From thence' (misham) — he leaves the region of judgment and moves toward the Negev, the arid southern region of Canaan. Gerar was a Philistine territory near the Mediterranean coast, on the border of Canaan. The name 'Kadesh' means 'holy' and 'Shur' means 'wall' — Abraham dwells between holiness and a barrier, a fitting metaphor for his spiritual state. The verb 'sojourned' (vayagar) indicates temporary residence as a stranger — Abraham is still a pilgrim with no permanent possession in the land God promised him.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham said of Sarah his wife, She is my sister: and Abimelech king of Gerar sent, and took Sarah.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם אֶל־שָׂרָה אִשְׁתּוֹ אֲחֹתִי הִוא וַיִּשְׁלַח אֲבִימֶלֶךְ מֶלֶךְ גְּרָר וַיִּקַּח אֶת־שָׂרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Avraham el-Sarah ishto achoti hi vayyishlach Avimelekh melekh Gerar vayyiqqach et-Sarah.
verses.study_note: Abraham repeats the identical deception from Genesis 12:13 — calling Sarah his 'sister' instead of his wife. This is the second time he uses this half-truth (she was indeed his half-sister, v.12), and Isaac will repeat the same pattern in Genesis 26:7. The repetition reveals that Abraham had not fully conquered this particular fear. Sarah was approximately 89-90 years old at this point, yet her beauty — perhaps renewed by God in preparation for Isaac's birth — attracted the king. 'Abimelech' means 'my father is king' and may be a dynastic title (like Pharaoh) rather than a personal name. 'Sent and took' implies royal authority; kings could claim women for their harems.
verses.doctrinal_note: The repetition of sin: Even the greatest saints struggle with recurring weaknesses. Abraham's repeated deception about Sarah shows that sanctification is progressive, not instantaneous. Yet God's covenant promise does not depend on Abraham's perfection — it rests on God's faithfulness alone.

verses.kjv_text: But God came to Abimelech in a dream by night, and said to him, Behold, thou art but a dead man, for the woman which thou hast taken; for she is a man’s wife.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא אֱלֹהִים אֶל־אֲבִימֶלֶךְ בַּחֲלוֹם הַלָּיְלָה וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ הִנְּךָ מֵת עַל־הָאִשָּׁה אֲשֶׁר־לָקַחְתָּ וְהִוא בְּעֻלַת בָּעַל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo Elohim el-Avimelekh bachalum hallaylah vayyomer lo hinnekha met al-ha'ishah asher-laqachta vehi be'ulat ba'al.
verses.study_note: God intervenes directly — not through Abraham, but despite Abraham. 'God came' (Elohim, not Yahweh) — the universal name for God is used when dealing with a Gentile king, reflecting God's sovereignty over all nations, not just Israel. The dream revelation demonstrates God communicates with non-covenant people when His purposes require it (cf. Pharaoh, Nebuchadnezzar, Pilate's wife). 'You are a dead man' — the death penalty for adultery, even unknowing, reflects the gravity of violating the marriage covenant. 'Be'ulat ba'al' — literally 'owned by an owner/husband,' emphasizing the binding nature of marriage. God Himself guards Sarah because she carries the covenant promise — Isaac must be Abraham's son.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's sovereign protection of His covenant: God will not allow human sin — whether Abraham's deception or Abimelech's unwitting appropriation — to derail His redemptive plan. The promised seed must come through Abraham and Sarah alone.

verses.kjv_text: But Abimelech had not come near her: and he said, LORD, wilt thou slay also a righteous nation?
verses.original_text: וַאֲבִימֶלֶךְ לֹא קָרַב אֵלֶיהָ וַיֹּאמַר אֲדֹנָי הֲגוֹי גַּם־צַדִּיק תַּהֲרֹג׃
verses.transliteration: Va'Avimelekh lo qarav eleha vayyomar Adonai hagoy gam-tzaddiq taharog.
verses.study_note: Abimelech's defense parallels Abraham's intercession for Sodom (Gen 18:23-25). The phrase 'will you slay a righteous nation?' echoes 'will you destroy the righteous with the wicked?' Both appeal to God's justice. 'Had not come near her' — God had prevented consummation (v.6), protecting Sarah and the covenant lineage. The term 'Adonai' (Lord/Master) shows Abimelech recognized a higher authority. A pagan king appeals to divine justice while the patriarch resorts to deception — a humbling reversal that Scripture records without softening.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's common grace: Even pagan rulers possess a moral sense and can appeal to justice. Abimelech's integrity here exceeds Abraham's, demonstrating that God's image in humanity functions even outside the covenant community.

verses.kjv_text: Said he not unto me, She is my sister? and she, even she herself said, He is my brother: in the integrity of my heart and innocency of my hands have I done this.
verses.original_text: הֲלֹא הוּא אָמַר־לִי אֲחֹתִי הִוא וְהִיא גַם־הִוא אָמְרָה אָחִי הוּא בְּתָם־לְבָבִי וּבְנִקְיֹן כַּפַּי עָשִׂיתִי זֹאת׃
verses.transliteration: Halo hu amar-li achoti hi vehi gam-hi amerah achi hu betom-levavi uveniqyon kappai asiti zot.
verses.study_note: Abimelech presents a threefold defense: (1) Abraham himself said she was his sister, (2) Sarah confirmed it — she was complicit in the deception, (3) Abimelech acted with 'integrity of heart' (tom-levav) and 'innocence of hands' (niqyon kappayim). These two phrases express internal purity of motive and external purity of action. The language is almost Psalm-like (cf. Psalm 24:4, 26:6). Note that Sarah is also implicated — 'she herself said' — both husband and wife participated in the deception. Abimelech's moral clarity stands in sharp contrast to Abraham's fearful scheming.

verses.kjv_text: And God said unto him in a dream, Yea, I know that thou didst this in the integrity of thy heart; for I also withheld thee from sinning against me: therefore suffered I thee not to touch her.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו הָאֱלֹהִים בַּחֲלֹם גַּם אָנֹכִי יָדַעְתִּי כִּי בְתָם־לְבָבְךָ עָשִׂיתָ זֹּאת וָאֶחְשֹׂךְ גַּם־אָנֹכִי אוֹתְךָ מֵחֲטוֹ־לִי עַל־כֵּן לֹא־נְתַתִּיךָ לִנְגֹּעַ אֵלֶיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer elav ha'Elohim bachalom gam anokhi yadati ki vetom-levavkha asita zot va'echsokh gam-anokhi otkha mechato-li al-ken lo-netattikha lingo'a eleha.
verses.study_note: God's extraordinary response: (1) He validates Abimelech's integrity — 'I know' (yadati), (2) He reveals His own sovereign restraint — 'I also withheld thee.' God is the unseen actor behind the scenes, preventing sin before it occurs. 'From sinning against ME' (mechato-li) — adultery is ultimately a sin against God, not merely against another human (cf. Psalm 51:4). 'Suffered I thee not to touch her' — God exercised providential restraint, likely including the physical afflictions mentioned in v.17-18. This verse reveals the doctrine of prevenient grace — God actively preventing sin, not merely reacting to it.
verses.doctrinal_note: Divine providence and restraining grace: God actively prevents sin, not just punishes it. He restrains evil, protects His people, and guards His covenant purposes — even when His own servants fail. This 'withholding' grace operates throughout history to limit the full expression of human depravity (cf. 2 Thessalonians 2:7).

verses.kjv_text: Now therefore restore the man his wife; for he is a prophet, and he shall pray for thee, and thou shalt live: and if thou restore her not, know thou that thou shalt surely die, thou, and all that are thine.
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה הָשֵׁב אֵשֶׁת־הָאִישׁ כִּי־נָבִיא הוּא וְיִתְפַּלֵּל בַּעַדְךָ וֶחְיֵה וְאִם־אֵינְךָ מֵשִׁיב דַּע כִּי־מוֹת תָּמוּת אַתָּה וְכָל־אֲשֶׁר־לָךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah hashev eshet-ha'ish ki-navi hu veyitpallel ba'adkha vechyeh ve'im-einkha meshiv da ki-mot tamut attah vekhol-asher-lakh.
verses.study_note: The most theologically significant verse in the chapter. Four groundbreaking revelations: (1) 'Restore' (hashev) — repentance means returning what was wrongly taken. (2) 'Prophet' (navi) — the FIRST use of this word in Scripture, applied to Abraham. A navi is one who speaks for God and intercedes before God on behalf of others. (3) 'He shall pray for thee' (yitpallel) — prophetic intercession has atoning power. The very man who wronged Abimelech is the one who must pray for him. (4) 'Surely die' (mot tamut) — the same emphatic formula from Genesis 2:17, the penalty for violating God's order. The irony is profound: Abraham, who caused the crisis through deception, is designated as the intercessor who alone can resolve it.
verses.doctrinal_note: The prophetic office: The first use of 'navi' (prophet) in the Bible defines the role: one who stands between God and man, interceding. Abraham is both the sinner who caused the problem and the prophet who must pray for its resolution — a pattern fulfilled ultimately in Christ, who bore our sin and intercedes for us (Romans 8:34, Hebrews 7:25).

verses.kjv_text: Therefore Abimelech rose early in the morning, and called all his servants, and told all these things in their ears: and the men were sore afraid.
verses.original_text: וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אֲבִימֶלֶךְ בַּבֹּקֶר וַיִּקְרָא לְכָל־עֲבָדָיו וַיְדַבֵּר אֶת־כָּל־הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה בְּאָזְנֵיהֶם וַיִּירְאוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים מְאֹד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashkem Avimelekh babboqer vayyiqra lekhol-avadav vaydabber et-kol-haddevarim ha'elleh be'ozneihem vayyir'u ha'anashim me'od.
verses.study_note: Abimelech 'rose early' (vayashkem) — the same phrase used of Abraham's obedient responses to God (Gen 19:27, 21:14, 22:3). Urgency in response to divine warning characterizes a God-fearing heart. He assembled his entire household — transparency and corporate responsibility. 'Sore afraid' (vayyir'u me'od) — the fear of God fell upon them. This holy fear in a pagan court contrasts with Abraham's claim that 'the fear of God is not in this place' (v.11) — Abraham misjudged these people entirely.

verses.kjv_text: Then Abimelech called Abraham, and said unto him, What hast thou done unto us? and what have I offended thee, that thou hast brought on me and on my kingdom a great sin? thou hast done deeds unto me that ought not to be done.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא אֲבִימֶלֶךְ לְאַבְרָהָם וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ מֶה־עָשִׂיתָ לָּנוּ וּמֶה־חָטָאתִי לָךְ כִּי־הֵבֵאתָ עָלַי וְעַל־מַמְלַכְתִּי חֲטָאָה גְדֹלָה מַעֲשִׂים אֲשֶׁר לֹא־יֵעָשׂוּ עָשִׂיתָ עִמָּדִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqra Avimelekh le'Avraham vayyomer lo meh-asita lanu umeh-chatati lakh ki-heveta alai ve'al-mamlakhti chata'ah gedolah ma'asim asher lo-ye'asu asita immadi.
