verses.study_note: Abimelech's rebuke consists of four piercing questions and accusations: (1) 'What have you done to us?' — the damage is corporate, (2) 'What have I offended you?' — demanding justification, (3) 'You brought a great sin upon me and my kingdom' — 'chata'ah gedolah' (great sin) is a technical term for adultery in ancient Near Eastern texts, (4) 'Deeds that ought not to be done' — a moral absolute. A pagan king rebukes the father of faith for ethical failure. This is one of Scripture's most uncomfortable scenes for the covenant community — God's own prophet is morally outperformed by a Gentile. Scripture never whitewashes its heroes.

verses.kjv_text: And Abimelech said unto Abraham, What sawest thou, that thou hast done this thing?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ אֶל־אַבְרָהָם מָה רָאִיתָ כִּי עָשִׂיתָ אֶת־הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Avimelekh el-Avraham mah ra'ita ki asita et-haddavar hazzeh.
verses.study_note: 'What did you see?' (mah ra'ita) — Abimelech demands Abraham's reasoning. What perceived threat justified such deception? The question is penetrating and fair — it forces Abraham to articulate the fear behind his actions. This is the question every believer must answer when caught in sin motivated by fear rather than faith: what did you see that made you stop trusting God?

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham said, Because I thought, Surely the fear of God is not in this place; and they will slay me for my wife’s sake.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם כִּי אָמַרְתִּי רַק אֵין־יִרְאַת אֱלֹהִים בַּמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה וַהֲרָגוּנִי עַל־דְּבַר אִשְׁתִּי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Avraham ki amarti raq ein-yir'at Elohim bamaqom hazzeh vaharuguni al-devar ishti.
verses.study_note: Abraham's first excuse: he assumed godlessness. 'I thought' (amarti) — he reasoned from human assessment, not divine promise. 'The fear of God is not in this place' — Abraham projected his fears onto Gerar. Ironically, Abimelech and his servants demonstrated more fear of God (v.8) than Abraham showed faith. The phrase 'yir'at Elohim' (fear of God) is a foundational Old Testament concept — the beginning of wisdom (Proverbs 9:10). Abraham assumed its absence in a Gentile culture, revealing a prejudice that God's own actions disprove. God-fearers exist outside the covenant community.
verses.doctrinal_note: The fear of God is not limited to Israel: Abimelech's response demonstrates that God's moral law is written on human hearts (Romans 2:14-15). Abraham's assumption that 'these people' lacked godly fear was a failure of both faith and charity. We must never assume God is absent from places we consider 'godless.'

verses.kjv_text: And yet indeed she is my sister; she is the daughter of my father, but not the daughter of my mother; and she became my wife.
verses.original_text: וְגַם־אָמְנָה אֲחֹתִי בַת־אָבִי הִוא אַךְ לֹא בַת־אִמִּי וַתְּהִי־לִי לְאִשָּׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vegam-omnah achoti vat-avi hi akh lo vat-immi vattehi-li le'ishah.
verses.study_note: Abraham's second excuse: the technical truth. Sarah was indeed his half-sister (same father Terah, different mothers). This was acceptable in patriarchal times but was later prohibited by Mosaic law (Leviticus 18:9, 20:17). Abraham attempts to defend his deception by noting it wasn't technically a lie — a classic rationalization. A half-truth used to deceive is still deception. The moral problem isn't the factual accuracy of the statement but the intent to mislead. This reveals the subtle danger of 'truthful' deception — using facts strategically to create false impressions.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when God caused me to wander from my father’s house, that I said unto her, This is thy kindness which thou shalt shew unto me; at every place whither we shall come, say of me, He is my brother.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כַּאֲשֶׁר הִתְעוּ אֹתִי אֱלֹהִים מִבֵּית אָבִי וָאֹמַר לָהּ זֶה חַסְדֵּךְ אֲשֶׁר תַּעֲשִׂי עִמָּדִי אֶל כָּל־הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר נָבוֹא שָׁמָּה אִמְרִי־לִי אָחִי הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi ka'asher hit'u oti Elohim mibbeit avi va'omar lah zeh chasdekh asher ta'asi immadi el kol-hammaqom asher navo shammah imri-li achi hu.
verses.study_note: Abraham's third excuse reveals the deepest problem: this was a premeditated, habitual strategy established from the very beginning of their journey. 'When God caused me to wander' (hit'u) — the verb is plural, some translate 'when the gods caused me to wander,' though Abraham may be using a plural of majesty or acknowledging God's leading appeared like wandering. He called Sarah's compliance 'chesed' (covenant kindness/loyalty) — reframing deception as marital loyalty. This prearranged deception had been their standard protocol at 'every place' — not a momentary lapse but a lifestyle pattern of fear-driven manipulation.
verses.doctrinal_note: Habitual sin: Abraham's confession reveals this deception was not a one-time failure but a deliberate life strategy adopted from the beginning of his call. Even the greatest saints can carry patterns of unbelief that surface repeatedly. True repentance requires identifying and dismantling root strategies of self-protection that substitute for trust in God.

verses.kjv_text: And Abimelech took sheep, and oxen, and menservants, and womenservants, and gave them unto Abraham, and restored him Sarah his wife.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח אֲבִימֶלֶךְ צֹאן וּבָקָר וַעֲבָדִים וּשְׁפָחֹת וַיִּתֵּן לְאַבְרָהָם וַיָּשֶׁב לוֹ אֵת שָׂרָה אִשְׁתּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach Avimelekh tzon uvaqar va'avadim ushfachot vayyitten le'Avraham vayyashev lo et Sarah ishto.
verses.study_note: Abimelech's response is remarkably generous. Rather than demanding restitution from the deceiver, he gives gifts and restores Sarah. The pattern mirrors Pharaoh's response in Genesis 12:16, but with a crucial difference: Pharaoh expelled Abraham, while Abimelech invites him to stay (v.15). The gifts — livestock and servants — represent substantial wealth. This generosity exceeds what justice requires and reflects either genuine magnanimity, fear of the prophet's God, or both. 'Restored' (vayyashev) — the same root God commanded in v.7, showing Abimelech's complete obedience to the divine command.

verses.kjv_text: And Abimelech said, Behold, my land is before thee: dwell where it pleaseth thee.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ הִנֵּה אַרְצִי לְפָנֶיךָ בַּטּוֹב בְּעֵינֶיךָ שֵׁב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Avimelekh hinneh artzi lefanekha battov be'einekha shev.
verses.study_note: 'My land is before you' — Abimelech extends an extraordinary invitation: choose any territory you wish and settle there. This is the opposite of Pharaoh's response in Gen 12:19 ('take her and go'). The phrase echoes God's earlier promise to Abraham — 'Lift up your eyes and look... all the land which you see I will give to you' (Gen 13:14-15). A pagan king unwittingly becomes an instrument of God's provision, granting Abraham free access to land in fulfillment of the divine promise. 'Dwell where it pleases you' (battov be'einekha) — literally 'in the good in your eyes.'

verses.kjv_text: And unto Sarah he said, Behold, I have given thy brother a thousand pieces of silver: behold, he is to thee a covering of the eyes, unto all that are with thee, and with all other: thus she was reproved.
verses.original_text: וּלְשָׂרָה אָמַר הִנֵּה נָתַתִּי אֶלֶף כֶּסֶף לְאָחִיךְ הִנֵּה הוּא־לָךְ כְּסוּת עֵינַיִם לְכֹל אֲשֶׁר אִתָּךְ וְאֵת כֹּל וְנֹכָחַת׃
verses.transliteration: Ulesarah amar hinneh natatti elef kesef le'achikh hinneh hu-lakh kesut einayim lekhol asher ittakh ve'et kol venokhachat.
verses.study_note: This verse is notoriously difficult to translate. Abimelech addresses Sarah directly and references 'your brother' (using Abraham's own deceptive term, perhaps with irony). The 'thousand pieces of silver' was an enormous sum — a public vindication of Sarah's honor. 'Covering of the eyes' (kesut einayim) likely means the silver serves as public proof of her innocence — a 'veil' that protects her reputation before all witnesses. 'Thus she was reproved' (venokhachat) — Sarah too receives correction; she was complicit in the deception. The rebuke is gentle but real — even the matriarch of the covenant is held accountable.

verses.kjv_text: So Abraham prayed unto God: and God healed Abimelech, and his wife, and his maidservants; and they bare children.
verses.original_text: וַיִּתְפַּלֵּל אַבְרָהָם אֶל־הָאֱלֹהִים וַיִּרְפָּא אֱלֹהִים אֶת־אֲבִימֶלֶךְ וְאֶת־אִשְׁתּוֹ וְאַמְהֹתָיו וַיֵּלֵדוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitpallel Avraham el-ha'Elohim vayyirpa Elohim et-Avimelekh ve'et-ishto ve'amhotav vayyeledu.
verses.study_note: Abraham fulfills his prophetic role: he prays, and God heals. 'Vayyitpallel' (he prayed) — the hitpael form suggests intense, persistent intercession. This is the first recorded intercessory prayer that results in healing in Scripture. The healing is comprehensive: Abimelech personally, his wife, and his maidservants — all had been affected by the divine restraint (v.18). 'They bore children' — the wombs had been closed. The deep irony: Abraham prays for the fertility of Abimelech's household while he himself still awaits the promised son. Abraham the intercessor mediates blessing for others before receiving his own. This pattern — the prophet interceding for others before being blessed himself — foreshadows Christ and echoes Job's restoration (Job 42:10).
verses.doctrinal_note: Intercessory prayer: The first recorded healing through prayer establishes a biblical pattern — the prophet/priest mediates between God and people. Abraham prays for the very man he wronged, demonstrating that God's grace flows even through imperfect vessels. The pattern reaches its fulfillment in Christ, who prays for those who crucified Him (Luke 23:34).

verses.kjv_text: For the LORD had fast closed up all the wombs of the house of Abimelech, because of Sarah Abraham’s wife.
verses.original_text: כִּי־עָצֹר עָצַר יְהוָה בְּעַד כָּל־רֶחֶם לְבֵית אֲבִימֶלֶךְ עַל־דְּבַר שָׂרָה אֵשֶׁת אַבְרָהָם׃
verses.transliteration: Ki-atzor atsar Yahweh be'ad kol-rechem leveit Avimelekh al-devar Sarah eshet Avraham.
verses.study_note: The chapter concludes by revealing the full extent of God's intervention: 'fast closed' (atzor atsar — emphatic doubling: 'closing He closed') — God had sealed every womb in Abimelech's entire household. This explains the urgency of v.8 — the affliction was already evident. Here the covenant name 'Yahweh' (LORD) appears for the first time in this chapter, used specifically in connection with His protective action regarding Sarah. The narrative comes full circle: God opened no womb in Abimelech's house because He was about to open Sarah's womb (Gen 21:1-2). The closing and opening of wombs belongs exclusively to God's sovereign authority. The profound irony continues: Abraham prayed for the opening of wombs in Abimelech's house, and immediately afterward (Gen 21:1-2) God opens Sarah's womb. The intercessor who blessed others received his own blessing.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's sovereignty over life: The opening and closing of wombs belongs to God alone (cf. Gen 29:31, 30:22, 1 Sam 1:5-6). This truth — that God is the Author of life — undergirds the biblical understanding of human dignity, the sanctity of life, and the hope of barren believers throughout Scripture.

meta.overview: Genesis 20 records Abraham's sojourn in Gerar and his repetition of the half-truth about Sarah being his sister — the same deception he employed in Egypt (Gen 12:10-20). God intervenes directly, warning the pagan king Abimelech in a dream and protecting Sarah's purity for the sake of the covenant promise. This chapter reveals that even the father of faith stumbles repeatedly in the same sin, yet God's sovereign purpose cannot be thwarted by human failure. Remarkably, the pagan king demonstrates greater moral integrity than the patriarch, and Abraham must intercede as a prophet for the very man he wronged. The chapter is a powerful study in divine providence, human weakness, and God's faithfulness to His covenant despite the unfaithfulness of His people.
meta.theme: God's Faithfulness Despite Human Failure
meta.keyWordMeaning: Prophet — one who speaks for God and intercedes. The first use of this word in Scripture, applied to Abraham.
outline: Abraham's Deception at Gerar (vv.1-2): Abraham journeys south and repeats his half-truth about Sarah
God Warns Abimelech (vv.3-7): The LORD appears in a dream, reveals the truth, and commands restoration
Abimelech Confronts Abraham (vv.8-10): The king rebukes Abraham for his deception
Abraham's Defense (vv.11-13): Abraham explains his fear and the technical truth of his claim
Restoration and Blessing (vv.14-16): Abimelech restores Sarah with generous gifts
Abraham's Intercession (vv.17-18): Abraham prays as prophet and God heals Abimelech's household

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD visited Sarah as he had said, and the LORD did unto Sarah as he had spoken.
verses.original_text: וַיהוָה פָּקַד אֶת־שָׂרָה כַּאֲשֶׁר אָמָר וַיַּעַשׂ יְהוָה לְשָׂרָה כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֵּר׃
verses.transliteration: VaYHVH paqad et-Sarah ka'asher amar vayya'as YHVH leSarah ka'asher dibber.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with a double affirmation of divine faithfulness: 'as he had said... as he had spoken.' The verb 'paqad' (visited) is rich and multivalent — it means to attend to, to intervene, to act with purpose. God does not merely observe; He intervenes. The LORD (YHVH — the covenant name) is used twice, emphasizing that this is covenant faithfulness in action. Twenty-five years after the initial promise (Gen 12:2), God fulfills His word. The double phrase structure (amar/dibber — said/spoken) is emphatic: God's words and God's deeds are perfectly aligned.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's promises are certain. The 25-year delay between promise (Gen 12) and fulfillment (Gen 21) did not diminish the promise — it revealed human inability and divine sovereignty. 'Has He said, and shall He not do it?' (Num 23:19). Every covenant promise God has made will be fulfilled in His timing, including the return of Christ.

verses.kjv_text: For Sarah conceived, and bare Abraham a son in his old age, at the set time of which God had spoken to him.
verses.original_text: וַתַּהַר וַתֵּלֶד שָׂרָה לְאַבְרָהָם בֵּן לִזְקֻנָיו לַמּוֹעֵד אֲשֶׁר־דִּבֶּר אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vattahar vateled Sarah le'Avraham ben lizqunav lamo'ed asher-dibber oto Elohim.
verses.study_note: Three remarkable details: (1) Sarah conceived and bore — the barren woman produces life by divine power. (2) 'In his old age' (lizqunav) — Abraham was 100 years old (v.5; cf. 17:17), emphasizing the miraculous nature of the birth. (3) 'At the set time' (lamo'ed) — the precise appointed time God designated. 'Mo'ed' is the same word used for Israel's appointed feasts (Lev 23) and the tabernacle of meeting. God operates on a divine schedule; nothing is early or late.
verses.doctrinal_note: Isaac's birth is a type of Christ's incarnation: born by divine intervention, at the appointed time (Galatians 4:4 — 'when the fulness of the time was come, God sent forth his Son, made of a woman'), to parents beyond natural ability, fulfilling a specific promise. Both births required supernatural power overriding natural impossibility.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham called the name of his son that was born unto him, whom Sarah bare to him, Isaac.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא אַבְרָהָם אֶת־שֶׁם־בְּנוֹ הַנּוֹלַד־לוֹ אֲשֶׁר־יָלְדָה־לּוֹ שָׂרָה יִצְחָק׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra Avraham et-shem-beno hannolad-lo asher-yaledah-lo Sarah Yitzchaq.
verses.study_note: Abraham obeys the divine command from 17:19: 'thou shalt call his name Isaac.' The name 'Yitzchaq' (Isaac) means 'he laughs' or 'he will laugh' — derived from 'tsachaq.' The name preserves three episodes of laughter: Abraham's laughter of wonder (17:17), Sarah's laughter of disbelief (18:12), and the joyful laughter of fulfillment (v.6). The phrase 'whom Sarah bare to him' emphasizes both parents — this is the son of the promise through this specific woman, not Hagar.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham circumcised his son Isaac being eight days old, as God had commanded him.
verses.original_text: וַיָּמָל אַבְרָהָם אֶת־יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ בֶּן־שְׁמֹנַת יָמִים כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyamol Avraham et-Yitzchaq beno ben-shemonat yamim ka'asher tzivvah oto Elohim.
verses.study_note: Abraham circumcises Isaac on the eighth day, exactly as commanded in 17:12. This is the sign of the Abrahamic covenant applied to the son of promise. 'As God had commanded' (ka'asher tzivvah) echoes the 'as He had said/spoken' of v.1 — a chapter saturated with divine faithfulness matched by human obedience. The eighth day has medical significance (vitamin K and prothrombin levels peak around day 8) and theological significance — eight represents new beginnings (Noah was the eighth person, resurrection on the first/eighth day).
verses.doctrinal_note: Circumcision on the eighth day marks Isaac as a covenant child. Paul interprets this sign as pointing to the spiritual reality of 'circumcision of the heart' (Rom 2:28-29, Col 2:11). The eighth day connects to resurrection theology — Christ rose on the first day of a new week, the 'eighth day,' inaugurating the new creation.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham was an hundred years old, when his son Isaac was born unto him.
verses.original_text: וְאַבְרָהָם בֶּן־מְאַת שָׁנָה בְּהִוָּלֶד לוֹ אֵת יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'Avraham ben-me'at shanah behivvaled lo et Yitzchaq beno.
verses.study_note: Abraham's age is stated emphatically: one hundred years old. The promise was first given when he was 75 (Gen 12:4), so 25 years of waiting have elapsed. Sarah was 90 (Gen 17:17). Romans 4:19 says Abraham 'considered not his own body now dead' — the birth required resurrection power. The number 100 in Hebrew thought carries a sense of completeness and divine fullness.

verses.kjv_text: And Sarah said, God hath made me to laugh, so that all that hear will laugh with me.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר שָׂרָה צְחֹק עָשָׂה לִי אֱלֹהִים כָּל־הַשֹּׁמֵעַ יִצְחַק־לִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomer Sarah tzechoq asah li Elohim kol-hashomea yitzchaq-li.
verses.study_note: Sarah's joyful declaration transforms the narrative of laughter. In 18:12 she laughed in disbelief and denied it (18:15); now she celebrates openly: 'God hath made me to laugh' (tzechoq asah li Elohim). The laughter is no longer secretive or skeptical — it is public worship. 'All that hear will laugh with me' — Isaac's birth becomes communal joy. The verb 'yitzchaq' (will laugh) is Isaac's name in verbal form — everyone who hears will 'Isaac' with her. God has turned her bitter, doubting laughter into the laughter of praise.

verses.kjv_text: And she said, Who would have said unto Abraham, that Sarah should have given children suck? for I have born him a son in his old age.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר מִי מִלֵּל לְאַבְרָהָם הֵינִיקָה בָנִים שָׂרָה כִּי־יָלַדְתִּי בֵן לִזְקֻנָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomer mi millel le'Avraham heiniqah vanim Sarah ki-yaladeti ven lizqunav.
verses.study_note: Sarah's second utterance is a rhetorical song of wonder. 'Who would have said' (mi millel) — the word 'millel' is rare, used only here and in Job 8:2 and Psalm 106:2, carrying a poetic, elevated tone. 'Sarah should nurse children' — the plural 'children' (vanim) is notable when she bore one son; she speaks with the fullness of one who has received more than she imagined. Her song anticipates Hannah's prayer (1 Sam 2) and Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:46-55) — barren women whose divine motherhood shatters expectations.

verses.kjv_text: And the child grew, and was weaned: and Abraham made a great feast the same day that Isaac was weaned.
verses.original_text: וַיִּגְדַּל הַיֶּלֶד וַיִּגָּמַל וַיַּעַשׂ אַבְרָהָם מִשְׁתֶּה גָדוֹל בְּיוֹם הִגָּמֵל אֶת־יִצְחָק׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyigdal hayyeled vayyiggamal vayya'as Avraham mishteh gadol beyom higgamel et-Yitzchaq.
verses.study_note: Weaning typically occurred between ages 2-5 in the ancient Near East (cf. 1 Sam 1:22-24, 2 Macc 7:27). The 'great feast' (mishteh gadol) celebrates a significant milestone — the child's survival past the dangerous infant years and his transition toward independence. 'Mishteh' (from 'shatah,' to drink) is a feast with drinking — a major celebration. This feast becomes the backdrop for the conflict that follows. Isaac is likely 2-3 years old, making Ishmael roughly 16-17 (Ishmael was born when Abraham was 86, Gen 16:16; Isaac when Abraham was 100).

verses.kjv_text: And Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian, which she had born unto Abraham, mocking.
verses.original_text: וַתֵּרֶא שָׂרָה אֶת־בֶּן־הָגָר הַמִּצְרִית אֲשֶׁר־יָלְדָה לְאַבְרָהָם מְצַחֵק׃
verses.transliteration: Vattere Sarah et-ben-Hagar hamMitzrit asher-yaledah le'Avraham metzacheq.
verses.study_note: The pivotal verse. Sarah sees Ishmael 'mocking' (metzacheq) — a Piel participle of 'tsachaq,' the same root as Isaac's name. The word is loaded: (1) At minimum, it means 'laughing' — Ishmael 'Isaac-ing,' usurping Isaac's identity. (2) The Piel intensive form suggests mockery, ridicule, or scornful play. (3) Paul interprets this as persecution: 'he that was born after the flesh persecuted him that was born after the Spirit' (Gal 4:29). Sarah sees a deeper threat than childish teasing — she recognizes a rival claim to the inheritance. Notice the narrator's distancing language: not 'Ishmael' but 'the son of Hagar the Egyptian' — lineage and identity are the issue.
verses.doctrinal_note: Paul uses this event as an allegory of the two covenants (Galatians 4:21-31): Hagar = Mount Sinai, the covenant of law, bondage, the earthly Jerusalem; Sarah = the covenant of promise, freedom, the heavenly Jerusalem. Ishmael (born of flesh) persecutes Isaac (born of the Spirit). The lesson: those born of human effort (works) will always oppose those born of divine promise (grace). 'Cast out the bondwoman' becomes Paul's call to reject salvation by works.

verses.kjv_text: Wherefore she said unto Abraham, Cast out this bondwoman and her son: for the son of this bondwoman shall not be heir with my son, even with Isaac.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר לְאַבְרָהָם גָּרֵשׁ הָאָמָה הַזֹּאת וְאֶת־בְּנָהּ כִּי לֹא יִירַשׁ בֶּן־הָאָמָה הַזֹּאת עִם־בְּנִי עִם־יִצְחָק׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomer le'Avraham garesh ha'amah hazzo't ve'et-benah ki lo yirash ben-ha'amah hazzo't im-beni im-Yitzchaq.
verses.study_note: Sarah's demand is sharp: 'Cast out' (garesh — expel, drive away, divorce). She refuses to use Hagar's name — 'this bondwoman' (ha'amah hazzo't) — reducing her to her legal status. The inheritance issue is central: 'shall not be heir with my son.' Under ancient Near Eastern law codes (Nuzi, Code of Hammurabi), a slave's son could share inheritance; Sarah insists on exclusive rights for Isaac. Paul quotes this verse directly in Galatians 4:30 as God's own word to the church. Sarah's demand, though harsh, aligns with God's covenantal purpose (v.12).

verses.kjv_text: And the thing was very grievous in Abraham’s sight because of his son.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּרַע הַדָּבָר מְאֹד בְּעֵינֵי אַבְרָהָם עַל אוֹדֹת בְּנוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyera haddavar me'od be'einei Avraham al odot beno.
verses.study_note: Abraham's anguish is real: 'very grievous' (vayyera... me'od — literally 'very evil/bad in his eyes'). The text says 'because of his son' (al odot beno) — Ishmael IS his son, whom he loves. Abraham had raised Ishmael for 14+ years before Isaac was born. He had prayed for Ishmael: 'O that Ishmael might live before thee!' (17:18). The pain of this demand cuts to Abraham's heart. This is not cold theology but a father's anguish over losing a child.

verses.kjv_text: And God said unto Abraham, Let it not be grievous in thy sight because of the lad, and because of thy bondwoman; in all that Sarah hath said unto thee, hearken unto her voice; for in Isaac shall thy seed be called.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים אֶל־אַבְרָהָם אַל־יֵרַע בְּעֵינֶיךָ עַל־הַנַּעַר וְעַל־אֲמָתֶךָ כֹּל אֲשֶׁר תֹּאמַר אֵלֶיךָ שָׂרָה שְׁמַע בְּקֹלָהּ כִּי בְיִצְחָק יִקָּרֵא לְךָ זָרַע׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Elohim el-Avraham al-yera be'einekha al-hanna'ar ve'al-amatekha kol asher tomar eleikha Sarah shema beqolah ki veYitzchaq yiqqare lekha zara.
verses.study_note: God intervenes directly. Three commands: (1) 'Let it not be grievous' — comfort. (2) 'Hearken unto her voice' — obey Sarah in this matter. (3) 'For in Isaac shall thy seed be called' — the theological reason. The phrase 'in Isaac shall thy seed be called' (veYitzchaq yiqqare lekha zara) is one of the most important covenant statements in Genesis. 'Called' (yiqqare) means 'named, summoned, identified' — the covenant line will be reckoned through Isaac, not Ishmael. Paul quotes this in Romans 9:7 and Hebrews 11:18 to establish that not all physical descendants of Abraham are covenant heirs — only those 'of promise.'
verses.doctrinal_note: Election and covenant identity: 'In Isaac shall thy seed be called' establishes the principle of divine election. Not all Abraham's physical offspring are covenant children — only those born according to the promise. Paul applies this to the church: 'They which are the children of the flesh, these are not the children of God: but the children of the promise are counted for the seed' (Rom 9:8). Spiritual birth, not natural descent, determines covenant membership.

verses.kjv_text: And also of the son of the bondwoman will I make a nation, because he is thy seed.
verses.original_text: וְגַם אֶת־בֶּן־הָאָמָה לְגוֹי אֲשִׂימֶנּוּ כִּי זַרְעֲךָ הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Vegam et-ben-ha'amah legoy asimenu ki zar'akha hu.
verses.study_note: Even as God confirms Isaac's primacy, He provides assurance about Ishmael: 'I will make a nation' (legoy asimenu). This echoes the earlier promise of 17:20. The reason is tender: 'because he is thy seed' (ki zar'akha hu) — Ishmael may not be the covenant heir, but he is Abraham's son, and God honors that relationship. God's election of Isaac does not mean the rejection of Ishmael from all blessing — it means a distinction in covenantal role. God's mercy extends beyond the covenant line.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham rose up early in the morning, and took bread, and a bottle of water, and gave it unto Hagar, putting it on her shoulder, and the child, and sent her away: and she departed, and wandered in the wilderness of Beersheba.
verses.original_text: וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אַבְרָהָם בַּבֹּקֶר וַיִּקַּח־לֶחֶם וְחֵמַת מַיִם וַיִּתֵּן אֶל־הָגָר שָׂם עַל־שִׁכְמָהּ וְאֶת־הַיֶּלֶד וַיְשַׁלְּחֶהָ וַתֵּלֶךְ וַתֵּתַע בְּמִדְבַּר בְּאֵר שָׁבַע׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashkem Avraham babboker vayyiqqach-lechem vechemat mayim vayyitten el-Hagar sam al-shikmah ve'et-hayyeled vayeshallecheha vattelekh vateta bemidbar Be'er Shava.
verses.study_note: Abraham obeys immediately — 'rose up early in the morning' (vayyashkem babboker), the same phrase used of his early rising to intercede for Sodom (19:27) and later to sacrifice Isaac (22:3). The provisions are meager: bread and a skin of water — not the wealth of a patriarch but a minimal provision. Abraham does not send her with servants, donkeys, or abundant supplies. The simplicity may reflect the pain of the moment or the testing of faith. 'Wandered' (vateta) — she lost her way, she went astray. The wilderness of Beersheba is the arid Negev — hostile, waterless terrain.

verses.kjv_text: And the water was spent in the bottle, and she cast the child under one of the shrubs.
verses.original_text: וַיִּכְלוּ הַמַּיִם מִן־הַחֵמֶת וַתַּשְׁלֵךְ אֶת־הַיֶּלֶד תַּחַת אַחַד הַשִּׂיחִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikhlu hamayim min-hachemet vattashlek et-hayyeled tachat achad hasiychim.
verses.study_note: The water runs out — the most terrifying reality in the desert. 'Cast' (vattashlek) is a strong word — she threw or placed the boy under a bush, too weak to carry him further or too distraught to hold him. The word 'hayyeled' (the child/boy) is notable — Ishmael is approximately 16-17, yet the narrative treats him as a dependent child. The shrubs (siychim) of the Negev are sparse desert bushes offering minimal shade. The scene is one of utter human extremity — the precise moment when God's provision is about to appear.

verses.kjv_text: And she went, and sat her down over against him a good way off, as it were a bow shot: for she said, Let me not see the death of the child. And she sat over against him, and lift up her voice, and wept.
verses.original_text: וַתֵּלֶךְ וַתֵּשֶׁב לָהּ מִנֶּגֶד הַרְחֵק כִּמְטַחֲוֵי קֶשֶׁת כִּי אָמְרָה אַל־אֶרְאֶה בְּמוֹת הַיָּלֶד וַתֵּשֶׁב מִנֶּגֶד וַתִּשָּׂא אֶת־קֹלָהּ וַתֵּבְךְּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattelekh vatteshev lah minneged harcheq kimtachavei qeshet ki amerah al-er'eh bemot hayyaled vatteshev minneged vattissa et-qolah vattevk.
verses.study_note: One of the most emotionally devastating scenes in Genesis. Hagar positions herself 'about a bowshot' away — close enough to see, far enough not to watch him die. 'Let me not see the death of the child' — a mother's most primal terror spoken aloud. Then she 'lifted up her voice and wept' (vattissa et-qolah) — the same phrase used of weeping throughout the Old Testament for the deepest grief (Gen 27:38, Judg 2:4). The narrator shifts attention from Hagar's weeping to God's hearing — but what God hears is the boy's voice (v.17), not Hagar's.

verses.kjv_text: And God heard the voice of the lad; and the angel of God called to Hagar out of heaven, and said unto her, What aileth thee, Hagar? fear not; for God hath heard the voice of the lad where he is.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁמַע אֱלֹהִים אֶת־קוֹל הַנַּעַר וַיִּקְרָא מַלְאַךְ אֱלֹהִים אֶל־הָגָר מִן־הַשָּׁמַיִם וַיֹּאמֶר לָהּ מַה־לָּךְ הָגָר אַל־תִּירְאִי כִּי־שָׁמַע אֱלֹהִים אֶל־קוֹל הַנַּעַר בַּאֲשֶׁר הוּא־שָׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishma Elohim et-qol hanna'ar vayyikra mal'akh Elohim el-Hagar min-hashamayim vayomer lah mah-lakh Hagar al-tir'i ki-shama Elohim el-qol hanna'ar ba'asher hu-sham.
verses.study_note: The turning point. 'God heard the voice of the lad' — this is the meaning of 'Ishmael' (yishma-El = 'God hears'), fulfilling the name given in 16:11. The angel calls from heaven — divine intervention from above. God's question 'What aileth thee?' (mah-lakh) is not ignorance but engagement, drawing Hagar into dialogue. 'Fear not' (al-tir'i) — the quintessential divine assurance. 'Where he is' (ba'asher hu-sham) — God meets people in their present condition, not where they should be or where they came from. This phrase contains extraordinary pastoral tenderness: God hears the boy right where he lies dying.
verses.doctrinal_note: God hears the outcry of the helpless. The name 'Ishmael' (God hears) is proven true in the moment of greatest desperation. 'Where he is' reveals a God who does not wait for people to reach Him — He meets them in their extremity. This is the God of Hagar, the outcast, the foreigner, the desperate mother — a God who hears and responds to human suffering regardless of covenant status.

verses.kjv_text: Arise, lift up the lad, and hold him in thine hand; for I will make him a great nation.
verses.original_text: קוּמִי שְׂאִי אֶת־הַנַּעַר וְהַחֲזִיקִי אֶת־יָדֵךְ בּוֹ כִּי־לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל אֲשִׂימֶנּוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Qumi se'i et-hanna'ar vehachaziki et-yadekh bo ki-legoy gadol asimenu.
verses.study_note: Three imperatives to Hagar: 'Arise' (qumi), 'lift up' (se'i), 'hold' (hachaziki) — God commands action in the midst of despair. 'Hold him in thine hand' (hachaziki et-yadekh bo) — literally 'strengthen your hand upon him' — using the same root (chazaq) as the angels seizing Lot's hand (19:16). There, angels dragged; here, a mother is empowered to hold. The promise is renewed: 'a great nation' (goy gadol) — the same language used of Abraham's promise in 12:2. Ishmael will be a great nation, though not the covenant nation.

verses.kjv_text: And God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water; and she went, and filled the bottle with water, and gave the lad drink.
verses.original_text: וַיִּפְקַח אֱלֹהִים אֶת־עֵינֶיהָ וַתֵּרֶא בְּאֵר מָיִם וַתֵּלֶךְ וַתְּמַלֵּא אֶת־הַחֵמֶת מַיִם וַתַּשְׁקְ אֶת־הַנָּעַר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyifqach Elohim et-eineyha vattere be'er mayim vattelekh vattemalle et-hachemet mayim vattashq et-hanna'ar.
verses.study_note: God 'opened her eyes' (vayyifqach) — the well was already there; she needed divine sight to see it. This is not a miracle of creation but a miracle of revelation. The same verb is used of Balaam's eyes being opened to see the angel (Num 22:31) and of Elisha's servant seeing the heavenly army (2 Kings 6:17). God's provision is often already present — we need eyes to see it. The skin is refilled, the boy drinks, and life is restored. 'Gave the lad drink' — the simplest act of motherhood, now laden with salvation.

verses.kjv_text: And God was with the lad; and he grew, and dwelt in the wilderness, and became an archer.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי אֱלֹהִים אֶת־הַנַּעַר וַיִּגְדָּל וַיֵּשֶׁב בַּמִּדְבָּר וַיְהִי רֹבֶה קַשָּׁת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi Elohim et-hanna'ar vayyigdal vayyeshev bammidbar vayehi roveh qashtat.
verses.study_note: 'God was with the lad' (vayehi Elohim et-hanna'ar) — a summary statement of divine provision outside the covenant line. The same phrase 'God was with' is used of Joseph (Gen 39:2,21), Samuel (1 Sam 3:19), and David (1 Sam 18:14). Ishmael grows, settles in the wilderness, and becomes an expert archer. The wilderness (midbar) becomes his domain — he is a man of the wild, as prophesied: 'a wild donkey of a man' (Gen 16:12). The bow (qesheth) will characterize his descendants, the desert warriors of Arabia.

verses.kjv_text: And he dwelt in the wilderness of Paran: and his mother took him a wife out of the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּשֶׁב בְּמִדְבַּר פָּארָן וַתִּקַּח־לוֹ אִמּוֹ אִשָּׁה מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyeshev bemidbar Pa'ran vattiqqach-lo immo ishah me'eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: Ishmael settles in the wilderness of Paran — the vast desert of the Sinai peninsula, between Canaan and Egypt. His mother arranges his marriage from Egypt, her homeland (Gen 16:1). This detail anchors Ishmael's descendants in the Egyptian-Arabian sphere, separate from the Canaanite/Mesopotamian world of the covenant line. Hagar's role as mother continues — she secures his future through marriage. The Paran wilderness later features in Israel's wilderness wandering (Num 10:12, 13:3) and becomes David's refuge (1 Sam 25:1).

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass at that time, that Abimelech and Phichol the chief captain of his host spake unto Abraham, saying, God is with thee in all that thou doest:
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בָּעֵת הַהִוא וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ וּפִיכֹל שַׂר־צְבָאוֹ אֶל־אַבְרָהָם לֵאמֹר אֱלֹהִים עִמְּךָ בְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר־אַתָּה עֹשֶׂה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi ba'et hahi vayomer Avimelekh uFikhol sar-tzeva'o el-Avraham lemor Elohim immekha bekhol asher-attah oseh.
verses.study_note: A new scene: Abimelech, king of Gerar (from ch.20), returns with his military commander Phichol. Their testimony is striking: 'God is with thee in all that thou doest' — a pagan king recognizes divine blessing on Abraham. This is the fulfillment of Genesis 12:2-3 in miniature: the nations recognize that God is with Abraham. Abimelech's approach is political — he seeks a treaty with a man he perceives as divinely favored and therefore powerful. 'Phichol' (Fikhol — possibly 'mouth of all') as army commander indicates this is a state visit, not a casual one.

verses.kjv_text: Now therefore swear unto me here by God that thou wilt not deal falsely with me, nor with my son, nor with my son’s son: but according to the kindness that I have done unto thee, thou shalt do unto me, and to the land wherein thou hast sojourned.
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה הִשָּׁבְעָה לִּי בֵאלֹהִים הֵנָּה אִם־תִּשְׁקֹר לִי וּלְנִינִי וּלְנֶכְדִּי כַּחֶסֶד אֲשֶׁר־עָשִׂיתִי עִמְּךָ תַּעֲשֶׂה עִמָּדִי וְעִם־הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר־גַּרְתָּה בָּהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah hishave'ah li veElohim hennah im-tishqor li ulenini ulenekdi kachesed asher-asiti immekha ta'aseh immadi ve'im-ha'aretz asher-gartah bah.
verses.study_note: Abimelech requests a covenant oath extending three generations: 'my son... my son's son' (nini... nekdi). He invokes 'chesed' (kindness/loyalty) — the covenant term — referring to his hospitality to Abraham in chapter 20. The request is mutual non-aggression and fidelity. 'Swear by God' (hishave'ah... veElohim) — Abimelech recognizes Abraham's God as the guarantor of oaths. The word 'tishqor' (deal falsely) means to lie or betray — Abimelech seeks assurance against treachery.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham said, I will swear.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם אָנֹכִי אִשָּׁבֵעַ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Avraham anokhi ishave'a.
verses.study_note: Abraham's reply is brief and decisive: 'I will swear' (anokhi ishave'a). He does not hesitate or negotiate the terms. This willingness to make covenants with surrounding peoples demonstrates Abraham's role as a man of peace among the nations — even while maintaining his distinct covenant identity with God. The emphatic 'anokhi' (I myself) conveys personal commitment.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham reproved Abimelech because of a well of water, which Abimelech’s servants had violently taken away.
verses.original_text: וְהוֹכִחַ אַבְרָהָם אֶת־אֲבִימֶלֶךְ עַל־אֹדוֹת בְּאֵר הַמַּיִם אֲשֶׁר גָּזְלוּ עַבְדֵי אֲבִימֶלֶךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehokhiach Avraham et-Avimelekh al-odot be'er hamayim asher gazelu avdei Avimelekh.
verses.study_note: Before completing the treaty, Abraham raises a grievance: Abimelech's servants have seized a well. In the arid Negev, wells are survival itself — they require enormous labor to dig and represent a permanent claim to territory. 'Reproved' (hokhiach) is a strong verb — to argue a case, to rebuke with evidence. 'Violently taken away' (gazelu) means to seize by force, to rob. Abraham insists on justice before peace. This principle — addressing wrongs before sealing covenants — is important: true peace requires honest dealing.

verses.kjv_text: And Abimelech said, I wot not who hath done this thing: neither didst thou tell me, neither yet heard I of it, but to day.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ לֹא יָדַעְתִּי מִי עָשָׂה אֶת־הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה וְגַם־אַתָּה לֹא־הִגַּדְתָּ לִּי וְגַם אָנֹכִי לֹא שָׁמַעְתִּי בִּלְתִּי הַיּוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Avimelekh lo yadati mi asah et-haddavar hazzeh vegam-attah lo-higgadta li vegam anokhi lo shamati bilti hayyom.
verses.study_note: Abimelech claims ignorance — he did not know, Abraham had not reported it, and he only hears of it now. The threefold denial ('I knew not... you told me not... I heard not') may be genuine or diplomatic. Either way, it preserves Abimelech's honor while opening the door to resolution. The incident mirrors the pattern of Genesis 26:15-22 where Isaac will face similar well disputes — water rights are a recurring source of conflict in the patriarchal narratives.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham took sheep and oxen, and gave them unto Abimelech; and both of them made a covenant.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח אַבְרָהָם צֹאן וּבָקָר וַיִּתֵּן לַאֲבִימֶלֶךְ וַיִּכְרְתוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם בְּרִית׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach Avraham tzon uvaqar vayyitten la'Avimelekh vayyikhre tu sheneihem berit.
verses.study_note: Abraham initiates the covenant with gifts: sheep and oxen. The phrase 'cut a covenant' (vayyikhre tu... berit) is the standard Hebrew expression — covenants are 'cut,' referring to the original practice of cutting animals and passing between the pieces (cf. Gen 15:9-18). Here the cutting may be ceremonial slaughter for a covenant meal. 'Both of them' (sheneihem) — this is a bilateral covenant, unlike the unilateral covenant God made with Abraham in chapter 15.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham set seven ewe lambs of the flock by themselves.
verses.original_text: וַיַּצֵּב אַבְרָהָם אֶת־שֶׁבַע כִּבְשֹׂת הַצֹּאן לְבַדְּהֶן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyatzev Avraham et-sheva kivsot hatzon levadehen.
verses.study_note: Abraham separates seven ewe lambs from the flock — a distinct set apart from the general covenant gifts. 'Seven' (sheva) is the number of fullness and oath-taking; the Hebrew word for 'swear' (shava) is built from the same root as 'seven' (sheva). These seven lambs become the visible, tangible witness of the oath. 'Ewe lambs' (kivsot) — female sheep, valuable for breeding and milk production. Setting them apart signals they have a special purpose beyond the general gift.

verses.kjv_text: And Abimelech said unto Abraham, What mean these seven ewe lambs which thou hast set by themselves?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ אֶל־אַבְרָהָם מָה הֵנָּה שֶׁבַע כְּבָשֹׂת הָאֵלֶּה אֲשֶׁר הִצַּבְתָּ לְבַדָּנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Avimelekh el-Avraham mah hennah sheva kevasot ha'elleh asher hitzavta levaddanah.
verses.study_note: Abimelech notices the separated lambs and asks their purpose. His question opens the way for Abraham to explain and establish a legal witness. In the ancient Near East, the meaning of symbolic acts had to be verbally declared to carry legal force. Abraham's response creates a permanent record of ownership of the well.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, For these seven ewe lambs shalt thou take of my hand, that they may be a witness unto me, that I have digged this well.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר כִּי אֶת־שֶׁבַע כְּבָשֹׂת תִּקַּח מִיָּדִי בַּעֲבוּר תִּהְיֶה־לִּי לְעֵדָה כִּי חָפַרְתִּי אֶת־הַבְּאֵר הַזֹּאת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer ki et-sheva kevasot tiqqach miyyadi ba'avur tihyeh-li le'edah ki chafarti et-habbe'er hazzo't.
verses.study_note: The seven lambs serve as legal 'witness' (edah) — proof of Abraham's ownership of the well. By accepting the lambs, Abimelech acknowledges Abraham's right. 'I have digged this well' (chafarti) — Abraham claims the labor of digging, which in ancient law established ownership. This is a brilliant piece of diplomacy: the gift becomes a legal instrument. The accepted gift is a publicly witnessed acknowledgment of Abraham's claim. Water rights, once established by this oath, could not be contested.

verses.kjv_text: Wherefore he called that place Beersheba; because there they sware both of them.
verses.original_text: עַל־כֵּן קָרָא לַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא בְּאֵר שָׁבַע כִּי שָׁם נִשְׁבְּעוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Al-ken qara lammakom hahu Be'er Shava ki sham nishbe'u sheneihem.
verses.study_note: The place receives its name: 'Beersheba' (Be'er Shava) — a name with a double meaning: (1) 'Well of the Oath' (be'er + shava/shevuah) and (2) 'Well of Seven' (be'er + sheva). Both meanings are present, as the oath was sealed with seven lambs. Beersheba becomes one of the most important sites in Israel's history — marking the southern boundary of the promised land ('from Dan to Beersheba,' Judges 20:1). Abraham, Isaac (Gen 26:33), and Jacob (Gen 46:1) all have significant encounters here. The naming of places after covenant events is a hallmark of patriarchal religion.

verses.kjv_text: Thus they made a covenant at Beersheba: then Abimelech rose up, and Phichol the chief captain of his host, and they returned into the land of the Philistines.
verses.original_text: וַיִּכְרְתוּ בְרִית בִּבְאֵר שָׁבַע וַיָּקָם אֲבִימֶלֶךְ וּפִיכֹל שַׂר־צְבָאוֹ וַיָּשֻׁבוּ אֶל־אֶרֶץ פְּלִשְׁתִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikhre tu verit biVe'er Shava vayyaqom Avimelekh uFikhol sar-tzeva'o vayyashuvu el-eretz Pelishtim.
verses.study_note: The covenant is concluded, and Abimelech returns to 'the land of the Philistines' (eretz Pelishtim). The reference to Philistines here is an anachronism or an editorial update — the major Philistine migration occurred around 1200 BC, centuries after Abraham. The term may refer to earlier settlers of the same coastal region, or Moses/a later editor used the current name for the territory. The departure of Abimelech leaves Abraham in sole possession of the well and the surrounding territory — a significant land claim in the promised land.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham planted a grove in Beersheba, and called there on the name of the LORD, the everlasting God.
verses.original_text: וַיִּטַּע אֶשֶׁל בִּבְאֵר שָׁבַע וַיִּקְרָא־שָׁם בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה אֵל עוֹלָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitta eshel biVe'er Shava vayyiqra-sham beshem YHVH El Olam.
verses.study_note: Two acts of devotion: (1) Abraham plants a tamarisk tree (eshel) — a slow-growing, long-lived desert tree that provides shade and indicates permanent settlement. Unlike an altar (temporary worship), a tree signals long-term presence and ongoing hospitality (some render 'eshel' as 'a grove' or even 'a lodging-place'). (2) He calls on 'the LORD, the everlasting God' (YHVH El Olam) — a new compound divine name. 'El Olam' reveals God as the God of eternity, the God of all ages, whose covenant spans past, present, and future. This name appears in the context of a treaty with a pagan king — Abraham declares that his covenant God transcends time itself.
verses.doctrinal_note: El Olam — The Everlasting God: This divine name reveals a new dimension of God's character: He is not bound by time. The God who promised Abraham a son 25 years earlier and fulfilled it is the God of ages. His covenants are eternal, His faithfulness spans generations, and His purposes extend beyond any human timeline. Isaiah 40:28 echoes: 'the everlasting God, the LORD, the Creator of the ends of the earth, fainteth not, neither is weary.'

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham sojourned in the Philistines’ land many days.
verses.original_text: וַיָּגָר אַבְרָהָם בְּאֶרֶץ פְּלִשְׁתִּים יָמִים רַבִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyagar Avraham be'eretz Pelishtim yamim rabbim.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes with Abraham sojourning (vayyagar — living as a resident alien) in the land 'many days' (yamim rabbim). The word 'gar' (sojourn) is the same root as 'ger' (stranger/alien) — Abraham lives in the promised land but does not yet possess it. He is the 'stranger and pilgrim' (Heb 11:13) who has received the promise but awaits its full realization. 'Many days' suggests a season of stability and peace following the covenant with Abimelech — a man at rest in the purposes of the Everlasting God. This peaceful interlude prepares for the supreme test of Genesis 22: the binding of Isaac.

meta.overview: Genesis 21 is the chapter of fulfillment — the long-promised son Isaac is finally born to Abraham and Sarah, vindicating decades of waiting and God's faithfulness to His word. The name 'Isaac' (Yitzchak — 'he laughs') transforms the laughter of disbelief (17:17, 18:12) into the laughter of joy. But fulfillment brings conflict: when Ishmael mocks at the weaning feast, Sarah demands the expulsion of Hagar and Ishmael. God confirms this painful separation as necessary for the covenant line, yet tenderly provides for Hagar and Ishmael in the wilderness, promising that Ishmael too will become a great nation. The chapter closes with Abraham making a covenant with Abimelech at Beersheba ('Well of the Oath'), planting a tamarisk tree, and calling on 'the LORD, the everlasting God' (El Olam) — a new divine name revealing God's eternal faithfulness. Three great themes converge: the faithfulness of God to His promises, the painful cost of covenant purity, and God's compassionate provision even for those outside the covenant line.
meta.theme: Covenant Fulfillment, Divine Faithfulness & the Cost of Election
meta.keyWordMeaning: To laugh — the verb that gives Isaac his name. Laughter transforms from disbelief to joy to mockery across chapters 17-21, revealing the full range of human response to God's promises.
outline: The Birth of Isaac — Promise Fulfilled (vv.1-7): God visits Sarah, Isaac born, circumcised on the eighth day, Sarah's song of joy
The Weaning Feast and Ishmael's Mockery (vv.8-13): Conflict between the sons, Sarah's demand, God's confirmation to Abraham
Hagar and Ishmael in the Wilderness (vv.14-21): Expulsion, despair, God hears the boy, the well of provision, Ishmael's future
Abraham's Covenant with Abimelech at Beersheba (vv.22-34): Dispute over the well, the oath, the tamarisk tree, El Olam

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass after these things, that God did tempt Abraham, and said unto him, Abraham: and he said, Behold, here I am.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה וְהָאֱלֹהִים נִסָּה אֶת־אַבְרָהָם וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו אַבְרָהָם וַיֹּאמֶר הִנֵּנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi achar haddevarim ha'elleh veha'Elohim nissah et-Avraham vayomer elav Avraham vayomer hinneni.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with the narrator's crucial theological lens: 'God did tempt Abraham' — but 'tempt' (nissah) means to test, to prove, to refine, not to entice to sin (James 1:13). The definite article on God (ha'Elohim — 'THE God') is unusual and emphatic, perhaps highlighting the solemnity. 'After these things' connects to chapters 20-21 — after Isaac's birth, after Ishmael's departure, after the Beersheba covenant. Abraham has finally received everything he desired, and now God asks for it back. 'Hinneni' (Here I am) — Abraham's immediate, total availability. This word appears three times in the chapter (vv.1, 7, 11) at each critical juncture, marking Abraham's readiness before God, before his son, and before the angel.
verses.doctrinal_note: God tests but does not tempt (James 1:13). Testing reveals and refines faith; temptation entices to sin. God tested Abraham not to discover what Abraham would do (God is omniscient) but to demonstrate Abraham's faith, to deepen it through exercise, and to create a prophetic picture of His own future sacrifice. Testing is grace — it produces proven character (Romans 5:3-4, James 1:2-4).

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Take now thy son, thine only son Isaac, whom thou lovest, and get thee into the land of Moriah; and offer him there for a burnt offering upon one of the mountains which I will tell thee of.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר קַח־נָא אֶת־בִּנְךָ אֶת־יְחִידְךָ אֲשֶׁר־אָהַבְתָּ אֶת־יִצְחָק וְלֶךְ־לְךָ אֶל־אֶרֶץ הַמֹּרִיָּה וְהַעֲלֵהוּ שָׁם לְעֹלָה עַל אַחַד הֶהָרִים אֲשֶׁר אֹמַר אֵלֶיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer qach-na et-binkha et-yechidkha asher-ahavta et-Yitzchaq velekh-lekha el-eretz hamMoriyyah veha'alehu sham le'olah al achad heharim asher omar eleikha.
verses.study_note: The most devastating command in Scripture, delivered with escalating intensity. God uses four descriptions, each narrowing and deepening the pain: (1) 'thy son' (binkha), (2) 'thine only son' (yechidkha — the unique, beloved one), (3) 'whom thou lovest' (asher-ahavta — the first use of 'love' in the Bible), and (4) 'Isaac' — the name itself, meaning laughter, joy. Each phrase twists the knife deeper. The Midrash says God paused between each description to let the weight sink in. 'Lekh-lekha' (get thee) is the same phrase used in Abraham's original call (12:1) — the journey of faith comes full circle. 'Moriah' connects to 'ra'ah' (to see) — the land of divine seeing/provision. 2 Chronicles 3:1 identifies Moriah as the site of Solomon's Temple — and by extension, the hill of Golgotha nearby.
verses.doctrinal_note: The typology of Christ is unmistakable: (1) 'Thy son, thine only son' — John 3:16: 'God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten Son.' (2) 'Whom thou lovest' — the first use of 'love' (ahav) in Scripture is a father's love for his son to be sacrificed. (3) 'Lekh-lekha' — the same call as 12:1, bookending Abraham's journey. (4) Moriah — the same mountain where Christ would be crucified ~2000 years later. God asked Abraham to do on Moriah what He Himself would do on Moriah. Abraham was willing; God actually accomplished it.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham rose up early in the morning, and saddled his ass, and took two of his young men with him, and Isaac his son, and clave the wood for the burnt offering, and rose up, and went unto the place of which God had told him.
verses.original_text: וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אַבְרָהָם בַּבֹּקֶר וַיַּחֲבֹשׁ אֶת־חֲמֹרוֹ וַיִּקַּח אֶת־שְׁנֵי נְעָרָיו אִתּוֹ וְאֵת יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ וַיְבַקַּע עֲצֵי עֹלָה וַיָּקָם וַיֵּלֶךְ אֶל־הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר־אָמַר־לוֹ הָאֱלֹהִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashkem Avraham babboker vayyachavosh et-chamoro vayyiqqach et-shenei ne'arav itto ve'et Yitzchaq beno vayevaqqa atzei olah vayyaqom vayyelekh el-hammakom asher-amar-lo ha'Elohim.
verses.study_note: Abraham's response is stunning in its immediacy and silence. No argument, no negotiation (contrast his intercession for Sodom in 18:23-32), no recorded hesitation. He 'rose up early in the morning' (vayyashkem babboker) — the same phrase used in 19:27 (looking at Sodom's destruction), 21:14 (sending Hagar away). Abraham performs early-morning obedience in the face of the hardest commands. He personally saddles his donkey, personally splits the wood — these are not servant tasks but a patriarch's deliberate, hands-on preparation. The narrative is spare, reporting actions without emotions, which paradoxically magnifies the emotional weight. Every detail — the wood, the donkey, the two servants, the son — becomes laden with typological significance.

verses.kjv_text: Then on the third day Abraham lifted up his eyes, and saw the place afar off.
verses.original_text: בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי וַיִּשָּׂא אַבְרָהָם אֶת־עֵינָיו וַיַּרְא אֶת־הַמָּקוֹם מֵרָחֹק׃
verses.transliteration: Bayyom hashelishi vayyissa Avraham et-einav vayyar et-hammakom merachoq.
verses.study_note: 'On the third day' (bayyom hashelishi) — three days of walking in silence with the knowledge of what lies ahead. The third day is theologically charged throughout Scripture: Joseph released prisoners on the third day (Gen 42:18), Israel was to meet God on the third day (Exod 19:11), Jonah was three days in the fish, and Christ rose on the third day. For Abraham, Isaac was 'as good as dead' from the moment of God's command; the third-day arrival at Moriah becomes a kind of resurrection — Hebrews 11:19 says Abraham received Isaac back 'in a figure' (parable) of resurrection. 'Saw the place afar off' — he recognizes the divinely appointed mountain from a distance.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham said unto his young men, Abide ye here with the ass; and I and the lad will go yonder and worship, and come again to you.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם אֶל־נְעָרָיו שְׁבוּ־לָכֶם פֹּה עִם־הַחֲמוֹר וַאֲנִי וְהַנַּעַר נֵלְכָה עַד־כֹּה וְנִשְׁתַּחֲוֶה וְנָשׁוּבָה אֲלֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Avraham el-ne'arav shevu-lakhem poh im-hachamor va'ani vehanna'ar nelkhah ad-koh venishttachaveh venashuvah aleikhem.
verses.study_note: Every word is weighed with theological significance. 'I and the lad will go yonder and worship' — Abraham calls the sacrifice 'worship' (venishttachaveh). The supreme act of obedience IS the supreme act of worship. Most critically: 'and come again to you' (venashuvah — first person PLURAL, 'WE will return'). Abraham uses the plural 'we.' Either Abraham is deceiving the servants, or he genuinely believes both he and Isaac will come back. Hebrews 11:19 resolves this: Abraham 'accounting that God was able to raise him up, even from the dead.' Abraham reasoned that if God commanded the sacrifice of the promised son, and the promise still stands ('in Isaac shall thy seed be called'), then God must raise Isaac from the dead. This is the first implicit belief in resurrection in the Bible.
verses.doctrinal_note: Abraham's faith in resurrection: The plural 'we will return' reveals Abraham's belief that God would raise Isaac from the dead — since the covenant promise required Isaac's survival. This is the logic of faith: God's promises are irrevocable, therefore death itself cannot prevent their fulfillment. Paul grounds resurrection faith in this same logic: 'if the dead rise not... your faith is vain' (1 Cor 15). Abraham's faith on Moriah is the prototype of Christian resurrection hope.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham took the wood of the burnt offering, and laid it upon Isaac his son; and he took the fire in his hand, and a knife; and they went both of them together.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח אַבְרָהָם אֶת־עֲצֵי הָעֹלָה וַיָּשֶׂם עַל־יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ וַיִּקַּח בְּיָדוֹ אֶת־הָאֵשׁ וְאֶת־הַמַּאֲכֶלֶת וַיֵּלְכוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם יַחְדָּו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach Avraham et-atzei ha'olah vayyasem al-Yitzchaq beno vayyiqqach beyado et-ha'esh ve'et-hamma'akhelet vayyelechu sheneihem yachdav.
verses.study_note: The imagery is overwhelming. Isaac carries the wood on his back — as Christ carried His cross to Calvary (John 19:17). Abraham carries the fire and the knife — the instruments of death remain in the father's hands, not the son's. 'The knife' (hamma'akhelet) is not the ordinary word for knife but a rare term meaning 'the consuming one' or 'the eater' — a large slaughtering blade. The chapter's most poignant phrase appears for the first time: 'they went both of them together' (vayyelechu sheneihem yachdav). Father and son, in unity, walking toward sacrifice. This phrase repeats in v.8 — their unity persists even after Isaac learns the truth.
verses.doctrinal_note: Isaac carrying the wood of his own sacrifice up the mountain is one of Scripture's clearest types of Christ bearing His cross to Golgotha. The father carries the fire (judgment) and the knife (death); the son carries the wood (the instrument of sacrifice). The unity of father and son ('both of them together') reflects the unity of the Father and Son in the plan of redemption: 'God was in Christ, reconciling the world unto himself' (2 Cor 5:19).

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac spake unto Abraham his father, and said, My father: and he said, Here am I, my son. And he said, Behold the fire and the wood: but where is the lamb for a burnt offering?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יִצְחָק אֶל־אַבְרָהָם אָבִיו וַיֹּאמֶר אָבִי וַיֹּאמֶר הִנֶּנִּי בְנִי וַיֹּאמֶר הִנֵּה הָאֵשׁ וְהָעֵצִים וְאַיֵּה הַשֶּׂה לְעֹלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yitzchaq el-Avraham aviv vayomer avi vayomer hinneni veni vayomer hinneh ha'esh veha'etzim ve'ayyeh haseh le'olah.
verses.study_note: The most heartbreaking dialogue in the Old Testament. 'My father' (avi) — 'Here am I, my son' (hinneni veni). The second 'hinneni' of the chapter (cf. v.1), now spoken to Isaac instead of God. Abraham is fully present to both God and his son. Isaac's question reveals careful observation: he sees fire and wood but notices the missing element — the lamb. 'Where is the lamb?' (ayyeh haseh) — this question echoes through all of Scripture until John the Baptist answers it: 'Behold the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world' (John 1:29). The question is the Bible's central question; the answer is the Bible's central message.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham said, My son, God will provide himself a lamb for a burnt offering: so they went both of them together.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם אֱלֹהִים יִרְאֶה־לּוֹ הַשֶּׂה לְעֹלָה בְּנִי וַיֵּלְכוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם יַחְדָּו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Avraham Elohim yireh-lo haseh le'olah beni vayyelechu sheneihem yachdav.
verses.study_note: Abraham's answer is simultaneously pastoral comfort and prophetic revelation. 'God will provide himself a lamb' (Elohim yireh-lo haseh) — the Hebrew is deliberately ambiguous: (1) 'God will see to it — the lamb for the offering, my son' (God will provide the lamb), or (2) 'God will provide himself — the lamb' (God will provide Himself as the lamb). Both readings are theologically true and may be intentionally layered. 'Yireh' (will see/provide) becomes the name of the place (v.14) and the verb that defines God's character: the God who sees the need and provides the answer. Then the devastating refrain returns: 'so they went both of them together' (vayyelechu sheneihem yachdav) — even after this exchange, their unity holds. Isaac now likely understands, yet walks on.
verses.doctrinal_note: Abraham's answer is the Gospel in seed form. 'God will provide himself a lamb' points forward two millennia to John 1:29 and to the cross, where God Himself provides the sacrifice. The double meaning — God providing a lamb AND God providing Himself as the lamb — captures the mystery of the incarnation and atonement. The lamb God provides is God Himself in the person of His Son. 'Yireh' (He will provide) names God's essential character: the God who sees our need before we can articulate it and provides the answer before we can imagine it.

verses.kjv_text: And they came to the place which God had told him of; and Abraham built an altar there, and laid the wood in order, and bound Isaac his son, and laid him on the altar upon the wood.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹאוּ אֶל־הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר אָמַר־לוֹ הָאֱלֹהִים וַיִּבֶן שָׁם אַבְרָהָם אֶת־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ וַיַּעֲרֹךְ אֶת־הָעֵצִים וַיַּעֲקֹד אֶת־יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ וַיָּשֶׂם אֹתוֹ עַל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ מִמַּעַל לָעֵצִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo'u el-hammakom asher amar-lo ha'Elohim vayyiven sham Avraham et-hammizbe'ach vayya'arokh et-ha'etzim vayya'aqod et-Yitzchaq beno vayyasem oto al-hammizbe'ach mimma'al la'etzim.
verses.study_note: The narrative moves with excruciating deliberation through five sequential actions: (1) arrived at the place, (2) built the altar, (3) arranged the wood, (4) bound Isaac, (5) laid him on the altar. Each verb is a separate act of obedience. 'Bound' (vayya'aqod) — this rare verb gives the entire event its name: the 'Aqedah' (the Binding). It appears only here in the Bible in this form. Isaac's binding suggests either his willing submission (Jewish tradition emphasizes Isaac's consent, as he was likely a young man strong enough to resist an elderly father) or his helpless surrender. 'Upon the wood' (mimma'al la'etzim) — Isaac lies upon the wood he himself carried. Christ was laid upon the cross He carried.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham stretched forth his hand, and took the knife to slay his son.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁלַח אַבְרָהָם אֶת־יָדוֹ וַיִּקַּח אֶת־הַמַּאֲכֶלֶת לִשְׁחֹט אֶת־בְּנוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishlach Avraham et-yado vayyiqqach et-hamma'akhelet lishchot et-beno.
verses.study_note: The climactic moment. Abraham's hand reaches for 'the knife' (hamma'akhelet — the consuming blade) 'to slay' (lishchot — the technical verb for sacrificial slaughter, the same word used for the Passover lamb in Exodus 12:6). This is not murder but sacrificial worship. The narrative slows to almost unbearable intensity: hand... knife... to slay... his son. Four beats, each one a universe of anguish. The text presents Abraham as fully intending to carry through — this is no pantomime. Hebrews 11:17 says 'Abraham... offered up Isaac' — in his heart, the sacrifice was complete. James 2:21 calls this the moment when 'faith wrought with his works, and by works was faith made perfect.'

verses.kjv_text: And the angel of the LORD called unto him out of heaven, and said, Abraham, Abraham: and he said, Here am I.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא אֵלָיו מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה מִן־הַשָּׁמַיִם וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם אַבְרָהָם וַיֹּאמֶר הִנֵּנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqra elav mal'akh YHVH min-hashamayim vayomer Avraham Avraham vayomer hinneni.
verses.study_note: The intervention comes at the last possible moment — the knife is raised, the arm is in motion. 'The angel of the LORD' (mal'akh YHVH) calls 'from heaven' (min-hashamayim). The double calling of the name — 'Abraham, Abraham' — occurs only at critical moments in Scripture: 'Moses, Moses' (Exod 3:4), 'Samuel, Samuel' (1 Sam 3:10), 'Saul, Saul' (Acts 9:4), 'Martha, Martha' (Luke 10:41). The double name conveys urgency and intimacy simultaneously. The third 'hinneni' of the chapter: to God (v.1), to Isaac (v.7), and now to the angel (v.11). At every crisis point, Abraham's response is the same: total availability.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Lay not thine hand upon the lad, neither do thou any thing unto him: for now I know that thou fearest God, seeing thou hast not withheld thy son, thine only son from me.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אַל־תִּשְׁלַח יָדְךָ אֶל־הַנַּעַר וְאַל־תַּעַשׂ לוֹ מְאוּמָה כִּי עַתָּה יָדַעְתִּי כִּי־יְרֵא אֱלֹהִים אַתָּה וְלֹא חָשַׂכְתָּ אֶת־בִּנְךָ אֶת־יְחִידְךָ מִמֶּנִּי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer al-tishlach yadkha el-hanna'ar ve'al-ta'as lo me'umah ki attah yadati ki-yere Elohim attah velo chasakhta et-binkha et-yechidkha mimmenni.
verses.study_note: The command reverses: 'Lay NOT thine hand' — the same verb 'shalach' from v.10 ('stretched forth') is now negated. God stops what God commanded. 'Now I know' (attah yadati) — not that God learned something new (He is omniscient) but that the test has produced demonstrated, public proof of Abraham's faith. 'Thou fearest God' (yere Elohim) — the fear of God is the foundation of wisdom (Prov 1:7) and the summary of covenant faithfulness. The critical phrase: 'thou hast not withheld thy son, thine only son FROM ME' (mimmenni). The angel speaks as God — 'from ME.' This echoes Romans 8:32: 'He that spared not his own Son, but delivered him up for us all.' The word 'withheld' (chasakhta) means to hold back, to spare. Abraham did not spare; God the Father did not spare.
verses.doctrinal_note: The phrase 'thou hast not withheld thy son, thine only son' directly parallels Romans 8:32: 'He that spared not his own Son.' What Abraham was willing to do, God actually did. Abraham received Isaac back from the dead 'in a figure' (Heb 11:19); God raised Christ from the dead in reality. The Aqedah is the shadow; the cross is the substance. 'From me' (mimmenni) — the angel speaks as God Himself, confirming this is a Christophany.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham lifted up his eyes, and looked, and behold behind him a ram caught in a thicket by his horns: and Abraham went and took the ram, and offered him up for a burnt offering in the stead of his son.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשָּׂא אַבְרָהָם אֶת־עֵינָיו וַיַּרְא וְהִנֵּה־אַיִל אַחַר נֶאֱחַז בַּסְּבַךְ בְּקַרְנָיו וַיֵּלֶךְ אַבְרָהָם וַיִּקַּח אֶת־הָאַיִל וַיַּעֲלֵהוּ לְעֹלָה תַּחַת בְּנוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyissa Avraham et-einav vayyar vehineh-ayil achar ne'echaz basevakh beqarnav vayyelekh Avraham vayyiqqach et-ha'ayil vayya'alehu le'olah tachat beno.
verses.study_note: Abraham looks up and sees — a ram (ayil), not a lamb (seh). The question was 'Where is the lamb?' (v.7); the answer is a ram. The full answer to the lamb question comes at Calvary. The ram is 'caught in a thicket' (ne'echaz basevakh) — ensnared by its horns, unable to free itself, provided by God at the exact moment of need. 'In the stead of his son' (tachat beno) — 'tachat' means 'in place of, instead of' — this is the clearest statement of substitutionary sacrifice in Genesis. One life replaces another. The ram dies so Isaac lives. This is the foundational logic of atonement: a substitute bears the penalty in place of the one who deserves it.
verses.doctrinal_note: Substitutionary atonement: 'In the stead of his son' (tachat beno) establishes the principle that undergirds the entire sacrificial system and its fulfillment in Christ. 'Tachat' (in place of) is the key word: the ram REPLACES Isaac; Christ REPLACES sinners. Isaiah 53:5: 'He was wounded for our transgressions.' 2 Corinthians 5:21: 'He hath made him to be sin for us, who knew no sin.' The ram caught by its horns (its 'crown') prefigures Christ wearing a crown of thorns — caught, unable to escape, dying as a substitute.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham called the name of that place Jehovahjireh: as it is said to this day, In the mount of the LORD it shall be seen.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא אַבְרָהָם שֵׁם־הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא יְהוָה יִרְאֶה אֲשֶׁר יֵאָמֵר הַיּוֹם בְּהַר יְהוָה יֵרָאֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqra Avraham shem-hammakom hahu YHVH Yireh asher ye'amer hayyom behar YHVH yera'eh.
verses.study_note: Abraham names the mountain 'YHVH Yireh' — 'The LORD Will See/Provide.' This is the chapter's key word (yireh — v.8) now enshrined as a place name and a permanent theological declaration. The narrator adds: 'as it is said to this day, In the mount of the LORD it shall be seen' (behar YHVH yera'eh) — the passive form 'yera'eh' means 'He will be seen' or 'it will be provided.' The proverb endured into the writer's own time. 'In the mount of the LORD' — this is Mount Moriah, later the Temple Mount (2 Chr 3:1), near the site where Christ would be crucified. Abraham named the place prophetically: on THIS mountain, God WILL provide. Two thousand years later, on this same mountain, God did.
verses.doctrinal_note: YHVH Yireh — The LORD Will Provide: This divine name encapsulates the entire Gospel. On Mount Moriah, God provided a ram instead of Isaac. On the same mountain range, God provided His Son instead of sinners. The name is prophetic — 'will provide' (yireh) is future tense, pointing beyond the Aqedah to Calvary. Every sacrifice on the Temple altar built on this mountain echoed this name until the final Lamb arrived. The God who sees is the God who provides. The God who provides is the God who gives His own Son.

verses.kjv_text: And the angel of the LORD called unto Abraham out of heaven the second time,
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה אֶל־אַבְרָהָם שֵׁנִית מִן־הַשָּׁמָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqra mal'akh YHVH el-Avraham shenit min-hashamayim.
verses.study_note: The angel calls a 'second time' (shenit) — the first call stopped the sacrifice (v.11); the second delivers the covenant oath. The double calling underscores the gravity of what follows. After proven obedience comes the greatest covenant affirmation Abraham has received — God swearing by Himself.

verses.kjv_text: And said, By myself have I sworn, saith the LORD, for because thou hast done this thing, and hast not withheld thy son, thine only son:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר בִּי נִשְׁבַּעְתִּי נְאֻם־יְהוָה כִּי יַעַן אֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתָ אֶת־הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה וְלֹא חָשַׂכְתָּ אֶת־בִּנְךָ אֶת־יְחִידְךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer bi nishbati ne'um-YHVH ki ya'an asher asita et-haddavar hazzeh velo chasakhta et-binkha et-yechidkha.
verses.study_note: 'By myself have I sworn' (bi nishbati) — the strongest possible oath. Since there is no one greater than God to swear by, God swears by His own being. Hebrews 6:13-18 explains: 'Because he could swear by no greater, he sware by himself.' This makes the promise 'immutable' — unchangeable by two immutable things: God's promise and God's oath. 'Ne'um-YHVH' (saith the LORD) — the prophetic oracle formula, marking this as direct divine speech. 'Thou hast not withheld thy son, thine only son' — the phrase from v.12 is repeated, now as the ground for the oath. The act of supreme obedience produces the covenant's ultimate confirmation.
verses.doctrinal_note: God swearing by Himself establishes the absolute certainty of the Abrahamic covenant. Hebrews 6:13-18 uses this oath as the foundation of Christian assurance: God cannot lie, and when He adds an oath to His promise, the result is 'two immutable things' upon which our hope rests as 'an anchor of the soul.' The covenant with Abraham — fulfilled in Christ — rests on the unshakeable foundation of God's own sworn word.

verses.kjv_text: That in blessing I will bless thee, and in multiplying I will multiply thy seed as the stars of the heaven, and as the sand which is upon the sea shore; and thy seed shall possess the gate of his enemies;
verses.original_text: כִּי־בָרֵךְ אֲבָרֶכְךָ וְהַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה אֶת־זַרְעֲךָ כְּכוֹכְבֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם וְכַחוֹל אֲשֶׁר עַל־שְׂפַת הַיָּם וְיִרַשׁ זַרְעֲךָ אֵת שַׁעַר אֹיְבָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Ki-varekh avarkhekha veharbah arbeh et-zar'akha kekokhevei hashamayim vekhachol asher al-sefat hayyam veyirash zar'akha et sha'ar oyevav.
verses.study_note: The content of the sworn oath unfolds in escalating promises. 'Blessing I will bless' and 'multiplying I will multiply' use the Hebrew infinitive absolute construction — the strongest way to intensify a verb. Two images of innumerability: stars (heavenly — spiritual descendants) and sand (earthly — physical descendants). A new element appears: 'thy seed shall possess the gate of his enemies' (veyirash zar'akha et sha'ar oyevav) — this is a promise of victory, dominion, and conquest. The 'gate' of a city represents its power center and governance. This looks beyond land possession to eschatological triumph — ultimately fulfilled in Christ's victory over sin, death, and Satan.

verses.kjv_text: And in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed; because thou hast obeyed my voice.
verses.original_text: וְהִתְבָּרֲכוּ בְזַרְעֲךָ כֹּל גּוֹיֵי הָאָרֶץ עֵקֶב אֲשֶׁר שָׁמַעְתָּ בְּקֹלִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vehitbarakhu vezar'akha kol goyei ha'aretz eqev asher shamata beqoli.
verses.study_note: The climactic promise: 'In thy seed shall ALL THE NATIONS of the earth be blessed.' This reaffirms Genesis 12:3 but with a crucial development — 'in thy SEED' (bezar'akha — singular). Galatians 3:16 identifies this singular seed as Christ: 'He saith not, And to seeds, as of many; but as of one, And to thy seed, which is Christ.' The universal scope — ALL nations — is the missional heart of the Bible. The reason is stunning: 'because thou hast obeyed my voice' (eqev asher shamata beqoli). The word 'eqev' (because/as a result of) is the same word as 'heel' — perhaps echoing Genesis 3:15 where the serpent strikes the seed's 'heel' (eqev). Obedience on Moriah unlocks blessing for all nations.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Gospel in the Abrahamic Covenant: Paul identifies the 'seed' as Christ (Gal 3:16) and this promise as 'the gospel preached beforehand to Abraham' (Gal 3:8). ALL nations blessed through ONE seed — this is the Great Commission embedded in the Abrahamic covenant. The cross (where the seed is 'bruised') becomes the means of universal blessing. Abraham's obedience on Moriah foreshadows and typifies Christ's obedience on Calvary, and both result in blessing to all nations.

verses.kjv_text: So Abraham returned unto his young men, and they rose up and went together to Beersheba; and Abraham dwelt at Beersheba.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשָׁב אַבְרָהָם אֶל־נְעָרָיו וַיָּקֻמוּ וַיֵּלְכוּ יַחְדָּו אֶל־בְּאֵר שָׁבַע וַיֵּשֶׁב אַבְרָהָם בִּבְאֵר שָׁבַע׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashov Avraham el-ne'arav vayyaqumu vayyelechu yachdav el-Be'er Shava vayyeshev Avraham biVe'er Shava.
verses.study_note: Abraham returns to the servants — 'they went together' (yachdav), the third and final use of this key word (vv.6, 8, 19). Isaac is not mentioned separately — he is subsumed in the group, quietly present. Some commentators note Isaac's silence after the Aqedah as a literary 'death and resurrection' — he disappears from the narrative and reemerges in chapter 24 when a bride is brought to him (paralleling Christ's death, absence, and return with His bride, the church). Abraham returns to Beersheba ('Well of the Oath'), the place of the tamarisk tree and the worship of El Olam (21:33) — he returns to the place of covenant and worship, now enriched by the supreme test of faith.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass after these things, that it was told Abraham, saying, Behold, Milcah, she hath also born children unto thy brother Nahor;
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי אַחֲרֵי הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה וַיֻּגַּד לְאַבְרָהָם לֵאמֹר הִנֵּה יָלְדָה מִלְכָּה גַם־הִוא בָּנִים לְנָחוֹר אָחִיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi acharei haddevarim ha'elleh vayyuggad le'Avraham lemor hinneh yaledah Milkah gam-hi vanim leNachor achikha.
verses.study_note: The chapter shifts from the supreme theological climax to a genealogical notice. 'After these things' (acharei haddevarim ha'elleh) — the identical phrase that opened the chapter (v.1), creating a literary bracket. News arrives from Mesopotamia about Abraham's brother Nahor's family. Milcah has borne children. This genealogy is not random filler — it introduces the lineage of Rebekah (v.23), who will become Isaac's wife in chapter 24. God provides the sacrifice AND the bride.

verses.kjv_text: Huz his firstborn, and Buz his brother, and Kemuel the father of Aram,
verses.original_text: אֶת־עוּץ בְּכֹרוֹ וְאֶת־בּוּז אָחִיו וְאֶת־קְמוּאֵל אֲבִי אֲרָם׃
verses.transliteration: Et-Utz bekhoro ve'et-Buz achiv ve'et-Qemu'el avi Aram.
verses.study_note: The genealogy lists Nahor's sons. 'Uz' (Utz) — his firstborn. The land of Uz is later identified as the home of Job (Job 1:1). 'Buz' — possibly the ancestor of Elihu (Job 32:2). 'Kemuel the father of Aram' — ancestor of the Aramean peoples. These names anchor Israel's kinship network in the ancient Near East.

verses.kjv_text: And Chesed, and Hazo, and Pildash, and Jidlaph, and Bethuel.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־כֶּשֶׂד וְאֶת־חֲזוֹ וְאֶת־פִּלְדָּשׁ וְאֶת־יִדְלָף וְאֵת בְּתוּאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-Kesed ve'et-Chazo ve'et-Pildash ve'et-Yidlaf ve'et Betu'el.
verses.study_note: Five more sons of Nahor by Milcah. The most significant name is the last: 'Bethuel' (Betu'el — 'dweller in God' or 'house of God'). Bethuel is the father of Rebekah (v.23) and Laban (Gen 24:29). The entire genealogy builds toward this name — God has been preparing Isaac's bride while Abraham was on Moriah. 'Chesed' (Kesed) may be the ancestor of the Chaldeans (Kasdim), connecting to Abraham's homeland of Ur of the Chaldees.

verses.kjv_text: And Bethuel begat Rebekah: these eight Milcah did bear to Nahor, Abraham’s brother.
verses.original_text: וּבְתוּאֵל יָלַד אֶת־רִבְקָה שְׁמֹנָה אֵלֶּה יָלְדָה מִלְכָּה לְנָחוֹר אֲחִי אַבְרָהָם׃
verses.transliteration: UVetu'el yalad et-Rivqah shemonah elleh yaledah Milkah leNachor achi Avraham.
verses.study_note: The genealogy arrives at its destination: 'Bethuel begat Rebekah' (Rivqah). Her name possibly derives from 'rivqah' ('captivating/fettering'). The placement of Rebekah's introduction immediately after the Aqedah is no accident: God provides the sacrifice (v.13), secures the covenant (vv.16-18), and prepares the bride — all within one chapter. Providence works on multiple fronts simultaneously.

verses.kjv_text: And his concubine, whose name was Reumah, she bare also Tebah, and Gaham, and Thahash, and Maachah.
verses.original_text: וּפִילַגְשׁוֹ וּשְׁמָהּ רְאוּמָה וַתֵּלֶד גַּם־הִוא אֶת־טֶבַח וְאֶת־גַּחַם וְאֶת־תַּחַשׁ וְאֶת־מַעֲכָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ufilaggesho ushemah Re'umah vateled gam-hi et-Tevach ve'et-Gacham ve'et-Tachash ve'et-Ma'akhah.
verses.study_note: Nahor's concubine Reumah bears four sons, bringing the total to twelve — echoing Ishmael's twelve princes (Gen 25:16) and Israel's twelve tribes. 'Maachah' becomes a significant place name in later Israelite history (2 Sam 10:6). The chapter that began with the supreme test of faith ends with a quiet genealogy — the ordinary details of family life through which God's extraordinary purposes advance. Providence works through both the dramatic (Moriah) and the mundane (births and marriages).

meta.overview: Genesis 22 is the theological summit of the Abrahamic narrative and one of the most profound chapters in all of Scripture. God commands Abraham to offer Isaac — the son of promise, the long-awaited heir, the child of laughter — as a burnt offering on Mount Moriah. Abraham obeys without recorded protest, rising early, traveling three days, and ascending the mountain with his son.
