Isaac carries the wood on his back and asks the devastating question: 'Where is the lamb?' Abraham's prophetic answer — 'God will provide himself a lamb' — becomes the thematic key of the entire Bible. At the moment of sacrifice, the Angel of the LORD intervenes, a ram is substituted, and Abraham names the place 'YHVH Yireh' (The LORD Will Provide). God then reaffirms the covenant with the strongest oath in Scripture: 'By myself have I sworn.' The chapter is the supreme Old Testament type of Christ's sacrifice: a father offers his beloved son, on the same mountain where the Temple would stand, with wood on the son's back, and God provides the substitute. The Aqedah reveals the heart of the Gospel two thousand years before Calvary.
meta.theme: The Supreme Test of Faith, Substitutionary Sacrifice & the Gospel Foreshadowed
meta.keyWordMeaning: He will see/He will provide — from 'ra'ah' (to see). God 'sees' the need and 'provides' the answer. Seeing and providing are the same act in the Hebrew mind. This word names the place and prophesies the cross.
outline: The Command — God Tests Abraham (vv.1-2): The terrifying call to sacrifice Isaac
The Journey — Three Days of Obedience (vv.3-6): Abraham rises early, travels to Moriah, wood upon the son
The Question — Where Is the Lamb? (vv.7-8): Isaac's question and Abraham's prophetic answer
The Altar — The Binding of Isaac (vv.9-10): Abraham builds, binds, and raises the knife
The Intervention — The Angel and the Ram (vv.11-14): Substitutionary sacrifice and YHVH Yireh
The Oath — God Swears by Himself (vv.15-19): The covenant reaffirmed with the strongest possible oath
Epilogue — The Family of Nahor (vv.20-24): Transition introducing Rebekah's lineage

verses.kjv_text: And Sarah was an hundred and seven and twenty years old: these were the years of the life of Sarah.
verses.original_text: וַיִּהְיוּ חַיֵּי שָׂרָה מֵאָה שָׁנָה וְעֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וְשֶׁבַע שָׁנִים שְׁנֵי חַיֵּי שָׂרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyihyu chayyei Sarah me'ah shanah ve'esrim shanah vesheva shanim shenei chayyei Sarah.
verses.study_note: Sarah is the only woman in the Bible whose age at death is recorded — 127 years. The Hebrew is unusual: literally 'one hundred years and twenty years and seven years' — the word 'years' (shanah) is repeated three times. The ancient rabbis (Rashi) saw significance in each grouping: at 100 she was like 20 in beauty, at 20 she was like 7 in innocence. While this is homiletical, the repetition is emphatic — every year of Sarah's life was significant. The phrase 'the years of the life of Sarah' forms an inclusio (bookends) around the verse, drawing solemn attention to the completeness of her life. Sarah lived 37 years after Isaac's birth (born when she was 90), meaning Isaac was 37 at the time of her death — and tradition connects her death to the trauma of the Aqedah.
verses.doctrinal_note: Sarah is honored in the New Testament as a model of faith (Hebrews 11:11) and of holy submission (1 Peter 3:6). She is the 'free woman' whose children are born according to promise (Galatians 4:22-31). That her death is recorded with such detail shows God's valuation of her life and role in redemptive history.

verses.kjv_text: And Sarah died in Kirjatharba; the same is Hebron in the land of Canaan: and Abraham came to mourn for Sarah, and to weep for her.
verses.original_text: וַתָּמָת שָׂרָה בְּקִרְיַת אַרְבַּע הִוא חֶבְרוֹן בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן וַיָּבֹא אַבְרָהָם לִסְפֹּד לְשָׂרָה וְלִבְכֹּתָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattamat Sarah beQiryat Arba hi Chevron be'eretz Kena'an vayyavo Avraham lispod leSarah velivkotah.
verses.study_note: Sarah died in Kiriath-arba ('City of the Four' — the old Canaanite name), identified as Hebron in Canaan. That Abraham 'came' to mourn suggests he may have been elsewhere — perhaps at Beersheba (22:19). Two distinct actions: 'mourn' (saphad — formal, public lamentation with eulogies and ceremonies) and 'weep' (bakah — personal, private tears of grief). The order is significant: public honor first, then private sorrow. In the Hebrew text, the letter 'kaf' in 'velivkotah' (and to weep for her) is written smaller than normal — the scribal tradition notes this 'small letter,' suggesting Abraham restrained his weeping and rose quickly to act in faith regarding the burial. Hebron means 'fellowship' or 'association' — the city of covenant friendship where Abraham had built an altar (Gen 13:18).

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham stood up from before his dead, and spake unto the sons of Heth, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיָּקָם אַבְרָהָם מֵעַל פְּנֵי מֵתוֹ וַיְדַבֵּר אֶל־בְּנֵי־חֵת לֵאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaqam Avraham me'al penei meto vayedabber el-benei-Chet lemor.
verses.study_note: Abraham 'rose up' (qum) from mourning — a powerful image of faith overcoming grief. He does not remain paralyzed by loss but acts purposefully. The 'sons of Heth' (benei-Chet) are the Hittites, a significant people group in Canaan. The Hittite Empire was one of the great powers of the ancient Near East. That Abraham must negotiate with them shows he owns nothing in the land God promised him — he is a landless pilgrim seeking even a burial plot. Faith turns grief into action.

verses.kjv_text: I am a stranger and a sojourner with you: give me a possession of a buryingplace with you, that I may bury my dead out of my sight.
verses.original_text: גֵּר־וְתוֹשָׁב אָנֹכִי עִמָּכֶם תְּנוּ לִי אֲחֻזַּת־קֶבֶר עִמָּכֶם וְאֶקְבְּרָה מֵתִי מִלְּפָנָי׃
verses.transliteration: Ger-vetoshav anokhi immakhem tenu li achuzzat-qever immakhem ve'eqberah meti millepanai.
verses.study_note: Abraham's self-description is theologically loaded: 'stranger' (ger — a resident alien without citizenship rights) and 'sojourner' (toshav — a temporary resident). He is a pilgrim in the very land God promised him. Yet he asks for an 'achuzzah' (a permanent, titled possession) — a legal stake in the land. This is the tension of faith: living as a stranger while claiming permanent rights in the promise. The word 'achuzzah' elevates this from a mere grave to a titled estate — Abraham's first tangible claim on the Promised Land.
verses.doctrinal_note: Hebrews 11:9-10,13-16 identifies Abraham as a 'stranger and pilgrim' who looked for a city whose builder and maker is God. All believers share this identity — citizens of heaven living as aliens on earth (1 Peter 2:11, Philippians 3:20). Yet like Abraham, we claim permanent stakes in God's promises while still sojourning.

verses.kjv_text: And the children of Heth answered Abraham, saying unto him,
verses.original_text: וַיַּעֲנוּ בְנֵי־חֵת אֶת־אַבְרָהָם לֵאמֹר לוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'anu venei-Chet et-Avraham lemor lo.
verses.study_note: The Hittites respond as a community. The narrative slows to record every formal exchange in this negotiation — the extensive detail validates the legality of the transaction. Moses records this carefully because the deed to Machpelah became the founding title for Israel's claim to the land.

verses.kjv_text: Hear us, my lord: thou art a mighty prince among us: in the choice of our sepulchres bury thy dead; none of us shall withhold from thee his sepulchre, but that thou mayest bury thy dead.
verses.original_text: שְׁמָעֵנוּ אֲדֹנִי נְשִׂיא אֱלֹהִים אַתָּה בְּתוֹכֵנוּ בְּמִבְחַר קְבָרֵינוּ קְבֹר אֶת־מֵתֶךָ אִישׁ מִמֶּנּוּ אֶת־קִבְרוֹ לֹא־יִכְלֶה מִמְּךָ מִקְּבֹר מֵתֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Shema'enu adoni nesi Elohim attah betokheinu bemivchar qevareinu qevor et-metekha ish mimmennu et-qivro lo-yikhleh mimmekha miqqevor metekha.
verses.study_note: The Hittites address Abraham with extraordinary honor: 'nesi Elohim' — literally 'a prince of God' or 'a mighty prince.' This title acknowledges his spiritual stature and divine favor. Despite being a landless stranger, Abraham commands enormous respect. They offer him the 'choice' (mivchar — the choicest, most select) of their burial sites. This appears generous, but in Near Eastern custom, such an offer often expects refusal or creates social obligation — accepting a gift makes you indebted. Abraham recognizes this and will insist on a proper commercial transaction.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham stood up, and bowed himself to the people of the land, even to the children of Heth.
verses.original_text: וַיָּקָם אַבְרָהָם וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ לְעַם־הָאָרֶץ לִבְנֵי־חֵת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaqam Avraham vayyishtachu le'am-ha'aretz livnei-Chet.
verses.study_note: Abraham rises and bows — not worship, but the formal bow of respect and gratitude in Near Eastern diplomacy. This is hishtachavah — a deep, prostrating bow of courtesy. Abraham is both humble (bowing to the people of the land) and dignified (negotiating from a position of spiritual authority). His gracious conduct is a model of how God's people should interact with the surrounding culture — with honor, integrity, and respect while maintaining their distinct identity and purpose.

verses.kjv_text: And he communed with them, saying, If it be your mind that I should bury my dead out of my sight; hear me, and intreat for me to Ephron the son of Zohar,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר אִתָּם לֵאמֹר אִם־יֵשׁ אֶת־נַפְשְׁכֶם לִקְבֹּר אֶת־מֵתִי מִלְּפָנַי שְׁמָעוּנִי וּפִגְעוּ־לִי בְּעֶפְרוֹן בֶּן־צֹחַר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayedabber ittam lemor im-yesh et-nafshekhem liqqbor et-meti millepanai shema'uni ufig'u-li be'Efron ben-Tzochar.
verses.study_note: Abraham diplomatically transitions from their general offer to his specific target. 'If it be your mind' (literally 'if it is your soul/desire') — 'nefesh' (soul) shows he appeals to their genuine will, not mere formality. He asks them to 'entreat' (paga — literally 'encounter, intercede with') Ephron on his behalf. Abraham knows exactly what he wants: Ephron's specific property. He has not chosen randomly — the cave of Machpelah is strategically located, and Abraham has clearly thought this through despite his grief.

verses.kjv_text: That he may give me the cave of Machpelah, which he hath, which is in the end of his field; for as much money as it is worth he shall give it me for a possession of a buryingplace amongst you.
verses.original_text: וְיִתֶּן־לִי אֶת־מְעָרַת הַמַּכְפֵּלָה אֲשֶׁר־לוֹ אֲשֶׁר בִּקְצֵה שָׂדֵהוּ בְּכֶסֶף מָלֵא יִתְּנֶנָּה לִי בְּתוֹכְכֶם לַאֲחֻזַּת־קָבֶר׃
verses.transliteration: Veyitten-li et-me'arat hammakhpelah asher-lo asher biqtzeh sadehu bekesef male yittenennah li betokhekkhem la'achuzzat-qaver.
verses.study_note: Abraham specifies: the cave of Machpelah ('the doubled cave' — possibly two chambers or a cave with a double entrance), located at the edge of Ephron's field. He emphatically states 'for full money' (bekesef male — literally 'silver of fullness'). Abraham refuses charity, refuses obligation, refuses anything that could later be challenged. He wants an irrevocable commercial transaction. Again the word 'achuzzah' (possession) — this is about permanent ownership, not a borrowed grave. 'In your midst' (betokhekkhem) — Abraham claims a place among them, permanently.

verses.kjv_text: And Ephron dwelt among the children of Heth: and Ephron the Hittite answered Abraham in the audience of the children of Heth, even of all that went in at the gate of his city, saying,
verses.original_text: וְעֶפְרוֹן יֹשֵׁב בְּתוֹךְ בְּנֵי־חֵת וַיַּעַן עֶפְרוֹן הַחִתִּי אֶת־אַבְרָהָם בְּאָזְנֵי בְנֵי־חֵת לְכֹל בָּאֵי שַׁעַר־עִירוֹ לֵאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'Efron yoshev betokh benei-Chet vayya'an Efron haChitti et-Avraham be'oznei venei-Chet lekhol ba'ei sha'ar-iro lemor.
verses.study_note: The negotiation becomes formally public. 'In the audience' (literally 'in the ears') of all the Hittites — witnesses at the city gate, the legal and commercial center of ancient towns. 'All that went in at the gate' is a legal phrase for the citizen assembly with authority to witness and ratify transactions. This is ancient contract law: public witnesses make the agreement legally binding and irrevocable. Every detail of this negotiation occurs in broad daylight before the full assembly.

verses.kjv_text: Nay, my lord, hear me: the field give I thee, and the cave that is therein, I give it thee; in the presence of the sons of my people give I it thee: bury thy dead.
verses.original_text: לֹא אֲדֹנִי שְׁמָעֵנִי הַשָּׂדֶה נָתַתִּי לָךְ וְהַמְּעָרָה אֲשֶׁר־בּוֹ לְךָ נְתַתִּיהָ לְעֵינֵי בְנֵי־עַמִּי נְתַתִּיהָ לָּךְ קְבֹר מֵתֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Lo adoni shema'eni hassadeh natatti lakh vehame'arah asher-bo lekha netattiha le'einei venei-ammi netattiha lakh qevor metekha.
verses.study_note: Ephron makes a seemingly extravagant offer: not just the cave but the entire field — for free! He repeats 'I give it to you' three times (natatti lakh). But this is classic Near Eastern bargaining. His 'generosity' actually expands the negotiation from a cave to an entire field (increasing the eventual price), and a gift would leave Abraham socially indebted. In many ancient cultures, a 'gift' of land also meant ongoing obligations, potential future claims, or reversibility. Abraham wisely recognizes this pattern and will counter with cash payment.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham bowed down himself before the people of the land.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ אַבְרָהָם לִפְנֵי עַם הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishtachu Avraham lifnei am ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: Abraham bows again — a second bow of courtesy (the first in v.7). He maintains perfect decorum throughout this emotionally charged negotiation. Even while grieving his wife, Abraham models patience, dignity, and cultural sensitivity. His repeated bowing is not weakness but wisdom — honoring others while firmly pursuing his purpose.

verses.kjv_text: And he spake unto Ephron in the audience of the people of the land, saying, But if thou wilt give it, I pray thee, hear me: I will give thee money for the field; take it of me, and I will bury my dead there.
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר אֶל־עֶפְרוֹן בְּאָזְנֵי עַם־הָאָרֶץ לֵאמֹר אַךְ אִם־אַתָּה לוּ שְׁמָעֵנִי נָתַתִּי כֶּסֶף הַשָּׂדֶה קַח מִמֶּנִּי וְאֶקְבְּרָה אֶת־מֵתִי שָׁמָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayedabber el-Efron be'oznei am-ha'aretz lemor akh im-attah lu shema'eni natatti kesef hassadeh qach mimmenni ve'eqberah et-meti shammah.
verses.study_note: Abraham politely but firmly declines the 'gift' and insists on payment. The Hebrew particle 'akh' (but, surely, only) introduces a strong contrast — 'BUT if you...' His response is masterful: he accepts the expanded scope (the field, not just the cave) but converts it from a gift to a purchase. 'I give the money for the field' — Abraham turns Ephron's language of giving back on him. 'Take it from me' (qach mimmenni) — a direct, clear commercial transaction. This is done publicly, 'in the ears of the people,' ensuring legal witnesses.
verses.doctrinal_note: David followed Abraham's example when purchasing the threshing floor of Araunah (2 Sam 24:24): 'Neither will I offer burnt offerings unto the LORD my God of that which doth cost me nothing.' True devotion involves personal cost. Grace is free but not cheap — and Abraham's insistence on paying full price models the principle that what is consecrated to God must be given with integrity, not convenience.

verses.kjv_text: And Ephron answered Abraham, saying unto him,
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַן עֶפְרוֹן אֶת־אַבְרָהָם לֵאמֹר לוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'an Efron et-Avraham lemor lo.
verses.study_note: The brief narrative pause before Ephron's next statement builds anticipation. Having failed to entangle Abraham in a gift-obligation, Ephron now pivots to naming his price — and it will be steep.

verses.kjv_text: My lord, hearken unto me: the land is worth four hundred shekels of silver; what is that betwixt me and thee? bury therefore thy dead.
verses.original_text: אֲדֹנִי שְׁמָעֵנִי אֶרֶץ אַרְבַּע מֵאֹת שֶׁקֶל־כֶּסֶף בֵּינִי וּבֵינְךָ מַה־הִוא וְאֶת־מֵתְךָ קְבֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Adoni shema'eni eretz arba me'ot sheqel-kesef beini uveinekha mah-hi ve'et-metkha qevor.
verses.study_note: Ephron names his price with theatrical nonchalance: 400 shekels of silver — then dismisses it as nothing: 'What is that between me and you?' This is the art of the high opening bid dressed in generosity. Jeremiah later purchased a field for only 17 shekels (Jer 32:9), and David bought the threshing floor of Araunah for 50 shekels (2 Sam 24:24). While direct comparisons are difficult across centuries, 400 shekels appears to be a premium price. Abraham does not haggle — he accepts immediately, demonstrating both his wealth (God has prospered him) and his determination to secure an unchallenged title.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham hearkened unto Ephron; and Abraham weighed to Ephron the silver, which he had named in the audience of the sons of Heth, four hundred shekels of silver, current money with the merchant.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁמַע אַבְרָהָם אֶל־עֶפְרוֹן וַיִּשְׁקֹל אַבְרָהָם לְעֶפְרֹן אֶת־הַכֶּסֶף אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר בְּאָזְנֵי בְנֵי־חֵת אַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שֶׁקֶל כֶּסֶף עֹבֵר לַסֹּחֵר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishma Avraham el-Efron vayyishqol Avraham le'Efron et-hakkesef asher dibber be'oznei venei-Chet arba me'ot sheqel kesef over lassocher.
verses.study_note: Abraham agrees without haggling and physically weighs out the silver (coinage did not yet exist — pre-monetary economy). 'Current money with the merchant' (over lassocher — literally 'passing for the merchant/trader') means silver of standardized commercial weight, acceptable in trade. This is not some obscure local transaction — it is conducted using the recognized commercial standard. The word 'weighed' (shaqal, root of 'shekel') emphasizes the physical, tangible, verifiable nature of the payment. Every detail — the public witnesses, the named price, the weighed silver — makes this deed legally unassailable.

verses.kjv_text: And the field of Ephron, which was in Machpelah, which was before Mamre, the field, and the cave which was therein, and all the trees that were in the field, that were in all the borders round about, were made sure
verses.original_text: וַיָּקָם שְׂדֵה עֶפְרוֹן אֲשֶׁר בַּמַּכְפֵּלָה אֲשֶׁר לִפְנֵי מַמְרֵא הַשָּׂדֶה וְהַמְּעָרָה אֲשֶׁר־בּוֹ וְכָל־הָעֵץ אֲשֶׁר בַּשָּׂדֶה אֲשֶׁר בְּכָל־גְּבֻלוֹ סָבִיב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaqam sedeh Efron asher bammakhpelah asher lifnei Mamre hassadeh vehame'arah asher-bo vekhol-ha'etz asher bassadeh asher bekhol-gevulo saviv.
verses.study_note: The legal description is extraordinarily detailed — like a modern property deed. It itemizes: (1) the field, (2) its location (Machpelah, near Mamre), (3) the cave within it, (4) all the trees within the field, (5) everything within its borders all around. The word 'vayyaqam' ('were made sure' — literally 'arose/stood') means the property legally 'stood up' or was 'established' as Abraham's. The detailed enumeration of trees was standard practice in ancient Hittite property law — trees had independent economic value and were specifically listed in transfers. Moses records this with legal precision because it establishes the deed.
verses.doctrinal_note: The first piece of the Promised Land was secured not by conquest but by purchase — not by sword but by silver. God's promises often begin with small, costly, faith-filled steps before they reach full fruition. Abraham bought a grave; his descendants inherited a nation. The cave of Machpelah became the patriarchal family tomb: Sarah, Abraham, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, and Leah were all buried there (Gen 49:29-32).

verses.kjv_text: Unto Abraham for a possession in the presence of the children of Heth, before all that went in at the gate of his city.
verses.original_text: לְאַבְרָהָם לְמִקְנָה לְעֵינֵי בְנֵי־חֵת בְּכֹל בָּאֵי שַׁעַר־עִירוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Le'Avraham lemiknah le'einei venei-Chet bekhol ba'ei sha'ar-iro.
verses.study_note: The transfer is completed: 'unto Abraham for a possession' (lemiknah — a purchased acquisition) 'in the presence' (literally 'in the eyes') of all the Hittites, before the entire citizen assembly at the city gate. The witnesses are named twice (vv.10,18), framing the transaction with double legal validation. This is the first recorded property deed in the Bible — the first tangible, legal evidence of God's land promise being fulfilled.

verses.kjv_text: And after this, Abraham buried Sarah his wife in the cave of the field of Machpelah before Mamre: the same is Hebron in the land of Canaan.
verses.original_text: וְאַחֲרֵי־כֵן קָבַר אַבְרָהָם אֶת־שָׂרָה אִשְׁתּוֹ אֶל־מְעָרַת שְׂדֵה הַמַּכְפֵּלָה עַל־פְּנֵי מַמְרֵא הִוא חֶבְרוֹן בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'acharei-khen qavar Avraham et-Sarah ishto el-me'arat sedeh hammakhpelah al-penei Mamre hi Chevron be'eretz Kena'an.
verses.study_note: The chapter reaches its solemn conclusion: Abraham buries Sarah. She is identified as 'his wife' (ishto) — their partnership in faith continues to define them. The location is described with four layers of specificity: (1) the cave, (2) of the field of Machpelah, (3) before Mamre, (4) which is Hebron in the land of Canaan. This precision ensures no future dispute about the site. Importantly, the text emphasizes 'in the land of Canaan' — Sarah's body rests in the Promised Land. Her burial there is a statement of faith: the family's future is in Canaan, not in Mesopotamia.

verses.kjv_text: And the field, and the cave that is therein, were made sure unto Abraham for a possession of a buryingplace by the sons of Heth.
verses.original_text: וַיָּקָם הַשָּׂדֶה וְהַמְּעָרָה אֲשֶׁר־בּוֹ לְאַבְרָהָם לַאֲחֻזַּת־קָבֶר מֵאֵת בְּנֵי־חֵת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaqam hassadeh vehame'arah asher-bo le'Avraham la'achuzzat-qaver me'et benei-Chet.
verses.study_note: The closing verse repeats the legal language of transfer for the final time: 'vayyaqam' (stood/established), 'achuzzat-qaver' (possession of a burial site), 'from the sons of Heth.' The repetition is not redundant — it is legal confirmation. Just as deeds are signed, witnessed, and recorded, this closing verse serves as the ratification clause. Abraham now owns the first piece of the Promised Land — a cemetery. He began his journey in Genesis 12 as a man with no land; he ends this chapter as a landowner in Canaan. The possession is small but the significance is immense.
verses.doctrinal_note: Abraham's entire earthly possession in the Promised Land was a graveyard. Hebrews 11:13 says the patriarchs 'died in faith, not having received the promises, but having seen them afar off.' Yet that graveyard was not a sign of despair but of hope — they buried their dead in Canaan because they believed God's promise for that land. The cave of Machpelah stands to this day in Hebron, now known as the Tomb of the Patriarchs, testifying to the enduring reality of Abraham's purchase and God's promise. Faith claims the future, even when the only evidence is a burial cave.

meta.overview: Genesis 23 records the death of Sarah — the only woman in Scripture whose age at death, mourning, and burial are detailed — and Abraham's purchase of the cave of Machpelah from the Hittites of Hebron. At 127 years old, Sarah dies in Kiriath-arba (Hebron). Abraham mourns and weeps for her, then rises to negotiate the purchase of a burial site. What follows is one of the most detailed business transactions in the Bible: a masterful negotiation between Abraham and Ephron the Hittite, conducted with elaborate Near Eastern courtesy. Abraham insists on paying full price — 400 shekels of silver — refusing to accept the land as a gift. This is profoundly significant: the cave of Machpelah becomes the first titled deed to the Promised Land, purchased by faith before any of the promise was fulfilled by conquest. It is Abraham's concrete, legal stake in the land God promised. The chapter is a theological statement dressed as a real-estate transaction: Abraham buries his dead in Canaan, not in Ur or Haran, demonstrating that his future — even in death — belongs to the Promised Land.
meta.theme: Faith That Buries Its Dead in the Promised Land — The First Deed to Canaan
meta.keyWordMeaning: A possession, a holding, a titled estate — from 'achaz' (to seize, to grasp). This word transforms a burial plot into a permanent, legal, irrevocable claim on the Promised Land. Abraham grasps the land by faith before inheriting it by promise.
outline: The Death of Sarah (vv.1-2): Sarah dies at 127 in Kiriath-arba (Hebron) — Abraham mourns and weeps
Abraham Approaches the Hittites (vv.3-6): He rises from mourning and requests a burial site as a 'stranger and sojourner'
The Negotiation Begins (vv.7-9): Abraham bows, requests the cave of Machpelah from Ephron son of Zohar
Ephron's Offer and Abraham's Insistence (vv.10-13): Ephron offers the field freely; Abraham insists on paying full price
The Purchase Completed (vv.14-16): 400 shekels of silver — weighed at the going merchant rate
The Legal Transfer (vv.17-18): The field, cave, and trees are deeded to Abraham before all witnesses
The Burial of Sarah (vv.19-20): Sarah is buried; the field becomes Abraham's permanent possession in Canaan

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham was old, and well stricken in age: and the LORD had blessed Abraham in all things.
verses.original_text: וְאַבְרָהָם זָקֵן בָּא בַּיָּמִים וַיהוָה בֵּרַךְ אֶת־אַבְרָהָם בַּכֹּל׃
verses.transliteration: VeAvraham zaken ba bayyamim vaYHVH berakh et-Avraham bakkol.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with three monumental facts: Abraham is old, he is advanced in days, and the LORD has blessed him in all things. 'Old' (zaken) and 'well stricken in age' (ba bayyamim — literally 'coming in the days') together emphasize that Abraham is at life's end — his remaining task is not personal survival but succession. The phrase 'blessed... in all things' (berakh... bakkol) sets the scene: Abraham is not a man of lack but of abundance, now channeling everything toward Isaac's future. The phrase echoes forward to v.36, where the servant will repeat it: 'The LORD hath blessed my master greatly.' The chapter's opening word, 'veAvraham' ('and Abraham'), links directly to chapter 23 — the burial of Sarah — reminding us that this mission begins in grief and moves toward hope.
verses.doctrinal_note: The blessing of God in 'all things' (bakkol) is the foundation for the commission that follows. Abraham does not send his servant from scarcity or anxiety but from the security of God's faithfulness. This models the posture of all kingdom mission: sent not from need but from abundance, to extend what God has already given.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham said unto his eldest servant of his house, that ruled over all that he had, Put, I pray thee, thy hand under my thigh:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם אֶל־עַבְדּוֹ זְקַן בֵּיתוֹ הַמֹּשֵׁל בְּכָל־אֲשֶׁר־לוֹ שִׂים־נָא יָדְךָ תַּחַת יְרֵכִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Avraham el-avdo zekan beto hamoshel bekhol-asher-lo sim-na yadekha tachat yerekhi.
verses.study_note: The servant is described as 'eldest servant of his house' (zekan beto — elder of his household) and 'that ruled over all that he had' (hamoshel bekhol asher lo). Most scholars identify him with Eliezer of Damascus (Gen 15:2), though the name is not given here — perhaps intentionally, as his role is to point entirely to another. The oath 'put thy hand under my thigh' (tachath yerekhi) is the most solemn form of oath in the ancient Near East. The 'thigh' (yarekh) is a euphemism for the loins, the seat of procreation and life — swearing by the covenant promise itself, the seed of Abraham. Only here (v.2) and in Gen 47:29 (Jacob and Joseph) is this oath form used in Scripture. The servant is being bound by Abraham's covenant life to fulfill this sacred mission.

verses.kjv_text: And I will make thee swear by the LORD, the God of heaven, and the God of the earth, that thou shalt not take a wife unto my son of the daughters of the Canaanites, among whom I dwell:
verses.original_text: וְאַשְׁבִּיעֲךָ בַּיהוָה אֱלֹהֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם וֵאלֹהֵי הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר לֹא־תִקַּח אִשָּׁה לִבְנִי מִבְּנוֹת הַכְּנַעֲנִי אֲשֶׁר אָנֹכִי יוֹשֵׁב בְּקִרְבּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'ashbi'akha baYHVH Elohei hashamayim ve'Elohei ha'aretz asher lo-tikkach ishah livni mibenot hakkena'ani asher anokhi yoshev bekirbo.
verses.study_note: The oath is sworn by 'the LORD, the God of heaven, and the God of the earth' — the most comprehensive divine title, affirming God's absolute sovereignty over both realms. This title anticipates the servant's prayer in v.7, where it appears again. The prohibition is explicit: no Canaanite wife for Isaac. This is not ethnic pride but theological necessity — the women of Canaan worshipped other gods; a Canaanite wife would corrupt the covenant line. The seed of Abraham must not be yoked to the seed of Canaan's idolatry (cf. Deut 7:3-4). Abraham himself 'dwells among them' (yoshev bekirbo) but refuses to become part of them — the pilgrim identity maintained to the end.
verses.doctrinal_note: Separation from spiritual corruption is essential for the preservation of covenant promise. The prohibition against Canaanite wives is not about ethnic purity but about the integrity of faith. This principle runs from here through Ezra-Nehemiah to 2 Corinthians 6:14 ('Be ye not unequally yoked') — the covenant community must not be entangled with those who serve other gods.

verses.kjv_text: But thou shalt go unto my country, and to my kindred, and take a wife unto my son Isaac.
verses.original_text: כִּי אֶל־אַרְצִי וְאֶל־מוֹלַדְתִּי תֵּלֵךְ וְלָקַחְתָּ אִשָּׁה לִבְנִי לְיִצְחָק׃
verses.transliteration: Ki el-artzi ve'el-moladti telekh velakachta ishah livni leYitzhak.
verses.study_note: Abraham's instruction: go 'to my country' (artzi) and 'to my kindred' (moladti — literally 'my birth/nativity'). This is Haran in Mesopotamia, where Abraham's family remained when he left for Canaan (Gen 12:1-5). Note that Abraham says 'my country' — still claiming it, though he left it decades ago. And yet he will not allow Isaac to return there (v.6-8). The land of Canaan is God's land of promise; Mesopotamia is the land of origin, not of destiny. The bride must come out; the bridegroom must remain.

verses.kjv_text: And the servant said unto him, Peradventure the woman will not be willing to follow me unto this land: must I needs bring thy son again unto the land from whence thou camest?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו הָעֶבֶד אוּלַי לֹא־תֹאבֶה הָאִשָּׁה לָלֶכֶת אַחֲרַי אֶל־הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת הֶהָשֵׁב אָשִׁיב אֶת־בִּנְךָ אֶל־הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר־יָצָאתָ מִשָּׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer elav ha'eved ulai lo-to'veh ha'ishah lalechet acharai el-ha'aretz hazzo't hehashev ashiv et-binkha el-ha'aretz asher-yatza'ta misham.
verses.study_note: The servant's practical question is entirely reasonable: what if the woman will not come? His alternative — bring Isaac there — reveals that he already understands the stakes: Abraham would rather the mission fail than Isaac leave the Promised Land. The servant is not being obstinate; he is seeking clarity about the mission parameters before undertaking an impossibly long journey. The form 'hehashev ashiv' (must I bring back/bring again) is emphatic — the servant wants Abraham to be completely clear.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham said unto him, Beware thou that thou bring not my son thither again.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו אַבְרָהָם הִשָּׁמֶר לְךָ פֶּן־תָּשִׁיב אֶת־בְּנִי שָׁמָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer elav Avraham hishamer lekha pen-tashiv et-beni shammah.
verses.study_note: Abraham's answer is absolute: 'Beware thou' (hishamer lekha — guard yourself) — a solemn warning. Isaac must not return to Mesopotamia under any circumstances. This prohibition is theologically loaded: the Promised Land is the land of God's covenant. To return to Mesopotamia would be to abandon the divine calling and the future God has ordained. The land itself is part of the promise. Even if the mission fails, Isaac stays in Canaan.

verses.kjv_text: The LORD God of heaven, which took me from my father’s house, and from the land of my kindred, and which spake unto me, and that sware unto me, saying, Unto thy seed will I give this land; he shall send his angel before thee, and thou shalt take a wife unto my son from thence.
verses.original_text: יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם אֲשֶׁר לְקָחַנִי מִבֵּית אָבִי וּמֵאֶרֶץ מוֹלַדְתִּי וַאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר־לִי וַאֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּע־לִי לֵאמֹר לְזַרְעֲךָ אֶתֵּן אֶת־הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת הוּא יִשְׁלַח מַלְאָכוֹ לְפָנֶיךָ וְלָקַחְתָּ אִשָּׁה לִבְנִי מִשָּׁם׃
verses.transliteration: YHVH Elohei hashamayim asher lekachani mibbeit avi ume'eretz moladti va'asher dibber-li va'asher nishba-li lemor lezar'akha etten et-ha'aretz hazzo't hu yishllach mal'akho lefanekha velakachta ishah livni misham.
verses.study_note: This is the theological heart of the commission. Abraham grounds the entire mission in three pillars of divine action: (1) 'who took me from my father's house' — the original call (Gen 12:1); (2) 'who spake unto me' — progressive revelation; (3) 'who sware unto me' — covenant oath. The entire mission rests on what GOD HAS ALREADY DONE. Now Abraham adds a fourth act: 'He shall send his angel before thee' (hu yishllach mal'akho lefanekha). The angel going before is the same language as the Exodus (Exod 23:20) — divine vanguard, clearing the path. Abraham makes a promise he cannot guarantee — except by faith in the God who has always kept His word.
verses.doctrinal_note: The sending of the angel before the servant establishes the pattern of divine accompaniment in all sacred mission. God does not commission without also going ahead. This 'angel' may be the Angel of the LORD, the pre-incarnate Christ (cf. Exodus 23:20-23, where the Angel carries God's name and cannot pardon transgression). The mission to bring the bride to the Son is carried out under divine, even messianic, superintendence.

verses.kjv_text: And if the woman will not be willing to follow thee, then thou shalt be clear from this my oath: only bring not my son thither again.
verses.original_text: וְאִם־לֹא תֹאבֶה הָאִשָּׁה לָלֶכֶת אַחֲרֶיךָ וְנִקִּיתָ מִשְּׁבֻעָתִי זֹאת רַק אֶת־בְּנִי לֹא תָשִׁיב שָׁמָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im-lo to'veh ha'ishah lalechet acharekha venikkita mishbu'ati zot rak et-beni lo tashiv shammah.
verses.study_note: Abraham releases the servant from the oath if the woman refuses to come. 'Thou shalt be clear' (venikkita — be innocent, acquitted) — legal language releasing the servant from liability. The condition is the woman's willingness: 'will not be willing' (lo to'veh — not desire/consent). Critically, Abraham insists on the woman's free consent — Rebekah must come willingly. This is echoed in v.5 and fulfilled in v.58 where the family asks Rebekah directly: 'Wilt thou go?' The bride's consent is essential to the covenant marriage. God does not coerce His bride.

verses.kjv_text: And the servant put his hand under the thigh of Abraham his master, and sware to him concerning that matter.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשֶׂם הָעֶבֶד אֶת־יָדוֹ תַּחַת יֶרֶךְ אַבְרָהָם אֲדֹנָיו וַיִּשָּׁבַע לוֹ עַל־הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyasem ha'eved et-yado tachat yerekh Avraham adonav vayyishava lo al-haddavar hazzeh.
verses.study_note: The oath is sealed. The servant accepts the commission with full solemnity. He is bound by the covenant — the most sacred obligation possible. Note the servant's title here: 'Abraham his master' (Avraham adonav). Throughout this chapter, the servant is defined entirely by his relationship to his master. He has no agenda of his own, no name given, no personal interest mentioned — only the mission. This self-effacing servant who lifts up only his master and his master's son is the profound type of the Holy Spirit.

verses.kjv_text: And the servant took ten camels of the camels of his master, and departed; for all the goods of his master were in his hand: and he arose, and went to Mesopotamia, unto the city of Nahor.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח הָעֶבֶד עֲשָׂרָה גְמַלִּים מִגְּמַלֵּי אֲדֹנָיו וַיֵּלֶךְ וְכָל־טוּב אֲדֹנָיו בְּיָדוֹ וַיָּקָם וַיֵּלֶךְ אֶל־אֲרַם נַהֲרַיִם אֶל־עִיר נָחוֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikkach ha'eved asarah gemalim migemalei adonav vayelekh vekhol-tuv adonav beyado vayyakam vayelekh el-Aram Naharayim el-ir Nachor.
verses.study_note: Ten camels — a significant caravan suitable for an ambassador. Camels were the long-haul transport of the ancient Near East, capable of carrying enormous loads over vast distances. 'All the goods of his master were in his hand' (vekhol-tuv adonav beyado) — he carries Abraham's treasures as gifts for the prospective bride. This detail is not incidental: the servant goes bearing the master's wealth to woo the bride. So the Holy Spirit goes bearing the riches of Christ — 'unsearchable riches' (Eph 3:8) — to draw the Bride to the Son. 'Aram Naharayim' (Mesopotamia — literally 'Aram of the two rivers') is the Tigris-Euphrates region, a journey of roughly 500-600 miles from Canaan.

verses.kjv_text: And he made his camels to kneel down without the city by a well of water at the time of the evening, even the time that women go out to draw water.
verses.original_text: וַיַּבְרֵךְ הַגְּמַלִּים מִחוּץ לָעִיר אֶל־בְּאֵר הַמַּיִם לְעֵת עֶרֶב לְעֵת צֵאת הַשֹּׁאֲבֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaverkh haggemaLim michhutz la'ir el-be'er hamayim le'et erev le'et tzet hasho'avot.
verses.study_note: The servant stations himself at the well at evening — precisely the hour when women come to draw water. This positioning is not accidental; it is strategic waiting. The well (be'er) is the critical meeting place in the patriarchal narratives — Isaac meets Rebekah's family through this scene, Jacob meets Rachel at a well (Gen 29:10), Moses meets Zipporah at a well (Exod 2:15-17). The well is the place of meeting, provision, and covenant formation. The servant places himself where God can work through natural cultural patterns. He does not force the encounter — he positions himself in the place of opportunity and prays.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, O LORD God of my master Abraham, I pray thee, send me good speed this day, and shew kindness unto my master Abraham.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמַר יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲדֹנִי אַבְרָהָם הַקְרֵה־נָא לְפָנַי הַיּוֹם וַעֲשֵׂה־חֶסֶד עִם־אֲדֹנִי אַבְרָהָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomar YHVH Elohei adonai Avraham hakreh-na lefanai hayyom va'aseh-chesed im-adonai Avraham.
verses.study_note: The servant's prayer is one of the most beautiful in Scripture. Before any action, before looking for the woman, he prays. He addresses God as 'LORD God of my master Abraham' — identifying himself entirely through his master's covenant. He has no standing before God except through Abraham. His two requests: 'send me good speed this day' (hakreh-na lefanai — cause to meet/happen before me) — a request for providential arrangement, and 'shew kindness' (va'aseh-chesed) unto Abraham. The first use of 'chesed' in this chapter is revelatory: the servant does not ask for anything personal. He asks for a gift to Abraham. His prayer is entirely for his master, entirely for another.
verses.doctrinal_note: This prayer is the model of Spirit-directed intercession. The servant does not pray for himself but for his master and his master's son. Similarly, the Holy Spirit 'intercedes for us' (Romans 8:26) and 'will not speak of himself' but 'will glorify' Christ (John 16:13-14). The prayer before action — before any human effort — establishes that the mission's success belongs entirely to God.

verses.kjv_text: Behold, I stand here by the well of water; and the daughters of the men of the city come out to draw water:
verses.original_text: הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי נִצָּב עַל־עֵין הַמָּיִם וּבְנוֹת אַנְשֵׁי הָעִיר יֹצְאֹת לִשְׁאֹב מָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Hinneh anokhi nitzav al-ein hamayim uvenot anshei ha'ir yotze'ot lish'ov mayim.
verses.study_note: The servant describes his situation to God: 'I stand here by the well' (anokhi nitzav al-ein hamayim). The word 'ein' here means a spring or water source — a living, flowing well, not a cistern. The servant does not ask God to send an angel to do the work supernaturally; he positions himself in the natural flow of life and asks God to work through it. This is the balance of providence: human positioning and divine appointment. 'The daughters of the men of the city come out' — the stage is set, the actors are about to appear.

verses.kjv_text: And let it come to pass, that the damsel to whom I shall say, Let down thy pitcher, I pray thee, that I may drink; and she shall say, Drink, and I will give thy camels drink also: let the same be she that thou hast appointed for thy servant Isaac; and thereby shall I know that thou hast shewed kindness unto my master.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה הַנַּעֲרָ אֲשֶׁר אֹמַר אֵלֶיהָ הַטִּי־נָא כַדֵּךְ וְאֶשְׁתֶּה וְאָמְרָה שְׁתֵה וְגַם־גְּמַלֶּיךָ אַשְׁקֶה אֹתָהּ הֹכַחְתָּ לְעַבְדְּךָ לְיִצְחָק וּבָהּ אֵדַע כִּי־עָשִׂיתָ חֶסֶד עִם־אֲדֹנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah hanna'ara asher omar eleha hatti-na khaddekh ve'eshteh ve'amerah shteh vegam-gemalekha ashkeh otah hokhakhta le'avdekha leYitzhak uvah eda ki-asita chesed im-adonai.
verses.study_note: The servant's prayer includes a specific, testable sign. He asks not for an angelic vision or a voice from heaven but for a character test: extraordinary generosity. Drawing water for ten camels was enormously laborious — camels can drink 20-25 gallons each, meaning this girl would draw perhaps 200-250 gallons of water. The servant asks God to identify Isaac's bride by her extraordinary initiative, generosity, and servant-heart. He is not looking for beauty or wealth — he is looking for a woman whose character reflects the quality of the master's house. 'Thou hast shewed kindness' (asita chesed) — second use of chesed — the sign will confirm that God's covenant love is active.
verses.doctrinal_note: The sign the servant requests is a character sign, not a magical one. God's guidance is here confirmed through the revelation of virtue: extraordinary generosity, servant-heartedness, and initiative. This establishes a principle of divine guidance: God often confirms His will through providential circumstances aligned with character excellence. The one who will be fit for the Son will reflect the Son's own generosity.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, before he had done speaking, that, behold, Rebekah came out, who was born to Bethuel, son of Milcah, the wife of Nahor, Abraham’s brother, with her pitcher upon her shoulder.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי הוּא טֶרֶם כִּלָּה לְדַבֵּר וְהִנֵּה רִבְקָה יֹצֵאת אֲשֶׁר יֻלְּדָה לִבְתוּאֵל בֶּן־מִלְכָּה אֵשֶׁת נָחוֹר אֲחִי אַבְרָהָם וְכַדָּהּ עַל־שִׁכְמָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi hu terem killah ledabber vehineh Rivkah yotze'et asher yulledah livtu'el ben-milkah eshet Nachor achi Avraham vekhaddah al-shikhmah.
verses.study_note: One of the most dramatic moments in Scripture: 'before he had done speaking' (terem killah ledabber), Rebekah appears. God's answer comes even before the prayer is complete. The narrative beautifully captures both the immediacy and the specificity of divine response. Rebekah is immediately identified with full genealogical precision: daughter of Bethuel, son of Milcah, wife of Nahor, Abraham's brother (Gen 22:20-24). She is family — she is Abraham's great-niece. She comes with her pitcher on her shoulder, exactly as the servant had specified in his prayer. 'Hineh' (behold!) signals the narrative's own wonder at the immediate answer.

verses.kjv_text: And the damsel was very fair to look upon, a virgin, neither had any man known her: and she went down to the well, and filled her pitcher, and came up.
verses.original_text: וְהַנַּעֲרָ טֹבַת מַרְאֶה מְאֹד בְּתוּלָה וְאִישׁ לֹא יְדָעָהּ וַתֵּרֶד הָעַיְנָה וַתְּמַלֵּא כַדָּהּ וַתַּעַל׃
verses.transliteration: Vehanna'ara tovath mar'eh me'od betulah ve'ish lo yeda'ah vatted ha'aynah vattemalle khaddah vatta'al.
verses.study_note: The narrator introduces Rebekah with a three-part description: (1) 'very fair to look upon' (tovath mar'eh me'od) — beautiful, (2) 'a virgin' (betulah), (3) 'neither had any man known her' (ve'ish lo yeda'ah) — the double emphasis on her purity is emphatic. The beauty is noted but not made primary — character will prove more important. Rebekah goes down to the spring, fills her pitcher, and comes back up — the routine action that will change the course of history. The verbs are simple and matter-of-fact: 'went down, filled, came up.' Into this ordinary moment, extraordinary providence steps.

verses.kjv_text: And the servant ran to meet her, and said, Let me, I pray thee, drink a little water of thy pitcher.
verses.original_text: וַיָּרָץ הָעֶבֶד לִקְרָאתָהּ וַיֹּאמֶר הַגְמִיאִינִי נָא מְעַט־מַיִם מִכַּדֵּךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaratz ha'eved likratah vayomer hagmi'ini na me'at-mayim mikkaddekh.
verses.study_note: The servant 'ran' (vayyaratz) to meet her — the same urgent movement as Abraham running to welcome his visitors (18:2) and as Laban running to meet the servant (v.29). Running signals eager, wholehearted initiative. His request is deliberately modest: 'a little water' (me'at-mayim) — just enough to initiate the test. He asks for the minimum, watching to see if she will give the maximum. The word 'hagmi'ini' (give me to drink/let me swallow) suggests he wants her to hold the pitcher to his lips — a direct personal service.

verses.kjv_text: And she said, Drink, my lord: and she hasted, and let down her pitcher upon her hand, and gave him drink.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר שְׁתֵה אֲדֹנִי וַתְּמַהֵר וַתֹּרֶד כַּדָּהּ עַל־יָדָהּ וַתַּשְׁקֵהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomer shteh adoni vattemahher vattoredh khaddah al-yadah vattashkehu.
verses.study_note: Rebekah's response is immediate and generous. She calls him 'my lord' (adoni) — showing respect for a stranger. She 'hastened' (vattemahher — same root as 'hurried') — she does not merely fulfill the minimum but moves with urgency. She lowers her pitcher onto her hand (a practical maneuver to offer it more easily) and gives him drink. The sign's first half is fulfilled: she offers water willingly and immediately. But the critical second half — volunteering to water the camels — has not yet appeared. The servant watches and waits.

verses.kjv_text: And when she had done giving him drink, she said, I will draw water for thy camels also, until they have done drinking.
verses.original_text: וַתְּכַל לְהַשְׁקֹתוֹ וַתֹּאמֶר גַּם לִגְמַלֶּיךָ אֶשְׁאָב עַד אִם־כִּלּוּ לִשְׁתֹּת׃
verses.transliteration: Vatekhal lehashkoto vattomer gam ligmalekha esh'av ad im-killu lishtot.
verses.study_note: The defining moment. When she finishes giving the man drink, she volunteers — entirely on her own initiative — to water all the camels 'until they have done drinking.' This is the sign. She does not wait to be asked. She sees a need and acts on it fully. 'Until they have done drinking' (ad im-killu lishtot) — she commits not to a token gesture but to the complete task, however long it takes. Ten camels, perhaps 200+ gallons of water, drawn by hand, one pitcher at a time. This extraordinary generosity — unsolicited, wholehearted, costly — is exactly what the servant asked God to send.

verses.kjv_text: And she hasted, and emptied her pitcher into the trough, and ran again unto the well to draw water, and drew for all his camels.
verses.original_text: וַתְּמַהֵר וַתְּעַר כַּדָּהּ אֶל־הַשֹּׁקֶת וַתָּרָץ עוֹד אֶל־הַבְּאֵר לִשְׁאֹב וַתִּשְׁאַב לְכָל־גְּמַלָּיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vattemahher vatte'ar khaddah el-hashsoket vattaratz od el-habber lish'ov vattiish'av lekhol-gemalav.
verses.study_note: Four action verbs capture Rebekah's explosive energy: she 'hastened' (vattemahher), 'emptied' (vatte'ar) her pitcher into the trough, 'ran' (vattaratz) back to the well, and 'drew' (vattiish'av) for all the camels. She empties, runs, draws — again and again, repeatedly, without complaint. The word 'ran' (vattaratz) is notable — she runs to the well, not walks. Her generosity is expressed with joyful, vigorous urgency. The servant watches in silence, evaluating every movement. The bride of the promised son runs to serve a stranger.

verses.kjv_text: And the man wondering at her held his peace, to wit whether the LORD had made his journey prosperous or not.
verses.original_text: וְהָאִישׁ מִשְׁתָּאֵה לָהּ מַחֲרִישׁ לָדַעַת הַהִצְלִיחַ יְהוָה דַּרְכּוֹ אִם־לֹא׃
verses.transliteration: Veha'ish mishta'eh lah macharish lada'at hahitzliach YHVH darko im-lo.
verses.study_note: One of the most beautifully restrained moments in all Scripture. The servant 'wondering at her' (mishta'eh — gazing in astonishment, marveling) while 'holding his peace' (macharish — silent, still). He watches Rebekah run back and forth to the well, his heart rising with the knowledge that this is the one — but he waits for complete confirmation before acting. The phrase 'to wit whether the LORD had made his journey prosperous' (lada'at hahitzliach YHVH darko) — he is still checking, still verifying. The Hebrew 'hatzliach' (made prosperous/successful) is the root of 'Tzelach' — divine enablement, the same root as in Joseph's prosperity (Gen 39:3). He waits on God, not on his own assessment.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, as the camels had done drinking, that the man took a golden earring of half a shekel weight, and two bracelets for her hands of ten shekels weight of gold;
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כַּאֲשֶׁר כִּלּוּ הַגְּמַלִּים לִשְׁתּוֹת וַיִּקַּח הָאִישׁ נֶזֶם זָהָב בֶּקַע מִשְׁקָלוֹ וּשְׁנֵי צְמִידִים עַל־יָדֶיהָ עֲשָׂרָה זָהָב מִשְׁקָלָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi ka'asher killu haggemaLim lishtot vayyikkach ha'ish nezem zahav beka mishkalo ushenei tzemidin al-yadeha asarah zahav mishkalam.
verses.study_note: When the camels finish drinking — when the sign is completely fulfilled — the servant acts. He produces gifts: a golden earring (nezem — a nose-ring in the ancient Near East) of half a shekel weight (beka, about 5.7 grams), and two gold bracelets (tzemidin) of ten shekels (about 114 grams) total. These are betrothal gifts — the first tokens of covenant commitment from the master to the prospective bride. The servant gives immediately upon confirmation. The lavishness of the gifts signals that these come from a wealthy house — he is representing Abraham's house, not his own.

verses.kjv_text: And said, Whose daughter art thou? tell me, I pray thee: is there room in thy father’s house for us to lodge in?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר בַּת־מִי אַתְּ הַגִּידִי נָא לִי הֲיֵשׁ בֵּית־אָבִיךְ מָקוֹם לָנוּ לָלִין׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer bat-mi at haggidi na li hayesh beit-avikh makom lanu lalin.
verses.study_note: Now the servant speaks. Two questions: (1) 'Whose daughter art thou?' — identity and lineage, and (2) 'Is there room in thy father's house for us to lodge?' — hospitality and access. He needs to know who she is before proceeding. The genealogy is everything — only from Abraham's family will God's provision come. His question is calm and direct, not presumptuous. The gifts already given are a signal of serious intent; now he seeks to understand whether the sign has been fully confirmed through her identity.

verses.kjv_text: And she said unto him, I am the daughter of Bethuel the son of Milcah, which she bare unto Nahor.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו בַּת־בְּתוּאֵל אָנֹכִי בֶּן־מִלְכָּה אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה לְנָחוֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomer elav bat-Betu'el anokhi ben-milkah asher yaledah leNachor.
verses.study_note: Rebekah's answer is complete and genealogically precise: 'I am the daughter of Bethuel the son of Milcah, which she bare unto Nahor.' This is exactly the family the servant was sent to find. The answer to the first question confirms that God has guided perfectly. She is Nahor's granddaughter — Abraham's grandniece. The servant is hearing exactly the words he had hoped and prayed to hear. The genealogy of grace: God did not leave the servant to fumble among strangers but guided him to the precise family, the precise woman, at the precise hour.

verses.kjv_text: She said moreover unto him, We have both straw and provender enough, and room to lodge in.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו גַּם־תֶּבֶן גַּם־מִסְפּוֹא רַב עִמָּנוּ גַּם־מָקוֹם לָלוּן׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomer elav gam-teven gam-mispo ra rab immanu gam-makom lalun.
verses.study_note: Rebekah answers both questions: both straw (teven) and feed (mispo'a — fodder) in abundance, and room to lodge. The threefold 'gam' (also, and) — 'also straw, also fodder, also room' — is enthusiastic and generous. The family's hospitality mirrors Rebekah's own character: abundance offered freely. Her family will prove to be of the same hospitable spirit. The servant has food and shelter promised before he has even spoken of his mission. Providence continues to arrange everything.

verses.kjv_text: And the man bowed down his head, and worshipped the LORD.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקֹּד הָאִישׁ וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikkod ha'ish vayyishtachu laYHVH.
verses.study_note: Before anything else — before going to the house, before negotiating, before celebrating — the servant bows and worships. The confirmation of providence produces immediate worship. 'Bowed down' (vayyikkod — a deep forward bow) and 'worshipped' (vayyishtachu — prostrated) together describe total physical and spiritual prostration before God. The servant had prayed (v.12), watched (v.21), and now, upon confirmation, worships before he does anything else. This response to answered prayer is a model: recognition of grace produces adoration, not self-congratulation. The mission's success is attributed entirely to God.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Blessed be the LORD God of my master Abraham, who hath not left destitute my master of his mercy and his truth: I being in the way, the LORD led me to the house of my master’s brethren.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר בָּרוּךְ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲדֹנִי אַבְרָהָם אֲשֶׁר לֹא־עָזַב חַסְדּוֹ וַאֲמִתּוֹ מֵעִם אֲדֹנִי אָנֹכִי בַּדֶּרֶךְ נָחַנִי יְהוָה בֵּית אֲחֵי אֲדֹנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer barukh YHVH Elohei adonai Avraham asher lo-azav chasdo va'amitto me'im adonai anokhi badderekh nachani YHVH beit achei adonai.
verses.study_note: This verse is the theological summit of the chapter. Three great affirmations: (1) 'Blessed be the LORD' — the servant's first word is praise (barukh — blessed, praised). (2) 'Who hath not left destitute my master of his mercy and his truth' (asher lo-azav chasdo va'amitto) — the third and culminating use of 'chesed' in this chapter, now paired with 'emet' (truth/faithfulness). 'Chesed va'emet' (lovingkindness and faithfulness) is one of the great paired expressions of God's covenant character, appearing throughout the Psalms (25:10, 40:11, 86:15, 89:14) and supremely in John 1:14 ('grace and truth'). (3) 'I being in the way, the LORD led me' (anokhi badderekh nachani YHVH) — one of the Bible's most concise and profound statements of divine providence. 'In the way' means while I was traveling, simply going about the commission — God guided. He did not receive a vision; he was guided while walking in obedient mission. Providence works through ordinary faithfulness.
verses.doctrinal_note: Chesed va'emet: The twin pillars of God's covenant character appear here for the first time as a formal pair. They recur throughout the Psalter and reach their fullest expression in John 1:14 — 'full of grace and truth' — where the same two attributes describe the incarnate Word. The servant's doxology teaches that all providential guidance is an expression of these two attributes working together: God's covenant love (chesed) is the motive; His covenant faithfulness (emet) is the guarantee.

verses.kjv_text: And the damsel ran, and told them of her mother’s house these things.
verses.original_text: וַתָּרָץ הַנַּעֲרָ וַתַּגֵּד לְבֵית אִמָּהּ כַּדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vattaratz hanna'ara vattagged leveit immah kaddevarim ha'elleh.
verses.study_note: Rebekah runs home. Again 'ran' (vattaratz) — the same urgent movement as before. She goes to 'her mother's house' (beit immah) — an interesting detail; normally one would expect 'her father's house.' The 'mother's house' appears in Song of Solomon (3:4, 8:2) as the intimate family circle. Perhaps her mother's tent was the domestic center where women gathered and decisions about daughters were made. She tells 'these things' — all that has happened at the well.

verses.kjv_text: And Rebekah had a brother, and his name was Laban: and Laban ran out unto the man, unto the well.
verses.original_text: וּלְרִבְקָה אָח וּשְׁמוֹ לָבָן וַיָּרָץ לָבָן אֶל־הָאִישׁ הַחוּצָה אֶל־הָעָיִן׃
verses.transliteration: UleRivkah ach ushemo Lavan vayyaratz Lavan el-ha'ish hachutzah el-ha'ayin.
verses.study_note: Laban runs — but the narrator's next verse (v.30) will reveal his motivation. The immediate introduction of Laban as Rebekah's brother is significant: this is the man who will later deceive Jacob (Gen 29-31). Here he runs to the well, ostensibly in hospitality. But v.30 shows that he sees the ring and the bracelets first. Laban is consistently a man motivated by material gain. His 'running' in this chapter is enthusiasm for wealth; his hospitality will prove transactional. The contrast with Rebekah's generous, spontaneous spirit is already implied.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when he saw the earring and bracelets upon his sister’s hands, and when he heard the words of Rebekah his sister, saying, Thus spake the man unto me; that he came unto the man; and, behold, he stood by the camels at the well.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כִּרְאֹת אֶת־הַנֶּזֶם וְאֶת־הַצְּמִדִים עַל־יְדֵי אֲחֹתוֹ וּכְשָׁמְעוֹ אֶת־דִּבְרֵי רִבְקָה אֲחֹתוֹ לֵאמֹר כֹּה־דִבֶּר אֵלַי הָאִישׁ וַיָּבֹא אֶל־הָאִישׁ וְהִנֵּה עֹמֵד עַל־הַגְּמַלִּים עַל־הָעָיִן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi kir'ot et-hannezem ve'et-hatztzmidim al-yedei achoto ukheshome'o et-divrei Rivkah achoto lemor koh-dibber elai ha'ish vayyavo el-ha'ish vehineh omed al-haggemaLim al-ha'ayin.
verses.study_note: The narrator is transparent about Laban's motivation: he comes when he sees the gold ('the earring and the bracelets upon his sister's hands') AND hears Rebekah's account. The gold comes first in the list. This is subtle but unmistakable characterization. The servant is still at the well, standing by the camels — patiently waiting, having worshipped (v.26). 'And behold' (vehineh) — when Laban arrives, he finds the servant exactly where Rebekah left him, standing faithfully by the camels. The servant's composure and patience speak of one who is settled in his mission.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Come in, thou blessed of the LORD; wherefore standest thou without? for I have prepared the house, and room for the camels.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר בֹּא בְּרוּךְ יְהוָה לָמָּה תַעֲמֹד בַּחוּץ וְאָנֹכִי פִּנִּיתִי הַבַּיִת וּמָקוֹם לַגְּמַלִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer bo berukh YHVH lammah ta'amod bacchutz ve'anokhi pinniti habbayit umakom laggemaLim.
verses.study_note: Laban's welcome is effusive: 'Come in, thou blessed of the LORD' (bo berukh YHVH). This invocation of divine blessing is either genuine or calculated flattery; in context, it seems likely to be socially formulaic in the mouth of a man who has been noticing gold rings. Yet the irony is real — the servant IS blessed of the LORD; the mission he carries IS from God. 'I have prepared the house' (pinniti habbayit) — moved furniture, cleared space. Laban is a competent host when there is something in it for him. The camels are accommodated — attention to the camels is attention to the wealth they carry.

verses.kjv_text: And the man came into the house: and he ungirded his camels, and gave straw and provender for the camels, and water to wash his feet, and the men’s feet that were with him.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא הָאִישׁ הַבַּיְתָה וַיְפַתַּח הַגְּמַלִּים וַיִּתֵּן תֶּבֶן וּמִסְפּוֹא לַגְּמַלִּים וּמַיִם לִרְחֹץ רַגְלָיו וְרַגְלֵי הָאֲנָשִׁים אֲשֶׁר אִתּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo ha'ish habbaytah vayefattach haggemaLim vayyitten teven umispo'a laggemaLim umayim lirchotz raglaiv veraglei ha'anashim asher itto.
verses.study_note: The hospitality of the ancient Near East in full detail: camels unloaded, straw and fodder provided, water for foot-washing given to the servant and his companions. The order is telling — camels first, then the men. This is practical wisdom; animals must be cared for before their owners can rest. The 'men with him' (ha'anashim asher itto) reveals the servant did not come alone — he had a retinue, appropriate for a diplomatic mission. Now the household has arranged welcome for both animals and men.

verses.kjv_text: And there was set meat before him to eat: but he said, I will not eat, until I have told mine errand. And he said, Speak on.
verses.original_text: וַיּוּשַׂם לְפָנָיו לֶאֱכֹל וַיֹּאמֶר לֹא אֹכַל עַד אִם־דִּבַּרְתִּי דְבָרָי וַיֹּאמֶר דַּבֵּר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyusam lefanav le'ekhol vayomer lo okhal ad im-dibbarti devarai vayomer dabber.
verses.study_note: The servant refuses food before speaking. 'I will not eat until I have told mine errand' (lo okhal ad im-dibbarti devarai). This is more than politeness — it is the statement of a man absolutely consumed by his mission. His master's business takes priority over his own physical needs. He will not rest, eat, or relax until the message is delivered. This single-minded devotion to the master's errand is the hallmark of the Spirit-type: the Holy Spirit does not seek His own comfort or satisfaction but the completion of the Father's mission for the Son. Laban's response is appropriately brief: 'Speak' (dabber).

verses.kjv_text: And he said, I am Abraham’s servant.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמַר עֶבֶד אַבְרָהָם אָנֹכִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomar eved Avraham anokhi.
verses.study_note: The shortest possible self-identification, and the most complete. 'I am Abraham's servant.' (Eved Avraham anokhi). He does not give his own name. He does not describe his own qualities, his rank, his personal history. He is defined entirely by his master. The emphatic 'anokhi' (I) is placed for stress — but the stress is not on himself; it is on the relationship. This is his identity: Abraham's servant. In the same way, the Holy Spirit's identity and mission are defined entirely by the One who sends Him. He speaks not of Himself but of the Father and the Son (John 16:13-14).

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD hath blessed my master greatly; and he is become great: and he hath given him flocks, and herds, and silver, and gold, and menservants, and maidservants, and camels, and asses.
verses.original_text: וַיהוָה בֵּרַךְ אֶת־אֲדֹנִי מְאֹד וַיִּגְדָּל וַיִּתֶּן־לוֹ צֹאן וּבָקָר וְכֶסֶף וְזָהָב וַעֲבָדִם וּשְׁפָחֹת וּגְמַלִּים וַחֲמֹרִים׃
verses.transliteration: VaYHVH berakh et-adonai me'od vayyigdal vayyitten-lo tzon uvakar vekhesef vezahav va'avadim ushfachot uggemaLim vachamorim.
verses.study_note: The servant begins not with the request but with the testimony. He starts where all good gospel testimony starts: with what God has done. 'The LORD hath blessed my master greatly' (YHVH berakh et-adonai me'od) — this echoes v.1 ('the LORD had blessed Abraham in all things'). He catalogs the blessing: flocks, herds, silver, gold, servants, camels, donkeys. This listing serves multiple purposes: it establishes Abraham's covenant credibility (God keeps His promises), it signals the wealth the bride will be entering, and it implicitly presents the gifts the servant carries as coming from a house of genuine abundance.

verses.kjv_text: And Sarah my master’s wife bare a son to my master when she was old: and unto him hath he given all that he hath.
verses.original_text: וַתֵּלֶד שָׂרָה אֵשֶׁת אֲדֹנִי בֵן לַאדֹנִי אַחֲרֵי זִקְנָתָהּ וַיִּתֶּן־לוֹ אֶת־כָּל־אֲשֶׁר־לוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vateled Sarah eshet adonai ven ladonai acharei ziknata vayyitten-lo et-kol-asher-lo.
verses.study_note: Now the servant introduces the son. Sarah bore a son 'when she was old' (acharei ziknata) — the miracle birth is not glossed over. The impossibility of the birth is part of the testimony: God gave a son when no human means could have. And everything is given to this son: 'unto him hath he given all that he hath' (vayyitten-lo et-kol-asher-lo). Isaac is heir of everything. This is the son the bride is being brought to. The servant presents Isaac's inheritance to motivate the family — but it is also a theological picture: the Son is 'heir of all things' (Heb 1:2).

verses.kjv_text: And my master made me swear, saying, Thou shalt not take a wife to my son of the daughters of the Canaanites, in whose land I dwell:
verses.original_text: וַיַּשְׁבִּעֵנִי אֲדֹנִי לֵאמֹר לֹא־תִקַּח אִשָּׁה לִבְנִי מִבְּנוֹת הַכְּנַעֲנִי אֲשֶׁר אָנֹכִי יֹשֵׁב בְּאַרְצוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashbi'eni adonai lemor lo-tikkach ishah livni mibenot hakkena'ani asher anokhi yoshev be'artzo.
verses.study_note: The servant accurately reports the prohibition against Canaanite wives. He repeats the commission almost word-for-word from vv.3-4, showing his fidelity to the exact instruction of his master. A faithful servant does not embellish, softens, or improvise the master's instructions — he delivers them precisely as received. The repetition also emphasizes the point to Laban and Bethuel: this is not the servant's preference but the master's solemn requirement.

verses.kjv_text: But thou shalt go unto my father’s house, and to my kindred, and take a wife unto my son.
verses.original_text: אִם־לֹא אֶל־בֵּית־אָבִי תֵּלֵךְ וְאֶל־מִשְׁפַּחְתִּי וְלָקַחְתָּ אִשָּׁה לִבְנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Im-lo el-beit-avi telekh ve'el-mishpachti velakachta ishah livni.
verses.study_note: The positive instruction: go to my father's house (beit-avi) and to my family (mishpachti — kindred, extended clan). The servant now brings his narrative to the household in which he currently sits — he is in the very house to which he was sent. This is providence confirmed in the telling: 'and here I am.' Every word of the commission has been accomplished; every element of the prayer has been answered; every step of the journey has been guided. The servant stands before the family of Abraham's kin, exactly as commanded.

verses.kjv_text: And I said unto my master, Peradventure the woman will not follow me.
verses.original_text: וָאֹמַר אֶל־אֲדֹנִי אֻלַי לֹא־תֵלֵךְ הָאִשָּׁה אַחֲרָי׃
verses.transliteration: Va'omar el-adonai ulai lo-telekh ha'ishah acharai.
verses.study_note: The servant honestly reports his own earlier hesitation. He told Abraham what might go wrong (v.5), and he tells Laban and Bethuel that he raised this concern. This honesty is characteristic of the servant throughout: he does not present himself as having been perfectly confident from the start. He had the same practical question anyone would have. His transparency builds credibility. Acknowledging doubt is not faithlessness — it is the prelude to faith that seeks divine guidance rather than human certainty.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto me, The LORD, before whom I walk, will send his angel with thee, and prosper thy way; and thou shalt take a wife for my son of my kindred, and of my father’s house:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלַי יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר־הִתְהַלַּכְתִּי לְפָנָיו יִשְׁלַח מַלְאָכוֹ אִתָּךְ וְהִצְלִיחַ דַּרְכֶּךָ וְלָקַחְתָּ אִשָּׁה לִבְנִי מִמִּשְׁפַּחְתִּי וּמִבֵּית אָבִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer elai YHVH asher-hithallakhti lefanav yishllach mal'akho ittakh vehitzliach darkkekha velakachta ishah livni mimmishpachti umibbeit avi.
verses.study_note: The servant reports Abraham's answer, with a significant addition: Abraham describes God as 'before whom I walk' (asher-hithallakhti lefanav). This is the language of covenant intimacy — the same phrase used of Enoch ('walked with God,' Gen 5:22) and of Abraham himself ('walk before me,' Gen 17:1). Abraham has earned the right to speak about God's angel going before because he himself has walked before God for a century. The servant's mission is anchored in Abraham's lifetime of covenant faithfulness. 'And prosper thy way' (vehitzliach darkkekha) — the same root as 'made his journey prosperous' in v.21.

verses.kjv_text: Then shalt thou be clear from this my oath, when thou comest to my kindred; and if they give not thee one, thou shalt be clear from my oath.
verses.original_text: אָז תִּנָּקֶה מֵאָלָתִי כִּי תָבוֹא אֶל־מִשְׁפַּחְתִּי וְאִם־לֹא יִתְּנוּ לָךְ וְהָיִיתָ נָקִי מֵאָלָתִי׃
verses.transliteration: Az tinnakeh me'alati ki tavo el-mishpachti ve'im-lo yittenu lakh vehayita nakki me'alati.
verses.study_note: The servant faithfully repeats the release clause from the oath (cf. v.8) — if the family refuses, he is absolved. This is important: the servant goes in faith but not in presumption. The mission's success depends on human consent as well as divine arrangement. Both sides must be willing. The servant presents the family with a clear choice: they can release Rebekah and gain a daughter-in-law in the family of God's covenant man, or they can refuse. The oath binds the servant to attempt, not to coerce.

verses.kjv_text: And I came this day unto the well, and said, O LORD God of my master Abraham, if now thou do prosper my way which I go:
verses.original_text: וָאָבֹא הַיּוֹם אֶל־הָעָיִן וָאֹמַר יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲדֹנִי אַבְרָהָם אִם־יֶשְׁךָ־נָּא מַצְלִיחַ דַּרְכִּי אֲשֶׁר אָנֹכִי הֹלֵךְ עָלֶיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Va'avo hayyom el-ha'ayin va'omar YHVH Elohei adonai Avraham im-yeshkha-na mattzliach darki asher anokhi holekh aleha.
verses.study_note: The servant now recounts his prayer at the well. 'I came this day' (avo hayyom) — he arrived today. The immediacy is remarkable: he just arrived, went straight to the well, and prayed before doing anything else. The prayer 'if now thou do prosper my way' (im-yeshkha matzliach darki) begins with a conditional — he places himself completely under God's guidance, not assuming success but asking for it. The humility of this prayer is striking: even after traveling hundreds of miles in obedience, the servant does not presume on divine favor but asks for it.

verses.kjv_text: Behold, I stand by the well of water; and it shall come to pass, that when the virgin cometh forth to draw water, and I say to her, Give me, I pray thee, a little water of thy pitcher to drink;
verses.original_text: הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי נִצָּב עַל־עֵין הַמָּיִם וְהָיָה הָעַלְמָה הַיֹּצֵאת לִשְׁאֹב וְאָמַרְתִּי אֵלֶיהָ הַשְׁקִינִי־נָא מְעַט־מַיִם מִכַּדֵּךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Hinneh anokhi nitzav al-ein hamayim vehayah ha'almah hayotze'et lish'ov ve'amarti eleha hashkini-na me'at-mayim mikkaddekh.
verses.study_note: The servant retells the sign-request nearly verbatim from v.14, but here uses a different word for the young woman: 'almah' (H5959) instead of 'na'arah' (H5291). 'Almah' specifically means a young woman of marriageable age, sometimes with connotations of purity and distinction. This is the same word used in Isaiah 7:14: 'Behold, a virgin (almah) shall conceive and bear a son' — the Messianic prophecy. The word choice may be intentional on the narrator's part, reinforcing the typological depth of Rebekah as a bride-figure.

verses.kjv_text: And she say to me, Both drink thou, and I will also draw for thy camels: let the same be the woman whom the LORD hath appointed out for my master’s son.
verses.original_text: וְאָמְרָה אֵלַי גַּם־אַתָּה שְׁתֵה וְגַם לִגְמַלֶּיךָ אֶשְׁאָב הִוא הָאִשָּׁה אֲשֶׁר־הֹכִיחַ יְהוָה לְבֶן־אֲדֹנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'amerah elai gam-attah shteh vegam liggemaLekha esh'av hi ha'ishah asher-hokhiach YHVH leven-adonai.
verses.study_note: The servant repeats the full sign — offer to water the camels too — and now attributes the appointment to God: 'the woman whom the LORD hath appointed' (asher-hokhiach YHVH — whom the LORD has shown/designated). The verb 'hokhiach' (from yakach — to appoint, decide, prove) appears again. God is the one who 'appoints' the bride. The servant is not choosing; God is revealing. This is the grammar of divine election: the servant discerns what God has already determined.

verses.kjv_text: And before I had done speaking in mine heart, behold, Rebekah came forth with her pitcher on her shoulder; and she went down unto the well, and drew water: and I said unto her, Let me drink, I pray thee.
verses.original_text: אֲנִי טֶרֶם אֲכַלֶּה לְדַבֵּר אֶל־לִבִּי וְהִנֵּה רִבְקָה יֹצֵאת וְכַדָּהּ עַל־שִׁכְמָהּ וַתֵּרֶד הָעַיְנָה וַתִּשְׁאָב וָאֹמַר אֵלֶיהָ הַשְׁקִינִי נָא׃
verses.transliteration: Ani terem akhalleh ledabber el-libbi vehineh Rivkah yotze'et vekhaddah al-shikhmah vatted ha'aynah vattiish'av va'omar eleha hashkini na.
verses.study_note: A remarkable detail not given in the original account: 'before I had done speaking in mine heart' (terem akhalleh ledabber el-libbi). The prayer was spoken 'in his heart' — it was a silent, inward prayer, not a public petition. The servant prayed privately and God answered publicly. This is the integrity of a man who does not make a spectacle of his prayer life — he prays in the secret of his heart and trusts God to act. The servant speaks to Laban with the same awed precision: before his silent prayer was even complete, Rebekah appeared.

verses.kjv_text: And she made haste, and let down her pitcher from her shoulder, and said, Drink, and I will give thy camels drink also: so I drank, and she made the camels drink also.
verses.original_text: וַתְּמַהֵר וַתּוֹרֶד כַּדָּהּ מֵעָלֶיהָ וַתֹּאמֶר שְׁתֵה וְגַם־גְּמַלֶּיךָ אַשְׁקֶה וָאֵשְׁתְּ וְגַם הַגְּמַלִּים הִשְׁקָתָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vattemahher vatored khaddah me'aleha vattomer shteh vegam-gemalekha ashkeh va'eshte vegam haggemaLim hishkatah.
verses.study_note: The servant retells Rebekah's response to Laban's household. She 'made haste' (vattemahher) — the urgency he already described, now confirmed again in the retelling. She spoke the exact words of the sign before he could ask: 'Drink, and I will give thy camels drink also.' The sign worked in reverse order to expectation: normally a sign requires the other party to respond to a condition you set. Here, Rebekah spoke the required words before the servant could even complete the sign-request — confirming that this was not scripted behavior but the natural overflow of her generous character.

verses.kjv_text: And I asked her, and said, Whose daughter art thou? And she said, The daughter of Bethuel, Nahor’s son, whom Milcah bare unto him: and I put the earring upon her face, and the bracelets upon her hands.
verses.original_text: וָאֶשְׁאַל אֹתָהּ וָאֹמַר בַּת־מִי אַתְּ וַתֹּאמֶר בַּת־בְּתוּאֵל בֶּן־נָחוֹר אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה־לוֹ מִלְכָּה וָאָשִׂם הַנֶּזֶם עַל־אַפָּהּ וְהַצְּמִידִים עַל־יָדֶיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Va'esh'al otah va'omar bat-mi at vattomer bat-Betu'el ben-Nachor asher yaledah-lo Milkah va'asim hannezem al-appah vehatztzmidim al-yadeha.
verses.study_note: The servant now reveals the order of events — he asked her identity AFTER placing the gifts ('I put the earring upon her face' — the gifts are placed before the question of identity is fully confirmed). This reveals the servant's faith: he had already concluded from the sign's fulfillment that this was the one, and he acted on that conclusion. Some see this as a slight discrepancy with v.22-23; more likely the narrator summarizes, while the servant in his report clarifies the rapidity of events. The gifts symbolize covenant claim; the identity question confirms the covenant right.

verses.kjv_text: And I bowed down my head, and worshipped the LORD, and blessed the LORD God of my master Abraham, which had led me in the right way to take my master’s brother’s daughter unto his son.
verses.original_text: וָאֶקֹּד וָאֶשְׁתַּחֲוֶה לַיהוָה וָאֲבָרֵךְ אֶת־יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲדֹנִי אַבְרָהָם אֲשֶׁר הִנְחַנִי בְּדֶרֶךְ אֱמֶת לָקַחַת אֶת־בַּת־אֲחִי אֲדֹנִי לִבְנוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Va'ekkod va'eshtachaveh laYHVH va'averekh et-YHVH Elohei adonai Avraham asher hinchani bedderekh emet lakachat et-bat-achi adonai livno.
verses.study_note: The servant's response to discovering Rebekah's identity: immediate worship. 'I bowed down, I worshipped, I blessed the LORD' — three consecutive acts of adoration. The phrase that describes God's guidance is exquisite: 'who led me in the right way' (hinchani bedderekh emet — led me in the way of truth/faithfulness). 'Emet' — truth, faithfulness — appears here as the description of the path God prepared. God's truth is not merely propositional; it is directional. God's faithfulness created a road that the servant walked, and at the end of that road stood Rebekah. The servant worshipped before asking for anything, before the negotiation was settled.

verses.kjv_text: And now if ye will deal kindly and truly with my master, tell me: and if not, tell me; that I may turn to the right hand, or to the left.
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה אִם־יֶשְׁכֶם עֹשִׂים חֶסֶד וֶאֱמֶת אֶת־אֲדֹנִי הַגִּידוּ לִי וְאִם־לֹא הַגִּידוּ לִי וְאֶפְנֶה עַל־יָמִין אוֹ עַל־שְׂמֹאל׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah im-yeshkhem osim chesed ve'emet et-adonai haggidu li ve'im-lo haggidu li ve'efneh al-yamin o al-semo'l.
verses.study_note: The servant's conclusion: 'deal kindly and truly' (chesed ve'emet — lovingkindness and faithfulness). Having attributed the mission to God's chesed va'emet in v.27, he now asks the family to respond with the same qualities. The request mirrors the divine character: he is asking humans to reflect God's own covenant virtues. 'Tell me' — he requires a clear answer, yes or no. 'That I may turn to the right hand, or to the left' — a Hebraism meaning 'so I may know which way to go next.' The servant is decisive and clear; he will not linger without a commitment.

verses.kjv_text: Then Laban and Bethuel answered and said, The thing proceedeth from the LORD: we cannot speak unto thee bad or good.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַן לָבָן וּבְתוּאֵל וַיֹּאמְרוּ מֵיְהוָה יָצָא הַדָּבָר לֹא נוּכַל דַּבֵּר אֵלֶיךָ רַע אוֹ־טוֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaan Lavan uBetu'el vayyomeru meYHVH yatza hadavar lo nukhal dabber eleikha ra o-tov.
verses.study_note: The family's response is one of the most striking acknowledgments of divine sovereignty in Genesis: 'The thing proceedeth from the LORD' (meYHVH yatza hadavar — from the LORD this word/matter has come forth). Even Laban — a man of mixed motives, later a deceiver — can see that this is God's doing. The evidence has been overwhelming: the prayer at the well, the sign's perfect fulfillment, the genealogical confirmation, the gifts already bestowed. They answer: 'we cannot speak unto thee bad or good' (lo nukhal dabber eleikha ra o-tov) — they are silenced before God's clear action. This is what Romans 3:19 calls being 'stopped' — when God's word has spoken, there is nothing left to say.
verses.doctrinal_note: Even those outside the covenant community can recognize the hand of God when His providence is clearly at work. Laban and Bethuel — neither of them models of faith — correctly identify the source of the entire mission. This witnesses to the fact that God's providential acts carry their own authentication. The mission's divine origin is self-evident to all who observe it.

verses.kjv_text: Behold, Rebekah is before thee, take her, and go, and let her be thy master’s son’s wife, as the LORD hath spoken.
verses.original_text: הִנֵּה־רִבְקָה לְפָנֶיךָ קַח וָלֵךְ וּתְהִי אִשָּׁה לְבֶן־אֲדֹנֶיךָ כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר יְהוָה׃
