verses.transliteration: Hinneh-Rivkah lefanekha kach valekh utehi ishah leven-adonekha ka'asher dibber YHVH.
verses.study_note: The family consents with striking simplicity: 'Behold, Rebekah is before thee, take her, and go' (hinneh-Rivkah lefanekha kach valekh). Three brief imperatives — take, go, let her be — all in rapid succession. The reasoning is theological: 'as the LORD hath spoken' (ka'asher dibber YHVH). Once they have acknowledged that the LORD ordained this (v.50), they cannot resist. The logic of providence: if this is from God, then to withhold Rebekah would be to resist God Himself. Note that Rebekah herself is not yet consulted — the family consent comes first, then hers (v.58).

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that, when Abraham’s servant heard their words, he worshipped the LORD, bowing himself to the earth.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כַּאֲשֶׁר שָׁמַע עֶבֶד אַבְרָהָם אֶת־דִּבְרֵיהֶם וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ אַרְצָה לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi ka'asher shama eved Avraham et-divrehem vayyishtachu artzah laYHVH.
verses.study_note: Again, the servant's first response to good news is worship — prostration before God, not celebration with men. This is the second time he worships in this chapter (cf. v.26). The pattern is unmistakable: every confirmation of divine guidance is met with immediate adoration. He heard their words of consent — and before eating, speaking, celebrating, or giving gifts, he falls before God. This posture of worship-first defines the servant throughout. He is a man whose reflexive response to providence is not self-congratulation but God-exaltation.

verses.kjv_text: And the servant brought forth jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and raiment, and gave them to Rebekah: he gave also to her brother and to her mother precious things.
verses.original_text: וַיּוֹצֵא הָעֶבֶד כְּלֵי־כֶסֶף וּכְלֵי זָהָב וּבְגָדִים וַיִּתֵּן לְרִבְקָה וּמִגְדָּנֹת נָתַן לְאָחִיהָ וּלְאִמָּהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyotze ha'eved kelei-khesef ukhelei zahav uvegadim vayyitten leRivkah umigdanot natan le'achihah ule'immah.
verses.study_note: Now come the gifts — held back until the mission was confirmed. Silver jewelry, gold jewelry, garments for Rebekah; precious things (migdanot — costly gifts, delicacies) for her brother and mother. The servant brought 'all the goods of his master' (v.10) for exactly this moment. These are the bridegroom's gifts to the bride and her family, sent through the servant. In the typology: the gifts the Holy Spirit brings are the gifts of Christ — spiritual riches lavished on those who will receive the Son (Eph 1:3-8). The bride is adorned before she reaches the bridegroom.

verses.kjv_text: And they did eat and drink, he and the men that were with him, and tarried all night; and they rose up in the morning, and he said, Send me away unto my master.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאכְלוּ וַיִּשְׁתּוּ הוּא וְהָאֲנָשִׁים אֲשֶׁר־עִמּוֹ וַיָּלִינוּ וַיָּקוּמוּ בַבֹּקֶר וַיֹּאמֶר שַׁלְּחֻנִי לַאדֹנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyokhelu vayyishtu hu veha'anashim asher-immo vayyalinu vayyakumu babboker vayomer shallechuni ladonai.
verses.study_note: Only after worship and the giving of gifts does the servant eat and drink. The feast he refused until the mission was declared (v.33) can now be enjoyed. He and his men lodge for the night. But the very first words at morning are: 'Send me away unto my master' (shallechuni ladonai). The servant's mind is already on the journey home. There is no lingering, no personal comfort-seeking. The mission is half complete — Rebekah must be brought to Isaac. The urgency of the returning servant mirrors the Spirit's work: not settling in the world but bringing the bride home.

verses.kjv_text: And her brother and her mother said, Let the damsel abide with us a few days, at the least ten; after that she shall go.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אָחִיהָ וְאִמָּהּ תֵּשֵׁב הַנַּעֲרָ אִתָּנוּ יָמִים אוֹ עָשׂוֹר אַחַר תֵּלֵךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer achihah ve'immah teshev hanna'ara ittanu yamim o asor achar telekh.
verses.study_note: The family's request is entirely natural: 'a few days, at least ten' (yamim o asor — literally 'days or ten'). They are being asked to send their daughter and sister into a 500-mile journey with a stranger to marry a man she has never met. Asking for ten days to say goodbye is completely understandable. Their request is not a refusal but a human desire to slow down an overwhelming change. The servant, however, will not accept delay. His refusal is not callous — he understands that the mission must move forward. The bride's departure from her homeland cannot be indefinitely postponed.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, Hinder me not, seeing the LORD hath prospered my way; send me away that I may go to my master.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם אַל־תְּאַחֲרוּ אֹתִי וַיהוָה הִצְלִיחַ דַּרְכִּי שַׁלְּחוּנִי וְאֵלְכָה לַאדֹנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer alehem al-te'acharu oti vaYHVH hitzliach darki shallechuni ve'elkhah ladonai.
verses.study_note: The servant's refusal is framed entirely in terms of God's action: 'the LORD hath prospered my way' (YHVH hitzliach darki). He is not being impatient for his own reasons — he cannot delay what God has ordained. 'Hinder me not' (al-te'acharu oti — do not cause me to delay) is urgent but not rude. He acknowledges the family's natural feelings implicitly but insists the divine mandate takes priority. His mission is not complete until Rebekah stands before Isaac. Everything that delays that moment is working against the purpose of God.

verses.kjv_text: And they said, We will call the damsel, and enquire at her mouth.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ נִקְרָא לַנַּעֲרָ וְנִשְׁאֲלָה אֶת־פִּיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru nikkra lanna'ara venish'alah et-pihah.
verses.study_note: The family's response is admirable: 'We will call the damsel and enquire at her mouth' (nish'alah et-pihah — ask at her mouth). Her consent is essential. They will not send Rebekah against her will, even if God has ordained this. Abraham required the woman's willingness (v.8); the family upholds it. This is the pattern of divine call: God ordains, the Spirit draws, but the person must willingly respond. The invitation is extended; the choice remains with the one called. Rebekah will be asked directly.

verses.kjv_text: And they called Rebekah, and said unto her, Wilt thou go with this man? And she said, I will go.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרְאוּ לְרִבְקָה וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֵלֶיהָ הֲתֵלְכִי עִם־הָאִישׁ הַזֶּה וַתֹּאמֶר אֵלֵךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikre'u leRivkah vayyomeru eleha hatelechi im-ha'ish hazzeh vattomer elekh.
verses.study_note: The most momentous two words in the chapter: 'Elekh' — I will go. Three words in Hebrew, two in English, one life-changing decision. The question is simple and direct: 'Wilt thou go with this man?' (hatelechi im-ha'ish hazzeh — will you go with this man?). The answer is immediate: 'Elekh' — I will go. No hesitation, no further negotiation, no request for more information about Isaac. She has heard the servant's testimony about his master's house, she has received the gifts, and now she is willing. This is the moment of consent — the bride's 'yes' to the journey and to the bridegroom she has never seen. It is one of the most profound acts of faith in the Old Testament.
verses.doctrinal_note: Rebekah's 'I will go' is the Old Testament parallel to Abraham's departure in Genesis 12:4 ('so Abram departed, as the LORD had spoken'). Both leave a known homeland for an unknown future based on a word they have received. Rebekah has not seen Isaac; she goes on the basis of testimony alone. This is the pattern of saving faith: 'Whom having not seen, ye love' (1 Pet 1:8). The Church comes to Christ not having seen Him in the flesh but having received the Spirit's testimony about Him and responding with willing commitment.

verses.kjv_text: And they sent away Rebekah their sister, and her nurse, and Abraham’s servant, and his men.
verses.original_text: וַיְשַׁלְּחוּ אֶת־רִבְקָה אֲחֹתָם וְאֶת־מֵינִקְתָּהּ וְאֶת־עֶבֶד אַבְרָהָם וְאֶת־אֲנָשָׁיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayeshallechu et-Rivkah achotam ve'et-meiniktatah ve'et-eved Avraham ve'et-anashav.
verses.study_note: Rebekah is sent with her nurse (meineketh — the woman who nursed her as an infant, identified later as Deborah in Gen 35:8). The nurse represents continuity — something of Rebekah's past life traveling with her into the new. She is also sent with 'Abraham's servant and his men' — the full diplomatic party that brought her. She does not travel alone. This detail is tender: the family who had to let her go ensures she is protected and accompanied. The party that came for the bride now returns with the bride.

verses.kjv_text: And they blessed Rebekah, and said unto her, Thou art our sister, be thou the mother of thousands of millions, and let thy seed possess the gate of those which hate them.
verses.original_text: וַיְבָרְכוּ אֶת־רִבְקָה וַיֹּאמְרוּ לָהּ אֲחֹתֵנוּ אַתְּ הֲיִי לְאַלְפֵי רְבָבָה וְיִירַשׁ זַרְעֵךְ אֵת שַׁעַר שֹׂנְאָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayevarekhu et-Rivkah vayyomeru lah achotenu at hayi le'alfei revavah veyirash zar'ekh et sha'ar sone'av.
verses.study_note: The family's parting blessing over Rebekah is one of the most powerful in Genesis: 'Be thou the mother of thousands of millions' (hayi le'alfei revavah — literally 'become thousands of ten-thousands') — a vast numerical multitude. And 'let thy seed possess the gate of his enemies' (veyirash zar'ekh et sha'ar sone'av) — virtually identical to the covenant blessing given to Abraham after the binding of Isaac (Gen 22:17): 'thy seed shall possess the gate of his enemies.' The Abrahamic blessing, given in response to covenant faithfulness, is now transferred to Rebekah as she enters the covenant family. Her seed — including Jacob, and through Jacob the twelve tribes, and through them the Messiah — will indeed possess the gate.
verses.doctrinal_note: The blessing Rebekah receives mirrors the Abrahamic covenant promise precisely (Gen 22:17). Her entry into the covenant family through marriage to Isaac places her within the stream of redemptive promise. The 'seed' (zera) that will possess the gate is the same seed promised in Genesis 3:15 and 22:18 — ultimately the Seed who is Christ (Gal 3:16). Rebekah unwittingly enters the covenant line of Messianic promise.

verses.kjv_text: And Rebekah arose, and her damsels, and they rode upon the camels, and followed the man: and the servant took Rebekah, and went his way.
verses.original_text: וַתָּקָם רִבְקָה וְנַעֲרֹתֶיהָ וַתִּרְכַּבְנָה עַל־הַגְּמַלִּים וַתֵּלַכְנָה אַחֲרֵי הָאִישׁ וַיִּקַּח הָעֶבֶד אֶת־רִבְקָה וַיֵּלַךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattakam Rivkah vena'aroteiha vattirrakhavnah al-haggemaLim vatttelakhnah acharei ha'ish vayyikkach ha'eved et-Rivkah vayelekh.
verses.study_note: Rebekah 'arose' (vattakam) — the same word used of Abraham rising early for acts of covenant obedience (Gen 22:3). She and her young women mount the camels and 'followed the man' (vatttelakhnah acharei ha'ish). The journey of faith begins with a rising and a following. The servant 'took Rebekah and went' (vayyikkach ha'eved et-Rivkah vayelekh) — the brevity of the Hebrew is stunning. Five centuries of covenant history, a 500-mile journey, a marriage, and a future nation — all compressed into the servant's action of taking Rebekah and going.

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac came from the way of the well Lahairoi; for he dwelt in the south country.
verses.original_text: וְיִצְחָק בָּא מִבּוֹא בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי וְהוּא יוֹשֵׁב בְּאֶרֶץ הַנֶּגֶב׃
verses.transliteration: VeYitzhak ba mibbo be'er lachai ro'i vehu yoshev be'eretz hanegev.
verses.study_note: Isaac now enters the narrative for the first time in the chapter. He is coming from 'Beer Lahai Roi' (the well of the Living One who sees me) — the very place where Hagar encountered the Angel of the LORD (Gen 16:13-14) and where Ishmael later settled (Gen 25:11). Isaac dwells 'in the south country' (eretz hanegev). Beer Lahai Roi is a place of divine encounter, a place where God is known as the One who sees (El Roi). Isaac coming from this place suggests he is a man familiar with the presence and attention of God. He is exactly where God can arrange the meeting.

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac went out to meditate in the field at the eventide: and he lifted up his eyes, and saw, and, behold, the camels were coming.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּצֵא יִצְחָק לָשׂוּחַ בַּשָּׂדֶה לִפְנוֹת עָרֶב וַיִּשָּׂא עֵינָיו וַיַּרְא וְהִנֵּה גְמַלִּים בָּאִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyetze Yitzhak lasuach basaddeh lifnot erev vayyissa einav vayyar vehineh gemalim ba'im.
verses.study_note: One of the most tender scenes in Genesis. Isaac goes out 'to meditate in the field at the eventide' (lasuach basaddeh lifnot erev). The word 'lasuach' (to meditate, to muse, to walk about in reflection) is rare — it may suggest prayer, contemplation, or simply quiet walking. Isaac goes to a field at evening — the same hour the servant had positioned himself at the well. He is alone, in the open, reflective. He lifts his eyes and sees the caravan coming. 'Hineh' (behold) — the narrative gasps with wonder at the convergence: the one coming is the one he has been, perhaps, waiting for. The contemplative bridegroom meets the coming bride in an open field at evening.

verses.kjv_text: And Rebekah lifted up her eyes, and when she saw Isaac, she lighted off the camel.
verses.original_text: וַתִּשָּׂא רִבְקָה אֶת־עֵינֶיהָ וַתֵּרֶא אֶת־יִצְחָק וַתִּפֹּל מֵעַל הַגָּמָל׃
verses.transliteration: Vattissa Rivkah et-eineiha vattera et-Yitzhak vattiippol me'al haggamal.
verses.study_note: Both lift their eyes — Isaac in v.63, Rebekah here. The parallel is exquisite. Rebekah sees Isaac and 'lighted off the camel' (vattiippol me'al haggamal — literally 'fell from the camel'). This is not a stumble but a deliberate, rapid dismount — she slides or steps down quickly. In the culture of the ancient Near East, a woman would dismount before a man of higher social standing. Her immediate recognition of Isaac's significance leads to an instinctive gesture of deference and respect. She who rode on the camel of the journey now stands on the ground of destiny.

verses.kjv_text: For she had said unto the servant, What man is this that walketh in the field to meet us? And the servant had said, It is my master: therefore she took a vail, and covered herself.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר אֶל־הָעֶבֶד מִי הָאִישׁ הַלָּזֶה הַהֹלֵךְ בַּשָּׂדֶה לִקְרָאתֵנוּ וַיֹּאמֶר הָעֶבֶד הוּא אֲדֹנִי וַתִּקַּח הַצָּעִיף וַתִּתְכָּס׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomer el-ha'eved mi ha'ish hallazeh hahholekh basaddeh likratenu vayomer ha'eved hu adonai vattiqqach hatzza'if vattitkkas.
verses.study_note: Rebekah's question: 'What man is this?' — not 'who is this?' but 'what man?' — as if his very bearing distinguishes him. The servant's answer is the most significant thing he has ever said in his own interest: 'It is my master' (hu adonai). He has spoken of his master throughout this chapter — now his master stands before them. Rebekah immediately takes her veil (tza'if — a covering, a bridal veil) and covers herself. This was the marriage custom: veiling before the husband signified modesty, honor, and covenant commitment. The bride covers herself for the bridegroom.

verses.kjv_text: And the servant told Isaac all things that he had done.
verses.original_text: וַיְסַפֵּר הָעֶבֶד לְיִצְחָק אֵת כָּל־הַדְּבָרִים אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayesapper ha'eved leYitzhak et kol-haddevarim asher asah.
verses.study_note: The servant's final act: he tells Isaac everything — 'all things that he had done' (kol-haddevarim asher asah). This is his report to the master's son, the account of the mission: the prayer at the well, the sign of Rebekah, the genealogical confirmation, the family's response, the journey home. The servant who would not speak his own name, who ate only after his mission was secured, who worshipped before celebrating — now gives a full accounting to the one he serves. His mission was speaking about his master's son to Rebekah; now he speaks about Rebekah to his master's son. He is the intermediary of the covenant union.

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac brought her into his mother Sarah’s tent, and took Rebekah, and she became his wife; and he loved her: and Isaac was comforted after his mother’s death.
verses.original_text: וַיְבִאֶהָ יִצְחָק הָאֹהֱלָה שָׂרָה אִמּוֹ וַיִּקַּח אֶת־רִבְקָה וַתְּהִי־לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה וַיֶּאֱהָבֶהָ וַיִּנָּחֵם יִצְחָק אַחֲרֵי אִמּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyeviehah Yitzhak ha'ohelah Sarah immo vayyikkach et-Rivkah vattehi-lo le'ishah vayyeehavehah vayyinnachem Yitzhak acharei immo.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes with four movements of extraordinary tenderness and theological weight: (1) Isaac brings Rebekah into his mother Sarah's tent — Sarah who died in Gen 23 and left her tent empty. The vacancy of grief is filled by covenant love. Her tent becomes Rebekah's tent — the continuity of the matriarchal covenant line is restored. (2) 'He took Rebekah' (vayyikkach et-Rivkah) — the formal covenant completion of marriage. (3) 'She became his wife' (vattehi-lo le'ishah) — the covenant union is sealed. (4) 'He loved her' (vayyeehavehah) — the Hebrew verb 'ahav' (love) appears here in its first use in the context of romantic/marital love between husband and wife in Genesis (cf. first use: father-son love in 22:2). Love follows covenant commitment — it grows within the covenant structure. (5) 'Isaac was comforted after his mother's death' — the grief of Gen 23 meets its resolution here. The bride who comes to the son brings healing. Where there was grief, there is now love. Where there was absence, there is presence. The entire chapter's journey — from Abraham's commission to this moment — arrives in these final words: covenant made, love given, grief healed.
verses.doctrinal_note: The marriage of Isaac and Rebekah is the fullest type of Christ and the Church in the Old Testament. The Father (Abraham) sends the Spirit (the servant) to call out a bride for the Son (Isaac). The Spirit speaks entirely of the Son, bears the Father's gifts to woo the bride, and brings her to the Son. The bride (Rebekah) leaves her homeland and comes to a bridegroom she has not seen, on the basis of testimony alone — just as the Church comes to Christ (1 Pet 1:8). The bridegroom (Isaac) goes out to meet her and brings her into his house. 'He loved her' — Paul quotes the analogy explicitly in Ephesians 5:25-32: 'Husbands, love your wives, even as Christ also loved the church.' The chapter ends in love, comfort, and covenant — exactly where redemptive history ends: the marriage supper of the Lamb (Rev 19:7-9), the bride made ready.

meta.overview: Genesis 24 is the longest chapter in Genesis and one of the most theologically rich narratives in all of Scripture. It tells the story of Abraham, now old, sending his trusted servant on a sacred mission: to find a wife for Isaac from among Abraham's own kindred in Mesopotamia. The servant travels to Haran, prays for providential guidance, meets Rebekah at a well, and receives confirmation through a sign. After negotiations with Laban and Bethuel, Rebekah consents to leave immediately. She journeys to Canaan, meets Isaac, and becomes his wife. The narrative operates simultaneously on two levels: as a beautiful human love story rich in cultural detail, and as a grand typological parable. Abraham, who holds everything and has a beloved son, is a type of God the Father. Isaac, the promised heir who stays in Canaan and receives the bride, is a type of Christ, the Bridegroom. The unnamed servant — arguably the true hero of the chapter — is a type of the Holy Spirit: sent by the Father, speaking entirely of the Son, never drawing attention to himself, faithfully bringing the bride to the bridegroom. Rebekah, called out of her homeland by the word alone, leaving everything to join one she has never seen, is a type of the Church. The chapter is Genesis's supreme portrait of providence, prayer, and covenant faithfulness — saturated with chesed (lovingkindness) and emet (faithfulness), the twin pillars of God's covenant character.
meta.theme: Providence, Prayer, and the Covenant Bride — The Holy Spirit's Mission
meta.keyWordMeaning: Lovingkindness, covenant loyalty, steadfast love — the character of God that governs the entire mission. Used three times in this chapter (vv.12,14,27), it names what both seeks and secures the bride.
outline: The Commission: Abraham's Oath (vv.1-9): The servant is charged to find a bride from Abraham's kin, not from Canaan
The Journey and the Prayer (vv.10-14): Arrival at Haran; the servant's extraordinary prayer for providential guidance
The Sign Confirmed: Rebekah at the Well (vv.15-27): God's immediate answer; the servant's worship
The Negotiation: Speaking in Laban's House (vv.28-49): The servant's report — from God's call to this moment
The Consent: Will You Go? (vv.50-61): Rebekah and her family respond; she says 'I will go'
The Meeting and the Marriage (vv.62-67): Isaac meets Rebekah; she becomes his wife; he is comforted

verses.kjv_text: Then again Abraham took a wife, and her name was Keturah.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּסֶף אַבְרָהָם וַיִּקַּח אִשָּׁה וּשְׁמָהּ קְטוּרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyosef Avraham vayyikkach ishah ushemah Keturah.
verses.study_note: \"Then again\" (vayyosef — literally 'and he added') suggests this follows the events of chapter 24 after Isaac's marriage to Rebekah. Keturah means 'incense' or 'fragrant one' — fitting for a woman who adds a new chapter to Abraham's household. The timing is remarkable: Abraham is now over 140 years old (he dies at 175, v.7; Isaac was born when Abraham was 100, Gen 21:5; and this follows chapter 24 where Isaac is about 40). This is the same Abraham to whom God had supernaturally granted renewed generative power (Rom 4:19-20; Heb 11:12). Keturah is called Abraham's 'concubine' (pilegesh) in 1 Chronicles 1:32, ranking below Sarah in the covenant hierarchy, though here she is designated 'wife' (ishah). The six sons she bears (v.2) fulfill in another register Abraham's destiny as 'father of many nations' (Gen 17:5) — but the covenant narrows through Isaac, not through Keturah's children.

verses.kjv_text: And she bare him Zimran, and Jokshan, and Medan, and Midian, and Ishbak, and Shuah.
verses.original_text: וַתֵּלֶד לוֹ אֶת־זִמְרָן וְאֶת־יָקְשָׁן וְאֶת־מֶדָן וְאֶת־מִדְיָן וְאֶת־יִשְׁבָּק וְאֶת־שׁוּחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Vatteled lo et-Zimran ve'et-Yokshan ve'et-Medan ve'et-Midyan ve'et-Yishbak ve'et-Shuach.
verses.study_note: Six sons are listed. The most significant is Midian — whose descendants become the Midianites, playing recurring roles in biblical history: they purchase Joseph (Gen 37:28,36), Moses flees to their land and marries Zipporah (Exod 2:15-21), the Midianite priest Jethro (also called Reuel/Hobab) advises Moses (Exod 18), and later the Midianites oppress Israel until Gideon's deliverance (Judg 6-8). Shuah may be the ancestor of Job's friend Bildad the Shuhite (Job 2:11), connecting the Wisdom literature to Abraham's extended family. The genealogy demonstrates that Abraham's blessing radiates outward beyond the covenant line — not all of Abraham's descendants bear the covenant promise, but all bear the marks of divine blessing through their father.

verses.kjv_text: And Jokshan begat Sheba, and Dedan. And the sons of Dedan were Asshurim, and Letushim, and Leummim.
verses.original_text: וְיָקְשָׁן יָלַד אֶת־שְׁבָא וְאֶת־דְּדָן וּבְנֵי דְדָן הָיוּ אַשּׁוּרִם וּלְטוּשִׁם וּלְאֻמִּים׃
verses.transliteration: VeYokshan yalad et-Sheva ve'et-Dedan uvnei Dedan hayu Ashurim uleTushim ule'Ummim.
verses.study_note: Sheba and Dedan are significant Arabian tribal names. Dedan appears in prophetic judgment oracles against Edom (Jer 49:8; Ezek 25:13) and in trade lists (Ezek 27:20). Sheba is associated with the Queen of Sheba who visits Solomon (1 Kings 10:1-13) — her realm likely in southwest Arabia (modern Yemen). Notably, another Sheba and Dedan appear among the descendants of Ham through Cush (Gen 10:7), suggesting tribal names could overlap between different ethnic groups through trade and intermarriage. The Arabian tribal world was Abraham's extended family.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Midian; Ephah, and Epher, and Hanoch, and Abidah, and Eldaah. All these were the children of Keturah.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי מִדְיָן עֵיפָה וָעֵפֶר וַחֲנֹךְ וַאֲבִידָע וְאֶלְדָּעָה כָּל־אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי קְטוּרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Uvnei Midyan Eifah va'Efer vachanokh va'Avida ve'Elda'ah kol-elleh benei Keturah.
verses.study_note: Ephah (the first son of Midian listed) appears explicitly in Isaiah 60:6 — 'the dromedaries of Midian and Ephah... they shall bring gold and incense' — connecting Abraham's Midianite descendants to the eschatological gathering of nations to worship the LORD. The prophet envisions Keturah's grandchildren bringing tribute to the Messiah. The summary 'all these were the children of Keturah' closes the genealogy of the non-covenant line, setting up the decisive narrowing to Isaac in the following verses.

verses.kjv_text: And Abraham gave all that he had unto Isaac.
verses.original_text: וַיִּתֵּן אַבְרָהָם אֶת־כָּל־אֲשֶׁר־לוֹ לְיִצְחָק׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitten Avraham et-kol-asher-lo leYitschak.
verses.study_note: A verse of sovereign clarity: 'all that he had' (kol-asher-lo) goes to Isaac. Despite six sons by Keturah and one by Hagar (Ishmael), the entire estate — and with it the covenant inheritance — flows exclusively to Isaac. This is not primogeniture by birth order (Ishmael was firstborn) but divine election: God had designated Isaac as the covenant heir (Gen 17:19-21; 21:12). Abraham's act of inheritance is the human ratification of God's divine selection. The verse is a single decisive statement, its brevity underscoring its absoluteness.
verses.doctrinal_note: The exclusive inheritance of Isaac establishes a crucial biblical principle: natural birth order does not determine covenant standing — divine election does. Ishmael was the firstborn, yet Isaac inherits. Later: Esau was the firstborn, yet Jacob inherits. God's pattern of 'the elder shall serve the younger' (v.23) is already anticipated here. Paul expounds this in Romans 9:6-13 to show that 'not all who are descended from Israel are Israel' — covenant membership is determined by God's call, not by fleshly descent.

verses.kjv_text: But unto the sons of the concubines, which Abraham had, Abraham gave gifts, and sent them away from Isaac his son, while he yet lived, eastward, unto the east country.
verses.original_text: וְלִבְנֵי הַפִּילַגְשִׁים אֲשֶׁר לְאַבְרָהָם נָתַן אַבְרָהָם מַתָּנֹת וַיְשַׁלְּחֵם מֵעַל יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ בְּעוֹדֶנּוּ חַי קֵדְמָה אֶל־אֶרֶץ קֶדֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Velivnei happilagshim asher leAvraham natan Avraham mattanot vayyeshallechem me'al Yitschak beno be'odenu chai kedmah el-eretz kedem.
verses.study_note: The sons of the concubines (pilagshim — includes Hagar and Keturah in 1 Chron 1:32) receive gifts (mattanot) but are sent 'eastward, unto the east country' — away from Canaan, away from Isaac. The geography is theological: the promised land is the covenant land; the east represents the periphery. Abraham acts while 'he yet lived' — a strategic decision to prevent future conflict over the inheritance (cf. the conflict between Hagar/Ishmael and Sarah/Isaac that had already erupted, Gen 21:8-10). The gifts acknowledge kinship; the dismissal acknowledges the exclusivity of the covenant. The 'east country' (eretz kedem) likely refers to Arabia and Mesopotamia — the broader ancient Near Eastern world.

verses.kjv_text: And these are the days of the years of Abraham’s life which he lived, an hundred threescore and fifteen years.
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה יְמֵי שְׁנֵי חַיֵּי אַבְרָהָם אֲשֶׁר חָי מְאַת שָׁנָה וְשִׁבְעִים שָׁנָה וְחָמֵשׁ שָׁנִים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh yemei shenei chayyei Avraham asher chai me'at shanah veshivim shanah vechamesh shanim.
verses.study_note: Abraham died at 175 years — a life spanning from pagan Ur (Gen 11:31) through radical call (Gen 12:1), covenant (Gen 15, 17), the birth of Isaac (Gen 21), the binding of Isaac (Gen 22), the death of Sarah (Gen 23), and Isaac's marriage (Gen 24). The exact phrasing — 'the days of the years of the lives of Abraham' (yemei shenei chayyei) — uses the same formula as for Ishmael (v.17), Isaac (Gen 35:28), and Jacob (Gen 47:28), marking each patriarch's obituary with identical dignity. Abraham lived 175 years: 100 years before Isaac's birth (in Ur, Haran, and Canaan); 75 years with Isaac; reaching to Jacob and Esau's 15th year (they were born when Isaac was 60, v.26; Abraham died when Isaac was 75).

verses.kjv_text: Then Abraham gave up the ghost, and died in a good old age, an old man, and full of years; and was gathered to his people.
verses.original_text: וַיִּגְוַע וַיָּמָת אַבְרָהָם בְּשֵׂיבָה טוֹבָה זָקֵן וְשָׂבֵעַ וַיֵּאָסֶף אֶל־עַמָּיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyigva vayyamat Avraham beseivah tovah zaken vesavea vayyei'asef el-ammav.
verses.study_note: Four phrases celebrate Abraham's death: (1) 'gave up the ghost' (vayyigva — expired); (2) 'in a good old age' (beseivah tovah — a ripe, blessed old age); (3) 'an old man' (zaken); (4) 'full of years' (savea — satisfied, full, complete — the same word used for eating one's fill). Abraham died satisfied. Most significantly: 'gathered to his people' (vayyei'asef el-ammav). This phrase implies an afterlife — he was gathered to those who had preceded him in death. Abraham's 'people' were not buried in Canaan (only Sarah was, Gen 23). The gathering to 'his people' is therefore not mere burial but a joining of the deceased in Sheol/the place of the dead. This is one of the earliest implicit affirmations of personal continuity beyond death in Scripture.
verses.doctrinal_note: Death is not the end for the covenant man. 'Gathered to his people' (vayyei'asef el-ammav) implies a conscious community beyond the grave — Abraham joins those who went before. Jesus himself uses Abraham's continued existence as proof of resurrection: 'God is not the God of the dead, but of the living' — for God is 'the God of Abraham' even centuries after his death (Matt 22:31-32). Abraham's death is blessed precisely because his life was blessed: to die 'full' (savea) is the reward of walking with God.

verses.kjv_text: And his sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him in the cave of Machpelah, in the field of Ephron the son of Zohar the Hittite, which is before Mamre;
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְבְּרוּ אֹתוֹ יִצְחָק וְיִשְׁמָעֵאל בָּנָיו אֶל־מְעָרַת הַמַּכְפֵּלָה אֶל־שְׂדֵה עֶפְרֹן בֶּן־צֹחַר הַחִתִּי אֲשֶׁר עַל־פְּנֵי מַמְרֵא׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikberu oto Yitschak veYishmael banav el-me'arat haMakhpelah el-sdeh Efron ben-Tzochar haHitti asher al-penei Mamre.
verses.study_note: Isaac and Ishmael stand together at their father's grave — the only time they appear together after Ishmael's expulsion (Gen 21:14). Abraham's death achieves what his life could not: a moment of unity between the two estranged sons. The burial at Machpelah is rich with covenant significance — Abraham purchased this field specifically for Sarah (Gen 23:1-20), and it becomes the patriarchal burial ground for Sarah, Abraham, Isaac, Rebekah, Leah, and Jacob (Gen 49:31; 50:13). Note the order: 'Isaac and Ishmael' — Isaac is named first, even though Ishmael is the elder, confirming the covenant hierarchy established in chapter 17.

verses.kjv_text: The field which Abraham purchased of the sons of Heth: there was Abraham buried, and Sarah his wife.
verses.original_text: הַשָּׂדֶה אֲשֶׁר־קָנָה אַבְרָהָם מֵאֵת בְּנֵי חֵת שָׁמָּה קֻבַּר אַבְרָהָם וְשָׂרָה אִשְׁתּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Hassadeh asher-kanah Avraham me'et benei Het shammah kubbar Avraham veSarah ishto.
verses.study_note: The purchased field unites Abraham and Sarah in death as in life. The emphasis on 'purchased' (kanah) is significant — Abraham owned not a single inch of Canaan by right of possession, yet he purchased this one field for 400 shekels of silver. His entire inheritance was held by promise, not by current possession. The writer of Hebrews captures this: Abraham 'confessed that they were strangers and pilgrims on the earth' (Heb 11:13). Machpelah is Abraham's only 'owned' land in Canaan — a foothold, a deposit, a first installment of the full inheritance that his descendants would one day receive.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass after the death of Abraham, that God blessed his son Isaac; and Isaac dwelt by the well Lahairoi.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי אַחֲרֵי מוֹת אַבְרָהָם וַיְבָרֶךְ אֱלֹהִים אֶת־יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ וַיֵּשֶׁב יִצְחָק עִם־בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi acharei mot Avraham vayyevarekh Elohim et-Yitschak beno vayyeshev Yitschak im-Be'er Lachai Ro'i.
verses.study_note: The great transition: the blessing passes from Abraham to Isaac. The placement is deliberate — God's blessing of Isaac is the very first thing noted after Abraham's burial. The covenant did not die with Abraham. Isaac dwells at Beer-lahai-roi — 'the well of the living one who sees me' — named by Hagar when God appeared to her in the wilderness (Gen 16:13-14). It is a place of divine visitation and survival. Isaac had already been to this well (Gen 24:62); it becomes a place of prayer and reflection for him. He who lives at 'the well of the Living One who sees' lives in conscious awareness of divine watchfulness.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's covenant blessing does not depend on its human bearer remaining alive. Abraham dies; God immediately blesses Isaac. The covenant is secured in God, not in man. This is the pattern throughout redemptive history: when Moses dies, Joshua leads; when David dies, Solomon builds; when Christ dies... He rises. The covenant Lord outlives every human instrument. 'The grass withereth, the flower fadeth: but the word of our God shall stand for ever' (Isa 40:8).

verses.kjv_text: Now these are the generations of Ishmael, Abraham’s son, whom Hagar the Egyptian, Sarah’s handmaid, bare unto Abraham:
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה תֹּלְדֹת יִשְׁמָעֵאל בֶּן־אַבְרָהָם אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה הָגָר הַמִּצְרִית שִׁפְחַת שָׂרָה לְאַבְרָהָם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh toldot Yishmael ben-Avraham asher yaledah Hagar haMitzrit shifchat Sarah leAvraham.
verses.study_note: \"Now these are the generations of Ishmael' — the tenth and final 'toledot' formula in Genesis (cf. Gen 2:4; 5:1; 6:9; 10:1; 11:10,27; 25:12,19; 36:1; 37:2) — though here it introduces a collateral line before returning to the main covenant line with Isaac in v.19. Ishmael receives his own genealogical honor: he is 'Abraham's son.' His mother is identified doubly: 'Hagar the Egyptian' (her ethnic identity) and 'Sarah's handmaid' (her social status). This double identification preserves the historical complexity — Ishmael is simultaneously of Abraham and marginally positioned relative to the covenant family. God had promised Ishmael twelve princes (Gen 17:20), and the following verses fulfill that promise exactly.

verses.kjv_text: And these are the names of the sons of Ishmael, by their names, according to their generations: the firstborn of Ishmael, Nebajoth; and Kedar, and Adbeel, and Mibsam,
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה שְׁמוֹת בְּנֵי יִשְׁמָעֵאל בִּשְׁמֹתָם לְתוֹלְדֹתָם בְּכוֹר יִשְׁמָעֵאל נְבָיֹת וְקֵדָר וְאַדְבְּאֵל וּמִבְשָׂם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh shemot benei Yishmael bishemotam letoldotam bekhor Yishmael Nevayot veKedar ve'Adbeel uMivsam.
verses.study_note: Ishmael's twelve sons are listed, fulfilling God's promise in Genesis 17:20. Nebaioth (Nevayot), the firstborn, is associated by some scholars with the Nabataeans — the kingdom centered at Petra. Kedar becomes the most prominent of Ishmael's sons in Scripture: his descendants are the 'Kedarites,' powerful Arabian pastoral tribes. Isaiah addresses Kedar directly (Isa 21:16-17; 42:11; 60:7) and links them to eschatological glory: 'All the flocks of Kedar shall be gathered together unto thee' (Isa 60:7). The New Testament connection: some traditions identify Ishmael's descendants as the ancestors of the Arab peoples, and Paul in Galatians 4 uses Hagar/Ishmael as a typological figure for the covenant of law versus the covenant of promise.

verses.kjv_text: And Mishma, and Dumah, and Massa,
verses.original_text: וּמִשְׁמָע וְדוּמָה וּמַשָּׂא׃
verses.transliteration: uMishma veDumah uMassa.
verses.study_note: Dumah appears in Isaiah 21:11 as the subject of a prophetic oracle: 'The burden of Dumah.' It is identified with an oasis in northwestern Arabia (modern Saudi Arabia). Massa may be connected to the superscription of Proverbs 30:1 ('the words of Agur the son of Jakeh, even the prophecy [massa]') and Proverbs 31:1 ('the words of king Lemuel, the prophecy that his mother taught him') — some scholars identify 'Massa' as an Ishmaelite tribe whose king produced wisdom literature that was incorporated into the Hebrew canon, suggesting that divine wisdom crossed ethnic boundaries even within the patriarchal world.

verses.kjv_text: Hadar, and Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah:
verses.original_text: חֲדַד וְתֵימָא יְטוּר נָפִישׁ וָקֵדְמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Chadad veTema Yetur Nafish vaKedmah.
verses.study_note: Tema is a significant location in Arabian trade routes (Job 6:19; Isa 21:14; Jer 25:23). Jetur and Naphish appear in 1 Chronicles 5:19 as Ishmaelite tribes against whom the Transjordanian tribes of Israel (Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh) fought. These names thus become military designations in later Israelite history. Kedemah ('eastward' — the same root as 'east country' in v.6) perhaps represents the easternmost extent of Ishmael's territorial reach. The precision of twelve sons exactly fulfills the divine promise of 'twelve princes' (Gen 17:20), demonstrating that God keeps His word even to those who are not the covenant heir.

verses.kjv_text: These are the sons of Ishmael, and these are their names, by their towns, and by their castles; twelve princes according to their nations.
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה הֵם בְּנֵי יִשְׁמָעֵאל וְאֵלֶּה שְׁמֹתָם בְּחַצְרֵיהֶם וּבְטִירֹתָם שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר נְשִׂיאִם לְאֻמֹּתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Elleh hem benei Yishmael ve'elleh shemotam bechatzereihem uvetirotam sheneim asar nesi'im le'ummotam.
verses.study_note: \"Twelve princes' (sheneim asar nesi'im) — God's promise to Abraham in Genesis 17:20 is fulfilled exactly. The precision is theological: God keeps His promises to the non-covenant line as well as to the covenant line. 'Princes' (nesi'im — from 'nasa,' to lift up) means elevated leaders, chieftains. The tribal structure of twelve princes becomes the model that Israel itself will later mirror with its own twelve tribes — a structural parallel suggesting that the pattern of 'twelve' is built into the family of Abraham. 'By their towns' (chatzereihem — literally 'their enclosures') and 'castles' (tirotam — tent encampments or permanent settlements) reflects a semi-nomadic people.

verses.kjv_text: And these are the years of the life of Ishmael, an hundred and thirty and seven years: and he gave up the ghost and died; and was gathered unto his people.
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה שְׁנֵי חַיֵּי יִשְׁמָעֵאל מְאַת שָׁנָה וּשְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה וְשֶׁבַע שָׁנִים וַיִּגְוַע וַיָּמָת וַיֵּאָסֶף אֶל־עַמָּיו׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh shenei chayyei Yishmael me'at shanah ushloshim shanah vesheva shanim vayyigva vayyamat vayyei'asef el-ammav.
verses.study_note: Ishmael died at 137 years — ten years before his brother Isaac (who died at 180, Gen 35:29), and 48 years before his father Abraham died at 175. Wait — the chronology: Isaac was born when Abraham was 100; Ishmael was 14 at Isaac's birth (born when Abraham was 86, Gen 16:16); so Ishmael was born when Abraham was 86, died at 137, meaning he died when Abraham was 86+137=223... but Abraham only lived to 175. The resolution: Ishmael was 14 at Isaac's birth (Gen 16:16 combined with Gen 21:5), died at 137, which means he died when Isaac was 137-14=123 years old — about the time Jacob and Esau were 63. Ishmael dies 'gathered unto his people' — the same formula as Abraham (v.8), the same dignified obituary, confirming that God honors Ishmael as a son of Abraham even outside the covenant line.

verses.kjv_text: And they dwelt from Havilah unto Shur, that is before Egypt, as thou goest toward Assyria: and he died in the presence of all his brethren.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁכְּנוּ מֵחֲוִילָה עַד־שׁוּר אֲשֶׁר עַל־פְּנֵי מִצְרַיִם בֹּאֲכָה אַשּׁוּרָה עַל פְּנֵי כָל־אֶחָיו נָפָל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishkenu mechAvilah ad-Shur asher al-penei Mitzrayim bo'akhah Ashurah al penei kol-echav nafal.
verses.study_note: The geographic extent of Ishmael's territory — from Havilah (northeastern Arabia, near the Persian Gulf) to Shur (the desert east of Egypt) — mirrors the extent of his father's promised blessing and echoes the earlier description of Ishmael in Gen 16:12: 'he shall dwell in the presence of all his brethren.' The final phrase, 'he died in the presence of all his brethren' (al penei kol-echav nafal), fulfills that earlier word exactly. 'Nafal' (fell) is an unexpected verb for death — it may mean he settled, or it may capture something of Ishmael's wild, restless character ('a wild man,' Gen 16:12) coming to rest at last. The Ishmaelite world is vast, real, and historically verifiable.

verses.kjv_text: And these are the generations of Isaac, Abraham’s son: Abraham begat Isaac:
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת יִצְחָק בֶּן־אַבְרָהָם אַבְרָהָם הוֹלִיד אֶת־יִצְחָק׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh toldot Yitschak ben-Avraham Avraham holid et-Yitschak.
verses.study_note: The eleventh use of 'toledot' pivots the narrative decisively to the covenant line. The double statement 'Isaac, son of Abraham — Abraham begat Isaac' seems redundant but is theologically pointed: it insists on direct lineal descent against any doubt. The Jewish Midrash notes that some mocked the claim that Abraham was Isaac's father (given Sarah's age), so the text doubles down. The formula also establishes paternity legally and covenantally — Isaac's inheritance is grounded in his being Abraham's son. What follows (the barrenness of Rebekah, the oracle of election, the birth of twins) forms the most theologically dense birth narrative in Genesis.

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac was forty years old when he took Rebekah to wife, the daughter of Bethuel the Syrian of Padanaram, the sister to Laban the Syrian.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי יִצְחָק בֶּן־אַרְבָּעִים שָׁנָה בְּקַחְתּוֹ אֶת־רִבְקָה בַּת־בְּתוּאֵל הָאֲרַמִּי מִפַּדַּן אֲרָם אֲחוֹת לָבָן הָאֲרַמִּי לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi Yitschak ben-arba'im shanah bekachto et-Rivkah bat-Betuel ha'Arammi miPaddan Aram achot Lavan ha'Arammi lo le'ishah.
verses.study_note: Isaac was 40 at marriage. The narrator identifies Rebekah's lineage precisely: daughter of Bethuel the Syrian (Arammi), sister of Laban, from Paddan-aram (northwestern Mesopotamia). The 'Syrian' designation is significant — Rebekah is an Aramean (the same people as the later 'wandering Aramean' of Deuteronomy 26:5). The covenant family's Aramean roots become a declaration in Israel's liturgy: 'A wandering Aramean was my father' (Deut 26:5 — referring to Jacob, Rebekah's son). Rebekah's family connection to Laban will drive the entire Jacob narrative of chapters 28-31.

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac intreated the LORD for his wife, because she was barren: and the LORD was intreated of him, and Rebekah his wife conceived.
verses.original_text: וַיֶּעְתַּר יִצְחָק לַיהוָה לְנֹכַח אִשְׁתּוֹ כִּי עֲקָרָה הִוא וַיֵּעָתֶר לוֹ יְהוָה וַתַּהַר רִבְקָה אִשְׁתּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyetar Yitschak laYHVH lenokhach ishto ki akarah hi vayyei'ater lo YHVH vattahar Rivkah ishto.
verses.study_note: Isaac 'intreated' (vayyetar — intensely entreated, prayed earnestly) the LORD for Rebekah. The verb carries urgency and persistence. The threefold repetition of this root — vayyetar... vayyei'ater lo (he prayed... the LORD was moved by his prayer) — creates a beautiful symmetry: the prayer offered and the prayer answered using the same word. This is one of the most explicit prayer-answer sequences in Genesis. The pattern of barren wives (Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel) is theologically consistent: the covenant seed is never a product of natural human capacity but always of divine intervention. Every patriarch is born of a miracle.
verses.doctrinal_note: The patriarchal birth narratives follow a consistent pattern: the covenant wife is barren, the patriarch prays, God opens the womb. Sarah (Gen 21:1-2), Rebekah (Gen 25:21), Rachel (Gen 30:22-23), Hannah (1 Sam 1:19-20), Elizabeth (Luke 1:13). In each case the barrenness makes the birth miraculous, and the miracle declares: this child is God's gift, not human achievement. The covenant seed is always supernaturally produced — ultimately pointing to the greatest supernatural birth: the virgin conception of Jesus Christ (Luke 1:35).

verses.kjv_text: And the children struggled together within her; and she said, If it be so, why am I thus? And she went to enquire of the LORD.
verses.original_text: וַיִּתְרֹצֲצוּ הַבָּנִים בְּקִרְבָּהּ וַתֹּאמֶר אִם־כֵּן לָמָּה זֶּה אָנֹכִי וַתֵּלֶךְ לִדְרֹשׁ אֶת־יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitrotzetzu habbanim bekirbah vattomer im-ken lammah zeh anokhi vattelekh lidrosh et-YHVH.
verses.study_note: The 'struggling' (vayyitrotzetzu — a rare, intensive verb suggesting violent collision, crushing against each other) within Rebekah's womb is so severe that she questions the meaning of her existence: 'If it be so, why am I thus?' (im-ken lammah zeh anokhi — literally 'if thus, why am I?'). Her cry is existential anguish, not mere physical discomfort. Rebekah's response, however, is exemplary: she goes to 'enquire of the LORD' (lidrosh et-YHVH — to seek, to inquire, the root of 'midrash'). She brings her confusion to God rather than to human counsel. This is the first recorded instance of a woman directly seeking an oracle from the LORD in the patriarchal narratives.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto her, Two nations are in thy womb, and two manner of people shall be separated from thy bowels; and the one people shall be stronger than the other people; and the elder shall serve the younger.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה לָהּ שְׁנֵי גֹיִים בְּבִטְנֵךְ וּשְׁנֵי לְאֻמִּים מִמֵּעַיִךְ יִפָּרֵדוּ וּלְאֹם מִלְאֹם יֶאֱמָץ וְרַב יַעֲבֹד צָעִיר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH lah shenei goyim bevitnekh ushenei le'ummim mimme'ayikh yiparedu ule'om mile'om ye'ematz verav ya'avod tza'ir.
verses.study_note: The oracle of divine election — one of the most theologically significant verses in the Torah. Four declarations: (1) Two nations are in thy womb — the struggle is national, not merely personal; (2) Two peoples shall be separated — the division is divinely ordered; (3) One people shall be stronger than the other — power will shift between them; (4) The elder shall serve the younger (verav ya'avod tza'ir) — the supremely counterintuitive reversal. 'Rav' (elder, greater) and 'tza'ir' (younger, lesser) are reversed. This reversal of primogeniture is God's sovereign pattern throughout Genesis: Cain/Abel, Ishmael/Isaac, Esau/Jacob, Manasseh/Ephraim. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 9:10-13 as the foundation of the doctrine of unconditional election — God's choice made before the twins were born or had done good or evil.
verses.doctrinal_note: The oracle of Romans 9: Paul expounds this verse as the definitive proof of unconditional election. Before Jacob and Esau were born, before either had done good or evil, 'it was said unto her, The elder shall serve the younger' (Rom 9:12). This establishes that God's electing purpose is not based on human foreseen merit, works, or decision but solely on 'him that calleth' (Rom 9:11). The doctrine of sovereign election is not a New Testament innovation — it is embedded in the patriarchal birth narratives of Genesis, rooted in the character of the God who 'hath mercy on whom he will have mercy' (Rom 9:15-18).

verses.kjv_text: And when her days to be delivered were fulfilled, behold, there were twins in her womb.
verses.original_text: וַיִּמְלְאוּ יָמֶיהָ לָלֶדֶת וְהִנֵּה תוֹמִם בְּבִטְנָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyimle'u yameiha laledet vehineh tomim bevitnah.
verses.study_note: \"Behold, there were twins in her womb' — the narrative's 'behold' (hinneh) signals a moment of wonder and revelation. The word 'twins' (tomim — from 'ta'om,' to pair or couple) first appears in Scripture here. Despite God's oracle explaining the struggle (v.23), the actual discovery of twins at birth still carries the force of surprise — the divine explanation precedes the human discovery. Tomim is connected to the word 'tam' (complete, perfect, blameless) — the same word used of Noah (Gen 6:9) and later of Jacob himself (v.27, 'a plain man' — ish tam). The twins' names will carry their character from birth.

verses.kjv_text: And the first came out red, all over like an hairy garment; and they called his name Esau.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּצֵא הָרִאשׁוֹן אַדְמוֹנִי כֻּלּוֹ כְּאַדֶּרֶת שֵׂעָר וַיִּקְרְאוּ שְׁמוֹ עֵשָׂו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyetze harishon admoni kullo ke'adderet se'ar vayyikre'u shemo Esav.
verses.study_note: Esau's birth description is precise and ominous: 'red' (admoni — from 'adom,' red, the same root as Edom and Adam) and 'hairy' (se'ar — the root of 'Seir,' Esau's future land). His very body foreshadows his destiny and his descendants. 'Admoni' (red) anticipates Esau's other name, Edom ('red'), given in verse 30. 'Hairy' anticipates the deception of chapter 27 where Jacob uses goat skins to mimic Esau's feel. The firstborn comes out fully formed and named by his appearance — he is what you see. Esau's name (Esav) likely means 'hairy' or 'rough,' further cementing the identity-in-appearance theme.

verses.kjv_text: And after that came his brother out, and his hand took hold on Esau’s heel; and his name was called Jacob: and Isaac was threescore years old when she bare them.
verses.original_text: וְאַחֲרֵי־כֵן יָצָא אָחִיו וְיָדוֹ אֹחֶזֶת בַּעֲקֵב עֵשָׂו וַיִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ יַעֲקֹב וְיִצְחָק בֶּן־שִׁשִּׁים שָׁנָה בְּלֶדֶת אֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'acharei-khen yatza achiv veyadav ochezet ba'akev Esav vayyikra shemo Yaakov veYitschak ben-shishim shanah beledet otam.
verses.study_note: Jacob emerges grasping Esau's heel (akev) — and from this act he is named Yaakov ('he grasps the heel'; colloquially 'supplanter, deceiver'). The heel-grasp is not merely a birth curiosity — it is a prophetic sign of the reversal the oracle declared in v.23. Jacob is trying to come first even before he draws his first breath. His entire life story is of one who strives, grasps, and schemes — until he meets God at the Jabbok and is broken (Gen 32). Isaac was 60 at the birth of the twins — 20 years after his marriage to Rebekah (v.20). Twenty years of prayer (v.21) answered in twin sons.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jacob's name means both 'heel-holder' and 'supplanter/deceiver' — a name he must grow out of. God will rename him Israel ('he who strives with God' or 'God strives') after his wrestling match at the Jabbok (Gen 32:28). This pattern — a flawed given name replaced by a God-given name — runs throughout Scripture (Abram to Abraham, Sarai to Sarah, Simon to Peter, Saul to Paul). God does not leave His people as they were named by circumstance; He names them according to what He is making them.

verses.kjv_text: And the boys grew: and Esau was a cunning hunter, a man of the field; and Jacob was a plain man, dwelling in tents.
verses.original_text: וַיִּגְדְּלוּ הַנְּעָרִים וַיְהִי עֵשָׂו אִישׁ יֹדֵעַ צַיִד אִישׁ שָׂדֶה וְיַעֲקֹב אִישׁ תָּם יֹשֵׁב אֹהָלִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyigdelu hanne'arim vayehi Esav ish yodea tsayid ish sadeh veYaakov ish tam yoshev ohalim.
verses.study_note: The grown sons are presented in sharp contrast. Esau: 'a cunning hunter' (ish yodea tsayid — literally 'a man who knows hunting'); 'a man of the field' (ish sadeh). The 'field' (sadeh) is the undomesticated, dangerous wilderness — the realm of Cain (Gen 4:8 — 'when they were in the field') and of the serpent (Gen 3:1 — 'the beast of the field'). Jacob: 'a plain man' (ish tam — blameless, complete, quiet, wholesome — the same word as 'perfect' in Job 1:1 and 'upright' in Ps 37:37); 'dwelling in tents' (yoshev ohalim). The tent is the patriarchal dwelling, the place of family, covenant, and domestic life. Jacob's tent-dwelling connects him to Abraham and Isaac; Esau's field-ranging disconnects him from the family order. 'Tam' (plain/blameless) is one of the highest character designations in Hebrew — applied to Noah (Gen 6:9), Abraham (Gen 17:1 — 'be thou perfect'), and Job.

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac loved Esau, because he did eat of his venison: but Rebekah loved Jacob.
verses.original_text: וַיֶּאֱהַב יִצְחָק אֶת־עֵשָׂו כִּי־צַיִד בְּפִיו וְרִבְקָה אֹהֶבֶת אֶת־יַעֲקֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyeehav Yitschak et-Esav ki-tsayid befi veRivkah ohevet et-Yaakov.
verses.study_note: One of the most tragic verses in the patriarchal narrative. Parental love divided along meal-preference lines: Isaac loved Esau 'because he did eat of his venison' (ki-tsayid befi — literally 'because game was in his mouth'). The father's love is stomach-driven — he loved the taste that Esau provided. Rebekah's love of Jacob is stated without qualification or cause: 'Rebekah loved Jacob.' The contrast is subtle but damning: Isaac's love is transactional; Rebekah's appears unconditional. The divided parental loyalty creates the fractured family dynamics that will drive the deception of chapter 27. Partiality among parents plants seeds of rivalry among children — a pattern from Cain and Abel (God's 'partiality' to Abel) through Joseph and his brothers (Gen 37:3).

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob sod pottage: and Esau came from the field, and he was faint:
verses.original_text: וַיָּזֶד יַעֲקֹב נָזִיד וַיָּבֹא עֵשָׂו מִן־הַשָּׂדֶה וְהוּא עָיֵף׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyazed Yaakov nazid vayavo Esav min-hassadeh vehu ayef.
verses.study_note: Jacob is cooking (vayyazed — boiling, simmering, from 'zud' which also means 'to act presumptuously'). The word for stew, 'nazid,' appears only here and in a few other places (Gen 25:34; 2 Kings 4:38-39 — Elisha's 'death in the pot' incident). Esau comes from the field 'faint' (ayef — exhausted, weary). The stage is set: Jacob has food; Esau has need. The scene is designed by the narrator to show the stark contrast — Jacob the domestic tent-dweller has provision; Esau the self-reliant hunter of the field comes home empty and depleted. His physical exhaustion will cost him everything.

verses.kjv_text: And Esau said to Jacob, Feed me, I pray thee, with that same red pottage; for I am faint: therefore was his name called Edom.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר עֵשָׂו אֶל־יַעֲקֹב הַלְעִיטֵנִי נָא מִן־הָאָדֹם הָאָדֹם הַזֶּה כִּי עָיֵף אָנֹכִי עַל־כֵּן קָרָא־שְׁמוֹ אֱדוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Esav el-Yaakov hal'itemni na min-ha'adom ha'adom hazzeh ki ayef anokhi al-ken kara-shemo Edom.
verses.study_note: Esau's request is linguistically remarkable: 'Hal'itemni' — stuff me, pour it into me — is the word used for force-feeding animals. It is a verb of raw physical appetite with no dignity of self-restraint. He does not even know or name what he is eating — 'that same red stuff, that red stuff' (min-ha'adom ha'adom hazzeh — doubled for emphasis, perhaps because he cannot think clearly or does not care what it is). His entire consciousness has narrowed to immediate physical need. 'For I am faint' (ki ayef anokhi) — he uses the same word as v.29, making 'faint' his only description of his condition. The narrator then delivers the satirical etymology: 'therefore was his name called Edom' — the great nation of Edom takes its name from the red stew for which its founding father sold his eternal inheritance.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob said, Sell me this day thy birthright.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב מִכְרָה כַיּוֹם אֶת־בְּכֹרָתְךָ לִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yaakov mikhrah khayom et-bekhoratekha li.
verses.study_note: Jacob's demand is precise and exploitative: 'Sell me this day' (mikhrah khayom) — the word 'khayom' (this day, today) creates urgency, foreclosing deliberation. He has been waiting for this moment. The birthright (bekhorah) encompassed the double portion of inheritance (Deut 21:17), family headship, the right to serve as priest of the household, and — in the covenant family — the promise of Gen 3:15, the land, and the blessing. Jacob knows what he is asking for. His opportunism reveals the 'supplanter' character his name implies — but also his recognition that the birthright has ultimate value. He who grasps the heel in birth now grasps the birthright in commerce. The tragedy is that he is right about the value of what he seeks, but wrong in his method of obtaining it.

verses.kjv_text: And Esau said, Behold, I am at the point to die: and what profit shall this birthright do to me?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר עֵשָׂו הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי הוֹלֵךְ לָמוּת וְלָמָּה־זֶּה לִי בְּכֹרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Esav hinneh anokhi holekh lamut velammah-zeh li bekhorah.
verses.study_note: Esau's response is the most self-revealing line in the chapter: 'Behold, I am at the point to die' (hinneh anokhi holekh lamut — literally 'I am going to die'). He is not, in fact, dying — he is hungry and tired. His hyperbole reveals a person who cannot tolerate momentary discomfort, who collapses present appetite into apocalyptic language: 'I am dying.' If he truly believed he was dying, the birthright would be irrelevant; but he uses death as a justification for despising what he is not dying enough to value. 'What profit shall this birthright do to me?' (velammah-zeh li bekhorah) — the birthright is reduced to a profit calculation against immediate need. Esau has no theology of inheritance, no eschatology, no long view. He is the man of the present.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob said, Swear to me this day; and he sware unto him: and he sold his birthright unto Jacob.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב הִשָּׁבְעָה לִּי כַּיּוֹם וַיִּשָּׁבַע לוֹ וַיִּמְכֹּר אֶת־בְּכֹרָתוֹ לְיַעֲקֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yaakov hishsav'ah li khayom vayyishava lo vayyimkor et-bekhorato leYaakov.
verses.study_note: Jacob demands an oath (hishsav'ah — swear to me). He will not accept a casual promise — he knows Esau well enough to require an irrevocable commitment. The oath was the most binding legal instrument in the ancient world, sworn by invoking God's name as witness and guarantor. 'This day' (khayom) appears again — the urgency is maintained. The irrevocability of an oath given by a man 'at the point to die' is total: Esau cannot reclaim what he swears away. The transaction is legally complete: 'he sold his birthright unto Jacob' (vayyimkor et-bekhorato). This verb 'makar' (to sell) is commercial and final. Esau will later weep bitterly and seek the blessing back but 'found no place for repentance, though he sought it carefully with tears' (Heb 12:17).

verses.kjv_text: Then Jacob gave Esau bread and pottage of lentiles; and he did eat and drink, and rose up, and went his way: thus Esau despised his birthright.
verses.original_text: וְיַעֲקֹב נָתַן לְעֵשָׂו לֶחֶם וּנְזִיד עֲדָשִׁים וַיֹּאכַל וַיֵּשְׁתְּ וַיָּקָם וַיֵּלַךְ וַיִּבֶז עֵשָׂו אֶת־הַבְּכֹרָה׃
verses.transliteration: VeYaakov natan le'Esav lechem unzid adasim vayyokhal vayyesht vayyakam vayelekh vayyivez Esav et-habbkhorah.
verses.study_note: The verse proceeds in five staccato verbs after the transaction: 'he did eat... and drink... and rose up... and went his way' — each a short, sharp action. There is no reflection, no pause, no regret recorded. Esau eats, drinks, stands, and leaves. The meal is consumed in moments; the birthright is gone forever. The narrator's verdict comes last, and it is absolute: 'thus Esau despised his birthright' (vayyivez Esav et-habbekhorah). 'Despised' (vayyivez — from 'bazah,' to hold in contempt, to treat as worthless) is the narrator's theological judgment, not merely a description. To 'despise' something sacred is the deepest form of profanity — it treats the holy as negligible. Hebrews 12:16 calls Esau a 'profane person' (bebelos — one who profanes holy things). He treated the eternal as though it were worth less than soup.
verses.doctrinal_note: \"Thus Esau despised his birthright' — the narrator's verdict is the key to understanding Esau. He did not lose the birthright through ignorance or coercion; he traded it with full awareness, preferring immediate physical satisfaction over eternal covenant privilege. Hebrews 12:16-17 uses Esau as a warning to believers: Do not be like the one who, for a single meal, sold his inheritance. The pastoral application is not anti-Edomite but profoundly personal: every day believers face the choice between the eternal and the immediate. The question is not whether we are hungry, but whether our hunger is for the Kingdom or for soup.

meta.overview: Genesis 25 is a chapter of endings and beginnings, of deaths and births, of inheritance lost and gained. It opens with Abraham's twilight years — his marriage to Keturah, his gifts to the sons of concubines, and his death at 175 years. The burial scene at Machpelah (vv.9-10) is one of Scripture's most significant — Isaac and Ishmael stand together at their father's grave, and the narrator affirms that 'God blessed his son Isaac' after Abraham's death, signalling the covenant continuity. The chapter's middle section catalogues the twelve princes of Ishmael (mirroring Israel's twelve tribes), closing with Ishmael's death at 137 years. The chapter then pivots to its theological heart: the birth of Jacob and Esau. Rebekah's difficult pregnancy becomes the occasion for the first oracle of divine election in Genesis — 'the elder shall serve the younger' (v.23) — establishing that God's sovereign choice operates before birth and before any human merit. The chapter closes with one of the most sobering scenes in the Pentateuch: Esau, returning famished from the hunt, sells his birthright for a bowl of red stew. 'Thus Esau despised his birthright' — the narrator's verdict is unsparing. Chapter 25 is thus the hinge chapter of the patriarchal narrative: Abraham departs, Isaac inherits, and the next generation is already divided by divine election and human decision.
meta.theme: Divine Election, Covenant Continuity & the Cost of Despising Inheritance
meta.keyWordMeaning: Birthright — the firstborn's portion, encompassing double inheritance, family leadership, and covenant privilege. Esau's sale of it for stew is the defining act of spiritual profanity in the patriarchal narrative.
outline: Abraham's Later Life and Death (vv.1-11): Keturah, gifts to sons, death at 175, burial at Machpelah
The Genealogy of Ishmael (vv.12-18): Twelve princes, death at 137, 'in the presence of all his brethren'
Isaac's Barren Wife and the Oracle of Election (vv.19-23): Rebekah's painful pregnancy and God's sovereign answer
The Birth of Esau and Jacob (vv.24-26): The red one and the heel-catcher
Two Sons, Two Nations (vv.27-28): Contrasting characters and divided parental loyalties
Esau Sells His Birthright (vv.29-34): Red stew for the right of the firstborn

verses.kjv_text: And there was a famine in the land, beside the first famine that was in the days of Abraham. And Isaac went unto Abimelech king of the Philistines unto Gerar.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי רָעָב בָּאָרֶץ מִלְּבַד הָרָעָב הָרִאשׁוֹן אֲשֶׁר הָיָה בִּימֵי אַבְרָהָם וַיֵּלֶךְ יִצְחָק אֶל־אֲבִימֶלֶךְ מֶלֶךְ פְּלִשְׁתִּים גְּרָרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi ra'av ba'aretz milevad hara'av harishon asher hayah bimei Avraham vayelekh Yitschak el-Avimelekh melekh Pelishtim Gerarah.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with a deliberate echo of Genesis 12:10 — 'there was a famine in the land' — immediately establishing that Isaac is being tested by the same trial that shaped Abraham. The narrator flags this explicitly: 'beside the first famine... in the days of Abraham.' The same pressure, a new generation. 'Abimelech' may be a dynastic title rather than a personal name (like 'Pharaoh'), as a different Abimelech appears in Gen 20 and 21. Gerar was a Philistine city-state in the coastal plain of Canaan. Isaac's movement toward Gerar — rather than Egypt — sets up God's decisive redirection in verse 2. The pattern of famine-as-test runs from Abraham through Isaac through Joseph, each time revealing whether the patriarch trusts in God's provision or in human strategies.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD appeared unto him, and said, Go not down into Egypt; dwell in the land which I shall tell thee of:
verses.original_text: וַיֵּרָא אֵלָיו יְהוָה וַיֹּאמֶר אַל־תֵּרֵד מִצְרָיְמָה שְׁכֹן בָּאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר אֹמַר אֵלֶיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyera elav YHVH vayomer al-tered Mitzraymah shkhon ba'aretz asher omar eleikha.
verses.study_note: God intervenes before Isaac reaches Egypt — the temptation Abraham yielded to (Gen 12:10-20). 'Go not down' (al-tered) is a preventive command, a divine guardrail at the moment of decision. Abraham went to Egypt without divine instruction and reaped deception and shame; Isaac is expressly forbidden the same path. 'Dwell in the land' — the command to remain is the command to trust. The land itself is the covenant promise; to flee it is to act as though God cannot sustain life within it. 'Which I shall tell thee of' (asher omar eleikha) — God will specify; Isaac must wait on revelation rather than act on survival instinct. This is the first of three divine appearances to Isaac in the chapter (vv.2, 24), each deepening the covenant reaffirmation.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's specific guidance overrides natural survival logic. Abraham's flight to Egypt was self-directed; Isaac's restraint is God-directed. The principle holds through Scripture: God's appointed place of blessing is often the place of apparent lack, not the abundant land we would choose. 'Where God guides, God provides' is the covenant logic of this verse.

verses.kjv_text: Sojourn in this land, and I will be with thee, and will bless thee; for unto thee, and unto thy seed, I will give all these countries, and I will perform the oath which I sware unto Abraham thy father;
verses.original_text: גּוּר בָּאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת וְאֶהְיֶה עִמְּךָ וַאֲבָרֶכְךָ כִּי־לְךָ וּלְזַרְעֲךָ אֶתֵּן אֶת־כָּל־הָאֲרָצֹת הָאֵל וַהֲקִמֹתִי אֶת־הַשְּׁבֻעָה אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּעְתִּי לְאַבְרָהָם אֲבִיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Gur ba'aretz hazzo't ve'ehyeh immekha va'avarekhekkha ki-lekha ulezar'akha etten et-kol-ha'aratzot ha'el vahakimoti et-hashvuah asher nishba'ti le'Avraham avikha.
verses.study_note: God gives Isaac the covenant in full: (1) Divine presence — 'I will be with thee' (ve'ehyeh immekha), the foundational promise that anchors all others; (2) Blessing — 'I will bless thee'; (3) Land — 'I will give all these countries' to Isaac and his seed; (4) Oath-fulfillment — 'the oath which I sware unto Abraham.' The covenant is explicitly traced to Abraham and transferred to Isaac, confirming that election is not by natural succession alone but by divine sovereign choice. 'Sojourn' (gur) implies temporary resident status — Isaac does not own the land yet but holds it by promise. The verb 'vahakimoti' (I will perform/establish) uses the covenant-establishment term from Gen 17:7.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Abrahamic covenant is unconditional and irrevocable. God does not renegotiate its terms with each generation — He 'performs the oath' (hakimoti et-hashvuah). Isaac inherits not because of his own merit but because God swore to Abraham. This is the theological root of covenant theology: God's promises run through His appointed seed regardless of individual worthiness. Paul expounds this in Galatians 3:14-29.

verses.kjv_text: And I will make thy seed to multiply as the stars of heaven, and will give unto thy seed all these countries; and in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed;
verses.original_text: וְהִרְבֵּיתִי אֶת־זַרְעֲךָ כְּכוֹכְבֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם וְנָתַתִּי לְזַרְעֲךָ אֵת כָּל־הָאֲרָצֹת הָאֵל וְהִתְבָּרְכוּ בְזַרְעֲךָ כֹּל גּוֹיֵי הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehirbeti et-zar'akha kekhochvei hashamayim venttati lezar'akha et kol-ha'aratzot ha'el vehitbarku vezar'akha kol goyei ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The three strands of the Abrahamic covenant are reaffirmed to Isaac: (1) innumerable descendants — 'as the stars of heaven' (Gen 15:5; 22:17); (2) the land — 'all these countries'; (3) the universal blessing — 'in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed.' The phrase 'in thy seed' (bezar'akha) is Messianic — Paul explicitly identifies this 'seed' as singular (Christ) in Galatians 3:16. The universal scope — 'all nations' (kol goyei ha'aretz) — breaks Israel's blessing outward into a global promise, the proto-gospel announced to Abraham now reiterated to his son.
verses.doctrinal_note: The gospel was preached beforehand to Abraham (Gal 3:8): 'in thee shall all nations be blessed.' That promise is now channeled through Isaac and ultimately fulfilled in Jesus Christ, who is 'the seed' (Gal 3:16). The universal scope of salvation — all nations — was God's intention from the patriarchal age. The Abrahamic covenant is not merely national but cosmological.

verses.kjv_text: Because that Abraham obeyed my voice, and kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws.
verses.original_text: עֵקֶב אֲשֶׁר־שָׁמַע אַבְרָהָם בְּקֹלִי וַיִּשְׁמֹר מִשְׁמַרְתִּי מִצְוֹתַי חֻקּוֹתַי וְתוֹרֹתָי׃
verses.transliteration: Ekev asher-shama Avraham bekolei vayyishmor mishmarti mitzvotai chukotai vetorotai.
verses.study_note: The reason for Isaac's inheritance: 'because Abraham obeyed my voice' (ekev asher-shama). 'Ekev' means 'heel, consequence' — the same root as Jacob's name. Abraham's obedience has consequences that ripple forward through generations. Four terms describe Abraham's faithfulness: (1) mishmarti — my charge/watch-duty; (2) mitzvotai — my commandments; (3) chukotai — my statutes (prescribed limits); (4) torotai — my laws/teachings (the root of 'Torah'). These legal terms are remarkable before Sinai — Abraham lived the spirit of the Torah before it was codified. This verse answers a key theological question: did Abraham earn the blessing? No — the covenant was established by grace (Gen 15); Abraham's obedience is the response, not the ground, of the covenant. His obedience demonstrates genuine faith (James 2:21-23).
verses.doctrinal_note: Abraham's obedience did not earn the covenant but confirmed the covenant. The covenant was made by promise and sealed by God's oath (Gen 15). Abraham's keeping of God's commands is the fruit of justifying faith — not its root. This is the pattern of all covenant obedience: 'we love him, because he first loved us' (1 John 4:19). The blessing flows to Isaac not because Isaac earned it but because Abraham believed God and it was counted to him for righteousness (Gen 15:6).

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac dwelt in Gerar:
verses.original_text: וַיֵּשֶׁב יִצְחָק בִּגְרָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyeshev Yitschak biGrar.
verses.study_note: One of the shortest verses in the patriarchal narratives — five words in English, four in Hebrew — but freighted with significance. Isaac obeys: he does not go to Egypt. He dwells (vayyeshev) in Gerar, exactly as commanded. The brevity is itself a narrative statement of compliance. Yet Gerar is still potentially dangerous territory — Philistine country — and the next verse reveals that the dangers are already forming in Isaac's mind.

verses.kjv_text: And the men of the place asked him of his wife; and he said, She is my sister: for he feared to say, She is my wife; lest, said he, the men of the place should kill me for Rebekah; because she was fair to look upon.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁאֲלוּ אַנְשֵׁי הַמָּקוֹם לְאִשְׁתּוֹ וַיֹּאמֶר אֲחֹתִי הִוא כִּי יָרֵא לֵאמֹר אִשְׁתִּי פֶּן־יַהַרְגֻנִי אַנְשֵׁי הַמָּקוֹם עַל־רִבְקָה כִּי־טוֹבַת מַרְאֶה הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyish'alu anshei hammakom le'ishto vayomer achoti hi ki yare lemor ishti pen-yaharguni anshei hammakom al-Rivkah ki-tovath mar'eh hi.
verses.study_note: The third 'wife-sister' deception in Genesis (cf. Gen 12:13; 20:2) — and the first in which the narrator gives us the patriarch's reasoning in his own words: 'I feared' (yare). Unlike Abraham, who had a half-truth (Sarah was his half-sister), Rebekah is clearly and only Isaac's wife. The deception is unambiguous. The same fear drives the same lie across two generations — showing that learned sin patterns transmit through family systems. 'Fair to look upon' (tovath mar'eh) echoes the description of Sarai in Gen 12:11 and Rachel in Gen 29:17. Beauty in the narrative is rarely uncomplicated — it provokes danger as often as delight.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when he had been there a long time, that Abimelech king of the Philistines looked out at a window, and saw, and, behold, Isaac was sporting with Rebekah his wife.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כִּי אָרְכוּ־לוֹ שָׁם הַיָּמִים וַיַּשְׁקֵף אֲבִימֶלֶךְ מֶלֶךְ פְּלִשְׁתִּים בְּעַד הַחַלּוֹן וַיַּרְא וְהִנֵּה יִצְחָק מְצַחֵק אֵת רִבְקָה אִשְׁתּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi ki arkhu-lo sham hayyamim vayyashkef Avimelekh melekh Pelishtim be'ad hachallon vayyar vehineh Yitschak metzachek et Rivkah ishto.
verses.study_note: The exposure comes not through confrontation but through an accidentally open window. 'Sporting' (metzachek — from the root 'tzachak,' to laugh) — the same verb that underlies Isaac's name (Yitschak — 'he laughs'). Isaac is behaving in a way that only a husband would behave toward a wife — the intimacy of their relationship, observed from above, gives the game away. Abimelech has a window into the truth that Isaac thought hidden. There is deliberate wordplay: the man named 'Laughter' is 'laughing/sporting' with his wife — his name betrays his secret. God arranged the exposure from an unexpected angle.

verses.kjv_text: And Abimelech called Isaac, and said, Behold, of a surety she is thy wife: and how saidst thou, She is my sister? And Isaac said unto him, Because I said, Lest I die for her.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא אֲבִימֶלֶךְ לְיִצְחָק וַיֹּאמֶר אַךְ הִנֵּה אִשְׁתְּךָ הִוא וְאֵיךְ אָמַרְתָּ אֲחֹתִי הִוא וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו יִצְחָק כִּי אָמַרְתִּי פֶּן־אָמוּת עָלֶיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra Avimelekh leYitschak vayomer akh hinneh ishtekha hi ve'eikh amarta achoti hi vayomer elav Yitschak ki amarti pen-amut aleha.
verses.study_note: Abimelech's rebuke is measured and direct: 'of a surety she is thy wife' (akh hinneh ishtekha hi). The 'akh' (surely, indeed) signals emphatic certainty. Isaac's only defense is naked fear: 'Lest I die for her' (pen-amut aleha). There is no theological rationalization, no half-truth defense as Abraham offered. Just fear. The pagan king again (as in Gen 20) holds higher moral ground in the immediate exchange than the patriarch — a humiliating structural irony that the narrator does not soften. Isaac trusted Philistine fear of God less than God Himself.

verses.kjv_text: And Abimelech said, What is this thou hast done unto us? one of the people might lightly have lien with thy wife, and thou shouldest have brought guiltiness upon us.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ מַה־זֹּאת עָשִׂיתָ לָּנוּ כִּמְעַט שָׁכַב אַחַד הָעָם אֶת־אִשְׁתֶּךָ וְהֵבֵאתָ עָלֵינוּ אָשָׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Avimelekh mah-zot asita lanu kim'at shakhav achad ha'am et-ishtekha veheveta aleinu asham.
verses.study_note: Abimelech articulates the precise danger: guilt (asham). The word 'asham' is the same word used for the 'guilt offering' in Leviticus 5-7 — a required sacrifice for inadvertent sin. Abimelech feared not just social harm but moral/cultic contamination. He understood that adultery — even unknowing — incurs liability before God. This awareness exactly mirrors Genesis 20:4-7, where the earlier Abimelech's defense before God was 'in the integrity of my heart.' The Philistine king demonstrates a moral theology that Isaac should have trusted rather than feared.

verses.kjv_text: And Abimelech charged all his people, saying, He that toucheth this man or his wife shall surely be put to death.
verses.original_text: וַיְצַו אֲבִימֶלֶךְ אֶת־כָּל־הָעָם לֵאמֹר הַנֹּגֵעַ בָּאִישׁ הַזֶּה וּבְאִשְׁתּוֹ מוֹת יוּמָת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyetzav Avimelekh et-kol-ha'am lemor hannoge'a ba'ish hazzeh uve'ishto mot yumat.
verses.study_note: Abimelech issues a royal decree: anyone who touches Isaac or Rebekah shall be put to death (mot yumat — the emphatic death penalty formula). This is remarkable: a Philistine king provides royal protection for the patriarch who deceived him. God's sovereign care for Isaac works even through the pagan king's indignation. The very mechanism of deception — Abimelech's authority — becomes the mechanism of protection. Providence overrides deception.

verses.kjv_text: Then Isaac sowed in that land, and received in the same year an hundredfold: and the LORD blessed him.
verses.original_text: וַיִּזְרַע יִצְחָק בָּאָרֶץ הַהִוא וַיִּמְצָא בַּשָּׁנָה הַהִוא מֵאָה שְׁעָרִים וַיְבָרְכֵהוּ יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyizra Yitschak ba'aretz hahi vayyimtza bashanah hahi me'ah she'arim vayyevarekhehu YHVH.
verses.study_note: The pivot of the chapter. After the deception (vv.7-11), the narrative turns to God's blessing — which no human failure can prevent. 'Hundredfold' (me'ah she'arim — literally 'a hundred gates/measures') is an extraordinary yield in ancient Near Eastern agriculture. Normal yields were perhaps 7-10 fold in good soil. A hundredfold harvest in a time of famine is a supernatural sign. Jesus will use precisely this image — 'some an hundredfold' — in the Parable of the Sower (Matt 13:8, 23), where it describes maximum covenant fruitfulness. The cause is unambiguous: 'the LORD blessed him' — not the soil, not Isaac's agricultural technique, but God's direct intervention.
verses.doctrinal_note: Supernatural blessing often follows or accompanies spiritual failure, demonstrating that God's grace is not dependent on human perfection. Isaac deceived Abimelech, then reaped a hundredfold harvest — not because the deception was acceptable, but because God's covenant purposes are not thwarted by human weakness. The blessing is by grace, not merit. Jesus' 'hundredfold' in the Parable of the Sower (Matt 13) connects covenant fruitfulness with the reception of the Word of God.

verses.kjv_text: And the man waxed great, and went forward, and grew until he became very great:
verses.original_text: וַיִּגְדַּל הָאִישׁ וַיֵּלֶךְ הָלוֹךְ וְגָדֵל עַד כִּי־גָדַל מְאֹד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyigdal ha'ish vayelekh halokh vegadel ad ki-gadal me'od.
verses.study_note: A triadic intensification in the Hebrew: vayyigdal... gadol... gadal me'od (he became great... growing greater... very great). The threefold repetition of the root 'gadal' (great, grow) depicts continuous upward progression. The phrase 'went forward and grew' (vayelekh halokh vegadel) uses two infinitive absolutes to express relentless advancement. This is covenant blessing in material form — visibly authenticating God's word. The surrounding Philistines cannot explain it by natural means.
