verses.kjv_text: For he had possession of flocks, and possession of herds, and great store of servants: and the Philistines envied him.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי לוֹ מִקְנֵה־צֹאן וּמִקְנֵה בָקָר וַעֲבֻדָּה רַבָּה וַיְקַנְאוּ אֹתוֹ פְּלִשְׁתִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi lo mikneh-tzon umikneh vakar va'avuddah rabbah vayyekane'u oto Pelishtim.
verses.study_note: Isaac's wealth is catalogued: flocks, herds, and 'great store of servants' (avuddah rabbah — literally 'a great household/service'). The accumulation mirrors Abraham's (Gen 13:2; 24:35). The response is envy (vayyekane'u — from 'kana,' jealousy/zeal). Envy is the natural reaction of those who see covenant blessing from outside the covenant. The Philistines cannot possess what Isaac has because they do not possess what Isaac has — the God of the covenant. Their envy is the worldly equivalent of recognizing that Isaac's blessing is real and inexplicable by natural means.

verses.kjv_text: For all the wells which his father’s servants had digged in the days of Abraham his father, the Philistines had stopped them, and filled them with earth.
verses.original_text: וְכָל־הַבְּאֵרֹת אֲשֶׁר חָפְרוּ עַבְדֵי אָבִיו בִּימֵי אַבְרָהָם אָבִיו סִתְּמוּם פְּלִשְׁתִּים וַיְמַלְאוּם עָפָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol-habbe'erot asher chaferu avdei aviv bimei Avraham aviv sittemum Pelishtim vaymale'um afar.
verses.study_note: The Philistines' weapon is not warfare but water-denial: they stop up Abraham's wells. In a semi-arid region, control of water is control of life. Wells were among the most valuable assets in the ancient Near East — treaties were made over them (Gen 21:25-31). To fill a well with earth (earth = afar, dust — the inert, life-denying substance) is to reverse creation: God breathed life into dust (Gen 2:7); the Philistines pack dust back into the source of life. The wells become the chapter's central symbol of contested blessing.

verses.kjv_text: And Abimelech said unto Isaac, Go from us; for thou art much mightier than we.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ אֶל־יִצְחָק לֵךְ מֵעִמָּנוּ כִּי־עָצַמְתָּ מִמֶּנּוּ מְאֹד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Avimelekh el-Yitschak lekh me'immanu ki-atzamta mimmenu me'od.
verses.study_note: Abimelech's expulsion is politely framed but real: 'thou art much mightier than we' (ki-atzamta mimmenu me'od). The word 'atzamta' (from 'atzam') means bone-strong, numerically powerful — the same word used of Israel becoming 'mightier than Egypt' (Exod 1:9). Isaac is the first small fulfillment of the covenant promise of a 'great nation' (Gen 12:2) — already the Philistines feel outmatched. The expulsion of the blessed man is an act of fear, and fear is the opposite of faith.

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac departed thence, and pitched his tent in the valley of Gerar, and dwelt there.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּלֶךְ מִשָּׁם יִצְחָק וַיַּחַן בְּנַחַל גְּרָר וַיֵּשֶׁב שָׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayelekh misham Yitschak vayyachan benahal Gerar vayyeshev sham.
verses.study_note: Isaac moves without quarrel — he does not resist the expulsion. He moves to the 'valley' (nachal) of Gerar, a wadi region outside the city proper. The movement is from city to valley, from settled civilization to the margins. This is the pattern of the covenant people throughout Genesis: Abraham was a sojourner, and now Isaac is pushed to the edges. The wilderness becomes the locale of covenant renewal, not the city.

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac digged again the wells of water, which they had digged in the days of Abraham his father; for the Philistines had stopped them after the death of Abraham: and he called their names after the names by which his father had called them.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשָׁב יִצְחָק וַיַּחְפֹּר אֶת־בְּאֵרֹת הַמַּיִם אֲשֶׁר חָפְרוּ בִּימֵי אַבְרָהָם אָבִיו וַיְסַתְּמוּם פְּלִשְׁתִּים אַחֲרֵי מוֹת אַבְרָהָם וַיִּקְרָא לָהֶן שֵׁמוֹת כַּשֵּׁמֹת אֲשֶׁר קָרָא לָהֶן אָבִיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashav Yitschak vayyachpor et-be'erot hamayim asher chafru bimei Avraham aviv vaysattemum Pelishtim acharei mot Avraham vayyikra lahen shemot kashemot asher kara lahen aviv.
verses.study_note: Isaac's response to opposition is remarkably constructive: he re-digs. He does not dig new wells — he restores his father's. 'He called their names after the names... his father had called them' — he is a restorer of the wells of Abraham, a preserver of inherited covenant memory. The act of naming the wells by Abraham's original names is an act of covenant continuity — asserting that the interrupted inheritance is still valid, still his. Restoration before innovation is Isaac's spiritual instinct.
verses.doctrinal_note: Isaac re-digging Abraham's wells is a pattern for spiritual restoration in every generation. The Reformation was a re-digging of Pauline wells stopped up by medieval accretion. Revival is always a return to the springs of Scripture and prayer that previous generations knew and that opposition has filled with the earth of tradition and compromise. 'Call upon the name of the LORD' at Beer-sheba (v.25) was Abraham's original act — Isaac restores it.

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac’s servants digged in the valley, and found there a well of springing water.
verses.original_text: וַיַּחְפְּרוּ עַבְדֵי יִצְחָק בַּנָּחַל וַיִּמְצְאוּ שָׁם בְּאֵר מַיִם חַיִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyachperu avdei Yitschak bannahal vayyimtze'u sham be'er mayim chayyim.
verses.study_note: In the valley, Isaac's servants find a new well — and it is no ordinary well. 'Mayim chayyim' — living water, springing water, water from a perpetual underground source (not a cistern holding rainwater). This precise phrase, 'mayim chayyim' (living water), becomes a profound theological term in Scripture: Jeremiah 2:13 calls God 'the fountain of living waters'; John 4:10-14 records Jesus offering 'living water' (hudor zon — the Greek equivalent); John 7:38 describes the rivers of living water from the believer's heart. The discovery of living water in a disputed valley is the theological climax of the well narratives.

verses.kjv_text: And the herdmen of Gerar did strive with Isaac’s herdmen, saying, The water is ours: and he called the name of the well Esek; because they strove with him.
verses.original_text: וַיָּרִיבוּ רֹעֵי גְרָר עִם־רֹעֵי יִצְחָק לֵאמֹר לָנוּ הַמָּיִם וַיִּקְרָא שֵׁם־הַבְּאֵר עֵשֶׂק כִּי הִתְעַשְּׂקוּ עִמּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyarivu ro'ei Gerar im-ro'ei Yitschak lemor lanu hamayim vayyikra shem-habbe'er Esek ki hit'assequ immo.
verses.study_note: The first contested well is named 'Esek' (contention, striving). The Philistine herdsmen claim the water: 'the water is ours.' Isaac does not fight — he yields and moves on. The pattern of the three wells (Esek-Sitnah-Rehoboth) traces a spiritual journey: from contention through accusation to rest. Isaac's non-resistance to the first and second wells is a form of peace-making that ultimately leads to Rehoboth (room) and Beer-sheba (the covenant). The man who trusts God's provision does not need to fight for every spring.

verses.kjv_text: And they digged another well, and strove for that also: and he called the name of it Sitnah.
verses.original_text: וַיַּחְפְּרוּ בְּאֵר אַחֶרֶת וַיָּרִיבוּ גַּם־עָלֶיהָ וַיִּקְרָא שְׁמָהּ שִׂטְנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyachperu be'er acheret vayyarivu gam-aleha vayyikra shmah Sitnah.
verses.study_note: The second well is named 'Sitnah' — accusation, enmity, adversity. The root 'satan' (adversary, accuser) underlies the name. This is the language of spiritual warfare: the opposition has escalated from mere contention (Esek) to active hostility (Sitnah). Isaac digs, finds water, loses it, and moves on again. His perseverance without retaliation is extraordinary. Each abandoned well represents covenant blessing voluntarily surrendered to keep the peace — a form of suffering that anticipates the servant who 'is oppressed, and he is afflicted, yet he opened not his mouth' (Isa 53:7).

verses.kjv_text: And he removed from thence, and digged another well; and for that they strove not: and he called the name of it Rehoboth; and he said, For now the LORD hath made room for us, and we shall be fruitful in the land.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעְתֵּק מִשָּׁם וַיַּחְפֹּר בְּאֵר אַחֶרֶת וְלֹא רָבוּ עָלֶיהָ וַיִּקְרָא שְׁמָהּ רְחֹבוֹת וַיֹּאמֶר כִּי־עַתָּה הִרְחִיב יְהוָה לָנוּ וּפָרִינוּ בָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyatek misham vayyachpor be'er acheret velo ravu aleha vayyikra shmah Rechavot vayomer ki-attah hirchiv YHVH lanu ufarinu va'aretz.
verses.study_note: The third well is named Rehoboth — 'wide places, room.' No opposition. The breakthrough comes not by fighting but by faithful perseverance through two rounds of loss. Isaac's confession is theologically luminous: 'the LORD hath made room for us' (hirchiv YHVH lanu). The verb 'hirchiv' (from 'rachav' — broad, wide) is the causative: God did the broadening. Isaac does not credit his own perseverance but God's sovereign opening. 'We shall be fruitful in the land' (ufarinu va'aretz) uses 'parah' (to be fruitful), the covenant word from Gen 1:28 and 17:20. Rehoboth is the fulfilment of patient trust.
verses.doctrinal_note: The three-well pattern — Esek (contention), Sitnah (accusation), Rehoboth (room) — is a theological template for perseverance under opposition. The breakthrough comes at the third attempt, after yielding twice. This pattern recurs in Scripture: the third day brings resurrection, the third trial produces victory. God makes room for those who trust Him through repeated loss. The only strategy that produces Rehoboth is not retaliation but perseverance with non-resistance.

verses.kjv_text: And he went up from thence to Beersheba.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַל מִשָּׁם בְּאֵר שָׁבַע׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaal misham Be'er Shava.
verses.study_note: A brief geographical transition of enormous theological weight. Isaac goes 'up' (vayyaal) from the valley to Beer-sheba — both literally (it is higher ground) and spiritually. Beer-sheba is Abraham's place of covenant encounter: there Abraham planted a grove and called on the name of the LORD (Gen 21:33), and there the covenant with the first Abimelech was ratified (Gen 21:31-32). Isaac is returning to the family's holy ground, the site saturated with covenant history. What follows (the theophany, the altar, the calling on God's name) precisely mirrors Abraham's worship at the same location.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD appeared unto him the same night, and said, I am the God of Abraham thy father: fear not, for I am with thee, and will bless thee, and multiply thy seed for my servant Abraham’s sake.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּרָא אֵלָיו יְהוָה בַּלַּיְלָה הַהוּא וַיֹּאמֶר אָנֹכִי אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם אָבִיךָ אַל־תִּירָא כִּי־אִתְּךָ אָנֹכִי וּבֵרַכְתִּיךָ וְהִרְבֵּיתִי אֶת־זַרְעֲךָ בַּעֲבוּר אַבְרָהָם עַבְדִּי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyera elav YHVH ballaylah hahu vayomer anokhi Elohei Avraham avikha al-tira ki-ittekha anokhi uverakhtikha vehirbeti et-zar'akha ba'avur Avraham avdi.
verses.study_note: The second divine appearance to Isaac (cf. v.2), this time at night — as with Jacob (Gen 32) and Gideon (Judg 6). Four elements: (1) Self-identification — 'I am the God of Abraham thy father.' The God who spoke to Abraham now personally speaks to Isaac. (2) Reassurance — 'Fear not' (al-tira). The 'fear not' command appears over 365 times in Scripture. (3) Promise — presence and blessing. (4) Grounding — 'for my servant Abraham's sake' (ba'avur Avraham avdi). Isaac's blessing is tethered to Abraham's covenant. God calls Abraham 'my servant' (avdi) — the highest relational honorific in the Hebrew Bible, used of Moses (Num 12:7), David (2 Sam 7:5), and prophetically of the Messiah (Isa 42:1).
verses.doctrinal_note: \"Fear not\" is the characteristic divine greeting when God appears in darkness and vulnerability. God's self-identification as 'the God of Abraham' signals covenant continuity — He is the same God across generations, bound by the same oath. The basis of Isaac's security is not his own standing but Abraham's covenant and God's faithfulness to it. This is the logic of imputed righteousness: we stand before God on the basis of Another's merit, not our own.

verses.kjv_text: And he builded an altar there, and called upon the name of the LORD, and pitched his tent there: and there Isaac’s servants digged a well.
verses.original_text: וַיִּבֶן שָׁם מִזְבֵּחַ וַיִּקְרָא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה וַיֶּט שָׁם אָהֳלוֹ וַיִּכְרוּ שָׁם עַבְדֵי יִצְחָק בְּאֵר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiven sham mizbei'ach vayyikra beshem YHVH vayyett sham oholo vayyikhru sham avdei Yitschak be'er.
verses.study_note: Isaac's response to the theophany is threefold and echoes Abraham exactly: (1) He builds an altar (Gen 12:7; 13:18; 22:9) — worship is the first response to divine encounter. (2) He calls upon the name of the LORD (Gen 12:8; 13:4; 21:33) — public invocation and prayer. (3) He pitches his tent — establishing residence, claiming the place. Then he digs a well — providing for his community. Altar before tent; worship before work; God before provision. Isaac replicates Abraham's spiritual pattern at the very location Abraham established it. The covenant family has a liturgical rhythm.

verses.kjv_text: Then Abimelech went to him from Gerar, and Ahuzzath one of his friends, and Phichol the chief captain of his army.
verses.original_text: וַאֲבִימֶלֶךְ הָלַךְ אֵלָיו מִגְּרָר וַאֲחֻזַּת מֵרֵעֵהוּ וּפִיכֹל שַׂר־צְבָאוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Va'Avimelekh halakh elav miGrar va'Achuzzat mer'eihu uFikhol sar-tzeva'o.
verses.study_note: The same Abimelech who expelled Isaac (v.16) now comes to him. He brings Ahuzzath ('possession' — perhaps an advisor) and Phichol ('mouth of all' — commander of his army). The same Phichol accompanied Abimelech to the covenant with Abraham in Gen 21:22. Whether the same individuals or their successors using the same titles, the pattern is identical: the Philistine king cannot ignore the visibly blessed patriarch and must come to terms with him. The world comes to the covenant man, not the reverse.

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac said unto them, Wherefore come ye to me, seeing ye hate me, and have sent me away from you?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם יִצְחָק מַדּוּעַ בָּאתֶם אֵלַי וְאַתֶּם שְׂנֵאתֶם אֹתִי וַתְּשַׁלְּחוּנִי מֵאִתְּכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer aleihem Yitschak maddua batem elai ve'attem sene'tem oti vateshallachuni me'ittekhem.
verses.study_note: Isaac's question is direct and honest: 'ye hate me' (sene'tem oti). He does not pretend the expulsion did not happen or that the hostility was not real. He names the truth before negotiating. The word 'sane' (hate) is strong — the same word used for Esau's hatred of Jacob (Gen 27:41) and for what God 'hates' in Malachi. Yet Isaac's directness is not bitterness — he will make the covenant. Honest acknowledgment of grievance is possible within covenant peace-making.

verses.kjv_text: And they said, We saw certainly that the LORD was with thee: and we said, Let there be now an oath betwixt us, even betwixt us and thee, and let us make a covenant with thee;
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ רָאוֹ רָאִינוּ כִּי־הָיָה יְהוָה עִמָּךְ וַנֹּאמֶר תְּהִי נָא אָלָה בֵּינוֹתֵינוּ בֵּינֵינוּ וּבֵינֶךָ וְנִכְרְתָה בְרִית עִמָּךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru ra'o ra'inu ki-hayah YHVH immakh vannomer tehi na alah beinoteinu beineinu uveinekha venikretah verit immakh.
verses.study_note: The Philistines' confession is striking: 'We saw certainly that the LORD was with thee' (ra'o ra'inu ki-hayah YHVH immakh). The double infinitive absolute (ra'o ra'inu — 'seeing we have seen') is emphatic certainty. The pagan king cannot deny the evidence of God's presence with Isaac. This is the missional power of covenant blessing — it generates a testimony visible to outsiders. The church's prosperity and unity is meant to make the world say what Abimelech says here (John 13:35; 17:21).

verses.kjv_text: That thou wilt do us no hurt, as we have not touched thee, and as we have done unto thee nothing but good, and have sent thee away in peace: thou art now the blessed of the LORD.
verses.original_text: אִם־תַּעֲשֵׂה עִמָּנוּ רָעָה כַּאֲשֶׁר לֹא נְגַעֲנוּךָ וְכַאֲשֶׁר עָשִׂינוּ עִמְּךָ רַק־טוֹב וַנְּשַׁלֵּחֲךָ בְּשָׁלוֹם אַתָּה עַתָּה בְּרוּךְ יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Im-ta'aseh immanu ra'ah ka'asher lo nega'anukkha vekha'asher asinu immekha rak-tov vanneshallechakha beshalom attah attah berukh YHVH.
verses.study_note: Abimelech requests a non-aggression covenant and concludes with the most remarkable statement in the chapter: 'thou art now the blessed of the LORD' (attah berukh YHVH). The Philistine king pronounces the blessing that God declared in vv.3-4. This echoes Genesis 12:3 — those who bless Abraham will be blessed. Abimelech cannot bless Isaac with the covenant blessing, but he can acknowledge it. The nations see and name the covenant blessing even when they cannot share it. 'Thou art... blessed of the LORD' becomes the theological climax of the chapter.

verses.kjv_text: And he made them a feast, and they did eat and drink.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַשׂ לָהֶם מִשְׁתֶּה וַיֹּאכְלוּ וַיִּשְׁתּוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'as lahem mishteh vayyokhelu vayyishtu.
verses.study_note: The covenant is sealed with a feast — the same pattern as Abraham's covenant meal with Abimelech (Gen 21:27) and the covenant meals throughout the Pentateuch (Exod 24:11). Covenant without table fellowship is incomplete in the Hebrew worldview. To eat and drink together is to enter shared life. The Lord's Table in the New Testament draws on this deep covenant pattern: the shared meal is the sign and seal of covenant relationship.

verses.kjv_text: And they rose up betimes in the morning, and sware one to another: and Isaac sent them away, and they departed from him in peace.
verses.original_text: וַיַּשְׁכִּימוּ בַבֹּקֶר וַיִּשָּׁבְעוּ אִישׁ לְאָחִיו וַיְשַׁלְּחֵם יִצְחָק וַיֵּלְכוּ מֵאִתּוֹ בְּשָׁלוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashkimu babboker vayyishaev'u ish le'achiv vayyeshallechem Yitschak vayyelechu me'itto beshalom.
verses.study_note: The formal covenant oath (vayyishav'u — from 'shava,' to swear by seven, linked to the number of completion) is exchanged the morning after the feast — a pattern of deliberate, morning-time commitment (cf. Gen 22:3, Isaac's own binding). They depart 'in peace' (beshalom). Shalom — wholeness, covenant completeness, the absence of strife — is the fruit of Isaac's patient, non-retaliatory journey from Esek through Sitnah to Rehoboth. Peacemaking is not passivity; it is persevering trust in God's provision until the opponent asks for peace.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass the same day, that Isaac’s servants came, and told him concerning the well which they had digged, and said unto him, We have found water.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא וַיָּבֹאוּ עַבְדֵי יִצְחָק וַיַּגִּדוּ לוֹ עַל־אֹדוֹת הַבְּאֵר אֲשֶׁר חָפָרוּ וַיֹּאמְרוּ לוֹ מָצָאנוּ מָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi bayyom hahu vayyavo'u avdei Yitschak vayyaggidu lo al-odot habbe'er asher chafaru vayyomeru lo matzanu mayim.
verses.study_note: The same day the covenant with Abimelech is ratified, Isaac's servants find water. Providence coordinates blessings: the covenant of peace and the discovery of water arrive simultaneously. 'We have found water' (matzanu mayim) — an announcement of life-supply. In a land of famine where Philistines stopped up wells, this is nothing less than divine provision completing the narrative. The wells that enemies stole are replaced; what opposition took, God restores — and more.

verses.kjv_text: And he called it Shebah: therefore the name of the city is Beersheba unto this day.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא אֹתָהּ שִׁבְעָה עַל־כֵּן שֵׁם־הָעִיר בְּאֵר שֶׁבַע עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra otah Shivah al-ken shem-ha'ir Be'er Shava ad hayyom hazzeh.
verses.study_note: The well is named Shebah — from 'sheva' (seven/oath). Beer-sheba can thus mean either 'Well of the Seven' (seven lambs of Abraham's covenant, Gen 21:28-31) or 'Well of the Oath.' Both meanings apply: the oath is now doubly confirmed — Abraham's covenant with Abimelech (Gen 21) and now Isaac's covenant with Abimelech (Gen 26). Beer-sheba becomes the southern boundary of the promised land ('from Dan to Beer-sheba' — Judg 20:1), a landmark of covenant geography. The narrator's 'unto this day' anchors the narrative in lived history.

verses.kjv_text: And Esau was forty years old when he took to wife Judith the daughter of Beeri the Hittite, and Bashemath the daughter of Elon the Hittite:
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי עֵשָׂו בֶּן־אַרְבָּעִים שָׁנָה וַיִּקַּח אִשָּׁה אֶת־יְהוּדִית בַּת־בְּאֵרִי הַחִתִּי וְאֶת־בָּשְׂמַת בַּת־אֵילֹן הַחִתִּי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi Esav ben-arba'im shanah vayyikkach ishah et-Yehudit bat-Be'eri haHitti ve'et-Basmat bat-Elon haHitti.
verses.study_note: The final two verses are a narrative thunderclap. Just as Isaac's covenant with Abimelech and the discovery of water at Beer-sheba have reached their climax, the narrator introduces Esau's marriages. Forty years old — the same age as Isaac at his own marriage (Gen 25:20) — but the parallel is broken: Isaac married Rebekah, a daughter of the covenant family (Gen 24); Esau marries two Hittite women. The Hittites are descendants of Canaan (Gen 10:15), the explicitly cursed line (Gen 9:25). Esau chooses wives from outside the covenant without seeking his parents' counsel — a Canaan-ward movement that foreshadows the great Jacob narrative to come.

verses.kjv_text: Which were a grief of mind unto Isaac and to Rebekah.
verses.original_text: וַתִּהְיֶיןָ מֹרַת רוּחַ לְיִצְחָק וּלְרִבְקָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vattihyeyna morat ruach leYitschak ulRevikah.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes not with triumph but with sorrow. 'A grief of mind' (morat ruach) — literally 'a bitterness of spirit.' The same word 'mar' (bitter) as Naomi's cry 'call me Mara' (Ruth 1:20) and Israel's bitter waters at Marah (Exod 15:23). Esau's Hittite wives are a 'bitterness of spirit' to Isaac and Rebekah — not merely a social preference but a theological grief. The covenant family understood that the covenant seed required covenant marriage. This one verse sets in motion the entire Jacob narrative: Rebekah will manipulate the blessing to Jacob (ch.27), partly because she has seen what Esau's choices portend for the covenant line. The shadow falls at the very moment of Beer-sheba's triumph.
verses.doctrinal_note: The grief of Isaac and Rebekah over Esau's marriages is not mere parental preference but covenant concern. Scripture consistently connects marriage to covenant continuity (Mal 2:15 — 'that he might seek a godly seed'). Esau 'despised his birthright' (Gen 25:34) and his choice of wives demonstrates the same contempt for covenant inheritance. Hebrews 12:16-17 calls Esau a 'profane person' — one who treats sacred things as ordinary. The grief of the covenant parents is the grief of God over misplaced inheritance.

meta.overview: Genesis 26 is the only chapter in Scripture devoted exclusively to Isaac — here he stands alone, not in Abraham's shadow, not yet overshadowed by Jacob. A new famine strikes the land, echoing Abraham's trial (Gen 12), and Isaac must navigate the same pressures his father faced: foreign territory, a beautiful wife, and the temptation to survive by deception rather than trust. God appears to Isaac and explicitly prohibits the Egyptian detour Abraham made — Isaac must stay in Canaan and trust the covenant. The chapter's heart is the covenant reaffirmation (vv.2-5), which ties Isaac's blessing to Abraham's obedience. Isaac then re-digs his father's stopped wells — Esek (contention), Sitnah (enmity), and finally Rehoboth (wide places) — a geographic and spiritual journey from strife to rest. At Beer-sheba, God appears again, and even the Philistines are compelled to acknowledge the LORD's hand upon Isaac. The chapter closes with the troubling marriage of Esau to Hittite women — a shadow falling over the covenant line as the patriarchal narrative prepares for its next crisis.
meta.theme: Covenant Continuity, Faithful Perseverance & the Journey from Strife to Rest
meta.keyWordMeaning: Well — the chapter's central symbol. Isaac's wells represent covenant claims, divine provision, and the living water of salvation. Each well is both a practical necessity and a theological statement about who owns the land and who provides life.
outline: The Famine and God's Command to Stay (vv.1-6): Covenant renewed to Isaac in Gerar
The Deception of Abimelech (vv.7-11): Isaac's fear and Abimelech's rebuke
Isaac's Prosperity and Philistine Envy (vv.12-16): The hundredfold harvest
The Wells Disputed and Re-dug (vv.17-22): Esek, Sitnah, and Rehoboth
God Appears at Beer-sheba (vv.23-25): Covenant reaffirmed, altar built
The Covenant with Abimelech (vv.26-33): Philistines acknowledge God's blessing
Esau's Hittite Wives (vv.34-35): A grief of mind to Isaac and Rebekah

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that when Isaac was old, and his eyes were dim, so that he could not see, he called Esau his eldest son, and said unto him, My son: and he said unto him, Behold, here am I.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כִּי־זָקֵן יִצְחָק וַתִּכְהֶיןָ עֵינָיו מֵרְאֹת וַיִּקְרָא אֶת־עֵשָׂו בְּנוֹ הַגָּדֹל וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו בְּנִי וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו הִנֵּנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi ki-zaken Yitzhak vattikheina einav mere'ot vayikra et-Esav beno hagadol vayomer elav beni vayomer elav hinneni.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with a narrative of physical decline that becomes the occasion for spiritual crisis. 'His eyes were dim' (vattikheina einav) — the same Hebrew root used of Eli's failing sight (1 Sam 3:2) and of Ahijah the prophet (1 Kings 14:4). Isaac's blindness is the physical hinge upon which the entire drama turns. Isaac's assumption that death is near shapes his urgency — yet he will live for decades more (Gen 35:27-29). The summons of 'my son' (beni) and the response 'hinneni' ('here am I') is the same formula used in moments of divine call (Gen 22:1, 7, 11; Ex 3:4). The ordinary word becomes charged with covenantal weight.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Behold now, I am old, I know not the day of my death:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר הִנֵּה־נָא זָקַנְתִּי לֹא יָדַעְתִּי יוֹם מוֹתִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer hinneh-na zakanti lo yada'ti yom moti.
verses.study_note: Isaac's confession of mortality drives the urgency. 'I know not the day of my death' (lo yada'ti yom moti) — a statement that is humanly humble but spiritually ironic: Isaac will live at least another twenty years (Gen 35:28). His urgency is premature, which will contribute to the crisis. The acknowledgment that he does not know the day of death reflects wisdom (cf. Eccl 9:12; Matt 24:36) — but here it becomes the occasion for acting apart from the divine oracle. He knows God's word from Gen 25:23 but appears to act against it.

verses.kjv_text: Now therefore take, I pray thee, thy weapons, thy quiver and thy bow, and go out to the field, and take me some venison;
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה שָׂא־נָא כֵלֶיךָ תֶּלְיְךָ וְקַשְׁתֶּךָ וְצֵא הַשָּׂדֶה וְצוּדָה לִּי צָיִד׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah sa-na kheileikha telyekha vekashetekha vetze hassadeh vetzudah li tzayid.
verses.study_note: Isaac's request is specifically tailored to Esau's identity: the hunter, the man of the field (25:27). 'Thy weapons, thy quiver and thy bow' — the tools of Esau's vocation become the instruments of the approaching blessing. The meal preceding the blessing follows ancient Near Eastern custom: a solemn feast ratified legal and covenantal transactions. Isaac is essentially calling for a covenant meal to precede the oral testament. The word 'tzayid' (venison/game) becomes a recurring motif (vv.3,5,7,19,25,31) — the physical food and the spiritual blessing are intertwined.

verses.kjv_text: And make me savoury meat, such as I love, and bring it to me, that I may eat; that my soul may bless thee before I die.
verses.original_text: וַעֲשֵׂה־לִי מַטְעַמִּים כַּאֲשֶׁר אָהַבְתִּי וְהָבִיאָה לִּי וְאֹכֵלָה בַּעֲבוּר תְּבָרֶכְךָ נַפְשִׁי בְּטֶרֶם אָמוּת׃
verses.transliteration: Va'aseh-li mat'ammim ka'asher ahavti veha vi'ah li ve'okhelah ba'avur tevarekha nafshi beterem amut.
verses.study_note: 'That my soul may bless thee' (tevarekha nafshi) — the blessing is not merely spoken with the lips but emanates from the whole person, the nephesh. This is a total-self act of covenant transfer. 'Before I die' (beterem amut) — the deathbed blessing carries irrevocable legal force in the patriarchal world, akin to a final will and testament. The crucial theological problem: Isaac is intentionally planning to bestow the Abrahamic covenant blessing on Esau, in direct contradiction to the divine oracle of Genesis 25:23 ('the elder shall serve the younger'). Parental favoritism (25:28) is about to collide with divine purpose.

verses.kjv_text: And Rebekah heard when Isaac spake to Esau his son. And Esau went to the field to hunt for venison, and to bring it.
verses.original_text: וְרִבְקָה שֹׁמַעַת בְּדַבֵּר יִצְחָק אֶל־עֵשָׂו בְּנוֹ וַיֵּלֶךְ עֵשָׂו הַשָּׂדֶה לָצוּד צַיִד לְהָבִיא׃
verses.transliteration: VeRivkah shoma'at bedabber Yitzhak el-Esav beno vayyeikh Esav hassadeh latzud tzayid lehavvi.
verses.study_note: The narrative hinge: 'Rebekah heard' (shoma'at — a participle indicating she was actively listening). This is not accidental eavesdropping but providential positioning. Rebekah had received the divine oracle directly (25:22-23) — she knows God's purpose for Jacob. She is now the instrument by which that purpose advances, though by deeply flawed means. The narrator gives no moral verdict — the tension is left for the reader to hold. Esau's departure to the field mirrors his identity (25:27), setting the window of opportunity.

verses.kjv_text: And Rebekah spake unto Jacob her son, saying, Behold, I heard thy father speak unto Esau thy brother, saying,
verses.original_text: וְרִבְקָה אָמְרָה אֶל־יַעֲקֹב בְּנָהּ לֵאמֹר הִנֵּה שָׁמַעְתִּי אֶת־אָבִיךָ מְדַבֵּר אֶל־עֵשָׂו אָחִיךָ לֵאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: VeRivkah amerah el-Ya'akov bena lemor hinneh shama'ti et-avikha medabber el-Esav achikha lemor.
verses.study_note: Rebekah approaches Jacob her son — the narrator's formulation 'Jacob her son' (bena) matches 'Esau his son' (beno) in v.5, subtly reinforcing the parental division first noted in 25:28. The divine oracle is not mentioned — Rebekah does not say 'God told me' but rather 'I heard.' She acts on information, not explicit divine command. This is characteristic of faith mixed with human strategy — she believes God's purpose but reaches for human means to accomplish it.

verses.kjv_text: Bring me venison, and make me savoury meat, that I may eat, and bless thee before the LORD before my death.
verses.original_text: הָבִיאָה לִּי צַיִד וַעֲשֵׂה־לִי מַטְעַמִּים וְאֹכֵלָה וַאֲבָרֶכְכָה לִפְנֵי יְהוָה לִפְנֵי מוֹתִי׃
verses.transliteration: Havi'ah li tzayid va'aseh-li mat'ammim ve'okhelah va'avarekkhah lifnei YHVH lifnei moti.
verses.study_note: Rebekah quotes Isaac but with a crucial addition: 'before the LORD' (lifnei YHVH). Interestingly, Isaac himself did not use this phrase in his original charge to Esau (v.4). Rebekah either adds this from her own understanding of the covenantal stakes, or she is paraphrasing to emphasize the divine dimension to Jacob. The blessing 'before the LORD' underscores that this is not a mere family ritual but a covenantal act with divine sanction — making the impending deception all the more weighty and all the more remarkable that God honors it.

verses.kjv_text: Now therefore, my son, obey my voice according to that which I command thee.
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה בְנִי שְׁמַע בְּקֹלִי לַאֲשֶׁר אֲנִי מְצַוָּה אֹתָךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah veni shema bekholi la'asher ani metzavveh otak.
verses.study_note: 'Obey my voice' (shema bekholi) uses the same Hebrew construction as God's charge to Abraham: 'obey my voice' (Gen 22:18; 26:5). Rebekah positions her maternal command in the grammar of divine instruction — perhaps unconsciously. The irony is that she is asking Jacob to obey her voice in order to receive the blessing that God has already promised — a mixture of faith and flesh that is characteristic of all patriarchal narratives. The tension is never fully resolved in the text.

verses.kjv_text: Go now to the flock, and fetch me from thence two good kids of the goats; and I will make them savoury meat for thy father, such as he loveth:
verses.original_text: לֶךְ־נָא אֶל־הַצֹּאן וְקַח־לִי מִשָּׁם שְׁנֵי גְּדָיֵי עִזִּים טֹבִים וְאֶעֱשֶׂה אֹתָם מַטְעַמִּים לְאָבִיךָ כַּאֲשֶׁר אָהֵב׃
verses.transliteration: Lekh-na el-hatzon vekach-li misham shenei gedayei izzim tovim ve'e'eseh otam mat'ammim le'avikha ka'asher ahev.
verses.study_note: 'Two good kids of the goats' (shenei gedayei izzim) — the substitution of domesticated goat for wild venison is the practical deception. The same Hebrew word 'gedayim' (goat kids) will later appear in Samson's riddle (Judges 14) and as the animal used in Judah's sin (Gen 38:17). Typologically, the slaying of the goat kids to secure the blessing for Jacob foreshadows the substitutionary sacrifice — an innocent animal dies so that the undeserving recipient may receive the covenantal favor.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt bring it to thy father, that he may eat, and that he may bless thee before his death.
verses.original_text: וְהֵבֵאתָ לְאָבִיךָ וְאָכָל בַּעֲבֻר אֲשֶׁר יְבָרֶכְךָ לִפְנֵי מוֹתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Veheveta le'avikha ve'akhal ba'avur asher yevarekha lifnei moto.
verses.study_note: Rebekah's plan is stated plainly: bring the food so that your father may eat and bless you. The purpose is unambiguous. The word 'ba'avur' (so that, in order that) establishes clear intentionality — this is not an accident or impulse. The irony throughout is that Rebekah believes she is securing for Jacob what God has already promised. Her lack of trust in God's ability to accomplish His own purpose through legitimate means leads to decades of family fracture.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob said to Rebekah his mother, Behold, Esau my brother is a hairy man, and I am a smooth man:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב אֶל־רִבְקָה אִמּוֹ הֵן עֵשָׂו אָחִי אִישׁ שָׂעִר וְאָנֹכִי אִישׁ חָלָק׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Ya'akov el-Rivkah immo hen Esav achi ish sa'ir ve'anokhi ish chalak.
verses.study_note: Jacob's objection focuses entirely on the practical risk of detection — not on the moral question. 'A hairy man' (ish sa'ir) and 'a smooth man' (ish chalak) — these physical differences echo the description of their births (25:25) and characterize their fundamental natures. 'Sa'ir' (hairy) is also the word for 'goat' — the very animal that will cover Jacob's hands. 'Chalak' (smooth) later appears in a negative moral sense (Prov 5:3; 7:5; 26:28) for deceptive flattery. The word plays in the Hebrew are intricate. Jacob's concern is not 'Is this wrong?' but 'Will it work?' — a telling moral silence.

verses.kjv_text: My father peradventure will feel me, and I shall seem to him as a deceiver; and I shall bring a curse upon me, and not a blessing.
verses.original_text: אוּלַי יְמֻשֵּׁנִי אָבִי וְהָיִיתִי בְעֵינָיו כִּמְתַעְתֵּעַ וְהֵבֵאתִי עָלַי קְלָלָה וְלֹא בְרָכָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ulai yemusheni avi vehayiti ve'einav kimeta'te'a veheveti alai kelalah velo verakhah.
verses.study_note: Jacob's fear articulates the stakes with precision: 'I shall seem to him as a deceiver' (kimeta'te'a) — the word carries the sense of mocking, making sport of someone. More significantly, Jacob's concern is that he might 'bring a curse upon me' (kelalah) rather than a blessing (berakhah) — the precise antonyms of the covenant. The entire Abrahamic covenant was structured around blessing and curse (Gen 12:3; Deut 27-28). Jacob fears receiving the wrong side. His theological instincts are correct; his moral compass, however, is oriented toward outcomes rather than righteousness.

verses.kjv_text: And his mother said unto him, Upon me be thy curse, my son: only obey my voice, and go fetch me them.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר לוֹ אִמּוֹ עָלַי קִלְלָתְךָ בְּנִי אַךְ שְׁמַע בְּקֹלִי וְלֵךְ קַח־לִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomer lo immo alai killatekha beni akh shema bekholi velekh kach-li.
verses.study_note: Rebekah's response — 'Upon me be thy curse, my son' (alai killatekha beni) — is one of the most self-sacrificial statements in the patriarchal narratives. She takes upon herself the potential consequence of the deception. This voluntary assumption of curse-bearing is unconsciously typological: it echoes the language of substitutionary atonement. Just as Rebekah says 'upon me' (alai), the Suffering Servant bears what others deserve (Isa 53:6). The tragedy is that Rebekah will indeed bear the curse — she will never see her beloved son again after he flees (v.45).
verses.doctrinal_note: Rebekah's self-imprecation ('upon me be thy curse') is a shadow of substitutionary logic that reaches its fulfillment in Christ, who became 'a curse for us' (Gal 3:13). The form is right; the means are wrong. God's redemptive patterns appear even in the failures of His servants.

verses.kjv_text: And he went, and fetched, and brought them to his mother: and his mother made savoury meat, such as his father loved.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּלֶךְ וַיִּקַּח וַיָּבֵא לְאִמּוֹ וַתַּעַשׂ אִמּוֹ מַטְעַמִּים כַּאֲשֶׁר אָהֵב אָבִיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyeikh vayyikkach vayyave le'immo vatta'as immo mat'ammim ka'asher ahev aviv.
verses.study_note: Jacob obeys. Three rapid verbs (went, fetched, brought) indicate swift execution. The mother's culinary skill becomes the instrument of the plan. 'Such as his father loved' — the same phrase from 25:28 that first revealed Isaac's preferential love for Esau. Rebekah now weaponizes that knowledge. The detail underscores how intimate knowledge of a person can be used either in love or in manipulation.

verses.kjv_text: And Rebekah took goodly raiment of her eldest son Esau, which were with her in the house, and put them upon Jacob her younger son:
verses.original_text: וַתִּקַּח רִבְקָה אֶת־בִּגְדֵי עֵשָׂו בְּנָהּ הַגָּדֹל הַחֲמֻדֹת אֲשֶׁר אִתָּהּ בַּבָּיִת וַתַּלְבֵּשׁ אֶת־יַעֲקֹב בְּנָהּ הַקָּטָן׃
verses.transliteration: Vatikkach Rivkah et-bigdei Esav bena hagadol hachamudot asher ittah babbayit vattalbbesh et-Ya'akov bena hakkatan.
verses.study_note: Rebekah clothes Jacob in Esau's 'goodly raiment' (bigdei hachamudot — precious/desirable garments). The act of clothing is the central act of identity substitution. Jacob does not become Esau, but he presents himself in Esau's garments, smell, and feel. The profound typological dimension: the recipient of the blessing is clothed in another's identity in order to receive what was meant for that other person. In Romans 13:14 and Galatians 3:27, believers are said to 'put on Christ' — clothed in His righteousness to stand before the Father. The pattern is ancient.

verses.kjv_text: And she put the skins of the kids of the goats upon his hands, and upon the smooth of his neck:
verses.original_text: וְאֵת עֹרֹת גְּדָיֵי הָעִזִּים הִלְבִּישָׁה עַל־יָדָיו וְעַל חֶלְקַת צַוָּארָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et orot gedayei ha'izzim hilbbishah al-yadav ve'al chelkat tzavvarav.
verses.study_note: The skins of the slain goat kids are placed on Jacob's hands and neck — the most sensitive points for Isaac's tactile examination. This is the crux of the physical disguise. Theologically, the slain animal's skin covers Jacob's true identity, allowing him to receive the blessing intended for another. This pattern will repeat in Scripture: Joseph clothed in a coat of many colors, Aaron robed in priestly garments, the High Priest on Yom Kippur sending away the scapegoat, and ultimately Christ's robe of righteousness covering the sinner before the Father. The innocent animal dies; the skin covers; the undeserving receives the blessing.

verses.kjv_text: And she gave the savoury meat and the bread, which she had prepared, into the hand of her son Jacob.
verses.original_text: וַתִּתֵּן אֶת־הַמַּטְעַמִּים וְאֶת־הַלֶּחֶם אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂתָה בְּיַד יַעֲקֹב בְּנָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattitten et-hammat'ammim ve'et-hallechem asher asetah beyad Ya'akov bena.
verses.study_note: The meal prepared — food plus bread. The complete covenant feast is now in Jacob's hands. 'Into the hand of' (beyad) — a transfer of agency. Jacob is now the instrument of a plan conceived and executed by his mother. His moral culpability is real but shared. The narrator's sympathy for Jacob is implicit in the structure: he is being used, dressed, equipped, and sent. He does not design the deception — he executes it.

verses.kjv_text: And he came unto his father, and said, My father: and he said, Here am I; who art thou, my son?
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא אֶל־אָבִיו וַיֹּאמֶר אָבִי וַיֹּאמֶר הִנֶּנִּי מִי אַתָּה בְּנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo el-aviv vayomer avi vayomer hinneni mi attah beni.
verses.study_note: The encounter begins. Jacob says simply 'My father' (avi); Isaac responds 'Here am I' (hinneni) — but immediately asks the pivotal question: 'Who art thou, my son?' (mi attah beni). The question is asked four times in various forms in this chapter (vv.18, 21, 24, 32) — a literary device emphasizing that identity is the central issue. 'Who are you?' is the question of the entire narrative. Isaac's blindness means he cannot see, but his other senses remain acute — he will interrogate by touch, smell, and voice.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob said unto his father, I am Esau thy first born; I have done according as thou badest me: arise, I pray thee, sit and eat of my venison, that thy soul may bless me.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב אֶל־אָבִיו אָנֹכִי עֵשָׂו בְּכֹרֶךָ עָשִׂיתִי כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְתָּ אֵלַי קוּם־נָא שְׁבָה וְאָכְלָה מִצֵּידִי בַּעֲבוּר תְּבָרְכַנִּי נַפְשֶׁךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Ya'akov el-aviv anokhi Esav bekhorekha asiti ka'asher dibbarta elai kum-na shevah ve'okhlah mitzeidi ba'avur tevarekanni nafshekha.
verses.study_note: The lie is explicit: 'I am Esau thy firstborn' (anokhi Esav bekhorekha) — one of the most direct false statements in the patriarchal narratives. The word 'anokhi' (I am) is emphatic, the same form used in God's self-revelation at Sinai ('Anokhi YHVH' — I am the LORD, Ex 20:2). Jacob invokes the most emphatic form of self-assertion to state a falsehood. Yet the narrative will ultimately turn this lie into a truth — Jacob IS the chosen heir; God had decreed it. The means are wrong; the end is divinely ordained. This is the theological paradox Paul explores in Romans 9.
verses.doctrinal_note: The doctrine of divine sovereignty and human responsibility reaches its sharpest expression here. Jacob lies, yet God's purpose is accomplished. This does not justify the lie — the consequences (exile, family fracture) demonstrate that sin always has costs. But it does demonstrate that God's sovereign election cannot be ultimately thwarted by human failure (Rom 9:11; 11:29).

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac said unto his son, How is it that thou hast found it so quickly, my son? And he said, Because the LORD thy God brought it to me.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יִצְחָק אֶל־בְּנוֹ מַה־זֶּה מִהַרְתָּ לִמְצֹא בְּנִי וַיֹּאמֶר כִּי הִקְרָה יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ לְפָנָי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yitzhak el-beno mah-zzeh maharta limtzo beni vayomer ki hikrah YHVH Eloheikha lefanai.
verses.study_note: Isaac's suspicion is aroused: 'How hast thou found it so quickly?' (maharta limtzo). The normal hunt would take hours. Jacob's answer is breathtaking in its audacity: 'Because the LORD thy God brought it to me' (ki hikrah YHVH Eloheikha lefanai). He invokes the divine name to support his deception. Yet the statement contains a deeper irony: it IS the LORD who has 'brought it to' Jacob — in the sense that God's sovereign purpose is indeed behind the blessing coming to Jacob. Jacob speaks a theological truth through a practical lie. Note: 'thy God' — Jacob does not yet say 'my God.' That will come at Bethel (28:21).

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac said unto Jacob, Come near, I pray thee, that I may feel thee, my son, whether thou be my very son Esau or not.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יִצְחָק אֶל־יַעֲקֹב גְּשָׁה־נָּא וַאֲמֻשְּׁךָ בְּנִי הַאַתָּה זֶה בְּנִי עֵשָׂו אִם־לֹא׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yitzhak el-Ya'akov geshah-na va'amushekha beni ha'attah zzeh beni Esav im-lo.
verses.study_note: Isaac calls for tactile verification. 'That I may feel thee' (va'amushekha) — the verb involves deliberate, careful handling. Isaac's four senses are engaged throughout this passage: voice (v.22), touch (v.22-23), smell (v.27), taste (v.25). The repetition of 'my son' (beni) is both tender and ironic — Isaac calls the deceiver by the word of intimacy. The question 'whether thou be my very son Esau or not' (ha'attah zzeh beni Esav im-lo) contains the word 'zzeh' (this very) — Isaac senses the anomaly.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob went near unto Isaac his father; and he felt him, and said, The voice is Jacob’s voice, but the hands are the hands of Esau.
verses.original_text: וַיִּגַּשׁ יַעֲקֹב אֶל־יִצְחָק אָבִיו וַיְמֻשֵּׁהוּ וַיֹּאמֶר הַקֹּל קוֹל יַעֲקֹב וְהַיָּדַיִם יְדֵי עֵשָׂו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiggash Ya'akov el-Yitzhak aviv vayemushehu vayomer hakkol kol Ya'akov vehayyadayim yedei Esav.
verses.study_note: One of the most celebrated lines in the Hebrew Bible for its literary artistry: 'The voice is the voice of Jacob, but the hands are the hands of Esau' (hakkol kol Ya'akov vehayyadayim yedei Esav). The chiastic rhythm in Hebrew (voice — Jacob; hands — Esau) captures Isaac's perplexity. His two senses give contradictory reports. This verse has become proverbial in rabbinic tradition. Theologically, the ambiguity serves the narrative: Isaac is undecided, weighing evidence. The deception succeeds not because it is flawless but because the stakes are so high that Isaac wants to believe.

verses.kjv_text: And he discerned him not, because his hands were hairy, as his brother Esau’s hands: so he blessed him.
verses.original_text: וְלֹא הִכִּירוֹ כִּי־הָיוּ יָדָיו כִּידֵי עֵשָׂו אָחִיו שְׂעִרֹת וַיְבָרְכֵהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Velo hikkiro ki-hayu yadav kidei Esav achiv se'irot vayevarekhehu.
verses.study_note: 'He discerned him not' (velo hikkiro) — Isaac's tactile examination failed to distinguish Jacob from Esau because the goat skins produced the expected texture. The critical word is 'se'irot' (hairy) — the same root as Esau's name and character. The goat skin made Jacob pass as Esau through the very characteristic that defined his brother. 'So he blessed him' (vayevarekhehu) — abrupt, immediate. Isaac resolves his doubt and proceeds. The blessing is about to be spoken; it cannot be taken back.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Art thou my very son Esau? And he said, I am.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אַתָּה זֶה בְּנִי עֵשָׂו וַיֹּאמֶר אָנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer attah zzeh beni Esav vayomer ani.
verses.study_note: Isaac asks one final time: 'Art thou my very son Esau?' — and Jacob simply says 'I am' (ani). The brevity of the answer is striking. Earlier he gave an elaborate response (v.19); now, perhaps emboldened by the physical verification having passed, he answers in one word. 'Ani' (I am) — the shorter, less emphatic form than 'anokhi' in v.19. Some commentators note that Jacob never actually says the name 'Esau' in his brief answer — the deception is technically narrower than it appears. The distinction is subtle and may be irrelevant; the intention is clear.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Bring it near to me, and I will eat of my son’s venison, that my soul may bless thee. And he brought it near to him, and he did eat: and he brought him wine, and he drank.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר הַגִּשָׁה לִּי וְאֹכְלָה מִצֵּיד בְּנִי לְמַעַן תְּבָרֶכְךָ נַפְשִׁי וַיַּגֶּשׁ־לוֹ וַיֹּאכַל וַיָּבֵא לוֹ יַיִן וַיֵּשְׁתְּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer haggishshah li ve'okhelah mitzeid beni lema'an tevarekha nafshi vayyaggesh-lo vayyokhal vayyave lo yayin vayeisht.
verses.study_note: The covenant meal is consumed — food and wine. This is the moment the covenant feast seals the transaction. In the ancient world, the shared meal ratified agreements. Isaac eats and drinks, completing the ceremonial framework for the blessing. 'That my soul may bless thee' (lema'an tevarekha nafshi) — the same phrase from v.4, now about to be fulfilled. The meal preceding the blessing prefigures the covenant meal structure throughout Scripture: Abraham's meal with Melchizedek (14:18), the Passover Seder, and ultimately the Lord's Supper.

verses.kjv_text: And his father Isaac said unto him, Come near now, and kiss me, my son.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו יִצְחָק אָבִיו גְּשָׁה־נָּא וּשֲׁקָה־לִּי בְּנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer elav Yitzhak aviv geshah-na ushakah-li beni.
verses.study_note: Isaac calls for a kiss — the gesture of intimate familial affection and covenantal sealing. The kiss in the ancient Near East confirmed relationship and agreement. Laban will kiss Jacob at departure (29:13); Jacob will kiss his sons (50:1). The paternal kiss before the blessing is both tender and devastating: Isaac kisses the wrong son with complete love, sealing a blessing that cannot be recalled. 'My son' (beni) — the endearment rings through the deception without irony or awareness on Isaac's part.

verses.kjv_text: And he came near, and kissed him: and he smelled the smell of his raiment, and blessed him, and said, See, the smell of my son is as the smell of a field which the LORD hath blessed:
verses.original_text: וַיִּגַּשׁ וַיִּשַּׁק־לוֹ וַיָּרַח אֶת־רֵיחַ בְּגָדָיו וַיְבָרְכֵהוּ וַיֹּאמֶר רְאֵה רֵיחַ בְּנִי כְּרֵיחַ שָׂדֶה אֲשֶׁר בֵּרְכוֹ יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiggash vayyishshak-lo vayyarach et-reach begadav vayevarekhehu vayomer re'eh reach beni kereach sadeh asher berakhо YHVH.
verses.study_note: The fourth sense engaged: smell. The garments of Esau — a hunter's field-worn clothes — carry the scent of the outdoors that Isaac associates with his beloved firstborn. The smell triggers the blessing. 'The smell of my son is as the smell of a field which the LORD hath blessed' — Isaac involuntarily invokes divine blessing in his perception. The field he smells is a blessed field; the clothing he smells is Esau's; the son he is blessing is Jacob. Every element of the scene is simultaneously true and false. The narrators' irony is at its peak.
verses.doctrinal_note: Isaac blesses Jacob 'in faith' — Hebrews 11:20 states this explicitly. Though the circumstances involve deception, the blessing itself was a genuine act of faith. The author of Hebrews does not comment on the deception but on the faith-character of the blessing. This suggests God can accomplish His sovereign purposes even through the broken, sinful means of His people — without endorsing the sin.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore God give thee of the dew of heaven, and the fatness of the earth, and plenty of corn and wine:
verses.original_text: וְיִתֶּן־לְךָ הָאֱלֹהִים מִטַּל הַשָּׁמַיִם וּמִשְׁמַנֵּי הָאָרֶץ וְרֹב דָּגָן וְתִירֹשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Veyitten-lekha ha'Elohim mittal hashamayim umishmannei ha'aretz verob dagan vetirosh.
verses.study_note: The blessing begins. It is structured in two movements: material blessing (vv.28) and relational/covenantal blessing (v.29). The material dimension: 'dew of heaven' (tal hashamayim) — the essential moisture for agriculture in a semi-arid land; 'fatness of the earth' (mishmannei ha'aretz) — rich, fertile soil; 'plenty of corn and wine' (rob dagan vetirosh) — the staples of abundance. This language echoes the covenant blessings of Deuteronomy 7:13 and 11:14. The blessing is agricultural and creational — dominion over the fruitfulness of the earth.
verses.doctrinal_note: The material dimensions of the patriarchal blessing are types of the spiritual blessings of the New Covenant. Paul describes believers as blessed 'with all spiritual blessings in heavenly places in Christ' (Eph 1:3) — the heavenly dew and fatness that are now available through the Second Adam, who is the ultimate recipient of all blessing.

verses.kjv_text: Let people serve thee, and nations bow down to thee: be lord over thy brethren, and let thy mother’s sons bow down to thee: cursed be every one that curseth thee, and blessed be he that blesseth thee.
verses.original_text: יַעַבְדוּךָ עַמִּים וְיִשְׁתַּחֲווּ לְךָ לְאֻמִּים הֱוֵה גְבִיר לְאַחֶיךָ וְיִשְׁתַּחֲווּ לְךָ בְּנֵי אִמֶּךָ אֹרְרֶיךָ אָרוּר וּמְבָרְכֶיךָ בָּרוּךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Ya'avdukha ammim veyishtachavu lekha le'ummim heveh gevir le'achekha veyishtachavu lekha benei immekha orereikha arur umevarkheikha barukh.
verses.study_note: The covenantal dimension of the blessing: dominion over peoples and nations, lordship over brethren, and the Abrahamic protective formula. 'Cursed be every one that curseth thee, and blessed be he that blesseth thee' (orereikha arur umevarkheikha barukh) — this is nearly verbatim from God's original covenant with Abraham in Genesis 12:3. Isaac is transferring the Abrahamic blessing in its fullness to Jacob. 'Be lord over thy brethren' (heveh gevir le'achekha) — the reversal of birth order; the younger becomes the lord. This is the theological heart of the chapter.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Abrahamic blessing formula (cursed/blessed — Gen 12:3) now passes formally to Jacob. This is the covenant chain: Abraham → Isaac → Jacob → the twelve tribes → the nation → the Messiah. Paul teaches in Galatians 3:14 that 'the blessing of Abraham' comes to the Gentiles through Christ Jesus. Jacob receives what is ultimately the promise of the Messianic line. The deception does not interrupt the chain — God's sovereign purpose moves through it.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, as soon as Isaac had made an end of blessing Jacob, and Jacob was yet scarce gone out from the presence of Isaac his father, that Esau his brother came in from his hunting.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כַּאֲשֶׁר כִּלָּה יִצְחָק לְבָרֵךְ אֶת־יַעֲקֹב וַיְהִי אַךְ יָצֹא יָצָא יַעֲקֹב מֵאֵת פְּנֵי יִצְחָק אָבִיו וְעֵשָׂו אָחִיו בָּא מִצֵּידוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi ka'asher killah Yitzhak levarekh et-Ya'akov vayehi akh yatzo yatza Ya'akov me'et penei Yitzhak aviv ve'Esav achiv ba mitzeido.
verses.study_note: The narrative timing is cinematographic: 'Jacob was yet scarce gone out' (akh yatzo yatza — barely, barely had he gone out) when Esau arrives. The emphatic infinitive absolute (yatzo yatza) underscores the razor-thin margin. Providence arranges the timing — had Esau arrived moments earlier, the plan collapses. The timing itself is a silent argument for divine sovereignty: the narrow window closes exactly when Jacob exits. The scene is set for one of the most emotionally devastating moments in the patriarchal narrative.

verses.kjv_text: And he also had made savoury meat, and brought it unto his father, and said unto his father, Let my father arise, and eat of his son’s venison, that thy soul may bless me.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַשׂ גַּם־הוּא מַטְעַמִּים וַיָּבֵא לְאָבִיו וַיֹּאמֶר לְאָבִיו יָקֻם אָבִי וְיֹאכַל מִצֵּיד בְּנוֹ בַּעֲבֻר תְּבָרְכַנִּי נַפְשֶׁךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'as gam-hu mat'ammim vayyave le'aviv vayomer le'aviv yakum avi veyokhal mitzeid beno ba'avur tevarekanni nafshekha.
verses.study_note: Esau arrives with the real venison and speaks the authentic version of the words Jacob counterfeited: 'Let my father arise, and eat of his son's venison' — he says 'his son' (beno), claiming his identity with confidence. 'That thy soul may bless me' — identical to Jacob's words (v.19). The parallel language highlights how perfectly Jacob imitated the expected formula. Esau has done exactly what was asked of him, faithfully and legitimately. The legitimate claim arrives too late.

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac his father said unto him, Who art thou? And he said, I am thy son, thy firstborn Esau.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ יִצְחָק אָבִיו מִי־אָתָּה וַיֹּאמֶר אֲנִי בִּנְךָ בְכֹרְךָ עֵשָׂו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer lo Yitzhak aviv mi-attah vayomer ani binkha bekhorekha Esav.
verses.study_note: 'Who art thou?' — the question of identity, asked for the fourth and final time in this chapter (vv.18, 21, 24, 32). Now it is the true Esau who must answer it. His answer is full and precise: 'I am thy son, thy firstborn Esau' (ani binkha bekhorekha Esav) — he gives the name Jacob withheld (v.24). He identifies himself completely. The irony is total: Esau's authentic self-presentation comes too late. The blessing has already established Jacob's identity in the covenantal sphere.

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac trembled very exceedingly, and said, Who? where is he that hath taken venison, and brought it me, and I have eaten of all before thou camest, and have blessed him? yea, and he shall be blessed.
verses.original_text: וַיֶּחֱרַד יִצְחָק חֲרָדָה גְּדֹלָה עַד־מְאֹד וַיֹּאמֶר מִי אֵפוֹא הוּא הַצָּד צַיִד וַיָּבֵא לִי וָאֹכַל מִכֹּל בְּטֶרֶם תָּבוֹא וָאֲבָרְכֵהוּ גַּם־בָּרוּךְ יִהְיֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyecherad Yitzhak charadah gedolah ad-me'od vayomer mi efo hu hatzad tzayid vayyave li va'okhal mikkol beterem tavo va'avarekhehu gam-barukh yihyeh.
verses.study_note: Isaac 'trembled very exceedingly' (charadah gedolah ad-me'od) — an intensified triple emphasis: trembling (charadah), great (gedolah), to a very great degree (ad-me'od). This is not merely shock but a recognition that something supernatural has occurred. The word 'charad' is used of the fear that attends divine encounters (Exod 19:16; 1 Sam 4:13). Isaac suddenly understands that God has overruled his intention. 'He shall be blessed' (gam-barukh yihyeh) — Isaac does not revoke the blessing but confirms it. His trembling is the recognition that divine purpose has moved through the deception. The blessing stands.
verses.doctrinal_note: Isaac's declaration 'he shall be blessed' (gam-barukh yihyeh) after recognizing the deception is the theological crux. He does not attempt reversal. He intuitively understands that the blessing, once given under divine sanction, has passed beyond his authority to revoke. This is confirmed in Hebrews 11:20, which attributes the blessing 'by faith.' The irrevocability of the divine blessing reflects the irrevocability of God's calling: 'The gifts and calling of God are without repentance' (Rom 11:29).

verses.kjv_text: And when Esau heard the words of his father, he cried with a great and exceeding bitter cry, and said unto his father, Bless me, even me also, O my father.
verses.original_text: כִּשְׁמֹעַ עֵשָׂו אֶת־דִּבְרֵי אָבִיו וַיִּצְעַק צְעָקָה גְּדֹלָה וּמָרָה עַד־מְאֹד וַיֹּאמֶר לְאָבִיו בָּרְכֵנִי גַם־אָנִי אָבִי׃
verses.transliteration: Kishmoa Esav et-divrei aviv vayyitz'ak tze'akah gedolah ummarah ad-me'od vayomer le'aviv barekeni gam-ani avi.
verses.study_note: Esau's cry is one of the most emotionally devastating moments in all of Scripture. 'He cried with a great and exceeding bitter cry' (tze'akah gedolah ummarah ad-me'od) — the same triple intensification as Isaac's trembling (v.33), now in grief and anguish. Hebrews 12:17 explicitly references this moment. The word 'tze'akah' is the cry of the oppressed, the cry of slaves in Egypt (Exod 2:23), the cry of the destitute. Esau uses this word — the language of one who has been wronged. 'Bless me, even me also' (barekeni gam-ani) — the desperate redundancy ('even me also, even me') conveys the depth of his grief.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Thy brother came with subtilty, and hath taken away thy blessing.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר בָּא אָחִיךָ בְּמִרְמָה וַיִּקַּח בִּרְכָתֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer ba achikha bemirmah vayyikkach birkatekha.
verses.study_note: Isaac's verdict: 'with subtilty' (bemirmah) — deceit, guile, treachery. This is a legal and moral indictment. 'Mirmah' is the word used for deceptive scales (Amos 8:5), false prophets (Jer 14:14), and later for Laban's deception of Jacob in return (Gen 31:26). Jacob himself will be deceived by Laban, and later by his own sons with Joseph's coat — measure for measure, the deception reaps its harvest in his own family. Isaac names the sin plainly. Yet he does not revoke the blessing.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Is not he rightly named Jacob? for he hath supplanted me these two times: he took away my birthright; and, behold, now he hath taken away my blessing. And he said, Hast thou not reserved a blessing for me?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר הֲכִי קָרָא שְׁמוֹ יַעֲקֹב וַיַּעְקְבֵנִי זֶה פַעֲמַיִם אֶת־בְּכֹרָתִי לָקָח וְהִנֵּה עַתָּה לָקַח בִּרְכָתִי וַיֹּאמֶר הֲלֹא־אָצַלְתָּ לִּי בְּרָכָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer haki kara shemo Ya'akov vayyak'veni zzeh fa'amayim et-bekhorati lakach vehineh attah lakach birkhati vayomer halo-atzalta li berakhah.
verses.study_note: Esau's bitter wordplay on Jacob's name: 'Is not he rightly named Jacob?' (haki kara shemo Ya'akov). 'Ya'akov' means 'heel-catcher' (from akev, heel — Gen 25:26) and carries the secondary meaning of 'one who supplants, who trips up, who deceives.' 'He hath supplanted me' (vayyak'veni) — the verb formed from Jacob's own name, meaning he has acted out his name against me. This is the only time in Scripture the name Ya'akov is given a negative interpretation. Esau sees his brother's name as a destiny of deception. He also explicitly links the two events: the birthright and the blessing, showing he understood the connection Jacob and Rebekah had long since acted upon.

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac answered and said unto Esau, Behold, I have made him thy lord, and all his brethren have I given to him for servants; and with corn and wine have I sustained him: and what shall I do now unto thee, my son?
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַן יִצְחָק וַיֹּאמֶר לְעֵשָׂו הֵן גְּבִיר שַׂמְתִּיו לָךְ וְאֶת־כָּל־אֶחָיו נָתַתִּי לוֹ לַעֲבָדִים וְדָגָן וְתִירֹשׁ סְמַכְתִּיו וּלְכָה אֵפוֹא מָה אֶעֱשֶׂה בְּנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'an Yitzhak vayomer le'Esav hen gevir samtiv lakh ve'et-kol-echav natatti lo la'avadim vedagan vetirosh semakhtiv ulkha efo mah e'eseh beni.
verses.study_note: Isaac confirms the irrevocability of the blessing in devastating detail: 'I have made him thy lord' — the very language of Gen 25:23; 'all his brethren I have given to him for servants' — the relational hierarchy is set; 'with corn and wine have I sustained him' — the material blessing is transferred. Then Isaac's anguished question: 'What shall I do now unto thee, my son?' (mah e'eseh beni). For all his power to bless, Isaac stands helpless before the finality of what God has accomplished through human agency. The blessing has created an ontological reality that surpasses Isaac's authority to alter.
verses.doctrinal_note: The irrevocability of the blessing demonstrates that God's covenantal decrees, once spoken and ratified, stand beyond human power to revoke. Numbers 23:20 expresses the same principle: 'I have received commandment to bless: and he hath blessed; and I cannot reverse it.' This immutability of divine blessing is the ground of the believer's security: 'The gifts and calling of God are without repentance' (Rom 11:29).

verses.kjv_text: And Esau said unto his father, Hast thou but one blessing, my father? bless me, even me also, O my father. And Esau lifted up his voice, and wept.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר עֵשָׂו אֶל־אָבִיו הַבְרָכָה אַחַת הִיא־לְךָ אָבִי בָּרְכֵנִי גַם־אָנִי אָבִי וַיִּשָּׂא עֵשָׂו קֹלוֹ וַיֵּבְךְּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Esav el-aviv haverakhah achat hi-lekha avi barekeni gam-ani avi vayyissa Esav kolo vayyevkk.
verses.study_note: Esau's second plea breaks into tears. 'Hast thou but one blessing, my father?' (haverakhah achat hi-lekha) — the question of a man who refuses to accept that the covenantal blessing is singular and has passed. He asks again with the same desperate redundancy: 'bless me, even me also, O my father' — three 'me/my father' words in one breath. Then: 'Esau lifted up his voice and wept' (vayyissa kolo vayyevk). The weeping of Esau is one of the great cries of Scripture. Hebrews 12:17 notes he 'found no place of repentance, though he sought it carefully with tears' — the tears were real but they were tears of loss, not of the repentance that leads to life.

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac his father answered and said unto him, Behold, thy dwelling shall be the fatness of the earth, and of the dew of heaven from above;
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַן יִצְחָק אָבִיו וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו הִנֵּה מִשְׁמַנֵּי הָאָרֶץ יִהְיֶה מוֹשָׁבֶךָ וּמִטַּל הַשָּׁמַיִם מֵעָל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'an Yitzhak aviv vayomer elav hinneh mishmannei ha'aretz yihyeh moshavekha umittal hashamayim me'al.
verses.study_note: Isaac gives Esau a secondary blessing — not the Abrahamic covenantal blessing but a territorial description. Note the subtle but significant grammatical difference in the Hebrew: Jacob's blessing used 'from' the dew of heaven (mittal hashamayim — Gen 27:28, indicating gift and provision); Esau's uses the same words but in a context that many interpreters read as a privative or distant form — the dew and fatness exist but in a harsher relationship. Esau's territory will be rugged and demanding rather than fruitful by covenant.

verses.kjv_text: And by thy sword shalt thou live, and shalt serve thy brother; and it shall come to pass when thou shalt have the dominion, that thou shalt break his yoke from off thy neck.
verses.original_text: וְעַל־חַרְבְּךָ תִחְיֶה וְאֶת־אָחִיךָ תַּעֲבֹד וְהָיָה כַּאֲשֶׁר תָּרִיד וּפָרַקְתָּ עֻלּוֹ מֵעַל צַוָּארֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'al-charbbekha tichyeh ve'et-achikha ta'avod vehayah ka'asher tarid ufrakta ullo me'al tzavvarekha.
verses.study_note: Esau's blessing is a prediction of his violent, independent character: 'by thy sword shalt thou live' — the warrior existence rather than the settled agricultural blessing of Jacob. 'Thou shalt serve thy brother' — confirms the Gen 25:23 oracle. Yet there is a conditional clause: 'when thou shalt have the dominion' (ka'asher tarid), 'thou shalt break his yoke.' This was fulfilled historically when Edom revolted from Judah's dominion (2 Kings 8:20-22; 2 Chron 21:8-10). The Edomites and Israel maintained a complex, antagonistic relationship throughout the monarchy period.
verses.doctrinal_note: The contrast between the two blessings reveals a theological pattern of election: Jacob receives the grace of covenant membership not because of superior merit but by divine choice. Esau receives the natural man's portion — strength, independence, earthly survival — but not the covenant. Romans 9:13 ('Jacob have I loved, but Esau have I hated') does not speak of personal damnation but of covenant election — the corporate choice of one lineage over another to bear the redemptive promise.

verses.kjv_text: And Esau hated Jacob because of the blessing wherewith his father blessed him: and Esau said in his heart, The days of mourning for my father are at hand; then will I slay my brother Jacob.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׂטֹם עֵשָׂו אֶת־יַעֲקֹב עַל־הַבְּרָכָה אֲשֶׁר בֵּרְכוֹ אָבִיו וַיֹּאמֶר עֵשָׂו בְּלִבּוֹ יִקְרְבוּ יְמֵי אֵבֶל אָבִי וְאַהַרְגָה אֶת־יַעֲקֹב אָחִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyistom Esav et-Ya'akov al-haberakhah asher berakhо aviv vayomer Esav belibbo yikrevu yemei evel avi ve'ahargeih et-Ya'akov achi.
verses.study_note: 'Esau hated Jacob' (vayyistom Esav et-Ya'akov) — 'satam' is a deep, abiding hatred of hostility. The same root appears in Job (Job 16:9) and Psalm 55:3 for the implacable hatred of enemies. Esau's murderous intention is chillingly practical: he will wait until after Isaac's death ('the days of mourning for my father') — to avoid grieving his blind father and perhaps to avoid disinheritance. The plan is premeditated, not impulsive. The hatred of Esau/Edom for Jacob/Israel becomes a recurring prophetic theme (Obadiah; Ezek 35; Malachi).

verses.kjv_text: And these words of Esau her elder son were told to Rebekah: and she sent and called Jacob her younger son, and said unto him, Behold, thy brother Esau, as touching thee, doth comfort himself, purposing to kill thee.
verses.original_text: וַיֻּגַּד לְרִבְקָה אֶת־דִּבְרֵי עֵשָׂו בְּנָהּ הַגָּדֹל וַתִּשְׁלַח וַתִּקְרָא לְיַעֲקֹב בְּנָהּ הַקָּטָן וַתֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו הִנֵּה עֵשָׂו אָחִיךָ מִתְנַחֵם לְךָ לְהָרְגֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayuggad leRivkah et-divrei Esav bena hagadol vattiʃlach vattikra leYa'akov bena hakkatan vattomer elav hinneh Esav achikha mitnahem lekha lahargekhah.
verses.study_note: Again Rebekah receives intelligence through an unnamed channel ('it was told to Rebekah' — a divine passive?). As she initiated the plan with overheard information, she now responds to overheard plans of murder. 'Esau... doth comfort himself, purposing to kill thee' — 'mitnahem' (comforting himself) is the same word for divine 'repentance' or change of mind; here it means Esau is consoling his grief by planning Jacob's death. The comfort of murderous revenge replaces the grief of loss.

verses.kjv_text: Now therefore, my son, obey my voice; and arise, flee thou to Laban my brother to Haran;
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה בְנִי שְׁמַע בְּקֹלִי וְקוּם בְּרַח־לְךָ אֶל־לָבָן אָחִי חָרָנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah beni shema bekholi vekum berach-lekha el-Lavan achi Charanah.
verses.study_note: 'Obey my voice' (shema bekholi) — the same phrase Rebekah used to initiate the deception (v.8). Now she uses it again to send Jacob away as its consequence. She says 'flee' (berach-lekha) — a reflexive flight, flee for yourself, your own safety. Laban at Haran is named. This is the same Laban who will become Jacob's father-in-law and a new source of deception and suffering — Jacob the deceiver will meet his match in Laban, the master deceiver.

verses.kjv_text: And tarry with him a few days, until thy brother’s fury turn away;
verses.original_text: וְיָשַׁבְתָּ עִמּוֹ יָמִים אֲחָדִים עַד אֲשֶׁר תָּשׁוּב חֲמַת אָחִיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: VeYashavta immo yamim achadim ad asher tashuv chamat achikha.
verses.study_note: 'A few days' (yamim achadim) — Rebekah's naïve hope. She expects Jacob's absence to be temporary. But 'a few days' will become twenty years (31:41). Rebekah never sees Jacob again. The woman who said 'upon me be thy curse' (v.13) now bears it in the most intimate way: permanent separation from her beloved son. 'The fury of thy brother' (chamat achikha) — 'chamah' is burning wrath, like a hot fever. Rebekah believes time will cool this fever. She is not wrong in principle, but the timeline far exceeds her expectations.

verses.kjv_text: Until thy brother’s anger turn away from thee, and he forget that which thou hast done to him: then I will send, and fetch thee from thence: why should I be deprived also of you both in one day?
verses.original_text: עַד אֲשֶׁר תָּשׁוּב אַף־אָחִיךָ מִמְּךָ וְשָׁכַח אֵת אֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתָ לּוֹ וְשָׁלַחְתִּי וּלְקַחְתִּיךָ מִשָּׁם לָמָה אֶשְׁכַּל גַּם־שְׁנֵיכֶם יוֹם אֶחָד׃
verses.transliteration: Ad asher tashuv af-achikha mimkha veshakhach et asher asita lo veshalach tilekachtikhah misham lamah eshkal gam-sheneikhcem yom echad.
verses.study_note: Rebekah reveals the depth of her maternal fear: 'Why should I be deprived also of you both in one day?' (lamah eshkal gam-sheneikhcem yom echad). She fears losing both sons — Esau by execution for murdering Jacob, Jacob by the murder itself. Her final words in the narrative are these — we never see her speak again. The woman who orchestrated the great blessing-drama disappears from the story, having sent Jacob away. The blessing she secured for him cost her the son himself.

verses.kjv_text: And Rebekah said to Isaac, I am weary of my life because of the daughters of Heth: if Jacob take a wife of the daughters of Heth, such as these which are of the daughters of the land, what good shall my life do me?
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר רִבְקָה אֶל־יִצְחָק קַצְתִּי בְחַיַּי מִפְּנֵי בְּנוֹת חֵת אִם־לֹקֵחַ יַעֲקֹב אִשָּׁה מִבְּנוֹת חֵת כָּאֵלֶּה מִבְּנוֹת הָאָרֶץ לָמָּה לִּי חַיִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomer Rivkah el-Yitzhak katzti vechayai mippenei benot Chet im-lokach Ya'akov ishah mibbenot Chet ka'elleh mibbenot ha'aretz lammah li chayyim.
verses.study_note: Rebekah does not tell Isaac the real reason for Jacob's departure — Esau's murderous intent. Instead, she presents the theologically legitimate concern: Jacob must not marry a Canaanite woman as Esau had done (26:34-35). This was a real concern — the covenant line could not be contaminated by Canaanite religious practice. 'I am weary of my life' (katzti vekhayai) — a profound personal declaration. Rebekah, the architect of great events, ends her speaking role in profound weariness. Her stratagem has worked at enormous personal cost. The chapter that began with Isaac's blindness closes with Rebekah's spiritual exhaustion.
verses.doctrinal_note: The final verse points forward to Jacob's journey to Paddan-Aram (ch.28-29), which will provide not only safety but the covenantal wife Leah/Rachel from whom the twelve tribes will come. Even Rebekah's cover story serves God's purpose: Isaac will formally bless Jacob and commission him to find a wife from Laban's family (ch.28:1-5). The sovereignty of God continues to advance through every human action, calculated or desperate, faithful or fearful.

meta.overview: Genesis 27 is one of the most dramatic and theologically complex chapters in the patriarchal narratives. Isaac, old and blind, summons his firstborn Esau to receive the covenantal blessing before death — an oral testament with legally binding force in the ancient world. Rebekah overhears and, recalling the divine oracle of Genesis 25:23 ('the elder shall serve the younger'), orchestrates a daring deception: Jacob impersonates Esau and receives the blessing meant for his brother. The plan succeeds — Isaac pronounces the full Abrahamic blessing upon Jacob. When Esau returns and the deception is discovered, Isaac trembles and Esau cries a great and bitter cry — one of the most haunting scenes in Scripture. Isaac confirms the blessing is irrevocable. Esau vows to kill Jacob after their father's death, and Rebekah, the architect of the plan, sends Jacob to Laban — never to see her son again. The chapter raises profound questions: How does God's sovereign purpose advance through human sin? What is the nature of the irrevocable blessing? And what does it cost to despise the birthright? Paul answers in Romans 9 — the election of Jacob over Esau was not about their deeds but about God's sovereign calling. The typological dimensions are rich: Jacob receiving blessing while clothed in another's identity, a slain animal's skin covering his hands, the father unable to see — all become shadows of deeper realities in the Gospel.
meta.theme: Sovereign Election, the Irrevocable Blessing & the Cost of Compromise
meta.keyWordMeaning: Blessing — the transfer of divine favor, authority, and covenant inheritance. The word occurs 12+ times in this chapter. It is not merely a wish but a performative act that determines destiny.
outline: Isaac's Request: The Blind Patriarch Summons Esau (vv.1-4)
Rebekah's Counter-Plan: The Deception Devised (vv.5-17)
The Stolen Blessing: Jacob Before Isaac (vv.18-29)
Esau's Return: The Discovery and the Bitter Cry (vv.30-40)
The Aftermath: Esau's Vow and Jacob's Flight (vv.41-46)

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac called Jacob, and blessed him, and charged him, and said unto him, Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא יִצְחָק אֶל־יַעֲקֹב וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתוֹ וַיְצַוֵּהוּ וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ לֹא־תִקַּח אִשָּׁה מִבְּנוֹת כְּנָעַן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra Yitschak el-Ya'akov vayevarekh oto vayetsavvehu vayomer lo lo-tikkach ishah mibbennot Kena'an.
verses.study_note: Isaac calls Jacob — the same Jacob who has just deceived him (ch.27) — and blesses him a second time, deliberately and knowingly. The first blessing was stolen; this one is given. The charge not to marry a Canaanite woman echoes Abraham's command to his servant in 24:3, establishing a theological principle: the covenant line must not be spiritually diluted through intermarriage with those outside the covenant community. Isaac, though deceived, now acts as the knowing patriarch transmitting God's purposes. The narrative does not allow Isaac to appear merely as a victim — he rises here to his full prophetic role.
verses.doctrinal_note: The prohibition of Canaanite marriage is not ethnic nationalism but covenantal fidelity. The concern is spiritual contamination — the Canaanites were given over to idolatry and moral corruption. The same principle underlies Paul's command: 'Be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers' (2 Corinthians 6:14). Covenant separation is always for the sake of covenant witness.

verses.kjv_text: Arise, go to Padanaram, to the house of Bethuel thy mother’s father; and take thee a wife from thence of the daughters of Laban thy mother’s brother.
verses.original_text: קוּם לֵךְ פַּדֶּנָה אֲרָם בֵּיתָה בְתוּאֵל אֲבִי אִמֶּךָ וְקַח־לְךָ מִשָּׁם אִשָּׁה מִבְּנוֹת לָבָן אֲחִי אִמֶּךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Kum lekh Paddennah Aram beitah Vetu'el avi immekha vekach-lekha misham ishah mibbennot Lavan achi immekha.
verses.study_note: The commission has a double purpose: to find a wife and to escape Esau (27:43-44). Isaac identifies Paddan-Aram ('the field of Aram,' in upper Mesopotamia, the region of Haran) as the destination. This is the same region from which Abraham sent his servant (ch.24). The household of Bethuel — grandfather through Rebekah — and Laban — uncle — is the approved marriage pool. Jacob is sent to find what Isaac found: a wife from within the broader covenant family, already demonstrating God's providential preparation before Jacob's departure.

verses.kjv_text: And God Almighty bless thee, and make thee fruitful, and multiply thee, that thou mayest be a multitude of people;
verses.original_text: וְאֵל שַׁדַּי יְבָרֵךְ אֹתְךָ וְיַפְרְךָ וְיַרְבֶּךָ וְהָיִיתָ לִקְהַל עַמִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'El Shaddai yevarekh otkha veyafrekkha veyarbekha vehayita likhal ammim.
verses.study_note: Isaac invokes 'El Shaddai' — God Almighty — the covenant name first revealed to Abraham in 17:1 at the institution of circumcision. This is the divine name most associated with the patriarchal blessing of fruitfulness and multiplication. Isaac speaks as a true prophet, transmitting the exact Abrahamic blessing: 'be fruitful and multiply' (peru urevu, echoing Gen 1:28 and 17:2). 'A congregation of peoples' (likhal ammim) — Jacob will become not merely a family but a nation of nations, an assembly of peoples. The blessing is bigger than Jacob can see on the night he sleeps alone on a stone.
verses.doctrinal_note: El Shaddai (God Almighty) is the name that characterizes God's relationship with the patriarchs before the Mosaic covenant (Exodus 6:3). It is associated with overwhelming power channeled through covenant promise. The repeated emphasis on fruitfulness and multiplication traces from Creation (Gen 1:28) through Noah (9:1), Abraham (17:2), and now Jacob — showing the unbroken redemptive thread of God's purposes for humanity.

verses.kjv_text: And give thee the blessing of Abraham, to thee, and to thy seed with thee; that thou mayest inherit the land wherein thou art a stranger, which God gave unto Abraham.
