verses.original_text: וְיִתֶּן־לְךָ אֶת־בִּרְכַּת אַבְרָהָם לְךָ וּלְזַרְעֲךָ אִתָּךְ לְרִשְׁתְּךָ אֶת־אֶרֶץ מְגֻרֶיךָ אֲשֶׁר־נָתַן אֱלֹהִים לְאַבְרָהָם׃
verses.transliteration: Veyitten-lekha et-birkat Avraham lekha ulezar'akha ittakha lerish'tekha et-eretz megurekha asher-natan Elohim le'Avraham.
verses.study_note: Isaac formally transfers 'the blessing of Abraham' (birkat Avraham) — the entire covenant package: seed, land, blessing to the nations. This is the first explicit use of this comprehensive phrase. Three elements: (1) the blessing itself, (2) extended to Jacob's seed, (3) the land of sojourning (eretz megurekha — literally 'the land of your sojournings'). The word 'megurekha' (sojournings) is the same root as 'ger' (stranger/sojourner) — Jacob is being commissioned to inherit a land in which his family has always lived as resident aliens. The gap between promise and possession defines the patriarchal condition.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Abrahamic blessing (birkat Avraham) is the Old Testament nucleus of the Gospel. Paul identifies it explicitly: 'And the scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the heathen through faith, preached before the gospel unto Abraham, saying, In thee shall all nations be blessed' (Galatians 3:8). The covenant transmitted here — seed, land, universal blessing — finds its ultimate fulfillment not in Israel as a nation but in Christ and His church (Galatians 3:16, 29).

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac sent away Jacob: and he went to Padanaram unto Laban, son of Bethuel the Syrian, the brother of Rebekah, Jacob’s and Esau’s mother.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁלַח יִצְחָק אֶת־יַעֲקֹב וַיֵּלֶךְ פַּדֶּנָה אֲרָם אֶל־לָבָן בֶּן־בְּתוּאֵל הָאֲרַמִּי אֲחִי רִבְקָה אֵם יַעֲקֹב וְעֵשָׂו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishllach Yitschak et-Ya'akov vayyeylekh Paddennah Aram el-Lavan ben-Vetu'el ha'Arami achi Rivkah em Ya'akov ve'Esav.
verses.study_note: The narrator's closing remark — 'the brother of Rebekah, Jacob's and Esau's mother' — is notably symmetrical. Both Jacob and Esau share the same mother; the covenant passes through one, not both. The mention of Esau alongside Jacob at this point underscores the divergence: Jacob departs toward the covenant future; Esau remains. The word 'shallach' (sent away) is the same word used of God sending Adam from Eden (3:23) — but here the sending is redemptive, not punitive.

verses.kjv_text: When Esau saw that Isaac had blessed Jacob, and sent him away to Padanaram, to take him a wife from thence; and that as he blessed him he gave him a charge, saying, Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan;
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא עֵשָׂו כִּי־בֵרַךְ יִצְחָק אֶת־יַעֲקֹב וְשִׁלַּח אֹתוֹ פַּדֶּנָה אֲרָם לָקַחַת לוֹ מִשָּׁם אִשָּׁה בְּבָרְכוֹ אֹתוֹ וַיְצַו עָלָיו לֵאמֹר לֹא־תִקַּח אִשָּׁה מִבְּנוֹת כְּנָעַן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyar Esav ki-berakh Yitschak et-Ya'akov veshillach oto Paddennah Aram lakachat lo misham ishah beverakho oto vayetsav alav lemor lo-tikkach ishah mibbennot Kena'an.
verses.study_note: Esau observes the scene — Jacob being blessed and commissioned — and the narrative pauses to show his reaction across verses 6-9. The word 'vayar' (he saw/perceived) is the same as in the forbidden observation that led to Esau's contemptuous sale of his birthright (25:34). Esau is always watching, always reacting to what Jacob receives, but his reactions consistently miss the spiritual dimension. He sees the prohibition against Canaanite wives but draws only a surface lesson.

verses.kjv_text: And that Jacob obeyed his father and his mother, and was gone to Padanaram;
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁמַע יַעֲקֹב אֶל־אָבִיו וְאֶל־אִמּוֹ וַיֵּלֶךְ פַּדֶּנָה אֲרָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishma Ya'akov el-aviv ve'el-immo vayyeylekh Paddennah Aram.
verses.study_note: A brief but significant note: 'Jacob obeyed' (vayyishma — literally 'Jacob heard and responded'). 'Shema' (hear, obey) is the covenantal listening-word, the same root as the Shema (Deut 6:4). Jacob's departure is an act of obedience to parental and therefore covenantal authority. This stands in deliberate contrast to Esau's next move.

verses.kjv_text: And Esau seeing that the daughters of Canaan pleased not Isaac his father;
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא עֵשָׂו כִּי רָעוֹת בְּנוֹת כְּנָעַן בְּעֵינֵי יִצְחָק אָבִיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyar Esav ki ra'ot bennot Kena'an be'einei Yitschak aviv.
verses.study_note: 'Esau seeing' (vayyar Esav) — again he sees but does not perceive the deeper reality. He now realizes that his Canaanite wives 'pleased not' (ra'ot be'einei — were evil/bad in the eyes of) Isaac. The word 'ra'ot' (evil, bad) is the same root as 'ra' — the same moral category applied to the wickedness of Sodom. Marrying Canaanite women was not merely culturally inappropriate; it was a covenant violation.

verses.kjv_text: Then went Esau unto Ishmael, and took unto the wives which he had Mahalath the daughter of Ishmael Abraham’s son, the sister of Nebajoth, to be his wife.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּלֶךְ עֵשָׂו אֶל־יִשְׁמָעֵאל וַיִּקַּח אֶת־מַחֲלַת בַּת־יִשְׁמָעֵאל בֶּן־אַבְרָהָם אֲחוֹת נְבָיוֹת עַל־נָשָׁיו לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyeylekh Esav el-Yishma'el vayyikkach et-Machalat bat-Yishma'el ben-Avraham achot Nevayot al-nashav lo le'ishah.
verses.study_note: Esau's response is the quintessence of legalism without transformation. He sees the outward rule (avoid Canaanites), so he goes to Ishmael — another line of Abraham — as if the issue were ethnicity rather than covenant fidelity. He adds a third wife to his existing Canaanite wives without renouncing them. He performs the letter of what he perceives as the standard, while completely missing its spirit. Ishmael, though Abraham's son, was the rejected line (21:12). Marrying into the Ishmaelite line does not bring Esau into the covenant; it merely adds a different kind of covenantal error.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob went out from Beersheba, and went toward Haran.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּצֵא יַעֲקֹב מִבְּאֵר שָׁבַע וַיֵּלֶךְ חָרָנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyetze Ya'akov mibbe'er Shava vayyeylekh Charanah.
verses.study_note: One of the great understated verses in Genesis. 'Jacob went out' (vayyetze) — this single word launches one of the most formative journeys in biblical history. Beersheba ('well of the oath/seven') was the sacred site of Abraham's covenant with Abimelech (21:31) and where God had appeared to Isaac (26:24). Jacob leaves the place of covenant and oath for the uncertain road to Haran. He carries no camel train (unlike Abraham's servant in ch.24), no wealth, and apparently no clear divine word — only his parents' blessing and commission. The contrast between this lonely departure and Abraham's servant's well-provisioned journey is stark and deliberate.

verses.kjv_text: And he lighted upon a certain place, and tarried there all night, because the sun was set; and he took of the stones of that place, and put them for his pillows, and lay down in that place to sleep.
verses.original_text: וַיִּפְגַּע בַּמָּקוֹם וַיָּלֶן שָׁם כִּי בָא הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ וַיִּקַּח מֵאַבְנֵי הַמָּקוֹם וַיָּשֶׂם מְרַאֲשֹׁתָיו וַיִּשְׁכַּב בַּמָּקוֹם הַהוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyifga bammakom vayyalen sham ki va hashemesh vayyikkach me'avnei hammakom vayyasem mera'ashotav vayyishkav bammakom hahu.
verses.study_note: Jacob 'lighted upon' (vayyifga) a certain place — the Hebrew word is powerful: 'paga' means to encounter, to meet, to intercept. Jacob thinks he is simply stopping for the night; in fact he is about to be encountered. The place is referred to three times simply as 'hammakom' (the place) — a loaded term in Hebrew theology, later used as a reverential name for God ('The Place,' HaMakom). The stone pillow is a detail of desolation: no tent, no servant, no provisions. He who deceived his brother and fled is now utterly alone — the perfect condition for divine revelation.

verses.kjv_text: And he dreamed, and behold a ladder set up on the earth, and the top of it reached to heaven: and behold the angels of God ascending and descending on it.
verses.original_text: וַיַּחֲלֹם וְהִנֵּה סֻלָּם מֻצָּב אַרְצָה וְרֹאשׁוֹ מַגִּיעַ הַשָּׁמָיְמָה וְהִנֵּה מַלְאֲכֵי אֱלֹהִים עֹלִים וְיֹרְדִים בּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyachalom vehineh sullam mutsav artzah verosho maggia hashamaymah vehineh mal'akhei Elohim olim veyoredim bo.
verses.study_note: One of the most iconic visions in Scripture. The 'sullam' (ladder/stairway) is unique — this word occurs nowhere else in the Hebrew Bible. Its root may relate to 'salal' (to cast up, to lift a highway) or to the Akkadian 'simmiltu' (stairway/ramp), perhaps evoking the stepped temple towers (ziggurats) of Mesopotamia — but transformed: pagan ziggurats were human attempts to reach heaven; this stairway comes down from God. 'Mutsav' (set up, established) — the same root later used of the pillar Jacob erects (matsevah). The angels 'ascending' before 'descending' is significant: they first go up (from earth), then come down (from heaven) — the traffic of providential governance runs from earth to God and back. The ladder is not decorative; it is the infrastructure of divine-human communication.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jesus explicitly claims fulfillment of this vision: 'Hereafter ye shall see heaven open, and the angels of God ascending and descending upon the Son of Man' (John 1:51). Christ is the true Sullam — the only Mediator between God and man (1 Tim 2:5), the one who is both 'set up on the earth' (Incarnation) and whose 'top reaches to heaven' (Ascension and exaltation). The ladder Jacob sees in sleep, the apostles will see in the flesh.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, the LORD stood above it, and said, I am the LORD God of Abraham thy father, and the God of Isaac: the land whereon thou liest, to thee will I give it, and to thy seed;
verses.original_text: וְהִנֵּה יְהוָה נִצָּב עָלָיו וַיֹּאמַר אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם אָבִיךָ וֵאלֹהֵי יִצְחָק הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר אַתָּה שֹׁכֵב עָלֶיהָ לְךָ אֶתְּנֶנָּה וּלְזַרְעֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehineh YHVH nitsav alav vayomar ani YHVH Elohei Avraham avikha vElohei Yitschak ha'aretz asher attah shokhev aleha lekha ettenennah ulezar'ekha.
verses.study_note: The LORD (YHVH) Himself appears at the top of the ladder (or 'beside it/beside him' — 'alav' can mean 'above it' or 'beside him'). His first words are self-identification — exactly as in Genesis 15:7 (to Abraham) and Exodus 20:2 (to Israel at Sinai). 'I am the LORD God of Abraham thy father, and the God of Isaac' — God names Himself through covenant history, not abstract philosophy. Remarkably, God gives Jacob the land on which he is sleeping — the land he is fleeing! He is being given the land as he runs away from it. 'The land whereon thou liest' — the desolate field where a fugitive sleeps on a stone becomes the title deed to the Promised Land.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's covenant name-formula — 'I am the LORD God of Abraham... and of Isaac' — is the pattern of divine self-revelation throughout Scripture. Jesus uses this exact formula to prove the resurrection: 'I am the God of Abraham, and the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob. God is not the God of the dead, but of the living' (Matthew 22:32). The covenant God is identified through history, through persons, through relationship — never merely as an abstract first principle.

verses.kjv_text: And thy seed shall be as the dust of the earth, and thou shalt spread abroad to the west, and to the east, and to the north, and to the south: and in thee and in thy seed shall all the families of the earth be blessed.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה זַרְעֲךָ כַּעֲפַר הָאָרֶץ וּפָרַצְתָּ יָמָּה וָקֵדְמָה וְצָפֹנָה וָנֶגְבָּה וְנִבְרְכוּ בְךָ כָּל־מִשְׁפְּחֹת הָאֲדָמָה וּבְזַרְעֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah zar'akha ka'afar ha'aretz ufartsa yammah vakkedmah vetsafonah vanegbah venivrekhu bekha kol-mishpechot ha'adamah ubeZar'ekha.
verses.study_note: God now explicitly renews all three Abrahamic promises simultaneously: (1) 'thy seed as the dust of the earth' — innumerable seed (cf. 13:16 to Abraham); (2) 'thou shalt spread abroad to the west, east, north, and south' — universal territorial expansion; (3) 'in thee and in thy seed shall all the families of the earth be blessed' — the universal Gospel promise. This third element is the culmination: 'venivrekhu' (shall be blessed) is a Niphal or Hithpael form — 'all families will find blessing in you.' The word 'mishpechot' (families/clans) is broader than 'goyim' (nations) — it encompasses the full sweep of human community. 'In thy seed' (uvezar'ekha) — singular seed — which Paul in Galatians 3:16 identifies as Christ.
verses.doctrinal_note: The promise 'in thy seed shall all families of the earth be blessed' is the Old Testament Gospel in seed form. Paul's argument in Galatians 3:16 rests on the singularity of 'seed' (zera) in passages like this — pointing not to Israel as a collective but to Christ as the singular representative. Every covenant renewal to the patriarchs culminates in this universal blessing — which finds its fulfillment in the Great Commission (Matthew 28:19) and the redeemed multitude 'from all nations' (Revelation 7:9).

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, I am with thee, and will keep thee in all places whither thou goest, and will bring thee again into this land; for I will not leave thee, until I have done that which I have spoken to thee of.
verses.original_text: וְהִנֵּה אָנֹכִי עִמָּךְ וּשְׁמַרְתִּיךָ בְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר־תֵּלֵךְ וַהֲשִׁבֹתִיךָ אֶל־הָאֲדָמָה הַזֹּאת כִּי לֹא אֶעֱזָבְךָ עַד אֲשֶׁר אִם־עָשִׂיתִי אֵת אֲשֶׁר־דִּבַּרְתִּי לָךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehineh anokhi immakh ushemartikha bekhol asher-teylekh vahashivotikha el-ha'adamah hazzo't ki lo e'ezavekha ad asher im-asiti et asher-dibbarti lakh.
verses.study_note: The climactic personal promise: five divine commitments in a single verse. (1) 'I am with thee' (anokhi immakh) — the Immanuel principle, God's personal presence. (2) 'I will keep thee' (shemartikha) — guarding, protecting. (3) 'in all places whither thou goest' — the protection is not location-dependent; it follows Jacob. (4) 'I will bring thee again into this land' — the return is guaranteed before Jacob even leaves. (5) 'I will not leave thee until I have done' — the divine fidelity is tied not to Jacob's faithfulness but to God's own word (dibbarti — what I have spoken). The word 'lo e'ezavekha' (I will not leave/forsake thee) echoes throughout Scripture as the covenant formula of divine presence.
verses.doctrinal_note: The five-fold promise of verse 15 becomes the pattern of divine accompaniment throughout Scripture. The Immanuel promise ('I am with thee') finds its ultimate fulfillment in Christ — 'they shall call his name Emmanuel, which being interpreted is, God with us' (Matthew 1:23) — and in the perpetual presence of the risen Christ: 'lo, I am with you alway, even unto the end of the world' (Matthew 28:20). The promise 'I will not leave thee' (lo e'ezavekha) is quoted verbatim in Hebrews 13:5 as the Christian's basis for contentment in all circumstances.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob awaked out of his sleep, and he said, Surely the LORD is in this place; and I knew it not.
verses.original_text: וַיִּיקַץ יַעֲקֹב מִשְּׁנָתוֹ וַיֹּאמֶר אָכֵן יֵשׁ יְהוָה בַּמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה וְאָנֹכִי לֹא יָדָעְתִּי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikats Ya'akov mishenatov vayomer akhen yesh YHVH bammakom hazzeh ve'anokhi lo yada'ti.
verses.study_note: The awakening. 'Vayyikats' — he woke suddenly, startled awake from a sleep that has been anything but ordinary. His first spoken words are a confession: 'Surely the LORD is in this place; and I knew it not.' 'Akhen' (surely, indeed, truly) introduces a solemn declaration — Jacob is not guessing, he is confessing. 'Yesh YHVH' — 'the LORD is present.' He came to a random field and found the throne room of God. The self-indictment is total: 'and I knew it not' (ve'anokhi lo yada'ti) — his ignorance was complete. The place revealed what Jacob could not have anticipated. This is the nature of divine grace — it finds us before we find it.

verses.kjv_text: And he was afraid, and said, How dreadful is this place! this is none other but the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven.
verses.original_text: וַיִּירָא וַיֹּאמַר מַה־נּוֹרָא הַמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה אֵין זֶה כִּי אִם־בֵּית אֱלֹהִים וְזֶה שַׁעַר הַשָּׁמָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyira vayomar mah-nora hammakom hazzeh ein zeh ki im-beit Elohim vezeh sha'ar hashamayim.
verses.study_note: Jacob's first response to divine presence is fear (vayyira — he feared). This is the proper response to genuine encounter with the holy — the same fear that seized Isaiah (Isa 6:5), Ezekiel (Ezek 1:28), and John (Rev 1:17). 'How dreadful' (mah-nora) — 'nora' comes from 'yare' (to fear); it means awesome, terrifying, worthy of reverent dread. He gives the place two names: (1) 'beit Elohim' — House of God, the dwelling place of the divine; (2) 'sha'ar hashamayim' — the gate of heaven, the opening through which heaven and earth communicate. The ladder has made this otherwise unremarkable field the axis mundi — the cosmic center where heaven and earth touch.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jacob's two names for the place — 'House of God' (Beit Elohim) and 'Gate of Heaven' (Sha'ar HaShamayim) — point forward to two New Testament fulfillments. Christ's body is the new temple (John 2:19-21), the true 'House of God.' And Christ, as the ladder (John 1:51), is the true 'Gate of Heaven' — He says explicitly 'I am the door' (John 10:9) and 'I am the way' (John 14:6). Every true sanctuary is a Bethel — a place where heaven and earth meet in Christ.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob rose up early in the morning, and took the stone that he had put for his pillows, and set it up for a pillar, and poured oil upon the top of it.
verses.original_text: וַיַּשְׁכֵּם יַעֲקֹב בַּבֹּקֶר וַיִּקַּח אֶת־הָאֶבֶן אֲשֶׁר־שָׂם מְרַאֲשֹׁתָיו וַיָּשֶׂם אֹתָהּ מַצֵּבָה וַיִּצֹק שֶׁמֶן עַל־רֹאשָׁהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashkem Ya'akov babboker vayyikkach et-ha'even asher-sam mera'ashotav vayyasem otah matsevah vayyitsok shemen al-roshhah.
verses.study_note: Jacob's immediate response: early rising ('vayyashkem babboker' — the same phrase used of Abraham's obedient early rising in 22:3), taking the stone from under his head, and consecrating it. The stone of desolation becomes the stone of dedication. The 'matsevah' (standing pillar/stele) is a sacred monument — Jacob's first act of worship. Anointing with oil ('vayyitsok shemen') is the earliest recorded anointing in Scripture — the stone is 'anointed,' consecrated to God. The Hebrew 'mashiach' (anointed one, Messiah) comes from the same root as this anointing act. Every stone that becomes a Bethel points forward to the Anointed One.

verses.kjv_text: And he called the name of that place Bethel: but the name of that city was called Luz at the first.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא אֶת־שֵׁם־הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא בֵּית־אֵל וְאוּלָם לוּז שֵׁם־הָעִיר לָרִאשֹׁנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra et-shem-hammakom hahu Beit-El ve'ulam Luz shem-ha'ir lari'shonah.
verses.study_note: Jacob names the place 'Beit-El' — House of God. This is one of the great naming acts of Genesis: God names creation (ch.1), Adam names the animals and Eve (chs.2-3), and now Jacob names the site of divine encounter. The old name 'Luz' ('almond tree,' a place of natural beauty) is replaced by a name of theological significance. Bethel becomes one of the most important sacred sites in Israel's history — the site of Abraham's early altar (12:8), the northern sanctuary in the divided monarchy (1 Kings 12:29), and the setting of Jacob's final covenant renewal (35:7,15). The dual name (Luz/Bethel) reminds the reader that sacred places are ordinary places transformed by divine encounter.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob vowed a vow, saying, If God will be with me, and will keep me in this way that I go, and will give me bread to eat, and raiment to put on,
verses.original_text: וַיִּדַּר יַעֲקֹב נֶדֶר לֵאמֹר אִם־יִהְיֶה אֱלֹהִים עִמָּדִי וּשְׁמָרַנִי בַּדֶּרֶךְ הַזֶּה אֲשֶׁר אָנֹכִי הוֹלֵךְ וְנָתַן־לִי לֶחֶם לֶאֱכֹל וּבֶגֶד לִלְבֹּשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiddar Ya'akov neder lemor im-yihyeh Elohim immadi ushemarani badderekh hazzeh asher anokhi holekh venatan-li lechem le'ekhol uveged lilbosh.
verses.study_note: Jacob makes the first recorded vow (neder) in Scripture. The vow begins with 'im' (if) — a conditional form that has troubled interpreters: is Jacob bargaining with God, or is this the natural language of faith expressing hope? The vow's conditions mirror exactly the five promises God made in verse 15: (1) God with me (v.15: 'I am with thee'), (2) keep me on the way (v.15: 'I will keep thee'), (3) return me to this land (implicit), (4) bread to eat — Jacob reduces God's grand promises to survival level: bread and clothing. The smallness of Jacob's requests reveals the smallness of his faith at this stage — he has heard God's cosmic promises and his first concern is food and clothes. But even this faith, mustard-seed small, is accepted by God.

verses.kjv_text: So that I come again to my father’s house in peace; then shall the LORD be my God:
verses.original_text: וְשַׁבְתִּי בְשָׁלוֹם אֶל־בֵּית אָבִי וְהָיָה יְהוָה לִי לֵאלֹהִים׃
verses.transliteration: Veshavti veshalom el-beit avi vehayah YHVH li lElohim.
verses.study_note: The vow's second component: return in peace (shalom — wholeness, completeness, safety) to his father's house, and then 'the LORD shall be my God' (YHVH li lElohim). This declaration is the crux of the vow. Jacob is not rejecting the LORD — he heard the divine promises clearly. He is making a personal covenant of allegiance, committing himself: if God performs these things, then I will claim the LORD as my God. There is something both moving and impoverished in this: moving because Jacob is genuinely reaching out in faith; impoverished because he is still treating the relationship as a transaction rather than a gift. This is faith at the beginning — real but not yet mature.

verses.kjv_text: And this stone, which I have set for a pillar, shall be God’s house: and of all that thou shalt give me I will surely give the tenth unto thee.
verses.original_text: וְהָאֶבֶן הַזֹּאת אֲשֶׁר־שַׂמְתִּי מַצֵּבָה יִהְיֶה בֵּית אֱלֹהִים וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר תִּתֶּן־לִי עַשֵּׂר אֲעַשְּׂרֶנּוּ לָךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Veha'even hazzo't asher-samti matsevah yihyeh beit Elohim vekhol asher titten-li asser a'asserenu lakh.
verses.study_note: Jacob concludes his vow with two acts of consecration: (1) the stone pillar (matsevah) shall be 'God's house' — he designates the sacred site permanently; (2) 'of all that thou shalt give me I will surely give the tenth' — the first recorded tithe in a personal vow. Tithing here predates the Mosaic Law, echoing Abraham's tithe to Melchizedek (14:20). The Hebrew 'asser a'asserenu' is an emphatic double construction — 'tithing I will tithe' — a solemn, absolute commitment. Jacob commits a tithe not yet received from a God he has not yet fully embraced. This is faith acting on the word: giving what you do not yet have in response to what you have been promised.
verses.doctrinal_note: The tithe in Jacob's vow, like Abraham's tithe to Melchizedek (Gen 14:20), establishes that the principle of returning a tenth to God predates and transcends the Mosaic Law. Hebrews 7:4-10 uses the pre-Mosaic tithe to argue for the superiority of the Melchizedek priesthood (and therefore of Christ) over the Levitical system. Tithing is ultimately a declaration of covenant allegiance: acknowledging that all we have comes from God and belongs to Him. 'The earth is the LORD's, and the fulness thereof' (Psalm 24:1).

meta.overview: Genesis 28 is one of the most luminous chapters in all of Scripture. Jacob — having just deceived his father and stolen his brother's blessing — is sent away from home, fleeing Esau's murderous rage and his mother's scheming. He is alone, resourceless, a fugitive sleeping in the open field with a stone for a pillow. It is at precisely this lowest point that heaven opens. In a dream Jacob sees a stairway (sullam) reaching from earth to heaven, with angels ascending and descending upon it, and the LORD Himself standing above it, renewing the Abrahamic covenant in language almost identical to the promises given in chapters 12, 15, 17, and 26. God does not wait for Jacob to clean up his life — He meets him in his flight. Jacob wakes in awe, consecrates the stone as a pillar, names the place Bethel ('House of God'), and makes a conditional vow that reveals both the infant faith and the self-centred bargaining of a man who will spend twenty years in the school of divine grace. The chapter is structurally pivotal: it marks the departure of the covenant line from Canaan, the initiation of God's direct relationship with Jacob, and the first naming of Bethel — which becomes one of Israel's most sacred sites. Typologically, the ladder prefigures Christ (John 1:51), who is the true stairway between heaven and earth, the meeting point of God and man.
meta.theme: The Covenant God Who Meets Us in Our Flight — Grace at Bethel
meta.keyWordMeaning: Ladder/Stairway — the cosmic stairway connecting heaven and earth, upon which angels ascend and descend. Used only here in all of Scripture. Jesus applies the image to Himself in John 1:51, making this one of the most Christ-saturated words in Genesis.
outline: Isaac Blesses and Commissions Jacob (vv.1-5): The Abrahamic blessing transmitted to Jacob
Esau's Reactive Marriage (vv.6-9): Esau marries a daughter of Ishmael — too little, too late
Jacob at Luz — Alone with a Stone Pillow (vv.10-11): The fugitive's first night alone
The Dream: The Ladder, Angels, and God's Voice (vv.12-15): Heaven opens over a fleeing man
Jacob Awakes in Holy Terror (vv.16-17): 'Surely the LORD is in this place'
The Pillar and the Name Bethel (vv.18-19): The stone becomes a sanctuary
Jacob's Vow — Conditional Faith at the Threshold (vv.20-22): The first recorded vow in Scripture

verses.kjv_text: Then Jacob went on his journey, and came into the land of the people of the east.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשָּׂא יַעֲקֹב רַגְלָיו וַיֵּלֶךְ אַרְצָה בְנֵי־קֶדֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayissa Yaakov raglav vayelekh artzah venei-kedem.
verses.study_note: The Hebrew is startlingly vivid: 'וַיִּשָּׂא יַעֲקֹב רַגְלָיו' (vayissa Yaakov raglav) — literally 'Jacob lifted up his feet.' This is the only occurrence of this expression in Scripture; it conveys buoyancy, eagerness, and renewed vigor. Jacob has just awakened from the Bethel vision (28:10-22) where God confirmed the Abrahamic covenant and promised His presence. The divine encounter transforms the fugitive into a pilgrim — his feet are lifted because his heart has been lifted. The rabbis noted this contrasts with his heavy flight from Esau: what was escape becomes expedition. 'The land of the people of the east' (artzah venei-kedem) — the same lands Abraham's servant traversed when seeking a bride for Isaac (Gen 24). The repetition is intentional: another covenant heir, another journey east, another bride-finding to come. In each case the journey east is a journey toward the future of the promise.

verses.kjv_text: And he looked, and behold a well in the field, and, lo, there were three flocks of sheep lying by it; for out of that well they watered the flocks: and a great stone was upon the well’s mouth.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא וְהִנֵּה בְאֵר בַּשָּׂדֶה וְהִנֵּה שָׁם שְׁלֹשָׁה עֶדְרֵי־צֹאן רֹבְצִים עָלֶיהָ כִּי מִן־הַבְּאֵר הַהִוא יַשְׁקוּ הָעֲדָרִים וְהָאֶבֶן גְּדֹלָה עַל־פִּי הַבְּאֵר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayar vehineh be'er bassadeh vehineh sham sheloshah edrei-tzon rovetziym aleha ki min-habe'er hahi yashku ha'adarim veha'even gedolah al-pi habe'er.
verses.study_note: This is a 'betrothal type-scene' — one of the Bible's recurring narrative patterns. Three such scenes exist (Hagar at the well, Gen 16; Isaac's servant, Gen 24; Jacob here, Gen 29), and the fourth is Jesus and the Samaritan woman (John 4). The pattern always involves: journey to foreign land, a well, a woman, drawing of water, return to family, and betrothal. The 'great stone' (ha'even gedolah) upon the well is a key detail — it requires community effort to remove (v.3), making what Jacob does alone in v.10 an act of extraordinary, love-driven strength. 'Behold' (vehineh) appears twice in this verse — the narrator signals Jacob's wonder and the reader's attention.

verses.kjv_text: And thither were all the flocks gathered: and they rolled the stone from the well’s mouth, and watered the sheep, and put the stone again upon the well’s mouth in his place.
verses.original_text: וְנֶאֶסְפוּ־שָׁמָּה כָל־הָעֲדָרִים וְגָלֲלוּ אֶת־הָאֶבֶן מֵעַל פִּי הַבְּאֵר וְהִשְׁקוּ אֶת־הַצֹּאן וְהֵשִׁיבוּ אֶת־הָאֶבֶן עַל־פִּי הַבְּאֵר לִמְקֹמָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vene'esfu-shammah kol-ha'adarim vegalallu et-ha'even me'al pi habe'er vehishku et-hatzon veheshivu et-ha'even al-pi habe'er likmomah.
verses.study_note: The stone is rolled away (galal — the root of Gilgal, 'rolling') only when all the flocks are present — a communal act. The verb 'galal' connects to the Bethel vision where Jacob 'rolled away' (galal) the stone that served as his pillar (28:18 uses a related root). This detail sets up the contrast in v.10: what requires all the region's shepherds together, Jacob does alone for one woman. The stone's return to its place (likmomah) emphasizes how fixed and heavy the custom was. Tombs in Israel were also sealed with great stones (Matt 27:60) — and sealed tombs are also rolled away by divine power (Matt 28:2).

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob said unto them, My brethren, whence be ye? And they said, Of Haran are we.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶם יַעֲקֹב אַחַי מֵאַיִן אַתֶּם וַיֹּאמְרוּ מֵחָרָן אֲנַחְנוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer lahem Yaakov achai me'ayin attem vayomeru meCharan anachnu.
verses.study_note: Jacob calls the unknown shepherds 'achai' (my brothers/brethren) — a greeting of solidarity, kinship, and hopefulness. He has come 500+ miles from Beersheba to Haran, fleeing with nothing (28:11), and the first word out of his mouth is 'brothers.' He is looking for belonging. The answer 'from Haran' (meCharan) is exactly what he hoped to hear — this is his destination, his mother Rebekah's homeland (27:43). This is the beginning of the fulfillment of his mother's instruction. Haran is also associated with Abraham's family's sojourn (Acts 7:2-4).

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, Know ye Laban the son of Nahor? And they said, We know him.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶם הֲיְדַעְתֶּם אֶת־לָבָן בֶּן־נָחוֹר וַיֹּאמְרוּ יָדָעְנוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer lahem heyede'tem et-Lavan ben-Nachor vayomeru yada'nu.
verses.study_note: Jacob asks for Laban 'son of Nahor' — correctly identifying him by patriarchal lineage, the standard ancient Near Eastern identification. Laban is actually the son of Bethuel, son of Nahor (Gen 22:22-23; 24:29) — so Jacob is using the grandfather's name as the family's identifying marker, a common practice. The shepherds' answer 'we know him' (yada'nu) is a simple two-word confirmation of providence — after 500 miles, Jacob has arrived precisely at the right place. God's promise at Bethel ('I am with you... wherever you go,' 28:15) is already being fulfilled.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, Is he well? And they said, He is well: and, behold, Rachel his daughter cometh with the sheep.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶם הֲשָׁלוֹם לוֹ וַיֹּאמְרוּ שָׁלוֹם וְהִנֵּה רָחֵל בִּתּוֹ בָּאָה עִם־הַצֹּאן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer lahem hashalom lo vayomeru shalom vehineh Rachel bitto ba'ah im-hatzon.
verses.study_note: Jacob asks 'hashalom lo' — literally 'Is there shalom upon him?' the Hebrew greeting meaning wholeness, wellbeing, and peace. The answer is 'shalom' — and then the narrator inserts 'vehineh Rachel bitto ba'ah' — and behold, Rachel his daughter comes! The 'vehineh' (and behold) signals the appearance of something providentially significant. Rachel arrives precisely as Jacob is speaking about her father. The name 'Rachel' (רָחֵל) means 'ewe' or 'female sheep' — and she arrives with sheep. Her very name describes her occupation and her gentle character.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Lo, it is yet high day, neither is it time that the cattle should be gathered together: water ye the sheep, and go and feed them.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר הֵן עוֹד הַיּוֹם גָּדוֹל לֹא־עֵת הֵאָסֵף הַמִּקְנֶה הַשְׁקוּ הַצֹּאן וּלְכוּ רְעוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer hen od hayom gadol lo-et he'asef hammikneh hashku hatzon ulechu re'u.
verses.study_note: Jacob's practical suggestion to the shepherds to water the flocks and return to pasture has a double motivation: hospitality/efficiency, and perhaps a desire to be alone with Rachel when she arrives. 'It is yet high day' (od hayom gadol — literally, 'the day is yet great') — mid-afternoon. The herding custom was to wait until all flocks could water together (v.3), but Jacob, with the urgency of love's anticipation, suggests they needn't wait. Some commentators see in his eagerness to have the shepherds move on an unconscious desire for privacy with the arriving Rachel.

verses.kjv_text: And they said, We cannot, until all the flocks be gathered together, and till they roll the stone from the well’s mouth; then we water the sheep.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ לֹא נוּכַל עַד אֲשֶׁר יֵאָסְפוּ כָּל־הָעֲדָרִים וְגָלֲלוּ אֶת־הָאֶבֶן מֵעַל פִּי הַבְּאֵר וְהִשְׁקִינוּ הַצֹּאן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomeru lo nukhal ad asher ye'asfu kol-ha'adarim vegalallu et-ha'even me'al pi habe'er vehishkinu hatzon.
verses.study_note: The shepherds' answer confirms the communal nature of the water-covenant: 'we cannot' (lo nukhal) until all are gathered and all roll together. This is a covenant of mutual dependence — the well serves everyone, so everyone shares in making it accessible. The communal rolling of the stone (galal) requires collective strength. This makes Jacob's single-handed rolling in v.10 even more remarkable: he bypasses the community protocol entirely, driven by one sight — Rachel.

verses.kjv_text: And while he yet spake with them, Rachel came with her father’s sheep: for she kept them.
verses.original_text: עוֹדֶנּוּ מְדַבֵּר עִמָּם וְרָחֵל בָּאָה עִם־הַצֹּאן אֲשֶׁר לְאָבִיהָ כִּי רֹעָה הִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Odenu medabber immam veRachel ba'ah im-hatzon asher le'aviha ki ro'ah hi.
verses.study_note: 'While he yet spake' (odenu medabber) — divine providence is precise to the second. Before Jacob has finished his sentence, Rachel appears. The timing is God's doing. 'For she kept them' (ki ro'ah hi) — Rachel is a shepherdess (ro'ah, the same word as shepherd). This is significant: Rachel tends her father's flock, much as Moses would tend Jethro's flock before his divine call (Exod 3:1), and as David kept his father's sheep before his anointing (1 Sam 16:11). Shepherdesses are figures of faithful, quiet service who are then called to greater destinies.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when Jacob saw Rachel the daughter of Laban his mother’s brother, and the sheep of Laban his mother’s brother, that Jacob went near, and rolled the stone from the well’s mouth, and watered the flock of Laban his mother’s brother.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כַּאֲשֶׁר רָאָה יַעֲקֹב אֶת־רָחֵל בַּת לָבָן אֲחִי אִמּוֹ וְאֶת־צֹאן לָבָן אֲחִי אִמּוֹ וַיִּגַּשׁ יַעֲקֹב וַיָּגֶל אֶת־הָאֶבֶן מֵעַל פִּי הַבְּאֵר וַיַּשְׁקְ אֶת־צֹאן לָבָן אֲחִי אִמּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi ka'asher ra'ah Yaakov et-Rachel bat Lavan achi immo ve'et tzon Lavan achi immo vayiggash Yaakov vayyagel et-ha'even me'al pi habe'er vayashk et-tzon Lavan achi immo.
verses.study_note: The most emphatic verse in the scene: 'Laban his mother's brother' appears THREE times in a single verse — a deliberate triple repetition underscoring that Jacob has found the right family. He has arrived. And what happens when he arrives? He single-handedly rolls away the stone that required all the region's shepherds together. The word 'vayagel' (he rolled) is singular, emphatic, immediate — he went (vayiggash) and rolled (vayyagel). No waiting, no asking for help. Seeing Rachel, Jacob is overcome with a strength he did not possess moments before. The Rabbis saw this as a picture of divine enablement — love, and particularly covenant love, empowers beyond natural capacity.
verses.doctrinal_note: The rolling away of the stone by Jacob alone at the sight of his beloved is one of Scripture's great typological images. The lover, driven by love, removes the barrier that separates him from the beloved — an act requiring supernatural strength. This becomes a type of the resurrection: at the sight of the Beloved (the Church), the stone of death is rolled away (Matt 28:2). Love empowers what community effort cannot accomplish. Jacob's act at the well in Paddan-aram casts a long shadow forward to Joseph's tomb in Jerusalem.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob kissed Rachel, and lifted up his voice, and wept.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשַּׁק יַעֲקֹב לְרָחֵל וַיִּשָּׂא אֶת־קֹלוֹ וַיֵּבְךְּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayishak Yaakov leRachel vayyissa et-kolo vayyevk.
verses.study_note: One of Scripture's most emotionally charged moments — Jacob kisses Rachel and weeps aloud. 'Lifted up his voice and wept' (vayyissa et-kolo vayyevk) — the same phrase used when Esau discovers Jacob's theft (27:38) and when Joseph reveals himself to his brothers (45:2). It is the language of overwhelming, ungoverned emotion. Jacob has fled home with nothing — no gift, no bridal price, no servants, no camels (contrast Abraham's servant in Gen 24). He arrives with only himself, and that self is trembling with relief, hope, gratitude, and perhaps the first stirrings of what will become the deepest love his narrative records. His tears are tears of homecoming.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob told Rachel that he was her father’s brother, and that he was Rebekah’s son: and she ran and told her father.
verses.original_text: וַיַּגֵּד יַעֲקֹב לְרָחֵל כִּי אֲחִי אָבִיהָ הוּא וְכִי בֶן־רִבְקָה הוּא וַתָּרָץ וַתַּגֵּד לְאָבִיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayagged Yaakov leRachel ki achi aviha hu veki ven-Rivkah hu vattatratz vattagged le'aviha.
verses.study_note: Jacob identifies himself in two ways: 'he is her father's brother' (a broad kinship term in Hebrew, meaning 'kinsman' — he is actually Laban's nephew) and 'he is Rebekah's son.' The name 'Rebekah' unlocks everything — Rebekah is Laban's sister (Gen 24:29), and this connection explains everything about why Jacob has appeared at this well. Rachel 'ran' (wataratz) immediately — the same eager running as Abraham (18:2), Rebekah herself (24:20), and Laban (24:29). Running signifies the response of one who perceives providential significance. The family responds to covenant moments with physical urgency.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when Laban heard the tidings of Jacob his sister’s son, that he ran to meet him, and embraced him, and kissed him, and brought him to his house. And he told Laban all these things.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כִשְׁמֹעַ לָבָן אֶת־שֵׁמַע יַעֲקֹב בֶּן־אֲחֹתוֹ וַיָּרָץ לִקְרָאתוֹ וַיְחַבֶּק־לוֹ וַיְנַשֶּׁק־לוֹ וַיְבִיאֵהוּ אֶל־בֵּיתוֹ וַיְסַפֵּר לְלָבָן אֵת כָּל־הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi khishmo'a Lavan et-shema Yaakov ben-achoto vayyaratz likrato vayyechabek-lo vayenashek-lo vayyevi'ehu el-beto vayesapper leLavan et kol-hadevarim ha'elleh.
verses.study_note: Laban 'runs' (vayyaratz) — exactly as he ran to Abraham's servant when he saw the gold jewelry (24:29-30). One may wonder about Laban's motivation then vs. now: in chapter 24, he saw wealth; now Jacob comes empty-handed. Yet he embraces and kisses Jacob warmly. The narrator notes that Jacob 'told Laban all these things' — presumably including his flight from Esau, his Bethel vision, and his mission. Whatever Laban's inner motives, the external reception is warm. 'All these things' implies Jacob did not conceal his situation: he came as a refugee, not as a rich suitor.

verses.kjv_text: And Laban said to him, Surely thou art my bone and my flesh. And he abode with him the space of a month.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ לָבָן אַךְ עַצְמִי וּבְשָׂרִי אָתָּה וַיֵּשֶׁב עִמּוֹ חֹדֶשׁ יָמִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer lo Lavan akh atzmi uvasari attah vayyeshev immo chodesh yamim.
verses.study_note: 'My bone and my flesh' (atzmi uvasari) — the language of the most intimate kinship bond in Hebrew. This exact phrase appears when Adam sees Eve (2:23), when the tribes accept David as king (2 Sam 5:1), and in Judges 9:2. It is covenant kinship language — the recognition of essential belonging. Laban's declaration sounds genuine and covenantal; subsequent chapters will reveal its hollowness. The month (chodesh yamim — literally 'a month of days') that Jacob spends gives Laban time to observe Jacob's capabilities and Jacob time to fall deeper in love with Rachel.

verses.kjv_text: And Laban said unto Jacob, Because thou art my brother, shouldest thou therefore serve me for nought? tell me, what shall thy wages be?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן לְיַעֲקֹב הֲכִי־אָחִי אַתָּה וַעֲבַדְתַּנִי חִנָּם הַגִּידָה לִּי מַה־מַּשְׂכֻּרְתֶּךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Lavan leYaakov haki-achi attah va'avadtani chinnam haggidah li mah-maskorotekha.
verses.study_note: Laban frames his offer as generosity ('Because you are my brother, should you serve me for nothing?') but this is a negotiating position. He has had a month to observe Jacob's prodigious strength and capability (v.10 at the well). Asking Jacob to name his wages appears gracious but actually places Laban in control of the negotiation — the one who names the offer defines the relationship. 'Chinnam' (for nothing/free) — the same word used when God offers grace without price (Isa 55:1). Laban is commercializing what God gives freely.

verses.kjv_text: And Laban had two daughters: the name of the elder was Leah, and the name of the younger was Rachel.
verses.original_text: וּלְלָבָן שְׁתֵּי בָנוֹת שֵׁם הַגְּדֹלָה לֵאָה וְשֵׁם הַקְּטַנָּה רָחֵל׃
verses.transliteration: UleLavan shetei vanot shem haggedolah Le'ah veshem hakkettannah Rachel.
verses.study_note: The narrator pauses to introduce both daughters with a literary device that creates dramatic tension: we already know Rachel (vv.6-12), and now we learn of a prior, older daughter named Leah. 'Leah' (לֵאָה) likely means 'weary' or 'wild cow' — both interpretations carry a pathos the narrative will exploit. 'Rachel' means 'ewe' — gentle, beautiful, beloved. The naming order (Leah first by age, Rachel first in Jacob's heart) creates the conflict that will drive the rest of the Jacob narrative. Two sisters, two name-meanings, two destinies — and one man caught between them, about to receive both.

verses.kjv_text: Leah was tender eyed; but Rachel was beautiful and well favoured.
verses.original_text: וְעֵינֵי לֵאָה רַכּוֹת וְרָחֵל הָיְתָה יְפַת־תֹּאַר וִיפַת מַרְאֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'einei Le'ah rakkot veRachel haytah yefat-to'ar vifat mar'eh.
verses.study_note: The contrast between the sisters is drawn in two precise strokes. Leah's eyes were 'rakkot' — the Hebrew is famously ambiguous. It can mean 'tender' (gentle, soft) or 'weak' (dim, delicate, perhaps a physical imperfection). Many ancient interpreters (LXX has 'without brightness'; Rashi says 'weak from crying') lean toward a slight visual deficiency. But 'tender' was also interpreted positively — eyes sensitive to beauty and sorrow. Whatever the precise meaning, it marks Leah as not the conventionally beautiful one. Rachel, by contrast, is described with a double superlative: 'beautiful in form and beautiful in appearance' (yefat-to'ar vifat mar'eh) — the same double-beauty phrase used of Joseph (Gen 39:6). Rachel's beauty is overwhelming and unambiguous.
verses.doctrinal_note: The contrast between Leah and Rachel anticipates the great theological paradox of election: God consistently chooses the unexpected, the overlooked, the 'unattractive' one. Leah — whose eyes are 'weak' — will be the one through whom the priestly line (Levi) and the royal-Messianic line (Judah) will come. Jacob chose Rachel; God chose Leah. This pattern runs through all of Scripture: Esau over Jacob, Jacob over Esau, Joseph over his brothers, David over his brothers. God's elections overturn human preference.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob loved Rachel; and said, I will serve thee seven years for Rachel thy younger daughter.
verses.original_text: וַיֶּאֱהַב יַעֲקֹב אֶת־רָחֵל וַיֹּאמֶר אֶעֱבָד לְךָ שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים בְּרָחֵל בִּתְּךָ הַקְּטַנָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vaye'ehav Yaakov et-Rachel vayomer e'evad lekha sheva shanim beRachel bittekha hakkettannah.
verses.study_note: 'Jacob loved Rachel' — the first use of 'ahav' (love) in the Jacob narrative, and one of the most profound love-declarations in the Old Testament. He offers seven years of labor as bride-price. In the ancient Near Eastern context, seven years of service for a bride was an extraordinarily generous offer — the standard mohar (bride-price) was monetary. Jacob, having no wealth, offers his labor. Seven (sheva) years — seven is the number of covenant completion in Hebrew thought ('shava,' to swear, comes from the same root as 'sheva,' seven). His love-covenant is sealed in the most complete number available. He names Rachel specifically as 'thy younger daughter' — making absolutely clear which daughter he is working for, a precision that will make Laban's switch in v.25 all the more calculated.

verses.kjv_text: And Laban said, It is better that I give her to thee, than that I should give her to another man: abide with me.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן טוֹב תִּתִּי אֹתָהּ לָךְ מִתִּתִּי אֹתָהּ לְאִישׁ אַחֵר שְׁבָה עִמָּדִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Lavan tov titti otah lekha mittitti otah le'ish acher shevah immadi.
verses.study_note: Laban's answer is characteristically crafty — he neither agrees clearly nor specifies which daughter. 'It is better that I give her to thee' — the pronoun 'her' (otah) is vague; he does not say 'Rachel' or repeat the younger daughter. This ambiguity is almost certainly intentional in retrospect. He concludes with 'abide with me' (shevah immadi) — a verb of settling, dwelling, committing. He gets Jacob's commitment without giving his own clearly. The contrast with Abraham's servant's negotiation (Gen 24) is total: there, everything was clear, witnessed, and covenanted; here, nothing is written, witnessed, or specific.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob served seven years for Rachel; and they seemed unto him but a few days, for the love he had to her.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעֲבֹד יַעֲקֹב בְּרָחֵל שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים וַיִּהְיוּ בְעֵינָיו כְּיָמִים אֲחָדִים בְּאַהֲבָתוֹ אֹתָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'avod Yaakov beRachel sheva shanim vayyihyu ve'einav keyamim achadim be'ahavato otah.
verses.study_note: Perhaps the most beautiful verse in the Jacob narrative, and one of the most celebrated love-statements in world literature. Seven years of manual labor — and they 'seemed unto him but a few days' (keyamim achadim — literally 'like a few days'). The Hebrew is exquisite in its paradox: the more time passed, the less it seemed; the longer the wait, the shorter it felt. This is the mystery of love — it transforms time itself. 'Keyamim achadim' uses 'achadim' (a few, several) — related to 'echad' (one), as if the seven years collapsed into a unity. The explanation is given plainly: 'for the love he had to her' (be'ahavato otah). Love was the alchemy.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is one of Scripture's great illustrations of the motivating power of love. Jacob's seven years as nothing foreshadows the truth that love bears all things (1 Cor 13:7). More profoundly, it becomes a type of Christ's eternal love for the Church — He who endured the cross 'for the joy set before him' (Heb 12:2), as one who counts the suffering as nothing for the sake of the beloved.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob said unto Laban, Give me my wife, for my days are fulfilled, that I may go in unto her.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב אֶל־לָבָן הָבָה אֶת־אִשְׁתִּי כִּי מָלְאוּ יָמָי וְאָבֹאָה אֵלֶיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yaakov el-Lavan havah et-ishti ki male'u yamai ve'avo'ah eleha.
verses.study_note: Jacob's demand is direct and entirely appropriate: 'Give me my wife' (havah et-ishti). He already calls Rachel 'my wife' — in his mind and heart the seven years have established the covenant; the formal ceremony is the completion. 'For my days are fulfilled' (ki male'u yamai) — the covenant period is complete, the debt paid. There is an urgency in the request (havah — a forceful imperative, 'Give!') that reflects seven years of patient waiting arriving at its terminus. He speaks plainly about the marital consummation ('that I may go in unto her') without embarrassment — this is his right, long deferred and honestly earned.

verses.kjv_text: And Laban gathered together all the men of the place, and made a feast.
verses.original_text: וַיֶּאֱסֹף לָבָן אֶת־כָּל־אַנְשֵׁי הַמָּקוֹם וַיַּעַשׂ מִשְׁתֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayye'esof Lavan et-kol-anshei hammakom vayya'as mishteh.
verses.study_note: Laban gathers 'all the men of the place' — the entire community is present at this wedding feast. The 'mishteh' (feast) literally means 'a drinking' — a celebration of drinking and feasting that in the ancient Near East could last seven days (v.27). The community witnesses create a problem for Jacob's later protest: the substitution was made with communal knowledge, or at least communal cover. Darkness and heavy veil (v.25) are the mechanics of the deception; the community feast is its social framework. What appears as hospitality is the preparation for betrayal.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass in the evening, that he took Leah his daughter, and brought her to him; and he went in unto her.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בָעֶרֶב וַיִּקַּח אֶת־לֵאָה בִתּוֹ וַיָּבֵא אֹתָהּ אֵלָיו וַיָּבֹא אֵלֶיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi va'erev vayyikkach et-Le'ah bitto vayyave otah elav vayavo eleha.
verses.study_note: 'In the evening' (ba'erev) — the darkness is the accomplice. This is a precise structural echo of Genesis 27: just as Jacob used darkness, goatskin disguise, and his blind father's dependence to deceive Isaac, now Laban uses evening darkness, heavy veil, and Jacob's compromised state (after a feast of drinking) to deceive him. The measure-for-measure principle of divine justice is operating — Jacob sowed deception; he reaps deception. He who made his brother cry out 'What is this thou hast done?' (27:35-36) will himself cry out the same words (29:25). Laban 'took' (vayyikkach) Leah — the same verb of taking that occurs in marriage contexts but also in the seizure of property. Laban exercises ownership over both daughters.
verses.doctrinal_note: The principle of measure-for-measure (middah k'neged middah) is one of the most consistent patterns in biblical narrative. Jacob deceived with darkness (27:27 — 'the smell of a field which the LORD hath blessed'), goat-hair, and a disguise. Now he is deceived in the dark, by a woman in a veil, after a feast. He who 'stole' the blessing will have his bride 'stolen.' This is not God's punishment in a mechanical sense — it is the built-in justice of a moral universe. Sowing and reaping are as certain as the seasons (Gal 6:7). And yet — God's purposes are not derailed: through Leah's sons, the Messiah will come.

verses.kjv_text: And Laban gave unto his daughter Leah Zilpah his maid for an handmaid.
verses.original_text: וַיִּתֵּן לָבָן לָהּ אֶת־זִלְפָּה שִׁפְחָתוֹ לְלֵאָה בִתּוֹ שִׁפְחָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayitten Lavan lah et-Zilpah shiphchato leLeah bitto shiphchah.
verses.study_note: A brief parenthetical that will have enormous consequences. Laban gives Leah a handmaid named Zilpah. This custom of giving a servant-girl to a bride was standard (as Rebekah received Deborah, Gen 24:59; Rachel will receive Bilhah, v.29). Zilpah will later bear Gad and Asher through Jacob (Gen 30:9-13) — two of the twelve tribes of Israel. A detail that reads as a minor transaction footnote actually carries the weight of tribal origins. The narrator includes it to complete the picture of the wedding and to set up the coming chapters.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that in the morning, behold, it was Leah: and he said to Laban, What is this thou hast done unto me? did not I serve with thee for Rachel? wherefore then hast thou beguiled me?
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בַבֹּקֶר וְהִנֵּה הִוא לֵאָה וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל־לָבָן מַה־זֹּאת עָשִׂיתָ לִּי הֲלֹא בְרָחֵל עָבַדְתִּי עִמָּךְ וְלָמָּה רִמִּיתָנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi babboker vehineh hi Le'ah vayomer el-Lavan mah-zot asita li halo veRachel avadti immakh velammah rimmitani.
verses.study_note: 'In the morning, behold, it was Leah' (babboker vehineh hi Le'ah) — one of the most devastating 'behold' moments in Scripture. The night's darkness is replaced by morning light, and what Jacob sees is not what he thought. 'Mah-zot asita li' — 'What is this thou hast done to me?' — is identical to the words Abimelech addressed to Abraham (Gen 20:9), and it is the very grievance Isaac could have lodged against Jacob: 'What hast thou done?' The sentence Jacob would not hear from his father, he now speaks himself. The deception has come full circle — not as revenge but as the natural fruit of the seed he planted. 'Wherefore hast thou beguiled me?' (rimmitani — from 'ramah,' to deceive, the same root as the name Miriam, possibly, and related to the idea of duplicity).

verses.kjv_text: And Laban said, It must not be so done in our country, to give the younger before the firstborn.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן לֹא־יֵעָשֶׂה כֵן בִּמְקוֹמֵנוּ לָתֵת הַצְּעִירָה לִפְנֵי הַבְּכִירָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Lavan lo-ye'aseh khen bimkomena latet hatze'irah lifnei habbekhirah.
verses.study_note: Laban's answer is masterful in its audacity. He invokes a custom ('it must not be so done in our country') that he never mentioned during the seven-year negotiation. If this custom was so well-established, why did he not mention it when Jacob offered to serve for the younger daughter? The custom may have been genuine — firstborn daughters were given precedence in some ancient cultures — but Laban's silence about it during the seven years makes its invocation now a calculated retrospective justification. He knew all along that he intended this substitution. He uses the same 'firstborn' (bekhirah) language that Jacob himself had violated in Genesis 25-27 — the elder before the younger.

verses.kjv_text: Fulfil her week, and we will give thee this also for the service which thou shalt serve with me yet seven other years.
verses.original_text: מַלֵּא שְׁבֻעַ זֹאת וְנִתְּנָה לְךָ גַּם־אֶת־זֹאת בַּעֲבֹדָה אֲשֶׁר תַּעֲבֹד עִמָּדִי עוֹד שֶׁבַע־שָׁנִים אֲחֵרוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Malle shevua zot venittenah lekha gam-et-zot ba'avodah asher ta'avod immadi od sheva-shanim acheroth.
verses.study_note: 'Fulfil her week' (malle shevua zot) — the week (shevua, from sheva/seven) of Leah's wedding feast, seven days of celebration, is customary. Laban offers Rachel as well — for another seven years of service. The audacity is complete: Laban has gotten fourteen years of labor from Jacob for the price of one daughter freely given. 'Also' (gam) — Rachel is added as a second wife before the first week is over. Jacob now has two wives, two maidservants, and a fourteen-year debt. Polygamy here is not presented as an ideal — it is the consequence of deception, and the narrative will show its painful fruit.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob did so, and fulfilled her week: and he gave him Rachel his daughter to wife also.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַשׂ יַעֲקֹב כֵּן וַיְמַלֵּא שְׁבֻעַ זֹאת וַיִּתֶּן־לוֹ אֶת־רָחֵל בִּתּוֹ לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'as Yaakov ken vayemalle shevua zot vayyitten-lo et-Rachel bitto lo le'ishah.
verses.study_note: Jacob complies — there is no other option within the social and legal framework of his time and place. He completes Leah's week and receives Rachel. The acceptance of his situation without immediate flight or counter-manipulation shows a maturity that is beginning to develop in Jacob. He cannot undo what has been done to him; he can only accept the terms and press forward. The Jacob who ran from Esau (Gen 27:43) is beginning, in this furnace of affliction, to become Israel.

verses.kjv_text: And Laban gave to Rachel his daughter Bilhah his handmaid to be her maid.
verses.original_text: וַיִּתֵּן לָבָן לְרָחֵל בִּתּוֹ אֶת־בִּלְהָה שִׁפְחָתוֹ לָהּ לְשִׁפְחָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayitten Lavan leRachel bitto et-Bilhah shiphchato lah leshiphchah.
verses.study_note: As he gave Zilpah to Leah (v.24), Laban gives Bilhah to Rachel. Bilhah will later bear Dan and Naphtali through Jacob (Gen 30:3-8) — two more of the twelve tribes. Four women — Leah, Rachel, Zilpah, Bilhah — are now in Jacob's household, and from these four will come the twelve sons who become the twelve tribes of Israel. What appears as a domestic arrangement is the founding of a nation. Providence works through painful, imperfect, even sinful human situations to accomplish its redemptive purposes.

verses.kjv_text: And he went in also unto Rachel, and he loved also Rachel more than Leah, and served with him yet seven other years.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא גַּם אֶל־רָחֵל וַיֶּאֱהַב גַּם־אֶת־רָחֵל מִלֵּאָה וַיַּעֲבֹד עִמּוֹ עוֹד שֶׁבַע־שָׁנִים אֲחֵרוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayavo gam el-Rachel vaye'ehav gam-et-Rachel miLe'ah vayya'avod immo od sheva-shanim acheroth.
verses.study_note: The narrator states plainly what the narrative has shown: Jacob loved Rachel 'more than Leah' (miLe'ah — the comparative 'more than'). This unequal love will drive the drama of chapters 29-30. The seven additional years are served — now after receiving Rachel, not in anticipation of her. The motivation shifts: before, hope drew him forward; now, love's fulfillment sustains him. And yet — it is Leah who will bear the sons through whom the covenant line continues, and it is Rachel who will die in childbirth on the road to Bethlehem (35:19), a sorrow that Jacob never fully recovers from.

verses.kjv_text: And when the LORD saw that Leah was hated, he opened her womb: but Rachel was barren.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא יְהוָה כִּי־שְׂנוּאָה לֵאָה וַיִּפְתַּח אֶת־רַחְמָהּ וְרָחֵל עֲקָרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayar YHVH ki-senu'ah Le'ah vayyiftach et-rachmah veRachel akarah.
verses.study_note: The chapter's theological climax. 'And the LORD saw' (vayar YHVH) — the same divine seeing that responded to Hagar's affliction (16:13), to the cry of the oppressed (Exod 3:7), and to human suffering throughout Scripture. 'Leah was hated' (senu'ah) — the Hebrew 'sane' can mean 'unloved' as a relative comparative (cf. Deut 21:15-17 where its legal use allows this interpretation), but the raw word is 'hated,' and the narrator does not soften it. Jacob's heart had no room for Leah. And God's response to being unloved: He 'opened her womb' (vayyiftach et-rachmah). Where human love is withheld, divine love compensates. 'Rachmah' (her womb) shares the root with 'rachamim' (compassion, mercy) — God's compassion literally opens the place of life.
verses.doctrinal_note: The theological principle of divine solidarity with the rejected runs through all of Scripture. God consistently positions Himself on the side of the unloved, the overlooked, the unexpected: Leah over Rachel, Joseph over his brothers, Moses the stutterer, David the youngest. He opens Leah's womb not because she earned it but because she is the one from whom no human tenderness is coming. This is the theology of grace: 'For ye see your calling, brethren, how that not many wise men after the flesh, not many mighty, not many noble, are called: But God hath chosen the foolish things of the world' (1 Cor 1:26-27). Leah is the unloved through whom love's greatest gift will come — the Lion of Judah.

verses.kjv_text: And Leah conceived, and bare a son, and she called his name Reuben: for she said, Surely the LORD hath looked upon my affliction; now therefore my husband will love me.
verses.original_text: וַתַּהַר לֵאָה וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן וַתִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ רְאוּבֵן כִּי אָמְרָה כִּי־רָאָה יְהוָה בְּעָנְיִי כִּי עַתָּה יֶאֱהָבַנִי אִישִׁי׃
verses.transliteration: Vattahar Le'ah vateled ven vattikra shemo Re'uven ki amrah ki-ra'ah YHVH be'onyei ki attah ye'ehavani ishi.
verses.study_note: The birth of Reuben (Re'uven) — the name is a wordplay on two Hebrew phrases: (1) 'ra'ah YHVH be'onyei' — 'the LORD has seen my affliction' (ra'ah = has seen; anyi = my affliction), and (2) the sound of 'Re'u-ven' meaning 'See, a son!' Leah's theology is profound in its simplicity: God saw. Not my husband — God. She does not say 'my husband saw' but 'the LORD hath looked' (ra'ah YHVH). And her hope: 'now therefore my husband will love me' (ye'ehavani ishi) — the desperate, heartbreaking hope of an unloved wife that a child will secure what affection could not. Reuben will grow up to be the eldest of the twelve, and through his line nothing notable comes — the hope Leah placed in him will be frustrated. God has other plans for the love she deserved.
verses.doctrinal_note: Reuben's name enshrines Leah's dual theology: divine seeing and hope for human love. This is the pattern of the unloved throughout Scripture — they see God when the beloved are too comfortable to look. Leah knows the LORD has seen because she has been forced to look to Him. Rachel, the beloved and beautiful, will struggle with barrenness before she looks to God (30:1-6). It is often the unloved, the afflicted, the Leahs of the world, who pray the most profound prayers.

verses.kjv_text: And she conceived again, and bare a son; and said, Because the LORD hath heard that I was hated, he hath therefore given me this son also: and she called his name Simeon.
verses.original_text: וַתַּהַר עוֹד וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן וַתֹּאמֶר כִּי־שָׁמַע יְהוָה כִּי־שְׂנוּאָה אָנֹכִי וַיִּתֶּן־לִי גַּם־אֶת־זֶה וַתִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ שִׁמְעוֹן׃
verses.transliteration: Vattahar od vateled ven vattomer ki-shama YHVH ki-senu'ah anokhi vayyitten-li gam-et-zeh vattikra shemo Shim'on.
verses.study_note: Simeon (Shim'on) is named from 'shama' — 'the LORD hath heard' (shama YHVH). Leah now moves from divine seeing (Reuben — 'He has seen') to divine hearing (Simeon — 'He has heard'). The pattern reflects the full attentiveness of God: He sees (v.32) and He hears (v.33). Leah's theology deepens with each birth: she is building a picture of a God who is not indifferent to her pain. 'Because the LORD hath heard that I was hated' — she identifies herself with senu'ah (hated) without self-pity but also without denial. Her situation is named honestly before God. Simeon's tribe will later receive no inheritance of land in Canaan (Joshua 19:1-9) but be scattered within Judah.

verses.kjv_text: And she conceived again, and bare a son; and said, Now this time will my husband be joined unto me, because I have born him three sons: therefore was his name called Levi.
verses.original_text: וַתַּהַר עוֹד וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן וַתֹּאמֶר עַתָּה הַפַּעַם יִלָּוֶה אִישִׁי אֵלַי כִּי־יָלַדְתִּי לוֹ שְׁלֹשָׁה בָנִים עַל־כֵּן קָרָא־שְׁמוֹ לֵוִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vattahar od vateled ven vattomer attah happa'am yillaveh ishi elai ki-yaladti lo sheloshah vanim al-ken kara-shemo Levi.
verses.study_note: Levi (Levi) is named from 'lavah' — to join, to attach. 'Now this time will my husband be joined unto me' (yillaveh ishi elai). Leah's hope with each son has been that Jacob will finally turn his heart toward her. Three sons, and still she hopes. Her naming theology has moved: with Reuben she appealed to divine seeing, with Simeon to divine hearing, with Levi she appeals to human joining. But the joining (lavah) that will matter most in Israel's history is not Levi to Jacob's heart, but Levi's tribe joining themselves to the service of God's sanctuary (Num 18:2-4). The very meaning of their tribal identity — the Levites who 'join' in temple service — comes from this mother's aching wish for her husband's love.
verses.doctrinal_note: Levi's tribe is called to priestly service — a calling that begins in Leah's unloved heart crying out for joining. The Levites will join themselves to Aaron (Num 18:2 uses this exact root: 'shall be joined unto thee'). They have no inheritance in the land (Deut 18:1-2) because the LORD is their inheritance — they who were not joined by human love are joined to God Himself. From Leah's son Levi will come Moses and Aaron, who will lead Israel from Egypt. From this chapter's pain comes the nation's greatest servants.

verses.kjv_text: And she conceived again, and bare a son: and she said, Now will I praise the LORD: therefore she called his name Judah; and left bearing.
verses.original_text: וַתַּהַר עוֹד וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן וַתֹּאמֶר הַפַּעַם אוֹדֶה אֶת־יְהוָה עַל־כֵּן קָרָאָה שְׁמוֹ יְהוּדָה וַתַּעֲמֹד מִלֶּדֶת׃
verses.transliteration: Vattahar od vateled ven vattomer happa'am odeh et-YHVH al-ken kara'ah shemo Yehudah vattagmod milledet.
verses.study_note: The most theologically significant birth of the chapter — perhaps of Genesis. Leah names her fourth son Judah (Yehudah) from 'yadah' — to praise, to give thanks, to acknowledge. And for the first time in her naming, the focus shifts entirely from herself and her longing to God alone: 'Now will I praise the LORD' (odeh et-YHVH). There is no mention of Jacob, no mention of her situation, no mention of being unloved. With Reuben she said 'God has seen my affliction.' With Simeon, 'God has heard.' With Levi, she hoped for Jacob's love. With Judah — she has arrived at pure praise. She has journeyed through affliction into doxology. This is the biblical pattern of lamentation: it begins in pain and ends in praise. And from this child — named from the praise of an unloved woman — will come the kings of Israel, the Christ of God.
verses.doctrinal_note: Judah's name is the hinge of redemptive history. The word for 'praise' (yadah) becomes the name of the tribe from which David descends (1 Sam 17:12), from which the kings of Israel come (1 Kings 12:17), and from which the Lion of Judah — Jesus Christ — takes His human lineage (Matthew 1:2-3; Revelation 5:5). The church herself is 'a royal priesthood' drawn from both Levi (the priesthood) and Judah (the kingship) — both sons of the unloved Leah. The woman whom Jacob passed over, God exalted to be the mother of worship itself. 'Judah' (Yehudah) is also the root of 'Jew' (Yehudi) — all Israel will eventually be called by the name of this son of tears. The world's salvation came through the praise of an unloved woman.

meta.overview: Genesis 29 is the pivot chapter between Jacob's two great worlds — Canaan and Haran — and forms the center of the Jacob narrative. Arriving at a well in the east, Jacob immediately meets Rachel and is overcome with love. After a month with Laban, he agrees to serve seven years for Rachel's hand. On the wedding night, Laban substitutes Leah — the great deceiver is himself deceived in the most painful and intimate way possible. The narrative is structured as a precise mirror of Jacob's earlier deception: just as Jacob used darkness and disguise to deceive his blind father, Laban uses darkness and substitution to deceive Jacob. The chapter closes with God's sovereign response to Leah's rejection — He opens her womb — and through her will come Levi (the priestly tribe) and Judah (the royal tribe, from which Christ descends). Every element of this chapter is freighted with typological and redemptive significance: the well as a betrothal scene, Rachel as the beloved for whom a price is paid, Leah as the unexpected chosen, and the birth of Judah as the culmination toward which all of Genesis has been moving.
meta.theme: Providence, Deception Repaid, Sovereign Love for the Rejected
meta.keyWordMeaning: To love — Jacob's love for Rachel is the governing word of the chapter, appearing 5 times (vv.18,20,30,31,32). But God's love for the unloved Leah is the chapter's deeper truth.
outline: Jacob Arrives at the Well (vv.1-3): The well in Haran, a gathering place of shepherds
Jacob Meets Rachel (vv.4-12): Recognition, the stone, the kiss and tears
Jacob with Laban (vv.13-20): One month passes; seven years promised for Rachel
The Wedding Night Deception (vv.21-27): Laban substitutes Leah; Jacob confronts him
Jacob Receives Rachel — Fourteen Years Total (vv.28-30): Rachel and Leah, two wives
God Sees Leah's Rejection (vv.31-35): The womb opened; Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah born

verses.kjv_text: And when Rachel saw that she bare Jacob no children, Rachel envied her sister; and said unto Jacob, Give me children, or else I die.
verses.original_text: וַתֵּרֶא רָחֵל כִּי לֹא יָלְדָה לְיַעֲקֹב וַתְּקַנֵּא רָחֵל בַּאֲחֹתָהּ וַתֹּאמֶר אֶל־יַעֲקֹב הָבָה לִּי בָנִים וְאִם־אַיִן מֵתָה אָנֹכִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vattera Rachel ki lo yaledah leYa'akov vatekanne Rachel ba'achotah vatomer el-Ya'akov havah li vanim ve'im-ayin metah anokhi.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with bitter contrast. Rachel — the beloved wife, the beautiful one (29:17) — is barren. Leah — the unloved wife — has borne four sons. Rachel's 'envy' (vatekanne) is the same root as 'jealous' (El kanna — Exod 20:5, describing God Himself). The word carries not just envy but consuming burning zeal. Her demand 'Give me children or else I die' (havah li vanim ve'im-ayin metah anokhi) is raw, desperate ultimatum. She speaks as if Jacob were God — which Jacob himself immediately corrects. Barrenness in the ancient Near East was social death — Rachel feels it acutely. Yet her crisis will become the occasion for God's sovereign display. The women Rachel envied and would eventually envy her (Leah and Zilpah, then Rachel) are both instruments of the covenant.
verses.doctrinal_note: The barrenness of the matriarchs (Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel) is a recurring pattern of divine sovereignty: God closes the womb so that when He opens it, the child born is manifestly His gift, not natural outcome. Joseph and Benjamin — Rachel's sons — are especially associated with divine favor. The pattern points forward to the ultimate barren-made-fruitful: the virgin birth.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob’s anger was kindled against Rachel: and he said, Am I in God’s stead, who hath withheld from thee the fruit of the womb?
verses.original_text: וַיִּחַר־אַף יַעֲקֹב בְּרָחֵל וַיֹּאמֶר הֲתַחַת אֱלֹהִים אָנֹכִי אֲשֶׁר מָנַע מִמֵּךְ פְּרִי בָטֶן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyichar-af Ya'akov beRachel vayomer hatachat Elohim anokhi asher mana mimmekh peri vaten.
verses.study_note: Jacob's anger is surprising — the only recorded moment of anger between him and Rachel in the narrative. But his rebuke is theologically precise: 'Am I in God's stead?' (hatachat Elohim anokhi) — he refuses to be made a substitute for God. He identifies clearly who controls the womb: it is God who 'withheld' (mana — the same word used of Abraham withholding Isaac in 22:12) the fruit of the womb. This moment reveals Jacob's genuine faith: he knows that fertility is God's sovereign gift, not a human achievement. His anger may also be wounded by the implicit accusation that he has somehow failed her. Yet his theology is correct even as his pastoral manner falls short — he answers her theological error accurately but without compassion.

verses.kjv_text: And she said, Behold my maid Bilhah, go in unto her; and she shall bear upon my knees, that I may also have children by her.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר הִנֵּה אֲמָתִי בִלְהָה בֹּא אֵלֶיהָ וְתֵלֵד עַל־בִּרְכַּי וְאִבָּנֶה גַם־אָנֹכִי מִמֶּנָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vatomer hinneh amati Bilhah bo eleyha veteled al-birkai ve'ibbaneh gam-anokhi mimmenah.
verses.study_note: Rachel employs the surrogate mother custom well-attested in ancient Near Eastern law (cf. Sarah and Hagar in Gen 16). 'Bear upon my knees' (veteled al-birkai) is the adoption formula — the child born would legally be Rachel's through the surrogate. 'That I may also have children' (ve'ibbaneh — literally 'I may be built') is crucial: the word 'ibbaneh' shares its root with 'ben' (son) and 'banah' (to build). In Hebrew thought, to have sons was to 'be built' — children construct the family. Bilhah means 'modesty' or 'troubled.' Rachel acts from faith in a custom rather than faith in God — she does not pray (contrast Hannah in 1 Sam 1). Yet God will use even this.

verses.kjv_text: And she gave him Bilhah her handmaid to wife: and Jacob went in unto her.
verses.original_text: וַתִּתֶּן־לוֹ אֶת־בִּלְהָה שִׁפְחָתָהּ לְאִשָּׁה וַיָּבֹא אֵלֶיהָ יַעֲקֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Vattitten-lo et-Bilhah shifchatah le'ishah vayyavo eleyha Ya'akov.
verses.study_note: Rachel gives Bilhah as a wife (le'ishah — an actual wife, not merely a concubine, though with lesser status). Jacob complies without recorded objection — as he complied when Laban switched Leah (29:25-28). The pattern of Jacob as a passive actor in his marital circumstances is consistent. He now has three wives: Leah (with four sons), Bilhah (about to bear), and Rachel (still barren). The family structure is complex and fraught — yet God will build His covenant people through it.

verses.kjv_text: And Bilhah conceived, and bare Jacob a son.
verses.original_text: וַתַּהַר בִּלְהָה וַתֵּלֶד לְיַעֲקֹב בֵּן׃
verses.transliteration: Vattahar Bilhah vateled leYa'akov ben.
verses.study_note: One of the most understated verses in the patriarchal narrative — the announcement of Bilhah's conception and birth is stripped to bare essentials. No theological commentary, no joy expressed, just the fact. This brevity itself communicates: this is not the son of promise. God had not opened Rachel's womb — a surrogate had been substituted. Yet even this child will be numbered among the tribes of Israel and receive a blessing from dying Jacob (Gen 49:16-17).

verses.kjv_text: And Rachel said, God hath judged me, and hath also heard my voice, and hath given me a son: therefore called she his name Dan.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר רָחֵל דָּנַנִּי אֱלֹהִים וְגַם שָׁמַע בְּקֹלִי וַיִּתֶּן־לִי בֵּן עַל־כֵּן קָרְאָה שְׁמוֹ דָּן׃
verses.transliteration: Vatomer Rachel dananni Elohim vegam shama beqoli vayyitten-li ben al-ken kar'ah shemo Dan.
verses.study_note: Rachel names the child with a theological declaration. 'God hath judged me' (dananni Elohim) — the verb is 'din' (to judge, to vindicate). Rachel reads the birth as God's vindication of her case against Leah, as if God has ruled in her favor in their rivalry. The name 'Dan' (H1835) means 'judge' or 'he judged' — it encodes this moment of perceived vindication. Rachel also acknowledges 'he heard my voice' (shama beqoli) — though her 'voice' was not recorded as a prayer but as a demand (v.1). God heard even her desperate cry. Dan's tribe later becomes associated with judgment and, in Revelation's tribal list, is notably absent (Rev 7:4-8), possibly due to their idolatry (Judges 18).

verses.kjv_text: And Bilhah Rachel’s maid conceived again, and bare Jacob a second son.
verses.original_text: וַתַּהַר עוֹד וַתֵּלֶד בִּלְהָה שִׁפְחַת רָחֵל בֵּן שֵׁנִי לְיַעֲקֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Vattahar od vateled Bilhah shifchat Rachel ben sheni leYa'akov.
verses.study_note: The terse narrative continues: Bilhah conceives and bears a second son. The phrase 'Rachel's maid' (shifchat Rachel) rather than 'his wife' (as in v.4) subtly signals the narrator's perspective — the child is legally Rachel's through the surrogate arrangement, even as he is biologically Jacob's. Two sons from Bilhah will become two of the twelve tribes.

verses.kjv_text: And Rachel said, With great wrestlings have I wrestled with my sister, and I have prevailed: and she called his name Naphtali.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר רָחֵל נַפְתּוּלֵי אֱלֹהִים נִפְתַּלְתִּי עִם־אֲחֹתִי גַּם־יָכֹלְתִּי וַתִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ נַפְתָּלִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vatomer Rachel naftulei Elohim niftalti im-achoti gam-yakholti vatikra shemo Naftali.
verses.study_note: 'Naftulei Elohim' — literally 'wrestlings of God' or 'mighty wrestlings.' The word 'naftulei' is related to 'pathal' (to twist, to wrestle), and the intensifying construction 'naftulei Elohim' uses 'Elohim' as a superlative (like 'mountains of God' = towering mountains, or 'a great city to God' in Jonah 3:3). Rachel declares she has wrestled 'mightily' against her sister. This prefigures Jacob's own wrestling with God at Peniel (32:22-28) — the family is defined by wrestling with impossible situations and persisting. The name 'Naphtali' (H5321) means 'my wrestling' or 'my struggle.' The tribe of Naphtali is later described as a 'hind let loose' (Gen 49:21), a beautiful, swift deer — freedom emerging from struggle.

verses.kjv_text: When Leah saw that she had left bearing, she took Zilpah her maid, and gave her Jacob to wife.
verses.original_text: וַתֵּרֶא לֵאָה כִּי עָמְדָה מִלֶּדֶת וַתִּקַּח אֶת־זִלְפָּה שִׁפְחָתָהּ וַתִּתֵּן אֹתָהּ לְיַעֲקֹב לְאִשָּׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vattera Leah ki amedah milldet vattikkach et-Zilpah shifchatah vattitten otah leYa'akov le'ishah.
verses.study_note: Leah responds to Rachel's surrogate strategy with her own. 'She had left bearing' (ki amedah milldet) — Leah's womb has temporarily closed after four sons. The same verb 'amad' (to stand still, cease) is used of the sun standing still in Joshua 10:13. Leah does not pray (contrast Hannah); she acts strategically, matching Rachel's move. Zilpah (H2153) means 'a fragrant drop' or 'drooping.' The competitive dynamic intensifies: two wives with two surrogates, all bearing sons for Jacob. Yet from this entirely human competition, God is building twelve tribes. Zilpah will contribute Gad and Asher — two more pillars of Israel.

verses.kjv_text: And Zilpah Leah’s maid bare Jacob a son.
verses.original_text: וַתֵּלֶד זִלְפָּה שִׁפְחַת לֵאָה לְיַעֲקֹב בֵּן׃
verses.transliteration: Vateled Zilpah shifchat Leah leYa'akov ben.
verses.study_note: The seventh son of Jacob is born through Zilpah. The narrator again uses the brief, understated formula — no theological commentary, just the fact of birth. The accumulation of sons is being counted toward twelve — the sacred number that will constitute the nation. Jacob has now fathered seven of his twelve sons.

verses.kjv_text: And Leah said, A troop cometh: and she called his name Gad.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר לֵאָה בגד וַתִּקְרָא אֶת־שְׁמוֹ גָּד׃
verses.transliteration: Vatomer Leah begad vatikra et-shemo Gad.
verses.study_note: The naming of Gad is textually intriguing. The Masoretic text has 'begad' (בגד) which some read as two words: 'ba gad' meaning 'fortune comes' or 'good fortune.' 'Gad' (H1410) means 'fortune' or 'a troop.' Leah exclaims her sense of fortunate abundance — more sons, even through her surrogate. The name Gad is also associated with the Canaanite god of fortune (cf. Isaiah 65:11 — 'those who set a table for Gad and fill cups of wine for Meni'). Moses' blessing over Gad in Deuteronomy 33:20-21 describes him as 'a lion that tears the arm with the crown of the head' — a fierce, military tribe that settled east of the Jordan.

verses.kjv_text: And Zilpah Leah’s maid bare Jacob a second son.
verses.original_text: וַתֵּלֶד זִלְפָּה שִׁפְחַת לֵאָה בֵּן שֵׁנִי לְיַעֲקֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Vateled Zilpah shifchat Leah ben sheni leYa'akov.
