verses.study_note: Zilpah bears a second son — the eighth of Jacob's eventual twelve. The pattern mirrors Bilhah's double contribution (vv. 5-8). Each handmaid contributes two sons. The symmetry is notable: four mothers (Leah, Rachel, Bilhah, Zilpah) each bear or claim children — yet God oversees the entire process to build His people.

verses.kjv_text: And Leah said, Happy am I, for the daughters will call me blessed: and she called his name Asher.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר לֵאָה בְּאָשְׁרִי כִּי אִשְּׁרוּנִי בָּנוֹת וַתִּקְרָא אֶת־שְׁמוֹ אָשֵׁר׃
verses.transliteration: Vatomer Leah be'oshri ki isheruni banot vatikra et-shemo Asher.
verses.study_note: 'Happy am I' (be'oshri) — Leah uses 'osher' (happiness, blessedness) from the root 'ashar' (to walk straight, to be happy). This is the same root as the Beatitudes' blessedness ('Blessed/Happy is the man' — Psalm 1:1). 'The daughters will call me blessed' (isheruni banot) — an anticipation of social recognition and respect. This language is strikingly similar to Mary's Magnificat: 'all generations shall call me blessed' (Luke 1:48). Asher means 'happy' or 'blessed,' and his tribe is associated with abundance of food (Gen 49:20 — 'his bread shall be fat'). In the New Testament, the prophetess Anna is from the tribe of Asher (Luke 2:36) — she who 'gave thanks' and 'spake of him' when she saw the infant Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: And Reuben went in the days of wheat harvest, and found mandrakes in the field, and brought them unto his mother Leah. Then Rachel said to Leah, Give me, I pray thee, of thy son’s mandrakes.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּלֶךְ רְאוּבֵן בִּימֵי קְצִיר חִטִּים וַיִּמְצָא דוּדָאִים בַּשָּׂדֶה וַיָּבֵא אֹתָם אֶל־לֵאָה אִמּוֹ וַתֹּאמֶר רָחֵל אֶל־לֵאָה תְּנִי־נָא לִי מִדּוּדָאֵי בְּנֵךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyeelekh Re'uven bimei ketzir chittim vayyimtza dudaim bassadeh vayyave otam el-Leah immo vatomer Rachel el-Leah teni-na li middudaei benekh.
verses.study_note: Reuben — the eldest son, probably 4-5 years old — finds mandrakes (dudaim, H1736) during wheat harvest (late spring, April-May). The mandrake (Mandragora officinarum) is a plant with yellow, fragrant berries; its forked root resembles a human figure. In the ancient world it was regarded as an aphrodisiac and fertility charm — the popular belief that it aided conception is why Rachel desires it so desperately. The word 'dudaim' is related to 'dod' (beloved, love) — Song of Solomon 7:13 associates mandrakes with romantic love. Rachel's request reveals her continued desperation and her reliance on folk remedies rather than prayer. The irony is sharp: Rachel will receive the mandrakes but Leah will conceive; Rachel must wait for God's timing, not mandrakes.

verses.kjv_text: And she said unto her, Is it a small matter that thou hast taken my husband? and wouldest thou take away my son’s mandrakes also? And Rachel said, Therefore he shall lie with thee to night for thy son’s mandrakes.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר לָהּ הַמְעַט קַחְתֵּךְ אֶת־אִישִׁי וְלָקַחַת גַּם אֶת־דּוּדָאֵי בְּנִי וַתֹּאמֶר רָחֵל לָכֵן יִשְׁכַּב עִמָּךְ הַלַּיְלָה תַּחַת דּוּדָאֵי בְנֵךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vatomer lah hame'at kachtekh et-ishi velakachat gam et-dudaei beni vatomer Rachel lakhen yishkav immakh hallaylah tachat dudaei venekh.
verses.study_note: Leah's pain erupts: 'Is it a small matter that thou hast taken my husband?' The word 'hame'at' (is it small/little?) is the same root as 'Zoar' (the little city in Gen 19:20). Leah perceives that Jacob's love for Rachel has effectively 'taken' him from her. The bargain struck is remarkable: Rachel trades a night of Jacob's conjugal company for mandrakes. This reverses the expected power dynamic — the beloved wife, desperate for fertility symbols, surrenders marital access to the unloved wife. Leah goes out to meet Jacob as he returns from the field: 'You must come in to me' (v.16). Jacob's willingness to be allocated between his wives reveals how passive and managed his household life has become. The irony is devastating: Rachel gets the mandrakes (the supposed fertility charm) and remains barren; Leah trades them away and immediately conceives.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob came out of the field in the evening, and Leah went out to meet him, and said, Thou must come in unto me; for surely I have hired thee with my son’s mandrakes. And he lay with her that night.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא יַעֲקֹב מִן־הַשָּׂדֶה בָּעֶרֶב וַתֵּצֵא לֵאָה לִקְרָאתוֹ וַתֹּאמֶר אֵלַי תָּבוֹא כִּי שָׂכֹר שְׂכַרְתִּיךָ בְּדוּדָאֵי בְּנִי וַיִּשְׁכַּב עִמָּהּ בַּלַּיְלָה הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo Ya'akov min-hassadeh ba'erev vateze Leah likrato vatomer elai tavo ki sakhor sekharticha bedudaei beni vayyishkav immah ballaylah hu.
verses.study_note: 'Thou must come in unto me' (elai tavo — literally 'to me you shall come') — Leah commands her husband. The phrase 'I have hired thee' (sakhor sekharticha) uses the emphatic infinitive absolute — she declares with legal finality: Jacob has been 'hired' as a husband for a night with mandrakes as the price. The word 'sakhor' (hired) anticipates the name Issachar ('there is hire/reward' v.18). The dignity of both parties suffers in this transaction — Jacob is reduced to a commodity, Leah to a purchaser of her own husband's attentions. Yet God's purposes run through even this. 'He lay with her that night' — the outcome Jacob submits to.

verses.kjv_text: And God hearkened unto Leah, and she conceived, and bare Jacob the fifth son.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁמַע אֱלֹהִים אֶל־לֵאָה וַתַּהַר וַתֵּלֶד לְיַעֲקֹב בֵּן חֲמִישִׁי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishma Elohim el-Leah vattahar vateled leYa'akov ben chamishi.
verses.study_note: The narrator inserts the key phrase: 'God hearkened unto Leah' (vayyishma Elohim el-Leah). This is remarkable — there is no recorded prayer from Leah in this transaction. She bargained, she commanded Jacob, she acquired mandrakes for Rachel. Yet God hears and responds. The mandrakes had nothing to do with it. God's sovereign choice to open Leah's womb again is a quiet rebuke to the whole transaction: fertility is His gift, not commerce. He had consistently heard Leah even when she was not praying (cf. 29:33 — 'the LORD heard that I was hated'). Leah's faithfulness is rewarded not through her strategy but through divine compassion.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's hearing is not conditioned on perfect faith or formal prayer. He heard Leah's situation, Hagar's cry, Rachel's eventual prayer, and Israel's groan in Egypt (Exod 2:24). The open ear of God toward those who suffer and are overlooked is a consistent revelation of His character.

verses.kjv_text: And Leah said, God hath given me my hire, because I have given my maiden to my husband: and she called his name Issachar.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר לֵאָה נָתַן אֱלֹהִים שְׂכָרִי אֲשֶׁר נָתַתִּי שִׁפְחָתִי לְאִישִׁי וַתִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ יִשָּׂשכָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vatomer Leah natan Elohim sekhari asher natatti shifchati le'ishi vatikra shemo Yissachar.
verses.study_note: Leah names this son Issachar — 'there is reward' (yesh sachar) or 'man of hire/reward.' She interprets his birth as God's payment (sachar) for her sacrifice in giving Zilpah to Jacob. Her theology is imperfect — she credits her own act of giving Zilpah — but the name nonetheless encodes a true principle: God rewards faithful service. The tribe of Issachar later becomes famous for wisdom: 'the children of Issachar, which were men that had understanding of the times, to know what Israel ought to do' (1 Chron 12:32).

verses.kjv_text: And Leah conceived again, and bare Jacob the sixth son.
verses.original_text: וַתַּהַר עוֹד לֵאָה וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן שִׁשִּׁי לְיַעֲקֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Vattahar od Leah vateled ben shishi leYa'akov.
verses.study_note: Leah conceives a sixth time — an extraordinary fruitfulness. She has now personally borne six sons (Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun) and contributed Zilpah who bore two more. Six sons by one mother is without parallel among the patriarchal wives. This sixth birth will be accompanied by Leah's final recorded speech in the narrative — a declaration of hope for marital restoration (v.20).

verses.kjv_text: And Leah said, God hath endued me with a good dowry; now will my husband dwell with me, because I have born him six sons: and she called his name Zebulun.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר לֵאָה זְבָדַנִי אֱלֹהִים אֹתִי זֵבֶד טוֹב הַפַּעַם יִזְבְּלֵנִי אִישִׁי כִּי יָלַדְתִּי לוֹ שִׁשָּׁה בָנִים וַתִּקְרָא אֶת־שְׁמוֹ זְבֻלוּן׃
verses.transliteration: Vatomer Leah zevadani Elohim oti zevad tov happa'am yizbbeleni ishi ki yaladti lo shishah vanim vatikra et-shemo Zevulun.
verses.study_note: Leah plays on two Hebrew roots in naming Zebulun: 'zavad' (to endow with a gift) and 'zaval' (to dwell, to honor). 'God hath endued me' (zevadani Elohim) — He has given me a good gift-portion. 'Now will my husband dwell with me' (yizbbeleni) — after six sons, Leah anticipates the marital closeness she has longed for since 'the LORD saw that Leah was hated' (29:31). Her hope is poignant: she still measures her worth by Jacob's attention. The name Zebulun ('honor' or 'dwelling') encodes this double meaning. Zebulun's territory in northern Canaan becomes the maritime tribe: 'Zebulun shall dwell at the haven of the sea' (Gen 49:13).

verses.kjv_text: And afterwards she bare a daughter, and called her name Dinah.
verses.original_text: וְאַחַר יָלְדָה בַּת וַתִּקְרָא אֶת־שְׁמָהּ דִּינָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'achar yaledah bat vatikra et-shmah Dinah.
verses.study_note: Dinah — the only daughter of Jacob named in the narrative. The narrator mentions her without explanation of her name's etymology (though Dinah likely derives from 'din' — to judge, the same root as Dan, suggesting 'justice' or 'vindicated'). The brevity of her introduction belies her importance: Dinah's story will erupt in Genesis 34, triggering Simeon and Levi's violent revenge on Shechem. She is listed among those who went to Egypt (Gen 46:15). The inclusion of a daughter in the birth register, when only sons are typically counted for tribal purposes, signals the narrative importance she will carry. In rabbinic tradition, Dinah's daughter Asenath (by Shechem) became Joseph's wife — a tradition suggested by some scholars though not confirmed in the text.

verses.kjv_text: And God remembered Rachel, and God hearkened to her, and opened her womb.
verses.original_text: וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים אֶת־רָחֵל וַיִּשְׁמַע אֵלֶיהָ אֱלֹהִים וַיִּפְתַּח אֶת־רַחְמָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyizkor Elohim et-Rachel vayyishma eleyha Elohim vayyiftach et-rachmah.
verses.study_note: The theological climax of the entire chapter — and one of the great sentences in the patriarchal narrative: 'God remembered Rachel.' 'Remembered' (vayyizkor) is the covenant word par excellence: God remembered Noah (8:1), God remembered Abraham and sent Lot out (19:29), God remembered His covenant and heard Israel groaning (Exod 2:24). To 'remember' in Hebrew is not to recall something forgotten but to act decisively on a covenant obligation. God has not forgotten Rachel — He has been working. Now He acts. Two verbs describe His action: 'hearkened' (vayyishma — He actively listened) and 'opened' (vayyiftach — He unlocked). The womb is God's to open and close. Every birth is ultimately His gift. Rachel's years of barrenness serve to make unmistakably clear: Joseph is God's child of promise, not the product of Rachel's strategies or mandrakes.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's 'remembering' is not passive recollection but active covenant intervention. He remembered Rachel after years of barrenness, Noah after the flood, Israel after 400 years of slavery, and ultimately — He remembers us in Christ. The womb that He opens and closes illustrates His absolute sovereignty over life. Joseph, born of this divine opening, becomes the great type of Christ: the beloved son, betrayed by his brothers, descending into a pit, rising to save the very ones who rejected him.

verses.kjv_text: And she conceived, and bare a son; and said, God hath taken away my reproach:
verses.original_text: וַתַּהַר וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן וַתֹּאמֶר אָסַף אֱלֹהִים אֶת־חֶרְפָּתִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vattahar vateled ben vatomer asaf Elohim et-cherpati.
verses.study_note: Rachel conceives and gives birth — and her first words interpret the event theologically: 'God hath taken away my reproach' (asaf Elohim et-cherpati). 'Reproach' (cherpah) is the social shame of barrenness — it implies divine disfavor, unworthiness, judgment. Rachel has borne this reproach for the entire chapter. Now God 'takes it away' (asaf — to gather, collect, remove). The word 'asaf' means to gather or take away — the same root becomes the first element in the name Joseph (Yosef), as Rachel immediately explains in v.24. Elizabeth uses nearly identical language when she conceives John: 'Thus hath the Lord dealt with me in the days wherein he looked on me, to take away my reproach among men' (Luke 1:25).

verses.kjv_text: And she called his name Joseph; and said, The LORD shall add to me another son.
verses.original_text: וַתִּקְרָא אֶת־שְׁמוֹ יוֹסֵף לֵאמֹר יֹסֵף יְהוָה לִי בֵּן אַחֵר׃
verses.transliteration: Vatikra et-shemo Yosef lemor yosef YHVH li ben acher.
verses.study_note: The naming of Joseph is double-layered, reflecting both the past and the future. (1) Looking back: 'asaf' — God has gathered away the reproach (v.23). (2) Looking forward: 'yosef' — 'may the LORD add another son' (yosef YHVH li ben acher). The name is a prayer of faith embedded in a name — Rachel does not just name what has happened, she names what she believes will happen. She will die giving birth to that promised second son, Benjamin (35:16-19). The name Joseph (Yosef — H3130) from 'yasaf' (to add, increase) becomes one of the most theologically dense names in Scripture. Joseph the patriarch is the supreme Old Testament type of Christ: beloved son, stripped and sold by brothers, imprisoned unjustly, raised to the right hand of power, dispensing bread to a starving world, and ultimately forgiving and restoring those who betrayed him.
verses.doctrinal_note: Joseph as Type of Christ: Beloved son (Gen 37:3 / Matt 3:17); stripped of his robe (Gen 37:23 / Matt 27:28); sold for silver (Gen 37:28 / Matt 26:15); falsely accused (Gen 39:14-18 / Matt 26:59-61); imprisoned between two criminals (Gen 40:1-3 / Luke 23:32-33); exalted to the right hand of power (Gen 41:39-40 / Acts 2:33); saves his people through bread (Gen 41:53-57 / John 6:35); forgives those who betrayed him (Gen 45:1-15 / Luke 23:34). No other figure in the Old Testament has as many typological correspondences with Christ.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when Rachel had born Joseph, that Jacob said unto Laban, Send me away, that I may go unto mine own place, and to my country.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כַּאֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה רָחֵל אֶת־יוֹסֵף וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב אֶל־לָבָן שַׁלְּחֵנִי וְאֵלְכָה אֶל־מְקוֹמִי וּלְאַרְצִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi ka'asher yaledah Rachel et-Yosef vayomer Ya'akov el-Lavan shallcheni ve'elkhah el-mekomi ule'artzi.
verses.study_note: Joseph's birth is the trigger for Jacob's departure request. Jacob has served his contracted fourteen years (seven for Leah, seven for Rachel — 29:18-27). Now with Joseph born — the son of the beloved wife, perhaps the sign to Jacob that his family is now complete enough to move — he requests release: 'Send me away' (shallcheni). This is the same verb used when Pharaoh would later refuse to send Israel away, and when God commissioned Moses to demand release (Exod 5:1 — 'Let my people go'). Jacob longs to return to 'my place and my country' — the land of Canaan, the land of the covenant promise, the land of his fathers.

verses.kjv_text: Give me my wives and my children, for whom I have served thee, and let me go: for thou knowest my service which I have done thee.
verses.original_text: תְּנָה אֶת־נָשַׁי וְאֶת־יְלָדַי אֲשֶׁר עָבַדְתִּי אֹתְךָ בָּהֵן וְאֵלֵכָה כִּי אַתָּה יָדַעְתָּ אֶת־עֲבֹדָתִי אֲשֶׁר עֲבַדְתִּיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Tennah et-nashai ve'et-yeladai asher avadeti otkha bahen ve'elekhah ki attah yadata et-avodati asher avadtikha.
verses.study_note: Jacob asserts his claims: he has served for the wives and children, and Laban has no legal hold over him beyond his contracted term. 'Thou knowest my service' — Jacob appeals to Laban's own knowledge of his faithfulness as a shepherd. The word 'avadeti' (I have served) is used six times in chapters 29-30, echoing Israel's later 'service' in Egypt. Jacob's service to Laban foreshadows Israel's servitude to Pharaoh — and in both cases, the people will leave with great wealth (Gen 15:14; 30:43).

verses.kjv_text: And Laban said unto him, I pray thee, if I have found favour in thine eyes, tarry: for I have learned by experience that the LORD hath blessed me for thy sake.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו לָבָן אִם־נָא מָצָאתִי חֵן בְּעֵינֶיךָ נִחַשְׁתִּי וַיְבָרֲכֵנִי יְהוָה בִּגְלָלֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer elav Laban im-na matzati chen be'einekha nichashti vayyevarakheni YHVH biglalelkha.
verses.study_note: 'I have learned by experience' (nichashti) — literally 'I have divined/observed by omens.' Laban uses the language of divination (nachash — to practice sorcery, to observe signs; cf. Gen 44:5). This reveals Laban's pagan background: he has 'divined' or 'read the signs' that YHVH has blessed him because of Jacob. Even this unbeliever recognizes divine blessing working through Jacob. 'The LORD hath blessed me for thy sake' (YHVH... biglalekha) — this is the Abrahamic promise working: 'in thee shall all families of the earth be blessed' (Gen 12:3). Laban is experiencing the overflow of covenant blessing through proximity to the covenant man.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Appoint me thy wages, and I will give it.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמַר נָקְבָה שְׂכָרְךָ עָלַי וְאֶתֵּנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomar nokevah sekharkhah alai ve'ettennah.
verses.study_note: 'Appoint' (nokevah — from 'nakav,' to designate, to pierce, to name specifically) — Laban offers Jacob the chance to name his own wages. 'Nakav' is the same root used for Nabal ('pierced one') and for specific designation. Laban's offer appears generous but his subsequent conduct (v.35-36) reveals that he will immediately manipulate the agreement. Jacob has been outwitted by Laban before (the Leah-Rachel switch, 29:25) — he is now alert to Laban's methods.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto him, Thou knowest how I have served thee, and how thy cattle was with me.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו אַתָּה יָדַעְתָּ אֵת אֲשֶׁר עֲבַדְתִּיךָ וְאֵת אֲשֶׁר הָיָה מִקְנְךָ אִתִּי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer elav attah yadata et asher avadtikha ve'et asher hayah miknekha itti.
verses.study_note: Jacob's negotiating position begins with establishing the record. 'Thou knowest' (attah yadata) — he puts the burden of acknowledgment on Laban's own knowledge. Jacob's service record cannot be disputed; Laban himself has acknowledged it in v.27. This is wise negotiation: begin from acknowledged facts. The word 'miknekha' (your cattle) encompasses all Laban's livestock — the flock Jacob has tended faithfully for fourteen years.

verses.kjv_text: For it was little which thou hadst before I came, and it is now increased unto a multitude; and the LORD hath blessed thee since my coming: and now when shall I provide for mine own house also?
verses.original_text: כִּי מְעַט אֲשֶׁר הָיָה לְךָ לְפָנַי וַיִּפְרֹץ לָרֹב וַיְבָרֶךְ יְהוָה אֹתְךָ לְרַגְלִי וְעַתָּה מָתַי אֶעֱשֶׂה גַם־אָנֹכִי לְבֵיתִי׃
verses.transliteration: Ki me'at asher hayah lekha lefanai vayyifrotz larov vayyevarekh YHVH otkha leragli ve'attah matai e'eseh gam-anokhi leveti.
verses.study_note: 'The LORD hath blessed thee since my coming' (leragli — literally 'at my foot') — Jacob credits YHVH, not his own skill, for Laban's prosperity. 'At my foot' suggests by following after me, or in my footsteps. The word 'vayyifrotz' (increased unto a multitude) shares its root with 'Perez' (break through) and anticipates Jacob's own 'breaking through' (vayyifrotz) in v.43. Jacob's rhetorical question 'when shall I provide for mine own house?' is the heart of his appeal — fourteen years of labor, and he has nothing for his own family.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, What shall I give thee? And Jacob said, Thou shalt not give me any thing: if thou wilt do this thing for me, I will again feed and keep thy flock.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מָה אֶתֶּן־לָּךְ וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב לֹא תִתֶּן־לִי מְאוּמָה אִם־תַּעֲשֶׂה לִי הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה אָשׁוּבָה אֶרְעֶה צֹאנְךָ אֶשְׁמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer mah etten-lakh vayomer Ya'akov lo titten-li me'umah im-ta'aseh li haddavar hazzeh ashuva er'eh tzonkha eshmor.
verses.study_note: Jacob's reply is strategically brilliant: 'Thou shalt not give me any thing' — he declines a cash gift, which Laban could control and reduce. Instead, Jacob proposes a performance-based arrangement where his wages emerge directly from the flock. This makes Laban believe he retains control while Jacob's proposal actually leverages God's blessing on his labor. 'I will again feed and keep thy flock' (ashuva er'eh... eshmor) — Jacob commits to continued faithful service under a new arrangement. He remains the shepherd; he seeks only the irregular or marked offspring.

verses.kjv_text: I will pass through all thy flock to day, removing from thence all the speckled and spotted cattle, and all the brown cattle among the sheep, and the spotted and speckled among the goats: and of such shall be my hire.
verses.original_text: אֶעֱבֹר בְּכָל צֹאנְךָ הַיּוֹם הָסֵר מִשָּׁם כָּל שֶׂה נָקֹד וְטָלוּא וְכָל שֶׂה חוּם בַּכְּשָׂבִים וְטָלוּא וְנָקֹד בָּעִזִּים וְהָיָה שְׂכָרִי׃
verses.transliteration: E'evor bekhol tzonkha hayyom haser misham kol seh nakod vetalua vekhol seh chum bakkesavim vetalua venakod ba'izzim vehayah sekhari.
verses.study_note: Jacob specifies three categories: (1) speckled (nakod — spotted irregularly), (2) spotted/patchy (talua — dappled with patches), (3) brown/dark (chum — in the sheep). These marked animals would be the minority in a typical flock of solid-colored sheep and goats in the Near East. Normally white sheep and black goats predominate — so Jacob's portion would initially appear small. The genius of Jacob's proposal is that he relies on God to produce what would otherwise be rare — and God confirms this plan in the dream of Genesis 31:10-13.

verses.kjv_text: So shall my righteousness answer for me in time to come, when it shall come for my hire before thy face: every one that is not speckled and spotted among the goats, and brown among the sheep, that shall be counted stolen with me.
verses.original_text: וְעָנְתָה בִּי צִדְקָתִי בְּיוֹם מָחָר כִּי תָבוֹא עַל שְׂכָרִי לְפָנֶיךָ כֹּל אֲשֶׁר אֵינֶנּוּ נָקֹד וְטָלוּא בָּעִזִּים וְחוּם בַּכְּשָׂבִים גָּנוּב הוּא אִתִּי׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'antah bi tzidkati beyom machar ki tavo al-sekhari lefanekha kol asher einennu nakod vetalua ba'izzim vechum bakkesavim ganub hu itti.
verses.study_note: 'My righteousness shall answer for me' (ve'antah bi tzidkati) — Jacob builds a transparent accountability clause into the agreement. Any solid-colored animal in his flock will be treated as stolen. This proposal is constructed for maximum transparency and Laban's satisfaction: Jacob is naming his own penalty for any irregularity. His 'righteousness' (tzidkati) will be self-evident and auditable. This echoes the legal language of covenant: 'his righteousness shall answer for him.' Jacob demonstrates that, whatever his past history of deception, he now positions himself as fully accountable.

verses.kjv_text: And Laban said, Behold, I would it might be according to thy word.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן הֵן לוּ יְהִי כִדְבָרֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Lavan hen lu yehi khidvarekha.
verses.study_note: Laban's response is deceptively simple: 'Behold, I would it might be according to thy word' (hen lu yehi khidvarekha — literally 'behold, would that it be according to your word'). This is not an enthusiastic 'yes' but a hedging expression — 'would that' (lu) indicates Laban is not fully committing but allowing it. His immediate action (v.35-36) reveals his real intention: he will immediately separate all the marked animals and put them three days' journey away, giving Jacob an essentially unmarked starting flock. Laban agrees in principle while moving to circumvent in practice.

verses.kjv_text: And he removed that day the he goats that were ringstraked and spotted, and all the she goats that were speckled and spotted, and every one that had some white in it, and all the brown among the sheep, and gave them into the hand of his sons.
verses.original_text: וַיָּסַר בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא אֶת הַתְּיָשִׁים הָעֲקֻדִּים וְהַטְּלֻאִים וְאֵת כָּל הָעִזִּים הַנְּקֻדּוֹת וְהַטְּלֻאֹת כֹּל אֲשֶׁר לָבָן בּוֹ וְכָל חוּם בַּכְּשָׂבִים וַיִּתֵּן בְּיַד בָּנָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyasar bayyom hahu et-hatteyashim ha'akuddim vehatteluim ve'et kol ha'izzim hannekudot vehattelu'ot kol asher lavan bo vekhol chum bakkesavim vayyitten beyad banav.
verses.study_note: Laban moves immediately and decisively, 'that same day' (bayyom hahu). He removes every marked animal — not just some but every category Jacob specified — and hands them to his own sons. Note the appearance of a new word: 'ringstraked' (akuddim — streaked, banded). This word will reappear in the dream God gives Jacob in 31:10-12. Laban's preemptive removal reveals his intention: leave Jacob with only solid-colored animals whose offspring would statistically all be solid, giving Jacob nothing. What Laban calculates as a trap, God will use as an opportunity for miraculous provision.

verses.kjv_text: And he set three days’ journey betwixt himself and Jacob: and Jacob fed the rest of Laban’s flocks.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשֶׂם דֶּרֶךְ שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים בֵּינוֹ וּבֵין יַעֲקֹב וְיַעֲקֹב רֹעֶה אֶת־צֹאן לָבָן הַנּוֹתָרוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyasem derekh sheloshet yamim beino uvein Ya'akov veYa'akov roeh et-tzon Lavan hannoterot.
verses.study_note: Three days' journey (derekh sheloshet yamim) — roughly 60-90 miles. Laban puts maximum distance between Jacob and the marked flocks Jacob was supposed to inherit. The irony is potent: 'three days' journey' is the same distance Moses will request of Pharaoh for Israel to go and worship God (Exod 3:18; 5:3). Laban's calculation seems to reduce Jacob's flock to zero. But he reckons without the God of Bethel. Jacob is left to tend the remaining solid-colored flock — precisely the position from which God will multiply him.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob took him rods of green poplar, and of the hazel and chestnut tree; and pilled white strakes in them, and made the white appear which was in the rods.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח לוֹ יַעֲקֹב מַקַּל לִבְנֶה לַח וְלוּז וְעַרְמוֹן וַיְפַצֵּל בָּהֵן פְּצָלוֹת לְבָנוֹת מַחְשֹׂף הַלָּבָן אֲשֶׁר עַל הַמַּקְלוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikkach lo Ya'akov makkhal livneh lach veluz ve'armon vayefatzzel bahen fetzalot levanot machsof hallavan asher al-hammaklot.
verses.study_note: Jacob's scheme involves peeling partial strips from fresh rods of three trees: (1) livneh — white poplar (libneh shares its root with 'white' — lavan, and with Laban — all suggesting whiteness); (2) luz — hazel or almond; (3) armon — plane tree or chestnut. By peeling streaks (fetzalot levanot — white stripes), Jacob creates rods with an alternating white-and-dark pattern — visually similar to the streaked and spotted animals he wants to produce. Modern biology confirms that visual stimuli during mating do not affect offspring genetics — so the scientific question is: how did Jacob's flock produce marked animals? The answer lies in v.12 and Genesis 31:10-12: God revealed to Jacob in a dream that the angels were producing the outcome, not the rods.

verses.kjv_text: And he set the rods which he had pilled before the flocks in the gutters in the watering troughs when the flocks came to drink, that they should conceive when they came to drink.
verses.original_text: וַיַּצֵּג אֶת הַמַּקְלוֹת אֲשֶׁר פִּצֵּל בָּרֳהָטִים בְּשִׁקֲתוֹת הַמַּיִם אֲשֶׁר תָּבֹאןָ הַצֹּאן לִשְׁתּוֹת לְנֹכַח הַצֹּאן וַיֵּחַמְנָה בְּבֹאָן לִשְׁתּוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyatzeg et-hammaklot asher fitzzel barohatim beshikatot hamayim asher tavo'na hattzon lishtot lenokach hattzon vayyechamna bevo'an lishtot.
verses.study_note: Jacob places the peeled rods at the watering troughs where the flocks come to drink and mate ('they should conceive' — vayyechamna, literally 'they were in heat'). The watering trough is the place of mating in pastoral culture — where animals gather in numbers and coupling occurs. Jacob's action is meticulous: he positions the visual stimuli precisely at the moment of conception. Whether this represents genuine folk wisdom or simply Jacob's faithful attempt to cooperate with God's revealed plan (31:10-12), the text presents it as part of how God worked. The water trough will become a site of divine multiplication.

verses.kjv_text: And the flocks conceived before the rods, and brought forth cattle ringstraked, speckled, and spotted.
verses.original_text: וַיֶּחֱמוּ הַצֹּאן אֶל הַמַּקְלוֹת וַתֵּלַדְןָ הַצֹּאן עֲקֻדִּים נְקֻדִּים וּטְלֻאִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyechemu hattzon el-hammaklot vatteledn'a hattzon akuddim nekuddim uteluim.
verses.study_note: The flocks 'conceived before the rods' and produce exactly the categories Jacob had requested: ringstraked (akuddim), speckled (nekuddim), and spotted (teluim). The three terms match precisely the description in Jacob's prophetic dream (Gen 31:10-12). Modern genetics makes clear that visual stimuli during conception cannot determine coat patterns in offspring — the genetic information is fixed. Therefore the production of marked animals in abnormal numbers is attributed in Genesis 31:9 directly to God: 'God hath taken away the cattle of your father.' Jacob's rods are the human action; God is the actual cause of the results.
verses.doctrinal_note: The interplay between human action and divine sovereignty is instructive. Jacob acts (places rods), but God causes the results (Gen 31:9-12). This pattern runs throughout Scripture: Noah builds an ark (God sends the rain), Moses strikes the rock (God provides water), Elijah prays (God sends fire). Human obedience and effort are the instrument; divine power is the cause. Jacob is not cheating — he is cooperating with a plan God revealed to him in a dream, using the tools of his trade while trusting God for the outcome.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob did separate the lambs, and set the faces of the flocks toward the ringstraked, and all the brown in the flock of Laban; and he put his own flocks by themselves, and put them not unto Laban’s cattle.
verses.original_text: וְהַכְּשָׂבִים הִפְרִיד יַעֲקֹב וַיִּתֵּן פְּנֵי הַצֹּאן אֶל עָקֹד וְכָל חוּם בְּצֹאן לָבָן וַיָּשֶׁת לוֹ עֲדָרִים לְבַדּוֹ וְלֹא שָׁתָם עַל צֹאן לָבָן׃
verses.transliteration: Vehakkesavim hifreid Ya'akov vayyitten penei hattzon el-akod vekhol chum betzon Lavan vayyashet lo adarim levaddo velo shatam al-tzon Lavan.
verses.study_note: Jacob adds a refinement: he makes the solid-colored females of Laban's flock face toward the marked males for breeding, while immediately separating any marked offspring into his own separate flocks. This creates a breeding program that increasingly builds his marked flocks while Laban's flock remains solid-colored. The word 'separated' (hifreid) echoes the separations of Genesis 1 — God separated light from darkness, waters from waters. Jacob creates holy separation for his covenant portion.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, whensoever the stronger cattle did conceive, that Jacob laid the rods before the eyes of the cattle in the gutters, that they might conceive among the rods.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה בְּכָל יַחֵם הַצֹּאן הַמְקֻשָּׁרוֹת וְשָׂם יַעֲקֹב אֶת הַמַּקְלוֹת לְעֵינֵי הַצֹּאן בָּרֳהָטִים לְיַחְמֵנָּה בַּמַּקְלוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah bekhol yachem hattzon hamekushkarot vesam Ya'akov et-hammaklot le'einei hattzon barohatim leyachmenah bammaklot.
verses.study_note: Jacob employs the rods selectively for the 'stronger cattle' (hamekushkarot — the more vigorous, earlier-mating females). This selective breeding targets the stronger breeders for his purposes while sparing Laban's flock the same treatment. The word 'mekulasharot' (stronger, literally 'bound' or 'bound in time' — the ones that conceive at the prime season) distinguishes the premium breeding stock.

verses.kjv_text: But when the cattle were feeble, he put them not in: so the feebler were Laban’s, and the stronger Jacob’s.
verses.original_text: וּבְהַעֲטִיף הַצֹּאן לֹא יָשִׂים וְהָיָה הָעֲטֻפִים לְלָבָן וְהַקְּשֻׁרִים לְיַעֲקֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Uvehea'atif hattzon lo yasim vehayah ha'atufim leLavan vehakkeshurim leYa'akov.
verses.study_note: Jacob withholds the rods from the feebler, later-mating animals (ha'atufim — the weak, feeble, or late-season breeders). These weaker animals are left for Laban. The result is a selective transfer: Laban's flock contains the weaker animals; Jacob's emerging flock contains the stronger. This breeding intelligence, combined with God's miraculous intervention (Gen 31:9-12), produces the extraordinary prosperity described in verse 43. What Laban designed to impoverish Jacob instead enriches him — precisely as God had promised at Bethel (Gen 28:15).

verses.kjv_text: And the man increased exceedingly, and had much cattle, and maidservants, and menservants, and camels, and asses.
verses.original_text: וַיִּפְרֹץ הָאִישׁ מְאֹד מְאֹד וַיְהִי לוֹ צֹאן רַבּוֹת וּשְׁפָחוֹת וַעֲבָדִים וּגְמַלִּים וַחֲמֹרִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyifrotz ha'ish me'od me'od vayehi lo tzon rabbot ushefachot va'avadim ugamalim vachamorim.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes with one of the great summary statements of Genesis: 'The man increased exceedingly and exceedingly' (vayyifrotz ha'ish me'od me'od). The double 'me'od' (very, very) is the same intensifier used at the end of Genesis 1 — 'God saw everything... and it was very (me'od) good.' Here the double 'me'od' signals superlative prosperity. The verb 'vayyifrotz' (broke through, burst out) shares its root with 'Perez' — the one who 'breaks out' in the line of Judah, ancestor of David and of Christ (Ruth 4:18; Matt 1:3). Jacob's multiplication becomes a template for Israel's later multiplication in Egypt (Exod 1:7 — 'the children of Israel were fruitful and increased abundantly') and ultimately for the spread of the gospel (Acts 6:7 — 'the word of God increased'). The Abrahamic promise — 'I will multiply thee exceedingly' (Gen 17:2) — is being fulfilled before Laban's eyes.
verses.doctrinal_note: The prosperity of Genesis 30:43 is the fulfillment of the Bethel promise (Gen 28:15): 'I am with thee, and will keep thee... and I will bring thee again into this land.' Jacob's wealth is the pledge of the covenant: God blesses those in covenant relationship so extravagantly that even those who try to defraud them end up enriching them. This pattern — the oppressor enriching the oppressed, the exploiter multiplying the exploited — recurs in Exodus and ultimately in the cross: what appeared to be Christ's defeat became the greatest victory and the richest inheritance for all who believe.

meta.overview: Genesis 30 is one of the most humanly complex chapters in the patriarchal narrative. It moves in two major movements: the fierce competition between Rachel and Leah for Jacob's children (vv.1-24), and Jacob's cunning scheme to build his own flocks at Laban's expense (vv.25-43). Through the rivalry, desperation, bartered conjugal rights, surrogate motherhood, and a mandrake transaction, God sovereignly fulfills His covenant purpose — the twelve tribes of Israel emerge from this tangled web of human passion and scheming. The chapter reaches its theological apex in verse 22: 'God remembered Rachel.' His sovereign act, not human strategy, opens the womb that will bear Joseph, the beloved son who will save his people. The second half reveals Jacob's intelligence and industry, yet it is the God who appeared to him at Bethel (Gen 28) and will speak to him in a dream (Gen 31:10-13) who is the true architect of the prosperity. Providence weaves through every verse — using even bartered mandrakes and peeled rods to accomplish His purposes.
meta.theme: Divine Sovereignty Through Human Rivalry & the Birth of the Twelve Tribes
meta.keyWordMeaning: To remember — God's active, covenantal remembering that moves from memory to merciful intervention. 'God remembered Rachel' (v.22) is the theological climax of the chapter.
outline: The Baby War Begins: Rachel's Desperation (vv.1-8): Rachel gives Bilhah; Dan and Naphtali born
Leah Responds: Zilpah Given (vv.9-13): Gad and Asher born
The Mandrake Negotiation (vv.14-16): Reuben's discovery and Rachel's bargain
Leah's Continued Fruitfulness (vv.17-21): Issachar, Zebulun, and Dinah
God Remembers Rachel: Joseph Born (vv.22-24): The theological climax of the chapter
Jacob Negotiates with Laban (vv.25-36): The wages agreement for speckled and spotted
Jacob's Selective Breeding Scheme (vv.37-43): The peeled rods and Jacob's growing prosperity

verses.kjv_text: And he heard the words of Laban’s sons, saying, Jacob hath taken away all that was our father’s; and of that which was our father’s hath he gotten all this glory.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁמַע אֶת־דִּבְרֵי בְנֵי־לָבָן לֵאמֹר לָקַח יַעֲקֹב אֵת כָּל־אֲשֶׁר לְאָבִינוּ וּמֵאֲשֶׁר לְאָבִינוּ עָשָׂה אֵת כָּל־הַכָּבוֹד הַזֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishma et-divrei venei-Lavan lemor lakach Yaakov et kol-asher le'avinu ume'asher le'avinu asah et-kol-hakkavod hazzeh.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with jealousy among Laban's sons. The word 'glory' (kavod  -  H3519) is striking: it is the same word used for God's glory, the weight of divine honor. They accuse Jacob of hijacking the family's wealth and 'honor.' The irony is sharp  -  Jacob's prosperity came entirely from God's blessing (30:27-43), not from theft. This grumbling mirrors the resentment that will later drive Israel's persecution in Egypt, and anticipates every age's hostility toward those whom God has visibly prospered. The sons never address Jacob directly  -  they speak among themselves, signaling passive aggression and a growing threat.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob beheld the countenance of Laban, and, behold, it was not toward him as before.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא יַעֲקֹב אֶת־פְּנֵי לָבָן וְהִנֵּה אֵינֶנּוּ עִמּוֹ כִּתְמוֹל שִׁלְשׁוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyar Yaakov et-penei Lavan vehineh einennu immo kitmol shilshom.
verses.study_note: Jacob 'beheld the countenance' (penei  -  face) of Laban  -  a face-reading moment that reveals the depth of relational deterioration. 'It was not toward him as before' (kitmol shilshom  -  literally 'as yesterday or the day before'): the exact Hebrew idiom for 'formerly, in times past.' The face of Laban communicates what his sons' words expressed. Jacob is now reading danger signals and preparing for action. The face as a moral mirror is a recurring biblical motif: God's face shining (Numbers 6:25) brings blessing; a turned face signals rejection.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Jacob, Return unto the land of thy fathers, and to thy kindred; and I will be with thee.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־יַעֲקֹב שׁוּב אֶל־אֶרֶץ אֲבוֹתֶיךָ וּלְמוֹלַדְתֶּךָ וְאֶהְיֶה עִמָּךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Yaakov shuv el-eretz avotekha ulemolad'tekha ve'ehyeh immakh.
verses.study_note: The divine commission cuts through all human complexity. God commands with a single word: 'Return' (shuv  -  H7725)  -  the same root as 'repentance' (teshuvah). The direction is home, to the land of covenant promise. But the most important clause is the final promise: 'I will be with thee' (ve'ehyeh immakh). This echoes God's promise at Bethel (28:15): 'Behold, I am with thee.' The same divine presence that sustained Jacob in his going out now accompanies his coming home. This is the covenant formula of God's presence  -  the same 'ehyeh' that will appear in Exodus 3:12 when God commissions Moses.
verses.doctrinal_note: God initiates Jacob's return. This is sovereign providence  -  not Jacob's planning, ambition, or discontent, but divine direction. The pattern recurs throughout Scripture: God calls Abraham from Ur, calls Israel from Egypt, calls His church from the world. 'Return' is God's word before it is man's act.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob sent and called Rachel and Leah to the field unto his flock,
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁלַח יַעֲקֹב וַיִּקְרָא לְרָחֵל וּלְלֵאָה הַשָּׂדֶה אֶל־צֹאנוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishlach Yaakov vayyikra leRachel uleLe'ah hassadeh el-tzono.
verses.study_note: Jacob summons Rachel and Leah away from the household  -  to the field, where he keeps his flocks and where private speech is possible. The order 'Rachel and Leah' (Rachel first) departs from the older-first convention, reflecting Jacob's preference, but also positions Rachel's later deception (the teraphim, v.19) as the act of the favored wife. The open field, away from Laban's house and ears, is the place of truth. Jacob will lay out his entire case for departure to his wives before they act.

verses.kjv_text: And said unto them, I see your father’s countenance, that it is not toward me as before; but the God of my father hath been with me.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶן רֹאֶה אָנֹכִי אֶת־פְּנֵי אֲבִיכֶן כִּי־אֵינֶנּוּ אֵלַי כִּתְמוֹל שִׁלְשׁוֹם וֵאלֹהֵי אָבִי הָיָה עִמָּדִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer lahen ro'eh anokhi et-penei avikhen ki-einennu elai kitmol shilshom velohei avi hayah immadi.
verses.study_note: Jacob frames his appeal in two parts: Laban's hostility (v.2 repeated verbatim  -  'not toward me as before') and God's faithfulness ('the God of my father hath been with me'). He does not begin with plans or logistics but with theology. The contrast is deliberate: the face of Laban is changed; the God of his father has not changed. 'God of my father' (Elohei avi) establishes continuity with the Abrahamic covenant  -  Jacob is not claiming a private deity but the God of covenant history.

verses.kjv_text: And ye know that with all my power I have served your father.
verses.original_text: וְאַתֵּנָה יְדַעְתֶּן כִּי בְכָל־כֹּחִי עָבַדְתִּי אֶת־אֲבִיכֶן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attenah yeda'ten ki vekhol-kochi avadti et-avikhen.
verses.study_note: Jacob appeals to the wives as witnesses: 'ye know' (yeda'ten). They have observed fourteen years of service for them personally and six years of flock-keeping. 'With all my power' (bekhol-kochi)  -  the word 'koach' (strength/might) is the full expenditure of physical and moral energy. Jacob's self-defense before his wives is a prelude to the great defense speech of vv.36-42. He will not flee like a guilty man; he flees as a man whose righteousness is documented by two wives who are witnesses.

verses.kjv_text: And your father hath deceived me, and changed my wages ten times; but God suffered him not to hurt me.
verses.original_text: וַאֲבִיכֶן הֵתֶל בִּי וְהֶחֱלִף אֶת־מַשְׂכֻּרְתִּי עֲשֶׂרֶת מֹנִים וְלֹא־נְתָנוֹ אֱלֹהִים לְהָרַע עִמָּדִי׃
verses.transliteration: Va'avikhen hetel bi vehechelf et-maskorti aseret monim velo-netano Elohim lehara immadi.
verses.study_note: 'Deceived me' (hetel bi  -  from 'halal,' to mock/deceive) and 'changed my wages ten times' (aseret monim  -  literally 'ten times' or 'ten-fold'). The number 'ten' in Hebrew idiom can mean 'many times' or 'repeatedly' (cf. Numbers 14:22  -  Israel tests God 'ten times' in the wilderness). Laban exploited every legal advantage. Yet Jacob's conclusion is theological: 'God suffered him not to hurt me' (velo-netano Elohim lehara). Divine restraint on the oppressor is the covenant protection of the patriarchs  -  and later of Israel in Egypt, where God's restraining hand limits Pharaoh's capacity to harm.

verses.kjv_text: If he said thus, The speckled shall be thy wages; then all the cattle bare speckled: and if he said thus, The ringstraked shall be thy hire; then bare all the cattle ringstraked.
verses.original_text: אִם־כֹּה יֹאמַר נְקֻדִּים יִהְיֶה שְׂכָרֶךָ וְיָלְדוּ כָל־הַצֹּאן נְקֻדִּים וְאִם־כֹּה יֹאמַר עֲקֻדִּים יִהְיֶה שְׂכָרֶךָ וְיָלְדוּ כָל־הַצֹּאן עֲקֻדִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Im-koh yomar nekuddim yihyeh sekharekha veyaldu khol-hatzon nekuddim ve'im-koh yomar akuddim yihyeh sekharekha veyaldu khol-hatzon akuddim.
verses.study_note: This verse is the narrative key to Jacob's prosperity. Laban kept changing the definition of Jacob's payment  -  now speckled (nekuddim), now ringstraked (akuddim)  -  but God overruled every change. The three categories of patterned animals (speckled, spotted, ringstraked) appear throughout the Jacob-Laban flock narrative (Gen 30:32-42). God's providence ensured that whatever Laban defined as Jacob's wages, the flocks produced in abundance. This is the anti-manipulation miracle: the more Laban tries to define Jacob into poverty, the richer Jacob becomes.

verses.kjv_text: Thus God hath taken away the cattle of your father, and given them to me.
verses.original_text: וַיַּצֵּל אֱלֹהִים אֶת־מִקְנֵה אֲבִיכֶן וַיִּתֶּן לִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyatzel Elohim et-mikneh avikhen vayyitten li.
verses.study_note: A breathtaking theological declaration. 'God hath taken away' (vayyatzel  -  same root as 'deliver/rescue') the cattle. The transfer of wealth is not Jacob's cleverness but God's act of rescue. The word 'natzel' typically means to deliver the weak from the powerful  -  the same root used for Israel's deliverance from Egypt (Exod 12:36, where Israel 'plundered' the Egyptians). The exodus typology is explicit: Jacob/Israel leaving a labor-exploiting oppressor with acquired wealth by divine provision. What Laban intended as wages to be withheld, God delivers to Jacob.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass at the time that the cattle conceived, that I lifted up mine eyes, and saw in a dream, and, behold, the rams which leaped upon the cattle were ringstraked, speckled, and grisled.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בְּעֵת יַחֵם הַצֹּאן וָאֶשָּׂא עֵינַי וָאֵרֶא בַּחֲלוֹם וְהִנֵּה הָעַתֻּדִים הָעֹלִים עַל־הַצֹּאן עֲקֻדִּים נְקֻדִּים וּבְרֻדִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi be'et yachem hatzon va'essa einai va'ere bachalon vehineh ha'attudim ha'olim al-hatzon akuddim nekuddim uvruddim.
verses.study_note: Jacob recounts a divine dream during the mating season ('in the time that the cattle conceived'  -  yachem, the heat of mating). In the dream, God reveals the mechanism of the miracle: the male goats mating with the flock are themselves marked (ringstraked, speckled, grisled)  -  supernaturally. This was not the result of Jacob's rods placed at the water troughs (30:37-41) but divine manipulation of genetics for covenant purposes. Jacob's dream-revelation corrects any naturalistic misreading of chapter 30  -  the striped rods were perhaps an act of faith, but the actual miracle was God's. Dreams as divine communication are characteristic of the Jacob narrative (28:12; here; 37:5 for Joseph).

verses.kjv_text: And the angel of God spake unto me in a dream, saying, Jacob: And I said, Here am I.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלַי מַלְאַךְ הָאֱלֹהִים בַּחֲלוֹם יַעֲקֹב וָאֹמַר הִנֵּנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer elai mal'akh ha'Elohim bachalon Yaakov va'omar hinneni.
verses.study_note: The 'angel of God' (mal'akh ha'Elohim) speaks  -  not merely a messenger but the divine Angel who in Genesis often carries the full divine authority (cf. 22:11-12, where the Angel of the LORD speaks as God). The pattern of naming followed by 'Here am I' (hinneni) is the classical covenant response  -  Abraham (22:1,11), Isaac (22:7), Moses (Exod 3:4), Samuel (1 Sam 3:4), Isaiah (6:8). Hinneni expresses full availability and readiness. Jacob's hinneni to the divine Angel mirrors his readiness to obey.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Lift up now thine eyes, and see, all the rams which leap upon the cattle are ringstraked, speckled, and grisled: for I have seen all that Laban doeth unto thee.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר שָׂא־נָא עֵינֶיךָ וּרְאֵה כָּל־הָעַתֻּדִים הָעֹלִים עַל־הַצֹּאן עֲקֻדִּים נְקֻדִּים וּבְרֻדִּים כִּי רָאִיתִי אֵת כָּל־אֲשֶׁר לָבָן עֹשֶׂה לָּךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer sa-na einekha ure'eh kol-ha'attudim ha'olim al-hatzon akuddim nekuddim uvruddim ki ra'iti et kol-asher Lavan oseh lakh.
verses.study_note: The divine explanation reveals the full picture: God has observed ('I have seen,' ra'iti) all that Laban has done. The same verb is used in Exodus 3:7  -  'I have surely seen the affliction of my people.' Divine seeing precedes divine action. The miracle of the patterned rams was God's direct response to Laban's exploitation. 'For' (ki) is the causal conjunction  -  the flocks were marked because God saw and acted. This is the covenant God who does not ignore injustice against His people.

verses.kjv_text: I am the God of Bethel, where thou anointedst the pillar, and where thou vowedst a vow unto me: now arise, get thee out from this land, and return unto the land of thy kindred.
verses.original_text: אָנֹכִי הָאֵל בֵּית־אֵל אֲשֶׁר מָשַׁחְתָּ שָּׁם מַצֵּבָה אֲשֶׁר נָדַרְתָּ לִּי שָׁם נֶדֶר עַתָּה קוּם צֵא מִן־הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת וְשׁוּב אֶל־אֶרֶץ מוֹלַדְתֶּךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Anokhi ha'El Beit-El asher mashachta sham matstsevah asher nadarta li sham neder attah kum tze min-ha'aretz hazzo't veshuv el-eretz molad'tekha.
verses.study_note: The Angel identifies Himself as 'the God of Bethel'  -  not an abstract deity but the specific, covenant-making God of Jacob's founding encounter (Gen 28:10-22). Two actions at Bethel are recalled: (1) anointing the pillar (mashachta matstsevah)  -  the stone marker, set up and consecrated as a memorial, (2) making a vow (nadarta neder)  -  Jacob's conditional promise to return and acknowledge God. Now God calls in that vow. Twenty years of labor have passed; it is time to 'arise' (kum), 'go out' (tze), and 'return' (shuv). The divine command perfectly fulfills the conditions Jacob himself stipulated at Bethel (28:20-22).
verses.doctrinal_note: God remembers vows. Jacob's vow at Bethel (28:20-22) was conditional and immature  -  'if God will...then I will.' Yet God honors even this tentative pledge and now calls Jacob to fulfill it. This establishes the binding nature of covenantal vows before God (Ecclesiastes 5:4-5). The God of Bethel is also a God who holds His people accountable to their word  -  and who makes it possible to keep it.

verses.kjv_text: And Rachel and Leah answered and said unto him, Is there yet any portion or inheritance for us in our father’s house?
verses.original_text: וַתַּעַן רָחֵל וְלֵאָה וַתֹּאמַרְנָה לוֹ הַעוֹד לָנוּ חֵלֶק וְנַחֲלָה בְּבֵית אָבִינוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vatta'an Rachel veLe'ah vattomarnah lo ha'od lanu chelek venachalah beveit avinu.
verses.study_note: For the only time in Genesis, Rachel and Leah speak together with one voice. Their answer is a series of rhetorical questions, each more damning of their father. 'Is there yet any portion or inheritance for us' (chelek venachalah)? Under ancient Near Eastern law, daughters who were 'sold' in marriage received no further inheritance rights  -  their bride price was their portion. Laban collected his full payment in Jacob's labor (vv.41) and then 'devoured' even the bride price (v.15). His daughters have no legal or economic claim on him.

verses.kjv_text: Are we not counted of him strangers? for he hath sold us, and hath quite devoured also our money.
verses.original_text: הֲלוֹא נָכְרִיּוֹת נֶחְשַׁבְנוּ לוֹ כִּי מְכָרָנוּ וַיֹּאכַל גַּם־אָכוֹל אֶת־כַּסְפֵּנוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Halo nokhriyyot nechshavnu lo ki mkharanu vayyokhal gam-akhol et-kaspeinu.
verses.study_note: 'He hath sold us' (mkharanu)  -  a devastating accusation from daughters to father. In Hebrew culture, the bride price (mohar) was paid to the bride's family and should have provided a dowry for the daughters. Laban took the fourteen years of Jacob's labor (the implicit bride price) and then 'devoured it whole' (vayyokhal gam-akhol  -  emphatic infinitive absolute: completely consumed it). His daughters are strangers (nokhriyyot  -  foreigners) in their own father's house. The moral case for departure is now complete: Laban has forfeited all claim on them.

verses.kjv_text: For all the riches which God hath taken from our father, that is ours, and our children’s: now then, whatsoever God hath said unto thee, do.
verses.original_text: כִּי כָל־הָעֹשֶׁר אֲשֶׁר הִצִּיל אֱלֹהִים מֵאָבִינוּ לָנוּ הוּא וּלְבָנֵינוּ וְעַתָּה כֹּל אֲשֶׁר אָמַר אֱלֹהִים אֵלֶיךָ עֲשֵׂה׃
verses.transliteration: Ki kol-ha'osher asher hitzil Elohim me'avinu lanu hu ulvaneinu ve'attah kol asher amar Elohim elekha aseh.
verses.study_note: Rachel and Leah echo Jacob's theological declaration from v.9: the wealth was 'delivered' (hitzil  -  same root as vayyatzel, v.9) by God. Then comes their climactic command: 'whatsoever God hath said unto thee, do' (aseh). Two women who might have been expected to cling to family and homeland give Jacob the clearest possible mandate. This echoes the women at Ruth 1:16-18 and 3:5, and anticipates the obedience theme of the New Testament. The wives become unexpected pillars of faithfulness at the decisive moment.

verses.kjv_text: Then Jacob rose up, and set his sons and his wives upon camels;
verses.original_text: וַיָּקָם יַעֲקֹב וַיִּשָּׂא אֶת־בָּנָיו וְאֶת־נָשָׁיו עַל־הַגְּמַלִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyakam Yaakov vayyissa et-banav ve'et-nashav al-haggemallim.
verses.study_note: Action follows immediately after divine command and wifely affirmation. 'Rose up' (vayyakam)  -  the decisive physical response to God's word. The same word appears in Ruth 1:6 (Naomi 'arose' to return to Bethlehem) and Acts 9:18 (Paul 'arose' after his conversion). Jacob loads his children and wives onto camels  -  the appropriate transport for a long patriarchal journey (cf. Abraham's servant, Gen 24:10). The speed of action after divine direction is characteristic of faithful response in Scripture.

verses.kjv_text: And he carried away all his cattle, and all his goods which he had gotten, the cattle of his getting, which he had gotten in Padanaram, for to go to Isaac his father in the land of Canaan.
verses.original_text: וַיִּנְהַג אֶת־כָּל־מִקְנֵהוּ וְאֶת־כָּל־רְכֻשׁוֹ אֲשֶׁר רָכָשׁ מִקְנֵה קִנְיָנוֹ אֲשֶׁר רָכַשׁ בְּפַדַּן אֲרָם לָבוֹא אֶל־יִצְחָק אָבִיו אַרְצָה כְּנָעַן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyinhag et-kol-mikneihu ve'et-kol-rekhusho asher rakhash mikneh kinyanno asher rakhash bePaddan Aram lavo el-Yitzhak aviv artzah Kena'an.
verses.study_note: The narrative notes the destination: 'to Isaac his father in the land of Canaan.' Jacob has not forgotten his father or the Promised Land despite twenty years. The covenant goal remains clear. 'Padanaram' appears here as the summary label for Haran and its region (first used in 25:20)  -  the place of the extended Aramean family. This journey echoes Abraham's original call from Mesopotamia to Canaan, and typologically anticipates Israel's eventual Exodus from 'the house of bondage' to the land of promise.

verses.kjv_text: And Laban went to shear his sheep: and Rachel had stolen the images that were her father’s.
verses.original_text: וְלָבָן הָלַךְ לִגְזֹז אֶת־צֹאנוֹ וַתִּגְנֹב רָחֵל אֶת־הַתְּרָפִים אֲשֶׁר לְאָבִיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: VeLavan halakh ligzoz et-tzono vattiggov Rachel et-hatterafim asher le'aviha.
verses.study_note: The most theologically complex verse of the chapter. While Laban is distracted by sheep-shearing  -  the great festive occasion of the pastoral calendar (cf. Gen 38:12; 1 Sam 25:2)  -  Rachel steals (vattiggov  -  root: ganav, to steal) the teraphim (household gods). Teraphim (singular: teraph  -  H8655) were small figurines used as oracles, talismans, and possibly indicators of inheritance rights (in Nuzi texts, possession of household gods could establish a claim to the family estate). Rachel's motive may be: (1) ensuring inheritance for herself and Jacob, (2) religious attachment from her upbringing, (3) depriving Laban of his oracle and protection. Whatever her motive, her act carries enormous consequences and will nearly expose Jacob to Laban's wrath.
verses.doctrinal_note: The teraphim represent the persistent pull of idolatry even within the covenant family. Rachel grew up in Laban's syncretistic household and brings its idols into her new life. This partial idolatry haunts the family until Jacob commands its purging at Bethel (35:2-4). The teraphim also appear later in Israel's history (Judges 17-18; 1 Sam 15:23; Hosea 3:4)  -  always negatively. The household gods must eventually give way to the God of Bethel.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob stole away unawares to Laban the Syrian, in that he told him not that he fled.
verses.original_text: וַיִּגְנֹב יַעֲקֹב אֶת־לֵב לָבָן הָאֲרַמִּי עַל בְּלִי הִגִּיד לוֹ כִּי בֹרֵחַ הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiggnov Yaakov et-lev Lavan ha'Arammi al beli higgid lo ki voreach hu.
verses.study_note: In the same verse that describes Rachel's theft (vattiggov, v.19), Jacob also 'stole'  -  but his theft is of Laban's heart (lev). 'Jacob stole the heart of Laban' (vayyiggnov et-lev Lavan)  -  a Hebrew idiom meaning he did something secretly, without Laban's knowledge. The narrator uses the same verb for both acts, creating an ironic parallel: Rachel steals objects; Jacob steals a moment. Laban is called 'the Syrian' (ha'Arammi  -  the Aramean)  -  his ethnic identity, marking his difference from Jacob who is the covenant heir. The description 'in that he told him not' reflects the tactical necessity of secrecy given Laban's demonstrated capacity for manipulation and obstruction.

verses.kjv_text: So he fled with all that he had; and he rose up, and passed over the river, and set his face toward the mount Gilead.
verses.original_text: וַיִּבְרַח הוּא וְכָל־אֲשֶׁר לוֹ וַיָּקָם וַיַּעֲבֹר אֶת־הַנָּהָר וַיָּשֶׂם אֶת־פָּנָיו הַר הַגִּלְעָד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyivrach hu vekhol-asher lo vayyakam vayya'avor et-hannahar vayyasem et-panav har haGil'ad.
verses.study_note: 'He fled' (vayyivrach  -  root: barach)  -  the word used repeatedly for emergency escape. Jacob had earlier fled (barach) from Esau (27:43; 28:5); now he flees from Laban. 'The river' (hannahar) is the Euphrates  -  the great boundary between Mesopotamia and Canaan. Crossing it is a declaration of departure. 'Set his face toward the mount Gilead'  -  the high country east of the Jordan, south of Damascus. The phrase 'set his face' (vayyasem et-panav) indicates determined, intentional direction  -  the language of irreversible resolve (cf. Luke 9:51, Jesus 'set his face to go to Jerusalem').

verses.kjv_text: And it was told Laban on the third day that Jacob was fled.
verses.original_text: וַיֻּגַּד לְלָבָן בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי כִּי בָרַח יַעֲקֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyuggad leLavan bayyom hashelishi ki varach Yaakov.
verses.study_note: 'On the third day'  -  Jacob has a three-day head start (Laban had moved the flocks three days' journey away in 30:36 to create distance). The biblical third day is consistently a day of revelation, decision, and turning points (Genesis 22:4; Exodus 19:11; Hosea 6:2; 1 Corinthians 15:4). The three-day gap proves providential  -  it gives Jacob's household sufficient distance to cross the Euphrates before Laban can mobilize pursuit. God's timing works through natural circumstances.

verses.kjv_text: And he took his brethren with him, and pursued after him seven days’ journey; and they overtook him in the mount Gilead.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח אֶת־אֶחָיו עִמּוֹ וַיִּרְדֹּף אַחֲרָיו דֶּרֶךְ שִׁבְעַת יָמִים וַיַּדְבֵּק אֹתוֹ בְּהַר הַגִּלְעָד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach et-echav immo vayyirdof acharav derekh shiv'at yamim vayyaddbeq oto behar haGil'ad.
verses.study_note: Laban's pursuit covers seven days  -  an intensive chase through hundreds of miles of difficult terrain. 'His brethren' (echav) likely refers to his kinsmen, an armed party. Seven days of pursuit at maximum speed closes the gap. He overtakes Jacob in the 'mount Gilead'  -  the elevated plateau east of the Jordan. The meeting on this mountain will become the site of the covenant (v.25). God's restraint of Laban must account for the fact that Laban actually catches Jacob  -  the restraint shapes what Laban does when he arrives, not whether he arrives.

verses.kjv_text: And God came to Laban the Syrian in a dream by night, and said unto him, Take heed that thou speak not to Jacob either good or bad.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא אֱלֹהִים אֶל־לָבָן הָאֲרַמִּי בַּחֲלוֹם הַלָּיְלָה וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ הִשָּׁמֶר לְךָ פֶּן־תְּדַבֵּר עִם־יַעֲקֹב מִטּוֹב עַד־רָע׃
verses.transliteration: Vayavo Elohim el-Lavan ha'Arammi bachalon hallaylah vayomer lo hishammer lekha pen-tedabber im-Yaakov mittov ad-ra.
verses.study_note: God intervenes directly. He speaks to Laban the Aramean  -  a non-covenant person  -  in a dream, prohibiting any speech to Jacob 'from good to bad' (mittov ad-ra  -  the merism meaning 'anything at all'). This is the same divine restraint mentioned in v.7 now made explicit. 'Take heed' (hishammer)  -  the strongest warning formula, indicating serious danger. God protects Jacob not by eliminating the threat but by restraining the threatener. This parallels Abimelech's warning concerning Sarah (20:3-6) and Balaam's restraint concerning Israel (Numbers 22:12, 20). God speaks to pagan rulers to protect His covenant people.
verses.doctrinal_note: God protects His people by restraining their enemies. He speaks to Laban  -  not a covenant member  -  demonstrating that His sovereign authority extends beyond the covenant community to all people and nations. No human hostility toward God's elect can proceed beyond what God permits. This is the doctrine of divine restraint: God sets limits on evil, not always by removing the threat but by governing what the threat can accomplish (cf. Job 1:12).

verses.kjv_text: Then Laban overtook Jacob. Now Jacob had pitched his tent in the mount: and Laban with his brethren pitched in the mount of Gilead.
verses.original_text: וַיַּשֵּׂג לָבָן אֶת־יַעֲקֹב וְיַעֲקֹב תָּקַע אֶת־אָהֳלוֹ בָּהָר וְלָבָן תָּקַע אֶת־אֶחָיו בְּהַר הַגִּלְעָד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaseg Lavan et-Yaakov veYaakov taka et-oholo bahar veLavan taka et-echav behar haGil'ad.
verses.study_note: The confrontation is described in careful, calm language  -  no violence, though Laban has an armed party. Both 'pitch tent' (taka et-oholo)  -  the same verb, a deliberate parallelism suggesting a standoff of equals on a mountain top. Gilead (literally 'heap of witness' from the same root as the cairn about to be erected) will become the location of the covenant. The mountain is neutral ground  -  neither Laban's Haran nor Jacob's Canaan  -  the perfect place for a boundary covenant.

verses.kjv_text: And Laban said to Jacob, What hast thou done, that thou hast stolen away unawares to me, and carried away my daughters, as captives taken with the sword?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן לְיַעֲקֹב מֶה עָשִׂיתָ וַתִּגְנֹב אֶת־לְבָבִי וַתְּנַהֵג אֶת־בְּנֹתַי כִּשְׁבֻיוֹת חָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Lavan leYaakov meh asita vattiggov et-levavi vattenaheg et-benotai kishevu'yot charev.
verses.study_note: Laban's opening speech is a masterwork of manipulation. He opens with 'What hast thou done?' (meh asita)  -  the accusatory question formula (cf. Gen 3:13; 4:10). Then two accusations: (1) 'thou hast stolen my heart' (vattiggov et-levavi  -  the same idiom as v.20 but now Laban frames it as theft), and (2) 'carried away my daughters as captives taken with the sword' (kishevu'yot charev  -  like women seized in war). The captive imagery is theatrical overreach  -  his daughters left willingly (vv.14-16). Laban casts himself as victim while ignoring twenty years of exploitation.

verses.kjv_text: Wherefore didst thou flee away secretly, and steal away from me; and didst not tell me, that I might have sent thee away with mirth, and with songs, with tabret, and with harp?
verses.original_text: לָמָּה נַחְבֵּאתָ לִבְרֹחַ וַתִּגְנֹב אֹתִי וְלֹא־הִגַּדְתָּ לִּי וַאֲשַׁלֵּחֲךָ בְּשִׂמְחָה וּבְשִׁרִים בְּתֹף וּבְכִנּוֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Lammah nachbeita livroach vattiggov oti velo-higgadta li va'ashalechakha besimchah uveshipm betof uvekinnor.
verses.study_note: Laban's second rhetorical move: wounded sentiment. 'I would have sent thee away with mirth, and with songs'  -  a farewell celebration with tambourine (tof) and harp (kinnor). This is almost certainly performance: given his history of manipulation and wage-changing, a public farewell could easily have become another delay or deception. The song-and-celebration motif, however, resonates with the joyful departure from Egypt (Exod 15:20  -  Miriam's tambourine at the sea).

verses.kjv_text: And hast not suffered me to kiss my sons and my daughters? thou hast now done foolishly in so doing.
verses.original_text: וְלֹא נְטַשְׁתַּנִי לְנַשֵּׁק לְבָנַי וְלִבְנֹתָי עַתָּה הִסְכַּלְתָּ עֲשׂוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Velo netashtanni lenashek levannai ulivenota'i attah hiskkalllta azo.
verses.study_note: 'Thou hast done foolishly' (hiskkallta  -  from sakhal, to act foolishly/unwisely)  -  Laban's only real accusation with moral weight here, though it is rhetorical. He claims deprivation of farewell kisses to his grandchildren. Noteworthy: he calls them 'my sons' (banai)  -  appropriating even Jacob's children as his own. His possessiveness extends to Jacob, his daughters, and his grandchildren. The farewell kiss (nashek) is a covenant and family custom of deep significance (cf. Jacob kissing Rachel, 29:11).

verses.kjv_text: It is in the power of my hand to do you hurt: but the God of your father spake unto me yesternight, saying, Take thou heed that thou speak not to Jacob either good or bad.
verses.original_text: יֶשׁ־לְאֵל יָדִי לַעֲשׂוֹת עִמָּכֶם רָע וֵאלֹהֵי אֲבִיכֶם אֶמֶשׁ אָמַר אֵלַי לֵאמֹר הִשָּׁמֶר לְךָ מִדַּבֵּר עִם־יַעֲקֹב מִטּוֹב עַד־רָע׃
verses.transliteration: Yesh-le'El yadi la'asot immakhem ra velohei avikhem emesh amar elai lemor hishammer lekha middabber im-Yaakov mittov ad-ra.
verses.study_note: A stunning moment of self-disclosure. Laban admits: (1) 'It is in the power of my hand to do you hurt'  -  he has the military and numerical strength to harm Jacob, (2) BUT the God of Jacob's father warned him last night. The phrase 'yesh-le'El yadi' (literally: 'there is El in my hand'  -  'El' both meaning God and 'strength') may be a wordplay: human el-power constrained by divine El-power. Laban's confession validates God's warning (v.24) and serves as the clearest possible statement that Jacob's safety was entirely due to divine intervention.

verses.kjv_text: And now, though thou wouldest needs be gone, because thou sore longedst after thy father’s house, yet wherefore hast thou stolen my gods?
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה הָלֹךְ הָלַכְתָּ כִּי נִכְסֹף נִכְסַפְתָּה לְבֵית אָבִיךָ לָמָּה גָנַבְתָּ אֶת־אֱלֹהָי׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah halokh halakhta ki nikhsof nikhsafta leveit avikha lammah ganavta et-elohai.
verses.study_note: Laban's real grievance emerges: 'wherefore hast thou stolen my gods?' (lamah ganavta et-elohai). The teraphim are 'my gods'  -  his most prized possession. The emphatic infinitive absolute (halokh halakhta  -  'you have surely gone') expresses resigned acceptance of the departure, quickly abandoned for the accusation. The question 'wherefore' (lammah) also carries an implicit threat. For Laban, the teraphim represent divine protection, inheritance authority, and the center of his household religion. Their theft strikes at every level of his security.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob answered and said to Laban, Because I was afraid: for I said, Peradventure thou wouldest take by force thy daughters from me.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַן יַעֲקֹב וַיֹּאמֶר לְלָבָן כִּי יָרֵאתִי כִּי אָמַרְתִּי פֶּן־תִּגְזֹל אֶת־בְּנוֹתֶיךָ מֵעִמִּי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'an Yaakov vayomer leLavan ki yareti ki amarti pen-tigzol et-benotekha me'immi.
verses.study_note: Jacob answers Laban's emotional speech with honesty and precision. His motive for secrecy was fear (yareti): the reasonable, documented fear that Laban would 'take by force' (tigzol  -  a different word from 'steal,' implying forcible seizure and robbery) his daughters. This fear was well-grounded  -  Laban had already demonstrated willingness to manipulate Jacob's marriage rights (29:23-25). Jacob then immediately addresses the theft accusation with a categorical denial and a reckless oath (v.32).

verses.kjv_text: With whomsoever thou findest thy gods, let him not live: before our brethren discern thou what is thine with me, and take it to thee. For Jacob knew not that Rachel had stolen them.
verses.original_text: עִם אֲשֶׁר תִּמְצָא אֶת־אֱלֹהֶיךָ לֹא יִחְיֶה נֶגֶד אַחֵינוּ הַכֶּר־לְךָ מָה עִמָּדִי וְקַח־לָךְ וְלֹא יָדַע יַעֲקֹב כִּי רָחֵל גְּנָבָתַם׃
verses.transliteration: Im asher timtza et-elohekha lo yichyeh neged achenu hakker-lekha mah immadi vekach-lakh velo yada Yaakov ki Rachel genavatum.
verses.study_note: One of the great tragic ironies of Genesis. Jacob, certain of his household's innocence, pronounces a death sentence on whoever has the teraphim: 'let him not live' (lo yichyeh). He does not know ('Jacob knew not,' velo yada Yaakov) that Rachel has them. His oath nearly destroys the woman he loves most. This reckless oath echoes Jephthah's (Judges 11:30-35) and anticipates Saul's curse on Jonathan (1 Sam 14:24-45). Human oaths made in ignorance carry devastating potential. The narrator's aside ('Jacob knew not') creates unbearable tension: the reader knows what Jacob does not.

verses.kjv_text: And Laban went into Jacob’s tent, and into Leah’s tent, and into the two maidservants’ tents; but he found them not. Then went he out of Leah’s tent, and entered into Rachel’s tent.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא לָבָן בְּאֹהֶל יַעֲקֹב וּבְאֹהֶל לֵאָה וּבְאֹהֶל שְׁתֵּי הָאֲמָהֹת וְלֹא מָצָא וַיֵּצֵא מֵאֹהֶל לֵאָה וַיָּבֹא בְּאֹהֶל רָחֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayavo Lavan be'ohel Yaakov ube'ohel Le'ah ube'ohel shetei ha'amahhot velo matza vayyetze me'ohel Le'ah vayavo be'ohel Rachel.
verses.study_note: The search narrative is given in deliberate detail  -  four tents are searched in order: Jacob's, Leah's, the two maidservants' (Bilhah and Zilpah's). Each search fails (velo matza  -  'he found them not'). The order saves Rachel's tent for last, building narrative tension. Laban has permission from Jacob himself (v.32) to search thoroughly. The thoroughness of the failed search will deepen the irony of Rachel's concealment.

verses.kjv_text: Now Rachel had taken the images, and put them in the camel’s furniture, and sat upon them. And Laban searched all the tent, but found them not.
verses.original_text: וְרָחֵל לָקְחָה אֶת־הַתְּרָפִים וַתְּשִׂמֵם בְּכַר הַגָּמָל וַתֵּשֶׁב עֲלֵיהֶם וַיְמַשֵּׁשׁ לָבָן אֶת־כָּל־הָאֹהֶל וְלֹא מָצָא׃
verses.transliteration: VeRachel lakchah et-hatterafim vatesimaem bekar haggamal vatteshev aleihem vaymashesh Lavan et-kol-ha'ohel velo matza.
verses.study_note: Rachel hides the teraphim in the camel's furniture (kar  -  a padded saddle bag or cushion) and sits on them. The scene is richly ironic: the gods of Laban are sat upon by a woman  -  a posture of contempt for idols in ancient culture (cf. Isaiah 44:20; Psalm 115:5-8  -  idols that cannot move). Laban 'felt throughout' (vaymashesh  -  groped/searched by touch) the entire tent and finds nothing. The teraphim, which Laban valued as divine protectors, cannot even reveal their own location.

verses.kjv_text: And she said to her father, Let it not displease my lord that I cannot rise up before thee; for the custom of women is upon me. And he searched, but found not the images.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר אֶל־אָבִיהָ אַל־יִחַר בְּעֵינֵי אֲדֹנִי כִּי לֹא אוּכַל לָקוּם מִפָּנֶיךָ כִּי דֶרֶךְ נָשִׁים לִי וַיְחַפֵּשׂ וְלֹא מָצָא אֶת־הַתְּרָפִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomer el-aviha al-yichar be'einei adoni ki lo ukhal lakum mippanekha ki derekh nashim li vaychappes velo matza et-hatterafim.
verses.study_note: Rachel's excuse is 'the custom of women' (derekh nashim  -  her menstrual period). Whether this is true or fabricated as cover, it is a brilliant tactical use of ritual purity law: Laban would not force a menstruating woman to rise or search thoroughly under her. The teraphim  -  objects associated with divination and divine power  -  are literally hidden under a woman in her period of ritual impurity, an ironic desacralization. The final line delivers the chapter's satisfying blow: 'he found not the teraphim.' The gods Laban trusted cannot be found, cannot act, cannot save even themselves.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob was wroth, and chode with Laban: and Jacob answered and said to Laban, What is my trespass? what is my sin, that thou hast so hotly pursued after me?
verses.original_text: וַיִּחַר לְיַעֲקֹב וַיָּרֶב בְּלָבָן וַיַּעַן יַעֲקֹב וַיֹּאמֶר לְלָבָן מַה־פִּשְׁעִי מַה חַטָּאתִי כִּי דָלַקְתָּ אַחֲרָי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyichar leYaakov vayarev beLavan vayya'an Yaakov vayomer leLavan mah-pish'i mah chattati ki dalakta achahai.
verses.study_note: The failed search vindicates Jacob and ignites a righteous anger. The language is striking: 'Jacob was wroth' (vayyichar leYaakov  -  'it burned for Jacob')  -  the same construction used when God was angered (Exod 4:14) and when Jonah was angered (Jonah 4:1). Righteous anger at injustice is legitimate. Jacob's great speech (vv.36-42) begins here. He opens with a two-part legal question: 'What is my trespass?' (mah pish'i  -  H6588, willful transgression) and 'what is my sin?' (mah chattati  -  H2403, moral failure). The legal terms escalate  -  from violation to sin, demanding Laban specify his charge.

verses.kjv_text: Whereas thou hast searched all my stuff, what hast thou found of all thy household stuff? set it here before my brethren and thy brethren, that they may judge betwixt us both.
verses.original_text: כִּי־מִשַּׁשְׁתָּ אֶת־כָּל־כֵּלַי מַה־מָּצָאתָ מִכֹּל כְּלֵי בֵיתֶךָ שִׂים כֹּה נֶגֶד אַחַי וְאַחֶיךָ וְיוֹכִיחוּ בֵּין שְׁנֵינוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ki-mishshashta et-kol-kelai mah-matza'ta mikkol kelei veitekha sim koh neged achai ve'achekha veyokhichu bein shenenu.
verses.study_note: Jacob inverts Laban's investigation. Laban searched everything (mishshashta  -  same root as vaymashesh, v.34); he found nothing. Jacob now calls for a public tribunal: 'set it here before my brethren and thy brethren'  -  witnesses from both families. 'Let them judge' (veyokhichu  -  from yakach, to arbitrate/judge, also the root of 'rebuke'). Jacob insists on due process, on public accountability. This appeal to a tribunal of witnesses anticipates the Mosaic principle that disputes require witnesses (Deut 17:6; 19:15). Jacob's confidence in the outcome is total.

verses.kjv_text: This twenty years have I been with thee; thy ewes and thy she goats have not cast their young, and the rams of thy flock have I not eaten.
verses.original_text: זֶה עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה אָנֹכִי עִמָּךְ רְחֵלֶיךָ וְעִזֶּיךָ לֹא שִׁכֵּלוּ וְאֵילֵי צֹאנְךָ לֹא אָכַלְתִּי׃
verses.transliteration: Zeh esrim shanah anokhi immakh recheelekha ve'izzekha lo shikkelu ve'eihei tzonekha lo akhalti.
verses.study_note: 'This twenty years' (zeh esrim shanah)  -  the opening words of Jacob's great defense speech. He counts the time precisely: fourteen years for the wives (29:20-30), six for the flocks (30:29-31). Twenty years of documented, faithful service. He begins with the ewes: no miscarriages (lo shikkelu  -  not a single lost lamb from poor management). And he never ate the rams  -  a shepherd who eats from the flock he guards reduces his employer's assets; Jacob refused. This was extraordinary professional integrity over two decades.

verses.kjv_text: That which was torn of beasts I brought not unto thee; I bare the loss of it; of my hand didst thou require it, whether stolen by day, or stolen by night.
verses.original_text: טְרֵפָה לֹא־הֵבֵאתִי אֵלֶיךָ אָנֹכִי אֲחַטֶּנָּה מִיָּדִי תְּבַקְשֶׁנָּה גְּנֻבְתִי יוֹם וּגְנֻבְתִי לָיְלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Terefah lo-heveti elekha anokhi achattenah miyadi tevakshennah genuv'ti yom ugenuvti lailah.
verses.study_note: Jacob's conduct exceeded legal requirement. Mosaic Law later stated that a shepherd was not responsible for animals killed by wild beasts if he could produce evidence (Exod 22:13). But Laban required Jacob to personally make good any torn animal (terefah  -  H2966). Jacob bore this unjust liability for twenty years without complaint. 'I bare the loss' (anokhi achattenah  -  'I paid the penalty')  -  a phrase with sacrificial resonance: to 'chata' is to bear sin/failure. Jacob absorbed the loss from his own wages. This detail reveals extraordinary integrity and self-sacrifice.

verses.kjv_text: Thus I was; in the day the drought consumed me, and the frost by night; and my sleep departed from mine eyes.
verses.original_text: הָיִיתִי בַיּוֹם אֲכָלַנִי חֹרֶב וְקֶרַח בַּלָּיְלָה וַתִּדַּד שְׁנָתִי מֵעֵינָי׃
verses.transliteration: Hayiti vayyom akhalani chorev vakerachh ballaylah vatiddad shenati me'einai.
verses.study_note: One of the most vivid personal testimonies in Genesis. Jacob describes the physical toll of twenty years of shepherding in the Mesopotamian climate: 'drought consumed me' (achalani chorev) by day  -  the scorching heat of the Near Eastern summer, and 'frost' (kerach) by night  -  the freezing temperatures of the highland desert nights. 'My sleep departed from mine eyes' (vatiddad shenati me'einai)  -  chronic sleep deprivation from night watching over the flock. This is the cost of faithful service, hidden from all human observers but known to God. The apostle Paul echoes this language in 2 Corinthians 11:27.

verses.kjv_text: Thus have I been twenty years in thy house; I served thee fourteen years for thy two daughters, and six years for thy cattle: and thou hast changed my wages ten times.
verses.original_text: זֶה־לִּי עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה בְּבֵיתֶךָ עֲבַדְתִּיךָ אַרְבַּע עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה בִּשְׁתֵּי בְנֹתֶיךָ וְשֵׁשׁ שָׁנִים בְּצֹאנֶךָ וַתַּחֲלֵף אֶת־מַשְׂכֻּרְתִּי עֲשֶׂרֶת מֹנִים׃
verses.transliteration: Zeh-li esrim shanah beveitekha avadtikha arba esreh shanah bishhtei venotekha veshesh shanim betzanekha vatachalef et-maskorti aseret monim.
verses.study_note: Jacob tallies the years with legal precision: fourteen for the two daughters (7+7, Gen 29:18-30), six for the flocks (30:29-36). The wages were changed 'ten times'  -  the accusation of v.7 is now repeated as Jacob's own testimony, sealed by twenty years of documentation. This verse is the fulcrum of the speech: it establishes the full scope of servitude, the fairness of Jacob's original agreement, and the scope of Laban's exploitation. It is essentially a legal deposition.

verses.kjv_text: Except the God of my father, the God of Abraham, and the fear of Isaac, had been with me, surely thou hadst sent me away now empty. God hath seen mine affliction and the labour of my hands, and rebuked thee yesternight.
verses.original_text: לוּלֵי אֱלֹהֵי אָבִי אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם וּפַחַד יִצְחָק הָיָה לִי כִּי עַתָּה רֵיקָם שִׁלַּחְתָּנִי אֶת־עָנְיִי וְאֶת־יְגִיעַ כַּפַּי רָאָה אֱלֹהִים וַיּוֹכַח אֶמֶשׁ׃
