verses.transliteration: Lulei Elohei avi Elohei Avraham uPachad Yitzhak hayah li ki attah reikam shillachtani et-'onyi ve'et-yegi'a kappai ra'ah Elohim vayyokach emesh.
verses.study_note: The climax of Jacob's great speech. Three names of God in one breath: (1) God of my father, (2) God of Abraham, (3) the Fear of Isaac (Pachad Yitzhak  -  unique to this chapter, vv.42,53). 'Pachad' can mean fear, dread, or the object of reverence/awe  -  the God whom Isaac worshipped with trembling. God is identified through covenant history and through a human patriarch's reverent relationship. 'Except God had been with me, thou hadst sent me away empty' (reikam)  -  the same word used for Israel leaving Egypt laden with silver and gold (Exod 3:21). 'God hath seen mine affliction' (ra'ah... et-onyi)  -  a direct echo of Exodus 3:7, where God sees Israel's affliction. Jacob's life is the individual anticipation of Israel's corporate experience.
verses.doctrinal_note: 'The Fear of Isaac' (Pachad Yitzhak) is one of the most evocative divine titles in Scripture. It captures the reverential awe with which Isaac approached God  -  particularly the near-sacrifice on Moriah (Gen 22), where Isaac learned to fear the Lord in the deepest possible way. This title anchors Jacob's appeal in the specific, personal, historically-rooted encounter of his father with God. It is the God before whom patriarchs trembled in holy awe, not the domesticated god of folk religion.

verses.kjv_text: And Laban answered and said unto Jacob, These daughters are my daughters, and these children are my children, and these cattle are my cattle, and all that thou seest is mine: and what can I do this day unto these my daughters, or unto their children which they have born?
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַן לָבָן וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל־יַעֲקֹב הַבָּנוֹת בְּנֹתַי וְהַבָּנִים בָּנַי וְהַצֹּאן צֹאנִי וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר אַתָּה רֹאֶה לִי הוּא וְלִבְנֹתַי מָה אֶעֱשֶׂה לָאֵלֶּה הַיּוֹם אוֹ לִבְנֵיהֶן אֲשֶׁר יָלָדוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'an Lavan vayomer el-Yaakov habbanot venotai vehannanim banai vehatzon tzoni vekhol asher attah ro'eh li hu velivnotai meh e'eseh la'elleh hayyom o livneihen asher yaladu.
verses.study_note: Laban's response to Jacob's unanswerable speech is remarkable for what it does NOT contain: no refutation, no counter-accusation that survives examination, no apology. He simply reasserts his claim over everything  -  daughters, children, flocks, all possessions. 'All that thou seest is mine' (kol asher attah ro'eh li hu). The claim is maximalist, possessive, and hollow. Immediately he pivots: 'what can I do?' (meh e'eseh)  -  the first admission of helplessness. The God of Bethel has constrained him. The speech collapses into a proposal for covenant (v.44).

verses.kjv_text: Now therefore come thou, let us make a covenant, I and thou; and let it be for a witness between me and thee.
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה לְכָה נִכְרְתָה בְרִית אֲנִי וָאָתָּה וְהָיָה לְעֵד בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah lekhah nikhretah verit ani va'attah vehayah le'ed beini uveinekha.
verses.study_note: 'Let us make a covenant' (nikhretah verit  -  literally 'let us cut a covenant'). The verb 'cut' (karat) is the standard Hebrew word for establishing a covenant  -  reflecting the ancient practice of cutting animals as part of the ceremony (cf. Gen 15:10; Jer 34:18). The parties 'cut' through the animals and walk between them, invoking on themselves the fate of the slaughtered animal if they violate the covenant. Laban proposes the covenant; Jacob accepts. The word 'witness' (ed) appears here and will be built into the very names of the memorial stones (vv.47-48,52).

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob took a stone, and set it up for a pillar.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח יַעֲקֹב אָבֶן וַיְרִימֶהָ מַצֵּבָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach Yaakov even vayyerimehah matstsevah.
verses.study_note: Jacob erects a pillar (matstsevah)  -  the same word used for the stone he erected at Bethel (28:18) and for the pillar promised in his vow (28:22). The pillar-monument is Jacob's characteristic form of covenant memorial. This single stone, erected by Jacob, functions as the first element of the memorial complex. The communal cairn (v.46) will be added next, involving all the servants as witnesses. Stone memorials throughout Genesis (28:18; 31:45; 35:14; 35:20) mark the places where God's covenant intersects with human history.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob said unto his brethren, Gather stones; and they took stones, and made an heap: and they did eat there upon the heap.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב לְאֶחָיו לִקְטוּ אֲבָנִים וַיִּקְחוּ אֲבָנִים וַיַּעֲשׂוּ גָל וַיֹּאכְלוּ שָׁם עַל־הַגָּל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yaakov le'echav liKtu avanim vayyikchu avanim vayya'asu gal vayyokhlu sham al-haggal.
verses.study_note: Jacob calls his 'brethren' (kinsmen, servants) to gather stones  -  a communal act creating a cairn (gal  -  heap of stones). The covenant meal 'upon the heap' is a sealing ceremony: eating together over the witness monument ratifies the agreement. Covenant meals appear throughout Scripture as acts of solemn confirmation (Gen 26:30; Exod 24:11; the Passover; the Last Supper). The communal gathering of stones differs from Jacob's single pillar  -  together they form the complete witness structure. All witnesses participate in building what will testify to their presence.

verses.kjv_text: And Laban called it Jegarsahadutha: but Jacob called it Galeed.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא־לוֹ לָבָן יְגַר שָׂהֲדוּתָא וְיַעֲקֹב קָרָא לוֹ גַּלְעֵד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra-lo Lavan Yegar Sahadutha veYaakov kara lo Gal'ed.
verses.study_note: A bilingual naming  -  a unique moment in Genesis. Laban names the cairn in Aramaic: 'Jegarsahadutha' (Yegar Sahadutha  -  'heap of witness' in Aramaic). Jacob names it in Hebrew: 'Galeed' (Gal'ed  -  'heap of witness' in Hebrew). Same meaning, two languages, two peoples, two worlds. Laban is an Aramean; Jacob is the Hebrew. The very name of the place records the cultural and linguistic boundary being formalized. Laban's Aramaic will become the language of the Jews in exile (Ezra, Daniel); Jacob's Hebrew remains the language of the covenant. The bilingual monument marks the threshold.

verses.kjv_text: And Laban said, This heap is a witness between me and thee this day. Therefore was the name of it called Galeed;
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן הַגַּל הַזֶּה עֵד בֵּינִי וּבֵינְךָ הַיּוֹם עַל־כֵּן קָרָא־שְׁמוֹ גַּלְעֵד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Lavan haggal hazzeh ed beini uveinekha hayyom al-ken kara-shmo Gal'ed.
verses.study_note: Laban formally declares the witness function of the cairn: 'This heap is a witness' (haggal hazzeh ed). The cairn becomes a permanent, public record of the covenant boundary. 'This day' (hayyom)  -  the legal formula marking the precise day of agreement. The name Galeed encodes the terms: 'ed' (witness) is built into the stone's name forever. The region of Gilead in Israel's later history takes its name from this moment  -  the land east of the Jordan will always carry the memory of Jacob's covenant separation from Laban.

verses.kjv_text: And Mizpah; for he said, The LORD watch between me and thee, when we are absent one from another.
verses.original_text: וְהַמִּצְפָּה אֲשֶׁר אָמַר יִצֹף יְהוָה בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶךָ כִּי נִסָּתֵר אִישׁ מֵרֵעֵהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehammmitzpah asher amar yitzof YHVH beini uveinekha ki nissater ish meree'hu.
verses.study_note: The 'Mizpah benediction'  -  one of the most famous verses in Genesis, inscribed on rings, jewelry, and church bulletins as a blessing: 'The LORD watch between me and thee.' But in its original context this is NOT a blessing but a surveillance pact between two men who cannot trust each other. 'When we are absent one from another'  -  when neither can watch the other. God is invoked as the mutual watchman because they distrust each other too much to rely on human witnesses alone. 'Mizpah' (Mitzpah  -  H4709) means 'watchtower' or 'lookout.' The LORD is the watchman on the tower, observing both parties from His elevated position to ensure the covenant is kept.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Mizpah verse illustrates the danger of context-stripped Bible use. As a mutual surveillance agreement between two estranged men, it is a solemn covenant warning, not a romantic blessing. However, God's role as Watcher (yitzof  -  the same root as tzafah, to look out, to keep watch) is theologically profound and true: God does watch over all human relationships and covenants, even those made between unbelievers. The God who sees all (El Roi, Gen 16:13) is also the God who watches all (Mitzpah).

verses.kjv_text: If thou shalt afflict my daughters, or if thou shalt take other wives beside my daughters, no man is with us; see, God is witness betwixt me and thee.
verses.original_text: אִם־תְּעַנֶּה אֶת־בְּנֹתַי וְאִם־תִּקַּח נָשִׁים עַל־בְּנֹתַי אֵין אִישׁ עִמָּנוּ רְאֵה אֱלֹהִים עֵד בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Im-te'anneh et-benotai ve'im-tiqqach nashim al-benotai ein ish immanu re'eh Elohim ed beini uveinekha.
verses.study_note: The specific covenant terms: Jacob must not 'afflict' (te'anneh  -  H6031, to oppress, to humble, to deal harshly with) his wives, and must not take additional wives beyond them. This is essentially a marriage covenant stipulation, enforced by God as sole witness. 'No man is with us' (ein ish immanu)  -  in the wilderness and distance, there will be no human enforcement mechanism. The covenant relies entirely on divine oversight. The term 'afflict' (anah) is significant  -  the same root describes Israel's affliction in Egypt (Exod 1:11-12).

verses.kjv_text: And Laban said to Jacob, Behold this heap, and behold this pillar, which I have cast betwixt me and thee:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן לְיַעֲקֹב הִנֵּה הַגַּל הַזֶּה וְהִנֵּה הַמַּצֵּבָה אֲשֶׁר יָרִיתִי בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Lavan leYaakov hinneh haggal hazzeh vehinneh hammattsevah asher yariti beini uveinekha.
verses.study_note: Laban formally points to both monuments  -  the cairn (gal, v.46, erected communally) and the pillar (matstsevah, v.45, erected by Jacob). Both serve as witnesses to the covenant boundary. 'Which I have cast betwixt me and thee' (yariti  -  from 'yarah,' to throw/hurl)  -  the pillar that Jacob set up, Laban now claims as his own contribution. This small possessiveness is characteristic of Laban, but the legal significance stands: both parties acknowledge the monuments as boundary markers.

verses.kjv_text: This heap be witness, and this pillar be witness, that I will not pass over this heap to thee, and that thou shalt not pass over this heap and this pillar unto me, for harm.
verses.original_text: עֵד הַגַּל הַזֶּה וְעֵדָה הַמַּצֵּבָה אִם־אֲנִי לֹא אֶעֱבֹר אֵלֶיךָ אֶת־הַגַּל הַזֶּה וְאִם־אַתָּה לֹא תַעֲבֹר אֵלַי אֶת־הַגַּל הַזֶּה וְאֶת־הַמַּצֵּבָה הַזֹּאת לְרָעָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ed haggal hazzeh ve'edah hammattsevah im-ani lo e'evor elekha et-haggal hazzeh ve'im-attah lo ta'avor elai et-haggal hazzeh ve'et-hammattsevah hazzo't lera'ah.
verses.study_note: The legal terms of the boundary covenant stated with full legal precision: neither party shall 'pass over' (avor  -  cross the boundary) for harm (ra'ah). The repeated phrase 'this heap' (haggal hazzeh) emphasizes the physical, permanent nature of the boundary. The covenant is bilateral and symmetrical: both Laban and Jacob are bound. This is not a territorial treaty (Canaan vs. Aram) but a relational boundary  -  Jacob's family will be protected from Laban's interference. Crossing for harm would invoke divine judgment (v.53).

verses.kjv_text: The God of Abraham, and the God of Nahor, the God of their father, judge betwixt us. And Jacob sware by the fear of his father Isaac.
verses.original_text: אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם וֵאלֹהֵי נָחוֹר יִשְׁפְּטוּ בֵינֵינוּ אֱלֹהֵי אֲבִיהֶם וַיִּשָּׁבַע יַעֲקֹב בְּפַחַד אָבִיו יִצְחָק׃
verses.transliteration: Elohei Avraham velohei Nachor yishpetu veineinu Elohei avihem vayyishava Yaakov bePachad aviv Yitzhak.
verses.study_note: Laban invokes the God of Abraham AND the God of Nahor (their common ancestor, Gen 11:27-29). The plural verb 'judge' (yishpetu) is grammatically significant  -  Laban treats 'God of Abraham' and 'God of Nahor' as potentially two different deities in a polytheistic framework. But Jacob swears only by the 'Fear of his father Isaac'  -  the one, specific, covenant God. He will not swear by any Nahorite deity. This single act of worship in the oath reveals the profound theological chasm between them: Laban is a syncretist; Jacob is a monotheist.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jacob's refusal to swear by 'the God of Nahor' reveals the exclusive monotheism of the covenant. Even while making a covenant with a pagan relative, Jacob will not acknowledge any other deity as his God. The Fear of Isaac is his God alone. This exclusivity anticipates the First Commandment (Exod 20:3: 'no other gods before Me') and the Shema (Deut 6:4: 'the LORD is one').

verses.kjv_text: Then Jacob offered sacrifice upon the mount, and called his brethren to eat bread: and they did eat bread, and tarried all night in the mount.
verses.original_text: וַיִּזְבַּח יַעֲקֹב זֶבַח בָּהָר וַיִּקְרָא לְאֶחָיו לֶאֱכָל לָחֶם וַיֹּאכְלוּ לֶחֶם וַיָּלִינוּ בָהָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyizbach Yaakov zevach bahar vayyikra le'echav le'ekhol lachem vayyokhlu lachem vayyalinu vahar.
verses.study_note: Jacob seals the covenant with sacrifice (zevach  -  a fellowship/peace offering, not a burnt offering  -  this is a shared meal with God). The covenant meal on the mountain  -  bread eaten together before God  -  is the ancient form of covenant ratification. All the 'brethren' (kinsmen from both parties) eat together: a communal act of solemn agreement. 'Tarried all night in the mount'  -  the covenant is witnessed by darkness and the following dawn, the moment of Laban's departure. This sacrificial meal anticipates every covenant meal in Scripture, culminating in the Lord's Supper.

verses.kjv_text: And early in the morning Laban rose up, and kissed his sons and his daughters, and blessed them: and Laban departed, and returned unto his place.
verses.original_text: וַיַּשְׁכֵּם לָבָן בַּבֹּקֶר וַיְנַשֵּׁק לְבָנָיו וְלִבְנוֹתָיו וַיְבָרֶךְ אֶתְהֶם וַיֵּלֶךְ וַיָּשָׁב לָבָן לִמְקֹמוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashkem Lavan labboker vayenashek levannav velivnotav vayevarekh ethem vayyeelekh vayyashov Lavan limkomo.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes with a poignant scene. Laban 'rose early' (vayyashkem  -  the same word used for Abraham's early rising on Moriah, Gen 22:3) and kissed his grandchildren and daughters. He 'blessed them' (vayevarekh)  -  the only recorded blessing Laban gives in the entire narrative. Then 'Laban departed' (vayyeelekh Lavan)  -  the long antagonist of Jacob's story walks back toward Haran, back to his teraphim-less household, back to 'his place' (limkomo). The phrase 'returned to his place' is final  -  Laban disappears from the biblical narrative. Jacob is now free, covenant-protected, and heading toward Canaan with the blessing of God and the boundary of Mizpah behind him.
verses.doctrinal_note: The closing of chapter 31 marks the end of Jacob's Mesopotamian exile and the beginning of his return to Canaan and covenant fulfillment. Everything God promised at Bethel (28:15: 'I will bring thee again into this land') is now in motion. The twenty years were not wasted  -  Jacob leaves with wives, children, and flocks. God's providence turned exploitation into blessing. The pattern is the pattern of Scripture: suffering, faithfulness, divine vindication, return. It is the pattern of Joseph, of Israel in Egypt, and ultimately of Christ Himself.

meta.overview: Genesis 31 is one of the most dramatically rich chapters in the patriarchal narratives. After twenty years of servitude under Laban  -  fourteen for his wives, six for his flocks  -  Jacob receives a divine command to return to Canaan. Sensing mounting hostility from Laban and his sons, Jacob secretly departs with his wives, children, and livestock. Rachel complicates the flight by stealing her father's household gods (teraphim), and when Laban pursues and catches them in the hill country of Gilead, a tense confrontation unfolds. Jacob's passionate speech (vv.36-42) stands as one of the great defenses of character in Scripture  -  twenty years of faithful labor summarized in burning words. The chapter resolves with a covenant of separation, sealed by a cairn of stones called both 'Galeed' and 'Mizpah'  -  the watchtower between two men who can no longer trust each other. The Mizpah benediction (v.49), often used as a blessing, is in its original context a mutual surveillance pact. Throughout, God's hand is visible: warning Laban in a dream, vindicating Jacob, and overseeing the transition of His covenant people from bondage to freedom  -  a pattern that foreshadows the Exodus.
meta.theme: Providence, Covenant Separation & the Vindication of the Faithful
meta.keyWordMeaning: The Fear/Dread of Isaac  -  a unique divine title found only in this chapter (vv.42, 53). It describes God as the awe-inspiring Deity whom Isaac reverenced, anchoring Jacob's appeal to covenantal history and the God who watches over the patriarchs.
outline: God Commands Jacob's Return (vv.1-13): Laban's hostility; the angel's instruction; Jacob recounts his dream of the striped flocks
Rachel and Leah Support the Departure (vv.14-16): The daughters denounce their father's greed
The Secret Flight  -  Teraphim Stolen (vv.17-21): Jacob departs; Rachel takes Laban's household gods
The Pursuit  -  Laban Warned by God (vv.22-24): Seven days of chase; God speaks to Laban in a night dream
Laban's Confrontation (vv.25-35): The accusation; the search for the teraphim; Rachel's concealment
Jacob's Great Defense Speech (vv.36-42): Twenty years of faithful service; the God of Abraham and the Fear of Isaac
The Covenant at Galeed/Mizpah (vv.43-55): The cairn of witness; the boundary covenant; Laban's departure

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob went on his way, and the angels of God met him.
verses.original_text: וְיַעֲקֹב הָלַךְ לְדַרְכּוֹ וַיִּפְגְּעוּ־בוֹ מַלְאֲכֵי אֱלֹהִים׃
verses.transliteration: Veya'akov halakh ledarko vayyifge'u-vo mal'akhei Elohim.
verses.study_note: Jacob departs from Laban and immediately encounters 'the angels of God' (mal'akhei Elohim). The verb 'vayifge'u' (met, encountered) is the same root used for the place-name Mahanaim and anticipates the night encounter at Jabbok. This is no coincidence — God frames Jacob's homeward journey with heavenly presence at both ends. Just as angels attended Abraham's servant (Gen 24), and as they appeared at Bethel on Jacob's outward journey (28:12), they appear again at this critical turn. After twenty years of exile, God's escort is visible. The encounter answers the Bethel promise: 'I am with thee, and will keep thee' (28:15).

verses.kjv_text: And when Jacob saw them, he said, This is God’s host: and he called the name of that place Mahanaim.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב כַּאֲשֶׁר רָאָם מַחֲנֵה אֱלֹהִים זֶה וַיִּקְרָא שֵׁם־הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא מַחֲנָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Ya'akov ka'asher ra'am machaneh Elohim zeh vayyikra shem-hammakom hahu Machanaim.
verses.study_note: Jacob names the place Mahanaim — 'Two Camps' or 'Double Camp' — from machaneh (camp, army, host). The dual form (-ayim) suggests two distinct camps: the heavenly host he has just seen and his own earthly company. The naming is theologically significant: Jacob perceives himself as flanked by divine protection. This name reappears throughout Israel's history — Mahanaim later becomes a Levitical city and a refuge for David (2 Sam 17:24). The double camp motif is also strategic: when Jacob divides his company in v.7-8, he is literally enacting the very name God revealed here.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob sent messengers before him to Esau his brother unto the land of Seir, the country of Edom.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁלַח יַעֲקֹב מַלְאָכִים לְפָנָיו אֶל־עֵשָׂו אָחִיו אַרְצָה שֵׂעִיר שְׂדֵה אֱדוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishlach Ya'akov mal'akhim lefanav el-Esav achiv artzah Se'ir sdeh Edom.
verses.study_note: Jacob sends 'messengers' (mal'akhim) — the same word as 'angels' in vv.1-2. The wordplay is deliberate: as God's mal'akhim met Jacob, now Jacob sends his own mal'akhim to Esau. But Jacob's messengers carry anxiety, not assurance. Twenty years have not healed his fear of Esau. 'Seir... Edom' — Esau settled southeast of Canaan in the rugged hill country. The territory becomes identified with his descendants throughout the prophets (Obadiah, Isaiah 34, Ezekiel 35). Edom and Israel represent two permanently diverging lines from one womb.

verses.kjv_text: And he commanded them, saying, Thus shall ye speak unto my lord Esau; Thy servant Jacob saith thus, I have sojourned with Laban, and stayed there until now:
verses.original_text: וַיְצַו אֹתָם לֵאמֹר כֹּה תֹאמְרוּן לַאדֹנִי לְעֵשָׂו כֹּה אָמַר עַבְדְּךָ יַעֲקֹב עִם־לָבָן גַּרְתִּי וָאֵחַר עַד־עָתָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayetzav otam lemor koh tomrun la'adoni leEsav koh amar avdekha Ya'akov im-Lavan garti va'echar ad-attah.
verses.study_note: Jacob instructs his messengers with carefully crafted diplomacy. He calls Esau 'my lord' (adoni) and refers to himself as 'your servant' (avdekha) — a complete reversal of the roles established by Isaac's blessing (27:29, 'be lord over thy brethren'). The man who supplanted the blessing now voluntarily surrenders the language of dominion. 'I have sojourned' (garti) — the same root as 'ger' (stranger, sojourner). Jacob presents himself as a temporary alien, not an aggressor. He says nothing of the blessing, nothing of Bethel's promises.

verses.kjv_text: And I have oxen, and asses, flocks, and menservants, and womenservants: and I have sent to tell my lord, that I may find grace in thy sight.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי־לִי שׁוֹר וַחֲמוֹר צֹאן וְעֶבֶד וְשִׁפְחָה וָאֶשְׁלְחָה לְהַגִּיד לַאדֹנִי לִמְצֹא־חֵן בְּעֵינֶיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi-li shor vachamor tzon ve'eved veshifchah va'eshlachah lehagid la'adoni limtzo-chen be'einekha.
verses.study_note: Jacob lists his wealth — oxen, donkeys, flocks, servants — not to boast but to signal that he does not want Esau's inheritance. He is not coming back to claim anything. The purpose statement is crucial: 'that I may find grace in thy sight' (limtzo-chen be'einekha). The word 'chen' (grace, favour) appears here for the first time in connection with Jacob. The man who grasped (Jacob means 'he grasps') now asks for grace — unmerited favour. This language anticipates his prayer (vv.9-12) where he asks God for the same thing.

verses.kjv_text: And the messengers returned to Jacob, saying, We came to thy brother Esau, and also he cometh to meet thee, and four hundred men with him.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשֻׁבוּ הַמַּלְאָכִים אֶל־יַעֲקֹב לֵאמֹר בָּאנוּ אֶל־אָחִיךָ אֶל־עֵשָׂו וְגַם הֹלֵךְ לִקְרָאתְךָ וְאַרְבַּע־מֵאוֹת אִישׁ עִמּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashuvu hammal'akhim el-Ya'akov lemor banu el-achikha el-Esav vegam holekh likratekha ve'arba-me'ot ish immo.
verses.study_note: The messengers return with terrifying news: Esau is coming — with four hundred men. Four hundred is a significant military number (1 Sam 22:2, 25:13, 30:10). The report gives no indication of Esau's intentions; the ambiguity is part of the terror. The messengers call Esau 'your brother' — a designation that doubles as comfort and threat. He is kin, but armed. The phrase 'to meet thee' (likratekha) is neutral; it can mean hostile approach or peaceful reception. Jacob does not wait to find out: his response in v.7 reveals the depth of his fear.

verses.kjv_text: Then Jacob was greatly afraid and distressed: and he divided the people that was with him, and the flocks, and herds, and the camels, into two bands;
verses.original_text: וַיִּירָא יַעֲקֹב מְאֹד וַיֵּצֶר לוֹ וַיַּחַץ אֶת־הָעָם אֲשֶׁר־אִתּוֹ וְאֶת־הַצֹּאן וְאֶת־הַבָּקָר וְהַגְּמַלִּים לִשְׁנֵי מַחֲנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyira Ya'akov me'od vayyetzer lo vayyachatz et-ha'am asher-itto ve'et-hatzon ve'et-habbakar vehaggemalim lishnei machanot.
verses.study_note: The emotional portrait is precise: Jacob was 'greatly afraid' (vayira me'od) and 'distressed' (vayyetzer lo — 'it was narrow for him,' a physical sense of being hemmed in, constricted). Two distinct emotional states are named: terror at the external threat and interior anguish. His strategic response is to divide (vayyachatz) his company into two camps (machanot) — the very word of Mahanaim (v.2). God had shown him two camps; Jacob now creates two camps. Even Jacob's fear becomes an unconscious enactment of the divine name he had just declared.

verses.kjv_text: And said, If Esau come to the one company, and smite it, then the other company which is left shall escape.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אִם־יָבוֹא עֵשָׂו אֶל־הַמַּחֲנֶה הָאַחַת וְהִכָּהוּ וְהָיָה הַמַּחֲנֶה הַנִּשְׁאָר לִפְלֵיטָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer im-yavo Esav el-hamachaneh ha'achat vehikkahu vehayah hamachaneh hannish'ar lifletah.
verses.study_note: Jacob's logic: divide so that if one camp is destroyed, the other escapes (lifletah — to deliverance/escape). The word 'peletah' is elsewhere used for the remnant that survives catastrophe (Ezra 9:8, Isaiah 37:32). Jacob thinks like a general — but the strategy is entirely self-reliant. He has learned nothing from twenty years of witnessing God's miraculous provision. The prayer that follows (vv.9-12) will correct this, but the prayer itself comes after the strategy, not before — revealing Jacob's default to human wisdom before divine dependence.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob said, O God of my father Abraham, and God of my father Isaac, the LORD which saidst unto me, Return unto thy country, and to thy kindred, and I will deal well with thee:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב אֱלֹהֵי אָבִי אַבְרָהָם וֵאלֹהֵי אָבִי יִצְחָק יְהוָה הָאֹמֵר אֵלַי שׁוּב לְאַרְצְךָ וּלְמוֹלַדְתְּךָ וְאֵיטִיבָה עִמָּךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Ya'akov Elohei avi Avraham vElohei avi Yitschak YHVH ha'omer elai shuv le'artzekha ulemolad'tekha ve'eitavah immakh.
verses.study_note: Jacob's prayer (vv.9-12) is one of the most carefully structured prayers in the Old Testament, constituting a masterclass in biblical intercession. It has four movements: (1) Address — God identified by covenant history (v.9a), (2) Claim — God's own command invoked (v.9b), (3) Confession of unworthiness (v.10), (4) Petition grounded in covenant promise (vv.11-12). He opens by naming God through Abraham and Isaac — not general deity but the God of specific covenant history. He then immediately cites God's own command to return (31:3) as the basis of his appeal. He is not asking for something new; he is asking God to fulfill what He already initiated.
verses.doctrinal_note: Biblical prayer begins with address rooted in covenant history. Jacob does not approach God as a stranger. He names God through the chain of promises — Abraham, Isaac, and now himself. This pattern becomes normative in Israel (Exod 3:6, Acts 3:13) and culminates in Christ who teaches us to address God as 'Father' — the ultimate covenant address. We pray on the basis of who God has proven Himself to be, not on our own worthiness.

verses.kjv_text: I am not worthy of the least of all the mercies, and of all the truth, which thou hast shewed unto thy servant; for with my staff I passed over this Jordan; and now I am become two bands.
verses.original_text: קָטֹנְתִּי מִכֹּל הַחֲסָדִים וּמִכָּל־הָאֱמֶת אֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתָ אֶת־עַבְדֶּךָ כִּי בְמַקְלִי עָבַרְתִּי אֶת־הַיַּרְדֵּן הַזֶּה וְעַתָּה הָיִיתִי לִשְׁנֵי מַחֲנוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Katonti mikol hachasadim umikol-ha'emet asher asita et-avdekha ki vemakli avarti et-haYarden hazzeh ve'attah hayiti lishnei machanot.
verses.study_note: The confession of unworthiness: 'I am not worthy of the least of all the mercies' (katonti — I have become small, I am too small). This is remarkable language from the man who seized the birthright and supplanted the blessing. Jacob now measures himself against God's goodness and finds himself utterly insufficient. He cites the covenant word pair: 'mercies' (chasadim — plural of chesed, covenant love) and 'truth' (emet — faithfulness). God's chesed and emet together form the foundation of His covenant character. Jacob left Canaan with only a staff (makli); he returns with two companies (machanot). The contrast quantifies God's faithfulness over twenty years.
verses.doctrinal_note: Genuine prayer requires genuine humility. 'Katonti' (I am too small) is the prayer posture that precedes answered prayer. This is the same spirit as Abraham ('I am but dust and ashes,' 18:27), as Gideon ('my family is the poorest,' Judges 6:15), and as Paul ('less than the least of all saints,' Eph 3:8). The pattern of grace: God enlarges those who make themselves small.

verses.kjv_text: Deliver me, I pray thee, from the hand of my brother, from the hand of Esau: for I fear him, lest he will come and smite me, and the mother with the children.
verses.original_text: הַצִּילֵנִי נָא מִיַּד אָחִי מִיַּד עֵשָׂו כִּי יָרֵא אָנֹכִי אֹתוֹ פֶּן־יָבוֹא וְהִכַּנִי אֵם עַל־בָּנִים׃
verses.transliteration: Hatzileni na miyad achi miyad Esav ki yare anokhi oto pen-yavo vehikkani em al-banim.
verses.study_note: The petition itself: 'Deliver me' (hatzileni) — the Hiphil imperative of 'natzal,' to rescue, to snatch away. This is the language of urgent, desperate rescue. Note that Jacob names the threat twice — 'from the hand of my brother' and 'from the hand of Esau' — as if the double identification intensifies the appeal. The phrase 'the mother with the children' (em al-banim) is a proverbial expression for the most vulnerable members of a family unit, the image of total annihilation. Jacob's transparency here is commendable: 'I fear him' (yare anokhi oto) — no pretense of courage, just honest admission of terror. God honours such transparency.

verses.kjv_text: And thou saidst, I will surely do thee good, and make thy seed as the sand of the sea, which cannot be numbered for multitude.
verses.original_text: וְאַתָּה אָמַרְתָּ הֵיטֵב אֵיטִיב עִמָּךְ וְשַׂמְתִּי אֶת־זַרְעֲךָ כְּחוֹל הַיָּם אֲשֶׁר לֹא־יִסָּפֵר מֵרֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah amarta heitav etiv immakh vesamti et-zar'akha kehol hayyam asher lo-yisafer merov.
verses.study_note: The prayer closes with Jacob's most powerful argument: 'thou saidst' (attah amarta). He does not plead his righteousness — he quotes God's promise back to God. 'I will surely do thee good' (heitav etiv immakh — the emphatic infinitive absolute construction meaning absolute certainty). This is the divine promise of Bethel (28:15) and the return command (31:3) now held before God as the ground of rescue. The 'sand of the sea' echo also invokes the Abrahamic covenant (22:17) — Jacob appeals not merely to personal promise but to the entire redemptive trajectory. His seed is covenant seed.
verses.doctrinal_note: The highest form of prayer is pleading God's promises back to Him. Jacob does not argue his merit — he argues God's word. This is the pattern of the greatest intercessors: Moses (Exod 32:13), David (2 Sam 7:25-29), Elijah (1 Kings 18:36-37), and supremely Christ (John 17:6, 'thine they were, and thou gavest them me'). Prayer is not persuading God to do what He does not want to do; it is claiming what He has already promised.

verses.kjv_text: And he lodged there that same night; and took of that which came to his hand a present for Esau his brother;
verses.original_text: וַיָּלֶן שָׁם בַּלַּיְלָה הַהוּא וַיִּקַּח מִן־הַבָּא בְיָדוֹ מִנְחָה לְעֵשָׂו אָחִיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyalen sham ballaylah hahu vayyikkach min-habba beyado minchah leEsav achiv.
verses.study_note: After praying, Jacob does not simply wait on God — he acts. He takes 'a present' (minchah) for Esau. The word minchah is the standard term for a tribute or gift offered to a superior; it is also the word for a grain offering in the sacrificial system. Jacob presents Esau with the language of worship, the posture of a supplicant before a king. The same night that contains his prayer also contains his planning — prayer and prudent action coexist. He does not use prayer as an excuse for passivity; neither does he use activity as a substitute for prayer.

verses.kjv_text: Two hundred she goats, and twenty he goats, two hundred ewes, and twenty rams,
verses.original_text: עִזִּים מָאתַיִם וּתְיָשִׁים עֶשְׂרִים רְחֵלִים מָאתַיִם וְאֵילִים עֶשְׂרִים׃
verses.transliteration: Izzim matayim uteyyashim esrim rechelim matayim ve'eilim esrim.
verses.study_note: The gift is staggering in its scale. The pattern is consistent: females 10:1 over males, with each sub-herd led by males who ensure future reproduction. Jacob is not giving mere surplus — he is transferring the capacity to generate future wealth. The mathematical precision (10:1 ratio throughout) reveals Jacob the strategist. Total animals: 550 from these two lists alone (vv.14-15). This is Jacob's wealth turned into a wave of appeasement that Esau must walk through before he reaches Jacob.

verses.kjv_text: Thirty milch camels with their colts, forty kine, and ten bulls, twenty she asses, and ten foals.
verses.original_text: גְּמַלִּים מֵינִיקוֹת וּבְנֵיהֶם שְׁלֹשִׁים פָּרוֹת אַרְבָּעִים וּפָרִים עֲשָׂרָה אֲתֹנֹת עֶשְׂרִים וַעֲיָרִם עֲשָׂרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Gemalim menikot uvneihem sheloshim parot arba'im ufarim asarah atonot esrim va'ayarim asarah.
verses.study_note: The full inventory continues: nursing camels with their young (especially valuable — nursing camels produce milk and their presence ensures their young's compliance), forty cows, ten bulls, twenty female donkeys, ten foals. The nursing camels are the premier luxury gift. Female camels in milk were among the most prized possessions in the ancient Near East. Jacob sends his best. The total gift catalogue amounts to 550 animals — a fortune. He who once tricked his way to a blessing now lavishes away wealth to seek reconciliation.

verses.kjv_text: And he delivered them into the hand of his servants, every drove by themselves; and said unto his servants, Pass over before me, and put a space betwixt drove and drove.
verses.original_text: וַיִּתֵּן בְּיַד־עֲבָדָיו עֵדֶר עֵדֶר לְבַדּוֹ וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל־עֲבָדָיו עִבְרוּ לְפָנַי וְרֶוַח תָּשִׂימוּ בֵּין עֵדֶר וּבֵין עֵדֶר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitten beyad-avadav eder eder levaddo vayomer el-avadav ivru lefanai verevach tasismu bein eder uvein eder.
verses.study_note: Jacob's military-diplomatic genius: each herd is separated by space (revach — interval, breathing room). Esau will encounter wave after wave of gifts before he ever reaches Jacob. The psychological effect is studied: each servant group has their own script (vv.17-20), and each encounter softens Esau before the next. This is wisdom applied to a relational crisis — not deception (as before) but strategic generosity. The space between droves is the space that gives Esau time to receive and reflect before the next gift arrives.

verses.kjv_text: And he commanded the foremost, saying, When Esau my brother meeteth thee, and asketh thee, saying, Whose art thou? and whither goest thou? and whose are these before thee?
verses.original_text: וַיְצַו אֶת־הָרִאשׁוֹן לֵאמֹר כִּי יִפְגָּשְׁךָ עֵשָׂו אָחִי וּשְׁאֵלְךָ לֵאמֹר לְמִי אַתָּה וְאָנָה תֵלֵךְ וּלְמִי אֵלֶּה לְפָנֶיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayetzav et-harishon lemor ki yifgashkha Esav achi ushe'elekha lemor lemi attah ve'anah telekh ulemi elleh lefanekha.
verses.study_note: Jacob scripts his servants' responses. The three questions he anticipates — Who are you? Where are you going? Whose are these? — are the natural questions of a lord encountering a procession on the road. Jacob prepares each servant with a uniform answer. The repetition of his script across three waves (vv.17-20) is a narrative technique that builds tension and reveals the mechanical precision of Jacob's planning. He has rehearsed every contingency except the most important one — the night alone at the ford.

verses.kjv_text: Then thou shalt say, They be thy servant Jacob’s; it is a present sent unto my lord Esau: and, behold, also he is behind us.
verses.original_text: וְאָמַרְתָּ לְעַבְדְּךָ לְיַעֲקֹב מִנְחָה הִיא שְׁלוּחָה לַאדֹנִי לְעֵשָׂו וְהִנֵּה גַם־הוּא אַחֲרֵינוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'amarta le'avdekha leYa'akov minchah hi shelucha la'adoni leEsav vehineh gam-hu acharenu.
verses.study_note: The scripted answer has a fourfold movement: (1) Identity — thy servant Jacob, (2) Purpose — a present (minchah), (3) Recipient — my lord Esau, (4) Announcement — he is behind us. The servant language frames Jacob entirely as a subordinate. 'My lord Esau' appears five times in these verses (cf. v.4,5,18) — Jacob deliberately dismantles the linguistic framework of the stolen blessing. He who once made Esau serve him now places himself below Esau in every statement.

verses.kjv_text: And so commanded he the second, and the third, and all that followed the droves, saying, On this manner shall ye speak unto Esau, when ye find him.
verses.original_text: וַיְצַו גַּם אֶת־הַשֵּׁנִי גַּם אֶת־הַשְּׁלִישִׁי גַּם אֶת־כָּל־הַהֹלְכִים אַחֲרֵי הָעֲדָרִים לֵאמֹר כַּדָּבָר הַזֶּה תְּדַבְּרוּן אֶל־עֵשָׂו בְּמֹצַאֲכֶם אֹתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayetzav gam et-hasheni gam et-hashelishi gam et-kol-haholkhim acharei ha'adarim lemor kaddavar hazzeh tedabbrun el-Esav bemotz'akhem oto.
verses.study_note: The repetition of commands to the second and third droves, and all who follow, underscores the systematic nature of Jacob's planning. The words 'kaddavar hazzeh' (according to this word/according to this manner) signal that Jacob's script is uniform — no variation, no improvisation. The repetition in the narrative mirrors the waves of gifts: the reader experiences what Esau will experience, wave after wave of the same submission.

verses.kjv_text: And say ye moreover, Behold, thy servant Jacob is behind us. For he said, I will appease him with the present that goeth before me, and afterward I will see his face; peradventure he will accept of me.
verses.original_text: וְאַמַרְתֶּם גַּם הִנֵּה עַבְדְּךָ יַעֲקֹב אַחֲרֵינוּ כִּי אָמַר אֲכַפְּרָה פָנָיו בַּמִּנְחָה הַהֹלֶכֶת לְפָנַי וְאַחֲרֵי כֵן אֶרְאֶה פָנָיו אוּלַי יִשָּׂא פָנָי׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'amartem gam hinneh avdekha Ya'akov acharenu ki amar akapprah fanav bamminchah haholekhet lefanai ve'acharei khen er'eh fanav ulai yissa fanai.
verses.study_note: The climax of Jacob's strategy is revealed in his internal reasoning: 'I will appease' (akapprah) — the word is the Piel form of 'kipper,' the root of the great atonement word 'kapparah.' Jacob uses atonement language for his appeasement strategy! 'Akapprah fanav' literally means 'I will cover his face' — the gift covering Esau's angry gaze. The same root will become the word for the mercy seat ('kapporet,' Exod 25:17). Jacob's gift as appeasement is a profound unintentional picture of the principle of atonement: covering wrath through an offering interposed between the offender and the offended.

verses.kjv_text: So went the present over before him: and himself lodged that night in the company.
verses.original_text: וַתַּעֲבֹר הַמִּנְחָה עַל־פָּנָיו וְהוּא לָן בַּלַּיְלָה הַהוּא בַּמַּחֲנֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vatta'avor hamminchah al-panav vehu lan ballaylah hahu bamachaneh.
verses.study_note: The gift goes on ahead — and Jacob remains in the camp for 'that night' (hallaylah hahu). The phrase picks up the 'night' of v.13 — this is the same fateful night as the prayer, the night before the crossing, the night of the wrestling. The narrative slows here deliberately. Jacob has prayed, planned, and prepared; now there is a still moment before the most decisive night of his life. 'He lodged' — the same verb as v.13. Jacob is stationary, in the camp, in the dark — waiting.

verses.kjv_text: And he rose up that night, and took his two wives, and his two womenservants, and his eleven sons, and passed over the ford Jabbok.
verses.original_text: וַיָּקָם בַּלַּיְלָה הוּא וַיִּקַּח אֶת־שְׁתֵּי נָשָׁיו וְאֶת־שְׁתֵּי שִׁפְחֹתָיו וְאֶת־אַחַד עָשָׂר יְלָדָיו וַיַּעֲבֹר אֵת מַעֲבַר יַבֹּק׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyakam ballaylah hu vayyikkach et-shetei nashav ve'et-shetei shifchotav ve'et-achad asar yeladav vayyavor et ma'avar Yabbok.
verses.study_note: In the night, Jacob rises and moves his entire family across the Jabbok ford. The Jabbok (Yabbok) is a tributary of the Jordan running east of the river, modern Wadi Zarqa. The name Jabbok (yabbok) is a wordplay on the name Jacob (ya'akov) and on the wrestling word (ye'avek) that follows in v.24 — all three words from the same consonantal cluster (Y-B-K). The geography becomes the grammar of the narrative. Jacob crosses eleven sons — Dinah, his daughter, is mentioned later (34:1); Benjamin has not yet been born (35:18).

verses.kjv_text: And he took them, and sent them over the brook, and sent over that he had.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקָּחֵם וַיַּעֲבִרֵם אֶת־הַנַּחַל וַיַּעֲבֵר אֶת־אֲשֶׁר־לוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikkachem vayyaabirem et-hannachal vayyaaver et-asher-lo.
verses.study_note: Jacob sends everyone and everything across. He is left alone on the north bank of the Jabbok. The repetition of the crossing verb ('he sent over... he sent over') creates a narrative pause — the camera, as it were, holds on the moment of Jacob's solitude. He has dispatched his prayer, his strategy, his gifts, his family, his possessions. He is stripped of everything. And it is precisely this stripping — this enforced aloneness — that prepares him for the encounter that follows. God meets Jacob when Jacob has nothing left to rely on.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob was left alone; and there wrestled a man with him until the breaking of the day.
verses.original_text: וַיִּוָּתֵר יַעֲקֹב לְבַדּוֹ וַיֵּאָבֵק אִישׁ עִמּוֹ עַד עֲלוֹת הַשָּׁחַר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyivvater Ya'akov levaddo vayyeeavek ish immo ad alot hashachar.
verses.study_note: The most dramatic verse in Genesis outside of the Fall. 'Jacob was left alone' (levaddo — the same word used of Isaac going alone to Mount Moriah, 22:6). Into this aloneness comes the mysterious Stranger. 'There wrestled a man' (vayyeeavek ish) — the verb 'avak' is used only here in the Bible, likely from a root meaning to get dusty in combat, to roll in the dust. 'A man' (ish) — the Stranger is not identified, and Jacob does not know who He is until it is almost over. The wrestling lasts 'until the breaking of the day' (ad alot hashachar) — from night through to dawn, hours of sustained, desperate combat. This is no vision or dream — it is physical, prolonged, and leaves a lasting wound.
verses.doctrinal_note: Who is the wrestler? The text says 'a man' (ish); Hosea 12:4 calls him 'an angel'; Jacob names the place 'I have seen God face to face' (v.30); and the angel himself speaks with divine authority ('a prince hast thou power with God,' v.28). This is a theophany — a pre-incarnate appearance of the Son of God. He who would later take on human flesh in Bethlehem first assumed human form here at the Jabbok. As the second Adam He engages Jacob; as the God of the covenant He blesses him. This wrestling is the Old Testament counterpart to Gethsemane: the night struggle where the will of man and the will of God meet in agonizing, transforming encounter.

verses.kjv_text: And when he saw that he prevailed not against him, he touched the hollow of his thigh; and the hollow of Jacob’s thigh was out of joint, as he wrestled with him.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא כִּי לֹא יָכֹל לוֹ וַיִּגַּע בְּכַף־יְרֵכוֹ וַתֵּקַע כַּף־יֶרֶךְ יַעֲקֹב בְּהֵאָבְקוֹ עִמּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyar ki lo yakol lo vayyigga bekaf-yerekho vatteka kaf-yerekh Ya'akov beheeaveko immo.
verses.study_note: The wrestling reaches its crisis. The divine Stranger 'saw that he prevailed not' (lo yakol lo) — He could not overcome Jacob by force alone. This is the theology of divine condescension: God chooses not to overwhelm but to engage. Then a single touch (vayyigga) dislocates Jacob's hip socket. 'The hollow of his thigh' (kaf-yerekh) — the hip socket or the sciatic area. The touch is paradoxical: the Stranger who could not pin Jacob disables him with a fingertip. This is not failure — it is revelation. The One who holds back the sea touches a man's hip. The wound is not punishment but preparation: Jacob must learn to be strong in weakness.
verses.doctrinal_note: The theology of the touched hip: God could have ended the wrestling at any moment. He chose instead to wound Jacob permanently — not in anger but in grace. The limp Jacob carries from this night becomes the sign of his encounter, the permanent testimony of wrestling with God. Paul will say 'when I am weak, then am I strong' (2 Cor 12:10). The wound that comes from genuine encounter with God is always also a gift. Jacob's limp is the mark of the man who has been with God.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Let me go, for the day breaketh. And he said, I will not let thee go, except thou bless me.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר שַׁלְּחֵנִי כִּי עָלָה הַשָּׁחַר וַיֹּאמֶר לֹא אֲשַׁלֵּחֲךָ כִּי אִם־בֵּרַכְתָּנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer shallacheni ki alah hashachar vayomer lo ashallechakha ki im-berakhttani.
verses.study_note: Dawn threatens to break and the Stranger requests release. Jacob's response is the great declaration of holy tenacity: 'I will not let thee go, except thou bless me' (lo ashallechakha ki im-berakhttani). Despite a dislocated hip, in pain, physically broken — Jacob tightens his grip. The Stranger who disabled Jacob cannot free Himself without Jacob's consent. This inversion is the theology of prayer: God has placed Himself in a position where the prayers of His people can 'hold' Him. Not because He is weak, but because He has made Himself accessible. Jesus will say 'ask, and ye shall receive' — the divine Stranger requests release and Jacob refuses to let go without a blessing.
verses.doctrinal_note: The great paradox of prevailing prayer: Jacob is physically defeated (his hip is dislocated) but spiritually victorious (the Stranger cannot leave without his consent). This is the pattern of all true intercession: not arguing with God from a position of strength but clinging from a position of utter weakness and need. The key phrase 'ki im' (except) is the language of unconditional persistence. It establishes the principle Jesus later enunciates in the parables of the persistent widow (Luke 18:1-8) and the friend at midnight (Luke 11:5-10): pray and do not lose heart.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto him, What is thy name? And he said, Jacob.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו מַה־שְּׁמֶךָ וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer elav mah-shemekha vayomer Ya'akov.
verses.study_note: The question is already the beginning of the transformation. 'What is thy name?' (mah-shemekha) — echoing the scene where Jacob deceived Isaac: 'Who art thou, my son? And Jacob said... I am Esau' (27:18-19). Now Jacob must answer the same question with complete honesty. 'Jacob' — the name means 'heel-grabber, supplanter, one who follows at the heel.' To confess his name here is to confess his nature. The Stranger does not ask because He does not know; He asks so that Jacob will say it — will own it — before the transformation is administered. Confession precedes transformation.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Thy name shall be called no more Jacob, but Israel: for as a prince hast thou power with God and with men, and hast prevailed.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לֹא יַעֲקֹב יֵאָמֵר עוֹד שִׁמְךָ כִּי אִם־יִשְׂרָאֵל כִּי שָׂרִיתָ עִם־אֱלֹהִים וְעִם־אֲנָשִׁים וַתּוּכָל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer lo Ya'akov ye'amer od shimkha ki im-Yisrael ki sarita im-Elohim ve'im-anashim vattukal.
verses.study_note: The climactic moment of the entire chapter — indeed one of the supreme moments in all of Scripture. The name change from Jacob to Israel is the birth of a nation in a single broken man. 'Israel' (Yisrael) means 'one who strives with God' or 'God strives/persists' — from 'sarah' (to strive, contend) and 'El' (God). The explanation: 'for as a prince hast thou power with God and with men, and hast prevailed' (sarita im-Elohim ve'im-anashim vattukal). Jacob has succeeded in the supreme test — not by strength but by persistent clinging. The nation Israel will bear this name: they are a people who wrestle with God and prevail not by might but by grace-empowered tenacity.
verses.doctrinal_note: The name Israel is the foundational name of an entire nation and a permanent theological category. 'Israel' does not mean 'he won' but rather 'one who strives/has striven with God.' The name records a relationship of intensity, intimacy, and persistence. The entire Old Testament narrative — Israel wrestling with God's covenant, God's law, God's prophets, God's patience — is prefigured in this single night. Ultimately, the true Israel is Christ (Matt 2:15, Hosea 11:1) who succeeds where Jacob's descendants fail, who wrestles in Gethsemane and rises, who prevails and distributes the blessing to all who are in Him.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob asked him, and said, Tell me, I pray thee, thy name. And he said, Wherefore is it that thou dost ask after my name? And he blessed him there.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁאַל יַעֲקֹב וַיֹּאמֶר הַגִּידָה־נָּא שְׁמֶךָ וַיֹּאמֶר לָמָּה זֶּה תִּשְׁאַל לִשְׁמִי וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתוֹ שָׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyish'al Ya'akov vayomer haggidah-na shemekha vayomer lammah zzeh tish'al lishmi vayevarekh oto sham.
verses.study_note: Jacob asks the wrestler's name — the natural response to receiving a new name is to know the Namer's name. The answer is a question: 'Wherefore dost thou ask after my name?' The divine Name remains veiled here — as it will remain veiled to Manoah (Judges 13:17-18, 'my name... is secret/wonderful'). God will reveal His name fully and formally to Moses at the burning bush (Exod 3:14, 'I AM THAT I AM'). Here the refusal to name is itself a revelation: this One is beyond Jacob's categories, beyond the naming power that Jacob has wielded throughout his life. Then, having withheld His name, He 'blessed him there' (vayevarekh oto sham). The blessing is given — what Jacob wrestled for all night is quietly granted.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob called the name of the place Peniel: for I have seen God face to face, and my life is preserved.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא יַעֲקֹב שֵׁם הַמָּקוֹם פְּנִיאֵל כִּי רָאִיתִי אֱלֹהִים פָּנִים אֶל־פָּנִים וַתִּנָּצֵל נַפְשִׁי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra Ya'akov shem hammakom Peni'el ki ra'iti Elohim panim el-panim vatinnatzel nafshi.
verses.study_note: Jacob names the place 'Peniel' (face of God) and gives the reason: 'I have seen God face to face, and my life is preserved' (vatinnatel nafshi). 'Face to face' (panim el-panim) is the language of the most intimate divine encounter — used of Moses (Exod 33:11, Deut 34:10). The wonder in Jacob's naming is the survival: seeing God face to face was understood to be lethal (Exod 33:20, Judges 13:22). Jacob expected to die; instead he was blessed. His survival is the mercy of God — the very mercy he had confessed in v.10. 'My life is preserved' (vatinnatel nafshi) — literally, my soul/life has been delivered. Peniel becomes a sacred site of divine encounter: the place where a man's name and nature were permanently changed.
verses.doctrinal_note: Peniel — the theology of the face of God: To 'see God face to face' and survive is grace alone. The Old Testament pattern is that direct encounter with the divine is lethal to fallen humanity. What Jacob experienced anticipates what the Gospel accomplishes: in Christ, fallen humanity encounters the face of God and instead of dying, is transformed. 'For God, who commanded the light to shine out of darkness, hath shined in our hearts, to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ' (2 Cor 4:6). Peniel is the Old Testament Calvary: the place where man meets God and lives — not because man deserves it, but because God is merciful.

verses.kjv_text: And as he passed over Penuel the sun rose upon him, and he halted upon his thigh.
verses.original_text: וַיִּזְרַח־לוֹ הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ כַּאֲשֶׁר עָבַר אֶת־פְּנוּאֵל וְהוּא צֹלֵעַ עַל־יְרֵכוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyizrach-lo hashemesh ka'asher avar et-Penu'el vehu tzole'a al-yerekho.
verses.study_note: A hauntingly beautiful verse. The sun rises upon Jacob as he crosses Penuel — the sunrise that ended the night of wrestling. The wordplay: in v.24, the wrestling began at night ('until the breaking of the day'); now the sun rises as Jacob limps forward into a new day, a new identity, a new chapter. 'He halted upon his thigh' (tzole'a al-yerekho) — to limp, to be lame. Jacob will limp for the rest of his life. The contrast is complete: he came to this place as Jacob the supplanter, vigorous and calculating; he crosses over as Israel the God-wrestler, blessed and broken. The rising sun upon the wounded man is one of Scripture's most powerful images of grace — new light on an old wound.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore the children of Israel eat not of the sinew which shrank, which is upon the hollow of the thigh, unto this day: because he touched the hollow of Jacob’s thigh in the sinew that shrank.
verses.original_text: עַל־כֵּן לֹא־יֹאכְלוּ בְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת־גִּיד הַנָּשֶׁה אֲשֶׁר עַל־כַּף הַיָּרֵךְ עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה כִּי נָגַע בְּכַף־יֶרֶךְ יַעֲקֹב בְּגִיד הַנָּשֶׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Al-ken lo-yokhlu venei Yisrael et-gid hannasheh asher al-kaf hayyarekh ad hayyom hazzeh ki naga bekaf-yerekh Ya'akov begid hannasheh.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes with a dietary ordinance that has been observed continuously in Jewish practice to this day: the prohibition of the sciatic nerve (gid hannasheh). 'Gid' means sinew/tendon; 'nasheh' means the displaced/relocated one — from the dislocation of Jacob's hip. Kosher butchers in Jewish practice still remove the sciatic nerve from the hindquarters of animals before consumption. This practice, lasting millennia, is a daily embodied reminder of the Peniel encounter. Food laws in Israel frequently serve as perpetual, bodily enacted theologies. The closing phrase — 'unto this day' (ad hayyom hazzeh) — connects the original reader to an unbroken chain of observance linking their dinner tables to Jacob's broken hip at the ford of the Jabbok.
verses.doctrinal_note: The gid hannasheh prohibition is a perpetual national sacrament: Israel carries in its very eating practices the memory of the night God wounded and blessed their father. This principle — that bodily, ritual practices keep alive the memory of divine encounters — runs through the entire Old Testament (Passover, circumcision, Sabbath) and continues in the New Testament (baptism, the Lord's Supper). The body does not forget what the mind might lose. Jacob's limp became Israel's food law; Israel's food law became a nation's living memory of Peniel.

meta.overview: Genesis 32 stands as one of the great pivotal chapters in the entire Bible — the night when the cunning supplanter Jacob became Israel, the one who strives with God. The chapter begins with a divine omen: angels of God meet Jacob as he departs from Laban, and he names the place Mahanaim (Two Camps). But the vision of angels quickly gives way to a more earthly terror — Esau is advancing with four hundred men. The chapter moves through four movements: the angelic encounter at Mahanaim, the crisis of Esau's approach and Jacob's frantic preparations, Jacob's extraordinary prayer — one of the finest in the Old Testament — and the climactic all-night wrestling match with the divine Stranger at the ford of the Jabbok. This wrestling is the supreme biographical moment in Jacob's life: a broken hip, a new name, and a face-to-face encounter with God. The man who had spent his life grasping (Esau's heel, Isaac's blessing, Laban's flocks) finally surrenders — and in that surrender, receives everything. The chapter closes with the dietary law of the sciatic sinew, still observed in Jewish practice, as a permanent embodiment of this transforming night.
meta.theme: Divine Encounter, Surrender & Transformation — The Birth of Israel
meta.keyWordMeaning: He wrestled — from a rare root ('avak') meaning to wrestle, to get dusty in combat, to grapple. The name Israel (Yisrael) derives from this same act: one who strives/wrestles with God. Used only here in all of Scripture.
outline: Mahanaim — Jacob Sees the Angels (vv.1-2): The divine escort as Jacob departs Laban
The Crisis — Esau Approaches with Four Hundred Men (vv.3-8): Terror and strategic division
Jacob's Prayer — A Model of Biblical Intercession (vv.9-12): Humility, covenant appeal, and petition
The Gifts Prepared — Twenty Years of Fear Meets Grace (vv.13-21): Waves of appeasement sent ahead
The Crossing — Family Sent Over the Jabbok (vv.22-23): Jacob left alone
The Night Wrestling — Transformation at Peniel (vv.24-29): Broken hip, new name, dawn blessing
Peniel — I Have Seen God Face to Face (vv.30-32): The permanent memorial in sinew and name

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob lifted up his eyes, and looked, and, behold, Esau came, and with him four hundred men. And he divided the children unto Leah, and unto Rachel, and unto the two handmaids.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשָּׂא יַעֲקֹב עֵינָיו וַיַּרְא וְהִנֵּה עֵשָׂו בָּא וְעִמּוֹ אַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת אִישׁ וַיַּחַץ אֶת־הַיְלָדִים עַל־לֵאָה וְעַל־רָחֵל וְעַל שְׁתֵּי הַשְּׁפָחוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyissa Yaakov einav vayar vehineh Esav ba ve'immo arba' me'ot ish vayyachatz et-hayeladim al-Le'ah ve'al-Rachel ve'al shtei hashefachot.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens mid-crisis — Jacob has just received his new name 'Israel' at Peniel (32:28-30), still limping from the encounter, and now must face the brother he has not seen in twenty years. 'Jacob lifted up his eyes' (vayyissa Yaakov einav) — this phrase in Genesis always signals a critical moment of perception (cf. 13:10, 18:2, 22:4). The approaching 400 men confirm Jacob's worst fears. His instinct is still to divide (vayyachatz — to split/halve) — the same separating mentality that has marked his entire life. He arranges the family in reverse order of vulnerability: handmaids and their children first (most expendable to Jacob's calculation), Leah and hers next, Rachel and Joseph last (most protected, most beloved).

verses.kjv_text: And he put the handmaids and their children foremost, and Leah and her children after, and Rachel and Joseph hindermost.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשֶׂם אֶת־הַשְּׁפָחוֹת וְאֶת־יַלְדֵיהֶן רִאשֹׁנָה וְאֶת־לֵאָה וִילָדֶיהָ אַחֲרֹנִים וְאֶת־רָחֵל וְאֶת־יוֹסֵף אַחֲרֹנִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyasem et-hashefachot ve'et-yaldeihen rishonah ve'et-Le'ah veyladeiha acharonim ve'et-Rachel ve'et-Yosef acharonim.
verses.study_note: The arrangement is spelled out with painful explicitness by the narrator. Bilhah and Zilpah's children lead — human shields in Jacob's tactical mind. Leah's family occupies the middle position. Rachel and Joseph are 'hindermost' (acharonim) — the very last, the most protected. The narrator does not editorialise — but Jewish and Christian readers alike have always noted the moral darkness of this ordering. Jacob the schemer has not yet fully become Israel the prince. The new name and the limp have not yet fully remade the old nature. This detail is essential for understanding the grace of what follows — Jacob is saved not because he is transformed, but because God is faithful.

verses.kjv_text: And he passed over before them, and bowed himself to the ground seven times, until he came near to his brother.
verses.original_text: וְהוּא עָבַר לִפְנֵיהֶם וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ אַרְצָה שֶׁבַע פְּעָמִים עַד־גִּשְׁתּוֹ עַד־אָחִיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vehu avar lifneihem vayyishtachu artzah sheva pe'amim ad-gishtto ad-achiv.
verses.study_note: Whatever else Jacob is, he is courageous here: 'he passed over before them' (vehu avar lifneihem) — he goes first, placing himself at the front and at greatest risk. The sevenfold prostration (vayyishtachu artzah sheva pe'amim) is the formal submission gesture of a vassal approaching a sovereign in ancient Near Eastern culture — documented in the Amarna letters where vassal kings prostrate seven times before the Pharaoh. Jacob addresses Esau with complete self-abasement. The sevenfold bow echoes the seven-year cycle of his labours for Laban and the seven-year mourning of Joseph (50:10). As he approaches, step by bow, the word 'brother' (achiv) appears for the first time in this chapter — the narrator has been holding it in reserve for this moment.

verses.kjv_text: And Esau ran to meet him, and embraced him, and fell on his neck, and kissed him: and they wept.
verses.original_text: וַיָּרָץ עֵשָׂו לִקְרָאתוֹ וַיְחַבְּקֵהוּ וַיִּפֹּל עַל־צַוָּארָיו וַיִּשָּׁקֵהוּ וַיִּבְכּוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaratz Esav likrato vayechabbekehу vayyippol al-tzavvarav vayyishshakehу vayyivku.
verses.study_note: Five verbs in rapid succession — the most emotional verse in the Jacob narrative and one of the most moving in all of Scripture: (1) ran (vayyaratz), (2) embraced (vayechabbekehу), (3) fell on his neck (vayyippol al-tzavvarav), (4) kissed him (vayyishshakehу), (5) wept (vayyivku). The same sequence — 'ran,' 'fell on his neck,' 'kissed' — appears in Luke 15:20 when the father runs to embrace the returning prodigal son, almost certainly a deliberate echo by Jesus. Esau, who had sworn to kill Jacob (27:41), runs not in vengeance but in reunion. Twenty years of God's sovereign work have softened his heart. In the Hebrew text, the dots above the word 'vayishshakehу' (and he kissed him) are one of the famous dotted words in the Torah ('nekudot' — scribal notations), which later rabbinic tradition interpreted variously — but the plain meaning is a genuine kiss of love.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Parable of the Prodigal Son (Luke 15:11-32) mirrors this scene with deliberate precision. Both feature a running embrace, falling on the neck, and kissing. Jesus invites His hearers to see the Father's welcome of sinners through the lens of Esau's unexpected grace. The elder brother's resentment in Luke 15 may carry an intentional irony — Jacob, the schemer who stole Esau's place, is the returning younger brother; God's grace transforms even the offended party into a figure of love.

verses.kjv_text: And he lifted up his eyes, and saw the women and the children; and said, Who are those with thee? And he said, The children which God hath graciously given thy servant.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשָּׂא אֶת־עֵינָיו וַיַּרְא אֶת־הַנָּשִׁים וְאֶת־הַיְלָדִים וַיֹּאמֶר מִי־אֵלֶּה לָּךְ וַיֹּאמַר הַיְלָדִים אֲשֶׁר־חָנַן אֱלֹהִים אֶת־עַבְדֶּךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyissa et-einav vayar et-hanashim ve'et-hayeladim vayyomer mi-elleh lakh vayyomar hayeladim asher-chanan Elohim et-avdekha.
verses.study_note: Now Esau 'lifted up his eyes' — the same gesture as Jacob in v.1 — and sees the family. His question 'Who are those with thee?' is warm curiosity, not challenge. Jacob's answer is theologically precise: 'The children which God hath graciously given' (asher-chanan Elohim). The verb 'chanan' — to show grace — is the verb root of the name 'John' (Yochanan) and is connected to 'chen' (grace). Jacob does not say 'the children I worked fourteen years for' or 'the fruit of my marriages' — he credits God. After Peniel, Jacob's language is being reshaped. The title 'thy servant' (avdekha) — Jacob formally submits to Esau in speech, reversing the stolen blessing of 27:29 where Isaac predicted the nations would 'serve' Jacob.

verses.kjv_text: Then the handmaidens came near, they and their children, and they bowed themselves.
verses.original_text: וַתִּגַּשְׁןָ הַשְּׁפָחוֹת הֵנָּה וְיַלְדֵיהֶן וַתִּשְׁתַּחֲוֶיןָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattigashna hashefachot hennah veyaldeihen vattishtachaveyna.
verses.study_note: The formal presentations to Esau proceed in reverse order of Jacob's affection — the handmaids first. Each group approaches with their children and bows (vayyishtachavu / vattishtachaveyna — the same prostration word as v.3). The repetition of the bowing gesture across three verses (3, 6, 7) gives the encounter a ritual quality — it is both emotional reunion and formal protocol. The handmaids are Bilhah (Rachel's) and Zilpah (Leah's), mothers of Dan, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher. Four of the twelve tribes of Israel bow before Esau in this moment.

verses.kjv_text: And Leah also with her children came near, and bowed themselves: and after came Joseph near and Rachel, and they bowed themselves.
verses.original_text: וַתִּגַּשׁ גַּם־לֵאָה וִילָדֶיהָ וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ וְאַחַר נִגַּשׁ יוֹסֵף וְרָחֵל וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattigash gam-Le'ah veyladeiha vayyishtachavu ve'achar niggash Yosef veRachel vayyishtachavu.
verses.study_note: A subtle but revealing detail: 'Joseph and Rachel' — Joseph is mentioned before his mother. In verse 2, Rachel was listed before Joseph, as is natural. Here, when they actually approach, Joseph goes ahead of Rachel — a small act of chivalric protection by the young child, or perhaps simply the narrator's order of bowing. Leah's approach with her children (Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun, and Dinah) means the future lineage of Christ through Judah bows before Esau. The messianic line passes through this moment of humiliation and grace.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, What meanest thou by all this drove which I met? And he said, These are to find grace in the sight of my lord.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מִי לְךָ כָּל־הַמַּחֲנֶה הַזֶּה אֲשֶׁר פָּגָשְׁתִּי וַיֹּאמַר לִמְצֹא חֵן בְּעֵינֵי אֲדֹנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer mi lekha kol-hammachaneh hazzeh asher pagashti vayyomar limtzo chen be'einei adoni.
verses.study_note: Esau asks about the procession of animals he encountered (described in 32:13-21). Jacob's answer distills his entire strategy: 'to find grace in the sight of my lord' (limtzo chen be'einei adoni). 'Chen' (grace, favor) appears here for the first time in chapter 33, and will appear again in verses 10 and 15. It is Jacob's governing word for the encounter. The word 'machaneh' (company/camp) ironically echoes 'Mahanaim' — the two camps of angels Jacob saw at the outset of this journey (32:2). Everything Jacob does is calculated toward the goal of 'chen' — a grace he cannot command or earn, only seek.

verses.kjv_text: And Esau said, I have enough, my brother; keep that thou hast unto thyself.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר עֵשָׂו יֶשׁ־לִי רָב אָחִי יְהִי לְךָ אֲשֶׁר־לָךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Esav yesh-li rav achi yehi lekha asher-lakh.
verses.study_note: Esau's magnanimity reaches its fullest expression: 'I have enough (rav), my brother; keep what you have.' Remarkably, Esau uses the word 'rav' (much/enough/great abundance), while Jacob in the next verse will use 'kol' (everything). Esau says 'I have much'; Jacob counters 'I have everything.' The contrast reveals their inner states: Esau assesses his blessings by quantity; Jacob names them as complete provision. Crucially, Esau calls Jacob 'my brother' (achi) — the first time Esau uses this word in the chapter. The two brothers address each other as 'brother' for the first time since the stolen blessing in chapter 27.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob said, Nay, I pray thee, if now I have found grace in thy sight, then receive my present at my hand: for therefore I have seen thy face, as though I had seen the face of God, and thou wast pleased with me.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב אַל־נָא אִם־נָא מָצָאתִי חֵן בְּעֵינֶיךָ וְלָקַחְתָּ מִנְחָתִי מִיָּדִי כִּי עַל־כֵּן רָאִיתִי פָנֶיךָ כִּרְאֹת פְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים וַתִּרְצֵנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Yaakov al-na im-na matzati chen be'einekha velakachta minchat mi-yadi ki al-ken ra'iti fanekha kir'ot penei Elohim vattirzeni.
verses.study_note: The theological climax of the chapter and one of the most profound verses in the Jacob narrative. Jacob makes an extraordinary equation: 'I have seen thy face, as though I had seen the face of God' (kir'ot penei Elohim). The previous night, at Peniel, Jacob literally saw God face-to-face and was preserved alive (32:30 — 'I have seen God face to face'). Now, in Esau's welcoming face, he sees the same grace, the same acceptance, the same 'I have not consumed you.' The face of God at Peniel and the face of Esau in Canaan are placed in explicit parallel. This is the theology of common grace in its purest Old Testament expression: God's grace can be perceived and reflected in human faces, even in the face of one's adversary. 'And thou wast pleased with me' (vattirzeni) — the verb 'ratzah' means to accept, to be favorably disposed, the same word used for God's acceptance of sacrifices. Esau has, without knowing it, acted toward Jacob as God acts toward the repentant.
verses.doctrinal_note: The face of God, encountered in the darkness at Peniel (32:30), is now encountered again in the welcoming face of a reconciled brother. This is the Imago Dei in action: when humans extend grace and forgiveness to each other, they reflect the very face of God. The New Testament deepens this: 'We love because he first loved us' (1 John 4:19), and 'Be ye kind one to another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, even as God for Christ's sake hath forgiven you' (Ephesians 4:32). Human forgiveness is always derivative — a reflection of divine grace seen 'as in a glass' (1 Cor 13:12).

verses.kjv_text: Take, I pray thee, my blessing that is brought to thee; because God hath dealt graciously with me, and because I have enough. And he urged him, and he took it.
verses.original_text: קַח־נָא אֶת־בִּרְכָתִי אֲשֶׁר הֻבָאת לָךְ כִּי־חַנַּנִי אֱלֹהִים וְכִי יֶשׁ־לִי כֹל וַיִּפְצַר־בּוֹ וַיִּקָּח׃
verses.transliteration: Kach-na et-birkati asher huvat lakh ki-channani Elohim vekhi yesh-li khol vayyiftsar-bo vayyikkach.
verses.study_note: Jacob names the gift 'my blessing' (birkati) — the same word as the stolen blessing of 27:35-36 ('He took away my blessing,' berakhati). By sending Esau 'my blessing,' Jacob symbolically returns what was stolen. Whether Esau recognizes the echo is unstated, but the narrator and reader do. Jacob's reason for giving is revelatory: 'God hath dealt graciously with me' (channani Elohim) and 'I have everything' (yesh-li khol). Contrast with Esau's 'I have much' (rav, v.9) — Jacob says 'everything' (kol). Jacob has learned the contentment of covenant sufficiency. Esau finally accepts after Jacob 'urged him' (vayyiftsar) — the same word Lot used when urging the angels in 19:3. Esau's initial refusal was generous; his final acceptance is gracious.
verses.doctrinal_note: The stolen blessing of Genesis 27 is symbolically redeemed here. Jacob cannot undo what he stole, but he can acknowledge it and return its equivalent. This is the closest the Genesis narrative comes to restitution between the brothers. It is incomplete — Jacob never confesses his deception directly — but the gift-as-blessing gesture is a theological acknowledgment. Zacchaeus in Luke 19:8 provides the New Testament fulfillment: genuine repentance produces genuine restitution.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Let us take our journey, and let us go, and I will go before thee.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר נִסְעָה וְנֵלֵכָה וְאֵלְכָה לְנֶגְדֶּךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer nis'ah venelekha ve'elkha lenegdekha.
verses.study_note: Esau invites Jacob to journey together toward Seir. 'I will go before thee' (ve'elkha lenegdekha) — Esau offers to lead, to escort, to protect. This is the reversal of 32:3, where Jacob sent messengers 'before him' to Esau. Now Esau wants to go before Jacob. The offer is genuinely generous, but it creates a dilemma Jacob handles carefully in the following verses. Seir is Esau's territory in Edom, south of the Dead Sea. Jacob's destiny lies in Canaan, not Edom — going to Seir would mean following Esau's path, not God's.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto him, My lord knoweth that the children are tender, and the flocks and herds with young are with me: and if men should overdrive them one day, all the flock will die.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו אֲדֹנִי יֹדֵעַ כִּי־הַיְלָדִים רַכִּים וְהַצֹּאן וְהַבָּקָר עָלוֹת עָלַי וּדְפָקוּם יוֹם אֶחָד וָמֵתוּ כָּל־הַצֹּאן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer elav adoni yode'a ki-hayeladim rakkm vehattzon vehabbakkar alot alai udefakum yom echad vametu kol-hattzon.
verses.study_note: Jacob's refusal is gracious, practical, and diplomatically brilliant. He appeals to Esau's own knowledge ('my lord knoweth') and makes the case on the grounds of vulnerability: the children are tender (rakkm — soft, delicate), the nursing animals cannot be pushed. 'If men should overdrive them one day, all the flock will die' — this is accurate pastoralism; forcing nursing flocks to march at military pace would indeed cause losses. The pastoral wisdom here is genuine. But there is also a deeper truth: Jacob's family is on a divine itinerary that does not route through Seir. He cannot follow Esau to Esau's territory without departing from the path God has set.

verses.kjv_text: Let my lord, I pray thee, pass over before his servant: and I will lead on softly, according as the cattle that goeth before me and the children be able to endure, until I come unto my lord unto Seir.
verses.original_text: יַעֲבָר־נָא אֲדֹנִי לִפְנֵי עַבְדּוֹ וַאֲנִי אֶתְנָהֲלָה לְאִטִּי לְרֶגֶל הַמְּלָאכָה אֲשֶׁר־לְפָנַי וּלְרֶגֶל הַיְלָדִים עַד אֲשֶׁר־אָבֹא אֶל־אֲדֹנִי שֵׂעִירָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ya'avar-na adoni lifnei avdo va'ani etnahalah le'itti leregel hammelakhah asher-lefanai uleregel hayeladim ad asher-avo el-adoni Se'irah.
verses.study_note: 'I will lead on softly' (etnahalah le'itti) — one of the most beautiful phrases in Genesis. 'Etnahalah' comes from 'nahal' (to lead to a watering place, to care for), and 'le'itti' means 'at my own pace' or 'gently.' The image is of a shepherd leading the flock to water at a sustainable pace. Isaiah draws on this imagery in Isaiah 40:11 ('He shall gently lead those that are with young'). Jacob promises to come to Esau at Seir — a promise he never fulfills (he goes to Succoth and then Shechem). Whether this is a deliberate evasion or a sincere but unfulfilled intention has been much debated in rabbinic tradition. The text is silent on his motive.

verses.kjv_text: And Esau said, Let me now leave with thee some of the folk that are with me. And he said, What needeth it? let me find grace in the sight of my lord.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר עֵשָׂו אַצִּיגָה־נָּא עִמְּךָ מִן־הָעָם אֲשֶׁר אִתִּי וַיֹּאמֶר לָמָּה זֶּה אֶמְצָא־חֵן בְּעֵינֵי אֲדֹנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Esav atzigah-na immekha min-ha'am asher itti vayyomer lammah zzeh emtza-chen be'einei adoni.
verses.study_note: Esau's final offer of generosity: a military escort for Jacob's protection. Jacob declines with characteristic diplomatic grace: 'What needeth it? let me find grace in the sight of my lord' (emtza-chen be'einei adoni). The third use of 'chen' in this chapter — grace is always Jacob's only appeal. He cannot command Esau's goodwill; he can only ask for grace. Accepting Esau's escort would mean submitting to Esau's protection — a dependence Jacob politely refuses. He trusts in God's protection, as he learned at Mahanaim (32:1-2) and Peniel (32:24-30).

verses.kjv_text: So Esau returned that day on his way unto Seir.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשָׁב בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא עֵשָׂו לְדַרְכּוֹ שֵׂעִירָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashav bayyom hahu Esav ledarko Se'irah.
verses.study_note: The farewell is narrated with brevity: Esau simply returns 'that day' (bayyom hahu) on his own way. The narrative closes the Esau subplot with quiet economy. He departs without bitterness, without demands, without resentment — the once-murderous brother walks away at peace. 'Ledarko' (on his way) — each man has his own 'way' (derekh), his own path and destiny. Esau's way leads to Seir/Edom; Jacob's to Canaan. The separation is not a rupture but a peaceful divergence of destinies. The brothers will meet once more — to bury their father Isaac together (35:29) — a final act of shared filial devotion.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob journeyed to Succoth, and built him an house, and made booths for his cattle: therefore the name of the place is called Succoth.
verses.original_text: וְיַעֲקֹב נָסַע סֻכֹּתָה וַיִּבֶן לוֹ בָּיִת וּלְמִקְנֵהוּ עָשָׂה סֻכֹּת עַל־כֵּן קָרָא שֵׁם־הַמָּקוֹם סֻכּוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Veya'akov nasa Sukkotah vayyiven lo bayit ulemiknehu asah sukkot al-ken kara shem-hammakom Sukkot.
verses.study_note: Jacob does not go to Seir. He goes west and north toward Canaan, stopping first at 'Succoth' (Sukkot — Booths). 'He built him a house' (vayyiven lo bayit) — the first time Jacob builds a permanent dwelling in the promised land. Until now he has lived in tents as a nomad; here he builds. 'He made booths (sukkot) for his cattle' — and the place is named for these booths. Succoth is east of the Jordan River, identified with Tell Deir Alla in modern Jordan. Later, 'Sukkot' becomes one of Israel's great annual festivals (Feast of Tabernacles, Leviticus 23:33-44) — named for the booths Israel lived in during the wilderness journey. The name stitches Jacob's pastoral stop into the fabric of later Israelite worship.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob came to Shalem, a city of Shechem, which is in the land of Canaan, when he came from Padanaram; and pitched his tent before the city.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא יַעֲקֹב שָׁלֵם עִיר שְׁכֶם אֲשֶׁר בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן בְּבֹאוֹ מִפַּדַּן אֲרָם וַיִּחַן אֶת־פְּנֵי הָעִיר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo Ya'akov shalem ir Shekhem asher be'eretz Kena'an bevo'o miPaddan Aram vayyichan et-penei ha'ir.
verses.study_note: Jacob arrives 'to Shechem' — the text can be read as 'Jacob came safely (shalem) to the city of Shechem' or 'Jacob came to Shalem, a city of Shechem.' The word 'shalem' means whole, complete, at peace — and the narrator may be using it adverbially ('safely') rather than as a place name. Either way, the word echoes Jacob's vow at Bethel: 'if I return in peace (shalom) to my father's house' (28:21). He has returned in 'shalom/shalem.' Shechem is in the heart of Canaan, near the modern city of Nablus. It is Abraham's first stop in Canaan (12:6) — Jacob now arrives at the same place, and God's covenantal geography circles back.

verses.kjv_text: And he bought a parcel of a field, where he had spread his tent, at the hand of the children of Hamor, Shechem’s father, for an hundred pieces of money.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקֶן אֶת־חֶלְקַת הַשָּׂדֶה אֲשֶׁר נָטָה־שָׁם אָהֳלוֹ מִיַּד בְּנֵי־חֲמוֹר אֲבִי שְׁכֶם בְּמֵאָה קְשִׂיטָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikken et-chelkat hassadeh asher natah-sham oholo miyad benei-Chamor avi Shekhem beme'ah kesitah.
verses.study_note: Jacob purchases land — the first legal property acquisition in the promised land since Abraham bought the cave of Machpelah (23:16-18). The purchase price is 'a hundred pieces of money' (me'ah kesitah). The 'kesitah' is a unit of weight/value, possibly a silver piece in the form of a lamb (the root may relate to 'kesev,' lamb). This is one of only two mentions of the kesitah in the OT (also Job 42:11).
