This purchase at Shechem becomes enormously significant: Jacob will later charge his sons to bury him in Machpelah, but Joseph's bones are specifically buried here (Joshua 24:32). This plot becomes 'Joseph's portion' — the word 'chelkat' (parcel/portion) is related to 'chelkah,' the share inherited by a son.

verses.kjv_text: And he erected there an altar, and called it EleloheIsrael.
verses.original_text: וַיַּצֶּב־שָׁם מִזְבֵּחַ וַיִּקְרָא־לוֹ אֵל אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyattsev-sham mizbe'ach vayyikra-lo El Elohei Yisra'el.
verses.study_note: The final verse is the chapter's theological capstone. Jacob builds an altar (mizbe'ach — a place of sacrifice) and names it 'El-Elohe-Israel' — 'God, the God of Israel.' This is the first time Jacob publicly identifies himself by the name Israel (his new name from 32:28) and the first time since Bethel (28:18-22) that he builds an altar. The naming is extraordinary: at Bethel he called the place 'Bethel' (house of God), marking the location; here he names the altar with God's relationship to him — 'God, the God of Israel.' He does not call it 'El-Elohe-Jacob' — he uses his covenant name, Israel. By proclaiming God as the God of Israel in Canaan, he plants a theological flag in the promised land. The God who wrestled with him, who preserved him through Laban's treachery and Esau's threat, who gave him grace in the face of his brother — this God is his God. And he is God's Israel.
verses.doctrinal_note: The altar name 'El-Elohe-Israel' is the supreme confession of the chapter: Jacob publicly claims his covenantal identity and publicly confesses his God. This is the Old Testament form of Christian confession: 'If thou shalt confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus... thou shalt be saved' (Romans 10:9). The altar seals what Peniel began — the transformation of Jacob the supplanter into Israel the prince with God. His entire return journey has been a movement from fear to trust, from 'if I return in peace' (28:21) to 'God, the God of Israel.' The name Israel — given in the dark at Peniel — is now declared in broad daylight in the heart of Canaan before the world.

meta.overview: Genesis 33 is the culmination of the longest-running tension in Genesis — the estrangement of Jacob and Esau stretching back to the stolen birthright (ch.25) and stolen blessing (ch.27). After twenty years of exile, Jacob returns and the dreaded reunion unfolds in an entirely unexpected way: Esau runs to embrace him, kisses him, and both brothers weep. The chapter's theological climax is Jacob's extraordinary statement in verse 10: "I have seen thy face, as though I had seen the face of God." The previous night at Peniel, Jacob wrestled with God and saw His face (32:30) — now he sees God's grace reflected in his brother's face. The chapter closes with Jacob purchasing land at Shechem and erecting an altar named "El-Elohe-Israel" — God, the God of Israel — publicly claiming his new identity. The trajectory moves from fear (ch.32) through grace (vv.1-11) to settlement and worship (vv.17-20), tracing the full arc of restored covenant life.
meta.theme: Reconciliation, Grace in the Face of Another & Covenant Identity
meta.keyWordMeaning: Face/Presence — the chapter's organizing theological image: Jacob bowing face to the ground (v.3), Esau's gracious face (v.10), and the face of God glimpsed at Peniel (32:30). To see the face of God is to receive grace; Esau's welcome becomes a visible parable of divine mercy.
outline: The Approach: Fear Meets Grace (vv.1-3): Jacob's strategic arrangement and sevenfold prostration
The Unexpected Embrace: Esau's Grace (vv.4-7): Running, kissing, weeping — reconciliation accomplished
The Exchange of Gifts (vv.8-11): Jacob presses his blessing on Esau, naming Esau's face as God's face
Parting of Ways: Jacob Declines Esau's Company (vv.12-16): Gentle refusal, Esau departs to Seir
Jacob at Succoth (v.17): First stop — Jacob builds a house
Jacob Arrives at Shechem (vv.18-19): Purchases land — first owned plot in Canaan
The Altar of Identity (v.20): El-Elohe-Israel — God claims Jacob, Jacob claims God

verses.kjv_text: And Dinah the daughter of Leah, which she bare unto Jacob, went out to see the daughters of the land.
verses.original_text: וַתֵּצֵא דִינָה בַּת־לֵאָה אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה לְיַעֲקֹב לִרְאוֹת בִּבְנוֹת הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattetzeh Dinah bat-Le'ah asher yaledah leYa'akov lir'ot bivnot ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The opening verse introduces Dinah with a double genealogy: 'daughter of Leah... which she bare unto Jacob.' The maternal lineage is foregrounded — an unusual detail that subtly invokes the Leah-Rachel rivalry and Jacob's family dynamics. Dinah (from 'din,' to judge) goes out (vattetzeh) to see (lir'ot) the 'daughters of the land' (vivnot ha'aretz) — the Canaanite women. 'Went out' uses the same verb as Simeon and Levi will use when they 'went out' to slay the city (v.26). The narrative offers no judgment on Dinah's action. She is curious, perhaps lonely, perhaps seeking the social world her father's nomadic separation has denied her. The family had just settled near Shechem (Gen 33:18-19), newly arrived in Canaan. The chapter immediately raises the question that runs through the entire patriarchal narrative: what happens when God's separated people venture into the world? Genesis 34 shows the catastrophic answer when the crossing goes wrong.

verses.kjv_text: And when Shechem the son of Hamor the Hivite, prince of the country, saw her, he took her, and lay with her, and defiled her.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא אֹתָהּ שְׁכֶם בֶּן־חֲמוֹר הַחִוִּי נְשִׂיא הָאָרֶץ וַיִּקַּח אֹתָהּ וַיִּשְׁכַּב אֹתָהּ וַיְעַנֶּהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyar otah Shekhem ben-Chamor haChivvi nesi ha'aretz vayyikkach otah vayyishkav otah vaye'anneha.
verses.study_note: Four verbs in rapid, brutal succession describe what Shechem does: he SAW her — he TOOK her — he LAY with her — he HUMBLED/DEFILED her (vaye'anneha). The narrative pace is deliberate: no pause, no hesitation, no resistance recorded. Shechem's genealogy is given with heavy irony — he is 'prince of the country' (nesi ha'aretz), a man of power and position, yet he acts as the embodiment of lawless power unconstrained by covenant. His name means 'shoulder/back' — the burden-bearer — now becomes a name associated with violation. 'Hamor' means 'donkey' — a detail that later carries symbolic weight when Shechem's offer to Jacob is evaluated. The key verb is 'vaye'anneha' from the root 'anah' (H6031) meaning to humble, afflict, violate. This same root is used for Egypt's oppression of Israel (Exod 1:11-12), for the Day of Atonement's self-affliction (Lev 16:29), and for fasting. It encompasses the idea of forced subjugation of another's will and dignity. The text uses this word, not the gentler 'shakhav' alone, to make the moral assessment unmistakable.
verses.doctrinal_note: The violation of Dinah is the moral hinge of the entire chapter. The text does not minimize it: three of the four verbs emphasize force and desecration. The dignity of every human person as image-bearer (Gen 1:27) makes this act a assault not only against Dinah but against the God whose image she bears. The Mosaic law later prescribes specific penalties precisely for this crime (Deut 22:25-27). The NT principle that the body is the temple of the Holy Spirit (1 Cor 6:19-20) grounds the same dignity in the new covenant.

verses.kjv_text: And his soul clave unto Dinah the daughter of Jacob, and he loved the damsel, and spake kindly unto the damsel.
verses.original_text: וַתִּדְבַּק נַפְשׁוֹ בְּדִינָה בַּת־יַעֲקֹב וַיֶּאֱהַב אֶת־הַנַּעֲרָ וַיְדַבֵּר עַל־לֵב הַנַּעֲרָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattidbak nafsho beDinah bat-Ya'akov vaye'ehav et-hanna'ara vaydabber al-lev hanna'ara.
verses.study_note: The moral complexity deepens immediately. After the act of violence, the text reports genuine emotional attachment: his soul 'clave' (vattidbak) to Dinah — the same word used for covenant fidelity between Ruth and Naomi (Ruth 1:14) and in the marriage formula of Gen 2:24 ('cleave to his wife'). He 'loved' (ahav) her and 'spake kindly' (vayedabber al-lev — literally 'spoke to the heart of') — an idiom of tender, consoling speech used in its most beautiful form in Hosea 2:14, where God promises to 'speak to the heart' of Israel in the wilderness. Shechem's attachment was real — the text takes it seriously. This creates the tragic irony of the chapter: genuine love expressed through an initial act of violence and then through elaborate, ultimately fatal, negotiation. The narrative refuses to make Shechem into a simple villain with no redeeming quality, which makes the eventual slaughter even more morally weighted.

verses.kjv_text: And Shechem spake unto his father Hamor, saying, Get me this damsel to wife.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר שְׁכֶם אֶל־חֲמוֹר אָבִיו לֵאמֹר קַח־לִי אֶת־הַיַּלְדָּה הַזֹּאת לְאִשָּׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Shekhem el-Chamor aviv lemor kach-li et-hayaldah hazzo't le'ishah.
verses.study_note: Shechem asks his father to 'get' (kach — take) the girl as wife. The word for Dinah shifts here: from 'na'ara' (young woman/damsel, v.3) to 'yaldah' (girl, child — H3207), a word that may emphasize her youth and vulnerability. Shechem's request to his father to negotiate follows ancient Near Eastern marriage protocol; in seeking legal marriage he attempts to regularize what he has already violated. The sequence — violation first, formal request second — is the inverse of covenant order (covenant commitment, then consummation). He wants the framework of marriage without having first submitted to its requirements.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob heard that he had defiled Dinah his daughter: now his sons were with his cattle in the field: and Jacob held his peace until they were come.
verses.original_text: וְיַעֲקֹב שָׁמַע כִּי טִמֵּא אֶת־דִּינָה בִתּוֹ וּבָנָיו הָיוּ אֶת־מִקְנֵהוּ בַּשָּׂדֶה וְהֶחֱרִשׁ יַעֲקֹב עַד־בֹּאָם׃
verses.transliteration: VeYa'akov shama ki timme' et-Dinah bitto uvanav hayu et-miknehu bassadeh vehecherish Ya'akov ad-bo'am.
verses.study_note: Jacob hears that Shechem has 'defiled' (timme') his daughter — a different word now than the 'anah' of v.2. 'Timme' (H2930, to make unclean, to defile, to render ceremonially/morally impure) is the word used throughout Leviticus for defilement. Both words will recur as refrains in this chapter. Jacob's response is strikingly passive: 'Jacob held his peace' (vayyecherish — he was silent, he restrained himself) until his sons returned. This silence has been interpreted variously: as prudent restraint until all parties are assembled, as patriarchal passivity (contrasted with the burning initiative of his sons), or as an indicator of Jacob's character flaw — the man who was so active in deception for his own benefit is curiously passive when his daughter is violated. His sons are in the field with the livestock — far from home — a detail that heightens the helplessness of Dinah's situation. The word 'bitto' (his daughter) emphasizes his paternal responsibility, making his silence more pointed.

verses.kjv_text: And Hamor the father of Shechem went out unto Jacob to commune with him.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּצֵא חֲמוֹר אֲבִי שְׁכֶם אֶל־יַעֲקֹב לְדַבֵּר אִתּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyetze Chamor avi Shekhem el-Ya'akov ledabber itto.
verses.study_note: Hamor (meaning 'donkey,' H2544) takes the diplomatic initiative, going out to Jacob to speak. He is identified as 'father of Shechem' — his identity is now bound to his son's action. That the father of the offender initiates contact with the offended father follows the formal structure of ancient Near Eastern negotiation; Hamor's approach is proper in form while its substance will prove deeply problematic. The verb 'ledabber' (to speak) will be used repeatedly in the negotiations that follow, highlighting that words — proposals, counter-proposals, deceit — are the weapons in the first phase of this conflict.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Jacob came out of the field when they heard it: and the men were grieved, and they were very wroth, because he had wrought folly in Israel in lying with Jacob’s daughter; which thing ought not to be done.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי יַעֲקֹב בָּאוּ מִן־הַשָּׂדֶה כְּשָׁמְעָם וַיִּתְעַצְּבוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים וַיִּחַר לָהֶם מְאֹד כִּי־נְבָלָה עָשָׂה בְיִשְׂרָאֵל לִשְׁכַּב אֶת בַּת־יַעֲקֹב וְכֵן לֹא יֵעָשֶׂה׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Ya'akov ba'u min-hassadeh kesham'am vayyit'atzvu ha'anashim vayyichar lahem me'od ki-nevalah asah veYisra'el lishkav et bat-Ya'akov vekhen lo ye'aseh.
verses.study_note: The sons of Jacob return and their emotional response is twofold and intense: they 'were grieved' (vayyit'atzvu — the same word as God's grief in Gen 6:6 at the corruption of creation) and 'very wroth' (vayyichar lahem me'od — burning anger). The narrator's own moral commentary appears in the word 'nevalah' — a word of profound significance in the Hebrew moral vocabulary. 'Nevalah' (H5039) is not merely a mistake or a social impropriety; it is an act of radical disorder, a violation of the covenant moral order, a sacrilege. The same word is used in the near-parallel story of the concubine at Gibeah (Judg 19:23-24) and for Amnon's rape of Tamar (2 Sam 13:12). The narrator then introduces for the first time the name 'Israel' in a moral/communal sense — 'he had wrought folly IN ISRAEL.' This is extraordinary: Jacob has only just received the name Israel (Gen 32:28, 35:10 will formally confirm it). The phrase 'in Israel' points beyond a family to a covenant community with communal standards of holiness. And the verdict: 'which thing ought not to be done' (vekhen lo ye'aseh) — this is the moral absolute of Scripture delivered in the plainest terms.
verses.doctrinal_note: 'Folly in Israel' (nevalah beYisrael) becomes a technical phrase in Hebrew Scripture for covenant-violating sexual sin. It appears in Judges 20:6,10 (Gibeah), 2 Samuel 13:12 (Tamar), and Joshua 7:15 (Achan's covenant breach). The phrase signals that sexual ethics are not private matters in the covenant community but communal ones — sin against a person is sin against the whole body of God's people. This foreshadows the NT principle that 'whether one member suffer, all the members suffer with it' (1 Cor 12:26).

verses.kjv_text: And Hamor communed with them, saying, The soul of my son Shechem longeth for your daughter: I pray you give her him to wife.
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר חֲמוֹר אִתָּם לֵאמֹר שְׁכֶם בְּנִי חָשְׁקָה נַפְשׁוֹ בְּבִתְּכֶם תְּנוּ נָא אֹתָהּ לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber Chamor ittam lemor Shekhem beni chashkah nafsho bevitkhem tenu na otah lo le'ishah.
verses.study_note: Hamor's speech to Jacob and his sons uses the language of passionate attachment: 'the soul of my son longeth' (chashkah nafsho). 'Chashak' (H2836) is a word of intense desire and attachment — used of God's love for Israel (Deut 7:7: 'the LORD did not set His love on you because you were more numerous...'). Hamor's diplomacy is skillful: he begins not with the violation but with his son's love, appealing to the universal sympathy a father feels for a son in love. His request is simple and direct: 'Give her to him as wife.' The word 'tenu' (give) — the same root as 'matan' (gift, marriage gift) — frames the negotiation as one of mutual gift exchange rather than acknowledging the prior violation.

verses.kjv_text: And make ye marriages with us, and give your daughters unto us, and take our daughters unto you.
verses.original_text: וְהִתְחַתְּנוּ אֹתָנוּ בְּנֹתֵיכֶם תִּתְּנוּ לָנוּ וְאֶת־בְּנֹתֵינוּ תִּקְחוּ לָכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehitchatenu otanu benotekhem tittenu lanu ve'et-benotenu tikkchu lakhem.
verses.study_note: Hamor now expands the proposal far beyond Dinah. He proposes full intermarriage between the two communities: 'make marriages with us' (hitchatenu — from 'chatan,' father-in-law/bridegroom, the root of covenant kinship by marriage). This is the theological heart of the danger. The proposal is precisely what the covenant forbids: assimilation through intermarriage with Canaan. Deuteronomy 7:3 expressly prohibits making 'marriages' with the Canaanites, using the identical word 'titchaten' (Deut 7:3). Hamor is unknowingly offering the covenant people their greatest temptation: prosperity, belonging, and peace — at the cost of their distinct identity as God's people.
verses.doctrinal_note: Intermarriage with Canaan was the defining threat to covenant identity throughout the Old Testament (Deut 7:1-4, Ezra 9-10, Neh 13:23-27). Hamor's proposal is seductive because it sounds like peace, prosperity, and social inclusion. But the price is the dissolution of the covenant community into the surrounding culture. The NT parallel is the warning against being 'unequally yoked' (2 Cor 6:14) — not racial separation but covenantal/spiritual separation. The people of God in every era face this precise temptation: belonging and prosperity through assimilation vs. faithfulness and distinction as a covenant people.

verses.kjv_text: And ye shall dwell with us: and the land shall be before you; dwell and trade ye therein, and get you possessions therein.
verses.original_text: וְאִתָּנוּ תֵּשֵׁבוּ וְהָאָרֶץ תִּהְיֶה לִפְנֵיכֶם שְׁבוּ וּסְחָרוּהָ וְהֵאָחֲזוּ בָּהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'ittanu teshevu veha'aretz tihyeh lifnekhem shevu uscharuha vehe'achazu vah.
verses.study_note: Hamor's offer extends to full civic integration: live with us, have freedom of movement in the land, trade ('scharuhah' — from 'sachar,' merchant/trading), and acquire property ('he'achazu' — gain a holding, take possession). This is a breathtaking offer. The land promised to Abraham (Gen 12:7, 15:18-21) is being offered by a Canaanite prince as a trade concession. The irony is profound: Hamor offers Jacob's family the very land God has already given them — but on Canaan's terms rather than God's. 'The land shall be before you' uses language that sounds like the divine promise but inverts its covenant context.

verses.kjv_text: And Shechem said unto her father and unto her brethren, Let me find grace in your eyes, and what ye shall say unto me I will give.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר שְׁכֶם אֶל־אָבִיהָ וְאֶל־אַחֶיהָ תִּמְצָא־חֵן בְּעֵינֵיכֶם אֲשֶׁר תֹּאמְרוּ אֵלַי אֶתֵּן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Shekhem el-aviha ve'el-acheha timtza-chen be'einikhem asher tomru elai etten.
verses.study_note: Shechem himself now speaks directly to Dinah's father and brothers. His plea — 'let me find grace (chen) in your eyes' — uses the language of covenant favor (the same word used of Noah finding favor with God, Gen 6:8, and of Abraham before the three visitors, Gen 18:3). He makes a blanket commitment: 'whatsoever ye shall say unto me I will give.' This is the offer of total compliance, a desperate declaration by a man who wants what was already done to be made right. The unlimited offer will be exploited to devastating effect.

verses.kjv_text: Ask me never so much dowry and gift, and I will give according as ye shall say unto me: but give me the damsel to wife.
verses.original_text: הַרְבּוּ עָלַי מְאֹד מֹהַר וּמַתָּן וְאֶתְּנָה כַּאֲשֶׁר תֹּאמְרוּ אֵלָי וּתְנוּ לִי אֶת־הַנַּעֲרָ לְאִשָּׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Harbu alai me'od mohar umattan ve'ettenah ka'asher tomru elai utennu li et-hanna'ara le'ishah.
verses.study_note: Shechem offers unlimited 'mohar' and 'mattan' — the two components of the ancient Israelite bride-price. The 'mohar' (H4119) is the formal bride-price paid to the girl's father as legal compensation and covenant payment. The 'mattan' (H4976) is the gift given directly to the bride or her family. Shechem's willingness to pay 'never so much' signals his genuine desire but also his profound misunderstanding: no financial payment can restore what was taken. The Law would later set a bride-price of 50 shekels specifically for this situation (Deut 22:29), capping the financial remedy precisely because the violation is ultimately not a financial matter. Shechem ends with his refrain: 'give me the damsel to wife' (nanna'ara le'ishah) — the deepest desire behind all the negotiation.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Jacob answered Shechem and Hamor his father deceitfully, and said, because he had defiled Dinah their sister:
verses.original_text: וַיַּעֲנוּ בְנֵי־יַעֲקֹב אֶת־שְׁכֶם וְאֶת־חֲמוֹר אָבִיו בְּמִרְמָה וַיְדַבְּרוּ אֲשֶׁר טִמֵּא אֵת דִּינָה אֲחֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'anu venei-Ya'akov et-Shekhem ve'et-Chamor aviv bemirmah vayedabberu asher timme' et Dinah achotam.
verses.study_note: The narrator inserts a devastating editorial comment before reporting what the sons said: 'deceitfully' (bemirmah). This single word pre-judges everything that follows and prevents the reader from being deceived along with Hamor and Shechem. 'Mirmah' (H4820) is the word used for Jacob's own deception of Isaac (Gen 27:35 — 'thy brother came with subtlety/mirmah'). Now his sons employ the same weapon — the text may be inviting reflection on the inheritance of character. The justification given for the deceit — 'because he had defiled Dinah' (asher timme' et Dinah) — repeats 'timme' for the third time in the chapter. The wrong is real; the question is whether deceit is a legitimate response to wrong.
verses.doctrinal_note: The sons of Jacob's deceit is not endorsed by the narrative. The text explicitly labels it 'mirmah' (deceit/treachery) — the same word Scripture uses for Jacob's deception of his father (Gen 27:35) and for those who deal treacherously (Ps 55:23, Prov 12:17). The end (justice for Dinah) does not justify the means (sacred deception using circumcision as a weapon). This stands as a case study in the distinction between righteous anger and sinful methods — a distinction Paul later articulates: 'Be ye angry, and sin not' (Eph 4:26).

verses.kjv_text: And they said unto them, We cannot do this thing, to give our sister to one that is uncircumcised; for that were a reproach unto us:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֲלֵיהֶם לֹא נוּכַל לַעֲשׂוֹת הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה לָתֵת אֶת־אֲחֹתֵנוּ לְאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר לוֹ עָרְלָה כִּי־חֶרְפָּה הִוא לָנוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomeru aleihem lo nukhal la'asot haddavar hazzeh latet et-achotenu le'ish asher lo 'orlah ki-cherpah hi lanu.
verses.study_note: The sons' stated reason for refusing intermarriage is real theology deployed as tactical cover: they cannot give their sister to one who is 'uncircumcised' ('orlah — literally 'having a foreskin'). Circumcision was the covenant sign given to Abraham (Gen 17) — the mark of belonging to the covenant people of God. To give Dinah to an uncircumcised man would indeed be a 'reproach' (cherpah — disgrace, shame). This is not invented theology; it reflects genuine covenant distinction. The deceit lies not in the theological principle but in the application: they use a holy sign as a military stratagem. Circumcision is being weaponized.

verses.kjv_text: But in this will we consent unto you: If ye will be as we be, that every male of you be circumcised;
verses.original_text: אַךְ בְּזֹאת נֵאוֹת לָכֶם אִם תִּהְיוּ כָמֹנוּ לְהִמֹּל לָכֶם כָּל־זָכָר׃
verses.transliteration: Akh bezot ne'ot lakhem im tihyu khamonu lehimmol lakhem kol-zakhar.
verses.study_note: The condition is stated: 'if ye will be as we be' (im tihyu khamonu) — literally, 'if you will become like us.' The sons demand complete biological assimilation into the covenant sign. Note the extraordinary demand: not Shechem alone but 'every male' (kol-zakhar) in the entire city. This escalation beyond any reasonable marriage negotiation exposes the plan as something other than a genuine condition for acceptance. No one requires the circumcision of an entire city to permit a single marriage. The demand is designed to render the Shechemites militarily vulnerable.

verses.kjv_text: Then will we give our daughters unto you, and we will take your daughters to us, and we will dwell with you, and we will become one people.
verses.original_text: וְנָתַנּוּ אֶת־בְּנֹתֵינוּ לָכֶם וְאֶת־בְּנֹתֵיכֶם נִקַּח לָנוּ וְיָשַׁבְנוּ אִתְּכֶם וְהָיִינוּ לְעַם אֶחָד׃
verses.transliteration: Venatannu et-benotenu lakhem ve'et-benotekhem niqqach lanu veyashavnu ittekhem vehayinu le'am echad.
verses.study_note: The sons promise intermarriage and the sharing of daughters on both sides, dwelling together, and most strikingly — 'becoming one people' (vehayinu le'am echad). The phrase 'am echad' (one people) is precisely what the covenant prohibits. Israel is called to be a 'people' (am) set apart, distinct, holy (Deut 7:6 — 'a holy people unto the LORD thy God'). The sons use covenant language — 'one people' — to describe what would be the dissolution of the covenant. There is deep irony in offering full assimilation as a negotiating tactic; this is the very outcome that Jacob and the patriarchal narrative have been straining against since Abraham.

verses.kjv_text: But if ye will not hearken unto us, to be circumcised; then will we take our daughter, and we will be gone.
verses.original_text: וְאִם לֹא תִשְׁמְעוּ אֵלֵינוּ לְהִמּוֹל וְלָקַחְנוּ אֶת־בִּתֵּנוּ וְהָלָכְנוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im lo tishme'u eleinu lehimmol velakachnu et-bittenu vehalakhnu.
verses.study_note: The ultimatum: comply or the deal is off and Dinah is taken home. 'We will take our daughter' (velakachnu et-bittenu) — the word 'lakach' (take) is the same verb used for Shechem's taking of Dinah in v.2. The brothers threaten to reclaim what was taken. The threat is credible — Hamor and Shechem know the sons are angry and capable of action. The threat of departure rather than war is the final element that makes the deal seem feasible to the Shechemites: comply and gain everything; refuse and lose the alliance.

verses.kjv_text: And their words pleased Hamor, and Shechem Hamor’s son.
verses.original_text: וַיִּיטְבוּ דִבְרֵיהֶם בְּעֵינֵי חֲמוֹר וּבְעֵינֵי שְׁכֶם בֶּן־חֲמוֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitvu divrehem be'einei Chamor uve'einei Shekhem ben-Chamor.
verses.study_note: Brief and ominous: the words 'pleased' (vayyitvu — were good in the eyes of) both Hamor and Shechem. The same Hebrew idiom for approval that God uses when He sees creation 'that it was good' (ki tov) is here applied to a deceitful plan received by unsuspecting victims. The Shechemites hear an offer of intermarriage, peaceful coexistence, and economic integration — what could be better? Their pleased acceptance is the moment the trap closes.

verses.kjv_text: And the young man deferred not to do the thing, because he had delight in Jacob’s daughter: and he was more honourable than all the house of his father.
verses.original_text: וְלֹא־אֵחַר הַנַּעַר לַעֲשׂוֹת הַדָּבָר כִּי חָפֵץ בְּבַת־יַעֲקֹב וְהוּא נִכְבָּד מִכֹּל בֵּית אָבִיו׃
verses.transliteration: Velo-echar hanna'ar la'asot haddavar ki chafetz bebat-Ya'akov vehu nikhbad mikkol beit aviv.
verses.study_note: Shechem did not delay (lo-echar). His speed is driven by love: 'he had delight' (chafetz) in Jacob's daughter. 'Chafetz' (H2654) is a word of genuine pleasure and desire — used of God's delight in His servants (Ps 18:19) and of true desire (Ps 37:4). Shechem is further described as 'more honourable' (nikhbad — honored, weighty, respected) than all his father's house. This positive characterization creates the chapter's deepest moral tension: Shechem is not a villain in the conventional mold. He is a man of standing, genuine love, and swift action who violated first and then sought to make it right. His eagerness to comply with an extreme demand speaks to the depth of his desire. And yet he will die for it.

verses.kjv_text: And Hamor and Shechem his son came unto the gate of their city, and communed with the men of their city, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא חֲמוֹר וּשְׁכֶם בְּנוֹ אֶל־שַׁעַר עִירָם וַיְדַבְּרוּ אֶל־אַנְשֵׁי עִירָם לֵאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayavo Chamor uShekhem beno el-sha'ar iram vaydabberu el-anshei iram lemor.
verses.study_note: Hamor and Shechem go to the gate of their city — the public forum where civic decisions are made and ratified (the same civic institution where Lot sat in v.1 of chapter 19, and where Boaz will later negotiate for Ruth in Ruth 4:1-11). They address 'the men of their city' — the full civic assembly. Their proposal to their own people in vv.21-23 is a masterwork of persuasive rhetoric: they present the alliance as economically advantageous and the condition as minor. What they do NOT mention is that the alliance began with a violation.

verses.kjv_text: These men are peaceable with us; therefore let them dwell in the land, and trade therein; for the land, behold, it is large enough for them; let us take their daughters to us for wives, and let us give them our daughters.
verses.original_text: הָאֲנָשִׁים הָאֵלֶּה שְׁלֵמִים הֵם אִתָּנוּ וְיֵשְׁבוּ בָאָרֶץ וְיִסְחֲרוּ אֹתָהּ וְהָאָרֶץ הִנֵּה רַחֲבַת־יָדַיִם לִפְנֵיהֶם אֶת־בְּנֹתָם נִקַּח לָנוּ לְנָשִׁים וְאֶת־בְּנֹתֵינוּ נִתֵּן לָהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Ha'anashim ha'elleh shelemim hem ittanu veyeshevu va'aretz veyischaru otah veha'aretz hinneh rachavat-yadayim lifneihem et-benotam niqqach lanu lenashim ve'et-benotenu nittenu lahem.
verses.study_note: Hamor's speech to his city is politically shrewd. He leads with the economic argument: the land is large enough (rachavat-yadayim — literally 'broad of hands'), there is room for everyone, and trade will enrich the city. He frames the intermarriage as mutual benefit. 'Shelemim' (peaceable, wholesome — from the same root as 'shalom') describes Jacob's family as safe neighbors. He presents this as an unambiguous win for Shechem — the sons of Jacob are commercial assets, not threats. The deception compounds: Jacob's sons have been deceptive to Hamor; Hamor is now being economically deceptive to his own people, downplaying the cost and the circumstance.

verses.kjv_text: Only herein will the men consent unto us for to dwell with us, to be one people, if every male among us be circumcised, as they are circumcised.
verses.original_text: אַךְ בְּזֹאת יֵאֹתוּ לָנוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים לָשֶׁבֶת אִתָּנוּ לִהְיוֹת לְעַם אֶחָד בְּהִמּוֹל לָנוּ כָּל־זָכָר כַּאֲשֶׁר הֵם נִמֹּלִים׃
verses.transliteration: Akh bezot ye'otu lanu ha'anashim lashevet ittanu lihyot le'am echad behimmol lanu kol-zakhar ka'asher hem nimmolem.
verses.study_note: Hamor now presents the condition: circumcision of all males, to become 'one people' (le'am echad — the same phrase from v.16). He presents this as a modest price for enormous gain. Circumcision, while physically significant, was a common practice in parts of the ancient Near East — Hamor may not have recognized the full theological weight of what the sons of Jacob were demanding. To Hamor, it is a ritual mark of alliance; to Jacob's sons, it is the covenant seal of God. The Shechemites are being asked to bear a sign whose meaning they do not share.

verses.kjv_text: Shall not their cattle and their substance and every beast of theirs be ours? only let us consent unto them, and they will dwell with us.
verses.original_text: מִקְנֵהֶם וְקִנְיָנָם וְכָל־בְּהֶמְתָּם הֲלוֹא לָנוּ הֵם אַךְ נֵאוֹתָה לָהֶם וְיֵשְׁבוּ אִתָּנוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Miknehem vekinyanam vekhol-behemtam halo lanu hem akh ne'otah lahem veyeshevu ittanu.
verses.study_note: Hamor closes with the economic clincher: 'shall not their cattle and their substance and every beast of theirs be ours?' — a candid appeal to greed. He hints that intermarriage with Jacob's prosperous family will eventually merge their considerable wealth with Shechem's. The rhetorical question expects the answer 'yes' — Jacob's herds and goods are great (Gen 33:13-14). This is Hamor being diplomatically candid with his own people in a way he was not with Jacob. He presents the circumcision as an investment with certain financial return. The irony is devastating: in v.29, Jacob's sons will indeed take 'their cattle and their substance' — as plunder.

verses.kjv_text: And unto Hamor and unto Shechem his son hearkened all that went out of the gate of his city; and every male was circumcised, all that went out of the gate of his city.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁמְעוּ אֶל־חֲמוֹר וְאֶל־שְׁכֶם בְּנוֹ כָּל־יֹצְאֵי שַׁעַר עִירוֹ וַיִּמֹּלוּ כָּל־זָכָר כָּל־יֹצְאֵי שַׁעַר עִירוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishme'u el-Chamor ve'el-Shekhem beno kol-yotze'ei sha'ar iro vayyimmolu kol-zakhar kol-yotze'ei sha'ar iro.
verses.study_note: The response is unanimous and complete. 'All that went out of the gate of his city' — the full civic male population — hearkened and were circumcised. The phrase 'went out of the gate' appears twice for emphasis. This is the same gate where Hamor and Shechem made their proposal (v.20) and where Lot sat in Genesis 19:1 — the gate as the place of communal decision and now communal vulnerability. The entire city accepts the covenant sign without the covenant reality behind it. This anticipates Paul's warnings about circumcision without faith (Rom 2:28-29, Gal 5:6) — the form of covenant without its substance.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass on the third day, when they were sore, that two of the sons of Jacob, Simeon and Levi, Dinah’s brethren, took each man his sword, and came upon the city boldly, and slew all the males.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בַיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי בִּהְיוֹתָם כֹּאֲבִים וַיִּקְחוּ שְׁנֵי בְנֵי יַעֲקֹב שִׁמְעוֹן וְלֵוִי אֲחֵי דִינָה אִישׁ חַרְבּוֹ וַיָּבֹאוּ עַל־הָעִיר בֶּטַח וַיַּהַרְגוּ כָּל־זָכָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi vayom hashelishi bihyotam ko'avim vayyikkchu shenei venei Ya'akov Shim'on veLevi achei Dinah ish charbo vayyavo'u al-ha'ir vetach vayyaharegu kol-zakhar.
verses.study_note: The third day is the day of maximum incapacitation after circumcision — when inflammation and pain peak and movement is most restricted. Simeon and Levi, specifically identified as 'Dinah's brethren' (full brothers through Leah), act alone and without authorization from Jacob. They enter the city 'boldly' (vetach — with confidence, securely — the same word used for Israel dwelling in safety under God's blessing, Deut 33:12,28; Jer 23:6). The word vetach (H983) is used almost universally for the security that comes from God's protection — here it is the confidence of two armed men who know their targets cannot fight back. They kill 'every male' (kol-zakhar) — every man who received the circumcision that was promised as a covenant of peace. The circumcision knife has become a death warrant. This is the darkest irony of the chapter: the covenant sign, meant to mark those set apart for life, here identifies those marked for slaughter.
verses.doctrinal_note: The third day resonates through Scripture as a day of decisive divine action: Abraham and Isaac on the third day (Gen 22:4), the Resurrection of Christ (1 Cor 15:4), Israel preparing for the covenant at Sinai (Exod 19:11,16), and Jonah emerging from the fish (Jon 1:17). Here 'the third day' brings not resurrection but slaughter. The misuse of the covenant sign as a weapon for vengeance foreshadows Paul's warning that circumcision without heart-transformation is worse than useless (Gal 5:2-6). Simeon and Levi will be rebuked by Jacob (v.30) and cursed in his final blessing (Gen 49:5-7) for exactly this action.

verses.kjv_text: And they slew Hamor and Shechem his son with the edge of the sword, and took Dinah out of Shechem’s house, and went out.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־חֲמוֹר וְאֶת־שְׁכֶם בְּנוֹ הָרְגוּ לְפִי חָרֶב וַיִּקְחוּ אֶת־דִּינָה מִבֵּית שְׁכֶם וַיֵּצֵאוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-Chamor ve'et-Shekhem beno haregu lefi charev vayyikkchu et-Dinah mibbeit Shekhem vayyetze'u.
verses.study_note: Hamor and Shechem are killed 'with the edge of the sword' (lefi cherev — literally 'to the mouth of the sword'). Then they take Dinah from Shechem's house. This detail reveals that Dinah has been in Shechem's house throughout the negotiations — she was never free while the talking continued. Shechem's hold on her was ongoing, not simply a past violation. The brothers' retrieval of Dinah is the stated justification; the killing of an entire city is the method that will horrify their father. 'They went out' (vayyetze'u) — with the same verb that began the chapter when Dinah 'went out' (vattetzeh). The chapter's opening and this verse are bound by the same word of departure.

verses.kjv_text: The sons of Jacob came upon the slain, and spoiled the city, because they had defiled their sister.
verses.original_text: בְּנֵי יַעֲקֹב בָּאוּ עַל־הַחֲלָלִים וַיָּבֹזּוּ הָעִיר אֲשֶׁר טִמְּאוּ אֲחֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Benei Ya'akov ba'u al-hachallelim vayyabozu ha'ir asher timme'u achotam.
verses.study_note: The other brothers — distinct from Simeon and Levi in v.25 — now come upon the slain and plunder (vayyabozu — spoil, loot) the city. The narrator provides the reason for the spoiling: 'because they had defiled their sister' (asher timme'u achotam). The fifth and final use of 'timme' in the chapter. The 'they' here is interesting — the entire population is held responsible for the defilement of one girl. This collective responsibility reflects the ancient Near Eastern practice of communal guilt, but its justice is debatable: the text records the reason without pronouncing it just.

verses.kjv_text: They took their sheep, and their oxen, and their asses, and that which was in the city, and that which was in the field,
verses.original_text: אֶת־צֹאנָם וְאֶת־בְּקָרָם וְאֶת־חֲמֹרֵיהֶם וְאֵת אֲשֶׁר־בָּעִיר וְאֶת־אֲשֶׁר בַּשָּׂדֶה לָקָחוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Et-tzonam ve'et-beqaram ve'et-chamoreihem ve'et asher-ba'ir ve'et asher bassadeh laqachu.
verses.study_note: The comprehensive list of plunder: sheep, oxen, donkeys, everything in the city and the field. Hamor had promised (v.23) that Jacob's family's livestock would eventually become Shechem's. Now it is Shechem's livestock that becomes Jacob's sons'. The ironic reversal of Hamor's economic argument is complete. Note the irony of 'chamoreihem' (their donkeys) — the livestock named after Hamor (whose name means donkey) now passes to his killers. Every detail of the plunder list underscores the total reversal.

verses.kjv_text: And all their wealth, and all their little ones, and their wives took they captive, and spoiled even all that was in the house.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־כָּל־חֵילָם וְאֶת־כָּל־טַפָּם וְאֶת־נְשֵׁיהֶם שָׁבוּ וַיָּבֹזּוּ וְאֵת כָּל־אֲשֶׁר בַּבָּיִת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-kol-cheilam ve'et-kol-tappam ve'et-nesheihem shavu vayyabozu ve'et kol-asher babbayit.
verses.study_note: The plunder extends to human captives: 'little ones' (tappam — children, dependents) and 'wives' (nesheihem). They are 'taken captive' (shavu — from shavah, to take as spoil or prisoner). This detail will provoke Jacob's rebuke. He had feared exactly this — that his family's violent action would make them enemies of the surrounding peoples (v.30). The comprehensive nature of the plunder — wealth, children, wives, household goods — is the full scale of conquest plunder. Jacob's sons have conducted an unauthorized war against a Canaanite city, an action that exceeds any mandate from God or from their father.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, Ye have troubled me to make me to stink among the inhabitants of the land, among the Canaanites and the Perizzites: and I being few in number, they shall gather themselves together against me, and slay me; and I shall be destroyed, I and my house.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב אֶל־שִׁמְעוֹן וְאֶל־לֵוִי עֲכַרְתֶּם אֹתִי לְהַבְאִישֵׁנִי בְּיֹשֵׁב הָאָרֶץ בַּכְּנַעֲנִי וּבַפְּרִזִּי וַאֲנִי מְתֵי מִסְפָּר וְנֶאֶסְפוּ עָלַי וְהִכּוּנִי וְנִשְׁמַדְתִּי אֲנִי וּבֵיתִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Ya'akov el-Shim'on ve'el-Levi akhartem oti lehavisheini beyoshev ha'aretz bakkena'ani uvefriizzi va'ani metei mispar vene'esfu alai vehikkunni venishhmadeti ani uveti.
verses.study_note: Jacob's rebuke is entirely pragmatic, not moral. He accuses Simeon and Levi of two things: 'ye have troubled me' (akhartem — from achar/akhor, to make turbid, to roil — used of Achan troubling Israel in Josh 7:25) and 'to make me to stink' (lehavisheini — to cause me to become a bad odor, to make me loathed). Jacob's concerns are reputational and survival-based: he fears coalition revenge from the Canaanites and Perizzites. He describes himself as 'few in number' (metei mispar) — militarily vulnerable. Notably absent from Jacob's rebuke is any moral condemnation of the killing itself, any mention of Dinah, any reference to God's justice or guidance. He speaks as a man afraid for his strategic position, not as a man of covenant righteousness. This silence indicts Jacob as much as the deed indicts Simeon and Levi. Compare Abraham's active intercession for Sodom (Gen 18) with Jacob's passive silence about Dinah and his pragmatic-only rebuke of his sons.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jacob's rebuke without moral content is a study in leadership failure. He did not act when he should have (v.5 — he held his peace), and he does not address the right issues when he finally speaks (v.30 — only strategic concerns, nothing about Dinah or divine justice). The contrast with God's own action as the vindicator of the violated is stark. The OT consistently calls God 'the avenger of blood' (Num 35:19) and 'judge of the widow and fatherless' (Ps 68:5). Jacob's failure to engage God's justice-framework is a spiritual failure. Genesis 49:5-7 reveals God's ultimate verdict on Simeon and Levi's action: Jacob will prophetically curse both tribes for this act of wrath.

verses.kjv_text: And they said, Should he deal with our sister as with an harlot?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ הַכְזוֹנָה יַעֲשֶׂה אֶת־אֲחוֹתֵנוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomeru hakhzonah ya'aseh et-achotenu.
verses.study_note: The final word of the chapter belongs to Simeon and Levi — and it is a question, not an answer. 'Shall he deal with our sister as with a harlot?' (hakhzonah ya'aseh et-achotenu). It is the most powerful seven Hebrew words in the chapter. 'Zonah' (H2181) is the word for prostitute or harlot — a woman treated as a sexual commodity without covenant protection, dignity, or legal standing. The brothers' question reframes everything they have done not as vengeance but as advocacy: if the covenant family will not defend its own against violation, what does the covenant mean? Their question pierces Jacob's pragmatic silence and stands unanswered. The chapter ends without narrative closure: no divine verdict, no resolution, no peace. The question hangs in the air. The reader is left to judge. Simeon and Levi were wrong in their method (Gen 49:5-7 confirms this); they were not wrong in their outrage. Jacob was right that their action endangered the family; he was wrong not to have spoken and acted before they did. Dinah — the one whose story began the chapter — is never heard from directly. She has been the object of every act and every speech but has no voice. This literary silence is itself a statement: the violated are often the last to be heard.
verses.doctrinal_note: The final question — 'should he deal with our sister as with a harlot?' — is one of Scripture's great unanswered interrogatives. Like the close of Jonah ('should I not spare Nineveh?') and the end of the prodigal son's parable (the older brother's question hanging in the air), Genesis 34 ends without resolving the tension it has created. This is intentional. Scripture frequently refuses to tidy up the moral complexity of human experience. The chapter raises questions about justice, method, honor, protection of the vulnerable, leadership failure, corporate responsibility, and the cost of compromise with the surrounding culture — and answers none of them definitively. The answers come progressively: in Gen 49 (Jacob's curse on the brothers' anger), in the Mosaic law (which sets specific remedies for this crime), in the Psalms (God as defender of the violated), and ultimately in Christ, who is both the perfect judge who does not deal with sinners as they deserve, and the perfect advocate who does not leave His own defenseless.

meta.overview: Genesis 34 is one of the most harrowing chapters in the patriarchal narratives. Dinah, daughter of Jacob and Leah, goes out to visit the women of the land and is seen by Shechem son of Hamor the Hivite, who seizes and defiles her. Yet Shechem's soul cleaves to Dinah and he asks his father to arrange a marriage. When Jacob's sons hear, they are filled with rage. Hamor and Shechem come to negotiate, offering intermarriage and land. Jacob's sons respond with cunning: they will agree only if every male in the city is circumcised. Hamor and Shechem accept, persuade their city, and all males submit to circumcision. On the third day, while the men are incapacitated, Simeon and Levi take their swords and kill every male, including Hamor and Shechem. The other brothers then plunder the city, taking flocks, women, children, and goods. Jacob rebukes Simeon and Levi for making him a stench among the Canaanites. They respond with a defiant counter-question: should our sister be treated as a harlot? The chapter raises profound questions about justice, deception, covenant identity, the purity of God's people in a corrupt land, and the terrible cost of vengeance that exceeds its mandate. It stands as a dark hinge between the covenant promises of chapter 33 and the divine redirection of chapter 35.
meta.theme: Defilement, Deception, Vengeance & the Cost of Compromise with Canaan
meta.keyWordMeaning: Humbled/Afflicted / Made unclean — the twin Hebrew roots for the defilement of Dinah (v.2,5,13,27). They saturate the chapter and drive every response in it.
outline: Dinah Defiled by Shechem (vv.1-4): She goes out; he takes her; yet his soul cleaves to her
Jacob Hears; His Sons Return from the Field (vv.5-7): Grief turns to fury — 'folly in Israel'
Hamor and Shechem Negotiate (vv.8-12): Intermarriage, land, and any bride-price offered
The Sons of Jacob's Deceitful Counter-Proposal (vv.13-17): Circumcision as the condition
Shechem's Eager Compliance — The City Agrees (vv.18-24): Every male circumcised
The Sword of Simeon and Levi (vv.25-26): The third day — all males slain, Dinah taken back
The Plunder of Shechem (vv.27-29): The brothers spoil the city
Jacob's Rebuke and the Final Counter-Question (vv.30-31): Fear of Canaanite retaliation vs. honor of the covenant people

verses.kjv_text: And God said unto Jacob, Arise, go up to Bethel, and dwell there: and make there an altar unto God, that appeared unto thee when thou fleddest from the face of Esau thy brother.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים אֶל־יַעֲקֹב קוּם עֲלֵה בֵית־אֵל וְשֶׁב־שָׁם וַעֲשֵׂה־שָׁם מִזְבֵּחַ לָאֵל הַנִּרְאֶה אֵלֶיךָ בְּבָרְחֲךָ מִפְּנֵי עֵשָׂו אֲחִיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Elohim el-Ya'akov kum aleh Veit-El veshev-sham va'aseh-sham mizbeach la-El hannir'eh eleikha bevorchakha mippnei Esav achikha.
verses.study_note: God speaks directly to Jacob — no angel, no vision, but the divine voice itself. The command is fourfold: 'Arise' (kum), 'Go up' (aleh), 'Dwell' (shev), 'Make an altar' (va'aseh mizbeach). The verb 'arise' (kum) is the word of resurrection-like new beginning throughout Scripture. God deliberately recalls the crisis of Jacob's original flight from Esau — 'when thou fleddest' (bevorchakha) — linking the command to Jacob's first encounter with God at Bethel in chapter 28. Some 20 years have elapsed since then. Jacob has since met God at Mahanaim and Peniel (32:1-32), acquired wealth, reconciled with Esau, and settled near Shechem — but he has not returned to Bethel as he vowed (28:20-22). God is now calling Jacob to fulfill his long-deferred vow. The reminder of Esau (the crisis that drove him from home) is not condemnation but pastoral — God is reminding Jacob of the grace that covered him then, and is still covering him now.
verses.doctrinal_note: God is the keeper of our vows. Twenty years after Jacob's vow at Bethel (28:20-22), God calls him to account — not with punishment but with a renewed invitation. The divine patience with Jacob's delay foreshadows the New Covenant patience: 'The Lord is not slack concerning his promise' (2 Peter 3:9). Bethel means 'House of God' — the call to return to Bethel is always a call back to God's presence, worship, and covenant priority.

verses.kjv_text: Then Jacob said unto his household, and to all that were with him, Put away the strange gods that are among you, and be clean, and change your garments:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב אֶל־בֵּיתוֹ וְאֶל כָּל־אֲשֶׁר עִמּוֹ הָסִרוּ אֶת־אֱלֹהֵי הַנֵּכָר אֲשֶׁר בְּתֹכְכֶם וְהִטַּהֲרוּ וְהַחֲלִיפוּ שִׂמְלֹתֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Ya'akov el-beito ve'el kol-asher immo hasiru et-elohei hanneikhar asher betokekhem vehitaharu vehachilafu simloteikhem.
verses.study_note: Jacob's response to God's call is immediate and comprehensive. He addresses 'his household and all that were with him' — his wives, sons, servants, and the Shechemite captives of chapter 34. Three commands form a theology of preparation for worship: (1) 'Put away' (hasiru — remove, cast out) the foreign gods; (2) 'Be clean' (hitaharu — purify yourselves, a ritual/moral cleansing); (3) 'Change your garments' (hachaliftu simloteikhem — put on fresh clothes). This triad anticipates the Mosaic preparatory rituals at Sinai (Exod 19:10-14) and the Day of Atonement protocols. The 'foreign gods' (elohei hanneikhar — gods of the foreigner) include Rachel's household idols stolen from Laban (31:19), the plunder from Shechem, and gods that had accumulated through association with pagan culture. Jacob does not ask whether these gods exist — he treats them as real spiritual contaminations that must be removed before approaching the living God.

verses.kjv_text: And let us arise, and go up to Bethel; and I will make there an altar unto God, who answered me in the day of my distress, and was with me in the way which I went.
verses.original_text: וְנָקוּמָה וְנַעֲלֶה בֵּית־אֵל וְאֶעֱשֶׂה־שָּׁם מִזְבֵּחַ לָאֵל הָעֹנֶה אֹתִי בְּיוֹם צָרָתִי וַיְהִי עִמָּדִי בַּדֶּרֶךְ אֲשֶׁר הָלָכְתִּי׃
verses.transliteration: Venakumah vena'aleh Beit-El ve'e'eseh-sham mizbeach la-El ha'oneh oti beyom tzarati vayehi immadi badderekh asher halakhti.
verses.study_note: Jacob's own words reveal his theology of personal testimony. He describes God by His acts in his life, not by abstract attributes: 'who answered me in the day of my distress' (ha-oneh oti beyom tzarati) and 'who was with me in the way.' The first phrase echoes Psalm 50:15 and 91:15 — God answers in the day of trouble. The second, 'was with me in the way' (vayehi immadi badderekh), recalls God's specific promise to Jacob at Bethel: 'I am with thee, and will keep thee in all places whither thou goest' (28:15). Jacob now testifies that the promise was kept. The word 'tzarah' (distress, trouble) is used throughout the Psalms and prophets for times of acute crisis. Jacob is modeling what it means to worship from personal, covenantal memory — building an altar not of obligation but of gratitude.
verses.doctrinal_note: True worship arises from covenant memory — from personal encounter with the God who answered. Jacob's testimony 'He answered me in the day of my distress' is the pattern of all biblical praise. Psalm 50:15 articulates this exactly: 'Call upon me in the day of trouble: I will deliver thee, and thou shalt glorify me.' The altar at Bethel is built from remembered grace.

verses.kjv_text: And they gave unto Jacob all the strange gods which were in their hand, and all their earrings which were in their ears; and Jacob hid them under the oak which was by Shechem.
verses.original_text: וַיִּתְּנוּ אֶל־יַעֲקֹב אֵת כָּל־אֱלֹהֵי הַנֵּכָר אֲשֶׁר בְּיָדָם וְאֶת־הַנְּזָמִים אֲשֶׁר בְּאָזְנֵיהֶם וַיִּטְמֹן אֹתָם יַעֲקֹב תַּחַת הָאֵלָה אֲשֶׁר עִם־שְׁכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyittenu el-Ya'akov et kol-elohei hanneikhar asher beyadam ve'et-hannezamim asher be'ozneihem vayyitmon otam Ya'akov tachat ha'elah asher im-Shekhem.
verses.study_note: The household surrenders both 'the foreign gods... in their hand' and 'the earrings... in their ears' (hannezamim). Earrings in the ancient Near East were commonly amulets associated with pagan worship, bearing images of deities, or used in divination — their surrender is therefore as spiritually significant as surrendering the idols themselves. Jacob 'hid them' (vayyitmon — buried, concealed) under the oak at Shechem. This is the same great tree associated with Abraham's first altar in Canaan (12:6) and with the covenant renewal under Joshua (Josh 24:26). The burial under the oak is a ritual act of entombment — these gods are dead and belong in the ground. Jacob does not destroy them (perhaps from practical concern) but buries them in the profane earth of Shechem, far from Bethel. This is an act of deliberate de-consecration.

verses.kjv_text: And they journeyed: and the terror of God was upon the cities that were round about them, and they did not pursue after the sons of Jacob.
verses.original_text: וַיִּסְעוּ וַיְהִי חִתַּת אֱלֹהִים עַל־הֶעָרִים אֲשֶׁר סְבִיבוֹתֵיהֶם וְלֹא רָדְפוּ אַחֲרֵי בְּנֵי יַעֲקֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiss'u vayehi chittat Elohim al-he'arim asher sevivotehem velo radfu acharei benei Ya'akov.
verses.study_note: This verse answers the concern Jacob raised in 34:30 after the slaughter of Shechem: 'Ye have troubled me to make me to stink among the inhabitants of the land... they shall gather themselves together against me, and slay me.' Now God vindicates His pilgrim people. 'The terror of God' (chittat Elohim) falls upon the surrounding cities — the same divine panic that routed armies and paralyzed enemies throughout Israel's history (Exod 15:16; 23:27; Josh 2:9). It is not Jacob's military prowess or Simeon and Levi's violence that protects them — it is supernatural divine fear. The phrase 'they did not pursue' is remarkable given the provocation of the Shechem massacre; human vendetta is overruled by divine protection. God protects His covenant household on the journey to His altar.
verses.doctrinal_note: God protects those who obey His call to return to Him. The 'terror of God' (chittat Elohim) is a recurring feature of redemptive history — God causes enemy fear as part of His providential care for His people. This is the principle of Exodus 23:27-28 (God will send His terror before Israel) and Psalm 34:7 ('the angel of the LORD encampeth round about them that fear him'). Obedience to God's call to worship triggers divine protection for the journey.

verses.kjv_text: So Jacob came to Luz, which is in the land of Canaan, that is, Bethel, he and all the people that were with him.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא יַעֲקֹב לוּזָה אֲשֶׁר בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן הִוא בֵּית־אֵל הוּא וְכָל־הָעָם אֲשֶׁר עִמּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayavo Ya'akov Luzah asher be'eretz Kena'an hi Beit-El hu vekhol-ha'am asher immo.
verses.study_note: The narrator uses both names: 'Luz' (the Canaanite name) and 'Bethel' (the name Jacob gave it, meaning 'House of God' — 28:19). The dual naming underscores the transformation of a place: what the world calls 'Luz' (possibly meaning 'almond tree' or 'twisted/crooked'), God has made into His house. The pattern of God taking something ordinary and making it sacred runs throughout Scripture — Luz/Bethel, Moriah/Jerusalem, Mamre/Hebron. The detail that 'all the people' arrived with him recalls the household solidarity established in v.2 — they came together through the purification and together to the worship.

verses.kjv_text: And he built there an altar, and called the place Elbethel: because there God appeared unto him, when he fled from the face of his brother.
verses.original_text: וַיִּבֶן שָׁם מִזְבֵּחַ וַיִּקְרָא לַמָּקוֹם אֵל בֵּית־אֵל כִּי שָׁם נִגְלוּ אֵלָיו הָאֱלֹהִים בְּבָרְחוֹ מִפְּנֵי אָחִיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiven sham mizbeach vayyikra lammakom El Beit-El ki sham niglu elav ha-Elohim bevorcho mippnei achiv.
verses.study_note: Jacob builds the altar God commanded and names the place 'El-Bethel' — 'God of the House of God.' This is more than renaming; it is a theological precision: not just the place (Bethel) but the God who meets at the place (El Bethel). A subtle but profound distinction — the altar is not to a location but to a Person. The phrase 'there God appeared unto him' uses a remarkable plural verb: 'niglu' (they revealed themselves) with the singular subject 'ha-Elohim' — a grammatical hint of the divine plurality-in-unity that surfaces repeatedly in Genesis (1:26; 3:22; 11:7). The KJV captures this with 'God appeared' but the Hebrew reads literally 'there the Gods revealed themselves to him.' Most commentators see here an early Trinitarian intimation. The phrase 'when he fled from his brother' again recalls the origin — God's grace meets Jacob at his most desperate moment.
verses.doctrinal_note: Worship is always directed to a Person, not a place. 'El Bethel' — the God of the House of God — is Jacob's declaration that the site matters only because of Who meets there. This corrects both superstition (magic of sacred sites) and formalism (ritual without the living God). The plural verb 'niglu' with singular 'Elohim' — 'there God (plural verb) appeared' — is one of the grammatical hints of divine plurality that runs through Genesis, pointing forward to the full Trinitarian revelation of the New Testament.

verses.kjv_text: But Deborah Rebekah’s nurse died, and she was buried beneath Bethel under an oak: and the name of it was called Allonbachuth.
verses.original_text: וַתָּמָת דְּבֹרָה מֵינֶקֶת רִבְקָה וַתִּקָּבֵר מִתַּחַת לְבֵית־אֵל תַּחַת הָאַלּוֹן וַיִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ אַלּוֹן בָּכוּת׃
verses.transliteration: Vattamat Devorah meineqet Rivkah vattikkaver mittachat leBeit-El tachat ha'allon vayyikra shemo Allon Bakhut.
verses.study_note: An unexpected note of grief in the midst of covenant renewal. Deborah was Rebekah's nurse — a woman not previously mentioned since Genesis 24:59 where she accompanied Rebekah to Canaan to marry Isaac. She has apparently joined Jacob's household at some point, perhaps when Rebekah sent Jacob to Laban (27:43-45) or during the Paddan-Aram years. The tree is named 'Allon-bachuth' — 'Oak of Weeping.' The singular mention of this servant woman's death and burial with a named memorial is striking; she mattered enough to be remembered. The tree of weeping beneath Bethel stands as a tender detail: even at the 'House of God,' there are tears. God's presence does not abolish grief — it hallows it. This is the second death notice of chapter 35, between Rachel's death (v.19) to follow and Isaac's death (v.29).

verses.kjv_text: And God appeared unto Jacob again, when he came out of Padanaram, and blessed him.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּרָא אֱלֹהִים אֶל־יַעֲקֹב עוֹד בְּבֹאוֹ מִפַּדַּן אֲרָם וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyera Elohim el-Ya'akov od bevo'o mippaddan Aram vayevarekh oto.
verses.study_note: God appears to Jacob for the second time at Bethel — the first was 20 years earlier during his flight (28:10-22). 'Again' (od) — the same God returns to the same person at the same place, but Jacob is a different man. The divine appearance (vayera) is the standard theophany language — the same verb used of appearances to Abraham (12:7; 17:1; 18:1) and Isaac (26:2). The appearance is immediately followed by blessing (vayevarekh) — the blessing comes before the covenant reaffirmation in vv.10-13. God blesses before He speaks, grace precedes law. The mention that Jacob 'came out of Padan-aram' is retrospective — this situates the divine appearance as the concluding ratification of the entire Padan-aram period (chs. 29-35).
verses.doctrinal_note: God appears again to the same person at the same place — faithfulness, not novelty, is the signature of covenant relationship. The God of Genesis is 'the God who appears again.' He is not a God of a single encounter but of returning, renewing, deepening relationship. The blessing precedes the covenant word — grace is always the foundation upon which God's covenant speech rests.

verses.kjv_text: And God said unto him, Thy name is Jacob: thy name shall not be called any more Jacob, but Israel shall be thy name: and he called his name Israel.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ אֱלֹהִים שִׁמְךָ יַעֲקֹב לֹא־יִקָּרֵא שִׁמְךָ עוֹד יַעֲקֹב כִּי אִם־יִשְׂרָאֵל יִהְיֶה שְׁמֶךָ וַיִּקְרָא אֶת־שְׁמוֹ יִשְׂרָאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer lo Elohim shimkha Ya'akov lo-yikkare shimkha od Ya'akov ki im-Yisrael yihyeh shemekha vayyikra et-shemo Yisrael.
verses.study_note: This is the formal divine ratification of the name 'Israel' — first given by the mysterious wrestler at Peniel (32:28), but now confirmed by God in a solemn covenant context. The Peniel renaming was provisional and personal; this Bethel renaming is formal and generational — the name now defines the covenant people. 'Jacob' (Ya'akov — 'he who supplants/grasps the heel') is his birth-name, reflecting his grasping nature. 'Israel' (Yisrael — from 'sarah,' to struggle/prevail, and 'El,' God) means 'He who struggles with God' or 'God prevails' or 'Prince of God.' Both meanings are valid — Jacob wrestled with God and prevailed (32:28) yet it was God who ultimately prevailed in shaping him. Note that Jacob continues to be called 'Jacob' even after this point in Scripture — the old name is not erased but transcended. Similarly, believers are simultaneously 'sinners saved by grace' and 'new creations in Christ.'
verses.doctrinal_note: The renaming of Jacob to Israel is the Old Testament type of new birth and new identity in Christ. God changes names to declare transformed destinies. Abram becomes Abraham (17:5), Sarai becomes Sarah (17:15), Jacob becomes Israel. In Revelation 2:17 Christ promises 'a new name' to the overcomer. The old nature (Jacob the supplanter) is not annihilated but redeemed — brought into the service of the new name. Jacob's twelve sons will bear the name Israel as a nation, and the church is 'the Israel of God' (Galatians 6:16).

verses.kjv_text: And God said unto him, I am God Almighty: be fruitful and multiply; a nation and a company of nations shall be of thee, and kings shall come out of thy loins;
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ אֱלֹהִים אֲנִי אֵל שַׁדַּי פְּרֵה וּרְבֵה גּוֹי וּקְהַל גּוֹיִם יִהְיֶה מִמֶּךָּ וּמְלָכִים מֵחֲלָצֶיךָ יֵצֵאוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer lo Elohim ani El Shaddai pereh urveh goy ukhal goyim yihyeh mimmekha umelakhim mechalaltzekha yetze'u.
verses.study_note: God self-identifies as 'El Shaddai' — the same divine name used when God established the covenant with Abraham (17:1) and with Isaac (28:3 — when Isaac blesses Jacob before his departure). This establishes the triple covenantal continuity: Abraham–Isaac–Jacob all received El Shaddai, the God of covenant power. The covenant content is fourfold: (1) 'Be fruitful and multiply' — echoing the creation mandate (1:28) applied to covenant fruitfulness; (2) 'A nation' (goy — singular) shall come from you; (3) 'A company of nations' (kehal goyim) shall come — echoing the promise to Abraham of many nations (17:4-6); (4) 'Kings shall come out of thy loins' — the royal messianic line. The word 'chalatzim' (loins) is the Hebrew term for the seed-bearing aspect of the man, referring to biological descent. The promise of kings flowing from Jacob's loins reaches its apex in David, and ultimately in Christ, 'the Son of David.'
verses.doctrinal_note: El Shaddai (God Almighty) is the name of covenant power — the God who can fulfill what is humanly impossible. He appears to Abraham at 99 (17:1), blesses Isaac's blessing on Jacob (28:3), and now speaks to Jacob directly. The triple promise — nation, community of nations, kings — is the covenant architecture that will eventually produce the Davidic kingdom and the Messianic throne. 'Kings shall come out of thy loins' is fulfilled in David (Ruth 4:17), Solomon, and ultimately in Jesus Christ, 'which is of the seed of David according to the flesh' (Romans 1:3).

verses.kjv_text: And the land which I gave Abraham and Isaac, to thee I will give it, and to thy seed after thee will I give the land.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר נָתַתִּי לְאַבְרָהָם וּלְיִצְחָק לְךָ אֶתְּנֶנָּה וּלְזַרְעֲךָ אַחֲרֶיךָ אֶתֵּן אֶת־הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-ha'aretz asher natatti le'Avraham uleyitzchak lekha ettenennah ulezar'akha acharekha ettten et-ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The land promise is solemnly restated — the third element of the Abrahamic covenant (after descendants and divine relationship). The threefold patriarchal chain is explicit: 'which I gave Abraham and Isaac' — now confirmed to Jacob. The double use of 'I will give' (ettenennah... ettten) — both Jacob personally and his seed (zera) after him — extends the promise across generations. The land of Canaan is not merely a political homeland but the covenantal inheritance where God's kingdom will take root. The New Testament interprets the land promise eschatologically: 'Abraham... looked for a city which hath foundations, whose builder and maker is God' (Hebrews 11:10) — the ultimate fulfillment is the New Jerusalem (Revelation 21).
verses.doctrinal_note: The land promise is the territorial dimension of God's kingdom. It flows from Abraham to Isaac to Jacob with unbroken consistency — God never revises or withdraws the covenant land promise. The New Testament sees the physical land as a type of the eschatological 'rest' (Hebrews 4:8-9) and the New Earth (Romans 4:13 — Abraham heir of the 'world'; Revelation 21:1).

verses.kjv_text: And God went up from him in the place where he talked with him.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַל מֵעָלָיו אֱלֹהִים בַּמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר אִתּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'al me'alav Elohim bammakom asher dibber itto.
verses.study_note: God 'went up' (vayya'al) — the same directional verb as 'go up' to Bethel (v.1). As Jacob ascended to Bethel to meet God, now God ascends from Jacob. The divine departure is as deliberate as the divine arrival. The phrase 'the place where he talked with him' (bammakom asher dibber itto) gives the encounter a spatial sanctity — this ground has been consecrated by divine speech. The ascent of God anticipates the later biblical motif of God's ascension and the 'going up' of the divine glory (Ezekiel 11:23) and Christ's ascension (Acts 1:9-11). The spatial movement of God — descending to meet, ascending after speaking — is the structure of all divine condescension and return.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob set up a pillar in the place where he talked with him, even a pillar of stone: and he poured a drink offering thereon, and he poured oil thereon.
verses.original_text: וַיַּצֵּב יַעֲקֹב מַצֵּבָה בַּמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר אִתּוֹ מַצֶּבֶת אָבֶן וַיַּסֵּךְ עָלֶיהָ נֶסֶךְ וַיִּצֹק עָלֶיהָ שָׁמֶן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyatsev Ya'akov matsevah bammakom asher dibber itto matsevet even vayyassekh aleha nesekh vayyitzoq aleha shamen.
verses.study_note: Jacob erects a 'matsevah' — a standing stone/pillar, the same act he performed at his first Bethel encounter (28:18). This is a deliberate parallel and a deliberate escalation: at Bethel 1 he poured only oil; at Bethel 2 he pours both a 'drink offering' (nesekh) and oil. The nesekh (libation) is wine or liquid poured out before God — it will later be formally incorporated into the Mosaic sacrificial system (Exodus 29:40; Numbers 28:7). Jacob is practicing proto-Mosaic worship, led by the Spirit before the Law codified it. The matsevah is a monument of covenant memory — 'here God spoke.' The stone that Jacob anointed becomes the seed of the later Temple, where God's name would dwell permanently.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob called the name of the place where God spake with him, Bethel.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא יַעֲקֹב אֶת־שֵׁם הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר אִתּוֹ שָׁם אֱלֹהִים בֵּית אֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra Ya'akov et-shem hammakom asher dibber itto sham Elohim Beit El.
verses.study_note: Jacob formally (re)names the place Bethel — 'House of God.' This is the second naming of Bethel by Jacob (first in 28:19). The repetition is deliberate: the name is now doubly confirmed, both by Jacob's first encounter and his second. Bethel will become one of the central sacred sites of Israel's history — the place where the ark rested (Judges 20:26-27), where Jeroboam installed golden calves as a counterfeit worship center (1 Kings 12:28-33), and which the prophets later called 'Beth-aven' (House of Nothing — Hosea 4:15) when idolatry corrupted it. The Bethel cycle of Genesis thus plants a seed of sacred geography that will be harvested, corrupted, lamented, and ultimately transcended in the New Jerusalem.

verses.kjv_text: And they journeyed from Bethel; and there was but a little way to come to Ephrath: and Rachel travailed, and she had hard labour.
verses.original_text: וַיִּסְעוּ מִבֵּית אֵל וַיְהִי עוֹד כִּבְרַת־הָאָרֶץ לָבוֹא אֶפְרָתָה וַתֵּלֶד רָחֵל וַתְּקַשׁ בְּלִדְתָּהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiss'u miBeit El vayehi od kibrat-ha'aretz lavo Efratah vatteled Rachel vatteqash belidetah.
verses.study_note: The journey from Bethel toward Hebron passes through the territory that will become Ephrath/Bethlehem. The phrase 'but a little way' (kibrat-ha'aretz — literally 'a piece of ground,' an indeterminate short distance) suggests Rachel goes into labor unexpectedly close to their destination. The verb 'vatteqash' (hard labour) is from 'qashah' — to be hard, difficult, cruel. Rachel's labor is described as life-threateningly difficult. Rachel had once cried to Jacob 'Give me children, or else I die' (30:1); God had 'remembered' Rachel and opened her womb (30:22). She will receive the son she could not survive receiving. The geographical detail — 'near Ephrath' — is of immense typological significance: this is Bethlehem, the eventual birthplace of David and of Jesus (Micah 5:2; Matthew 2:1). The ground watered by Rachel's death-labor will one day witness the birth of the Shepherd-King.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when she was in hard labour, that the midwife said unto her, Fear not; thou shalt have this son also.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בְהַקְשֹׁתָהּ בְּלִדְתָּהּ וַתֹּאמֶר לָהּ הַמְיַלֶּדֶת אַל־תִּירְאִי כִּי גַם־זֶה לָךְ בֵּן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi behaqshotah belidetah vattomer lah hameyalledet al-tiri ki gam-zeh lakh ven.
verses.study_note: The midwife's words of comfort — 'Fear not' (al-tiri) — are the same divine words of reassurance spoken throughout Genesis (15:1 to Abraham; 21:17 to Hagar; 26:24 to Isaac). Here they come from a human servant, but they carry the same covenantal resonance: 'thou shalt have this son also' (gam-zeh lakh ben). 'Also' (gam) — this son is in addition to Joseph. The midwife knows Rachel's history: she is not merely a birth attendant but a witness to the continuation of the covenant family. 'Fear not; thou shalt have a son' is the language of divine announcement — the midwife becomes, unknowingly, a messenger of the covenant promise in its most immediate, bloodied form.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, as her soul was in departing, (for she died) that she called his name Benoni: but his father called him Benjamin.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בְּצֵאת נַפְשָׁהּ כִּי מֵתָה וַתִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ בֶּן־אוֹנִי וְאָבִיו קָרָא לוֹ בִנְיָמִין׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi betzetze nafshah ki metah vattikra shemo Ben-Oni ve'aviv kara lo Binyamin.
verses.study_note: One of the most poignant verses in Genesis. 'Her soul was in departing' (betzetze nafshah — as her nefesh was going out). The word 'nefesh' (soul/life-breath) that God breathed into Adam (2:7) is now 'going out' from Rachel. Her dying name for the child is 'Ben-Oni' — 'Son of my sorrow/pain/affliction' (from 'on' — sorrow, or 'aven' — trouble). Jacob immediately renames him 'Benjamin' — 'Son of my right hand' (from 'yamin' — right hand, the hand of strength, honor, and blessing). The renaming is an act of fatherly love and theological interpretation: what Rachel in her dying agony named 'Sorrow,' Jacob names 'Honor' — transforming grief into dignity. The right hand (yamin) is the hand of covenantal oath, royal honor, and divine favor throughout Scripture (Psalm 110:1; Matthew 26:64).
verses.doctrinal_note: Jacob's renaming of Benoni to Benjamin is a type of divine redemption — God takes what sorrow names and grace renames. What death calls 'son of my sorrow' God calls 'son of my right hand.' This is the pattern of the Gospel: Christ bore our sorrow (Isaiah 53:4) and is now seated 'at the right hand of God' (Hebrews 10:12), making believers 'sons of the right hand' through union with Him. Rachel's death in giving life to her son, near Bethlehem, on the road to Jerusalem, carries unmistakable typological resonance with the Passion narrative.

verses.kjv_text: And Rachel died, and was buried in the way to Ephrath, which is Bethlehem.
verses.original_text: וַתָּמָת רָחֵל וַתִּקָּבֵר בְּדֶרֶךְ אֶפְרָתָה הִוא בֵּית לָחֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vattamat Rachel vattikkaver bederekh Efratah hi Beit-Lachem.
verses.study_note: Rachel dies and is buried 'in the way to Ephrath, which is Bethlehem' — the narrator provides the double identification for clarity. This is the only matriarch buried outside of the cave of Machpelah (where Sarah, Rebekah, Leah, Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob would be buried — Gen 49:29-32; 50:13). Rachel's roadside grave near Bethlehem becomes one of the most emotionally charged sites in Israel's sacred geography. Jeremiah 31:15 pictures Rachel weeping from her tomb for her children carried into exile — a passage Matthew 2:17-18 applies to Herod's slaughter of the innocents in Bethlehem. The grave of Rachel near Bethlehem and the birth of Christ in Bethlehem are the two Bethlehem events that define the prophetic corridor between Genesis and Matthew.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob set a pillar upon her grave: that is the pillar of Rachel’s grave unto this day.
verses.original_text: וַיַּצֵּב יַעֲקֹב מַצֵּבָה עַל־קְבֻרָתָהּ הִוא מַצֶּבֶת קְבֻרַת־רָחֵל עַד הַיּוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyatsev Ya'akov matsevah al-kevuratah hi matsevet kevurat-Rachel ad hayyom.
verses.study_note: Jacob erects a matsevah — a standing stone — over Rachel's grave. This is the third 'pillar' Jacob erects in the narrative (Bethel 1: 28:18; Bethel 2: 35:14; Rachel's grave: 35:20). The pillar at Bethel marked where God spoke; the pillar at Rachel's grave marks where love lost its companion. Jacob's standing stones map his spiritual and emotional journey. The narrator adds 'unto this day' (ad hayyom) — by the time Genesis was composed, Rachel's pillar was a well-known landmark, implying centuries of continued recognition. The grave would later be identified near Ramah (1 Samuel 10:2), consistent with Jeremiah 31:15's location. The matsevah of grief, like the matsevah of worship, stands as testimony — some encounters and some losses mark the landscape permanently.

verses.kjv_text: And Israel journeyed, and spread his tent beyond the tower of Edar.
verses.original_text: וַיִּסַּע יִשְׂרָאֵל וַיֵּט אָהֳלֹה מֵהָלְאָה לְמִגְדַּל־עֵדֶר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyissa Yisrael vayyett oholoh mehe'alah lemigdal-Eder.
verses.study_note: The narrator now uses 'Israel' — Jacob's new name — for the first time in ordinary narrative prose (not divine speech). Jacob is becoming Israel in daily life. 'The tower of Edar' (Migdal-Eder — 'Tower of the Flock') is a watchtower near Bethlehem used by shepherds to guard their flocks. Micah 4:8 references 'Migdal-Eder' as the place from which the former dominion of Israel would return to Jerusalem's daughter — a Messianic prophecy. That Jacob pitches his tent at the Shepherd's Watchtower, immediately after the birth of Benjamin in Bethlehem and before the sin of Reuben, gives the location an ominous and typologically rich resonance: this is shepherd country, the land where the Great Shepherd will one day be born.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when Israel dwelt in that land, that Reuben went and lay with Bilhah his father’s concubine: and Israel heard it. Now the sons of Jacob were twelve:
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בִּשְׁכֹּן יִשְׂרָאֵל בָּאָרֶץ הַהִוא וַיֵּלֶךְ רְאוּבֵן וַיִּשְׁכַּב אֶת־בִּלְהָה פִּילֶגֶשׁ אָבִיו וַיִּשְׁמַע יִשְׂרָאֵל׃ וַיִּהְיוּ בְנֵי־יַעֲקֹב שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi bishkon Yisrael ba'aretz hahi vayyeylekh Re'uven vayyishkav et-Bilhah pilegesh aviv vayyishma Yisrael. Vayyihyu venei-Ya'akov sheneim asar.
verses.study_note: The narrator records Reuben's sin with shocking brevity — five words in Hebrew — before pivoting immediately to the enumeration of the twelve sons. The act is the gravest possible violation of the father's household honor. Bilhah was Rachel's servant, Rachel's sons' secondary mother, and Jacob's concubine. Reuben, as firstborn, may have sought to assert dominance in the hierarchy after Rachel's death — a political grab disguised as sexual immorality. That 'Israel heard it' (vayyishma Yisrael) implies Jacob knew immediately and said nothing recorded here — his silence being as significant as his eventual pronouncement in Genesis 49:3-4: 'Reuben, thou art my firstborn... unstable as water, thou shalt not excel; because thou wentest up to thy father's bed.' The firstborn's blessing and double-portion would pass from Reuben to Joseph's sons (1 Chronicles 5:1). The chapter then turns immediately to the complete list of twelve — as if to say: Reuben's sin does not undo the covenant; twelve sons still stand.
verses.doctrinal_note: Reuben's sin forfeits his birthright — the clearest Old Testament precedent for the principle that God's covenant structure is not overthrown by human sin but that personal consequences for sin remain. The birthright passed to Joseph (1 Chronicles 5:1), the kingship to Judah, and the priesthood to Levi. No sin is without consequence, yet the covenant number — twelve sons, twelve tribes — remains intact. God works around human failure without erasing it.

verses.kjv_text: The sons of Leah; Reuben, Jacob’s firstborn, and Simeon, and Levi, and Judah, and Issachar, and Zebulun:
verses.original_text: בְּנֵי לֵאָה רְאוּבֵן בְּכוֹר יַעֲקֹב וְשִׁמְעוֹן וְלֵוִי וִיהוּדָה וְיִשָּׂשכָר וּזְבוּלֻן׃
verses.transliteration: Benei Le'ah Re'uven bekhor Ya'akov veShim'on veLevi viYhudah veYissachar uZevulun.
verses.study_note: The six sons of Leah are listed first — Leah being the first and primary wife by legal status, though not by Jacob's affection. Reuben is listed first as 'bekhor' (firstborn) despite his sin in v.22 — the title remains even after the birthright blessing is reallocated. From Leah's sons: Reuben (tribe lost, becomes minor), Simeon (scattered in Israel — Gen 49:5-7), Levi (scattered but redeemed into priesthood — Gen 49:7; Deut 10:8), Judah (receives the scepter and Messianic line — Gen 49:10), Issachar (strong tribe of Zebulun region), and Zebulun. This is the group from whom the royal (Judah) and priestly (Levi) lines emerge — all from Leah, the 'unloved' wife. God consistently works through the overlooked and undervalued.

verses.kjv_text: The sons of Rachel; Joseph, and Benjamin:
verses.original_text: בְּנֵי רָחֵל יוֹסֵף וּבִנְיָמִין׃
verses.transliteration: Benei Rachel Yosef uVinyamin.
verses.study_note: Rachel's two sons are listed together — Joseph and Benjamin. This is the only listing of just two sons, and it carries immense weight.
