Joseph (Yosef — 'may He add' — the name expressing Rachel's hope in 30:24) and Benjamin ('Son of the right hand' — renamed by Jacob from 'Benoni' in v.18) are the two sons of Jacob's deepest love. Joseph will become the deliverer of the family in Egypt; Benjamin will be the youngest, most beloved, and most protected — the one over whom Jacob almost breaks when asked to send him to Egypt (42:36-38). The two 'Rachel tribes' — Ephraim and Manasseh (from Joseph) and Benjamin — remain closely allied throughout Israel's history, forming the nucleus of the Northern Kingdom (Ephraim) and the tribe that will produce Israel's first king (Saul, a Benjaminite) and the apostle Paul.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Bilhah, Rachel’s handmaid; Dan, and Naphtali:
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי בִלְהָה שִׁפְחַת רָחֵל דָּן וְנַפְתָּלִי׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Vilhah shifchat Rachel Dan veNaftali.
verses.study_note: Dan ('he judged' — Rachel's naming in 30:6, 'God hath judged me') and Naphtali ('my wrestling' — 30:8) are the sons of Bilhah, Rachel's handmaid. Their tribal identities: Dan will receive the northernmost territory and later become infamous for the idolatrous shrine established there (Judges 18:30-31; 1 Kings 12:29); Naphtali occupies the beautiful region of Galilee — 'Naphtali, by the way of the sea, beyond Jordan, Galilee of the nations' (Isaiah 9:1), the region where Jesus would begin His ministry (Matthew 4:12-16).

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Zilpah, Leah’s handmaid; Gad, and Asher: these are the sons of Jacob, which were born to him in Padanaram.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי זִלְפָּה שִׁפְחַת לֵאָה גָּד וְאָשֵׁר אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי יַעֲקֹב אֲשֶׁר יֻלַּד־לוֹ בְּפַדַּן אֲרָם׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Zilpah shifchat Le'ah Gad ve'Asher elleh venei Ya'akov asher yullad-lo bePaddan Aram.
verses.study_note: Gad ('good fortune' — Leah's naming in 30:11) and Asher ('happy' — 30:13) complete the twelve-tribe roster. Gad will settle east of the Jordan, a warrior tribe (Deut 33:20-21); Asher will receive the fertile coastal territory of Galilee and Phoenicia (Deut 33:24-25). The closing clause 'born to him in Padan-aram' is technically imprecise — Benjamin was born in Canaan (v.16-18) — but the phrase refers to the eleven born during Jacob's stay with Laban. Benjamin is implicitly separated as the one born in the Promised Land itself. This makes Benjamin the only patriarch born on covenant soil — a subtle typological distinction.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob came unto Isaac his father unto Mamre, unto the city of Arbah, which is Hebron, where Abraham and Isaac sojourned.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא יַעֲקֹב אֶל־יִצְחָק אָבִיו מַמְרֵאה קִרְיַת הָאַרְבַּע הִוא חֶבְרוֹן אֲשֶׁר גָּר אַבְרָהָם וְיִצְחָק׃
verses.transliteration: Vayavo Ya'akov el-Yitzhak aviv Mamreh Kiryat ha-Arba hi Hevron asher gar Avraham veYitzhak.
verses.study_note: Jacob arrives at Mamre/Hebron — the covenant heartland. The city has three names: Mamre (the Amorite ally of Abraham — 13:18), Kiryat ha-Arba ('City of the Four' — perhaps named for four Anakite giants — Josh 14:15), and Hebron (the name that becomes permanent). Abraham had dug the well there; purchased the cave of Machpelah there; it is where Sarah was buried (23:19), and where Abraham had first built an altar to YHWH (13:18). Jacob's arrival at Hebron to be with his father Isaac closes a 20-year exile. The prodigal has come home — though Rebekah, who sent him away, is not mentioned here (she has died; presumably in Hebron and buried at Machpelah, cf. 49:31). Jacob will remain with Isaac for the final years of the patriarch's life.

verses.kjv_text: And the days of Isaac were an hundred and fourscore years.
verses.original_text: וַיִּהְיוּ יְמֵי יִצְחָק מְאַת שָׁנָה וּשְׁמֹנִים שָׁנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyihyu yemei Yitzhak me'at shanah ushmonim shanah.
verses.study_note: Isaac lived 180 years — 5 more than Abraham (175 — 25:7) and 3 fewer than Jacob (147 — 47:28). The rounding to 180 carries the gematric sense of completion. Isaac is the most passive of the three great patriarchs in the narrative — the suffering servant figure, the one who was laid on the altar (ch.22), who was deceived by his wife (ch.26), who blessed the wrong son (ch.27), and who lived long enough to see the covenant continue without fully understanding how. His long life (180 years) witnesses the entirety of Jacob's story and the births of all twelve of his grandsons — the twelve who will become Israel.

verses.kjv_text: And Isaac gave up the ghost, and died, and was gathered unto his people, being old and full of days: and his sons Esau and Jacob buried him.
verses.original_text: וַיִּגְוַע יִצְחָק וַיָּמָת וַיֵּאָסֶף אֶל־עַמָּיו זָקֵן וּשְׂבַע יָמִים וַיִּקְבְּרוּ אֹתוֹ עֵשָׂו וְיַעֲקֹב בָּנָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyigva Yitzhak vayyamat vayyei'asef el-ammav zaken useva yamim vayyiqberu oto Esav veYa'akov banav.
verses.study_note: Isaac's death is described with the full patriarchal death formula used for Abraham (25:8) and later Jacob (49:33): 'gave up the ghost' (vayyigva — expired, breathed his last), 'died' (vayyamat), and was 'gathered unto his people' (vayyei'asef el-ammav — a phrase suggesting the soul's reunion with those who died before him, an implicit affirmation of personal continuity after death). He died 'old and full of days' (zaken useva yamim) — the same phrase used of Abraham (25:8) and Job (42:17), denoting a life lived to its intended fullness. Most remarkably: 'his sons Esau and Jacob buried him' — the same pairing that buried Abraham at Machpelah (Ishmael and Isaac — 25:9). The estranged sons reunited at the grave. At the Machpelah cave, Esau and Jacob stand side by side as brothers. Death achieves the reconciliation that life could not fully sustain.
verses.doctrinal_note: The phrase 'gathered unto his people' (vayyei'asef el-ammav) is one of the earliest scriptural affirmations of personal existence after death — not merely the body returning to earth but the person being gathered to a community of those who have gone before. The Patriarchs lived and died in the confidence of this reunion (Hebrews 11:13-16). Jesus uses the Patriarchs' ongoing relationship with God to prove the resurrection: 'God is not the God of the dead, but of the living' (Matthew 22:32).

meta.overview: Genesis 35 is one of the great turning-point chapters of the patriarchal narrative. It gathers together the loose threads of Jacob's life and brings them to a series of resolutions — spiritual, typological, and historical. God commands Jacob to return to Bethel, the site of his first encounter with the divine (ch.28), and Jacob responds with comprehensive household purification: foreign gods are buried, earrings are removed, garments are changed, and the family sets its face toward God. God ratifies the covenant at Bethel — renaming Jacob 'Israel' a second and definitive time (the first being 32:28) and formally repeating the Abrahamic promises of land, fruitfulness, and a royal lineage. Three deaths frame the chapter: Deborah (Rebekah's nurse, v.8), Rachel (in childbirth, vv.16-20), and Isaac (v.29). The birth of Benjamin closes the roll of the twelve tribal patriarchs, and Reuben's sin with Bilhah (v.22) introduces the moral failure that will cost him his birthright. The chapter is simultaneously a biography of transformation and a theological declaration: God is faithful to His covenant despite human failure, delay, and idolatry.
meta.theme: Covenant Renewal, Purification & the Faithfulness of God
meta.keyWordMeaning: Go up/Arise and go — the recurring verb of ascent to Bethel, connoting spiritual pilgrimage, return, and the upward movement toward God's presence. Jacob is called to arise and go up three times in his life (28:10; 31:3; 35:1).
outline: Return to Bethel Commanded (vv.1-4): God's call, Jacob's response — purification of the household
Bethel Reached — God's Protection En Route (v.5): Divine terror upon surrounding cities
The Altar Built and Deborah Buried (vv.6-8): Arrival, worship, and the death of the nurse
God Appears — Covenant Confirmed (vv.9-13): The Abrahamic promises formally reaffirmed
The Pillar Erected (vv.14-15): Bethel named, sacred stone anointed
Rachel Dies — Benjamin Born (vv.16-20): The last of the twelve patriarchs and the sorrow of his mother
Reuben's Sin (vv.21-22a): The forfeiture of the firstborn's inheritance
The Twelve Sons Enumerated (vv.22b-26): The complete roll of Israel's tribal fathers
Jacob Returns to Isaac — Isaac Dies (vv.27-29): The passing of the third patriarch

verses.kjv_text: Now these are the generations of Esau, who is Edom.
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה תֹּלְדֹת עֵשָׂו הוּא אֱדוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh toledot Esav hu Edom.
verses.study_note: The sixth of thirteen 'toledot' (generations/family history) formulas that structure the entire book of Genesis. This opening sentence identifies Esau and Edom as the same person — an equation the narrator will repeat three more times in this chapter (vv.8, 19, 43), ensuring no ambiguity. The double identification ('Esau, who is Edom') closes the loop on the earlier red-stew episode that gave him the nickname (Gen 25:30). The name 'Edom' (red) defines his identity by his moment of profane compromise. The toledot formula signals that what follows is a complete, honorable account — God does not erase Esau but records his legacy with full dignity before the narrative passes to Jacob.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's election of Jacob over Esau (Rom 9:10-13) is sovereign, unconditional, and not based on merit — but it does not mean Esau is destroyed or dishonoured. He receives land, wealth, and national identity. Election is unto covenant responsibility, not merely material privilege. The recording of Esau's toledot alongside Jacob's shows that God's purposes encompass the 'rejected' as well as the 'chosen.'

verses.kjv_text: Esau took his wives of the daughters of Canaan; Adah the daughter of Elon the Hittite, and Aholibamah the daughter of Anah the daughter of Zibeon the Hivite;
verses.original_text: עֵשָׂו לָקַח אֶת נָשָׁיו מִבְּנוֹת כְּנָעַן אֶת עָדָה בַּת אֵילוֹן הַחִתִּי וְאֶת אָהֳלִיבָמָה בַּת עֲנָה בַּת צִבְעוֹן הַחִוִּי׃
verses.transliteration: Esav lakach et nashav mibnot Kena'an et Adah bat Eilon haChitti ve'et Oholivamah bat Anah bat Tziv'on haChivvi.
verses.study_note: Esau's Canaanite wives are listed with full genealogical detail. This is the third catalogue of Esau's wives in Genesis (cf. 26:34, 28:9) — the variation in names between accounts has generated much scholarly discussion, likely reflecting nickname/alternate-name practices common in the ancient Near East. 'Adah' means ornament/beauty; 'Aholibamah' means 'tent of the high place' — a name that may indicate Canaanite cultic associations. Isaac and Rebekah had specifically sent Jacob to Paddan-Aram to avoid Canaanite wives (28:1-2). Esau's persistent choice of Canaanite women reflects his ongoing disregard for covenant boundaries, a pattern that 'grieved' his parents (26:35).

verses.kjv_text: And Bashemath Ishmael’s daughter, sister of Nebajoth.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת בָּשְׂמַת בַּת יִשְׁמָעֵאל אֲחוֹת נְבָיֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et Bashemat bat Yishma'el achot Nevayot.
verses.study_note: Bashemath ('fragrant/spice') is the daughter of Ishmael — making her a granddaughter of Abraham through the non-covenant line. Esau had taken her specifically after observing that Canaanite wives displeased his parents and that Jacob had gone to Paddan-Aram (28:8-9). This third wife was a strategic move to gain parental approval, but she too falls outside the covenant lineage. Nebajoth, her brother, becomes the ancestor of the Nabataeans — an Arabian people whose territory includes Petra. The Ishmaelite connection links Edom distantly to the Abrahamic family — both Edom and Arabia are 'half-family' nations that orbit the covenant without entering it.

verses.kjv_text: And Adah bare to Esau Eliphaz; and Bashemath bare Reuel;
verses.original_text: וַתֵּלֶד עָדָה לְעֵשָׂו אֶת אֱלִיפַז וּבָשְׂמַת יָלְדָה אֶת רְעוּאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vateled Adah le'Esav et Eliphaz uBashemat yaledah et Re'u'el.
verses.study_note: Two of Esau's sons are named here. Eliphaz ('my God is gold/pure') becomes the father of Teman and the ancestor of Amalek (v.12) — names resonant across the Old Testament. Eliphaz is also the name of Job's first and most prominent friend (Job 2:11) — likely a Temanite, suggesting the wisdom tradition of Edom (Jer 49:7: 'Is wisdom no more in Teman?'). Reuel ('friend of God') becomes significant as the name of Moses' father-in-law (Exod 2:18), though the Midianite Reuel is a different person, the name itself reflects the theological character Edom's descendants occasionally displayed.

verses.kjv_text: And Aholibamah bare Jeush, and Jaalam, and Korah: these are the sons of Esau, which were born unto him in the land of Canaan.
verses.original_text: וְאָהֳלִיבָמָה יָלְדָה אֶת יְעוּשׁ וְאֶת יַעְלָם וְאֶת קֹרַח אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי עֵשָׂו אֲשֶׁר יֻלְּדוּ לוֹ בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'Oholivamah yaledah et Ye'ush ve'et Ya'lam ve'et Korach elleh venei Esav asher yulledu lo be'eretz Kena'an.
verses.study_note: Aholibamah bears three sons: Jeush ('he will assemble/hurry'), Jaalam ('he will be hidden/young man'), and Korah ('baldness/ice'). The name Korah will later appear prominently in the rebellion against Moses and Aaron (Numbers 16). Whether Edom's Korah had any connection to Israel's Korah is unknown, but the name's associations in Israelite memory would have been negative. All five of Esau's sons were born in Canaan — the same land God promised to Jacob's descendants. Yet unlike Abraham and Isaac, who sojourned in the land as pilgrims, Esau simply relocates when the land cannot support both him and Jacob.

verses.kjv_text: And Esau took his wives, and his sons, and his daughters, and all the persons of his house, and his cattle, and all his beasts, and all his substance, which he had got in the land of Canaan; and went into the country from the face of his brother Jacob.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח עֵשָׂו אֶת נָשָׁיו וְאֶת בָּנָיו וְאֶת בְּנֹתָיו וְאֶת כָּל נַפְשׁוֹת בֵּיתוֹ וְאֶת מִקְנֵהוּ וְאֶת כָּל בְּהֶמְתּוֹ וְאֵת כָּל קִנְיָנוֹ אֲשֶׁר רָכַשׁ בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן וַיֵּלֶךְ אֶל אֶרֶץ מִפְּנֵי יַעֲקֹב אָחִיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikkach Esav et nashav ve'et banav ve'et benotav ve'et kol nafshot beito ve'et mikneihu ve'et kol behemto ve'et kol kinyano asher rakhash be'eretz Kena'an vayelekh el eretz mippenei Ya'akov achiv.
verses.study_note: Esau voluntarily leaves Canaan with his entire household — wives, sons, daughters, servants, livestock, and accumulated wealth. The phrase 'from the face of his brother Jacob' (mippenei Ya'akov) echoes Cain fleeing 'from the face of the LORD' (Gen 4:16) and suggests the pressure of coexistence, though Esau's departure is peaceful. The narrative deliberately parallels the separation of Abram and Lot (Gen 13:6-11): both separations occur because 'the land could not bear them' (v.7, cf. 13:6), both involve a choice of territory, but Abram stayed in Canaan by faith while Lot chose Sodom by sight. Esau, like Lot, chooses by sight — and unlike Jacob, he leaves the promised land entirely.

verses.kjv_text: For their riches were more than that they might dwell together; and the land wherein they were strangers could not bear them because of their cattle.
verses.original_text: כִּי הָיָה רְכוּשָׁם רָב מִשֶּׁבֶת יַחְדָּו וְלֹא יָכְלָה אֶרֶץ מְגוּרֵיהֶם לָשֵׂאת אֹתָם מִפְּנֵי מִקְנֵיהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Ki hayah rekusham rav mishevet yachdav velo yakhelah eretz megureichem laset otam mippenei mikneihem.
verses.study_note: This verse is nearly verbatim from Genesis 13:6 ('And the land was not able to bear them, that they might dwell together'). The parallel is intentional, linking Esau/Jacob to Abraham/Lot. Both separations arise from prosperity — God's blessing producing abundance that necessitates division. But the spiritual parallel diverges: Abraham separated to stay in Canaan in faith; Lot chose the fertile plain but moved toward Sodom. Similarly, Jacob stays in Canaan while Esau moves to Seir. The 'land of their sojourning' (eretz megureichem) uses the word for a stranger's temporary residence — emphasizing that both brothers were pilgrims in Canaan, not yet possessors of the promise.

verses.kjv_text: Thus dwelt Esau in mount Seir: Esau is Edom.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּשֶׁב עֵשָׂו בְּהַר שֵׂעִיר עֵשָׂו הוּא אֱדוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyeshev Esav behar Se'ir Esav hu Edom.
verses.study_note: The equation 'Esau is Edom' appears again, sealing the identification. 'Mount Seir' (har Se'ir — the hairy/shaggy mountain) becomes Edom's permanent homeland, a rugged terrain southeast of the Dead Sea. Deuteronomy 2:5 records that God had given Seir to Esau as his possession, just as Canaan was given to Israel — a divine allocation of territory to both brothers. Seir was previously inhabited by Horites (vv.20-30) whom Esau gradually displaced, just as Israel would later displace Canaanites. Joshua 24:4 explicitly states: 'I gave unto Esau mount Seir, to possess it; but Jacob and his children went down into Egypt.' God is the giver of both inheritances.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's provision extends beyond the covenant community. Esau receives land, blessing, and national dignity — just as Ishmael received his own nation (Gen 17:20). The covenant with Abraham flows exclusively through Isaac and Jacob, but God's common grace sustains and blesses those outside the covenant line. This prefigures the NT distinction between the church (the Israel of God) and the nations, who also receive God's rain and fruitfulness (Matt 5:45, Acts 14:17).

verses.kjv_text: And these are the generations of Esau the father of the Edomites in mount Seir:
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת עֵשָׂו אֲבִי אֱדוֹם בְּהַר שֵׂעִיר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh toledot Esav avi Edom behar Se'ir.
verses.study_note: The second toledot of Esau in this chapter — a unique feature. Genesis nowhere else opens two consecutive toledot sections with the same subject. The first (v.1) introduces the family born in Canaan; the second (v.9) begins the more expansive genealogy of Edom as a settled nation in Seir. This double formula is deliberate: it gives Esau's account the same grandeur as the double account of other major figures. The title 'father of the Edomites' (avi Edom) is the highest honorific in genealogical literature — the founding patriarch of a nation. Esau achieves national fatherhood; but Jacob achieves covenant fatherhood.

verses.kjv_text: These are the names of Esau’s sons; Eliphaz the son of Adah the wife of Esau, Reuel the son of Bashemath the wife of Esau.
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה שְׁמוֹת בְּנֵי עֵשָׂו אֱלִיפַז בֶּן עָדָה אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו רְעוּאֵל בֶּן בָּשְׂמַת אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו׃
verses.transliteration: Elleh shemot benei Esav Eliphaz ben Adah eshet Esav Re'u'el ben Bashemat eshet Esav.
verses.study_note: The genealogy restates the sons of v.4, now formally tracking them as founders of tribal sub-groups. Eliphaz (son of Adah the Hittite) and Reuel (son of Bashemath the Ishmaelite) head the two main branches of Edom's family. Their sons will become the eleven dukes of Edom (vv.15-19). The careful maternal identification throughout the genealogy reflects the importance of matrilineal connections in tribal identity — knowing who your mother is establishes your clan rights.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Eliphaz were Teman, Omar, Zepho, and Gatam, and Kenaz.
verses.original_text: וַיִּהְיוּ בְּנֵי אֱלִיפַז תֵּימָן אוֹמָר צְפוֹ וְגַעְתָּם וּקְנַז׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyihyu venei Eliphaz Teman Omar Tzepho ve'Ga'tam uKenaz.
verses.study_note: Eliphaz's five sons become eponyms for Edomite clans and territories. Teman ('south/right hand') is the most prominent — Teman becomes a byword for Edomite wisdom (Jer 49:7: 'Is wisdom no more in Teman? has counsel perished from the prudent?'). The Temanites were renowned sages; Eliphaz 'the Temanite' in Job 2:11 likely descends from this Teman, and chapters of Job contain some of the most profound wisdom poetry in Scripture, ironically placed in the mouth of an Edomite. Kenaz becomes significant as an ancestor of Caleb the Kenizzite (Num 32:12) — a non-Israelite who attached himself to Judah and became a hero of the conquest.

verses.kjv_text: And Timna was concubine to Eliphaz Esau’s son; and she bare to Eliphaz Amalek: these were the sons of Adah Esau’s wife.
verses.original_text: וְתִמְנַע הָיְתָה פִילֶגֶשׁ לֶאֱלִיפַז בֶּן עֵשָׂו וַתֵּלֶד לֶאֱלִיפַז אֶת עֲמָלֵק אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי עָדָה אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו׃
verses.transliteration: VeTimna haytah filegesh le'Eliphaz ben Esav vateled le'Eliphaz et Amalek elleh venei Adah eshet Esav.
verses.study_note: A verse of enormous prophetic weight, delivered in a single subordinate clause. Timna — a Horite princess (v.22 names her as the sister of Lotan) — could not become Eliphaz's full wife but accepted the role of concubine. The Midrash (Sanhedrin 99b) interprets Timna's motivation as a desire to join the Abrahamic family; being refused, she attached herself to it through Eliphaz. The result is Amalek — Israel's most enduring and existential enemy. Amalek attacks Israel unprovoked at Rephidim (Exod 17:8-16), where God swears eternal war; they are the paradigm of anti-Israel hostility (Deut 25:17-19); Saul's failure to destroy Amalek completely costs him the kingdom (1 Sam 15); Haman the Agagite, Esther's nemesis, may be of Amalekite royal descent. The birth of Amalek from a union that was marginalized — a concubine, not a wife — prefigures the bitter, illegitimate character of Amalek's warfare against Israel.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Amalek narrative illustrates how the rejected outside the covenant can become instruments of persecution against the elect. This pattern recurs: Ishmael mocks Isaac, Esau threatens Jacob, Amalek attacks Israel at its weakest. God's response to Amalek (Exod 17:14-16, Deut 25:19) establishes a principle: those who oppose the covenant people and exploit their vulnerability come under divine judgment. In Revelation, the beast who wars against the saints represents this same principle in its eschatological form.

verses.kjv_text: And these are the sons of Reuel; Nahath, and Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah: these were the sons of Bashemath Esau’s wife.
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה בְּנֵי רְעוּאֵל נַחַת וָזֶרַח שַׁמָּה וּמִזָּה אֵלֶּה הָיוּ בְּנֵי בָשְׂמַת אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh venei Re'u'el Nachat vaZerach Shammah uMizzah elleh hayu venei Bashemat eshet Esav.
verses.study_note: Reuel's four sons become four more Edomite clans. Nahath ('rest/quiet'), Zerah ('rising/shining' — the same name as Judah's twin, Gen 38:30), Shammah ('desolation/astonishment'), and Mizzah ('fear/terror'). These become four of the eleven dukes of Edom (v.17). The name Zerah appears in both Judah's line and Edom's — a small detail noting that parallel names cross both lineages, the elect and the non-elect, without affecting God's purposes.

verses.kjv_text: And these were the sons of Aholibamah, the daughter of Anah the daughter of Zibeon, Esau’s wife: and she bare to Esau Jeush, and Jaalam, and Korah.
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה הָיוּ בְנֵי אָהֳלִיבָמָה בַּת עֲנָה בַּת צִבְעוֹן אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו וַתֵּלֶד לְעֵשָׂו אֶת יְעוּשׁ וְאֶת יַעְלָם וְאֶת קֹרַח׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh hayu venei Oholivamah bat Anah bat Tziv'on eshet Esav vateled le'Esav et Ye'ush ve'et Ya'lam ve'et Korach.
verses.study_note: Aholibamah's three sons are repeated from v.5. The careful identification of her lineage — daughter of Anah, son of Zibeon the Hivite — will become important in v.24 where Anah is identified as the discoverer of hot springs. Edom's genealogy intersects with the Horite population of Seir through this marriage: Aholibamah is already of Seir's indigenous stock, which explains how Esau integrates with and eventually absorbs the Horites rather than displacing them violently as Israel displaced Canaanites.

verses.kjv_text: These were dukes of the sons of Esau: the sons of Eliphaz the firstborn son of Esau; duke Teman, duke Omar, duke Zepho, duke Kenaz,
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי בְנֵי עֵשָׂו בְּנֵי אֱלִיפַז בְּכוֹר עֵשָׂו אַלּוּף תֵּימָן אַלּוּף אוֹמָר אַלּוּף צְפוֹ אַלּוּף קְנַז׃
verses.transliteration: Elleh allufei venei Esav benei Eliphaz bekhor Esav aluf Teman aluf Omar aluf Tzepho aluf Kenaz.
verses.study_note: The word 'dukes' (alluf — plural allufim) is a uniquely Edomite term appearing exclusively in this chapter in the Pentateuch. From the root 'eleph' (thousand/clan), it designates tribal chieftains — leaders of clan-units, roughly analogous to the Israelite 'princes' (nasi). The list that follows (11 dukes total) represents a settled tribal confederacy with defined territorial identities. This is sophisticated political organization — Edom has chieftaincy before Israel has judges or kings. The first group descends from Eliphaz, Esau's firstborn, ensuring primogeniture is honored in Edomite succession.

verses.kjv_text: Duke Korah, duke Gatam, and duke Amalek: these are the dukes that came of Eliphaz in the land of Edom; these were the sons of Adah.
verses.original_text: אַלּוּף קֹרַח אַלּוּף גַּעְתָּם אַלּוּף עֲמָלֵק אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי אֱלִיפַז בְּאֶרֶץ אֱדוֹם אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי עָדָה׃
verses.transliteration: Aluf Korach aluf Ga'tam aluf Amalek elleh allufei Eliphaz be'eretz Edom elleh venei Adah.
verses.study_note: Amalek is listed among the dukes of Edom — an Edomite chief alongside his half-brothers. Though his origin was through a concubine (v.12), he achieves the same chieftain status. The locator 'in the land of Edom' indicates these are not roving clans but settled territorial leaders with defined domains. Seven dukes descend from Eliphaz alone — reflecting the extraordinary fertility and political success of Esau's firstborn line.

verses.kjv_text: And these are the sons of Reuel Esau’s son; duke Nahath, duke Zerah, duke Shammah, duke Mizzah: these are the dukes that came of Reuel in the land of Edom; these are the sons of Bashemath Esau’s wife.
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה בְּנֵי רְעוּאֵל בֶּן עֵשָׂו אַלּוּף נַחַת אַלּוּף זֶרַח אַלּוּף שַׁמָּה אַלּוּף מִזָּה אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי רְעוּאֵל בְּאֶרֶץ אֱדוֹם אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי בָשְׂמַת אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh venei Re'u'el ben Esav aluf Nachat aluf Zerach aluf Shammah aluf Mizzah elleh allufei Re'u'el be'eretz Edom elleh benei Bashemat eshet Esav.
verses.study_note: Reuel's four sons become four dukes. The listing 'Reuel Esau's son' carefully identifies the second generation, maintaining the genealogical chain. Nahath later appears as an Edomite king (v.33, 'Jobab' is from Bozrah near Reuel's territory), and the Reuelite connection to the Midianites may explain why Moses' father-in-law bore the same name (Exod 2:18) — the name 'Reuel' (friend of God) was honored across the Semitic world outside Israel.

verses.kjv_text: And these are the sons of Aholibamah Esau’s wife; duke Jeush, duke Jaalam, duke Korah: these were the dukes that came of Aholibamah the daughter of Anah, Esau’s wife.
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה בְּנֵי אָהֳלִיבָמָה אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו אַלּוּף יְעוּשׁ אַלּוּף יַעְלָם אַלּוּף קֹרַח אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי אָהֳלִיבָמָה בַּת עֲנָה אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh venei Oholivamah eshet Esav aluf Ye'ush aluf Ya'lam aluf Korach elleh allufei Oholivamah bat Anah eshet Esav.
verses.study_note: Aholibamah's three sons complete the eleven dukes of Esau. The three mothers produced 5, 4, and 3 sons respectively — 12 grandsons if Amalek's line through Timna is counted separately. The parallel to Jacob's twelve sons is striking: Esau produces a comparable tribal confederacy, but without the covenant promises attached. Edom is a mirror-image of Israel — similarly organized, similarly numerous, but outside the covenant frame.

verses.kjv_text: These are the sons of Esau, who is Edom, and these are their dukes.
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה בְנֵי עֵשָׂו וְאֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵיהֶם הוּא אֱדוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Elleh venei Esav ve'elleh allufeihem hu Edom.
verses.study_note: A formal closing formula for the first genealogical section. The triple identification of Esau/Edom in this chapter (vv.1, 8, 19) functions as a legal affirmation of national identity — Edom is fully established, fully named, fully documented. The narrator closes the curtain on Esau's record with dignity before the long Joseph narrative (chapters 37-50) carries Israel into Egypt and toward the fulfillment of covenant promise. Esau/Edom has his chapter; now the story belongs to Jacob.

verses.kjv_text: These are the sons of Seir the Horite, who inhabited the land; Lotan, and Shobal, and Zibeon, and Anah,
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה בְנֵי שֵׂעִיר הַחֹרִי יֹשְׁבֵי הָאָרֶץ לוֹטָן וְשׁוֹבָל וְצִבְעוֹן וַעֲנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Elleh venei Se'ir haChori yoshvei ha'aretz Lotan veShoval veTziv'on va'Anah.
verses.study_note: The narrative pivots to the original inhabitants of Seir — the Horites (Chorites). 'Horites' (Chorim) may derive from 'chor' (cave), describing cave-dwellers in the rugged mountain terrain. Deuteronomy 2:12 explicitly records: 'The Horites also dwelt in Seir beforetime; but the children of Esau succeeded them when they had destroyed them from before them, and dwelt in their stead.' Seir the Horite eponymously named the mountain. The recording of the Horite genealogy serves three purposes: (1) it shows Esau absorbed an existing people, (2) it explains the Horite connections in Esau's family (Aholibamah, Timna), and (3) it demonstrates that God's allocation of Seir to Esau displaced an existing population — a 'mini-conquest' that prefigures Israel's conquest of Canaan.

verses.kjv_text: And Dishon, and Ezer, and Dishan: these are the dukes of the Horites, the children of Seir in the land of Edom.
verses.original_text: וְדִשׁוֹן וְאֵצֶר וְדִישָׁן אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי הַחֹרִי בְּנֵי שֵׂעִיר בְּאֶרֶץ אֱדוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: VeDishon ve'Etzer veDishan elleh allufei haChori benei Se'ir be'eretz Edom.
verses.study_note: Seven sons of Seir produce seven Horite clans. The Horites use the same title 'alluf' (duke/chief) as the Edomites — suggesting the same political vocabulary was shared across the region. Notably, the Horites are listed as 'dukes in the land of Edom' — the narrative already uses the future name of the territory for the pre-Edomite period, reflecting the perspective of the final author recording established historical geography. The seven Horite clans are gradually absorbed into Esau's eleven Edomite dukes through intermarriage (Timna, Aholibamah) and military displacement.

verses.kjv_text: And the children of Lotan were Hori and Hemam; and Lotan’s sister was Timna.
verses.original_text: וַיִּהְיוּ בְנֵי לוֹטָן חֹרִי וְהֵימָם וַאֲחוֹת לוֹטָן תִּמְנָע׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyihyu venei Lotan Chori veHeimam va'achot Lotan Timna.
verses.study_note: The crucial genealogical note: Timna was Lotan's sister — making her a Horite princess, daughter of Seir himself (v.20). She did not marry into Esau's family as a wife but only as a concubine to Eliphaz (v.12). The Midrash (Sanhedrin 99b) reads her story as a parable: Timna sought to convert and join the Abrahamic family; when rejected, she settled for concubinage — and the result was Amalek. Whether or not this traditional interpretation captures the history, the genealogical fact is stark: Israel's most relentless enemy was born from a union that the covenant community declined to fully embrace. The name 'Hori' (son of Lotan) is the same as the ethnic name 'Horite' — he is the eponymous ancestor of the people.

verses.kjv_text: And the children of Shobal were these; Alvan, and Manahath, and Ebal, Shepho, and Onam.
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה בְּנֵי שׁוֹבָל עַלְוָן וּמָנַחַת וְעֵיבָל שְׁפוֹ וְאוֹנָם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh venei Shoval Alvan uManachat ve'Eival Shepho ve'Onam.
verses.study_note: Shobal's five sons include Manahath — an important name, as 'Manahath' also appears in Judah's tribal territory (1 Chr 8:6) and as a Calebite family name. Ebal is notable as the name of the mountain of cursing in Deuteronomy 27:13, where half of Israel stood to pronounce curses for covenant violation. The coincidence of names between Edomite and Israelite lists hints at the interconnected Semitic world of the ancient Near East.

verses.kjv_text: And these are the children of Zibeon; both Ajah, and Anah: this was that Anah that found the mules in the wilderness, as he fed the asses of Zibeon his father.
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה בְנֵי צִבְעוֹן וְאַיָּה וַעֲנָה הוּא עֲנָה אֲשֶׁר מָצָא אֶת הַיֵּמִם בַּמִּדְבָּר בִּרְעֹתוֹ אֶת הַחֲמֹרִים לְצִבְעוֹן אָבִיו׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh venei Tziv'on ve'Ayyah va'Anah hu Anah asher matza et hayemim bamidbar bir'oto et hachamorim leTziv'on aviv.
verses.study_note: The genealogy suddenly pauses for a historical anecdote — one of the most debated details in all of Genesis. Anah, tending his father's donkeys in the wilderness, 'found the yemim' (KJV: 'mules'). The word 'yemim' is a hapax legomenon — appearing only here in all of Scripture. The ancient versions disagree: the Septuagint renders it 'warm springs' (implying hot springs or geothermal water), the Vulgate says 'water'; rabbinic tradition (see Maimonides) has 'wild mules' or hybrid animals. The most likely reading is that Anah discovered natural warm springs — a significant find in the arid wilderness of Seir, valuable for watering flocks. The detail humanizes the genealogy: even in a list of names, a man's notable discovery is preserved. This is Anah who becomes the grandfather of Aholibamah, Esau's wife (v.2).

verses.kjv_text: And the children of Anah were these; Dishon, and Aholibamah the daughter of Anah.
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה בְנֵי עֲנָה דִּשׁוֹן וְאָהֳלִיבָמָה בַּת עֲנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh venei Anah Dishon ve'Oholivamah bat Anah.
verses.study_note: Anah's children include Dishon (a son who becomes a chief, v.30) and Aholibamah — the very Aholibamah who married Esau (v.2, v.14). This single verse reveals the full genealogical connection: Esau's wife was Anah's daughter, Zibeon's granddaughter, Seir's great-granddaughter. Esau did not merely move into Seir's territory — he married into its royal family, ensuring a peaceful integration rather than a purely military conquest. This political marriage strategy allowed the Edomite nation to absorb the Horites while maintaining social stability.

verses.kjv_text: And these are the children of Dishon; Hemdan, and Eshban, and Ithran, and Cheran.
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה בְנֵי דִישׁוֹן חֶמְדָּן וְאֶשְׁבָּן וְיִתְרָן וּכְרָן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh venei Dishon Chemdan ve'Eshban veYitran uKhran.
verses.study_note: Dishon ('threshing') has four sons. The name Hemdan ('desirable, pleasant') appears as 'Amram' in 1 Chronicles 1:41 — a variant name or textual variant. Ithran ('excellence, advantage') is the same name as an Asherite (1 Chr 7:37), again illustrating the cross-tribal naming patterns of the ancient Near East. The Horite genealogy is recorded with the same care as Israel's — God maintains the records of all peoples.

verses.kjv_text: The children of Ezer are these; Bilhan, and Zaavan, and Akan.
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי אֵצֶר בִּלְהָן וְזַעֲוָן וַעֲקָן׃
verses.transliteration: Elleh venei Etzer Bilhan veZa'avan va'Akan.
verses.study_note: Ezer ('help/treasure') has three sons. Bilhan is also a Benjaminite name (1 Chr 7:10), and Akan ('twisted/troublesome') appears as 'Jaakan' in 1 Chronicles 1:42 — the Bene-Jaakan of Numbers 33:31, who have a wilderness encampment. These repeated name patterns across Israelite and Edomite/Horite genealogies are evidence of the shared cultural milieu of Transjordanian Semitic peoples.

verses.kjv_text: The children of Dishan are these; Uz, and Aran.
verses.original_text: בְּנֵי דִישָׁן עוּץ וַאֲרָן׃
verses.transliteration: Benei Dishan Utz va'Aran.
verses.study_note: Dishan ('threshing' — variant of Dishon) has two sons. Uz ('counsel/strength') is a significant name — the land of Uz is the setting of the book of Job (Job 1:1). Whether Job's Uz is connected to this Horite Uz is debated, but the geographical possibility is real: Job's setting in the east, with its Edomite and Arabian connections, fits the Seir-Edomite milieu. Lamentations 4:21 places Edom in 'the land of Uz,' providing the strongest evidence that Job's Uz overlaps with Edomite/Horite territory — making Job's story potentially set among or near Esau's descendants.

verses.kjv_text: These are the dukes that came of the Horites; duke Lotan, duke Shobal, duke Zibeon, duke Anah,
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי הַחֹרִי אַלּוּף לוֹטָן אַלּוּף שׁוֹבָל אַלּוּף צִבְעוֹן אַלּוּף עֲנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Elleh allufei haChori aluf Lotan aluf Shoval aluf Tziv'on aluf Anah.
verses.study_note: The seven Horite sons of Seir now become seven Horite dukes — their names recycled as chiefly titles. The same formula used for Esau's eleven dukes applies to Seir's seven, establishing the Horites as a parallel (if now superseded) political entity. Their listing with dignity after their genealogy shows that even displaced peoples receive an honorable record in God's accounting of history.

verses.kjv_text: Duke Dishon, duke Ezer, duke Dishan: these are the dukes that came of Hori, among their dukes in the land of Seir.
verses.original_text: אַלּוּף דִּשׁוֹן אַלּוּף אֵצֶר אַלּוּף דִּישָׁן אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי הַחֹרִי לְאַלֻּפֵיהֶם בְּאֶרֶץ שֵׂעִיר׃
verses.transliteration: Aluf Dishon aluf Etzer aluf Dishan elleh allufei haChori le'allufeihem be'eretz Se'ir.
verses.study_note: The Horite record closes: seven dukes 'according to their chiefs in the land of Seir.' The phrase 'according to their chiefs' (le-allufeihem) suggests an ordered hierarchy within the Horite confederacy. They are now history — displaced by Esau's descendants — but their record is preserved. The narrative has documented both the conquerors (Esau's eleven dukes) and the conquered (Seir's seven dukes), showing God's sovereign oversight of all nations and their histories, not only Israel's.

verses.kjv_text: And these are the kings that reigned in the land of Edom, before there reigned any king over the children of Israel.
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה הַמְּלָכִים אֲשֶׁר מָלְכוּ בְּאֶרֶץ אֱדוֹם לִפְנֵי מְלָךְ מֶלֶךְ לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh hammelakhim asher malekhu be'eretz Edom lifnei melokh melekh livnei Yisra'el.
verses.study_note: The most theologically charged single verse in Genesis 36. The narrator explicitly notes that Edom had a monarchy 'before' Israel had any king at all. This temporal comparison is startling: the rejected brother achieved organized royal government generations before the elected people. At the time of writing, God had promised Abraham kings in his line (Gen 17:6, 'kings shall come out of thee') and had repeated this to Jacob (Gen 35:11). The Israelites reading this in Moses' day had never had a king — they were a tribal confederacy under judges and elders. Yet Edom had already cycled through eight monarchs. The verse thus functions as both a historical record and a prophetic anticipation: Israel will have kings (see Deut 17:14-20), and the list of Edomite kings shows what that will look like. The Edomite monarchy appears to be elective rather than hereditary — no son succeeds his father in the list, suggesting clan-based selection similar to the Israelite judge period.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's promise of kings to Abraham and Jacob is not negated by Edom's prior monarchy — it is previewed and anticipated by it. The rejected line (Esau) models the form of institution (kingship) that the elected line (Jacob) will eventually adopt. This pattern of the 'outside' prefiguring the 'inside' appears throughout redemptive history: Melchizedek (a pre-Levitical priest) foreshadows Christ's priesthood; the Gentile magi worship the infant King before Jerusalem does; the church is grafted into Israel's olive tree. Election does not mean isolation — God's purposes are displayed even through those outside the covenant.

verses.kjv_text: And Bela the son of Beor reigned in Edom: and the name of his city was Dinhabah.
verses.original_text: וַיִּמְלֹךְ בֶּאֱדוֹם בֶּלַע בֶּן בְּעוֹר וְשֵׁם עִירוֹ דִּנְהָבָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyimlokh be'Edom Bela ben Be'or veshem iro Dinhabah.
verses.study_note: The first Edomite king: Bela son of Beor, reigning from Dinhabah. The name 'Bela' ('swallowing, destruction') and 'Beor' ('burning') share names with Balaam's father (Num 22:5 'Balaam the son of Beor') — an intriguing possible connection, though Balaam is Mesopotamian and clearly distinct. Dinhabah ('give thou judgment') is unidentified but was Bela's capital. Notably, this Edomite royal list gives each king's capital city — a detail of political geography suggesting these are records from actual Edomite administrative documents preserved in Israelite tradition.

verses.kjv_text: And Bela died, and Jobab the son of Zerah of Bozrah reigned in his stead.
verses.original_text: וַיָּמָת בָּלַע וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו יוֹבָב בֶּן זֶרַח מִבָּצְרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyamat Bela vayyimlokh tachtav Yovav ben Zerach miBozrah.
verses.study_note: The formula 'and he died, and X reigned in his stead' (vayyamat... vayyimlokh tachtav) repeats seven times. The non-hereditary succession is emphasized — 'in his stead' indicates replacement rather than dynastic continuation. Jobab son of Zerah 'of Bozrah' — Bozrah ('sheepfold/enclosure') is Edom's major city, later referenced extensively in Isaiah's oracles against Edom (Isa 34:6, 63:1) and Jeremiah (Jer 49:13, 22). Isaiah 63:1 asks 'Who is this that cometh from Edom, with dyed garments from Bozrah?' — a messianic passage describing Christ's judgment of Edom, making Bozrah a theologically weighted city in the prophetic imagination.

verses.kjv_text: And Jobab died, and Husham of the land of Temani reigned in his stead.
verses.original_text: וַיָּמָת יוֹבָב וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו חֻשָׁם מֵאֶרֶץ הַתֵּימָנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyamat Yovav vayyimlokh tachtav Chusham me'eretz haTeiymani.
verses.study_note: Husham ('haste') comes from 'the land of the Temanite' — confirming that Teman (v.11) had become a recognized district of Edom by this period. The Temanite connection gives Husham a geographical identity rather than a patronymic one — he is known by region, not father. This suggests that by the third Edomite king, tribal-regional identity was more significant than family lineage in determining succession — another indication of an elective or confederate rather than hereditary monarchy.

verses.kjv_text: And Husham died, and Hadad the son of Bedad, who smote Midian in the field of Moab, reigned in his stead: and the name of his city was Avith.
verses.original_text: וַיָּמָת חֻשָׁם וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו הֲדַד בֶּן בְּדַד הַמַּכֶּה אֶת מִדְיָן בִּשְׂדֵה מוֹאָב וְשֵׁם עִירוֹ עֲוִית׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyamat Chusham vayyimlokh tachtav Hadad ben Bedad hammakkeh et Midyan bisdeh Mo'av veshem iro Avit.
verses.study_note: The fourth Edomite king receives an extraordinary distinction: he is identified not only by name and father but by a military achievement — 'who smote Midian in the field of Moab.' This is the only Edomite king given a biographical honor. The victory over Midian in Moabite territory indicates Edom had expanded its power northward, engaging the Midianite and Moabite peoples. Hadad ('thunder/the thunderer') shares its name with the Syrian storm-deity Hadad/Baal — the name was common across the Semitic world as a royal/divine title. His city 'Avith' ('ruin/heap') may reflect later destruction of the original site.

verses.kjv_text: And Hadad died, and Samlah of Masrekah reigned in his stead.
verses.original_text: וַיָּמָת הֲדַד וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו שַׂמְלָה מִמַּשְׂרֵקָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyamat Hadad vayyimlokh tachtav Samlah miMasrekah.
verses.study_note: Samlah ('garment') comes from Masrekah ('vineyard of noble vines') — the root 'sorek' (choicest vines) appears in Isaiah 5:2 and Jeremiah 2:21 in metaphors for Israel as God's vine. The geographical detail of Masrekah indicates Samlah's regional origin. The absence of patronymic (no father named) for Samlah, Shaul (v.37), and Baal-hanan (v.38) may suggest unknown or untraceable lineage — in a hereditary system, fathers are always named; in an elective system, the region of origin matters more.

verses.kjv_text: And Samlah died, and Saul of Rehoboth by the river reigned in his stead.
verses.original_text: וַיָּמָת שַׂמְלָה וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו שָׁאוּל מֵרְחֹבוֹת הַנָּהָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyamat Samlah vayyimlokh tachtav Sha'ul meRechevot hannahar.
verses.study_note: Shaul ('asked for' — the same name as Israel's first king) reigns from Rehoboth by the river (hannahar — 'the river', a term typically meaning the Euphrates). This is geographically startling: if hannahar means the Euphrates, Edom's sixth king came from Mesopotamia — suggesting either an Edomite outpost far north, or a king recruited from a distant branch of the family. Some scholars suggest 'the river' here may be the Zered or another Transjordanian waterway. The name Shaul — shared with Israel's first king — may be a detail the original Israelite audience noted with irony: Edom had a 'Shaul' before Israel did.

verses.kjv_text: And Saul died, and Baalhanan the son of Achbor reigned in his stead.
verses.original_text: וַיָּמָת שָׁאוּל וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו בַּעַל חָנָן בֶּן עַכְבּוֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyamat Sha'ul vayyimlokh tachtav Ba'al Chanan ben Akhbor.
verses.study_note: The seventh king: Baalhanan son of Achbor — the name 'Baal-hanan' ('Baal is gracious') incorporates the name of the Canaanite storm-deity Baal, indicating that Baal veneration had penetrated Edomite royal culture. Achbor ('mouse') is the father's name — a humble animal-name common in the ancient world. The same name appears in Jeremiah 26:22 and 2 Kings 22:12 as Israelite names, showing the name's cross-cultural use. Baalhanan is the only king in this list to be named with a theophoric element referencing a Canaanite deity, suggesting some degree of religious syncretism in Edom by this period.

verses.kjv_text: And Baalhanan the son of Achbor died, and Hadar reigned in his stead: and the name of his city was Pau; and his wife’s name was Mehetabel, the daughter of Matred, the daughter of Mezahab.
verses.original_text: וַיָּמָת בַּעַל חָנָן בֶּן עַכְבּוֹר וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו הֲדַר וְשֵׁם עִירוֹ פָּעוּ וְשֵׁם אִשְׁתּוֹ מְהֵיטַבְאֵל בַּת מַטְרֵד בַּת מֵי זָהָב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyamat Ba'al Chanan ben Akhbor vayyimlokh tachtav Hadar veshem iro Pa'u veshem ishto Meheyitav'el bat Matred bat Mei Zahav.
verses.study_note: The eighth and final king, Hadar (KJV: Hadar; parallel text in 1 Chr 1:50 reads Hadad), is unique in the list: (1) no death formula follows — suggesting he may have still been reigning when the list was compiled; (2) his wife Mehetabel is named — the only royal wife named in the entire chapter; and (3) his city, Pau ('screaming/bleating'), and three generations of his wife's ancestry are recorded. 'Mehetabel' means 'God does good' — a name containing the element 'El' (God), suggesting the Edomite royalty also acknowledged the God El/Elohim. Her grandmother is 'Mezahab' ('waters of gold') — an evocative, poetic name. The extraordinary level of detail for the final king suggests authentic archival sourcing, not literary invention.

verses.kjv_text: And these are the names of the dukes that came of Esau, according to their families, after their places, by their names; duke Timnah, duke Alvah, duke Jetheth,
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה שְׁמוֹת אַלּוּפֵי עֵשָׂו לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לִמְקֹמֹתָם בִּשְׁמֹתָם אַלּוּף תִּמְנָע אַלּוּף עַלְוָה אַלּוּף יְתֵת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh shemot allufei Esav lemishpechotam limekomotam bishemotam aluf Timna aluf Alvah aluf Yetet.
verses.study_note: The final list in the chapter: thirteen territorial chiefs (different from the eleven clan dukes of vv.15-19). These chiefs are organized 'according to their families, after their places' — suggesting a later, more settled administrative geography. Timnah appears here as a chief's name — the same name as Eliphaz's concubine (v.12), now the name of a territorial district, likely her home region. This second list of chiefs may represent a reorganized Edomite administrative system after the eight kings — a return to chieftancy or a parallel territorial administration. The tribe-to-territory principle mirrors Israel's own organization under Joshua.

verses.kjv_text: Duke Aholibamah, duke Elah, duke Pinon,
verses.original_text: אַלּוּף אָהֳלִיבָמָה אַלּוּף אֵלָה אַלּוּף פִּינֹן׃
verses.transliteration: Aluf Oholivamah aluf Elah aluf Pinon.
verses.study_note: Aholibamah appears now as a territorial chieftaincy — the same name as Esau's wife (v.2). The region may have been named after her, or she may have ruled it. Elah ('terebinth oak') later appears as a place name — the valley of Elah where David defeated Goliath (1 Sam 17:2), a Shephelah location that may preserve this Edomite connection. Pinon ('darkness/uncertain') is identified with Punon — a site in Edomite territory where Israel camped during the wilderness wandering (Num 33:42-43), famous for its copper mines.

verses.kjv_text: Duke Kenaz, duke Teman, duke Mibzar,
verses.original_text: אַלּוּף קְנַז אַלּוּף תֵּימָן אַלּוּף מִבְצָר׃
verses.transliteration: Aluf Kenaz aluf Teman aluf Mivtzar.
verses.study_note: Kenaz and Teman recur from the earlier genealogy (v.11) — now as territorial districts rather than merely personal names. This confirms that the great-grandsons of Esau had given their names permanently to their territories. Teman appears here as a district alongside Kenaz — which explains why Eliphaz, the father of both Kenaz and Teman (v.11), had descendants spread across both territories. Mibzar ('fortification/fortress') likely refers to a fortified Edomite stronghold — possibly related to Bozrah (also from a root of 'enclosure').

verses.kjv_text: Duke Magdiel, duke Iram: these be the dukes of Edom, according to their habitations in the land of their possession: he is Esau the father of the Edomites.
verses.original_text: אַלּוּף מַגְדִּיאֵל אַלּוּף עִירָם אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי אֱדוֹם לְמֹשְׁבֹתָם בְּאֶרֶץ אֲחֻזָּתָם הוּא עֵשָׂו אֲבִי אֱדוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Aluf Magdi'el aluf Iram elleh allufei Edom lemoshvotam be'eretz achuzzatam hu Esav avi Edom.
verses.study_note: The final verse closes the entire chapter with the fourth and final equation: 'he is Esau the father of the Edomites' (hu Esav avi Edom). The closing formula 'in the land of their possession' (eretz achuzzatam) uses the same word as Israel's land grant — 'achuzzah' (possession, inheritance) — deliberately paralleling the language used for Israel's Canaan (Gen 17:8, 48:4). God gave Edom their 'achuzzah' in Seir just as He gave Israel their 'achuzzah' in Canaan. The last names: Magdiel ('God is my excellence/tower of God') contains the element 'El' (God), again noting Edomite theophoric naming. Iram ('city/their tent city') may preserve a memory of Edomite urban settlements. The chapter ends where it began: with Esau's identity fully established, honoured, and archived — so that the story of Jacob's descendants may proceed without ambiguity about who Edom is and what became of him.
verses.doctrinal_note: The fourfold repetition of 'Esau is Edom' (vv.1, 8, 19, 43) is a literary and theological closure. It means: this record is complete; Esau has received everything outside the covenant; the covenant story now belongs exclusively to Jacob. The closing of Esau's toledot launches the opening of Joseph's story (ch.37) — the pivotal narrative through which God preserves Israel. The two brothers' stories bookend: Esau achieves greatness by worldly measures (wealth, territory, kings, dukes); Jacob achieves greatness through suffering, servitude, and covenant — through which God blesses the world. This contrast is the permanent typological backdrop for the New Testament distinction between flesh and Spirit, between Ishmael and Isaac, between the first Adam and the Last Adam (Rom 9:6-13, Gal 4:21-31).

meta.overview: Genesis 36 is the great genealogical charter of Edom — Esau's descendants catalogued with royal gravity. It is one of the most theologically concentrated "lists" in Scripture. Three movements constitute the chapter: (1) Esau's immediate family and his separation from Canaan (vv.1-8); (2) His sons, grandsons, and the eleven chiefs of Edom (vv.9-19); (3) The Horite pre-existing inhabitants of Seir whom Esau displaced (vv.20-30); and (4) the astonishing list of eight Edomite kings who ruled before Israel had a single monarch (vv.31-39), closing with thirteen Edomite dukes according to territorial clans (vv.40-43). The chapter's placement is deliberate: it is the 'Esau scroll' that must be fully closed before the narrative turns irrevocably to Jacob and the twelve tribes of Israel. God's election of Jacob over Esau (Gen 25:23, Mal 1:2-3, Rom 9:13) does not mean Esau is cursed — he receives extraordinary material blessing, political organization, and national identity. But the line of covenant promise runs through Jacob alone. The Edomite king list (vv.31-39) is one of the most prophetically charged passages in Genesis: it anticipates the monarchy God promised to Abraham (17:6) and Jacob (35:11), while simultaneously recording that the 'rejected' line achieved kingship generations before Israel. This will be answered by Saul, David, and ultimately Christ. The chapter also preserves the birth of Amalek (v.12) — from Eliphaz and a Horite concubine — introducing Israel's most implacable enemy into the narrative. Throughout, the prose moves with quiet dignity, honoring Esau's legacy even as the spotlight passes permanently to his brother.
meta.theme: The Blessed Rejected — God's Providence in the Line of Esau
meta.keyWordMeaning: Generations/Family history — the structural formula of Genesis appearing here twice (vv.1,9), uniquely applied to Esau, signaling his complete and honoured account before the narrative pivots to Jacob.
outline: Esau's Wives and Sons in Canaan (vv.1-5): The family of the man who is Edom
Esau's Separation to Seir (vv.6-8): Departure from Jacob mirrors Lot and Abram
Esau's Sons and Grandsons (vv.9-14): The second toledot — Edom's family tree
The Eleven Chiefs of Esau (vv.15-19): Edom's tribal organization
The Horites of Seir (vv.20-30): The dispossessed inhabitants Esau absorbed
The Eight Kings of Edom (vv.31-39): Kingship before Israel — a prophetic bombshell
The Thirteen Territorial Chiefs (vv.40-43): Edom's permanent settlement and legacy

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob dwelt in the land wherein his father was a stranger, in the land of Canaan.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּשֶׁב יַעֲקֹב בְּאֶרֶץ מְגוּרֵי אָבִיו בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyeshev Ya'akov be'eretz megurei aviv be'eretz Kena'an.
verses.study_note: The narrative opens with a deliberate contrast. Chapter 36 closed with Esau's genealogy — Esau who settled and built a kingdom in Edom. Jacob, by contrast, 'dwells' (vayyeshev — from yashav, to sit/settle) in the land of his father's sojourning. 'Megurei aviv' (the sojournings of his father) uses the same word as the patriarchal condition: a pilgrim-stranger in a land of promise not yet possessed. Jacob is heir to the covenant but not yet to the land. The word 'vayyeshev' opens the Joseph narrative and is echoed in the name of the weekly Torah portion (Vayeshev), capturing the tension: a man trying to settle, yet about to be violently unsettled. As Calvin observed, God permits no permanent rest for His people in this age so that their eyes remain fixed on the promised inheritance.

verses.kjv_text: These are the generations of Jacob. Joseph, being seventeen years old, was feeding the flock with his brethren; and the lad was with the sons of Bilhah, and with the sons of Zilpah, his father’s wives: and Joseph brought unto his father their evil report.
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת יַעֲקֹב יוֹסֵף בֶּן־שְׁבַע עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה הָיָה רֹעֶה אֶת־אֶחָיו בַּצֹּאן וְהוּא נַעַר אֶת־בְּנֵי בִלְהָה וְאֶת־בְּנֵי זִלְפָּה נְשֵׁי אָבִיו וַיָּבֵא יוֹסֵף אֶת־דִּבָּתָם רָעָה אֶל־אָבִיהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Elleh toledot Ya'akov — Yosef ben-sheva esreh shanah hayah ro'eh et-echav batzon vehu na'ar et-benei Bilhah ve'et-benei Zilpah neshei aviv vayyave Yosef et-dibbatam ra'ah el-avihem.
verses.study_note: The formula 'These are the generations of' (toledot) normally introduces a genealogical list, but here it opens a narrative — the story of Jacob's family is the story of Joseph. At seventeen, Joseph is still a 'na'ar' (youth/lad — a term covering ages roughly 12–30). He shepherds with the sons of the servant women (Bilhah and Zilpah) — the lower-ranking brothers — suggesting Jacob has already structured relationships around Joseph's primacy. The 'evil report' (dibbatam ra'ah) Joseph brings is a matter of dispute: some read it as faithful witness against genuine wrongdoing; others see youthful tattling. The word 'dibbah' appears in Numbers 13:32 for the spies' slanderous report — it carries a connotation of false or damaging speech. Either way, the report deepens the brothers' resentment.
verses.doctrinal_note: Joseph is introduced as a shepherd at seventeen — the same age range at which David would tend flocks before his anointing. The shepherd-king typology runs throughout Scripture, culminating in Christ the Good Shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep (John 10:11). Joseph's 'report' introduces a pattern of rejected truth-telling that recurs throughout his story.

verses.kjv_text: Now Israel loved Joseph more than all his children, because he was the son of his old age: and he made him a coat of many colours.
verses.original_text: וְיִשְׂרָאֵל אָהַב אֶת־יוֹסֵף מִכָּל־בָּנָיו כִּי־בֶן־זְקֻנִים הוּא לוֹ וְעָשָׂה לוֹ כְּתֹנֶת פַּסִּים׃
verses.transliteration: VeYisra'el ahav et-Yosef mikkol-banav ki-ven-zkunim hu lo ve'asah lo ketonet passim.
verses.study_note: The narrator uses 'Israel' (not Jacob) — the covenant name, the man who wrestled with God. Israel's love for Joseph is presented as the structural reality that drives the entire narrative. 'Ben-zkunim' (son of old age) signals Joseph's special birth — the first son of Rachel, the beloved wife (Benjamin had not yet displaced him). More precisely, it may mean 'son of his wisdom/old age' — some Targums understand it as the son to whom he transmitted his learning. The 'ketonet passim' is the central symbol. The KJV 'coat of many colours' follows the LXX (chiton poikilos); the Hebrew passim means either 'many-colored' or more likely 'reaching to the palms and soles' — a long-sleeved, ankle-length robe. This was a garment of princely distinction, worn by those who did not perform manual labor (cf. 2 Samuel 13:18-19 where Tamar wears the same garment as a king's daughter). By giving Joseph this robe, Israel communicates publicly: this son will not work as a shepherd; he is the heir.
verses.doctrinal_note: The father's singular love for the son — setting him apart with a garment of distinction — is the Old Testament's deepest type of the Father's love for the Son: 'This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased' (Matthew 3:17). As Israel placed on Joseph the robe of privilege, the Father clothes the Son with glory. Joseph's robe, like Christ's seamless garment (John 19:23), becomes the object of violent stripping by envious hands.

verses.kjv_text: And when his brethren saw that their father loved him more than all his brethren, they hated him, and could not speak peaceably unto him.
verses.original_text: וַיִּרְאוּ אֶחָיו כִּי־אֹתוֹ אָהַב אֲבִיהֶם מִכָּל־אֶחָיו וַיִּשְׂנְאוּ אֹתוֹ וְלֹא יָכְלוּ דַּבְּרוֹ לְשָׁלֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiru echav ki-oto ahav avihem mikkol-echav vayyisne'u oto velo yakhlu dabbero leshalom.
verses.study_note: The hatred (sane — the strongest Hebrew word for hatred, used of Esau's hatred of Jacob after the birthright) arises directly from the father's visible favor. The brothers' inability to speak 'shalom' to Joseph is remarkable: shalom (wholeness, peace, wellbeing) is the customary greeting between kin. They cannot even perform the ritual of peace with their brother. This is not merely awkwardness — it is the breakdown of the most basic covenant of family. 'Could not speak peaceably' (lo yakhlu dabbero leshalom) — the word 'yakhlu' (they were unable) suggests something deeper than refusal: hatred had so consumed them that genuine peace-speaking had become impossible. The same root 'sane' will appear repeatedly through the chapter (vv.4, 5, 8) — hatred is the drumbeat driving the narrative toward the pit.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph dreamed a dream, and he told it his brethren: and they hated him yet the more.
verses.original_text: וַיַּחֲלֹם יוֹסֵף חֲלוֹם וַיַּגֵּד לְאֶחָיו וַיּוֹסִפוּ עוֹד שְׂנֹא אֹתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyachalom Yosef chalom vayyagged le'echav vayyosifu od seno oto.
verses.study_note: The dream (chalom) arrives. Dreaming is in Genesis a primary vehicle of divine revelation — to Abimelech (20:3), to Jacob at Bethel (28:12) and Peniel context, to Laban (31:24). Joseph's dreams come directly from God, as the narrative itself confirms (41:25, 32). The narrator plays on Joseph's own name: 'vayyosifu' (they added/continued to hate) is from the same root as 'Yosef' (to add). Joseph's name means increase — and with each dream the brothers' hatred increases. Joseph's telling the dream to his brothers shows either prophetic boldness or adolescent naivety — or both. He cannot keep silent about what God shows him. The dream is not his; it is given, and it will be fulfilled.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, Hear, I pray you, this dream which I have dreamed:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵיהֶם שִׁמְעוּ־נָא הַחֲלוֹם הַזֶּה אֲשֶׁר חָלָמְתִּי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer alehem shime'u-na hachalom hazzeh asher chalamti.
verses.study_note: 'Hear, I pray you' (shime'u-na) — the imperative with the particle of entreaty. Joseph speaks with earnest urgency. This is the language of the prophet summoning the congregation. The dream is not Joseph's interpretation of a vision; it is the dream itself, given by God, that he announces. Later (40:8, 41:16), Joseph will insist that interpretation belongs to God — he is always the conduit, never the source.

verses.kjv_text: For, behold, we were binding sheaves in the field, and, lo, my sheaf arose, and also stood upright; and, behold, your sheaves stood round about, and made obeisance to my sheaf.
verses.original_text: וְהִנֵּה אֲנַחְנוּ מְאַלְּמִים אֲלֻמִּים בְּתוֹךְ הַשָּׂדֶה וְהִנֵּה קָמָה אֲלֻמָּתִי וְגַם־נִצָּבָה וְהִנֵּה תְסֻבֶּינָה אֲלֻמֹּתֵיכֶם וַתִּשְׁתַּחֲוֶיןָ לַאֲלֻמָּתִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vehineh anachnu me'allamim alummim betokh hassadeh vehineh kamah alumati vegam-nitzavah vehineh tesuvbeynah alumoteikhem vattish'tachaveyna la'alumati.
verses.study_note: The dream is saturated with the agricultural imagery of Canaan. Three 'hineh' (behold!) markers draw attention to the three dramatic moments: (1) the sheaves being bound together, (2) Joseph's sheaf arising and standing upright, (3) the brothers' sheaves surrounding and bowing. 'Kamah' (arose) and 'nitzavah' (stood upright/erect) are posture words — Joseph's sheaf stands in a position of authority. 'Vattish'tachaveyna' (they bowed down) uses the hishtachavah verb — the deepest prostration, face to ground, the word used for worship of God (Gen 22:5, Exod 34:8). This dream will be literally fulfilled when Joseph's brothers bow before him in Egypt (Gen 42:6). The imagery of grain connects to Joseph's eventual role as the administrator of Egypt's grain supply — what he dreams, he becomes.
verses.doctrinal_note: The sheaves bowing is a prophetic picture of Christ's ultimate supremacy. The brothers' rejection of the dream does not prevent its fulfillment — 'The stone which the builders refused is become the head stone of the corner' (Psalm 118:22). Dreams given by God cannot be frustrated by human opposition. Proverbs 19:21: 'Many are the plans in the mind of a man, but it is the purpose of the LORD that will stand.'

verses.kjv_text: And his brethren said to him, Shalt thou indeed reign over us? or shalt thou indeed have dominion over us? And they hated him yet the more for his dreams, and for his words.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ לוֹ אֶחָיו הֲמָלֹךְ תִּמְלֹךְ עָלֵינוּ אִם־מָשׁוֹל תִּמְשֹׁל בָּנוּ וַיּוֹסִפוּ עוֹד שְׂנֹא אֹתוֹ עַל־חֲלֹמֹתָיו וְעַל־דְּבָרָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru lo echav hamalokh timlokh aleinu im-mashol timshol banu vayyosifu od seno oto al-chalomotav ve'al-devarav.
verses.study_note: The brothers use two emphatic constructions — the infinitive absolute before the finite verb: 'Shalt thou indeed reign?' (hamalokh timlokh) and 'shalt thou indeed have dominion?' (mashol timshol). The doubled verb in Hebrew intensifies certainty or indignation — they are outraged at the very idea. 'Malakh' (to reign as king) and 'mashal' (to rule/have dominion) are the two principal words for royal authority in the Old Testament. The brothers hear the dream correctly — they understand it as a claim to kingship. Their rejection is the rejection of God's chosen king. The chapter's refrain of 'vayyosifu' (they added to their hatred) appears again — the dreams intensify what was already there.

verses.kjv_text: And he dreamed yet another dream, and told it his brethren, and said, Behold, I have dreamed a dream more; and, behold, the sun and the moon and the eleven stars made obeisance to me.
verses.original_text: וַיַּחֲלֹם עוֹד חֲלוֹם אַחֵר וַיְסַפֵּר אֹתוֹ לְאֶחָיו וַיֹּאמֶר הִנֵּה חָלַמְתִּי חֲלוֹם עוֹד וְהִנֵּה הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ וְהַיָּרֵחַ וְאַחַד עָשָׂר כּוֹכָבִים מִשְׁתַּחֲוִים לִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyachalom od chalom acher vaysapper oto le'echav vayomer hinneh chalamti chalom od vehineh hashemesh vehayareach ve'achad asar kokhavim mishtachavim li.
verses.study_note: A second dream follows immediately — the celestial vision. In Genesis, double dreams signify certainty and divine determination: 'because the dream was doubled... it is because the thing is established by God, and God will shortly bring it to pass' (41:32). The sun, moon, and eleven stars bow to Joseph. The imagery is cosmic: celestial rulers prostrate before him. Eleven stars = eleven brothers (Benjamin presumably not yet counted as a dreaming patriarch). The sun and moon = Jacob and Leah (Rachel having died, Gen 35:19). This second dream extends the scope of Joseph's dominion beyond the brothers to encompass the entire family — even the parental generation. Note that Joseph again tells the dream to his brothers despite the response to the first. He cannot be silent about divine revelation.
verses.doctrinal_note: The cosmic imagery (sun, moon, stars bowing) connects to the language of Revelation 12:1 where Israel is depicted as 'clothed with the sun, and the moon under her feet, and upon her head a crown of twelve stars.' The Joseph typology points forward: the One whom all nations and authorities will ultimately bow before is Christ, 'at whose name every knee should bow, of things in heaven, and things in earth, and things under the earth' (Philippians 2:10). Genesis 41:32 provides the theological key to the double dream: what is doubled by God is certain and imminent.

verses.kjv_text: And he told it to his father, and to his brethren: and his father rebuked him, and said unto him, What is this dream that thou hast dreamed? Shall I and thy mother and thy brethren indeed come to bow down ourselves to thee to the earth?
verses.original_text: וַיְסַפֵּר אֶל־אָבִיו וְאֶל־אֶחָיו וַיִּגְעַר־בּוֹ אָבִיו וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ מָה הַחֲלוֹם הַזֶּה אֲשֶׁר חָלָמְתָּ הֲבוֹא נָבוֹא אֲנִי וְאִמְּךָ וְאַחֶיךָ לְהִשְׁתַּחֲוֺת לְךָ אָרְצָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vaysapper el-aviv ve'el-echav vayyig'ar-bo aviv vayomer lo mah hachalom hazzeh asher chalamta havo navo ani ve'immekha ve'achekha lehishtachavot lekha artzah.
verses.study_note: Jacob now hears the second dream. He 'rebuked' (vayyig'ar — a strong word, used for rebukes in Psalm 106:9 and Zechariah 3:2 of divine rebuke) but the text immediately adds that 'he kept the saying in his heart' (v.11). The rebuke is public — protective of Joseph and dismissive to the brothers. The mention of 'thy mother' is puzzling since Rachel had already died (35:18-19); commentators suggest: (1) Leah as the surrogate mother, (2) Bilhah the nurse, (3) Jacob's rhetorical hyperbole without exact identification. The same form as v.7 — 'havo navo' (infinitive absolute) — shall we indeed come? — echoes the brothers' own outraged rhetorical question in v.8.

verses.kjv_text: And his brethren envied him; but his father observed the saying.
verses.original_text: וַיְקַנְאוּ־בוֹ אֶחָיו וְאָבִיו שָׁמַר אֶת־הַדָּבָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayekanne'u-vo echav ve'aviv shamar et-haddavar.
verses.study_note: A verse of profound contrast. The brothers moved from hatred (sane, vv.4-8) to a deeper hostility: 'envied' (vayyekanne'u — from qana, jealousy/zealousness, the same root as 'qanna,' the word for God's holy jealousy — Exod 20:5). Jealousy burns where love should be. Meanwhile Jacob 'kept/observed the saying' (shamar et-haddavar) — 'shamar' means to guard carefully, to keep watch over something precious. The same word is used for 'keeping' the Law (Exod 12:17), for God keeping His covenant (Deut 7:9), and remarkably for Mary 'keeping' the words of the angel in her heart (Luke 2:19 LXX equivalent). Jacob is doing what a theologian does with a hard prophecy: he does not understand it, but he does not dismiss it either. He holds it.

verses.kjv_text: And his brethren went to feed their father’s flock in Shechem.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּלְכוּ אֶחָיו לִרְעוֹת אֶת־צֹאן אָבִיהֶם בִּשְׁכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyelechu echav lir'ot et-tzon avihem biShkhem.
verses.study_note: The brothers go to Shechem — the very city where Simeon and Levi had massacred the Hivites (Gen 34). Their willingness to graze there suggests either boldness or that enough time has passed. But Shechem is a place of violence in the Jacob narrative — the place where blood was wrongly shed. The narrator's mention of Shechem may subtly prepare the reader for the violence that is about to erupt again.

verses.kjv_text: And Israel said unto Joseph, Do not thy brethren feed the flock in Shechem? come, and I will send thee unto them. And he said to him, Here am I.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל־יוֹסֵף הֲלוֹא אַחֶיךָ רֹעִים בִּשְׁכֶם לְכָה וְאֶשְׁלָחֲךָ אֲלֵיהֶם וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ הִנֵּנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yisra'el el-Yosef halo achekha ro'im biShkhem lekhah ve'eshlachakha aleihem vayomer lo hinneni.
verses.study_note: The father sends the son. 'Come, I will send thee' (ve'eshlachakha — from shalach, to send). Joseph's response is one of Scripture's most charged moments: 'Hinneni' — Here am I. This is the word of the committed servant, the available one. Abraham said hinneni at the call to sacrifice Isaac (22:1, 11), Moses before the burning bush (Exod 3:4), Isaiah at the divine summons (Isa 6:8). It is the word of readiness and absolute availability. Joseph goes knowing his brothers hate him. He has no illusions. Yet he says: I am here, I am ready, send me.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Christological parallel is overwhelming. The Father sends the Son to His brethren (John 20:17, 'Go to my brethren'). The Son responds in perfect obedience: 'Lo, I come... I delight to do thy will, O my God' (Psalm 40:7-8). Hebrews 10:7 quotes this psalm as Christ's words at the incarnation. The same sequence — father sends, son is sent, son answers 'here I am' — runs from Joseph through Isaiah (6:8) to Christ himself. 'Hinneni' is the answer of all the redeemers of Scripture.

verses.kjv_text: And he said to him, Go, I pray thee, see whether it be well with thy brethren, and well with the flocks; and bring me word again. So he sent him out of the vale of Hebron, and he came to Shechem.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ לֶךְ־נָא רְאֵה אֶת־שְׁלוֹם אַחֶיךָ וְאֶת־שְׁלוֹם הַצֹּאן וַהֲשִׁבֵנִי דָּבָר וַיִּשְׁלָחֵהוּ מֵעֵמֶק חֶבְרוֹן וַיָּבֹא שְׁכֶמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer lo lekh-na re'eh et-shalom achekha ve'et-shalom hatzon vahashiveni davar vayyishlachemhu me'emek Chevron vayyavo Shekhemah.
verses.study_note: 'Go, see the shalom of thy brethren' — the mission is to see their peace (shalom), the very thing the brothers denied Joseph in v.4 ('could not speak peaceably'). The irony is exquisite: Jacob sends Joseph to check on the shalom of those who cannot extend shalom to Joseph. 'Vale of Hebron' (emek Chevron — the valley of Hebron) — Hebron is where Abraham was buried, the patriarchal heartland. Joseph leaves from the place of promise and covenant ancestry. He is the covenant's representative going out to his brothers. This is also his last visit to his father. He and Jacob will not meet again for twenty-two years.

verses.kjv_text: And a certain man found him, and, behold, he was wandering in the field: and the man asked him, saying, What seekest thou?
verses.original_text: וַיִּמְצָאֵהוּ אִישׁ וְהִנֵּה תֹעֶה בַּשָּׂדֶה וַיִּשְׁאָלֵהוּ הָאִישׁ לֵאמֹר מַה־תְּבַקֵּשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyimtza'ehu ish vehineh to'eh bassadeh vayyish'alehu ha'ish lemor mah-tevakesh.
verses.study_note: An unnamed man finds the 'wandering' Joseph (to'eh — wandering, straying, lost) in the field. This anonymous figure is Providence in human form — directing Joseph toward Dothan and his destiny. The rabbis (Midrash Rabbah) identified him as the angel Gabriel; Calvin simply calls him 'a certain man' sent by God to redirect Joseph. Without this encounter, Joseph turns back and returns to Hebron. The 'wandering' (to'eh) echoes Deuteronomy 26:5 — 'A wandering Aramean was my father' — Israel's foundational self-description as the wandering, vulnerable people of God. Joseph is the son of that wandering people.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, I seek my brethren: tell me, I pray thee, where they feed their flocks.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֶת־אַחַי אָנֹכִי מְבַקֵּשׁ הַגִּידָה־נָּא לִי אֵיפֹה הֵם רֹעִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer et-achai anokhi mevakesh haggidah-na li eifoh hem ro'im.
verses.study_note: 'I seek my brethren' — in Hebrew, 'et-achai anokhi mevakesh' places 'my brethren' emphatically first. Joseph's mission is relational before it is reportorial. He seeks his brothers. The same pathos applies to Christ's mission — He came to seek what was lost (Luke 19:10). The urgency and directness of Joseph's answer shows no hesitation, no complaint about the difficulty of the journey, no thought of turning back. He came to find his brothers, and he will not stop looking until he does — a resolve that will cost him everything.

verses.kjv_text: And the man said, They are departed hence; for I heard them say, Let us go to Dothan. And Joseph went after his brethren, and found them in Dothan.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר הָאִישׁ נָסְעוּ מִזֶּה כִּי שָׁמַעְתִּי אֹמְרִים נֵלְכָה דֹּתָיְנָה וַיֵּלֶךְ יוֹסֵף אַחַר אֶחָיו וַיִּמְצָאֵם בְּדֹתָן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer ha'ish nas'u mizzeh ki shamati omrim nelkhah Dotaynah vayyelek Yosef achar echav vayyimtze'em beDothan.
verses.study_note: The man redirects Joseph to Dothan — approximately fifteen miles north of Shechem. Dothan ('two wells/two cisterns') is a fertile valley on the trade route between Canaan and Egypt. The name itself may be Providence's signature — Joseph's brothers are near the wells/cisterns into which they will throw him. Joseph goes 'after his brethren' — pursuing, following. The same word will be used in Psalm 23: 'goodness and mercy shall follow me.' Joseph follows his brothers; eventually God's goodness follows Joseph into the pit and into Egypt.

verses.kjv_text: And when they saw him afar off, even before he came near unto them, they conspired against him to slay him.
verses.original_text: וַיִּרְאוּ אֹתוֹ מֵרָחֹק וּבְטֶרֶם יִקְרַב אֲלֵיהֶם וַיִּתְנַכְּלוּ אֹתוֹ לַהֲמִיתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiru oto merachok uveterem yikrav aleihem vayyithnakkelu oto lahamito.
verses.study_note: 'They conspired' (vayyithnakkelu — Hithpael of nakal, to be crafty, to plot with cunning deceit). The conspiracy forms 'afar off' — before Joseph even arrives, the murder is decided. Distance gives cruelty the space it needs. The verb 'nakal' suggests malicious cunning, a deeply premeditated evil. The same pattern occurs in Psalm 2:1-2: 'Why do the heathen rage... the kings of the earth set themselves... against the LORD, and against his anointed.' And in Matthew 26:3-4: 'the chief priests... and the elders... consulted that they might take Jesus by subtilty, and kill him.' The conspiracy against the beloved son from afar — this is the ancient pattern of rejected grace.
verses.doctrinal_note: Matthew 21:38 records the parable of the wicked tenants: 'But when the husbandmen saw the son, they said among themselves, This is the heir; come, let us kill him.' The verbal and structural parallel to Genesis 37:18 is precise. The conspiracy against Joseph is the Old Testament type of the conspiracy against Christ. Seeing him coming from afar, they plot his death. The beloved son is killed by his own brethren (John 1:11: 'He came unto his own, and his own received him not').

verses.kjv_text: And they said one to another, Behold, this dreamer cometh.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ אִישׁ אֶל־אָחִיו הִנֵּה בַּעַל הַחֲלֹמוֹת הַלָּזֶה בָּא׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru ish el-achiv hinneh ba'al hachalomot hallazeh ba.
verses.study_note: One of the most memorable sentences in Genesis. 'Ba'al hachalomot' — the master/owner/lord of dreams. The word 'ba'al' means owner, master, lord — the same word used for 'husband' and for the Canaanite deity. Their contemptuous title for Joseph becomes, in God's irony, his true description: Joseph is the man whose dreams God owns and fulfills. The brothers' contempt functions as a divine title. In their mockery they speak more truly than they know — this one is indeed the Lord of Dreams, and nothing they do will prevent his dreams from coming true.

verses.kjv_text: Come now therefore, and let us slay him, and cast him into some pit, and we will say, Some evil beast hath devoured him: and we shall see what will become of his dreams.
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה לְכוּ וְנַהַרְגֵהוּ וְנַשְׁלִכֵהוּ בְּאַחַד הַבֹּרוֹת וְאָמַרְנוּ חַיָּה רָעָה אֲכָלָתְהוּ וְנִרְאֶה מַה־יִּהְיוּ חֲלֹמֹתָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah lekhu venaharggehu venashlikkhehu be'achad habborot ve'amarnu chayyah ra'ah akhalathu venireh mah-yihyu chalomotav.
verses.study_note: 'We shall see what will become of his dreams' — the taunt of those who believe they can destroy a divine word by destroying its bearer. The brothers think that killing the dreamer kills the dream. They will discover the opposite: the pit is the first step toward the throne. The phrase 'bor' (pit/cistern) becomes a loaded word — in Psalm 40:2 (a Messianic psalm), God lifts the psalmist 'out of an horrible pit' (bor); in Zechariah 9:11, by 'the blood of thy covenant' God sends forth 'prisoners out of the pit wherein is no water' — the exact description of the pit in v.24.
