verses.doctrinal_note: Matthew 21:38 is the New Testament parallel: 'This is the heir; come, let us kill him, and let us seize on his inheritance.' The logic is identical — kill the heir, inherit the dreams. But God's purposes cannot be thwarted by human violence. Acts 2:23 captures the paradox: 'him, being delivered by the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God, ye have taken, and by wicked hands have crucified and slain.' The brothers' wickedness is the instrument of God's plan, not its obstacle.

verses.kjv_text: And Reuben heard it, and he delivered him out of their hands; and said, Let us not kill him.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁמַע רְאוּבֵן וַיַּצִּלֵהוּ מִיָּדָם וַיֹּאמֶר לֹא נַכֶּנּוּ נָפֶשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishma Reuven vayyatzilehu miyadam vayomer lo nakkennu nafesh.
verses.study_note: Reuben, the eldest and therefore responsible for the household (Gen 35:22 shows him already overstepping), intervenes. 'He delivered him' (vayyatzilehu — from natzal, to rescue, to snatch away) — a strong rescue word used for God delivering Israel (Exod 6:6). Reuben's motivation is revealed in v.22: he plans to restore Joseph to his father, perhaps to recover his own standing after the Bilhah incident. His intervention is partial and ultimately unsuccessful — he chooses the pit over the father's house — but it spares immediate bloodshed. 'Let us not kill him' (lo nakkennu nafesh — let us not strike him in the soul/life) — Reuben refuses to make them murderers, though he has no plan to actually bring Joseph home.

verses.kjv_text: And Reuben said unto them, Shed no blood, but cast him into this pit that is in the wilderness, and lay no hand upon him; that he might rid him out of their hands, to deliver him to his father again.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם רְאוּבֵן אַל־תִּשְׁפְּכוּ־דָם הַשְׁלִיכוּ אֹתוֹ אֶל־הַבּוֹר הַזֶּה אֲשֶׁר בַּמִּדְבָּר וְיָד אַל־תִּשְׁלְחוּ־בוֹ לְמַעַן הַצִּיל אֹתוֹ מִיָּדָם לַהֲשִׁיבוֹ אֶל־אָבִיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer aleihem Reuven al-tishpeku-dam hashliku oto el-habbor hazzeh asher bamidbar veyad al-tishlechu-vo lema'an hatzil oto miyadam lahashivo el-aviv.
verses.study_note: 'Shed no blood' (al-tishpeku-dam) — the prohibition against bloodshed. Reuben appeals to the deep biblical principle: blood shed cries out from the ground (Gen 4:10). The blood of the innocent is sacred. The pit in the wilderness (bor bamidbar) is a dry cistern — the rabbis note it was empty of water (confirmed in v.24), but filled with serpents and scorpions according to the Midrash. The narrator lets us hear Reuben's private intention: 'to deliver him to his father again.' Reuben's plan has the right destination but the wrong method. He is trying to save Joseph secretly rather than directly confronting his brothers.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when Joseph was come unto his brethren, that they stript Joseph out of his coat, his coat of many colours that was on him;
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כַּאֲשֶׁר־בָּא יוֹסֵף אֶל־אֶחָיו וַיַּפְשִׁיטוּ אֶת־יוֹסֵף אֶת־כֻּתָּנְתּוֹ אֶת־כְּתֹנֶת הַפַּסִּים אֲשֶׁר עָלָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi ka'asher-ba Yosef el-echav vayyafshitu et-Yosef et-kuttanto et-ketonet happassim asher alav.
verses.study_note: The moment of arrival. The ketonet passim is stripped from Joseph (vayyafshitu — to strip off violently). The robe of distinction, the garment of the father's love, is removed. This stripping is the inverse of the giving — as Jacob gave the robe as a declaration of election, the brothers remove it as a declaration of rejection. The stripping precedes the pit and anticipates the later stripping: in Egypt, Potiphar's wife will seize his garment (39:12). The beloved son is repeatedly stripped of his covering. Yet each stripping is followed by a greater clothing: pit → Potiphar's house; prison → the robes of Egypt's second-in-command (41:42).

verses.kjv_text: And they took him, and cast him into a pit: and the pit was empty, there was no water in it.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקָּחֻהוּ וַיַּשְׁלִכוּ אֹתוֹ הַבֹּרָה וְהַבּוֹר רֵק אֵין בּוֹ מָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikkachuhu vayyashlikhu oto habboorah vehabbor reik ein bo mayim.
verses.study_note: The pit (bor) is described precisely: empty (reik — hollow, vacant), no water in it. The double emphasis is significant. An empty cistern means no accidental drowning — Joseph will not die here. But 'no water' also means deprivation: the pit of suffering where even the basic necessity of life is absent. Zechariah 9:11 explicitly evokes this pit: 'by the blood of thy covenant I have sent forth thy prisoners out of the pit wherein is no water.' The waterless pit is the type of the covenant curse from which only blood can ransom. Lamentations 3:53-55 has Jeremiah crying from just such a pit (the prophets' experience follows the pattern). From the pit, Joseph cried out (Gen 42:21: 'we saw the anguish of his soul, when he besought us') — as from the pit, Christ cried 'My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?'
verses.doctrinal_note: The empty, waterless pit is the type of death itself — the place of abandonment, void of life-sustaining water. Joseph in the pit anticipates Christ in the tomb: three days in darkness (Matthew 12:40), buried in a sealed cave, cut off from the living. Yet as the pit could not hold Joseph — he would emerge to reign — so the tomb could not hold Christ. The pit is never the end of the story; it is always the threshold of resurrection and exaltation.

verses.kjv_text: And they sat down to eat bread: and they lifted up their eyes and looked, and, behold, a company of Ishmeelites came from Gilead with their camels bearing spicery and balm and myrrh, going to carry it down to Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּשְׁבוּ לֶאֱכָל־לֶחֶם וַיִּשְׂאוּ עֵינֵיהֶם וַיִּרְאוּ וְהִנֵּה אֹרְחַת יִשְׁמְעֵאלִים בָּאָה מִגִּלְעָד וּגְמַלֵּיהֶם נֹשְׂאִים נְכֹאת וּצְרִי וָלֹט הוֹלְכִים לְהוֹרִיד מִצְרָיְמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyeishevu le'ekhol-lechem vayyis'u eineihem vayyiru vehineh orchat Yishma'elim ba'ah miGil'ad ugemaleihem nos'im nekhot utzri valot holkhim lehorid Mitzraymah.
verses.study_note: The brothers sit to eat while Joseph is in the pit. Their callousness is depicted without editorial comment — the narrator allows the action to speak. 'They sat down to eat bread' while their brother cried for help below (cf. Gen 42:21). A caravan of Ishmaelites approaches from Gilead — carrying the spices of trade: 'nekhot' (gum tragacanth), 'tzri' (balm/balsam), 'lot' (myrrh/ladanum). These fragrant spices were used in burial, medicine, and perfumery — and they are heading to Egypt. Providence has arranged the mechanism of Joseph's transit to his destiny. The route from Gilead through Canaan to Egypt was the ancient caravan highway — well-traveled, well-known.
verses.doctrinal_note: The brothers eat bread while the innocent suffers below. This is the terrible normalcy of human hardness — life continues around the suffering of the innocent. At Calvary too, those who crucified Christ 'sitting down... watched him there' (Matt 27:36). The parallel extends further: the caravan carries burial spices to Egypt — the very land where Joseph will 'die' to his old life and be 'resurrected' to authority. Providence uses a trade caravan as the instrument of a divine plan no one present can see.

verses.kjv_text: And Judah said unto his brethren, What profit is it if we slay our brother, and conceal his blood?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה אֶל־אֶחָיו מַה־בֶּצַע כִּי נַהֲרֹג אֶת־אָחִינוּ וְכִסִּינוּ אֶת־דָּמוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yehudah el-echav mah-betza ki naharog et-achinu vekissinu et-damo.
verses.study_note: Judah speaks — not from conscience but from calculation. 'What profit' (mah-betza) — betza is the word for unjust gain, dishonest profit (Exod 18:21 uses it for 'covetousness'; Jeremiah 6:13 for the prophets' greed). Judah's argument is pragmatic: killing Joseph and hiding the evidence yields nothing. His question does acknowledge a key moral fact — 'conceal his blood' (kissinu et-damo) — they know the blood of the innocent cannot be truly hidden (Gen 4:10). Judah is the ancestor of David and of Christ (Gen 49:10; Matt 1:2-3) — and this moment shows him at his worst, yet he becomes the instrument of Joseph's survival and later the instrument of his own transformation (Gen 44:33).

verses.kjv_text: Come, and let us sell him to the Ishmeelites, and let not our hand be upon him; for he is our brother and our flesh. And his brethren were content.
verses.original_text: לְכוּ וְנִמְכְּרֶנּוּ לַיִּשְׁמְעֵאלִים וְיָדֵנוּ אַל־תְּהִי בוֹ כִּי־אָחִינוּ בְשָׂרֵנוּ הוּא וַיִּשְׁמְעוּ אֶחָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Lekhu venimkkerennu laYishma'elim veyadeinu al-tehi vo ki-achinu vesarenu hu vayyishme'u echav.
verses.study_note: 'For he is our brother and our flesh' — Judah's only concession to humanity. The kinship argument saves Joseph's life but not his freedom. Slavery replaces murder. 'And his brethren were content' (vayyishme'u — they listened, agreed) — the sale is decided. To sell a person into slavery is itself a capital offense in Mosaic Law (Exod 21:16: 'he that stealeth a man, and selleth him... shall surely be put to death'). The brothers incur blood guilt in a different form. Yet Providence uses even this crime as the instrument of deliverance.

verses.kjv_text: Then there passed by Midianites merchantmen; and they drew and lifted up Joseph out of the pit, and sold Joseph to the Ishmeelites for twenty pieces of silver: and they brought Joseph into Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַבְרוּ אֲנָשִׁים מִדְיָנִים סֹחֲרִים וַיִּמְשְׁכוּ וַיַּעֲלוּ אֶת־יוֹסֵף מִן־הַבּוֹר וַיִּמְכְּרוּ אֶת־יוֹסֵף לַיִּשְׁמְעֵאלִים בְּעֶשְׂרִים כָּסֶף וַיָּבִיאוּ אֶת־יוֹסֵף מִצְרָיְמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavru anashim Midyanim sochrim vayyimshekhu vayyya'alu et-Yosef min-habbor vayyimkeru et-Yosef laYishma'elim be'esrim kasef vayyavi'u et-Yosef Mitzraymah.
verses.study_note: The sale: twenty pieces of silver (esrim kasef). Twenty shekels of silver was the standard redemption price for a male slave aged five to twenty under Mosaic Law (Leviticus 27:5). Joseph's brothers set his worth at the lowest commercial estimate of a young male. The Midianites and Ishmaelites are treated interchangeably (the two groups were intermarried caravan traders). 'They drew and lifted up' (vayyimshekhu vayyya'alu) — they pulled Joseph out of the pit. The one who goes into the pit is brought out — already the pattern of descent and ascent that will define Joseph's story. Joseph is now in Egypt. The dream's context has been established.
verses.doctrinal_note: Twenty pieces of silver — the blood price of Joseph — is the typological forerunner of the thirty pieces of silver for which Christ was betrayed (Zechariah 11:12-13; Matthew 26:15). The price increased (reflecting inflation over 1,400 years of biblical history, and the greater value of the antitype), but the act is the same: the beloved is sold by those who should have loved him, for money. Acts 7:9: 'the patriarchs, moved with envy, sold Joseph into Egypt: but God was with him.' The 'but' is the hinge of history.

verses.kjv_text: And Reuben returned unto the pit; and, behold, Joseph was not in the pit; and he rent his clothes.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשָׁב רְאוּבֵן אֶל־הַבּוֹר וְהִנֵּה אֵין יוֹסֵף בַּבּוֹר וַיִּקְרַע אֶת־בְּגָדָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashav Reuven el-habbor vehineh ein Yosef babbor vayyikra et-begadav.
verses.study_note: 'And behold, Joseph was not in the pit' — the narrator places 'vehineh' (behold!) before the absence, making the discovery a moment of shock. Reuben returns alone (he had apparently left while the sale was made) with a plan to rescue Joseph, and finds the pit empty. This 'vehineh' of emptiness echoes and anticipates: the women come to the tomb and find it empty — 'He is not here' (Matt 28:6). Reuben's tearing of his garments (vayyikra et-begadav) is the ancient gesture of grief and mourning. The torn garment becomes a motif: Joseph's garment will be torn by the brothers and by Potiphar's wife; now the distraught Reuben tears his own.

verses.kjv_text: And he returned unto his brethren, and said, The child is not; and I, whither shall I go?
verses.original_text: וַיָּשָׁב אֶל־אֶחָיו וַיֹּאמַר הַיֶּלֶד אֵינֶנּוּ וַאֲנִי אָנָה אֲנִי־בָא׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashav el-echav vayomar hayyeled einennu va'ani anah ani-va.
verses.study_note: 'The child is not' (hayyeled einennu) — the same construction used in Lamentations 5:7. Reuben calls Joseph 'the child' (yeled) — perhaps an indication of his protective feelings. 'And I, whither shall I go?' — Reuben's lament is personal: he is the eldest, accountable. Where can he go if Joseph is lost? He cannot face his father. The question has no answer. Reuben's partial integrity has produced a complete disaster. His attempt to play both sides — spare Joseph without confronting his brothers — has left him exposed.

verses.kjv_text: And they took Joseph’s coat, and killed a kid of the goats, and dipped the coat in the blood;
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְחוּ אֶת־כְּתֹנֶת יוֹסֵף וַיִּשְׁחֲטוּ שְׂעִיר עִזִּים וַיִּטְבְּלוּ אֶת־הַכֻּתֹּנֶת בַּדָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikchu et-ketonet Yosef vayyishchatu se'ir izzim vayyitbelu et-hakkuttonet baddam.
verses.study_note: The garment reappears — now it will be turned into a lie. A 'se'ir izzim' (a young goat/kid) is slaughtered and its blood used to stain the coat. This is a further and darker echo: Isaac was deceived by Jacob using a kid of the goats and garments (Gen 27:9, 15-16) — now Jacob's sons use the same instruments to deceive Jacob. Sin reproduces its own patterns. The ketonet passim, given in love, is now dipped in animal blood — a substitutionary deception. The innocent animal dies so that the brothers' lie can live. This is a dark inversion of substitutionary sacrifice: instead of the animal dying so the guilty may live, the animal dies so that the guilty may deceive.

verses.kjv_text: And they sent the coat of many colours, and they brought it to their father; and said, This have we found: know now whether it be thy son’s coat or no.
verses.original_text: וַיְשַׁלְּחוּ אֶת־כְּתֹנֶת הַפַּסִּים וַיָּבִיאוּ אֶל־אֲבִיהֶם וַיֹּאמְרוּ זֹאת מָצָאנוּ הַכֶּר־נָא הַכְּתֹנֶת בִּנְךָ הִוא אִם־לֹא׃
verses.transliteration: Vayeshallechu et-ketonet happassim vayyavi'u el-avihem vayyomeru zot matzanu hakker-na hakkuttonet binkha hi im-lo.
verses.study_note: 'Hakkir-na' — 'Recognize this, please.' The same root (nakar) used when Jacob tricked his own father Isaac (27:23). The brothers use the very weapon of deception that their father used against his father — they are the children of a deceiver, now deceiving the deceiver. 'Know now' (hakker-na) places the burden of recognition on Jacob — he must identify the garment himself, making the lie a kind of self-inflicted wound. They say 'this have we found' — they technically have not murdered; they have not said a false word, only presented false evidence. They let the blood-stained garment speak.

verses.kjv_text: And he knew it, and said, It is my son’s coat; an evil beast hath devoured him; Joseph is without doubt rent in pieces.
verses.original_text: וַיַּכִּירָהּ וַיֹּאמֶר כְּתֹנֶת בְּנִי חַיָּה רָעָה אֲכָלָתְהוּ טָרֹף טֹרַף יוֹסֵף׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyakkirah vayomer ketonet beni chayyah ra'ah akhalathu tarof toraf Yosef.
verses.study_note: Jacob recognizes the coat immediately — the garment of his own making, his act of love, is now before him stained with blood. His conclusion: 'an evil beast hath devoured him' (chayyah ra'ah akhalathu) — exactly the lie the brothers constructed in v.20. The infinitive absolute 'tarof toraf' (he is without doubt rent to pieces) expresses Jacob's absolute certainty — a certainty founded entirely on false evidence. The man who deceived his own father with a disguise (Gen 27) is now deceived by his own sons with a disguise. Yet the one Jacob mourns as dead is alive.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob rent his clothes, and put sackcloth upon his loins, and mourned for his son many days.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרַע יַעֲקֹב שִׂמְלֹתָיו וַיָּשֶׂם שַׂק בְּמָתְנָיו וַיִּתְאַבֵּל עַל־בְּנוֹ יָמִים רַבִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra Ya'akov simlotav vayyasem sak bematanav vayyit'abbel al-beno yamim rabbim.
verses.study_note: Jacob puts on the full costume of mourning: torn garments and sackcloth. 'Yamim rabbim' (many days) — the length of Jacob's mourning is not given precisely, but it will last twenty-two years until he sees Joseph alive in Egypt. The biblical narrative of a father mourning a son as dead — only to find him alive and exalted — runs from this passage through the parable of the prodigal son ('this my son was dead, and is alive again,' Luke 15:24) to the resurrection itself. Jacob in mourning becomes a type of the mourning father who does not yet know that the dead is alive.

verses.kjv_text: And all his sons and all his daughters rose up to comfort him; but he refused to be comforted; and he said, For I will go down into the grave unto my son mourning. Thus his father wept for him.
verses.original_text: וַיָּקֻמוּ כָל־בָּנָיו וְכָל־בְּנֹתָיו לְנַחֲמוֹ וַיְמָאֵן לְהִתְנַחֵם וַיֹּאמֶר כִּי־אֵרֵד אֶל־בְּנִי אָבֵל שְׁאֹלָה וַיֵּבְךְּ אֹתוֹ אָבִיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyakumu khol-banav vekhol-benotav lenachamo vayyema'en lehitnachem vayomer ki-ered el-beni avel She'olah vayyevkk oto aviv.
verses.study_note: All Jacob's children — the very ones who caused the grief — attempt to comfort him. Their comfort is a lie perpetuated by their presence. Jacob refuses comfort (vayyema'en lehitnachem — he persistently refused to be comforted). His refusal is not stubborn self-pity; it is a kind of profound faithfulness: 'I will go down into the grave (Sheol) unto my son mourning.' Jacob cannot be comforted because he cannot receive a false comfort. The one who would truly comfort him — Joseph himself, alive and victorious — is not there. No substitute will do. Jeremiah 31:15 uses the same language for 'Rachel weeping for her children... refused to be comforted' — the grief that only resurrection can answer.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jacob's refusal of comfort is a dark foretaste of the grief that only resurrection can resolve. In the New Testament, the disciples could not be comforted after the crucifixion — until the resurrection. The Father sent the Son into death (Sheol in Jacob's words) and the Son went down ('Christ also hath once suffered for sins... being put to death in the flesh, but quickened by the Spirit: by which also he went and preached unto the spirits in prison,' 1 Peter 3:18-19). Jacob says he will go down to Sheol to be with his son in death — but God will instead bring the son back from Sheol to the father (Genesis 46).

verses.kjv_text: And the Midianites sold him into Egypt unto Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh’s, and captain of the guard.
verses.original_text: וְהַמְּדָנִים מָכְרוּ אֹתוֹ אֶל־מִצְרָיִם לְפוֹטִיפַר סְרִיס פַּרְעֹה שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vehammadanim makhru oto el-Mitzrayim leFotifar seris Par'oh sar hattabbachim.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes with a single verse of momentous compression: Joseph is sold in Egypt to Potiphar, 'an officer of Pharaoh's and captain of the guard' (sar hattabbachim — chief of the executioners/slaughterers). Potiphar's title is ominous — the chief killer. Joseph has arrived at the place of maximum danger: sold to the head of Pharaoh's security forces. Yet this placement is precisely where God needs Joseph to be, in proximity to Pharaoh's household and Pharaoh's prisoners. Providence sells the dreamer to the keeper of prisoners, so that the dreamer can interpret prisoners' dreams, so that Pharaoh can hear of his gift, so that Joseph can be exalted to second in all of Egypt. What appears to be the end of all hope is the precise starting point of the redemption. 'And the LORD was with Joseph' (39:2) — the three most important words that follow this closing verse.
verses.doctrinal_note: Acts 7:9-10 is the New Testament commentary: 'And the patriarchs, moved with envy, sold Joseph into Egypt: but God was with him, and delivered him out of all his afflictions, and gave him favour and wisdom in the sight of Pharaoh king of Egypt.' The final word of the chapter belongs not to Potiphar's title nor to the brothers' cruelty but to the unstated yet governing reality: God is with Joseph. Genesis 50:20 is the theological key to the entire Joseph narrative and to all of Providence: 'Ye thought evil against me; but God meant it unto good, to bring to pass, as it is this day, to save much people alive.' This is also the word of the cross: what men intended as execution, God intended as salvation.

meta.overview: Genesis 37 opens the Joseph narrative — the longest continuous story in Genesis, spanning fourteen chapters (37–50). It is also the most sustained typological portrait of Christ in the entire Old Testament. Joseph is the beloved son of his father Jacob, set apart by the distinctive tunic (ketonet passim), rejected by his brothers, sold into slavery for twenty pieces of silver, and descends into the pit — only to ascend to a throne from which he will save the very ones who destroyed him. The chapter introduces all the key actors: the favored son, the jealous brothers, the aging patriarch, the dreamer. At its core, Genesis 37 is the story of a father's love for a son, and what that love costs in a broken world. The dreams of Joseph — which the brothers hate — are the word of God. The conspiracy to silence the dreamer cannot silence God. What the brothers intend for evil, God is already turning for good (50:20). This chapter is not primarily a moral tale about sibling rivalry; it is the opening movement of a symphony that resolves in the preservation of the covenant line, the type of Christ crucified and exalted, and the sovereignty of God over history's darkest moments.
meta.theme: The Beloved Son Rejected — Providence Through Betrayal
meta.keyWordMeaning: The ornamented/long-sleeved tunic — the garment of distinction and favor that marks Joseph as heir and separates him from his brothers; it becomes the central symbol of the chapter, appearing in blessing (v.3), in hatred (v.4), dipped in blood (v.31), and presented to Jacob as false evidence of death.
outline: The Beloved Son and the Hated Dreams (vv.1-11): Joseph's special status, the tunic, and the two prophetic dreams
Sent by the Father (vv.12-17): Jacob sends Joseph to his brothers — the last act of tender fatherly commission
The Conspiracy at Dothan (vv.18-24): 'The dreamer cometh' — the plot to kill, the pit, Reuben's intervention
Sold for Silver (vv.25-28): Judah's proposal, the Ishmaelite traders, twenty pieces of silver
Jacob's Grief and the Bloody Coat (vv.29-35): Reuben's horror, the deception, a father inconsolable
Joseph Enters Egypt (v.36): The Midianites sell Joseph to Potiphar — Providence's hidden hand

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass at that time, that Judah went down from his brethren, and turned in to a certain Adullamite, whose name was Hirah.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בָּעֵת הַהִוא וַיֵּרֶד יְהוּדָה מֵאֵת אֶחָיו וַיֵּט עַד־אִישׁ עֲדֻלָּמִי וּשְׁמוֹ חִירָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi ba'et hahi vayyered Yehudah me'et echav vayyett ad-ish Adulami ushmo Chirah.
verses.study_note: The phrase 'at that time' (ba'et hahi) is a deliberate narrative hinge, interrupting the Joseph story mid-stream (Gen 37 and 39). The placement is not accidental — the narrator sets Judah's moral decline alongside Joseph's providential journey to show the two contrasting trajectories among Jacob's sons. 'Judah went down' (vayyered Yehudah) — the verb 'yarad' (to go down, descend) recurs throughout the Joseph cycle and typically signals moral or geographical decline. From Hebron down to the Shephelah, then further into Canaanite territory. His separation from his brothers (me'et echav) echoes his earlier initiative in selling Joseph (37:26-27) — Judah distances himself from his family and his covenant heritage. He 'turns aside' (vayyett) to an Adullamite — gravitating toward Canaanite relationship rather than kin. Adullam was a prominent Canaanite city in the Shephelah lowland. Hirah's name means 'nobility' — Judah chooses a respected pagan companion.

verses.kjv_text: And Judah saw there a daughter of a certain Canaanite, whose name was Shuah; and he took her, and went in unto her.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא־שָׁם יְהוּדָה בַּת־אִישׁ כְּנַעֲנִי וּשְׁמוֹ שׁוּעַ וַיִּקָּחֶהָ וַיָּבֹא אֵלֶיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyar-sham Yehudah bat-ish Kena'ani ushmo Shua vayyiqqachehah vayyavo eleihah.
verses.study_note: Three verbs of swift, unconsidered action: he 'saw' (vayyar), he 'took' (vayyiqqach), he 'went in to her' (vayyavo eleihah). This trilogy of impulse recalls the sin pattern of Genesis 6:2 (the sons of God 'saw' and 'took') and the pattern of Shechem with Dinah (34:2). No prayer is recorded, no consultation with his father Isaac or grandfather Abraham's example of seeking a non-Canaanite wife (Gen 24; 28:1-2). The explicit identification of the woman as 'Canaanite' (Kena'ani) flags the covenant transgression — Jacob had been specifically commanded to avoid Canaanite women (28:1). The woman is identified only through her father's name Shua (שׁוּעַ — 'wealth' or 'cry'), never by her own name — she remains a peripheral figure defined by her ethnic identity and paternal connection. Judah's rapid, desire-driven marriage stands in deliberate contrast to the prayerful, covenant-conscious wife-seeking of Abraham and Isaac.

verses.kjv_text: And she conceived, and bare a son; and he called his name Er.
verses.original_text: וַתַּהַר וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן וַיִּקְרָא אֶת־שְׁמוֹ עֵר׃
verses.transliteration: Vattahar vateled ben vayyikra et-shmo Er.
verses.study_note: Three rapid verbs mirror the three of verse 2: conception, birth, naming — life begins with the same compressed speed that marriage did. Judah names the firstborn, exercising his patriarchal authority. Er (עֵר) means 'watchful' or 'awake' — deeply ironic for a son whose brief life will be ended by God before he accomplishes anything. The name is also the reverse spelling of the Hebrew word 'ra' (רַע — 'evil'), which the narrator will use to describe Er's character in v.7. Some scholars note this as a subtle wordplay embedded in the name itself.

verses.kjv_text: And she conceived again, and bare a son; and she called his name Onan.
verses.original_text: וַתַּהַר עוֹד וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן וַתִּקְרָא אֶת־שְׁמוֹ אוֹנָן׃
verses.transliteration: Vattahar od vateled ben vatikra et-shmo Onan.
verses.study_note: A small but significant shift: for the second son, the mother names him rather than the father. Onan (אוֹנָן) comes from the root 'on' (אוֹן), meaning 'strength, vigor, virility' — possibly 'wealth' or 'power.' The name chosen by his mother may reflect her pride in his vitality or her own aspirations for this son. The irony is total: a son named for virile strength will be struck dead precisely for the misuse and deliberate waste of his procreative power (v.9-10). Biblical names frequently contain prophetic reversals in Genesis.

verses.kjv_text: And she yet again conceived, and bare a son; and called his name Shelah: and he was at Chezib, when she bare him.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּסֶף עוֹד וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן וַתִּקְרָא אֶת־שְׁמוֹ שֵׁלָה וְהָיָה בִכְזִיב בְּלִדְתָּהּ אֹתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattosef od vateled ben vatikra et-shmo Shelah vehayah biKheziv beledetah oto.
verses.study_note: The third son is Shelah (שֵׁלָה — possibly 'peaceful,' 'petition,' or related to 'shalach,' to send/release). The narrator adds the precise detail that Judah was in Chezib (כְּזִיב — from 'kazav,' meaning 'falsehood, deception, lie') when Shelah was born. This geographic detail functions as a narrative foreshadow: Judah will later lie to Tamar about giving her Shelah in marriage (v.11). The place of Shelah's birth is the Place of Deception. Chezib is identified with Achzib (Josh 15:44; Micah 1:14), and 'Achzib' in prophetic usage means 'a deceitful thing' (Micah 1:14 — a place that will prove false to the kings of Israel). The pun is theological: the son through whom Judah hopes to perpetuate his line is born in the land of lies, and it is through deception that Judah will try to withhold him.

verses.kjv_text: And Judah took a wife for Er his firstborn, whose name was Tamar.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח יְהוּדָה אִשָּׁה לְעֵר בְּכוֹרוֹ וּשְׁמָהּ תָּמָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach Yehudah ishah le'Er bekhoro ushmah Tamar.
verses.study_note: Judah fulfills his patriarchal duty by arranging his firstborn's marriage. Tamar (תָּמָר) means 'date palm' — a symbol of upright beauty, fruitfulness, and nobility. The date palm's tall, straight trunk made it a Hebrew image of the righteous person: 'The righteous shall flourish like the palm tree' (Psalm 92:12). Tamar's name establishes her character before the narrative demonstrates it. Rabbinic tradition debated her origin — some argued she was a Canaanite (which would add irony to her later righteousness), others that she was a Shemite, even the daughter of Shem's priest-king Melchizedek. The text is silent on her ethnic background, perhaps deliberately so — what matters is not her pedigree but her covenant faithfulness. She is introduced without parentage or backstory, defined solely by her name and her marriage to Er: a woman whose identity will be forged by the circumstances thrust upon her.

verses.kjv_text: And Er, Judah’s firstborn, was wicked in the sight of the LORD; and the LORD slew him.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי עֵר בְּכוֹר יְהוּדָה רַע בְּעֵינֵי יְהוָה וַיְמִתֵהוּ יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi Er bekhor Yehudah ra be'einei YHVH vayyamitehu YHVH.
verses.study_note: One of the most economical divine judgments in Scripture — seven Hebrew words pronounce wickedness and execute judgment simultaneously. 'Wicked in the sight of the LORD' (ra be'einei YHVH) is the same moral formula used of the pre-Flood generation (6:5; 8:21) and anticipates the formula that will be applied throughout Israel's history. The nature of Er's sin is left entirely unnamed — this silence is remarkable and deliberate. It strips the evaluation of all mitigating context: whatever he did was sufficiently severe that God Himself intervened with death. The parallel with Onan (v.10) shows that both sons were judged, but for different named and unnamed reasons respectively. 'The LORD slew him' (vayyamitehu YHVH) — unmediated divine execution, as with the Egyptian firstborn (Exod 12:29), Nadab and Abihu (Lev 10:2), and Ananias and Sapphira (Acts 5:5). God's patience has limits, and those limits are sovereignly determined.
verses.doctrinal_note: Divine judgment on individuals: God exercises direct executive judgment over human lives when moral evil reaches a threshold He alone determines (Deut 32:39; 1 Sam 2:6). The unnamed nature of Er's sin establishes the principle that judgment is not always explained to human observers — God's moral knowledge exceeds our visibility. This is not arbitrariness but absolute justice operating from complete knowledge.

verses.kjv_text: And Judah said unto Onan, Go in unto thy brother’s wife, and marry her, and raise up seed to thy brother.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה לְאוֹנָן בֹּא אֶל־אֵשֶׁת אָחִיךָ וְיַבֵּם אֹתָהּ וְהָקֵם זֶרַע לְאָחִיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yehudah le'Onan bo el-eshet achikha veyyabem otah vehakkem zera le'achikha.
verses.study_note: Judah invokes the levirate principle (yibbum — from יָבָם, 'brother-in-law'). This pre-Mosaic custom required a surviving brother to marry his deceased brother's childless widow in order to 'raise up seed' (hakkeim zera) — to produce an heir who would carry the deceased brother's name and inherit his portion. The practice acknowledges that a man's identity and presence persisted through his offspring; to die without descendants was to be 'blotted out' from Israel. This obligation is later codified with its refusal mechanism (chalitzah — the sandal ceremony) in Deuteronomy 25:5-10. The verb 'yibbem' (וְיַבֵּם) appears here for its first occurrence in Scripture — the technical term for performing this duty. Judah's command is legally and morally correct in itself. His failure lies in what follows with Shelah (v.11) and his own subsequent behavior.
verses.doctrinal_note: Levirate marriage (yibbum): This pre-Mosaic institution reveals that God embedded principles of covenant loyalty and family solidarity into social practice long before Sinai. The purpose — preserving the name and inheritance of the deceased — anticipates the New Testament concept of Christ as the ultimate 'kinsman-redeemer' who raises up new life for those who were dead (Hebrews 7:25; Romans 8:34). The 'seed' (zera) that must be raised up echoes the Messianic seed promise of Genesis 3:15 — it is through the preservation of family lines that the Redeemer will come.

verses.kjv_text: And Onan knew that the seed should not be his; and it came to pass, when he went in unto his brother’s wife, that he spilled it on the ground, lest that he should give seed to his brother.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּדַע אוֹנָן כִּי לֹּא לוֹ יִהְיֶה הַזָּרַע וְהָיָה אִם־בָּא אֶל־אֵשֶׁת אָחִיו וְשִׁחֵת אַרְצָה לְבִלְתִּי נְתָן־זֶרַע לְאָחִיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyeda Onan ki lo lo yihyeh hazzara vehayah im-ba el-eshet achiv veshichet artzah levilti netan-zera le'achiv.
verses.study_note: The narrator reveals Onan's inner calculation with blunt precision. He 'knew' (vayyeda) the legal implications: any son born to Tamar through the levirate union would be counted as Er's legal heir, not his own. This meant Onan would sacrifice a portion of his future inheritance. His strategy: enjoy the marital privileges of Tamar while refusing the marital responsibility. 'He spilled it on the ground' (veshichet artzah) — the verb 'shichet' (from shachar, to destroy/corrupt) is the same word used of the corruption that brought the Flood (6:11-12, 'the earth was corrupt'). The deliberate, repeated nature of the act is underscored by the habitual force of the Hebrew construction ('whenever he went in...'). This is not a single lapse but a calculated, ongoing deception of both Tamar and his father. His sin is twofold: (1) covenant treachery against his brother's legacy, and (2) using Tamar as an object of gratification while denying her the dignity of motherhood. The greed that drove the sale of Joseph (Judah's earlier sin) now drives Onan's covenant refusal.
verses.doctrinal_note: Onan's sin: The church has variously applied this text to the general wrongness of contraception (Augustine, Catholic tradition) or more precisely to the specific sin of covenant betrayal and selfish deception. The text's own condemnation focuses on the refusal of levirate duty and the exploitation of Tamar — the act was evil because of Onan's intent and its context (covenantal treachery), not merely its mechanics. The broader principle is that God takes seriously our obligations to one another within His covenant structure, particularly when those obligations involve the vulnerable.

verses.kjv_text: And the thing which he did displeased the LORD: wherefore he slew him also.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּרַע בְּעֵינֵי יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה וַיָּמֶת גַּם־אֹתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyera be'einei YHVH asher asah vayyamet gam-oto.
verses.study_note: God's judgment falls on Onan with the same brevity as on Er. 'What he did displeased the LORD' (vayyera be'einei YHVH asher asah) — the 'thing' (asher asah) is the sustained, deliberate act of v.9, not a single failure. The word 'gam' (also/likewise) is theologically devastating: it links the two judgments, showing that God guards both moral uprightness generally (Er) and covenant obligation specifically (Onan). Onan's calculated treachery against his dead brother's legacy, his exploitation of Tamar, and his deception of Judah constitute a compound sin that God finds intolerable. The repetition of divine capital punishment within a single family sends a clear signal about God's seriousness regarding covenant faithfulness. Judah should have learned from this pattern, but his response in v.11 shows he draws the wrong lesson entirely.

verses.kjv_text: Then said Judah to Tamar his daughter in law, Remain a widow at thy father’s house, till Shelah my son be grown: for he said, Lest peradventure he die also, as his brethren did. And Tamar went and dwelt in her father’s house.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה לְתָמָר כַּלָּתוֹ שְׁבִי אַלְמָנָה בֵית־אָבִיךְ עַד־יִגְדַּל שֵׁלָה בְנִי כִּי אָמַר פֶּן־יָמוּת גַּם־הוּא כְּאֶחָיו וַתֵּלֶךְ תָּמָר וַתֵּשֶׁב בֵּית אָבִיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yehudah leTamar kalato shevi almanah beit-avikh ad-yigdal Shelah beni ki amar pen-yamut gam-hu ke'echav vattelekh Tamar vateshev beit aviha.
verses.study_note: Judah's response to two divine judgments is to draw the wrong conclusion entirely: he blames Tamar rather than his sons' sin. Privately ('for he said' — an internal thought) he fears that Tamar is somehow lethal to his sons — the ancient superstition of the 'black widow.' His public instruction to Tamar is to 'remain a widow' (shevi almanah) in her father's house 'until Shelah grows up' — a promise with no specified timeline, a verbal pledge with no real intention behind it. The wordplay on Chezib (v.5 — 'deception') is now fulfilled: Judah is deceiving Tamar. He places her in a legal limbo: she cannot remarry (she is obligated to the levirate institution) but he has no intention of giving her Shelah. 'Remain a widow' is a living death — no status, no security, no future. Tamar obeys without protest, returning to her father's house in quiet dignity. Her silent compliance here will be answered by bold action later. The narrator subtly vindicates her: she acts in good faith; Judah does not.

verses.kjv_text: And in process of time the daughter of Shuah Judah’s wife died; and Judah was comforted, and went up unto his sheepshearers to Timnath, he and his friend Hirah the Adullamite.
verses.original_text: וַיִּרְבּוּ הַיָּמִים וַתָּמָת בַּת־שׁוּעַ אֵשֶׁת יְהוּדָה וַיִּנָּחֶם יְהוּדָה וַיַּעַל עַל־גֹּזְזֵי צֹאנוֹ הוּא וְחִירָה רֵעֵהוּ הָעֲדֻלָּמִי תִּמְנָתָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyirbu hayyamim vatammat bat-Shua eshet Yehudah vayyinachem Yehudah vayyaal al-gozzei tzono hu veChirah re'ehu ha'Adulami Timnatha.
verses.study_note: Time passes ('the days multiplied' — vayyirbu hayyamim). Judah's unnamed Canaanite wife dies. After a period of mourning, he is 'comforted' (vayyinachem — the same root as Noah/Nechama and the comfort offered to Jacob after Joseph's apparent death in 37:35). Grief has passed and normal activity resumes. Sheepshearing was a festive social occasion in the ancient Near East — a time of celebration, drinking, and feasting (cf. Nabal's shearing in 1 Sam 25, Absalom's feast in 2 Sam 13:23-24). The Timnah (תִּמְנָתָה) of this passage is in the Shephelah, different from the Timnah of Samson's story. Judah goes up with his Canaanite companion Hirah — his continued intimacy with his pagan friend shows that years have passed without spiritual growth or covenant renewal.

verses.kjv_text: And it was told Tamar, saying, Behold thy father in law goeth up to Timnath to shear his sheep.
verses.original_text: וַיֻּגַּד לְתָמָר לֵאמֹר הִנֵּה חָמִיךְ עֹלֶה תִמְנָתָה לָגֹז צֹאנוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayuggad leTamar lemor hinneh chamikh oleh Timnata lagoz tzono.
verses.study_note: Tamar receives intelligence — from an unnamed source. She has been sitting in her father's house, watching and waiting. She has not given up on the covenant promise — her identity remains tied to Judah's household. When she learns that her father-in-law is on the move toward Timnah — within reach — she sees the moment she has been waiting for. The narrator uses 'hinneh' (behold) to mark the significance of the moment. Judah is going 'up' (oleh) to the shearing — while in v.1 he had 'gone down' (vayyered). The physical and moral geography of the chapter is precise.

verses.kjv_text: And she put her widow’s garments off from her, and covered her with a vail, and wrapped herself, and sat in an open place, which is by the way to Timnath; for she saw that Shelah was grown, and she was not given unto him to wife.
verses.original_text: וַתָּסַר בִּגְדֵי אַלְמְנוּתָהּ מֵעָלֶיהָ וַתְּכַס בַּצָּעִיף וַתִּתְעַלָּף וַתֵּשֶׁב בְּפֶתַח עֵינַיִם אֲשֶׁר עַל־דֶּרֶךְ תִּמְנָתָה כִּי רָאֲתָה כִּי־גָדַל שֵׁלָה וְהִיא לֹא־נִתְּנָה לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vattasar bigdei almenutah me'aleihah vattekhas batza'if vattit'allaf vateshev befetach einayim asher al-derekh Timnata ki ra'etah ki-gadal Shelah vehi lo-nittena lo le'ishah.
verses.study_note: Tamar's actions are described in five precise verbs: removed, covered, wrapped herself, sat, waited. The narrator then gives her motivation (ki ra'etah — 'for she saw'): Shelah has grown up and Judah has broken his promise. Her plan is bold, risky, and driven by covenant desperation, not sexual desire. She sits at 'Petah Einayim' (פֶּתַח עֵינַיִם — 'the opening of eyes' or 'the gate of two springs/eyes') — a location whose name carries rich irony: it is the place where Judah will 'open his eyes' and finally 'see' (v.15) — but not see clearly, mistaking his daughter-in-law for a prostitute. The name anticipates the moment of revelation in v.26 when his eyes are truly opened. The veil (tza'if) was not exclusively the garment of a prostitute — it could also be worn by a bride (Gen 24:65) — but it conceals Tamar's face sufficiently to prevent recognition.

verses.kjv_text: When Judah saw her, he thought her to be an harlot; because she had covered her face.
verses.original_text: וַיִּרְאֶהָ יְהוּדָה וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ לְזוֹנָה כִּי כִסְּתָה פָּנֶיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyir'ehah Yehudah vayyachsheveha lezonah ki kisseta paneihah.
verses.study_note: The verb 'saw' (vayyir'ehah) has driven Judah's choices throughout: he 'saw' the Canaanite woman (v.2), now he 'sees' what he thinks is a harlot. His vision is consistently corrupted by desire and assumption. 'He thought her to be' (vayyachsheveha) — the verb 'chashav' means to reckon, to account, to think. This same root will return climactically in Genesis 50:20 ('you meant it for evil, but God meant/thought/reckoned it for good'). Judah's false 'reckoning' of Tamar is about to be overturned by a true divine reckoning. The word 'zonah' (prostitute) here is the common term for a prostitute; in v.21-22, Hirah uses the more elevated term 'kedeshah' (a consecrated/cult prostitute) — either unwilling to use the cruder term or attempting social face-saving for Judah. The irony of Judah approaching a 'prostitute' who is actually his daughter-in-law bound to him in covenant obligation could not be more pointed.

verses.kjv_text: And he turned unto her by the way, and said, Go to, I pray thee, let me come in unto thee; (for he knew not that she was his daughter in law.) And she said, What wilt thou give me, that thou mayest come in unto me?
verses.original_text: וַיֵּט אֵלֶיהָ אֶל־הַדֶּרֶךְ וַיֹּאמֶר הָבָה־נָּא אָבוֹא אֵלַיִךְ כִּי לֹא יָדַע כִּי כַלָּתוֹ הִוא וַתֹּאמֶר מַה־תִּתֶּן־לִּי כִּי תָבוֹא אֵלָי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyett eleihah el-hadderekh vayomer havah-na avo eleikh ki lo yada ki khalato hi vatomer mah-titten-li ki tavo elai.
verses.study_note: The same verb 'vayyett' (turned aside/stretched toward) used in v.1 when Judah diverted his path toward Hirah now recurs: he turns aside toward this veiled woman on the road. The narrator parenthetically protects Judah from the worst construction — 'for he knew not (lo yada) that she was his daughter-in-law' — but this does not excuse him from what he does know: that he is proposing to a woman he believes to be a prostitute. As a recent widower, the patriarchal standard of Abraham and Isaac is far from his behavior. Tamar responds with shrewd business acumen: 'What will you give me?' She is negotiating, yes — but she is negotiating for something specific (v.18) that will serve as irrefutable identification. Her entire transaction is calculated to secure justice, not payment.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, I will send thee a kid from the flock. And she said, Wilt thou give me a pledge, till thou send it?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אָנֹכִי אֲשַׁלַּח גְּדִי־עִזִּים מִן־הַצֹּאן וַתֹּאמֶר אִם־תִּתֵּן עֵרָבוֹן עַד שָׁלְחֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer anokhi ashalach gedi-izzim min-hattzon vatomer im-titten eravon ad shalchekha.
verses.study_note: Judah offers a kid (gedi-izzim — a young goat from the flock). This is the same commodity that will become the instrument of his exposure — he cannot produce the kid through Hirah (v.20), and the pledge he gives instead becomes the evidence of his guilt. The young goat also echoes Jacob's deception of Isaac using goat skins (27:9-16) and the brothers' cover-up of Joseph using a goat's blood (37:31). The goat recurs in Judah's family story as a symbol of deception and exposure. Tamar's request for a pledge (eravon — H6162, a security deposit) is brilliant: she asks for something he cannot easily replace or deny.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, What pledge shall I give thee? And she said, Thy signet, and thy bracelets, and thy staff that is in thine hand. And he gave it her, and came in unto her, and she conceived by him.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מָה הָעֵרָבוֹן אֲשֶׁר אֶתֶּן־לָּךְ וַתֹּאמֶר חֹתָמְךָ וּפְתִילֶךָ וּמַטְּךָ אֲשֶׁר בְּיָדֶךָ וַיִּתֶּן־לָּהּ וַיָּבֹא אֵלֶיהָ וַתַּהַר לוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer mah ha'eravon asher etten-lakh vatomer chotamkha ufetilekha umattekha asher beyadekha vayyitten-lah vayyavo eleihah vattahar lo.
verses.study_note: Tamar's genius: she asks for three items that constitute Judah's personal identity — his signet (chotam — seal ring or cylinder seal used to authenticate documents and transactions, the ancient equivalent of a signature), his cord (petil — the cord or chain on which the signet hung, itself distinctive), and his staff (matteh — a personal, carved walking stick that would be recognizable to anyone who knew Judah). These three items together form an irrefutable identification — like asking a man for his passport, his driver's license, and his fingerprint simultaneously. Tamar is not collecting payment; she is securing the evidence of justice. Judah surrenders all three without hesitation, and she conceives. The narrative moves with compressed speed: the transaction, the union, and the conception are described in three verbs. God's providential purpose is at work even in this morally compromised moment.
verses.doctrinal_note: The signet, cord, and staff: These items function as a covenant seal in the narrative — Judah literally signs himself to Tamar without knowing it. The concept of pledge (eravon) becomes theologically rich in the New Testament: the Holy Spirit Himself is described as God's 'pledge' (arrabon) — the down payment and guarantee of future inheritance (2 Cor 1:22; 5:5; Eph 1:14). God gives Himself as the ultimate surety of His promises.

verses.kjv_text: And she arose, and went away, and laid by her vail from her, and put on the garments of her widowhood.
verses.original_text: וַתָּקָם וַתֵּלֶךְ וַתָּסַר צְעִיפָהּ מֵעָלֶיהָ וַתִּלְבַּשׁ בִּגְדֵי אַלְמְנוּתָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattakam vattelekh vattasar tze'ifah me'aleihah vattilbash bigdei almenutah.
verses.study_note: The reversal of v.14's costume change is narrated with exact symmetry. In v.14 she 'put off' her widow's garments and covered herself with a veil; now she 'puts away' the veil and puts back on her widow's garments. This precision shows that Tamar's act was entirely bounded and purposeful — she did what was necessary and then returned immediately to her legitimate identity. She returns to her father's house with Judah's pledge in her possession and his seed in her womb. The plan has worked. Now she waits for the next act.

verses.kjv_text: And Judah sent the kid by the hand of his friend the Adullamite, to receive his pledge from the woman’s hand: but he found her not.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁלַח יְהוּדָה אֶת־גְּדִי הָעִזִּים בְּיַד רֵעֵהוּ הָעֲדֻלָּמִי לָקַחַת הָעֵרָבוֹן מִיַּד הָאִשָּׁה וְלֹא מְצָאָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishlach Yehudah et-gedi ha'izzim beyad re'ehu ha'Adulami lakachat ha'eravon miyyad ha'ishah velo metza'ah.
verses.study_note: Judah honors his payment obligation — sending the kid through Hirah — but he cannot find the woman. Judah himself does not go to retrieve the pledge, sending his friend instead. This delegation may reflect embarrassment or an attempt to maintain deniability. The failure to find Tamar leaves Judah holding the loss — no pledge recovered, no woman found, and his personal identification items missing. Providence is arranging the circumstances for Judah's exposure.

verses.kjv_text: Then he asked the men of that place, saying, Where is the harlot, that was openly by the way side? And they said, There was no harlot in this place.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁאַל אֶת־אַנְשֵׁי מְקֹמָהּ לֵאמֹר אַיֵּה הַקְּדֵשָׁה הִוא בָעֵינַיִם עַל־הַדָּרֶךְ וַיֹּאמְרוּ לֹא־הָיְתָה בָזֶה קְדֵשָׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyish'al et-anshei mekomah lemor ayyeh hakkdeshah hi ve'einayim al-haddarekh vayyomeru lo-haytah bazeh kdeshah.
verses.study_note: Hirah uses the elevated term 'kedeshah' (קְדֵשָׁה — from 'kadash,' to be holy/set apart) rather than Judah's 'zonah' (prostitute). This more dignified term refers to a cult prostitute associated with pagan temples. Hirah may have used this term to avoid social embarrassment for Judah, or because the veil suggested cultic association. The locals respond that no such woman existed at that place — Tamar was never a real cult prostitute; she was only temporarily in disguise. The community's response is theologically clean: 'there was no kedeshah here.' Tamar has left no trace of her temporary persona. The detail 'in Enaim on the road' (be'einayim al-hadderekh) reiterates the 'eyes' location where Judah failed to see truly (v.14-15).

verses.kjv_text: And he returned to Judah, and said, I cannot find her; and also the men of the place said, that there was no harlot in this place.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשָׁב אֶל־יְהוּדָה וַיֹּאמֶר לֹא מְצָאתִיהָ וְגַם אַנְשֵׁי הַמָּקוֹם אָמְרוּ לֹא־הָיְתָה בָזֶה קְדֵשָׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashav el-Yehudah vayomer lo metza'tiha vegam anshei hammakom ameru lo-haytah vazeh kdeshah.
verses.study_note: Hirah's report confirms the mystery: the woman has vanished and the locals deny any such woman existed. Judah is left with a failed mission, missing personal items, and a growing awareness that something unusual has occurred. The word 'gam' (also) — 'and also the men of the place said' — amplifies the total failure. There is no trail. What Judah does not yet know is that the woman was not a prostitute at all, and that she now carries proof of his identity and his seed.

verses.kjv_text: And Judah said, Let her take it to her, lest we be shamed: behold, I sent this kid, and thou hast not found her.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה תִּקַּח־לָּהּ פֶּן נִהְיֶה לָבוּז הִנֵּה שָׁלַחְתִּי הַגְּדִי הַזֶּה וְאַתָּה לֹא מְצָאתָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yehudah tiqqach-lah pen nihyeh lavuz hinneh shalachti haggedi hazzeh ve'attah lo metza'tah.
verses.study_note: Judah's response reveals his priorities: he is more concerned about shame ('lest we be shamed' — lavuz) than about recovering his signet, cord, and staff. He lets the matter drop, telling Hirah to let the woman keep everything. This is a man trying to bury an embarrassing episode. But his personal identification cannot be buried — it is in Tamar's hands, growing more significant by the day. Judah's attempt at reputation management will soon be spectacularly reversed. He who fears shame is about to face far greater shame than the loss of a pledge.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass about three months after, that it was told Judah, saying, Tamar thy daughter in law hath played the harlot; and also, behold, she is with child by whoredom. And Judah said, Bring her forth, and let her be burnt.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כְּמִשְׁלֹשׁ חֳדָשִׁים וַיֻּגַּד לִיהוּדָה לֵאמֹר זָנְתָה תָּמָר כַּלָּתֶךָ וְגַם הִנֵּה הָרָה לִזְנוּנִים וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה הוֹצִיאוּהָ וְתִשָּׂרֵף׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi kemishlosh chadashim vayyuggad liYhudah lemor zanetah Tamar kallatekha vegam hinneh harah lizenunim vayomer Yehudah hotzi'uha vetissaref.
verses.study_note: Three months have passed — long enough for the pregnancy to become visible and undeniable. Tamar is reported to Judah: 'Tamar your daughter-in-law has played the harlot (zanetah); and behold, she is pregnant by harlotry.' The accusation uses the very word Judah had applied to the veiled woman (zonah, v.15). Judah's response is immediate and furious: 'Bring her out and let her be burned!' Capital punishment by burning — the harshest form of execution in the ancient Near East (Lev 21:9 prescribes this for a priest's daughter who becomes a prostitute). The bitter irony is absolute: Judah condemns Tamar for the very act he himself committed — with her. The man who visited a prostitute by the roadside now pronounces death upon the woman he condemns for harlotry. His patriarchal authority is being exercised to destroy the evidence of his own sin. Jesus' principle cuts to the heart of this scene: 'For with what judgment ye judge, ye shall be judged' (Matt 7:2).

verses.kjv_text: When she was brought forth, she sent to her father in law, saying, By the man, whose these are, am I with child: and she said, Discern, I pray thee, whose are these, the signet, and bracelets, and staff.
verses.original_text: הִוא מוּצֵאת וְהִיא שָׁלְחָה אֶל־חָמִיהָ לֵאמֹר לְאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר־אֵלֶּה לּוֹ אָנֹכִי הָרָה וַתֹּאמֶר הַכֶּר־נָא לְמִי הַחֹתֶמֶת וְהַפְּתִילִים וְהַמַּטֶּה הָאֵלֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Hi mutzet vehi shalechah el-chamiha lemor le'ish asher-elleh lo anokhi harah vatomer hakker-na lemi hachottemet vehapetilim vehamatteh ha'elleh.
verses.study_note: The most dramatic verse in the chapter. Tamar does not cry out her own defense. She does not publicly accuse Judah. She simply sends him the three items — signet, cord, staff — with the quiet, devastating message: 'By the man to whom these belong, I am with child.' And then the three-word Hebrew plea: 'Discern, I pray thee' (hakker-na lemi) — the exact same words (hakker-na) used by Jacob's sons when they brought Joseph's blood-soaked robe and asked their father to identify it (37:32, vayyakkeru). Judah had asked his father to 'recognize' the robe and draw a terrible conclusion; now he is asked to 'recognize' his own possessions and draw another. What he did to his father is being done to him. The signet, cord, and staff function as three witnesses — the biblical requirement for legal judgment (Deut 19:15). Tamar has provided overwhelming evidence. Her self-defense is entirely objective: she makes no emotional appeal, no accusation, no plea for mercy — only evidence.

verses.kjv_text: And Judah acknowledged them, and said, She hath been more righteous than I; because that I gave her not to Shelah my son. And he knew her again no more.
verses.original_text: וַיַּכֵּר יְהוּדָה וַיֹּאמֶר צָדְקָה מִמֶּנִּי כִּי־עַל־כֵּן לֹא־נְתַתִּיהָ לְשֵׁלָה בְנִי וְלֹא־יָסַף עוֹד לְדַעְתָּהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyakker Yehudah vayomer tzadkah mimmenni ki-al-ken lo-netattihah leShela beni velo-yasaf od leda'tah.
verses.study_note: One of the greatest moments of self-recognition in all of Scripture. Judah 'recognized' (vayyakker) his own possessions — using the same root (nakkar) as 'discern' in v.25 and in 37:32-33. The recognition is total and immediate. His response: 'She is more righteous than I' (tzadkah mimmenni). The comparative 'mimmenni' (from me, more than I) is a stunning concession: he does not merely say she is innocent — he declares her morally superior to himself. This is the only verdict in the entire chapter delivered by a human being rather than the narrator, and it is the verdict that the narrator endorses. Judah admits the specific sin: 'because I did not give her to Shelah my son.' He traces the chain of causation — his deception of Tamar drove her to the desperate act for which he almost burned her alive. His final act of restraint — 'he knew her no more' — shows this encounter had a genuinely transforming effect. Judah's recognition here is the beginning of the Judah who will later offer himself for Benjamin (Gen 44:33-34) — the most Christlike act in the patriarchal narratives.
verses.doctrinal_note: Justification and righteousness in unexpected places: Tamar's 'righteousness' (tzedakah) is declared not by God directly but by the patriarch whose verdict matters most in context. This is not moral relativism — it is the recognition that covenant faithfulness (Tamar's intent to secure the covenant seed line) can manifest in unconventional forms. The verb 'vadkah' (she is righteous) shares the root of the great Pauline term 'dikaiosune' (righteousness, justification). God declares righteous those whose faith — however imperfectly expressed — clings to His covenant purposes. Tamar joins a remarkable company: Rahab the harlot (Josh 2), Ruth the Moabitess, and others whose unconventional stories are woven into the Messianic line.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass in the time of her travail, that, behold, twins were in her womb.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בְּעֵת לִדְתָּהּ וְהִנֵּה תְאוֹמִים בְּבִטְנָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi be'et lidetah vehineh te'omim bevitnah.
verses.study_note: The birth reveals another divine surprise: twins (te'omim). The same word is used in the Song of Solomon for the beloved's physical beauty (4:5; 7:3) and for the twin fawns of a gazelle — but most significantly, twins echo Isaac's sons Jacob and Esau (Gen 25:24). That narrative also featured a birth-order reversal (25:22-26, Esau first then Jacob grabbing the heel). The parallel prepares the reader for the reversal about to unfold. God has consistently worked through second sons and unexpected reversals: Abel over Cain, Shem over Ham's descendants, Isaac over Ishmael, Jacob over Esau, Joseph over Reuben. Now Perez over Zerah.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when she travailed, that the one put out his hand: and the midwife took and bound upon his hand a scarlet thread, saying, This came out first.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בְלִדְתָּהּ וַיִּתֶּן־יָד וַתִּקַּח הַמְיַלֶּדֶת וַתִּקְשֹׁר עַל־יָדוֹ שָׁנִי לֵאמֹר זֶה יָצָא רִאשֹׁנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi beledetah vayyitten-yad vattiqqach hameyalledet vattikshor al-yado shani lemor zeh yatza rishonah.
verses.study_note: The midwife's action is swift and decisive: seeing the hand emerge first, she immediately ties a scarlet thread (shani — a crimson/bright red thread used in priestly and Nazirite purity rites, Ex 28:28; Lev 14:4) to identify the firstborn. The scarlet thread (shani) becomes one of the most typologically rich details in Genesis. It anticipates the scarlet cord (tiqvat chut hashani) by which Rahab identified her household for safety in Jericho (Joshua 2:18, 21) — also a crimson thread on a hand/window that marked a household for preservation from judgment. Both threads mark the preserved remnant in a moment of crisis. The color 'shani' (scarlet) appears throughout the Tabernacle furnishings and priestly garments as a color associated with both blood sacrifice and divine service.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, as he drew back his hand, that, behold, his brother came out: and she said, How hast thou broken forth? this breach be upon thee: therefore his name was called Pharez.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כְּמֵשִׁיב יָדוֹ וְהִנֵּה יָצָא אָחִיו וַתֹּאמֶר מַה־פָּרַצְתָּ עָלֶיךָ פָּרֶץ וַיִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ פָּרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi kemeshiv yado vehineh yatza achiv vatomer mah-paratztah aleikha parets vayyikra shemo Parets.
verses.study_note: The hand withdraws — and the other brother bursts out first. The midwife's exclamation captures the shock: 'What a breach you have broken out!' (mah-paratztah aleikha parets). The word 'parats' (to break through, burst out, breach) becomes his name: Perez (פֶּרֶץ). This is the supreme reversal of the chapter — the one without the scarlet thread, the one not designated firstborn, forces his way through first. But the reversal is not a mistake; it is Providence. Perez becomes the direct ancestor of David (Ruth 4:18-22) and therefore of Jesus Christ (Matthew 1:3). The 'breach' child, the unexpected one, the one who pushed through against expectation, carries the Messianic line. God's ways consistently involve breakthrough where human arrangements would have expected order. The Messiah Himself is described as 'the one who breaks open the way' (hamepharetz, Micah 2:13) — the same root as Perez.
verses.doctrinal_note: Perez — the Breakthrough Child in the Messianic Line: The name Perez ('breach/breakthrough') becomes one of the most significant names in redemptive history. Ruth 4:12 — 'May your house be like the house of Perez.' Matthew 1:3 — Perez is listed in the genealogy of Jesus Christ. Micah 2:13 describes the coming Deliverer as 'the one who breaks open the way' (hammepharetz) — the same root. God chooses the one who bursts through expected order to carry the line of the King of kings. The theology of election consistently favors the unexpected: Abel over Cain, Isaac over Ishmael, Jacob over Esau, Joseph over Reuben, David over his brothers, and now Perez over Zerah.

verses.kjv_text: And afterward came out his brother, that had the scarlet thread upon his hand: and his name was called Zarah.
verses.original_text: וְאַחַר יָצָא אָחִיו אֲשֶׁר עַל־יָדוֹ הַשָּׁנִי וַיִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ זָרַח׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'achar yatza achiv asher al-yado hashani vayyikra shemo Zarach.
verses.study_note: Zerah (זָרַח — 'brightness/dawning/rising') carries the scarlet thread but was born second despite being marked first. His name suggests the sunrise — light dawning — and yet his brother broke through before the dawn arrived. The scarlet thread on his hand has a haunting typological echo in Rahab's scarlet cord (Joshua 2:18-21): both involve a crimson thread marking a line for preservation, both appear at moments of crisis, and both point beyond themselves to the blood that marks God's redeemed. Zerah appears in 1 Chronicles 2:6 with five sons of renown. His line and Perez's line both contribute to Israel's story. The chapter ends with two names — Perez and Zerah — but the entire narrative arc of Scripture follows Perez, through Hezron, Ram, Amminadab, Nahshon, Salmon, Boaz, Obed, Jesse, David, and finally to Jesus Christ the Son of David. The 'breach' at Tamar's delivery becomes the opening through which the Redeemer of the world enters human history.
verses.doctrinal_note: The scarlet thread of Zerah and Rahab's cord: The scarlet thread (shani) on Zerah's hand anticipates the scarlet cord (tiqvat chut hashani — Joshua 2:18) that Rahab hung from her window as a sign of covenant protection. Both threads mark a household set apart for preservation — one at birth, one before conquest. The crimson color points theologically to blood — the blood that marks the redeemed (Exod 12), the blood of atonement (Lev 17:11), and ultimately the blood of Christ (Heb 9:14). The entire chapter of Genesis 38 — morally dark, theologically uncomfortable — is, at its deepest level, a chapter about the sovereign grace of God threading the scarlet line of redemption through the most broken of human circumstances, bending even sin and failure toward the coming of the Promised Seed.

meta.overview: Genesis 38 is one of the most theologically dense and structurally deliberate chapters in the Bible. It appears to interrupt the Joseph narrative (Gen 37 and 39) but this placement is entirely intentional: while Joseph is being carried into Egypt, God is simultaneously working through — and in spite of — the moral failures of Judah, the very tribe through whom the Messiah will come. The chapter chronicles Judah's descent after leaving his brothers: his Canaanite marriage, the divine judgment on his wicked sons Er and Onan, his deception of his daughter-in-law Tamar, and Tamar's extraordinary act of covenant loyalty that forces Judah's self-recognition. The climax — Judah's confession 'She is more righteous than I' (v.26) — is one of the most remarkable admissions in all of Scripture. The chapter closes with the birth of twins Perez and Zerah, whose very birth involves a reversal of expected order — Perez ('breach') bursting through to become the direct ancestor of David and ultimately of Jesus Christ (Matthew 1:3, Ruth 4:18-22). Far from an embarrassing interruption, this chapter is a theological masterpiece revealing that God accomplishes His redemptive purposes not only through the faithful but through the weak, the sinful, and the unexpected.
meta.theme: Covenant Failure, Righteousness in Unlikely Places & God's Sovereign Grace in the Messianic Line
meta.keyWordMeaning: She is righteous / more righteous — the moral verdict that overturns all human expectations and reveals God's unconventional pathway to the Messiah
outline: The Descent of Judah: Canaanite Marriage & Three Sons (vv.1-5): Judah separates, marries a Canaanite, begets Er, Onan, and Shelah
Tamar Acquires a Husband and Then Loses Him (v.6-7): Er married to Tamar; Er judged by God
The Levirate Duty and Onan's Sin (vv.8-10): Judah commands Onan; Onan's calculated treachery; God's judgment
Judah's Deception of Tamar (vv.11): Shelah withheld; Tamar returned to her father's house
Tamar's Bold Plan: Disguise and Pledge (vv.12-19): Judah goes to Timnath; Tamar takes matters into her own hands
The Search, the Cover-up & the Reckoning (vv.20-26): Judah seeks the pledge; Tamar identified; Judah's confession
The Birth of Perez and Zerah: The Messianic Breach (vv.27-30): Twins born; scarlet thread; Perez breaks through

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph was brought down to Egypt; and Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh, captain of the guard, an Egyptian, bought him of the hands of the Ishmeelites, which had brought him down thither.
verses.original_text: וְיוֹסֵף הוּרַד מִצְרָיְמָה וַיִּקְנֵהוּ פּוֹטִיפַר סְרִיס פַּרְעֹה שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים אִישׁ מִצְרִי מִיַּד הַיִּשְׁמְעֵאלִים אֲשֶׁר הוֹרִדֻהוּ שָׁמָּה׃
verses.transliteration: VeYosef hurad Mitzraymah vayyikneihu Potifar seris Par'oh sar hattabbachim ish Mitzri miyyad haYishme'elim asher horiduhu shammah.
verses.study_note: The narrative resumes from 37:36 after the Judah-Tamar interlude (ch.38). 'Was brought down' (hurad) — the passive Hophal form emphasizes that Joseph did not go willingly; he was forcibly taken down. The verb 'yarad' (to go down) is theologically loaded: Jacob will later 'go down' to Egypt (46:3-4), and Israel will 'go down' into bondage. Yet in God's sovereignty, going down becomes the path to exaltation. Potiphar is identified with three titles: 'seris' (officer/official), 'sar hattabbachim' (captain of the guard, literally 'chief of the slaughterers/executioners'), and 'ish Mitzri' (an Egyptian man) — emphasizing Joseph is now in a thoroughly pagan environment under a powerful military official.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD was with Joseph, and he was a prosperous man; and he was in the house of his master the Egyptian.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי יְהוָה אֶת־יוֹסֵף וַיְהִי אִישׁ מַצְלִיחַ וַיְהִי בְּבֵית אֲדֹנָיו הַמִּצְרִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi YHVH et-Yosef vayehi ish matzliach vayehi beveit adonav haMitzri.
verses.study_note: The first and most important statement of the chapter: 'the LORD was with Joseph' (YHVH et-Yosef). This refrain (repeated in vv.3,21,23) is the theological key to everything that follows. Joseph is a slave, yet he is called 'ish matzliach' — a prospering/successful man. The Hebrew 'matzliach' (from 'tzalach') means to push forward, to advance, to cause to prosper. The threefold 'vayehi' (and he was) creates a rhythmic emphasis: the LORD was with him, he was prospering, he was in his master's house. Prosperity in Scripture is defined not by circumstances but by God's presence.
verses.doctrinal_note: The presence of God is not contingent on circumstances. Joseph is a slave — sold, betrayed, exiled — yet 'the LORD was with him.' This is the foundational truth of biblical suffering: God's presence does not remove hardship but transforms it. The same truth sustains David (1 Sam 16:18), Daniel (Dan 6:22), and ultimately defines the Incarnation itself: 'God with us' (Matt 1:23). Prosperity in God's economy is measured by divine presence, not material comfort.

verses.kjv_text: And his master saw that the LORD was with him, and that the LORD made all that he did to prosper in his hand.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא אֲדֹנָיו כִּי יְהוָה אִתּוֹ וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר־הוּא עֹשֶׂה יְהוָה מַצְלִיחַ בְּיָדוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyar adonav ki YHVH itto vekhol asher-hu oseh YHVH matzliach beyado.
verses.study_note: A remarkable statement: Potiphar, a pagan Egyptian, 'saw that the LORD was with him.' The divine name YHVH is used — not a generic 'god' but the covenant God of Israel. God's presence in Joseph was so visible that even a pagan recognized it. 'In his hand' (beyado) — everything Joseph touched prospered. This visible blessing serves God's purposes: it elevates Joseph to a position from which he can later save his family and the nations. The phrase 'the LORD made all that he did to prosper' attributes the success entirely to God, not to Joseph's natural abilities.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph found grace in his sight, and he served him: and he made him overseer over his house, and all that he had he put into his hand.
verses.original_text: וַיִּמְצָא יוֹסֵף חֵן בְּעֵינָיו וַיְשָׁרֶת אֹתוֹ וַיַּפְקִדֵהוּ עַל־בֵּיתוֹ וְכָל־יֶשׁ־לוֹ נָתַן בְּיָדוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyimtza Yosef chen be'einav vayesharet oto vayyafkideihu al-beto vekhol-yesh-lo natan beyado.
verses.study_note: Joseph 'found grace' (chen) in Potiphar's eyes — the same language used of Noah (Gen 6:8). 'He served him' (vayesharet) — from 'sharet,' meaning to minister or serve in a distinguished capacity (the same word used for priestly ministry, Num 3:6). Joseph's service was not grudging slavery but excellent ministry. Potiphar responds by making Joseph 'overseer' (vayyafkideihu — from 'paqad,' to appoint, commission) over his entire household. The pattern of Joseph rising to second-in-command repeats here (Potiphar's house), in the prison (v.22), and ultimately in all Egypt (41:40).

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass from the time that he had made him overseer in his house, and over all that he had, that the LORD blessed the Egyptian’s house for Joseph’s sake; and the blessing of the LORD was upon all that he had in the house, and in the field.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי מֵאָז הִפְקִיד אֹתוֹ בְּבֵיתוֹ וְעַל כָּל־אֲשֶׁר יֶשׁ־לוֹ וַיְבָרֶךְ יְהוָה אֶת־בֵּית הַמִּצְרִי בִּגְלַל יוֹסֵף וַיְהִי בִּרְכַּת יְהוָה בְּכָל־אֲשֶׁר יֶשׁ־לוֹ בַּבַּיִת וּבַשָּׂדֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi me'az hifkid oto beveto ve'al kol-asher yesh-lo vayevarekh YHVH et-beit haMitzri biglal Yosef vayehi birkat YHVH bekhol-asher yesh-lo babbayit uvasadeh.
verses.study_note: A critical theological principle: 'the LORD blessed the Egyptian's house FOR JOSEPH'S SAKE' (biglal Yosef). God's blessing on Joseph overflows to those around him — even a pagan household. This 'mediatorial blessing' pattern runs through Scripture: God blesses Laban for Jacob's sake (30:27), the world through Abraham's seed (12:3), and ultimately all nations through Christ. 'In the house and in the field' — the blessing is comprehensive, covering domestic and agricultural domains.
verses.doctrinal_note: The principle of mediatorial blessing: God's covenant people become channels of blessing to others. As Abraham was blessed to be a blessing (Gen 12:2-3), Joseph becomes a conduit of divine favor to the Gentile world. This pattern culminates in Christ, through whom 'all the families of the earth' are blessed (Gen 12:3, Gal 3:14). The righteous person's presence sanctifies their environment (cf. 1 Cor 7:14).

verses.kjv_text: And he left all that he had in Joseph’s hand; and he knew not ought he had, save the bread which he did eat. And Joseph was a goodly person, and well favoured.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעֲזֹב כָּל־אֲשֶׁר־לוֹ בְּיַד־יוֹסֵף וְלֹא־יָדַע אִתּוֹ מְאוּמָה כִּי אִם־הַלֶּחֶם אֲשֶׁר־הוּא אוֹכֵל וַיְהִי יוֹסֵף יְפֵה־תֹאַר וִיפֵה מַרְאֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'azov kol-asher-lo beyad-Yosef velo-yada itto me'umah ki im-hallechem asher-hu okhel vayehi Yosef yefeh-to'ar vifeh mar'eh.
verses.study_note: Potiphar entrusts EVERYTHING to Joseph except 'the bread which he did eat' — possibly a reference to Egyptian dietary laws that prohibited eating with Hebrews (cf. Gen 43:32). The Hebrew 'lo-yada... me'umah' (he did not know anything) echoes Adam's innocence — Potiphar is carefree. Then the narrator pivots sharply: 'And Joseph was a goodly person, and well favoured' (yefeh-to'ar vifeh mar'eh — beautiful in form and beautiful in appearance). This exact phrase was used of his mother Rachel (29:17). The description sets up the temptation scene — Joseph's physical beauty becomes the occasion for moral testing.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass after these things, that his master’s wife cast her eyes upon Joseph; and she said, Lie with me.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה וַתִּשָּׂא אֵשֶׁת־אֲדֹנָיו אֶת־עֵינֶיהָ אֶל־יוֹסֵף וַתֹּאמֶר שִׁכְבָה עִמִּי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi achar haddevarim ha'elleh vattissa eshet-adonav et-eineha el-Yosef vattomer shikhvah immi.
verses.study_note: The temptation arrives bluntly. 'Cast her eyes upon' (vattissa... et-eineha) — literally 'she lifted her eyes toward' — a predatory, calculating gaze. The demand 'lie with me' (shikhvah immi) is shockingly direct — two words in Hebrew, stripped of all pretense. She is identified only as 'his master's wife' (eshet-adonav), never by name — her identity is defined by the trust she violates. The phrase 'after these things' suggests the temptation comes after Joseph has been established in his position — testing often follows seasons of blessing and stability.

verses.kjv_text: But he refused, and said unto his master’s wife, Behold, my master wotteth not what is with me in the house, and he hath committed all that he hath to my hand;
verses.original_text: וַיְמָאֵן וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל־אֵשֶׁת אֲדֹנָיו הֵן אֲדֹנִי לֹא־יָדַע אִתִּי מַה־בַּבָּיִת וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר־יֶשׁ־לוֹ נָתַן בְּיָדִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayema'en vayomer el-eshet adonav hen adoni lo-yada itti mah-babbayit vekhol asher-yesh-lo natan beyadi.
verses.study_note: Joseph's response begins with 'he refused' (vayema'en) — a flat, decisive refusal. The Hebrew is emphatic: he refused categorically before giving reasons. His argument develops in three stages: (1) Trust — his master knows nothing of the house because he trusts Joseph completely; (2) Authority — everything is given into Joseph's hand; (3) Therefore, to violate this trust would be betrayal of both man and God. Joseph reasons morally, not emotionally. He does not say 'I don't find you attractive' — he says 'this trust is sacred.'

verses.kjv_text: There is none greater in this house than I; neither hath he kept back any thing from me but thee, because thou art his wife: how then can I do this great wickedness, and sin against God?
verses.original_text: אֵינֶנּוּ גָדוֹל בַּבַּיִת הַזֶּה מִמֶּנִּי וְלֹא־חָשַׂךְ מִמֶּנִּי מְאוּמָה כִּי אִם־אוֹתָךְ בַּאֲשֶׁר אַתְּ אִשְׁתּוֹ וְאֵיךְ אֶעֱשֶׂה הָרָעָה הַגְּדֹלָה הַזֹּאת וְחָטָאתִי לֵאלֹהִים׃
verses.transliteration: Einennu gadol babbayit hazzeh mimmenni velo-chasakh mimmenni me'umah ki im-otakh ba'asher att ishto ve'eikh e'eseh hara'ah hagedolah hazzo't vechatati lElohim.
verses.study_note: The climax of Joseph's refusal — and one of the greatest theological statements in Genesis. Three arguments: (1) No one is greater in this house — Joseph acknowledges his authority; (2) Nothing is withheld except you, because you are his wife — Joseph names the sacred boundary of marriage; (3) 'How then can I do this great wickedness, and sin against God?' (ve'eikh e'eseh hara'ah hagedolah hazzo't vechatati lElohim). Joseph elevates the issue from social ethics to theology. This is not merely adultery against Potiphar — it is 'sin against God' (chatati lElohim). The word 'hara'ah' (wickedness, evil) is the definite article with the adjective 'great' — THE great evil. Joseph sees sexual sin as fundamentally an offense against the Creator. This anticipates David's confession: 'Against thee, thee only, have I sinned' (Ps 51:4).
verses.doctrinal_note: Joseph's theological vision: all sin is ultimately against God. The horizontal violation (betraying Potiphar) is subsumed under the vertical reality (sinning against Elohim). This is the foundational ethic of holiness: we resist sin not primarily because of social consequences but because God is holy and is watching. Joseph's clarity here — with no written Law, no community of believers, no accountability partner — comes from an internalized knowledge of God's character. This is what the New Testament calls the 'law written on the heart' (Rom 2:15).

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, as she spake to Joseph day by day, that he hearkened not unto her, to lie by her, or to be with her.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כְּדַבְּרָהּ אֶל־יוֹסֵף יוֹם יוֹם וְלֹא־שָׁמַע אֵלֶיהָ לִשְׁכַּב אֶצְלָהּ לִהְיוֹת עִמָּהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi kedabberah el-Yosef yom yom velo-shama eleha lishkav etzlah lihyot immah.
verses.study_note: The temptation was not a one-time event but a daily siege: 'day by day' (yom yom). Persistent temptation reveals persistent character. Joseph 'hearkened not' (lo-shama) — the same verb used for obeying God's commandments. He refused three things: (1) 'to lie by her' (lishkav etzlah) — the sexual act itself, (2) 'to be with her' (lihyot immah) — even innocent proximity that could lead to temptation. Joseph practiced what Paul would later teach: 'Flee also youthful lusts' (2 Tim 2:22). He avoided not only the sin but the occasion of sin.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass about this time, that Joseph went into the house to do his business; and there was none of the men of the house there within.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כְּהַיּוֹם הַזֶּה וַיָּבֹא הַבַּיְתָה לַעֲשׂוֹת מְלַאכְתּוֹ וְאֵין אִישׁ מֵאַנְשֵׁי הַבַּיִת שָׁם בַּבָּיִת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi kehayom hazzeh vayyavo habbayetah la'asot melakhto ve'ein ish me'anshei habbayit sham babbayit.
verses.study_note: The crisis moment. Joseph enters the house 'to do his business' (la'asot melakhto — literally 'to do his work') — he is not seeking temptation but fulfilling his duty. 'There was none of the men of the house there' — complete isolation, the perfect setup. The narrative tension is extreme. Potiphar's wife has likely engineered this opportunity (cf. v.10, the persistent daily attempts). The word 'melakhto' (his work) uses the same root as 'angel' (malakh) — work as sacred service.

verses.kjv_text: And she caught him by his garment, saying, Lie with me: and he left his garment in her hand, and fled, and got him out.
verses.original_text: וַתִּתְפְּשֵׂהוּ בְּבִגְדוֹ לֵאמֹר שִׁכְבָה עִמִּי וַיַּעֲזֹב בִּגְדוֹ בְּיָדָהּ וַיָּנָס וַיֵּצֵא הַחוּצָה׃
