verses.transliteration: Vattitpesehu bevigdo lemor shikhvah immi vayya'azov bigdo beyadah vayyanas vayyetze hachutzah.
verses.study_note: She seizes his garment (bevigdo) — physical aggression. This is the second time Joseph's garment plays a pivotal role in his suffering: his brothers stripped his coat of many colors (37:23) and used it as false evidence to Jacob; now Potiphar's wife seizes his garment and will use it as false evidence to Potiphar. Both times, Joseph loses his garment and is condemned. Joseph's response is decisive: 'he fled and got him out' (vayyanas vayyetze hachutzah). Three verbs in rapid succession — left, fled, went out. This is not cowardice but wisdom. When temptation grabs hold, the only righteous response is to flee (cf. 2 Tim 2:22, 1 Cor 6:18).
verses.doctrinal_note: The garment motif in Joseph's life is deeply typological. Each stripping leads to a deeper descent (pit, slavery, prison) but ultimately to greater exaltation. Christ likewise was stripped of His garments at the cross (Matt 27:28,35) — the ultimate unjust suffering leading to ultimate exaltation. Joseph's willingness to lose his garment rather than his integrity mirrors the principle: 'What shall it profit a man, if he shall gain the whole world, and lose his own soul?' (Mark 8:36).

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when she saw that he had left his garment in her hand, and was fled forth,
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כִּרְאוֹתָהּ כִּי־עָזַב בִּגְדוֹ בְּיָדָהּ וַיָּנָס הַחוּצָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi kir'otah ki-azav bigdo beyadah vayyanas hachutzah.
verses.study_note: The turning point. Potiphar's wife holds the garment — the physical evidence she will weaponize. 'When she saw' (kir'otah) — her perception shifts from desire to revenge. Joseph's garment in her hand becomes a parallel to Joseph's coat in his brothers' hands (37:31-32). In both cases, a garment is used to construct a false narrative. The text pauses to let the reader feel the weight of what comes next: rejected desire turns to destructive fury.

verses.kjv_text: That she called unto the men of her house, and spake unto them, saying, See, he hath brought in an Hebrew unto us to mock us; he came in unto me to lie with me, and I cried with a loud voice:
verses.original_text: וַתִּקְרָא לְאַנְשֵׁי בֵיתָהּ וַתֹּאמֶר לָהֶם לֵאמֹר רְאוּ הֵבִיא לָנוּ אִישׁ עִבְרִי לְצַחֶק בָּנוּ בָּא אֵלַי לִשְׁכַּב עִמִּי וָאֶקְרָא בְּקוֹל גָּדוֹל׃
verses.transliteration: Vattikra le'anshei vetah vattomer lahem lemor re'u hevi lanu ish Ivri letzachek banu ba elai lishkav immi va'ekra bekol gadol.
verses.study_note: Her accusation is masterfully deceptive. Note her rhetorical strategy: (1) She calls the household servants first — establishing her narrative before Potiphar hears. (2) She blames Potiphar: 'HE brought in a Hebrew to mock us' — distancing herself and creating solidarity with the servants against both Potiphar and Joseph. (3) She uses the ethnic slur 'ish Ivri' (Hebrew man) — invoking class prejudice. (4) 'To mock us' (letzachek banu) — the same root as 'Isaac' (tzachak), used earlier in the Ishmael incident (21:9). (5) She inverts the truth completely: the pursuer claims to be the victim.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when he heard that I lifted up my voice and cried, that he left his garment with me, and fled, and got him out.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כְשָׁמְעוֹ כִּי־הֲרִימֹתִי קוֹלִי וָאֶקְרָא וַיַּעֲזֹב בִּגְדוֹ אֶצְלִי וַיָּנָס וַיֵּצֵא הַחוּצָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi kheshom'o ki-harimoti koli va'ekra vayya'azov bigdo etzli vayyanas vayyetze hachutzah.
verses.study_note: She uses Joseph's flight as proof of guilt — the very evidence of his innocence (he fled from sin) is twisted into evidence of his guilt (he fled because he was caught). The garment 'left with me' (etzli) becomes the planted evidence. The irony is painful: Joseph left his garment to preserve his honor, and the garment is used to destroy his honor. Truth is inverted at every level. This pattern of false accusation against the righteous recurs throughout Scripture and culminates at Christ's trial.

verses.kjv_text: And she laid up his garment by her, until his lord came home.
verses.original_text: וַתַּנַּח בִּגְדוֹ אֶצְלָהּ עַד־בּוֹא אֲדֹנָיו אֶל־בֵּיתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattannach bigdo etzlah ad-bo adonav el-beto.
verses.study_note: She 'laid up' (vattannach — placed, set down) the garment beside her — preserving the 'evidence' for Potiphar. The verb suggests deliberate, calculated staging. She waits for 'his lord' (adonav) — the same word used for Joseph's master throughout. The premeditation is clear: she has rehearsed her story with the servants (v.14) and now prepares the physical evidence for the master. The cold deliberation contrasts sharply with Joseph's honest, impulsive flight.

verses.kjv_text: And she spake unto him according to these words, saying, The Hebrew servant, which thou hast brought unto us, came in unto me to mock me:
verses.original_text: וַתְּדַבֵּר אֵלָיו כַּדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה לֵאמֹר בָּא־אֵלַי הָעֶבֶד הָעִבְרִי אֲשֶׁר־הֵבֵאתָ לָּנוּ לְצַחֶק בִּי׃
verses.transliteration: Vattedabber elav kaddevarim ha'elleh lemor ba-elai ha'eved ha'Ivri asher-heveta lanu letzachek bi.
verses.study_note: She repeats the accusation to Potiphar with modifications: to the servants she said 'Hebrew man' (ish Ivri); to Potiphar she says 'the Hebrew SLAVE' (ha'eved ha'Ivri) — further demeaning Joseph. To the servants she said 'to mock US'; to Potiphar she says 'to mock ME' — making it personal. She again blames Potiphar: 'which THOU hast brought unto us' — a veiled accusation that this is his fault. The accusation is a masterclass in manipulation: blame-shifting, status degradation, and emotional weaponization.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, as I lifted up my voice and cried, that he left his garment with me, and fled out.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כַּהֲרִימִי קוֹלִי וָאֶקְרָא וַיַּעֲזֹב בִּגְדוֹ אֶצְלִי וַיָּנָס הַחוּצָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi kaharimi koli va'ekra vayya'azov bigdo etzli vayyanas hachutzah.
verses.study_note: The third telling of the same lie (vv.14-15, 17, 18) — repetition reinforcing the false narrative. The garment is presented as proof. The parallels to Genesis 37 are unmistakable: in both chapters a garment is stripped from Joseph, blood/false evidence is added, and the garment is presented to a father figure as proof of a lie. Joseph's brothers used goat blood on his robe; Potiphar's wife uses the garment itself. Both times, the deception succeeds in the short term.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when his master heard the words of his wife, which she spake unto him, saying, After this manner did thy servant to me; that his wrath was kindled.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כִשְׁמֹעַ אֲדֹנָיו אֶת־דִּבְרֵי אִשְׁתּוֹ אֲשֶׁר דִּבְּרָה אֵלָיו לֵאמֹר כַּדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה עָשָׂה לִי עַבְדֶּךָ וַיִּחַר אַפּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi khishmo'a adonav et-divrei ishto asher dibberah elav lemor kaddevarim ha'elleh asah li avdekha vayyichar appo.
verses.study_note: Potiphar's 'wrath was kindled' (vayyichar appo — literally 'his nose burned'). Yet several details suggest Potiphar may have doubted his wife: (1) Joseph is sent to prison, not executed — the expected punishment for a slave assaulting the master's wife was death; (2) He is sent to the 'king's prison' (v.20), a royal facility, not a slave dungeon; (3) The text says his anger 'burned' but does not specify against whom — it may include anger at the situation or even at his wife. Potiphar is caught between his wife's word and the evidence of Joseph's years of faithful service.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph’s master took him, and put him into the prison, a place where the king’s prisoners were bound: and he was there in the prison.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח אֲדֹנֵי יוֹסֵף אֹתוֹ וַיִּתְּנֵהוּ אֶל־בֵּית הַסֹּהַר מְקוֹם אֲשֶׁר־אֲסִירֵי הַמֶּלֶךְ אֲסוּרִים וַיְהִי־שָׁם בְּבֵית הַסֹּהַר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikkach adonei Yosef oto vayyitteneihu el-beit hassohar mekom asher-asirei hammelekh asurim vayehi-sham beveit hassohar.
verses.study_note: Joseph descends again: from favored son to pit, from pit to slave, from slave to prison. Yet God is providentially ordering every step. The 'prison' (beit hassohar — literally 'house of roundness/enclosure') is specifically the royal prison where 'the king's prisoners' are held. This is significant: in this specific prison Joseph will meet Pharaoh's butler and baker (ch.40), who will be the chain-link to Pharaoh himself (ch.41). What appears as the lowest point is actually a divine positioning. The phrase 'and he was there in the prison' echoes 'and he was in the house of his master' (v.2) — wherever Joseph is, the next verse reveals, the LORD is with him.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's sovereignty in suffering: Joseph's path — beloved son → pit → slavery → false accusation → prison — mirrors Christ's descent: beloved Son → incarnation → servant → false accusation → cross. Both descents are divinely orchestrated paths to exaltation and salvation for many. Joseph's unjust imprisonment places him exactly where God needs him to fulfill His redemptive plan. 'You meant it for evil, but God meant it for good' (Gen 50:20) is already being worked out.

verses.kjv_text: But the LORD was with Joseph, and shewed him mercy, and gave him favour in the sight of the keeper of the prison.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי יְהוָה אֶת־יוֹסֵף וַיֵּט אֵלָיו חָסֶד וַיִּתֵּן חִנּוֹ בְּעֵינֵי שַׂר בֵּית־הַסֹּהַר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi YHVH et-Yosef vayyet elav chesed vayyitten chinno be'einei sar beit-hassohar.
verses.study_note: The refrain returns with glorious force: 'But the LORD was with Joseph' (vayehi YHVH et-Yosef). The 'but' (va — a simple conjunction used adversatively) marks the reversal: prison BUT God. Two divine acts follow: (1) 'showed him mercy' (vayyet elav chesed — literally 'inclined/extended chesed toward him'). 'Chesed' — God's covenant lovingkindness — reaches into the prison. (2) 'Gave him favour' (vayyitten chinno — gave his grace/charm) in the eyes of the prison keeper. The same pattern as v.4 (Potiphar's house) now repeats in prison. The structure is unmistakable: wherever Joseph goes — house or prison — YHVH is with him, chesed is shown, and favor is given. This is the pattern of irresistible grace.
verses.doctrinal_note: The persistence of grace: God's chesed (covenant mercy) follows Joseph from prosperity into prison. The LORD's presence is not location-dependent or circumstance-dependent. This is the strongest Old Testament anticipation of Romans 8:38-39: 'neither death, nor life... shall be able to separate us from the love of God.' Joseph in prison with God's chesed is better off than Potiphar's wife in a mansion without it.

verses.kjv_text: And the keeper of the prison committed to Joseph’s hand all the prisoners that were in the prison; and whatsoever they did there, he was the doer of it.
verses.original_text: וַיִּתֵּן שַׂר בֵּית־הַסֹּהַר בְּיַד־יוֹסֵף אֵת כָּל־הָאֲסִירִם אֲשֶׁר בְּבֵית הַסֹּהַר וְאֵת כָּל־אֲשֶׁר עֹשִׂים שָׁם הוּא הָיָה עֹשֶׂה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitten sar beit-hassohar beyad-Yosef et kol-ha'asirim asher beveit hassohar ve'et kol-asher osim sham hu hayah oseh.
verses.study_note: The pattern repeats with remarkable precision: just as Potiphar put everything in Joseph's hand (v.4-6), the prison keeper puts all prisoners and all operations in Joseph's hand. Joseph rises to second-in-command — again. This threefold pattern (Potiphar's house → prison → all Egypt) reveals God's method: faithfulness in small things leads to authority in greater things (cf. Luke 16:10). 'He was the doer of it' (hu hayah oseh) — Joseph was the effective administrator behind everything. The pattern is practice for his ultimate role: second only to Pharaoh over all Egypt.

verses.kjv_text: The keeper of the prison looked not to any thing that was under his hand; because the LORD was with him, and that which he did, the LORD made it to prosper.
verses.original_text: אֵין שַׂר בֵּית־הַסֹּהַר רֹאֶה אֶת־כָּל־מְאוּמָה בְּיָדוֹ בַּאֲשֶׁר יְהוָה אִתּוֹ וַאֲשֶׁר־הוּא עֹשֶׂה יְהוָה מַצְלִיחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Ein sar beit-hassohar ro'eh et-kol-me'umah beyado ba'asher YHVH itto va'asher-hu oseh YHVH matzliach.
verses.study_note: The chapter's closing verse is almost verbatim the language of vv.3-6 — a deliberate literary echo. In Potiphar's house: 'his master saw that the LORD was with him... the LORD made all that he did to prosper' (v.3). In prison: 'the keeper of the prison looked not to any thing... because the LORD was with him, and that which he did, the LORD made it to prosper.' The structural parallel declares: nothing has changed in God's relationship with Joseph. Prison did not diminish God's presence. False accusation did not revoke God's blessing. Suffering did not interrupt God's purpose. The chapter that began with Joseph 'brought down' (v.1) ends with Joseph prospering in chains — because the LORD was with him.
verses.doctrinal_note: The closing refrain completes the chapter's theological architecture. Four times the narrative declares 'the LORD was with Joseph' (vv.2,3,21,23) — framing both his prosperity AND his suffering in divine presence. This is the answer to the ancient question: Why do the righteous suffer? Not because God is absent, but because God is present and purposeful in the suffering. Joseph in prison, blessed by God, is being prepared for the throne. The prison is not a detour from God's plan — it IS God's plan. Psalm 105:17-19 confirms: 'He sent a man before them, even Joseph, who was sold for a servant: whose feet they hurt with fetters... until the time that his word came: the word of the LORD tried him.' The trial was the preparation.

meta.overview: Genesis 39 resumes Joseph's story after the Judah-Tamar interlude of chapter 38. Sold into Egypt by his brothers, Joseph enters the household of Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh and captain of the guard. The chapter is structured around a repeated refrain: 'the LORD was with Joseph' (vv.2,3,21,23). Despite slavery, false accusation, and imprisonment, God's sovereign presence never departs. Joseph rises to prominence in Potiphar's house, is tempted by Potiphar's wife, resists with theological clarity ('how then can I do this great wickedness, and sin against God?'), is falsely accused, and is cast into prison — where the LORD is with him again. The chapter is one of the most powerful typological portraits of Christ in the Old Testament: the beloved son, sold by his brothers, falsely accused, unjustly condemned, yet exalted by God through suffering. It also establishes the theological foundation that faithfulness to God does not guarantee worldly comfort but guarantees God's presence and ultimate vindication.
meta.theme: God's Sovereign Presence in Suffering & the Integrity of Faith
meta.keyWordMeaning: The LORD was with him — the refrain that frames the entire chapter (vv.2,3,21,23), declaring that divine presence persists through slavery, temptation, false accusation, and imprisonment.
outline: Joseph Prospers in Potiphar's House (vv.1-6a): The LORD blesses everything through Joseph
The Temptation — Potiphar's Wife (vv.6b-10): Daily seduction resisted with theological conviction
The Crisis — False Accusation (vv.11-18): Joseph flees, the garment becomes false evidence
Joseph Imprisoned (vv.19-20a): Potiphar's wrath and Joseph's unjust imprisonment
The LORD with Joseph in Prison (vv.20b-23): Favor and promotion even in chains

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass after these things, that the butler of the king of Egypt and his baker had offended their lord the king of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה חָטְאוּ מַשְׁקֵה מֶלֶךְ־מִצְרַיִם וְהָאֹפֶה לַאֲדֹנֵיהֶם לְמֶלֶךְ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi achar haddevarim ha'elleh chat'u mashkeh melekh-Mitzrayim veha'ofeh la'adoneihem lemelekh Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: The phrase 'after these things' (achar haddevarim ha'elleh) links this chapter to Joseph's unjust imprisonment (ch. 39). The 'butler' (mashkeh — literally 'one who gives drink,' the chief cupbearer) held one of the most trusted positions in the ancient Near East, personally tasting the king's wine as a safeguard against poisoning. The 'baker' (ofeh) was equally trusted with the king's food. The verb 'had offended' (chat'u) is literally 'sinned' — the same word used for sin against God. Their sin against Pharaoh parallels the false accusation of sin against Joseph, creating an ironic juxtaposition: truly guilty men are imprisoned alongside a truly innocent one.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh was wroth against two of his officers, against the chief of the butlers, and against the chief of the bakers.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְצֹף פַּרְעֹה עַל שְׁנֵי סָרִיסָיו עַל שַׂר הַמַּשְׁקִים וְעַל שַׂר הָאוֹפִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiktzof Par'oh al shenei sarisav al sar hamashkim ve'al sar ha'ofim.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh's 'wrath' (vayyiktzof — from 'qatsaf,' burning anger) mirrors Potiphar's anger at Joseph (39:19). Both are cases of royal fury leading to imprisonment, but with ironic differences: Potiphar's anger was based on a lie; Pharaoh's on actual offense. The word 'sarisav' (his officers/eunuchs) can mean court officials or literal eunuchs — high-ranking servants in the royal household. They are designated 'sar' (chief/prince) — these are not low-ranking servants but men of significant authority and responsibility.

verses.kjv_text: And he put them in ward in the house of the captain of the guard, into the prison, the place where Joseph was bound.
verses.original_text: וַיִּתֵּן אֹתָם בְּמִשְׁמַר בֵּית שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים אֶל־בֵּית הַסֹּהַר מְקוֹם אֲשֶׁר יוֹסֵף אָסוּר שָׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitten otam bemishmar beit sar hattabbachim el-beit hassohar mekom asher Yosef asur sham.
verses.study_note: Providence orchestrates the convergence. The officers are placed in the same prison where Joseph is held — 'the house of the captain of the guard' (beit sar hattabbachim), which is Potiphar's jurisdiction (39:1). The narrator emphasizes this is 'the place where Joseph was bound' (asur) — the word highlights ongoing injustice. What appears as coincidence is divine arrangement: God brings the right people to Joseph at the right time. The entire sequence — Potiphar's wife's lie, Joseph's imprisonment, the officers' offense — forms a chain of providential events leading to Pharaoh's court.

verses.kjv_text: And the captain of the guard charged Joseph with them, and he served them: and they continued a season in ward.
verses.original_text: וַיִּפְקֹד שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים אֶת־יוֹסֵף אִתָּם וַיְשָׁרֶת אֹתָם וַיִּהְיוּ יָמִים בְּמִשְׁמָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyifkod sar hattabbachim et-Yosef ittam vayesharet otam vayyihyu yamim bemishmar.
verses.study_note: Joseph is 'charged with' (vayyifkod — appointed over) these officers and 'served them' (vayesharet — from 'sharat,' to minister/attend). The irony is profound: the innocent prisoner serves the guilty ones. This is Joseph's pattern — he rises to responsibility everywhere he is placed (Potiphar's house, the prison, and later Pharaoh's court). The verb 'sharat' is the same word used for priestly and Levitical ministry (Num 3:6), foreshadowing Joseph's mediatorial role. 'A season' (yamim — literally 'days') indicates an unspecified period — long enough for Joseph to build trust and relationship with the officers.

verses.kjv_text: And they dreamed a dream both of them, each man his dream in one night, each man according to the interpretation of his dream, the butler and the baker of the king of Egypt, which were bound in the prison.
verses.original_text: וַיַּחַלְמוּ חֲלוֹם שְׁנֵיהֶם אִישׁ חֲלֹמוֹ בְּלַיְלָה אֶחָד אִישׁ כְּפִתְרוֹן חֲלֹמוֹ הַמַּשְׁקֶה וְהָאֹפֶה אֲשֶׁר לְמֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם אֲשֶׁר אֲסוּרִים בְּבֵית הַסֹּהַר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyachalmu chalom sheneihem ish chalomo belaylah echad ish kefitron chalomo hamashkeh veha'ofeh asher lemelekh Mitzrayim asher asurim beveith hassohar.
verses.study_note: Both men dream 'in one night' (belaylah echad) — the simultaneity is divinely orchestrated. The phrase 'each man according to the interpretation of his dream' (ish kefitron chalomo) is significant: each dream contained its own specific meaning, custom-designed by God for each individual. Dreams in the ancient Near East were considered messages from the gods, and Egyptians had elaborate dream interpretation manuals. But Genesis consistently shows that true interpretation belongs to the God of Israel alone — not to human technique or pagan ritual.
verses.doctrinal_note: God communicates through dreams to those outside the covenant community. These pagan officers receive divine revelation — not because of their religious merit but because God sovereignly chooses to reveal Himself. This anticipates the broader biblical principle that God's revelation extends to all nations (cf. Nebuchadnezzar's dreams in Daniel 2 and 4), while true interpretation remains a gift of His Spirit.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph came in unto them in the morning, and looked upon them, and, behold, they were sad.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא אֲלֵיהֶם יוֹסֵף בַּבֹּקֶר וַיַּרְא אֹתָם וְהִנָּם זֹעֲפִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo aleihem Yosef babboker vayyar otam vehinnam zo'afim.
verses.study_note: Joseph's attentiveness is a quiet revelation of character. He 'came in... in the morning' — faithfully serving. He 'looked upon them' (vayyar — he saw, he noticed) and perceived they were 'sad' (zo'afim — troubled, downcast, agitated). In a prison where everyone has reason to be sad, Joseph notices specific distress in these two men. This compassionate observation — seeing the individual in a crowd of suffering — is what opens the door to God's purpose. Had Joseph been consumed with his own injustice, he might never have noticed. His selflessness becomes the instrument of his deliverance.

verses.kjv_text: And he asked Pharaoh’s officers that were with him in the ward of his lord’s house, saying, Wherefore look ye so sadly to day?
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁאַל אֶת־סְרִיסֵי פַרְעֹה אֲשֶׁר אִתּוֹ בְמִשְׁמַר בֵּית אֲדֹנָיו לֵאמֹר מַדּוּעַ פְּנֵיכֶם רָעִים הַיּוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyish'al et-serisei Far'oh asher itto bemishmar beit adonav lemor maddua peneikhem ra'im hayyom.
verses.study_note: Joseph asks the question that changes history: 'Why do your faces look so evil/troubled today?' (maddua peneikhem ra'im hayyom). The word 'ra'im' (evil/bad) describes their facial expression — they look worse than usual. Joseph's question is pastoral: he notices, he asks, he engages. The phrase 'in the ward of his lord's house' (bemishmar beit adonav) reminds the reader that Potiphar is still Joseph's master — the unjust accusation has never been corrected. Yet Joseph serves without bitterness.

verses.kjv_text: And they said unto him, We have dreamed a dream, and there is no interpreter of it. And Joseph said unto them, Do not interpretations belong to God? tell me them, I pray you.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֵלָיו חֲלוֹם חָלַמְנוּ וּפֹתֵר אֵין אֹתוֹ וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם יוֹסֵף הֲלוֹא לֵאלֹהִים פִּתְרֹנִים סַפְּרוּ־נָא לִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru elav chalom chalamnu ufoter ein oto vayomer alehem Yosef halo lElohim pitronim sapperu-na li.
verses.study_note: The pivotal verse of the chapter. The officers lament: 'there is no interpreter' (foter ein) — in prison they have no access to Egypt's professional dream interpreters. Joseph's response is electrifying: 'Do not interpretations belong to God?' (halo lElohim pitronim). In one sentence, Joseph (1) redirects all credit to God, (2) distinguishes the God of Israel from pagan dream techniques, (3) offers himself as God's instrument, and (4) asks to hear the dreams. This is the same principle he will state before Pharaoh (41:16): 'It is not in me: God shall give Pharaoh an answer of peace.' Joseph consistently refuses personal credit and points to God — a pattern of humility that distinguishes true prophets from false ones.
verses.doctrinal_note: Interpretation of divine revelation belongs to God alone. Joseph denies that dream interpretation is a human skill that can be learned or purchased — it is a gift of God's Spirit. This principle runs through Scripture: Daniel says the same (Dan 2:27-28), Peter affirms that 'no prophecy of scripture is of any private interpretation' (2 Pet 1:20), and Paul teaches that spiritual things are 'spiritually discerned' (1 Cor 2:14). True understanding of God's word and will is always a divine gift, never a human achievement.

verses.kjv_text: And the chief butler told his dream to Joseph, and said to him, In my dream, behold, a vine was before me;
verses.original_text: וַיְסַפֵּר שַׂר־הַמַּשְׁקִים אֶת־חֲלֹמוֹ לְיוֹסֵף וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ בַּחֲלוֹמִי וְהִנֵּה־גֶפֶן לְפָנָי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayesapper sar-hammashkim et-chalomo leYosef vayomer lo bachalomi vehineh-gefen lefanai.
verses.study_note: The cupbearer begins his dream. The 'vine' (gefen) is one of the most theologically rich images in Scripture. Israel is repeatedly called God's vine (Ps 80:8-16, Isa 5:1-7, Jer 2:21), and Jesus declares 'I am the true vine' (John 15:1). In the context of the cupbearer's role, the vine naturally represents wine production — but the symbol resonates far beyond its immediate application. The imagery is vivid and sensory, characteristic of divinely given dreams.

verses.kjv_text: And in the vine were three branches: and it was as though it budded, and her blossoms shot forth; and the clusters thereof brought forth ripe grapes:
verses.original_text: וּבַגֶּפֶן שְׁלֹשָׁה שָׂרִיגִם וְהִיא כְפֹרַחַת עָלְתָה נִצָּהּ הִבְשִׁילוּ אַשְׁכְּלֹתֶיהָ עֲנָבִים׃
verses.transliteration: Uvaagefen sheloshah sarigim vehi kheferachat aletah nitzah hivshilu eshkeloteha anavim.
verses.study_note: The dream unfolds with miraculous speed: budding, blossoming, and ripening happen before the dreamer's eyes. The 'three branches' (sheloshah sarigim) will be interpreted as three days (v.12). The rapid growth sequence — bud, blossom, cluster, ripe grapes — compresses an entire growing season into an instant, suggesting divine acceleration. Life bursts forth in this dream, contrasting sharply with the baker's dream of dead, consumed food. The vitality of the vine points to restoration and life.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh’s cup was in my hand: and I took the grapes, and pressed them into Pharaoh’s cup, and I gave the cup into Pharaoh’s hand.
verses.original_text: וְכוֹס פַּרְעֹה בְּיָדִי וָאֶקַּח אֶת־הָעֲנָבִים וָאֶשְׂחַט אֹתָם אֶל־כּוֹס פַּרְעֹה וָאֶתֵּן אֶת־הַכּוֹס עַל־כַּף פַּרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhoh Far'oh beyadi va'ekkach et-ha'anavim va'eschat otam el-kos Far'oh va'etten et-hakkos al-kaf Far'oh.
verses.study_note: The dream climaxes with the cupbearer performing his office: pressing grapes into Pharaoh's cup and placing it in his hand. 'Pharaoh's cup' (kos Par'oh) appears three times in this verse, emphasizing the cupbearer's direct, personal service to the king. The act of squeezing fresh grapes directly into the cup (rather than serving fermented wine) may reflect an ancient Egyptian practice for royal freshness and safety. The 'cup' (kos) becomes a significant biblical symbol: the cup of salvation (Ps 116:13), the cup of God's wrath (Isa 51:17, Jer 25:15), and the cup of the new covenant (Luke 22:20). Jesus in Gethsemane prays about 'this cup' (Matt 26:39).

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph said unto him, This is the interpretation of it: The three branches are three days:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ יוֹסֵף זֶה פִּתְרֹנוֹ שְׁלֹשֶׁת הַשָּׂרִיגִם שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים הֵם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer lo Yosef zeh pitrono sheloshet hassarigim sheloshet yamim hem.
verses.study_note: Joseph interprets with confidence and specificity: 'three branches are three days.' The interpretation is not vague or ambiguous — it is precise, concrete, and verifiable. This precision distinguishes true divine revelation from pagan fortune-telling, which was typically couched in generalities. The number three recurs in both dreams, linking them as parallel revelations of a single divine decree. The 'three days' motif carries deep theological resonance: Abraham's three-day journey to Moriah (22:4), Jonah's three days in the fish (Jonah 1:17), and supremely Christ's resurrection 'on the third day' (1 Cor 15:4).

verses.kjv_text: Yet within three days shall Pharaoh lift up thine head, and restore thee unto thy place: and thou shalt deliver Pharaoh’s cup into his hand, after the former manner when thou wast his butler.
verses.original_text: בְּעוֹד שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת־רֹאשֶׁךָ וַהֲשִׁיבְךָ עַל־כַּנֶּךָ וְנָתַתָּ כוֹס־פַּרְעֹה בְּיָדוֹ כַּמִּשְׁפָּט הָרִאשׁוֹן אֲשֶׁר הָיִיתָ מַשְׁקֵהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Be'od sheloshet yamim yissa Far'oh et-roshekha vahashivekha al-kannekha venatatta kos-Far'oh beyado kammishpat harishon asher hayita mashkehu.
verses.study_note: The phrase 'lift up thine head' (yissa Far'oh et-roshekha) is a Hebrew idiom meaning to show favor, to honor, to raise from a low position (cf. 2 Kings 25:27, where Jehoiachin's head is 'lifted up' from prison). The same phrase will be used with devastating double meaning for the baker (v.19), where 'lifting the head' means decapitation. Joseph prophecies complete restoration: same position ('thy place' — kannekha), same duties ('deliver Pharaoh's cup'), same status ('the former manner' — kammishpat harishon). The cupbearer's release prefigures redemption and new life.
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'lifting of the head' on the third day creates a powerful typological connection to Christ's resurrection. Within three days, one is raised to life and honor, the other to death. This pattern of simultaneous salvation and judgment at the same event echoes the crucifixion: two thieves, one saved and one condemned (Luke 23:39-43); the cross as both salvation and judgment (John 12:31-32).

verses.kjv_text: But think on me when it shall be well with thee, and shew kindness, I pray thee, unto me, and make mention of me unto Pharaoh, and bring me out of this house:
verses.original_text: כִּי אִם־זְכַרְתַּנִי אִתְּךָ כַּאֲשֶׁר יִיטַב לָךְ וְעָשִׂיתָ־נָּא עִמָּדִי חָסֶד וְהִזְכַּרְתַּנִי אֶל־פַּרְעֹה וְהוֹצֵאתַנִי מִן־הַבַּיִת הַזֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Ki im-zekhartani ittekha ka'asher yitav lakh ve'asita-na immadi chesed vehizkartani el-Par'oh vehotzetani min-habbayit hazzeh.
verses.study_note: Joseph's only self-advocacy in the entire narrative. He asks for 'chesed' (covenant faithfulness/kindness) — a significant word choice, implying that the interpretation he has given creates a bond of obligation. 'Remember me' (zekhartani) — the same root used when 'God remembered' Noah (Gen 8:1) and Abraham (19:29). Joseph asks the cupbearer to do what only God consistently does: remember. 'Bring me out of this house' (hotzetani min-habbayit hazzeh) — he calls the prison 'this house,' not even naming it as a prison, perhaps to avoid offense to Potiphar. The plea is restrained, dignified, and specific.

verses.kjv_text: For indeed I was stolen away out of the land of the Hebrews: and here also have I done nothing that they should put me into the dungeon.
verses.original_text: כִּי־גֻנֹּב גֻּנַּבְתִּי מֵאֶרֶץ הָעִבְרִים וְגַם־פֹּה לֹא־עָשִׂיתִי מְאוּמָה כִּי־שָׂמוּ אֹתִי בַּבּוֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Ki-gunnov gunnabti me'eretz ha'Ivrim vegam-foh lo-asiti me'umah ki-samu oti babbor.
verses.study_note: Joseph provides his defense in two parts: (1) 'I was stolen away' (gunnov gunnabti — emphatic infinitive absolute, 'I was truly, absolutely stolen') from the land of the Hebrews — referring to his brothers' selling him, which he frames as kidnapping rather than revealing the shameful family details. (2) 'Here also I have done nothing' — he declares his innocence without naming Potiphar's wife or her accusation, again showing remarkable restraint. The 'dungeon' (bor — pit/cistern) is the same word used for the pit his brothers threw him into (37:24). Joseph has gone from one 'bor' to another — a pattern of descent that will reverse into dramatic ascent. 'The land of the Hebrews' (eretz ha'Ivrim) is the first use of this ethnic designation for Canaan.

verses.kjv_text: When the chief baker saw that the interpretation was good, he said unto Joseph, I also was in my dream, and, behold, I had three white baskets on my head:
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא שַׂר־הָאוֹפִים כִּי טוֹב פָּתָר וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל־יוֹסֵף אַף־אֲנִי בַּחֲלוֹמִי וְהִנֵּה שְׁלֹשָׁה סַלֵּי חֹרִי עַל־רֹאשִׁי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyar sar-ha'ofim ki tov patar vayomer el-Yosef af-ani bachalomi vehineh sheloshah sallei chori al-roshi.
verses.study_note: The baker is encouraged by the favorable interpretation of the cupbearer's dream: 'he saw that the interpretation was good' (ki tov patar). He assumes his dream will be equally positive. Three baskets (sheloshah sallei) parallel the three branches, but the content and outcome will be devastatingly different. 'White baskets' (sallei chori) — 'chori' may mean 'white' or 'wicker/open-weave' (allowing birds access). Baskets carried on the head was standard Egyptian practice, confirmed by tomb paintings and reliefs. The baker's dream imagery relates directly to his profession, just as the cupbearer's did.

verses.kjv_text: And in the uppermost basket there was of all manner of bakemeats for Pharaoh; and the birds did eat them out of the basket upon my head.
verses.original_text: וּבַסַּל הָעֶלְיוֹן מִכֹּל מַאֲכַל פַּרְעֹה מַעֲשֵׂה אֹפֶה וְהָעוֹף אֹכֵל אֹתָם מִן־הַסַּל מֵעַל רֹאשִׁי׃
verses.transliteration: Uvasal ha'elyon mikkol ma'akhal Par'oh ma'aseh ofeh veha'of okhel otam min-hassal me'al roshi.
verses.study_note: The ominous detail: birds are eating the food intended for Pharaoh. In the cupbearer's dream, the dreamer actively served Pharaoh — pressing grapes and placing the cup in his hand. In the baker's dream, the dreamer is passive while birds consume what should have been Pharaoh's food. The contrast is stark: one dream shows service fulfilled; the other shows service failed. Birds eating from above a person's head was a symbol of death and disgrace in the ancient Near East (cf. Deut 28:26, 1 Sam 17:44). The baker's inability to protect Pharaoh's food symbolizes his failure in his office — the very offense for which he was imprisoned.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph answered and said, This is the interpretation thereof: The three baskets are three days:
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַן יוֹסֵף וַיֹּאמֶר זֶה פִּתְרֹנוֹ שְׁלֹשֶׁת הַסַּלִּים שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים הֵם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'an Yosef vayomer zeh pitrono sheloshet hassallim sheloshet yamim hem.
verses.study_note: Joseph interprets with the same structure as before: 'three baskets are three days.' The parallel is exact — same number, same time frame. But the content will be opposite. Joseph does not soften or avoid the hard truth. He speaks faithfully what God reveals, even when the message is devastating. This is a mark of true prophetic integrity — the true prophet delivers God's word regardless of whether it is welcome (cf. 1 Kings 22:14, Jer 1:17). Joseph does not say 'the interpretation is bad' — he simply states what God has shown.

verses.kjv_text: Yet within three days shall Pharaoh lift up thy head from off thee, and shall hang thee on a tree; and the birds shall eat thy flesh from off thee.
verses.original_text: בְּעוֹד שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת־רֹאשְׁךָ מֵעָלֶיךָ וְתָלָה אוֹתְךָ עַל־עֵץ וְאָכַל הָעוֹף אֶת־בְּשָׂרְךָ מֵעָלֶיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Be'od sheloshet yamim yissa Far'oh et-roshekha me'alekha vetalah otkha al-etz ve'akhal ha'of et-besarkha me'alekha.
verses.study_note: The devastating counterpart to v.13. The same phrase 'Pharaoh shall lift up thy head' (yissa Far'oh et-roshekha) is now followed by 'from off thee' (me'alekha) — transforming restoration into decapitation. The wordplay is chilling: the idiom for honor becomes the idiom for execution. 'Shall hang thee on a tree' (vetalah otkha al-etz) — public display of the executed body, a practice common in the ancient Near East signifying shame and divine curse. Deuteronomy 21:23 declares 'cursed is every one that hangeth on a tree,' a passage Paul applies to Christ's crucifixion (Gal 3:13). The birds consuming his flesh fulfills the dream's imagery and ancient curse formulas.
verses.doctrinal_note: The same phrase — 'lift up thy head' — means life for one and death for another. This dual outcome from a single authority (Pharaoh) typologically foreshadows the cross, where the same event produces salvation and condemnation. The two thieves beside Christ (Luke 23:39-43) are the New Testament fulfillment of this pattern: one is 'remembered' into paradise, the other dies unforgiven. The phrase 'hanged on a tree' anticipates Deuteronomy 21:23 and Galatians 3:13 — Christ became a curse for us on the tree.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass the third day, which was Pharaoh’s birthday, that he made a feast unto all his servants: and he lifted up the head of the chief butler and of the chief baker among his servants.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי יוֹם הֻלֶּדֶת אֶת־פַּרְעֹה וַיַּעַשׂ מִשְׁתֶּה לְכָל־עֲבָדָיו וַיִּשָּׂא אֶת־רֹאשׁ שַׂר הַמַּשְׁקִים וְאֶת־רֹאשׁ שַׂר הָאֹפִים בְּתוֹךְ עֲבָדָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi bayyom hashlishi yom hulledet et-Par'oh vayya'as mishteh lekhol-avadav vayyissa et-rosh sar hamashkim ve'et-rosh sar ha'ofim betokh avadav.
verses.study_note: Everything Joseph predicted comes to pass 'on the third day' (bayyom hashlishi) — the exact timeline. Pharaoh's birthday celebration provides the occasion for royal clemency and judgment alike. Egyptian records confirm that pharaohs released prisoners and held grand feasts on birthdays. The narrator uses 'lifted up the head' (vayyissa et-rosh) for both officers simultaneously, maintaining the double meaning until the next verses resolve each fate separately. The feast (mishteh — a drinking banquet) ironically connects to the cupbearer's restoration to wine service. One man's feast day becomes another man's execution day — the same event brings life and death.

verses.kjv_text: And he restored the chief butler unto his butlership again; and he gave the cup into Pharaoh’s hand:
verses.original_text: וַיָּשֶׁב אֶת־שַׂר הַמַּשְׁקִים עַל־מַשְׁקֵהוּ וַיִּתֵּן הַכּוֹס עַל־כַּף פַּרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashev et-sar hamashkim al-mashkehu vayyitten hakkos al-kaf Par'oh.
verses.study_note: Exact fulfillment of Joseph's interpretation (v.13). The cupbearer is 'restored' (vayyashev — from 'shuv,' to return) to his exact former position. He places the cup in Pharaoh's hand — precisely as the dream depicted. The precision of fulfillment validates Joseph as a true interpreter of God's word. Every detail matches: the timing (three days), the action (restoration to office), and the duty (giving the cup to Pharaoh). This establishes the credibility that will matter when Pharaoh himself has dreams in chapter 41.

verses.kjv_text: But he hanged the chief baker: as Joseph had interpreted to them.
verses.original_text: וְאֵת שַׂר הָאוֹפִים תָּלָה כַּאֲשֶׁר פָּתַר לָהֶם יוֹסֵף׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et sar ha'ofim talah ka'asher patar lahem Yosef.
verses.study_note: The baker's fate is reported with stark brevity: 'he hanged' (talah) — one word in Hebrew for the execution, compared to the elaborate description of the cupbearer's restoration. The narrator adds the critical validation: 'as Joseph had interpreted to them' (ka'asher patar lahem Yosef). Both dreams, both interpretations, both outcomes — exactly as Joseph declared. This phrase establishes the principle that God's word through His servants is wholly reliable. What He reveals, He fulfills. The brevity of the execution report may reflect the narrator's focus on theological significance rather than graphic detail.

verses.kjv_text: Yet did not the chief butler remember Joseph, but forgat him.
verses.original_text: וְלֹא־זָכַר שַׂר־הַמַּשְׁקִים אֶת־יוֹסֵף וַיִּשְׁכָּחֵהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Velo-zakhar sar-hamashkim et-Yosef vayyishkachehu.
verses.study_note: The chapter's devastating final line. After Joseph's kindness, accurate interpretation, and specific plea to be remembered (v.14), the cupbearer 'did not remember' (lo-zakhar) and 'forgot him' (vayyishkachehu). The double negative — did not remember AND forgot — emphasizes the totality of the failure. The word 'zakhar' (remember) is the exact word Joseph used in his plea (v.14, zekhartani). Human gratitude fails where divine faithfulness never does. This forgetting extends Joseph's suffering by two full years (41:1). Yet this is not merely human ingratitude — it is divine timing. Had the cupbearer remembered immediately, Joseph would have been released as a freed prisoner. God's plan required Joseph to stand before Pharaoh as an interpreter of Pharaoh's own dreams, which had not yet occurred. The cupbearer's forgetfulness is the painful mechanism of God's perfect timing.
verses.doctrinal_note: Human forgetfulness versus divine remembrance: The cupbearer's failure to remember Joseph stands in sharp contrast to God who 'remembered Abraham' (19:29), 'remembered Rachel' (30:22), and 'remembered his covenant' (Exod 2:24). Humans forget their benefactors; God never forgets His people. Joseph's suffering in these forgotten years (Psalm 105:17-19) becomes the refining fire: 'the word of the LORD tried him.' This is the biblical pattern: the path to exaltation runs through unjust suffering and forgotten faithfulness. Christ Himself was 'despised and rejected' (Isa 53:3) before being exalted to the highest place (Phil 2:9).

meta.overview: Genesis 40 marks a pivotal turning point in the Joseph narrative, bridging his unjust imprisonment with his eventual exaltation. Two of Pharaoh's officers — the chief cupbearer (butler) and the chief baker — are imprisoned and each receives a dream on the same night. Joseph, attentive to their distress, offers to interpret, crediting God alone as the source of interpretation. The cupbearer's dream of a vine with three branches foretells his restoration in three days; the baker's dream of three baskets foretells his execution. Both interpretations are fulfilled exactly. Yet the chapter ends on a note of painful irony: the restored cupbearer forgets Joseph entirely. This forgotten faithfulness becomes the crucible of Joseph's suffering — and the mechanism of God's precise timing. The chapter is saturated with typological connections to Christ: the innocent sufferer, the one who brings life and death, the forgotten intercessor, and the servant who is ultimately exalted. Dreams, which Joseph's brothers once mocked (37:19-20), now become instruments of divine revelation through which Joseph demonstrates that God alone holds the future.
meta.theme: Divine Sovereignty Over Dreams, Forgotten Faithfulness & God's Timing
meta.keyWordMeaning: To interpret (a dream) — used exclusively for dream interpretation. Joseph insists the ability belongs to God alone, establishing that true interpretation of the future is a divine prerogative, not a human skill.
outline: Two Officers Imprisoned (vv.1-4): The cupbearer and baker offend Pharaoh and are placed in Joseph's charge
Dreams in the Night (vv.5-8): Both dream on the same night; Joseph credits God with interpretation
The Cupbearer's Dream Interpreted (vv.9-13): Three branches = three days until restoration
Joseph's Plea (vv.14-15): Joseph asks to be remembered — his only self-advocacy in the narrative
The Baker's Dream Interpreted (vv.16-19): Three baskets = three days until execution
Fulfillment and Forgetting (vv.20-23): Pharaoh's birthday feast — one restored, one hanged, Joseph forgotten

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass at the end of two full years, that Pharaoh dreamed: and, behold, he stood by the river.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי מִקֵּץ שְׁנָתַיִם יָמִים וּפַרְעֹה חֹלֵם וְהִנֵּה עֹמֵד עַל־הַיְאֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi mikketz shenatayim yamim uFar'oh cholem vehineh omed al-haYe'or.
verses.study_note: Two full years (shenatayim yamim — literally 'two years of days,' emphasizing every single day) have passed since the chief butler was restored (40:23). Joseph has been completely forgotten by the man he helped. The phrase 'mikketz' (at the end) opens the narrative with a sense of divine timing — God's clock, not man's, governs the story. Pharaoh dreams while standing 'by the river' (haYe'or) — the Nile, Egypt's lifeblood. The Nile was deified in Egypt as Hapi; Pharaoh's dream by the Nile signals that Egypt's prosperity is about to be addressed by the true God.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, there came up out of the river seven well favoured kine and fatfleshed; and they fed in a meadow.
verses.original_text: וְהִנֵּה מִן־הַיְאֹר עֹלֹת שֶׁבַע פָּרוֹת יְפוֹת מַרְאֶה וּבְרִיאֹת בָּשָׂר וַתִּרְעֶינָה בָּאָחוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehineh min-haYe'or olot sheva parot yefot mar'eh uveriot basar vattir'enah ba'achu.
verses.study_note: Seven cows emerge from the Nile — 'well favoured' (yefot mar'eh — beautiful in appearance) and 'fatfleshed' (beriot basar — healthy of flesh). Cows rising from the Nile is perfectly Egyptian: the Nile's annual inundation produced the pastureland on which cattle thrived, and the cow-goddess Hathor represented fertility and abundance. 'Achu' (meadow/reed-grass) is an Egyptian loanword in Hebrew, adding local color. God speaks to Pharaoh through the symbols of his own culture.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, seven other kine came up after them out of the river, ill favoured and leanfleshed; and stood by the other kine upon the brink of the river.
verses.original_text: וְהִנֵּה שֶׁבַע פָּרוֹת אֲחֵרוֹת עֹלוֹת אַחֲרֵיהֶן מִן־הַיְאֹר רָעוֹת מַרְאֶה וְדַקּוֹת בָּשָׂר וַתַּעֲמֹדְנָה אֵצֶל הַפָּרוֹת עַל־שְׂפַת הַיְאֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vehineh sheva parot acherot olot achareihen min-haYe'or ra'ot mar'eh vedakkot basar vatta'amodnah etzel haparot al-sefat haYe'or.
verses.study_note: Seven more cows emerge — the polar opposite: 'ill favoured' (ra'ot mar'eh — ugly in appearance) and 'leanfleshed' (dakkot basar — thin of flesh). They stand beside the fat cows on the Nile's bank. The juxtaposition is startling and ominous: prosperity and famine standing side by side at Egypt's lifeline. The detail that both sets come 'from the river' shows that both abundance and scarcity come from the same divine source.

verses.kjv_text: And the ill favoured and leanfleshed kine did eat up the seven well favoured and fat kine. So Pharaoh awoke.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאכַלְנָה הַפָּרוֹת רָעוֹת הַמַּרְאֶה וְדַקֹּת הַבָּשָׂר אֵת שֶׁבַע הַפָּרוֹת יְפֹת הַמַּרְאֶה וְהַבְּרִיאֹת וַיִּיקַץ פַּרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vattokhalenah haparot ra'ot hammar'eh vedakkot habbasar et sheva haparot yefot hammar'eh vehabberiot vayyikatz Par'oh.
verses.study_note: The thin cows devour the fat cows — an unnatural, disturbing image. Cows do not eat other cows; the impossibility signals this is no ordinary dream. Pharaoh awakes disturbed but the dream is only half complete. In prophetic symbolism, the famine does not merely follow the plenty — it 'consumes' it, leaving no trace of the abundance. This detail will be explicitly stated in Joseph's interpretation (v.30).

verses.kjv_text: And he slept and dreamed the second time: and, behold, seven ears of corn came up upon one stalk, rank and good.
verses.original_text: וַיִּישַׁן וַיַּחֲלֹם שֵׁנִית וְהִנֵּה שֶׁבַע שִׁבֳּלִים עֹלוֹת בְּקָנֶה אֶחָד בְּרִיאוֹת וְטֹבוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishan vayyachalom shenit vehineh sheva shibbolim olot bekaneh echad beri'ot vetovot.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh falls asleep again and receives a second dream with the same message in different imagery. Seven ears of grain grow on a single stalk — 'rank and good' (beri'ot vetovot — healthy and good). Grain (shibbolim) was Egypt's other staple alongside cattle. God doubles the dream in two cultural symbols — livestock and agriculture — confirming its certainty. Joseph will explain this doubling principle in v.32.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, seven thin ears and blasted with the east wind sprung up after them.
verses.original_text: וְהִנֵּה שֶׁבַע שִׁבֳּלִים דַּקּוֹת וּשְׁדוּפֹת קָדִים צֹמְחוֹת אַחֲרֵיהֶן׃
verses.transliteration: Vehineh sheva shibbolim dakkot ushedufot kadim tzomechot achareihen.
verses.study_note: Seven thin ears 'blasted with the east wind' (ushedufot kadim). The east wind (kadim) in Egypt is the khamsin — a hot, dry desert wind from the Sinai/Arabian desert that withers vegetation. This wind becomes a recurring instrument of God's judgment: it parts the Red Sea (Exod 14:21), blasts Jonah's gourd (Jonah 4:8), and symbolizes divine displeasure (Hosea 13:15). What the east wind scorches, no human power can restore.

verses.kjv_text: And the seven thin ears devoured the seven rank and full ears. And Pharaoh awoke, and, behold, it was a dream.
verses.original_text: וַתִּבְלַעְנָה הַשִּׁבֳּלִים הַדַּקּוֹת אֵת שֶׁבַע הַשִּׁבֳּלִים הַבְּרִיאוֹת וְהַמְּלֵאוֹת וַיִּיקַץ פַּרְעֹה וְהִנֵּה חֲלוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vattivla'nah hashibbolim haddakkot et sheva hashibbolim habberiot vehamme'lot vayyikatz Par'oh vehineh chalom.
verses.study_note: The thin ears 'devoured' (vattivla'nah — swallowed up) the full ears. The verb 'bala' (swallow) is even more dramatic than 'ate' — total consumption with no trace remaining. Pharaoh awakes and realizes 'it was a dream' (chalom) — but a dream that grips his spirit and will not release him. The combination of two dreams with the same impossible imagery signals divine communication that demands interpretation.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass in the morning that his spirit was troubled; and he sent and called for all the magicians of Egypt, and all the wise men thereof: and Pharaoh told them his dream; but there was none that could interpret them unto Pharaoh.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בַבֹּקֶר וַתִּפָּעֶם רוּחוֹ וַיִּשְׁלַח וַיִּקְרָא אֶת־כָּל־חַרְטֻמֵּי מִצְרַיִם וְאֶת־כָּל־חֲכָמֶיהָ וַיְסַפֵּר פַּרְעֹה לָהֶם אֶת־חֲלֹמוֹ וְאֵין־פּוֹתֵר אוֹתָם לְפַרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi vabboker vatippa'em rucho vayyishlach vayyikra et-kol-chartummei Mitzrayim ve'et-kol-chakhameiha vayesapper Par'oh lahem et-chalomo ve'ein-poter otam lePhar'oh.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh's spirit is 'troubled' (vatippa'em — agitated, pounded, like a hammer striking). He summons 'all the magicians' (chartummei) and 'all the wise men' (chakhameiha) — the complete intellectual and occult apparatus of Egypt, the most advanced civilization of the ancient world. Yet 'there was none that could interpret' (ein-poter). The same pattern appears in Daniel 2:2-11 when Nebuchadnezzar's wise men fail. Human wisdom, even at its highest, cannot penetrate divine revelation.
verses.doctrinal_note: The failure of Egypt's wisdom establishes a foundational biblical principle: the things of God are discerned only by the Spirit of God (1 Cor 2:14). No amount of human learning, occult practice, or natural intelligence can interpret what God has sealed. This prepares the way for Joseph's declaration: 'It is not in me; God shall give Pharaoh an answer' (v.16). True wisdom begins with the fear of the LORD.

verses.kjv_text: Then spake the chief butler unto Pharaoh, saying, I do remember my faults this day:
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר שַׂר הַמַּשְׁקִים אֶת־פַּרְעֹה לֵאמֹר אֶת־חֲטָאַי אֲנִי מַזְכִּיר הַיּוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber sar hamashekim et-Par'oh lemor et-chata'ai ani mazkir hayyom.
verses.study_note: Two years of silence finally break. The chief butler says 'I do remember my faults' (chata'ai — my sins/offenses). His 'fault' was forgetting Joseph (40:23). The word 'mazkir' (remember/bring to remembrance) echoes Joseph's plea 'think on me' (40:14, from 'zakar' — remember). Human memory fails, but God's timing does not. The butler remembers precisely when God needs him to — not a day early, not a day late. Joseph had to wait until Pharaoh needed him, not merely until the butler could help him.

verses.kjv_text: Pharaoh was wroth with his servants, and put me in ward in the captain of the guard’s house, both me and the chief baker:
verses.original_text: פַּרְעֹה קָצַף עַל־עֲבָדָיו וַיִּתֵּן אֹתִי בְּמִשְׁמַר בֵּית שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים אֹתִי וְאֵת שַׂר הָאֹפִים׃
verses.transliteration: Par'oh katzaf al-avadav vayyitten oti bemishmar beit sar hattabbachim oti ve'et sar ha'ofim.
verses.study_note: The butler recounts the backstory — his imprisonment with the chief baker in 'the captain of the guard's house' (beit sar hattabbachim). This was Potiphar's jurisdiction (Gen 39:1). The providential chain becomes visible: Potiphar's house → prison → Pharaoh's officials → Pharaoh's dream → Joseph's exaltation. What seemed like random misfortune was God weaving every thread toward this moment.

verses.kjv_text: And we dreamed a dream in one night, I and he; we dreamed each man according to the interpretation of his dream.
verses.original_text: וַנַּחַלְמָה חֲלוֹם בְּלַיְלָה אֶחָד אֲנִי וָהוּא אִישׁ כְּפִתְרוֹן חֲלֹמוֹ חָלָמְנוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vannachalemah chalom belaylah echad ani vahu ish kifitron chalomo chalamnu.
verses.study_note: Both men dreamed 'in one night' — simultaneous, divinely coordinated dreams, each 'according to the interpretation of his dream' (kifitron chalomo). The phrase indicates that each dream carried its own specific meaning tailored to the dreamer. God communicates individually yet providentially — the butler's dream leads to restoration, the baker's to execution, and both encounters lead to this moment before Pharaoh.

verses.kjv_text: And there was there with us a young man, an Hebrew, servant to the captain of the guard; and we told him, and he interpreted to us our dreams; to each man according to his dream he did interpret.
verses.original_text: וְשָׁם אִתָּנוּ נַעַר עִבְרִי עֶבֶד לְשַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים וַנְּסַפֶּר־לוֹ וַיִּפְתָּר־לָנוּ אֶת־חֲלֹמֹתֵינוּ אִישׁ כַּחֲלֹמוֹ פָּתָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vesham ittanu na'ar Ivri eved lesar hattabbachim vanesapper-lo vayyiftar-lanu et-chalomotenu ish kachalomo patar.
verses.study_note: The butler describes Joseph with three diminishing terms: 'a young man' (na'ar — a youth, possibly a term of lower status), 'a Hebrew' (Ivri — a foreigner, an outsider), 'a servant' (eved — a slave) to the captain of the guard. Three strikes against Joseph in Egyptian eyes: young, foreign, enslaved. Yet this triple humiliation parallels Christ's: despised, rejected, a servant (Isaiah 53:3, Phil 2:7). God chooses the foolish, weak, and despised to confound the wise (1 Cor 1:27-28).

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, as he interpreted to us, so it was; me he restored unto mine office, and him he hanged.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כַּאֲשֶׁר פָּתַר־לָנוּ כֵּן הָיָה אֹתִי הֵשִׁיב עַל־כַּנִּי וְאֹתוֹ תָלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi ka'asher patar-lanu ken hayah oti heshiv al-kanni ve'oto talah.
verses.study_note: The butler's testimony provides the critical credential: 'as he interpreted to us, so it was' (ka'asher patar-lanu ken hayah). Joseph's interpretation was 100% accurate — verified by historical fulfillment. This is the biblical test of a true prophet (Deut 18:22). The butler was restored; the baker was hanged. Joseph's track record is now presented before Pharaoh as evidence of his God-given gift.

verses.kjv_text: Then Pharaoh sent and called Joseph, and they brought him hastily out of the dungeon: and he shaved himself, and changed his raiment, and came in unto Pharaoh.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁלַח פַּרְעֹה וַיִּקְרָא אֶת־יוֹסֵף וַיְרִיצֻהוּ מִן־הַבּוֹר וַיְגַלַּח וַיְחַלֵּף שִׂמְלֹתָיו וַיָּבֹא אֶל־פַּרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishlach Par'oh vayyikra et-Yosef vayeritzihu min-habbor vayegallach vaychalef simlotav vayyavo el-Par'oh.
verses.study_note: The most dramatic transition in Joseph's life, compressed into a single verse. 'They brought him hastily' (vayeritzihu — literally 'made him run') 'out of the dungeon' (min-habbor — from the pit/cistern, the same word used when his brothers threw him into the pit in 37:24). He 'shaved' — Egyptian custom required a clean-shaven face before Pharaoh (Hebrews wore beards). He 'changed his raiment' — exchanging prison garments for court clothing. From the pit to the palace in a single day. This parallels Christ's trajectory: from the grave to the throne, from humiliation to exaltation.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh said unto Joseph, I have dreamed a dream, and there is none that can interpret it: and I have heard say of thee, that thou canst understand a dream to interpret it.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה אֶל־יוֹסֵף חֲלוֹם חָלַמְתִּי וּפֹתֵר אֵין אֹתוֹ וַאֲנִי שָׁמַעְתִּי עָלֶיךָ לֵאמֹר תִּשְׁמַע חֲלוֹם לִפְתֹּר אֹתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Par'oh el-Yosef chalom chalamti ufoter ein oto va'ani shamati alekha lemor tishma chalom liftor oto.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh presents Joseph with both the problem ('there is none that can interpret') and the expectation ('thou canst understand a dream to interpret it'). The phrase 'tishma chalom' (literally 'you hear a dream') suggests more than intellectual understanding — it implies spiritual perception, hearing what others cannot. Pharaoh places upon this Hebrew slave the weight that all Egypt's wisdom could not bear.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph answered Pharaoh, saying, It is not in me: God shall give Pharaoh an answer of peace.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַן יוֹסֵף אֶת־פַּרְעֹה לֵאמֹר בִּלְעָדָי אֱלֹהִים יַעֲנֶה אֶת־שְׁלוֹם פַּרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'an Yosef et-Par'oh lemor bil'adai Elohim ya'aneh et-shelom Par'oh.
verses.study_note: Joseph's response is one of the most important theological declarations in Genesis. 'It is not in me' (bil'adai — apart from me, without me, not my ability). Joseph immediately deflects all credit to God. This is not false humility — it is theological precision. He had said the same to the prisoners: 'Do not interpretations belong to God?' (40:8). Now before the most powerful ruler on earth, he says it again. 'God shall give Pharaoh an answer of peace' (shelom) — God will answer with wholeness and well-being. Joseph presents himself as nothing more than a vessel.
verses.doctrinal_note: Joseph's 'bil'adai' (not in me) is the posture of every true prophet and servant of God. The ability to reveal divine truth never originates in human wisdom. Daniel echoes this (Dan 2:27-28), as does Paul: 'not I, but the grace of God' (1 Cor 15:10). This is the fundamental distinction between biblical prophecy and pagan divination: the magicians rely on technique; the prophet relies entirely on God.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh said unto Joseph, In my dream, behold, I stood upon the bank of the river:
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר פַּרְעֹה אֶל־יוֹסֵף בַּחֲלֹמִי הִנְנִי עֹמֵד עַל־שְׂפַת הַיְאֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber Par'oh el-Yosef bachalomi hineni omed al-sefat haYe'or.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh begins retelling his dream. The repetition serves literary and theological purposes: it emphasizes the dream's importance and allows the reader to note subtle differences between the original dream (vv.1-7) and Pharaoh's retelling. The Nile's bank (sefat haYe'or) is where Pharaoh stands in both the dream and Egypt's cosmology — he is 'lord' of the Nile, yet the Nile's fate is beyond his control.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, there came up out of the river seven kine, fatfleshed and well favoured; and they fed in a meadow:
verses.original_text: וְהִנֵּה מִן־הַיְאֹר עֹלֹת שֶׁבַע פָּרוֹת בְּרִיאוֹת בָּשָׂר וִיפֹת תֹּאַר וַתִּרְעֶינָה בָּאָחוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehineh min-haYe'or olot sheva parot beri'ot basar vifot to'ar vattir'enah ba'achu.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh's retelling adds 'vifot to'ar' (beautiful in form) — a slightly different description than v.2's 'yefot mar'eh' (beautiful in appearance). The variation is natural and realistic — memory modifies details while preserving substance. The seven fat cows feeding in the meadow represent the abundant Nile flooding that sustains Egypt's agriculture.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, seven other kine came up after them, poor and very ill favoured and leanfleshed, such as I never saw in all the land of Egypt for badness:
verses.original_text: וְהִנֵּה שֶׁבַע־פָּרוֹת אֲחֵרוֹת עֹלוֹת אַחֲרֵיהֶן דַּלּוֹת וְרָעוֹת תֹּאַר מְאֹד וְרַקּוֹת בָּשָׂר לֹא־רָאִיתִי כָהֵנָּה בְכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם לָרֹעַ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehineh sheva-parot acherot olot achareihen dallot vera'ot to'ar me'od verakkot basar lo-ra'iti kahenah vekhol-eretz Mitzrayim laro'a.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh adds personal commentary absent from the original narrative: 'such as I never saw in all the land of Egypt for badness.' This addition reveals the dream's emotional impact — in Egypt, the land of the Nile's bounty, such emaciated cattle were unprecedented. The word 'dallot' (poor/weak) adds to the description — these cows are not merely thin but destitute. Pharaoh is shaken by what he saw.

verses.kjv_text: And the lean and the ill favoured kine did eat up the first seven fat kine:
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאכַלְנָה הַפָּרוֹת הָרַקּוֹת וְהָרָעוֹת אֵת שֶׁבַע הַפָּרוֹת הָרִאשֹׁנוֹת הַבְּרִיאֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vattokhalenah haparot harakkot vehara'ot et sheva haparot harishonot habberiot.
verses.study_note: The thin cows devour the fat ones. Pharaoh's retelling preserves the central horror of the dream: the impossible image of emaciated cattle consuming healthy ones. The unnatural act signifies that the coming famine will be no ordinary shortage — it will be of such severity that it erases all memory of the preceding abundance.

verses.kjv_text: And when they had eaten them up, it could not be known that they had eaten them; but they were still ill favoured, as at the beginning. So I awoke.
verses.original_text: וַתָּבֹאנָה אֶל־קִרְבֶּנָה וְלֹא נוֹדַע כִּי־בָאוּ אֶל־קִרְבֶּנָה וּמַרְאֵיהֶן רַע כַּאֲשֶׁר בַּתְּחִלָּה וָאִיקָץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattavonaah el-kirbenah velo noda ki-va'u el-kirbenah umar'eihen ra ka'asher battechillah va'ikatz.
verses.study_note: The most chilling detail — unique to Pharaoh's retelling: 'it could not be known that they had eaten them.' The thin cows showed no improvement after consuming the fat. The famine will be so total that the preceding abundance will leave absolutely no trace. This detail, absent from the narrator's account in v.4, reveals the depth of Pharaoh's disturbance and becomes crucial to Joseph's interpretation (v.30-31).

verses.kjv_text: And I saw in my dream, and, behold, seven ears came up in one stalk, full and good:
verses.original_text: וָאֵרֶא בַּחֲלֹמִי וְהִנֵּה שֶׁבַע שִׁבֳּלִים עֹלֹת בְּקָנֶה אֶחָד מְלֵאֹת וְטֹבוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Va'ereh bachalomi vehineh sheva shibbolim olot bekaneh echad me'lot vetovot.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh moves to the second dream. Seven ears of grain, 'full and good' (me'lot vetovot) — plump with kernel, healthy and productive. The word 'me'lot' (full) adds to the original 'beri'ot' (healthy) of v.5, suggesting Pharaoh perceived even greater abundance than the narrator initially described.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, seven ears, withered, thin, and blasted with the east wind, sprung up after them:
verses.original_text: וְהִנֵּה שֶׁבַע שִׁבֳּלִים צְנֻמוֹת דַּקּוֹת שְׁדוּפֹת קָדִים צֹמְחוֹת אַחֲרֵיהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehineh sheva shibbolim tzenumot dakkot shedufot kadim tzomechot achareihem.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh adds 'tzenumot' (withered/dried up) to the description — a word used only here and in v.23 of this retelling, adding 'withered' to 'thin and blasted.' Three adjectives of devastation: withered, thin, scorched. The east wind (kadim) remains central — divine judgment carried on desert winds that no human technology can resist.

verses.kjv_text: And the thin ears devoured the seven good ears: and I told this unto the magicians; but there was none that could declare it to me.
verses.original_text: וַתִּבְלַעְןָ הַשִּׁבֳּלִים הַדַּקֹּת אֵת שֶׁבַע הַשִּׁבֳּלִים הַטֹּבוֹת וָאֹמַר אֶל־הַחַרְטֻמִּים וְאֵין מַגִּיד לִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vattivla'an hashibbolim haddakkot et sheva hashibbolim hattovot va'omar el-hachartummim ve'ein maggid li.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh concludes: 'I told this to the magicians; but there was none that could declare it (maggid) to me.' The word 'maggid' (one who tells/declares) implies that the magicians could not even suggest a meaning, let alone offer the correct one. Egypt's entire wisdom tradition has been exposed as bankrupt before this divine riddle. The stage is perfectly set for Joseph's God to speak where Egypt's gods have been silent.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph said unto Pharaoh, The dream of Pharaoh is one: God hath shewed Pharaoh what he is about to do.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל־פַּרְעֹה חֲלוֹם פַּרְעֹה אֶחָד הוּא אֵת אֲשֶׁר הָאֱלֹהִים עֹשֶׂה הִגִּיד לְפַרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yosef el-Par'oh chalom Par'oh echad hu et asher ha'Elohim oseh higgid lePhar'oh.
verses.study_note: Joseph begins with a bold theological declaration: 'The dream of Pharaoh is one' (echad hu) — two dreams, one message. Then the stunning claim: 'God hath shewed Pharaoh what HE is about to do' (et asher ha'Elohim oseh). Not what might happen, not a possibility — what God is doing. Joseph attributes the revelation to 'ha'Elohim' (THE God, the true God) — not to Egypt's deities. Before the most powerful man on earth, Joseph proclaims the sovereignty of Israel's God over Egypt's future.
verses.doctrinal_note: God reveals His plans before executing them: 'Surely the Lord GOD will do nothing, but he revealeth his secret unto his servants the prophets' (Amos 3:7). This principle — that God warns before He judges and reveals before He acts — runs throughout Scripture. Pharaoh's dream is not fortune-telling but divine disclosure, demonstrating that history is not random but directed by God's sovereign will.

verses.kjv_text: The seven good kine are seven years; and the seven good ears are seven years: the dream is one.
verses.original_text: שֶׁבַע פָּרֹת הַטֹּבֹת שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים הֵנָּה וְשֶׁבַע הַשִּׁבֳּלִים הַטֹּבֹת שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים הֵנָּה חֲלוֹם אֶחָד הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Sheva parot hattovot sheva shanim hennah vesheva hashibbolim hattovot sheva shanim hennah chalom echad hu.
verses.study_note: Joseph decodes the symbolism directly: cows = years, ears = years. Both symbols point to the same reality — seven years. The repetition 'the dream is one' reinforces that God has spoken a single, unified message through two complementary images. The interpretation is clear, authoritative, and brooking no ambiguity — the hallmark of genuine prophecy versus the vague oracles of pagan seers.

verses.kjv_text: And the seven thin and ill favoured kine that came up after them are seven years; and the seven empty ears blasted with the east wind shall be seven years of famine.
verses.original_text: וְשֶׁבַע הַפָּרוֹת הָרַקּוֹת וְהָרָעֹת הָעֹלֹת אַחֲרֵיהֶן שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים הֵנָּה וְשֶׁבַע הַשִּׁבֳּלִים הָרֵקוֹת שְׁדוּפֹת הַקָּדִים יִהְיוּ שֶׁבַע שְׁנֵי רָעָב׃
verses.transliteration: Vesheva haparot harakkot vehara'ot ha'olot achareihen sheva shanim hennah vesheva hashibbolim hareikot shedufot hakkadim yihyu sheva shenei ra'av.
verses.study_note: Joseph names the famine directly: 'seven years of famine' (sheva shenei ra'av). The word 'ra'av' (famine/hunger) will dominate the rest of Genesis. The empty ears 'blasted with the east wind' represent not merely poor harvests but years where nature itself turns hostile. This is the first prophetic announcement of a specific timeline in Scripture — seven years, precisely.

verses.kjv_text: This is the thing which I have spoken unto Pharaoh: What God is about to do he sheweth unto Pharaoh.
verses.original_text: הוּא הַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְתִּי אֶל־פַּרְעֹה אֲשֶׁר הָאֱלֹהִים עֹשֶׂה הֶרְאָה אֶת־פַּרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Hu haddavar asher dibbarti el-Par'oh asher ha'Elohim oseh her'ah et-Par'oh.
verses.study_note: Joseph reiterates: 'What God is about to do he sheweth unto Pharaoh' (her'ah — has caused to see). God has given Pharaoh a preview of coming events. The verb 'her'ah' (caused to see) implies that the dream was not random but a deliberate divine act of disclosure. Even a pagan king is not beyond God's capacity to reveal His plans.

verses.kjv_text: Behold, there come seven years of great plenty throughout all the land of Egypt:
verses.original_text: הִנֵּה שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים בָּאוֹת שָׂבָע גָּדוֹל בְּכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Hinneh sheva shanim ba'ot sava gadol bekhol-eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: Joseph now lays out the timeline: 'seven years of great plenty' (sava gadol — great abundance/satiety). The word 'sava' means satisfaction, fullness to the point of surfeit. This abundance is 'throughout all the land of Egypt' — nationwide prosperity that will tempt complacency. The very abundance becomes the opportunity for salvation: only by wisely using the years of plenty can Egypt survive the years of famine.

verses.kjv_text: And there shall arise after them seven years of famine; and all the plenty shall be forgotten in the land of Egypt; and the famine shall consume the land;
verses.original_text: וְקָמוּ שֶׁבַע שְׁנֵי רָעָב אַחֲרֵיהֶן וְנִשְׁכַּח כָּל־הַשָּׂבָע בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם וְכִלָּה הָרָעָב אֶת־הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vekamu sheva shenei ra'av achareihen venishkach kol-hassava be'eretz Mitzrayim vekhillah hara'av et-ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The famine will 'arise' (kamu) — the same verb used for an enemy rising in battle. 'All the plenty shall be forgotten' (venishkach kol-hassava) — fulfilling the dream detail of v.21 where the thin cows showed no benefit from devouring the fat. The verb 'vekhillah' (shall consume/finish off) is devastating: the famine will 'finish' the land, bringing it to complete exhaustion. This is Joseph's interpretation of the fat cows being consumed without trace.

verses.kjv_text: And the plenty shall not be known in the land by reason of that famine following; for it shall be very grievous.
verses.original_text: וְלֹא־יִוָּדַע הַשָּׂבָע בָּאָרֶץ מִפְּנֵי הָרָעָב הַהוּא אַחֲרֵי־כֵן כִּי־כָבֵד הוּא מְאֹד׃
verses.transliteration: Velo-yivvada hassava ba'aretz mippenei hara'av hahu acharei-khen ki-khaved hu me'od.
verses.study_note: Joseph emphasizes: 'it shall be very grievous' (khaved hu me'od — literally 'heavy it is exceedingly'). 'Khaved' (heavy/severe) shares the root with 'kavod' (glory/weight); the famine will be weighty, burdensome, crushing. This detail explains why mere prudent planning is insufficient — the famine will be of supernatural severity, requiring extraordinary preparation under divine guidance.

verses.kjv_text: And for that the dream was doubled unto Pharaoh twice; it is because the thing is established by God, and God will shortly bring it to pass.
verses.original_text: וְעַל הִשָּׁנוֹת הַחֲלוֹם אֶל־פַּרְעֹה פַּעֲמָיִם כִּי־נָכוֹן הַדָּבָר מֵעִם הָאֱלֹהִים וּמְמַהֵר הָאֱלֹהִים לַעֲשֹׂתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'al hishanot hachalom el-Par'oh pa'amayim ki-nakhon haddavar me'im ha'Elohim umemaher ha'Elohim la'asoto.
verses.study_note: Joseph explains the doubling: 'the thing is established' (nakhon haddavar — the matter is certain, fixed, determined) 'by God' (me'im ha'Elohim — from the presence of God). Two confirmations: (1) the decree is irreversible, and (2) God will 'shortly bring it to pass' (umemaher — is hastening). The doubling principle appears elsewhere: two witnesses establish truth (Deut 19:15), dreams doubled confirm certainty. This verse is a masterclass in biblical hermeneutics — repetition in Scripture signals divine certainty.
verses.doctrinal_note: The doubling of divine revelation signals certainty and imminence. This principle operates throughout Scripture: God spoke creation twice ('Let there be... and there was'), the Law was given on two tablets, and prophetic fulfillment often comes in twofold patterns (near and far). When God repeats Himself, the matter is sealed and the execution is imminent.

verses.kjv_text: Now therefore let Pharaoh look out a man discreet and wise, and set him over the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה יֵרֶא פַרְעֹה אִישׁ נָבוֹן וְחָכָם וִישִׁיתֵהוּ עַל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah yere Far'oh ish navon vechakham vishitehu al-eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: Joseph transitions remarkably from prophet to advisor. Having interpreted the dream, he now offers practical counsel — uninvited but divinely inspired. 'A man discreet and wise' (navon vechakham): 'navon' means discerning, insightful, able to perceive connections; 'chakham' means wise, skilled, practically intelligent. Joseph is not nominating himself — but he perfectly describes himself. This blend of prophetic insight and practical wisdom characterizes the Spirit-filled leader.

verses.kjv_text: Let Pharaoh do this, and let him appoint officers over the land, and take up the fifth part of the land of Egypt in the seven plenteous years.
verses.original_text: יַעֲשֶׂה פַרְעֹה וְיַפְקֵד פְּקִדִים עַל־הָאָרֶץ וְחִמֵּשׁ אֶת־אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם בְּשֶׁבַע שְׁנֵי הַשָּׂבָע׃
verses.transliteration: Ya'aseh Far'oh veyafked pekidim al-ha'aretz vechimmesh et-eretz Mitzrayim besheva shenei hassava.
verses.study_note: Joseph proposes a 20% grain tax ('the fifth part' — chimesh) during the years of plenty. This is remarkably moderate — enough to build reserves without crushing the population. 'Pekidim' (officers/overseers) are appointed administrators. Joseph's plan combines prophetic foresight with administrative genius: a centralized storage system with regional oversight. It is the first recorded national economic policy in human history.

verses.kjv_text: And let them gather all the food of those good years that come, and lay up corn under the hand of Pharaoh, and let them keep food in the cities.
verses.original_text: וְיִקְבְּצוּ אֶת־כָּל־אֹכֶל הַשָּׁנִים הַטֹּבוֹת הַבָּאֹת הָאֵלֶּה וְיִצְבְּרוּ־בָר תַּחַת יַד־פַּרְעֹה אֹכֶל בֶּעָרִים וְשָׁמָרוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Veyikbetzu et-kol-okhel hashanim hattovot habba'ot ha'elleh veyitzberu-var tachat yad-Par'oh okhel be'arim veshamaru.
verses.study_note: A decentralized storage system: grain collected nationally but stored locally in cities. 'Under the hand of Pharaoh' (tachat yad-Par'oh) — royal authority guarantees and protects the reserves. The verb 'yitzberu' (heap up/store) and 'shamaru' (guard/keep) imply not just collection but careful preservation. Joseph's plan anticipates modern grain reserve management by millennia.

verses.kjv_text: And that food shall be for store to the land against the seven years of famine, which shall be in the land of Egypt; that the land perish not through the famine.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה הָאֹכֶל לְפִקָּדוֹן לָאָרֶץ לְשֶׁבַע שְׁנֵי הָרָעָב אֲשֶׁר תִּהְיֶיןָ בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם וְלֹא־תִכָּרֵת הָאָרֶץ בָּרָעָב׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah ha'okhel lefikkafon la'aretz lesheva shenei hara'av asher tihyena be'eretz Mitzrayim velo-tikkaret ha'aretz bara'av.
verses.study_note: The purpose: 'that the land perish not' (velo-tikkaret — not be cut off/destroyed). The verb 'karat' (cut off) is the covenant-cutting word used in Genesis 15:18. To be 'cut off' means total termination. Joseph's plan is ultimately about preserving life — a mission that will extend far beyond Egypt to his own family, fulfilling God's covenant promise to Abraham. The food is 'for store' (lefikkafon — a deposit, a reserve held in trust).

verses.kjv_text: And the thing was good in the eyes of Pharaoh, and in the eyes of all his servants.
verses.original_text: וַיִּיטַב הַדָּבָר בְּעֵינֵי פַרְעֹה וּבְעֵינֵי כָּל־עֲבָדָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitav haddavar be'einei Far'oh uve'einei kol-avadav.
verses.study_note: Universal approval: 'good in the eyes of Pharaoh, and in the eyes of all his servants.' The word 'vayyitav' (was good) echoes God's evaluation of creation (Gen 1). Where Egypt's entire wisdom class had failed (v.8), a Hebrew slave from prison has provided both revelation and solution. The court unanimously recognizes the wisdom — a testimony to the undeniable quality of Spirit-given insight.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh said unto his servants, Can we find such a one as this is, a man in whom the Spirit of God is?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה אֶל־עֲבָדָיו הֲנִמְצָא כָזֶה אִישׁ אֲשֶׁר רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים בּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Par'oh el-avadav hanimtza khazeh ish asher ruach Elohim bo.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh's rhetorical question is one of the most remarkable pagan testimonies to God in the Old Testament: 'a man in whom the Spirit of God is' (ruach Elohim bo). A pagan king recognizes what the text has been showing: Joseph's wisdom is not human but divine. 'Ruach Elohim' (Spirit of God) — the same phrase from Genesis 1:2 where the Spirit hovered over creation. The Spirit who brought order from chaos in creation now brings order from impending chaos through Joseph.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Spirit of God is recognizable even to unbelievers. Pharaoh cannot interpret the dream, but he can recognize the Spirit behind the interpretation. This demonstrates that the fruit of the Spirit (wisdom, discernment, practical skill) has an undeniable quality that even the world acknowledges. Nebuchadnezzar makes the same observation about Daniel (Dan 4:8-9, 5:11-14). The Spirit's presence is self-authenticating.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh said unto Joseph, Forasmuch as God hath shewed thee all this, there is none so discreet and wise as thou art:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה אֶל־יוֹסֵף אַחֲרֵי הוֹדִיעַ אֱלֹהִים אוֹתְךָ אֶת־כָּל־זֹאת אֵין־נָבוֹן וְחָכָם כָּמוֹךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Par'oh el-Yosef acharei hodia Elohim otekha et-kol-zot ein-navon vechakham kamokha.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh uses Joseph's own words back to him: 'navon vechakham' (discreet and wise) — the very qualities Joseph recommended in v.33. Pharaoh's logic is impeccable: if God has revealed this to Joseph, then Joseph is the wisest man available. The one who can diagnose the problem is best positioned to administer the solution. Pharaoh credits 'Elohim' (God) four times in vv.38-39, showing genuine recognition of divine power.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt be over my house, and according unto thy word shall all my people be ruled: only in the throne will I be greater than thou.
verses.original_text: אַתָּה תִּהְיֶה עַל־בֵּיתִי וְעַל־פִּיךָ יִשַּׁק כָּל־עַמִּי רַק הַכִּסֵּא אֶגְדַּל מִמֶּךָּ׃
verses.transliteration: Attah tihyeh al-beti ve'al-pikha yishak kol-ammi rak hakkisse egdal mimmekka.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh's decree elevates Joseph to the second highest position in Egypt: 'over my house' (al-beti — over the royal household), 'all my people shall be ruled' by his word (al-pikha yishak — literally 'by your mouth shall all my people kiss/obey'). 'Only in the throne will I be greater' — Joseph receives all authority except the throne itself. This mirrors Christ's exaltation: 'all authority in heaven and earth' under the Father's sovereignty (Matt 28:18, Phil 2:9-11). From dungeon to dominion in a single audience.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh said unto Joseph, See, I have set thee over all the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה אֶל־יוֹסֵף רְאֵה נָתַתִּי אֹתְךָ עַל כָּל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
