verses.study_note: Their explanation begins deferentially: 'O sir' (bi adoni — 'please, my lord'). 'We came indeed down' (yarod yaradnu — emphatic infinitive absolute) stresses the innocence of their original purpose. They establish the baseline: they came to buy food, nothing more.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when we came to the inn, that we opened our sacks, and, behold, every man’s money was in the mouth of his sack, our money in full weight: and we have brought it again in our hand.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כִּי־בָאנוּ אֶל־הַמָּלוֹן וַנִּפְתְּחָה אֶת־אַמְתְּחֹתֵינוּ וְהִנֵּה כֶסֶף־אִישׁ בְּפִי אַמְתַּחְתּוֹ כַּסְפֵּנוּ בְּמִשְׁקָלוֹ וַנָּשֶׁב אֹתוֹ בְּיָדֵנוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi ki-vanu el-hammalon vannifttechah et-amtechotenu vehineh khesef-ish befi amtachtto kaspenu bemishkalo vannashev oto beyadenu.
verses.study_note: They describe finding their money 'in full weight' (bemishkalo) — they are not returning partial amounts but the exact sum. Their honesty is painstaking. 'We have brought it again in our hand' — they physically returned the money. This act of integrity, though motivated by fear, demonstrates a growing moral conscience in the brothers.

verses.kjv_text: And other money have we brought down in our hands to buy food: we cannot tell who put our money in our sacks.
verses.original_text: וְכֶסֶף אַחֵר הוֹרַדְנוּ בְיָדֵנוּ לִשְׁבָּר־אֹכֶל לֹא יָדַעְנוּ מִי־שָׂם כַּסְפֵּנוּ בְּאַמְתְּחֹתֵינוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhesef acher horadnu veyadenu lishbar-okhel lo yadanu mi-sam kaspenu be'amtechotenu.
verses.study_note: They brought additional money for new purchases and honestly confess ignorance: 'we cannot tell who put our money in our sacks' (lo yadanu — 'we do not know'). Their transparency is complete. They withhold nothing, fabricate nothing. This is a marked change from the brothers who once deceived their father with a bloodied coat (Gen 37:31-32). The process of repentance involves learning to tell the whole truth.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Peace be to you, fear not: your God, and the God of your father, hath given you treasure in your sacks: I had your money. And he brought Simeon out unto them.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר שָׁלוֹם לָכֶם אַל־תִּירָאוּ אֱלֹהֵיכֶם וֵאלֹהֵי אֲבִיכֶם נָתַן לָכֶם מַטְמוֹן בְּאַמְתְּחֹתֵיכֶם כַּסְפְּכֶם בָּא אֵלָי וַיּוֹצֵא אֲלֵהֶם אֶת־שִׁמְעוֹן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer shalom lakhem al-tira'u Eloheikhem ve'Elohei avikhem natan lakhem matmon be'amtechoteikem kaspekhem ba elai vayyotze aleihem et-Shim'on.
verses.study_note: The steward's response is astonishing: 'Peace be to you, fear not' (shalom lakhem al-tira'u) — the exact words God speaks to the fearful throughout Scripture (Gen 15:1, Judg 6:23, Luke 2:10). Then a theological statement the brothers did not expect: 'Your God, and the God of your father, hath given you treasure' (matmon — hidden treasure). The steward attributes the money to divine gift, not human error. 'I had your money' (kaspekhem ba elai — 'your money came to me') — the transaction was complete, the money accepted. Then Simeon is released — the hostage returned. Grace upon grace: forgiveness of debt, a gift of treasure, and the restoration of the captive.
verses.doctrinal_note: The steward's words picture the Gospel: sinners who expect punishment hear 'peace, fear not.' Their debt has already been paid ('I had your money'), and what they tried to return was actually a gift from God ('treasure in your sacks'). The release of Simeon — the captive brother — pictures liberation from bondage. All of this was arranged by Joseph, unseen and unrecognized, just as Christ works behind the scenes of our lives.

verses.kjv_text: And the man brought the men into Joseph’s house, and gave them water, and they washed their feet; and he gave their asses provender.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֵא הָאִישׁ אֶת־הָאֲנָשִׁים בֵּיתָה יוֹסֵף וַיִּתֶּן־מַיִם וַיִּרְחֲצוּ רַגְלֵיהֶם וַיִּתֵּן מִסְפּוֹא לַחֲמֹרֵיהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyave ha'ish et-ha'anashim betah Yosef vayyitten-mayim vayyirchatzu ragleihem vayyitten mispo la'chamorehem.
verses.study_note: Hospitality is extended: water for foot-washing, fodder for the animals. These are the acts of a generous host, not a tyrant preparing to enslave them. The foot-washing echoes Abraham's hospitality (Gen 18:4) and foreshadows Jesus washing His disciples' feet (John 13:5). The brothers who came expecting chains receive service instead — the ruler's household treats them as honored guests.

verses.kjv_text: And they made ready the present against Joseph came at noon: for they heard that they should eat bread there.
verses.original_text: וַיָּכִינוּ אֶת־הַמִּנְחָה עַד־בּוֹא יוֹסֵף בַּצָּהֳרָיִם כִּי שָׁמְעוּ כִּי־שָׁם יֹאכְלוּ לָחֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyakhinu et-hamminchah ad-bo Yosef batzohorayim ki shamu ki-sham yokhelu lachem.
verses.study_note: The brothers prepare their gift (minchah) for Joseph's arrival at noon. 'They heard that they should eat bread there' — the unexpected news that they are being hosted, not interrogated, begins to register. 'Eat bread' (yokhelu lachem) — in Hebrew culture, sharing a meal implies covenant relationship and peace. They are being invited into fellowship with the one they wronged, though they do not yet know it.

verses.kjv_text: And when Joseph came home, they brought him the present which was in their hand into the house, and bowed themselves to him to the earth.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא יוֹסֵף הַבַּיְתָה וַיָּבִיאוּ לוֹ אֶת־הַמִּנְחָה אֲשֶׁר־בְּיָדָם הַבָּיְתָה וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ־לוֹ אָרְצָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo Yosef habbayetah vayyavi'u lo et-hamminchah asher-beyadam habbayetah vayyishtachavu-lo artzah.
verses.study_note: The dreams fulfilled — again. 'They bowed themselves to him to the earth' (vayyishtachavu-lo artzah). This is at least the third fulfillment of Joseph's boyhood dreams (Gen 37:7,9): first when they arrived (42:6), now with Benjamin present. The gift (minchah) is presented with the bow — offering tribute to the ruler they do not recognize as their brother. The scene is laden with dramatic irony: the one they bow to is the one they cast down into a pit.

verses.kjv_text: And he asked them of their welfare, and said, Is your father well, the old man of whom ye spake? Is he yet alive?
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁאַל לָהֶם לְשָׁלוֹם וַיֹּאמֶר הֲשָׁלוֹם אֲבִיכֶם הַזָּקֵן אֲשֶׁר אֲמַרְתֶּם הַעוֹדֶנּוּ חָי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyish'al lahem leshalom vayomer hashalom avikhem hazzaken asher amartem ha'odennu chai.
verses.study_note: Joseph inquires about Jacob — 'Is your father well?' (hashalom avikhem). The word 'shalom' here is not merely 'peace' but 'wellness, wholeness.' Joseph asks about 'the old man of whom ye spake' — maintaining his disguise while aching to know about his father. 'Is he yet alive?' (ha'odennu chai) — the most personal question, revealing Joseph's deep longing. Over twenty years of separation, and every day he wondered if his father still lived.

verses.kjv_text: And they answered, Thy servant our father is in good health, he is yet alive. And they bowed down their heads, and made obeisance.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ שָׁלוֹם לְעַבְדְּךָ לְאָבִינוּ עוֹדֶנּוּ חָי וַיִּקְּדוּ וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲוּוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru shalom le'avdekha le'avinu odennu chai vayyikkdu vayyishtachavu.
verses.study_note: They call Jacob 'thy servant our father' (avdekha avinu) — a deeply ironic title. Jacob, the patriarch, is called 'servant' before his own lost son. 'They bowed down their heads, and made obeisance' (vayyikkdu vayyishtachavu) — two verbs of prostration, emphasizing complete submission. Every bow brings the dreams closer to their ultimate fulfillment and draws the family closer to reconciliation.

verses.kjv_text: And he lifted up his eyes, and saw his brother Benjamin, his mother’s son, and said, Is this your younger brother, of whom ye spake unto me? And he said, God be gracious unto thee, my son.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשָּׂא עֵינָיו וַיַּרְא אֶת־בִּנְיָמִין אָחִיו בֶּן־אִמּוֹ וַיֹּאמֶר הֲזֶה אֲחִיכֶם הַקָּטֹן אֲשֶׁר אֲמַרְתֶּם אֵלָי וַיֹּאמַר אֱלֹהִים יָחְנְךָ בְּנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyissa einav vayyar et-Binyamin achiv ben-immo vayomer hazeh achikhem hakkaton asher amartem elai vayomar Elohim yachonekha beni.
verses.study_note: The emotional heart of the chapter. Joseph 'lifted up his eyes' (vayyissa einav) — a deliberate, intentional looking. He sees 'his brother Benjamin, his mother's son' (achiv ben-immo) — the narrator specifies 'his mother's son,' emphasizing the unique bond through Rachel. Joseph blesses Benjamin: 'God be gracious unto thee, my son' (Elohim yachonekha beni). The word 'yachonekha' is from 'chanan' — to show grace, to be merciful — the same root as 'chen' (grace). Joseph calls Benjamin 'my son' (beni) — an expression of tender affection that nearly reveals his identity.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph made haste; for his bowels did yearn upon his brother: and he sought where to weep; and he entered into his chamber, and wept there.
verses.original_text: וַיְמַהֵר יוֹסֵף כִּי־נִכְמְרוּ רַחֲמָיו אֶל־אָחִיו וַיְבַקֵּשׁ לִבְכּוֹת וַיָּבֹא הַחַדְרָה וַיֵּבְךְּ שָׁמָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayemaher Yosef ki-nikhmeru rachamav el-achiv vayevakkesh livkot vayyavo hachadrah vayyevk shammah.
verses.study_note: Joseph can no longer contain his emotions. 'His bowels did yearn' (nikhmeru rachamav — literally 'his compassions were kindled/heated') — 'rachamim' (compassion, from 'rechem,' womb) describes the deepest, most visceral emotion. This is the same word Jacob prayed for in verse 14 — and God is answering that prayer through Joseph. Joseph 'sought where to weep' — he must hide his tears to maintain the disguise and complete the test. He retreats to his chamber (hachadrah — the inner room). This is the first of three times Joseph weeps in the reunion narrative (43:30, 45:2, 46:29), each more public than the last.

verses.kjv_text: And he washed his face, and went out, and refrained himself, and said, Set on bread.
verses.original_text: וַיִּרְחַץ פָּנָיו וַיֵּצֵא וַיִּתְאַפַּק וַיֹּאמֶר שִׂימוּ לָחֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyirchatz panav vayyetze vayyit'appak vayomer simu lachem.
verses.study_note: Joseph composes himself: washes his face, restrains himself (vayyit'appak — from 'afak,' to hold in, to control). The verb 'it'appak' appears again at the climax in 45:1: 'Joseph could not refrain himself' — when the restraint finally breaks and he reveals himself. Here, the test is not yet complete. 'Set on bread' (simu lachem) — with remarkable self-control, Joseph returns to his role as host and commands the feast to begin.

verses.kjv_text: And they set on for him by himself, and for them by themselves, and for the Egyptians, which did eat with him, by themselves: because the Egyptians might not eat bread with the Hebrews; for that is an abomination unto the Egyptians.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשִׂימוּ לוֹ לְבַדּוֹ וְלָהֶם לְבַדָּם וְלַמִּצְרִים הָאֹכְלִים אִתּוֹ לְבַדָּם כִּי לֹא יוּכְלוּן הַמִּצְרִים לֶאֱכֹל אֶת־הָעִבְרִים לֶחֶם כִּי־תוֹעֵבָה הִוא לְמִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyasimu lo levaddo velahem levaddam velamMitzrim ha'okhelim itto levaddam ki lo yukhelun haMitzrim le'ekhol et-ha'Ivrim lechem ki-to'evah hi leMitzrayim.
verses.study_note: Three separate tables: one for Joseph, one for the brothers, one for the Egyptians. Eating with Hebrews was 'an abomination' (to'evah) to Egyptians — a cultural taboo related to dietary, religious, and social distinctions. Joseph sits apart, maintaining his Egyptian identity. The word 'Ivrim' (Hebrews) identifies the brothers ethnically — from 'ever' (to cross over), marking them as the people who crossed over, the outsiders. This social separation anticipates the larger separation between Egypt and Israel that defines the Exodus narrative.

verses.kjv_text: And they sat before him, the firstborn according to his birthright, and the youngest according to his youth: and the men marvelled one at another.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּשְׁבוּ לְפָנָיו הַבְּכוֹר כִּבְכֹרָתוֹ וְהַצָּעִיר כִּצְעִרָתוֹ וַיִּתְמְהוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים אִישׁ אֶל־רֵעֵהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyeshvu lefanav habbekhor kikhvorato vehatza'ir kitze'irato vayyitmahu ha'anashim ish el-re'ehu.
verses.study_note: One of the most striking details in the narrative. The brothers are seated 'the firstborn according to his birthright, and the youngest according to his youth' — in exact birth order. With eleven brothers, the probability of random correct seating is approximately 1 in 40 million. 'The men marvelled one at another' (vayyitmahu — they were astonished, bewildered). The word 'tamah' means to be stunned with wonder. How could this Egyptian lord know their birth order? The detail serves multiple purposes: it demonstrates Joseph's intimate knowledge of them, it unsettles their composure, and it signals divine sovereignty — the One who orders the stars also orders the seating.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's omniscience displayed through Joseph: the brothers are 'known' more deeply than they realize. This mirrors the believer's experience before God — 'O LORD, thou hast searched me, and known me' (Psalm 139:1). The precise ordering speaks to God's sovereign arrangement of all things according to His purpose.

verses.kjv_text: And he took and sent messes unto them from before him: but Benjamin’s mess was five times so much as any of theirs. And they drank, and were merry with him.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשָּׂא מַשְׂאֹת מֵאֵת פָּנָיו אֲלֵהֶם וַתֵּרֶב מַשְׂאַת בִּנְיָמִן מִמַּשְׂאֹת כֻּלָּם חָמֵשׁ יָדוֹת וַיִּשְׁתּוּ וַיִּשְׁכְּרוּ עִמּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyissa mas'ot me'et panav aleihem vatterev mas'at Binyamin mimmeas'ot kullam chamesh yadot vayyishtu vayyishkeru immo.
verses.study_note: The feast reaches its climax. Joseph sends 'messes' (mas'ot — portions) from his own table to each brother — a sign of honor. But Benjamin receives 'five times so much' (chamesh yadot — five hands/portions). The number five is significant in the Joseph narrative: five changes of garments for Benjamin (45:22), five brothers presented to Pharaoh (47:2), and a fifth of the harvest for Pharaoh (47:24). The fivefold portion tests the brothers: will they resent this favoritism toward Rachel's son, as they once resented Joseph's favored status? 'They drank, and were merry with him' (vayyishkeru — literally 'became drunk/satisfied') — they pass the test. No jealousy, no bitterness. The brothers who sold Joseph out of envy over a coat now celebrate when Benjamin receives excess. Transformation is underway.
verses.doctrinal_note: Joseph's lavish, disproportionate generosity to Benjamin tests whether the brothers have truly changed. The feast pictures divine grace: the Ruler provides abundantly from his own table, the undeserving receive far more than they expected, and the one specially favored receives superabundant blessing. 'Where sin abounded, grace did much more abound' (Romans 5:20). The brothers' peaceful acceptance of Benjamin's favor shows the fruit of genuine repentance.

meta.overview: Genesis 43 marks the dramatic turning point in the Joseph narrative. The famine intensifies, forcing Jacob to send his sons back to Egypt — this time with Benjamin, the one he swore never to release. Judah steps forward with a surety pledge that echoes substitutionary atonement: he personally guarantees Benjamin's safety, bearing the blame forever if he fails. The brothers return to Egypt carrying double money and gifts, trembling with fear. Joseph, seeing Benjamin for the first time in over twenty years, is overcome with emotion and must withdraw to weep privately. The chapter climaxes with a lavish feast where the brothers are seated in birth order — an impossibility by chance — and Benjamin receives five times the portion of the others. Throughout, the narrative weaves themes of substitutionary guarantee, unmerited grace, sovereign providence, and the slow, painful process of reconciliation. Joseph tests his brothers to see whether they have changed, while God orchestrates every detail toward the reunion He promised.
meta.theme: Substitutionary Surety, Unmerited Grace & the Path to Reconciliation
meta.keyWordMeaning: To pledge, to be surety — Judah's guarantee for Benjamin, bearing the guilt personally. A profound type of Christ's substitutionary work.
outline: The Famine Forces a Decision (vv.1-7): Jacob resists sending Benjamin; the brothers insist
Judah's Surety Pledge (vv.8-10): Judah guarantees Benjamin's return — a type of Christ
Preparation and Departure (vv.11-15): Jacob sends gifts, double money, and Benjamin
Fear at Joseph's House (vv.16-25): The brothers fear a trap; the steward reassures them
Joseph Sees Benjamin (vv.26-30): Overwhelmed with emotion, Joseph weeps privately
The Feast — Grace Upon Grace (vv.31-34): The brothers seated in order; Benjamin receives fivefold

verses.kjv_text: And he commanded the steward of his house, saying, Fill the men’s sacks with food, as much as they can carry, and put every man’s money in his sack’s mouth.
verses.original_text: וַיְצַו אֶת־אֲשֶׁר עַל־בֵּיתוֹ לֵאמֹר מַלֵּא אֶת־אַמְתְּחֹת הָאֲנָשִׁים אֹכֶל כַּאֲשֶׁר יוּכְלוּן שְׂאֵת וְשִׂים כֶּסֶף־אִישׁ בְּפִי אַמְתַּחְתּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vaytzav et-asher al-beto lemor male et-amtechot ha'anashim okhel ka'asher yukhlun set ve'sim kesef-ish befi amtachto.
verses.study_note: Joseph commands his steward in secret — the brothers know nothing of what is being arranged. This is the third time money is placed in the brothers' sacks: first in chapter 42 as an unintended act that terrified them, second when they brought it back and received more (43:21-22), and now deliberately as part of a trap. The repetition of the sack-and-money motif runs through chapters 42-44, each occurrence ratcheting up the tension. Joseph's command 'as much as they can carry' (ka'asher yukhlun set) reveals that even in devising a test, he is generous — the grace and the trial come from the same hand. The steward is Joseph's instrument, just as Egyptian overseers were instruments in the earlier narrative; now Joseph directs rather than suffers such power.

verses.kjv_text: And put my cup, the silver cup, in the sack’s mouth of the youngest, and his corn money. And he did according to the word that Joseph had spoken.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־גְּבִיעִי גְּבִיעַ הַכֶּסֶף תָּשִׂים בְּפִי אַמְתַּחַת הַקָּטֹן וְאֵת כֶּסֶף שִׁבְרוֹ וַיַּעַשׂ כִּדְבַר יוֹסֵף אֲשֶׁר דִּבֵּר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-gevi'i gavia hakesef tasim befi amtachat hakkaton ve'et kesef shivro vayya'as kidvar Yosef asher dibber.
verses.study_note: The cup receives extraordinary emphasis: 'my cup, the silver cup' — the doubling (gevi'i gavia hakesef) is emphatic and possessive. This is not merely tableware but a personal, distinctive object, likely a ceremonial goblet used in Joseph's household. The cup is placed in Benjamin's sack specifically — Joseph targets the full brother, the son of Rachel, to bring the maximum emotional weight to bear. Benjamin is the pivot point: the brothers once abandoned Joseph (Rachel's firstborn); will they now abandon Benjamin (Rachel's second son)? 'He did according to the word that Joseph had spoken' — the steward's quiet obedience mirrors the narrative's pattern: what Joseph orders comes to pass exactly, foreshadowing divine Providence working through his authority.

verses.kjv_text: As soon as the morning was light, the men were sent away, they and their asses.
verses.original_text: הַבֹּקֶר אוֹר וְהָאֲנָשִׁים שֻׁלְּחוּ הֵמָּה וַחֲמֹרֵיהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Habboker or veha'anashim shullchu hemmah vachamorehem.
verses.study_note: The dawn dismissal is deliberately paced — the narrator captures the moment of their departure with spare, vivid detail. 'The morning was light' (habboker or) — the same construction of light breaking over a new scene that marks other decisive mornings in Genesis: the dawn of Sodom's destruction (19:23), Abraham's early rising for Moriah (22:3). The brothers leave in confidence; they have feasted (43:34), they have been released, and they believe the ordeal in Egypt is behind them. The 'asses' (chamorehim) detail is practical but also signals the full company departing — the journey home was fully underway. The irony is intense: they do not know the cup is already in Benjamin's sack.

verses.kjv_text: And when they were gone out of the city, and not yet far off, Joseph said unto his steward, Up, follow after the men; and when thou dost overtake them, say unto them, Wherefore have ye rewarded evil for good?
verses.original_text: הֵם יָצְאוּ אֶת־הָעִיר לֹא הִרְחִיקוּ וְיוֹסֵף אָמַר לַאֲשֶׁר עַל־בֵּיתוֹ קוּם רְדֹף אַחֲרֵי הָאֲנָשִׁים וְהִשַּׂגְתָּם וְאָמַרְתָּ אֲלֵהֶם לָמָּה שִׁלַּמְתֶּם רָעָה תַּחַת טוֹבָה׃
verses.transliteration: Hem yatze'u et-ha'ir lo hirchiqu veYosef amar la'asher al-beto kum redof acharei ha'anashim vehissagtam ve'amarta alehem lammah shillamtem ra'ah tachat tovah.
verses.study_note: Joseph times the pursuit with precision — 'not yet far off' (lo hirchiqu). He waits until they are out of the city gate, allowing a sense of freedom before the reversal. 'Up, follow after the men' (kum redof acharei) — urgent pursuit language, the same urgency used for rescuing Lot (Gen 19). The rhetorical accusation 'wherefore have ye rewarded evil for good?' (lammah shillamtem ra'ah tachat tovah) is enormously ironic: Joseph once received evil for the good of his faithful service; now his brothers are accused of precisely what they actually did to him years before. The lex talionis principle — evil for good — is the exact dynamic Joseph is about to expose, not about the cup, but about their entire past.

verses.kjv_text: Is not this it in which my lord drinketh, and whereby indeed he divineth? ye have done evil in so doing.
verses.original_text: הֲלוֹא זֶה אֲשֶׁר יִשְׁתֶּה אֲדֹנִי בּוֹ וְהוּא נַחֵשׁ יְנַחֵשׁ בּוֹ הֲרֵעֹתֶם אֲשֶׁר עֲשִׂיתֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Halo zeh asher yishteh adoni bo vehu nachesh yenachesh bo hare'otem asher asitem.
verses.study_note: The accusation layers two charges: (1) theft of Joseph's personal drinking cup, and (2) theft of a divination instrument. 'Whereby indeed he divineth' (nachesh yenachesh — emphatic infinitive absolute) — this phrase has troubled commentators for centuries. Does Joseph actually practice divination? Most likely this is part of the dramatic fiction Joseph constructs to make his role as an Egyptian lord convincing. The doubling 'nachesh yenachesh' (from the root 'nachash') mimics the language of pagan court oracles, reinforcing the brothers' belief that they are dealing with an Egyptian potentate of supernatural insight. The irony is that Joseph does not need a cup to discern — God has given him genuine prophetic gifts (dreams, interpretations). The cup is the cultural costume of divination, not the source of his knowledge.

verses.kjv_text: And he overtook them, and he spake unto them these same words.
verses.original_text: וַיַּשִּׂגֵם וַיְדַבֵּר אֲלֵהֶם אֶת־הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyassigem vaydabber alehem et-haddevarim ha'elleh.
verses.study_note: The steward's obedient execution is reported in brief — 'these same words' (et-haddevarim ha'elleh). The narrative economy here is deliberate: the accusation has already been stated; there is no need to repeat it. The effect on the reader is that of a trap closing. The brothers' world, which had been brightening through Benjamin's safe inclusion (43:30-34), now collapses suddenly. This verse is the pivot point between Joseph's plan and the brothers' response.

verses.kjv_text: And they said unto him, Wherefore saith my lord these words? God forbid that thy servants should do according to this thing:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֵלָיו לָמָּה יְדַבֵּר אֲדֹנִי כַּדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה חָלִילָה לַעֲבָדֶיךָ מֵעֲשׂוֹת כַּדָּבָר הַזֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru elav lammah yedabber adoni kaddevarim ha'elleh chalilah la'avadekha me'asot kaddavar hazzeh.
verses.study_note: The brothers' defense begins with genuine shock and moral outrage. 'God forbid' (chalilah) — this is one of the strongest negations in Hebrew, a term of horrified refusal. It means 'far be it, a profanation, an abomination.' The root is 'chalal' (to defile, to pollute). Their indignation is sincere — they genuinely did not steal the cup. Yet the irony is profound: their protest 'chalilah la'avadekha' (far be it from your servants) is a perfect description of the very act they committed years before against Joseph. They are outraged at a false accusation while being guilty of an even greater unacknowledged crime. Their self-defense here will give way, within a few verses, to confession.

verses.kjv_text: Behold, the money, which we found in our sacks’ mouths, we brought again unto thee out of the land of Canaan: how then should we steal out of thy lord’s house silver or gold?
verses.original_text: הֵן כֶּסֶף אֲשֶׁר מָצָאנוּ בְּפִי אַמְתְּחֹתֵינוּ הֱשִׁיבֹנוּ אֵלֶיךָ מֵאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן וְאֵיךְ נִגְנֹב מִבֵּית אֲדֹנֶיךָ כֶּסֶף אוֹ זָהָב׃
verses.transliteration: Hen kesef asher matzanu befi amtechotenu heshivonu eleikha me'eretz Kena'an ve'eikh nignov mibeit adonekha kesef o zahav.
verses.study_note: The brothers construct a compelling argument from prior behavior: they returned the money found in their sacks — a clear demonstration of integrity that any thief would not have shown. The logic is sound: 'If we returned money we found, why would we steal silver or gold?' (ve'eikh nignov). The argument from precedent is powerful and would be convincing to any impartial judge. Yet it is precisely this confidence — this moral self-assurance that they are innocent of this particular charge — that makes the moment of discovery so devastating. They are innocent of this; they are not innocent of everything.

verses.kjv_text: With whomsoever of thy servants it be found, both let him die, and we also will be my lord’s bondmen.
verses.original_text: אֲשֶׁר יִמָּצֵא אִתּוֹ מֵעֲבָדֶיךָ וָמֵת וְגַם־אֲנַחְנוּ נִהְיֶה לַאדֹנִי לַעֲבָדִים׃
verses.transliteration: Asher yimmatze itto me'avadekha vamet vegam-anachnu nihyeh la'adoni la'avadim.
verses.study_note: The brothers' offer is staggering in its confidence — and tragic in its irony. They propose: death for the thief and slavery for the rest. 'Let him die' (vamet) — they volunteer the severest possible penalty because they are absolutely certain of their innocence. But this is the same penalty they effectively proposed for Joseph (their hands were clean of 'blood,' they said, 37:22, as they sold him into slavery). Now, unknowingly, they offer for Benjamin the same fate — death or slavery — that they once inflicted on their brother. Providence turns the brothers' own words into a perfect mirror of their past.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Now also let it be according unto your words: he with whom it is found shall be my servant; and ye shall be blameless.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר גַּם־עַתָּה כְדִבְרֵיכֶם כֶּן־הוּא אֲשֶׁר יִמָּצֵא אִתּוֹ יִהְיֶה־לִּי עָבֶד וְאַתֶּם תִּהְיוּ נְקִיִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer gam-attah khedivreikhem khen-hu asher yimmatze itto yihyeh-li eved ve'attem tihyu nekiyyim.
verses.study_note: The steward modifies the brothers' proposal: only the one with whom the cup is found shall be enslaved — the others go free. 'Ye shall be blameless' (nekiyyim — clean, innocent, acquitted). This is actually more merciful than the brothers' own offer (they proposed all becoming slaves). Yet it will prove more agonizing: the prospect of the rest going free while Benjamin alone is enslaved will become the precise moral crucible that tests whether they will abandon a son of Rachel a second time. The word 'nekiyyim' (blameless/acquitted) is deeply ironic — they are about to be found 'guilty' of one crime while being 'blameless' of another.

verses.kjv_text: Then they speedily took down every man his sack to the ground, and opened every man his sack.
verses.original_text: וַיְמַהֲרוּ וַיּוֹרִידוּ אִישׁ אֶת־אַמְתַּחְתּוֹ אָרְצָה וַיִּפְתְּחוּ אִישׁ אַמְתַּחְתּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vaymaharu vayyoridu ish et-amtachto artzah vayyiftchu ish amtachto.
verses.study_note: The speed of compliance — 'they speedily' (vaymaharu) — reflects their absolute confidence. They cannot unsling their sacks fast enough, cannot open them quickly enough to prove their innocence. The narrative uses the individualizing formula 'every man his sack' (ish et-amtachto) twice in the same verse, emphasizing the completeness and willingness of the search. This confidence will be shattered in the next verse. The faster they move, the more devastating the discovery. Speed here serves irony.

verses.kjv_text: And he searched, and began at the eldest, and left at the youngest: and the cup was found in Benjamin’s sack.
verses.original_text: וַיְחַפֵּשׂ בַּגָּדוֹל הֵחֵל וּבַקָּטֹן כִּלָּה וַיִּמָּצֵא הַגָּבִיעַ בְּאַמְתַּחַת בִּנְיָמִן׃
verses.transliteration: Vaychappes baggadol hechel uvakkaton killah vayyimmtze hagavia be'amtachat Binyamin.
verses.study_note: The search is conducted in deliberate, eldest-to-youngest order — 'began at the eldest and left at the youngest' (baggadol hechel uvakkaton killah). The steward has been instructed to do this; the sequence maximizes suspense and allows each brother a moment of relief before the next is searched. The final phrase is one of the most structurally devastating in the Joseph narrative: 'and the cup was found in Benjamin's sack' (vayyimmtze hagavia be'amtachat Binyamin). Seven Hebrew words that overturn everything. The form 'vayyimmtze' (was found — Niphal passive) is particularly significant: the cup presents itself, is 'found' — the passive voice suggests inevitability, as if Providence itself disclosed what was hidden.

verses.kjv_text: Then they rent their clothes, and laded every man his ass, and returned to the city.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרְעוּ שִׂמְלֹתָם וַיַּעֲמֹס אִישׁ עַל־חֲמֹרוֹ וַיָּשֻׁבוּ הָעִירָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikre'u simlotam vayya'amos ish al-chamoro vayyashuvu ha'irah.
verses.study_note: The tearing of garments (vayyikre'u simlotam) is the most vivid gesture of grief and mourning in the ancient Near East — it signals catastrophic, uncontainable sorrow. Jacob tore his garments when he believed Joseph was dead (37:34); now all the brothers tear theirs. The irony is exquisite: once they tore Joseph's coat and dipped it in blood (37:31-33), causing Jacob to tear his garment in grief. Now they tear their own. The cycle of grief they initiated has returned upon them. They return to the city — they do not abandon Benjamin and go home. This is the first indication of change. Twenty-two years earlier, they 'sat down to eat' (37:25) after disposing of Joseph. Now, they tear their clothes and return together.

verses.kjv_text: And Judah and his brethren came to Joseph’s house; for he was yet there: and they fell before him on the ground.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא יְהוּדָה וְאֶחָיו בֵּיתָה יוֹסֵף וְהוּא עוֹדֶנּוּ שָׁם וַיִּפְּלוּ לְפָנָיו אָרְצָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayavo Yehudah ve'echav betah Yosef vehu odenu sham vayyippelu lefanav artzah.
verses.study_note: The narrative now names Judah first — 'Judah and his brethren' — a grammatically significant shift. Judah now leads. He will carry the entire weight of the coming confrontation. 'He was yet there' (vehu odenu sham) — Joseph has waited, knowing they would return. The brothers 'fell before him on the ground' (vayyippelu lefanav artzah) — the exact fulfillment of Joseph's dream (37:7, 37:9): his brothers' sheaves and stars bowing before him. The brothers bowed three times in chapters 42-44 — once as a group (42:6), again with Benjamin (43:26,28), and now in this moment of crisis. Each prostration deepens the dream's fulfillment without their awareness.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph said unto them, What deed is this that ye have done? wot ye not that such a man as I can certainly divine?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶם יוֹסֵף מָה־הַמַּעֲשֶׂה הַזֶּה אֲשֶׁר עֲשִׂיתֶם הֲלוֹא יְדַעְתֶּם כִּי נַחֵשׁ יְנַחֵשׁ אִישׁ אֲשֶׁר כָּמֹנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer lahem Yosef mah-hamma'aseh hazzeh asher asitem halo yeda'tem ki nachesh yenachesh ish asher kamoni.
verses.study_note: Joseph's opening words are a rhetorical double-strike. First, 'What deed is this that ye have done?' (mah-hamma'aseh hazzeh asher asitem) — on the surface about the cup, but these are almost the exact words God spoke to Adam and Cain after their transgressions (3:13; 4:10). The question penetrates to the deeper deed. Second, the divination claim is repeated — 'such a man as I can certainly divine' (nachesh yenachesh ish asher kamoni). Joseph plays his Egyptian role to the last, maintaining the mystery of who he is. His 'divination' is actually God-given insight; the cup is theater. He knows already what they have done, just as he knew Simeon's guilt (42:24) and arranged the seating (43:33).

verses.kjv_text: And Judah said, What shall we say unto my lord? what shall we speak? or how shall we clear ourselves? God hath found out the iniquity of thy servants: behold, we are my lord’s servants, both we, and he also with whom the cup is found.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה מַה־נֹּאמַר לַאדֹנִי מַה־נְּדַבֵּר וּמַה־נִּצְטַדָּק הָאֱלֹהִים מָצָא אֶת־עֲוֹן עֲבָדֶיךָ הִנֶּנּוּ עֲבָדִים לַאדֹנִי גַּם־אֲנַחְנוּ גַּם אֲשֶׁר־נִמְצָא הַגָּבִיעַ בְּיָדוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yehudah mah-nomar la'adoni mah-nedabber umah-nittztaddak ha'Elohim matza et-avon avadekha hinnenu avadim la'adoni gam-anachnu gam asher-nimmtze hagavia beyado.
verses.study_note: This is the most theologically significant verse in chapter 44. Judah speaks three questions of total rhetorical surrender: 'What shall we say... what shall we speak... how shall we clear ourselves?' Three unanswerable questions. All human defense collapses. Then comes the confession: 'God hath found out the iniquity of thy servants' (ha'Elohim matza et-avon avadekha). Judah does not say 'we did not steal the cup' — he says God has found our iniquity. He is speaking of more than the cup. He is confessing the guilt of selling Joseph, the years of Jacob's grief, the weight of a sin they had never fully confessed or atoned for. The word 'avon' (iniquity) carries not just the sin itself but the guilt and its punishment — identical to its use in 4:13 when Cain cries 'My punishment is greater than I can bear.' Providence has cornered them into truth.
verses.doctrinal_note: The theology of exposure: Judah's confession 'God has found out the iniquity of thy servants' is one of the most theologically precise statements of divine omniscience and providential justice in Genesis. God does not find out sin in the sense of discovering what He did not know — He causes the hidden to surface in its season (Luke 8:17; Numbers 32:23). Judah's words anticipate the New Testament truth that all sin is ultimately against God (Psalm 51:4) and that true repentance involves acknowledging God's righteous exposure of what we have done, not merely apologizing for consequences. This moment of confession is the gateway to the reconciliation that will come in chapter 45.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, God forbid that I should do so: but the man in whose hand the cup is found, he shall be my servant; and as for you, get you up in peace unto your father.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר חָלִילָה לִּי מֵעֲשׂוֹת זֹאת הָאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר נִמְצָא הַגָּבִיעַ בְּיָדוֹ הוּא יִהְיֶה־לִּי עָבֶד וְאַתֶּם עֲלוּ לְשָׁלוֹם אֶל־אֲבִיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer chalilah li me'asot zo't ha'ish asher nimmtze hagavia beyado hu yihyeh-li eved ve'attem alu leshalom el-avikhem.
verses.study_note: Joseph's response is the pivot of the test. He uses the same word Chalilah (God forbid!) that the brothers used in verse 7 — now he refuses to take them all as slaves. He limits the punishment to Benjamin alone: 'he shall be my servant' (hu yihyeh-li eved). And then the devastating invitation: 'as for you, get you up in peace unto your father' (attem alu leshalom el-avikhem). Joseph gives them the perfect opportunity to do exactly what they did to him: abandon the favored son of Rachel and walk away free. The brothers can go home. The test is complete — except that Judah will not take it. The whole architecture of the test has been constructed for this moment.

verses.kjv_text: Then Judah came near unto him, and said, Oh my lord, let thy servant, I pray thee, speak a word in my lord’s ears, and let not thine anger burn against thy servant: for thou art even as Pharaoh.
verses.original_text: וַיִּגַּשׁ אֵלָיו יְהוּדָה וַיֹּאמֶר בִּי אֲדֹנִי יְדַבֶּר־נָא עַבְדְּךָ דָבָר בְּאָזְנֵי אֲדֹנִי וְאַל־יִחַר אַפְּךָ בְּעַבְדֶּךָ כִּי כָמוֹךָ כְּפַרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiggash elav Yehudah vayomer bi adoni yedabber-na avdekha davar be'oznei adoni ve'al-yichar appekha be'avdekha ki khamokha kePhar'oh.
verses.study_note: Judah 'came near' (vayyiggash elav) — the verb 'nagash' signals a decisive, deliberate approach, often used of drawing near to God or a superior in supplication (Abraham 'drew near' to intercede in 18:23). This is the hinge of the entire chapter. Judah steps forward to speak while others stay silent. 'Let thy servant speak a word in my lord's ears' — he requests a private audience, a privilege only intimates receive. 'For thou art even as Pharaoh' (ki khamokha kePhar'oh) — Judah acknowledges supreme authority, placing Joseph at the highest level of human power. The intercession that follows (vv.18-34) is the longest speech by a single human character in the book of Genesis and one of the most rhetorically powerful in the entire Old Testament.
verses.doctrinal_note: Judah as type of Christ the Intercessor: Judah's approach to Joseph foreshadows Christ's approach to the Father on behalf of sinners. He steps into the gap (Ezekiel 22:30) when all others are silent. He acknowledges the authority of the one he addresses ('thou art even as Pharaoh'). He pleads not his own merit but the vulnerability of others (Jacob's grief, Benjamin's youth). He ends by offering himself. The entire shape of the intercession — approach, plea, substitution — mirrors the intercessory work of Christ who 'ever liveth to make intercession' (Hebrews 7:25).

verses.kjv_text: My lord asked his servants, saying, Have ye a father, or a brother?
verses.original_text: אֲדֹנִי שָׁאַל אֶת־עֲבָדָיו לֵאמֹר הֲיֵשׁ־לָכֶם אָב אוֹ־אָח׃
verses.transliteration: Adoni sha'al et-avadav lemor hayesh-lakhem av o-ach.
verses.study_note: Judah begins his speech by reminding Joseph of how this situation began — at Joseph's own initiative: 'My lord asked his servants' (adoni sha'al et-avadav). This is a subtle but important rhetorical move. Judah does not begin with accusation or argument but with narrative — he reconstructs the history to show how Jacob's family arrived at this crisis through a chain of events initiated by Joseph's own interrogation. The question 'Have ye a father, or a brother?' (hayesh-lakhem av o-ach) is quoted exactly from 43:7. By returning to this starting point, Judah is about to show Joseph the full human consequences of that question.

verses.kjv_text: And we said unto my lord, We have a father, an old man, and a child of his old age, a little one; and his brother is dead, and he alone is left of his mother, and his father loveth him.
verses.original_text: וַנֹּאמֶר אֶל־אֲדֹנִי יֶשׁ־לָנוּ אָב זָקֵן וְיֶלֶד זְקֻנִים קָטָן וְאָחִיו מֵת וַיִּוָּתֵר הוּא לְבַדּוֹ לְאִמּוֹ וְאָבִיו אֲהֵבוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vanomer el-adoni yesh-lanu av zaken veyeled zekukim katan ve'achiv met vayyivvater hu levado le'immo ve'aviv ahevo.
verses.study_note: Judah's account of Benjamin is drenched in pathos — each phrase adds a layer of vulnerability. 'An old man' (av zaken) — Jacob is ancient and fragile. 'A child of his old age' (yeled zekunim katan) — Benjamin born when Jacob was elderly. 'His brother is dead' (ve'achiv met) — the other son of Rachel is gone (they believe). 'He alone is left of his mother' (hu levado le'immo) — the last link to Rachel, the beloved wife. 'And his father loveth him' (ve'aviv ahevo) — the final, most devastating detail. Joseph is hearing a description of himself: 'an old man... a child of old age... his brother is dead... alone left of his mother... his father loves him.' He is hearing Jacob's grief for himself described as the reason Benjamin cannot be lost.

verses.kjv_text: And thou saidst unto thy servants, Bring him down unto me, that I may set mine eyes upon him.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר אֶל־עֲבָדֶיךָ הוֹרִידֻהוּ אֵלַי וְאָשִׂימָה עֵינִי עָלָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomer el-avadekha horidhuhu elai ve'asimah eini alav.
verses.study_note: Judah quotes Joseph's own demand: 'Bring him down unto me, that I may set mine eyes upon him' (ve'asimah eini alav). The phrase 'set mine eyes upon' (asimah eini al) can mean either favorable attention (as in Ruth 2:9, where Boaz extends protection) or close scrutiny/control. Joseph's original phrase was deliberately ambiguous — the brothers feared it meant surveillance; it actually meant love. 'Set mine eyes upon him' is precisely what Joseph did when Benjamin appeared (43:29-30): he hurried away to weep. Judah, recounting the command, does not know that 'setting eyes upon' Benjamin was an act of overflowing fraternal love from a brother Benjamin does not know he has.

verses.kjv_text: And we said unto my lord, The lad cannot leave his father: for if he should leave his father, his father would die.
verses.original_text: וַנֹּאמֶר אֶל־אֲדֹנִי לֹא־יוּכַל הַנַּעַר לַעֲזֹב אֶת־אָבִיו וְעָזַב אֶת־אָבִיו וָמֵת׃
verses.transliteration: Vanomer el-adoni lo-yukhal hanna'ar la'azov et-aviv ve'azav et-aviv vamet.
verses.study_note: The argument Judah rehearses is built on a simple but emotionally overwhelming logic: if Benjamin leaves Jacob, Jacob will die. The word 'azov' (to leave/forsake) is particularly sharp — it is often used for abandonment or desertion (as when Ruth 'clave' to Naomi and would not 'forsake' her, Ruth 1:16, same root). Benjamin's absence would be experienced by Jacob not as a journey but as a death — and Jacob has already been 'dying' since the supposed death of Joseph (37:35). Judah is building the case for why Benjamin cannot be kept in Egypt: it is not merely about one boy; it is about the life of a grieving old man who cannot survive another loss.

verses.kjv_text: And thou saidst unto thy servants, Except your youngest brother come down with you, ye shall see my face no more.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר אֶל־עֲבָדֶיךָ אִם־לֹא יֵרֵד אֲחִיכֶם הַקָּטֹן אִתְּכֶם לֹא תֹסִפוּן לִרְאוֹת פָּנָי׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomer el-avadekha im-lo yered achikhem hakkaton ittkhem lo tosifu lirot panai.
verses.study_note: Judah quotes Joseph's ultimatum from 43:3: 'Ye shall see my face no more' (lo tosifu lirot panai). This is the condition that drove everything that followed — the brothers had to return to Jacob and report the ultimatum, which forced Jacob to confront the choice of sending Benjamin or starving. By quoting Joseph's own words back to him, Judah is subtly holding Joseph accountable for the chain of events he set in motion. The phrase 'see my face' (lirot panai) is an idiom for access, audience, and approval throughout Genesis (cf. 33:10; 46:30). Ironically, the face they 'cannot see' without Benjamin is the face of their own brother.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass when we came up unto thy servant my father, we told him the words of my lord.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כִּי עָלִינוּ אֶל־עַבְדְּךָ אָבִי וַנַּגֶּד־לוֹ אֵת דִּבְרֵי אֲדֹנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi ki alinu el-avdekha avi vannaged-lo et divrei adoni.
verses.study_note: Judah calls Jacob 'thy servant my father' (avdekha avi) — a remarkable double designation. To Judah, Jacob is his 'father'; to the powerful Egyptian lord he addresses, he presents his father as a 'servant,' placing even the patriarch under Joseph's authority. This is not demeaning but diplomatic — Judah situates the entire family within Joseph's sphere of sovereign care. 'We told him the words of my lord' — full transparency with Jacob is here affirmed. The brothers did not hide the ultimatum; they relayed it faithfully, and now Judah relays Jacob's response faithfully too.

verses.kjv_text: And our father said, Go again, and buy us a little food.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אָבִינוּ שֻׁבוּ שִׁבְרוּ־לָנוּ מְעַט־אֹכֶל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer avinu shuvu shivru-lanu me'at-okhel.
verses.study_note: Jacob's command 'Go again' (shuvu) is laced with dramatic irony. The word 'shuvu' (return, go back) is the same root as repentance (teshuvah) — Jacob unknowingly sends his sons back toward the crisis that will produce their genuine 'return.' 'A little food' (me'at okhel) — the diminutive captures Jacob's helplessness. He needs provisions but dreads sending Benjamin. The famine compels what love would prevent. Providence uses the pinch of hunger to bring about the meeting of reconciliation.

verses.kjv_text: And we said, We cannot go down: if our youngest brother be with us, then will we go down: for we may not see the man’s face, except our youngest brother be with us.
verses.original_text: וַנֹּאמֶר לֹא נוּכַל לָרֶדֶת אִם־יֵשׁ אָחִינוּ הַקָּטֹן אִתָּנוּ וְיָרַדְנוּ כִּי־לֹא נוּכַל לִרְאוֹת פְּנֵי הָאִישׁ וְאָחִינוּ הַקָּטֹן אֵינֶנּוּ אִתָּנוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vanomer lo nukhal laredet im-yesh achinu hakkaton ittanu veyaradnu ki-lo nukhal lirot penei ha'ish ve'achinu hakkaton einenu ittanu.
verses.study_note: The brothers' refusal to go without Benjamin is replayed to Joseph's face. The condition 'if our youngest brother be with us' is their ironclad boundary — a boundary they held firm even under Jacob's reluctance. This tells the watching Joseph something important: these brothers did not plan to exploit Benjamin or abandon him. They made his safety the precondition of their own return. Their insistence on Benjamin's inclusion is a measure of changed hearts — men who sell brothers do not insist on their accompaniment.

verses.kjv_text: And thy servant my father said unto us, Ye know that my wife bare me two sons:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר עַבְדְּךָ אָבִי אֵלֵינוּ אַתֶּם יְדַעְתֶּם כִּי שְׁנַיִם יָלְדָה־לִּי אִשְׁתִּי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer avdekha avi eleinu attem yeda'tem ki shenayim yaledah-li ishti.
verses.study_note: Judah now quotes Jacob's own words directly — 'my wife' (ishti) — Rachel. Jacob does not say 'my wives' but singles out Rachel as 'my wife' — the one who held the supreme place in his heart. Jacob acknowledges only two sons from Rachel: Joseph and Benjamin. By invoking Rachel, Judah pulls on the most emotionally significant thread in Jacob's life — the wife he loved first and longest, who died giving birth to Benjamin. To take Benjamin is to take the last living representative of that love.

verses.kjv_text: And the one went out from me, and I said, Surely he is torn in pieces; and I saw him not since:
verses.original_text: וַיֵּצֵא הָאֶחָד מֵאִתִּי וָאֹמַר אַךְ טָרֹף טֹרַף וְלֹא רְאִיתִיו עַד־הֵנָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyetze ha'echad me'itti va'omar akh tarof toraf velo re'itiv ad-hennah.
verses.study_note: Jacob's words about Joseph, relayed by Judah, now reach Joseph's ears as direct speech: 'the one went out from me, and I said, Surely he is torn in pieces' (tarof toraf — emphatic infinitive absolute). This is the very conclusion the brothers induced — they brought the coat dipped in blood and 'asked' Jacob to identify it (37:32-33). Jacob's words about Joseph's death are unknowingly addressed to Joseph himself. The phrase 'I saw him not since' (velo re'itiv ad-hennah) — Jacob has grieved every day. Joseph is now hearing, for the first time, the full weight of what his disappearance meant to his father. This verse is the emotional center of Judah's speech.

verses.kjv_text: And if ye take this also from me, and mischief befall him, ye shall bring down my gray hairs with sorrow to the grave.
verses.original_text: וּלְקַחְתֶּם גַּם־אֶת־זֶה מֵעִם פָּנַי וְקָרָהוּ אָסוֹן וְהוֹרַדְתֶּם אֶת־שֵׂיבָתִי בְּרָעָה שְׁאֹלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ulekachtem gam-et-zeh me'im panai vekkarahhu ason vehoradtem et-seivati bera'ah she'olah.
verses.study_note: Jacob's feared phrase 'bring down my gray hairs with sorrow to the grave' (vehoradtem et-seivati bera'ah she'olah) is almost identical to what he said in 42:38. The repetition in Judah's mouth gives it doubled weight — Joseph hears it twice in rapid succession (once in the narrative of ch.42, now from Judah). 'Gray hairs' (seivah) represents the dignity and fragility of old age; to bring them down 'in sorrow' (bera'ah) to 'Sheol' (she'olah — the realm of the dead) is to kill Jacob with grief. Judah is saying: what you do to Benjamin, you do to Jacob. And since Joseph knows who Jacob is, and loves him, this argument carries ultimate force.

verses.kjv_text: Now therefore when I come to thy servant my father, and the lad be not with us; seeing that his life is bound up in the lad’s life;
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה כְּבֹאִי אֶל־עַבְדְּךָ אָבִי וְהַנַּעַר אֵינֶנּוּ אִתָּנוּ וְנַפְשׁוֹ קְשׁוּרָה בְנַפְשׁוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah kevo'i el-avdekha avi vehanna'ar einenu ittanu venáfsho keshurah venáfsho.
verses.study_note: The most emotionally concentrated phrase in Judah's speech: 'his life is bound up in the lad's life' (venafsho keshurah benáfsho). 'Nafsho' — his soul, his life-breath — 'keshurah' (bound, tied, knotted together) — 'benáfsho' (in his soul/life). Jacob's very life-breath is knit to Benjamin's. The word 'kashar' means to bind or tie — a near-permanent attachment. Jonathan's soul was 'knit' to David's soul with the same verb (1 Sam 18:1). To separate what is bound together is to tear. This is not poetic hyperbole; it is Judah's explanation of why returning without Benjamin is equivalent to bringing Jacob's death.

verses.kjv_text: It shall come to pass, when he seeth that the lad is not with us, that he will die: and thy servants shall bring down the gray hairs of thy servant our father with sorrow to the grave.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה כִּרְאוֹתוֹ כִּי־אֵין הַנַּעַר וָמֵת וְהוֹרִידוּ עֲבָדֶיךָ אֶת־שֵׂיבַת עַבְדְּךָ אָבִינוּ בְּיָגוֹן שְׁאֹלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah kire'oto ki-ein hanna'ar vamet vehoridu avadekha et-seivat avdekha avinu beyagon she'olah.
verses.study_note: Judah draws the narrative to its logical, terrible end: if Jacob sees the lad is gone, 'he will die' (vamet). The word 'beyagon' (with sorrow/grief) replaces 'bera'ah' (with evil/trouble) from verse 29 and Jacob's earlier statement (42:38). 'Yagon' is a deeper, more specific word for mourning grief — it is the grief that consumes, the grief that is itself a kind of death. Judah has now made Jacob's death not a metaphor but the certain, stated consequence of the decision Joseph must make. The argument is complete. The only thing left is the offer.

verses.kjv_text: For thy servant became surety for the lad unto my father, saying, If I bring him not unto thee, then I shall bear the blame to my father for ever.
verses.original_text: כִּי עַבְדְּךָ עָרַב אֶת־הַנַּעַר מֵעִם אָבִי לֵאמֹר אִם־לֹא אֲבִיאֶנּוּ אֵלֶיךָ וְחָטָאתִי לְאָבִי כָּל־הַיָּמִים׃
verses.transliteration: Ki avdekha arav et-hanna'ar me'im avi lemor im-lo avi'enu eleikha vechatati le'avi kol-hayyamim.
verses.study_note: The climactic theological statement of the chapter: 'Thy servant became surety for the lad' (avdekha arav et-hanna'ar). The word 'arav' (to be surety, to pledge, to stand guarantee) is the technical term for a legal guarantor — one who binds himself to answer for another's debt or safety. Judah bound himself personally to Jacob for Benjamin's safe return. This is not casual; it is a binding covenant commitment with personal consequences. 'I shall bear the blame to my father for ever' (vechatati le'avi kol-hayyamim). 'Chatati' — 'I will have sinned' — Judah owns the moral burden. He is not merely making an argument; he is confessing the weight of a promise made and kept, and the impossibility of returning to his father without the one he guaranteed.
verses.doctrinal_note: Judah as type of Christ the Surety: This verse contains the most explicit typological anticipation of substitutionary atonement in Genesis. Judah binds himself as 'arev' (surety/guarantor) for another. In Hebrew law (Proverbs 6:1-5; 11:15), a surety assumes the liability of the one guaranteed. In Hebrews 7:22, Jesus is called 'the surety of a better covenant' (engyos — the Greek equivalent of the Hebrew 'arev'). Judah stands guarantee for Benjamin before Jacob; Christ stands guarantee for sinners before the Father. The parallel is precise: Judah's surety was freely entered, personally costly, and morally binding. Christ's surety was eternal (Psalm 22:10), voluntarily assumed (John 10:18), and legally sufficient (Romans 5:19).

verses.kjv_text: Now therefore, I pray thee, let thy servant abide instead of the lad a bondman to my lord; and let the lad go up with his brethren.
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה יֵשֶׁב־נָא עַבְדְּךָ תַּחַת הַנַּעַר עֶבֶד לַאדֹנִי וְהַנַּעַר יַעַל עִם־אֶחָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah yeshev-na avdekha tachat hanna'ar eved la'adoni vehanna'ar ya'al im-echav.
verses.study_note: The moral and theological summit of the entire Joseph narrative: 'Let thy servant abide instead of the lad' (yeshev-na avdekha tachat hanna'ar). The word 'tachat' (instead of, in the place of) is the key term for substitution throughout the Old Testament — it is used in the Aqedah when the ram is offered 'instead of' his son (22:13, the same word). Judah offers himself as a substitute slave so that Benjamin may go free. The man who proposed selling Joseph into slavery (37:26-27) now voluntarily enslaves himself to redeem the son of Rachel. This is complete moral transformation. 'Let the lad go up with his brethren' — Judah asks nothing for himself; his only concern is Benjamin's freedom and Jacob's life.
verses.doctrinal_note: The great Christological word: TACHAT (instead of, in the place of). This single word is the grammatical foundation of substitutionary atonement throughout the Old Testament. The ram instead of (tachat) Isaac (22:13). Israel's sins placed on the scapegoat (Leviticus 16:21-22). The Suffering Servant wounded for (tachat) our transgressions in Isaiah 53. In New Testament Greek, the equivalent is 'anti' (Matthew 20:28: 'a ransom anti many' — instead of many) and 'huper' (in behalf of). Judah's offer of himself tachat Benjamin is the most direct typological act of substitution in Genesis, anticipating the One who would give Himself 'the righteous for the unrighteous' (1 Peter 3:18).

verses.kjv_text: For how shall I go up to my father, and the lad be not with me? lest peradventure I see the evil that shall come on my father.
verses.original_text: כִּי־אֵיךְ אֶעֱלֶה אֶל־אָבִי וְהַנַּעַר אֵינֶנּוּ אִתִּי פֶּן אֶרְאֶה בָרָע אֲשֶׁר יִמְצָא אֶת־אָבִי׃
verses.transliteration: Ki-eikh e'eleh el-avi vehanna'ar einenu itti pen er'eh vara'asher yimtza et-avi.
verses.study_note: Judah's final words are not a demand but an anguished impossibility: 'How shall I go up to my father, and the lad be not with me?' (eikh e'eleh el-avi vehanna'ar einenu itti). The question is rhetorical — there is no answer, no acceptable alternative. 'Lest peradventure I see the evil that shall come on my father' (pen er'eh vara' asher yimtza et-avi) — Judah cannot bear to witness Jacob's destruction. The one who once looked on as his father was destroyed by the news of Joseph's 'death' (37:35) now says he cannot be the agent of a second such destruction. This final word — 'my father' (avi) — is the last note of Judah's speech, and it is the very word that breaks Joseph. Chapter 45 opens with Joseph weeping aloud and revealing himself. Judah's speech — from the surety promise to the substitution offer to this final broken question — is what God uses to bring the whole drama to its redemptive resolution.
verses.doctrinal_note: Judah's transformation completed: Judah began in chapter 37 as the brother who said 'what profit is it if we slay our brother... let us sell him' (37:26-27) — a cold calculation of self-interest that treated Joseph as a commodity. Here, in chapter 44, he cannot bear to return to his father without the son he was entrusted to protect, even at the cost of his own permanent slavery. The distance between Genesis 37:26 and Genesis 44:33-34 is the distance between hardness and love, between self-preservation and self-sacrifice. It is the moral arc of genuine repentance. Judah's speech ends the long test. It reveals that the brothers have changed — they are no longer the men who sat down to eat after selling Joseph. They are men who tear their clothes and return together; men who hold together through the entire ordeal; men who, when given the chance to abandon Benjamin and go home free, refuse. This is what Joseph needed to see. This is what God had been preparing through twenty-two years of His sovereign Providence.

meta.overview: Genesis 44 is the climactic hinge of the Joseph narrative and one of the most theologically rich chapters in the Pentateuch. Joseph, still unrecognized by his brothers, devises a final, decisive test: his silver cup is planted in Benjamin's sack. When the brothers are overtaken and the cup discovered, they are brought back to Egypt in despair. Their response to this crisis reveals whether they are the same men who callously sold Joseph twenty-two years before — or whether genuine transformation has occurred. The answer comes in Judah's magnificent intercession (vv.18-34), the longest speech by a human character in Genesis, in which he offers himself as a substitute slave for Benjamin. This act of voluntary substitution — Judah, who once proposed selling Joseph (37:26-27), now offering his own freedom to save the son of Rachel — is one of the most profound moral transformations in Scripture and the most explicit typological anticipation of Christ's substitutionary atonement in the entire book of Genesis.
meta.theme: Substitutionary Intercession, Testing, Repentance & Typology of Christ
meta.keyWordMeaning: To be surety, to pledge oneself — Judah binds himself as guarantee for Benjamin (v.32), becoming a substitute and standing in another's place at personal cost. The root that becomes 'eravon' (pledge/surety) in Hebrew law and ultimately points to the surety Christ provides in the New Covenant.
outline: The Cup Planted — Joseph's Test Devised (vv.1-5): The silver cup hidden in Benjamin's sack
The Brothers Overtaken — Accusation and Denial (vv.6-13): The steward's charge and the brothers' shocked response
Brought Back — Prostration Before Joseph (vv.14-17): Judah's first response; Joseph's verdict isolating Benjamin
Judah's Great Intercession (vv.18-34): The longest speech in Genesis — a full account of Jacob's grief and Judah's offer of substitution

verses.kjv_text: Then Joseph could not refrain himself before all them that stood by him; and he cried, Cause every man to go out from me. And there stood no man with him, while Joseph made himself known unto his brethren.
verses.original_text: וְלֹא־יָכֹל יוֹסֵף לְהִתְאַפֵּק לְכֹל הַנִּצָּבִים עָלָיו וַיִּקְרָא הוֹצִיאוּ כָל־אִישׁ מֵעָלָי וְלֹא־עָמַד אִישׁ אִתּוֹ בְּהִתְוַדַּע יוֹסֵף אֶל־אֶחָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Velo-yakhol Yosef lehit'appek lekhol hannitzavim alav vayyikra hotzi'u khol-ish me'alai velo-amad ish itto behitvadda Yosef el-echav.
verses.study_note: The emotional dam breaks. Joseph 'could not refrain himself' (lo-yakhol lehit'appek) — the Hebrew 'hit'appek' means to hold oneself together, to restrain forcefully. Judah's speech in chapter 44, offering himself as a substitute for Benjamin, shattered Joseph's composure. He orders all Egyptians out — the revelation is for family alone. 'Made himself known' (hitvadda) is a reflexive form of 'yada' (to know) — he made himself be known, an active self-disclosure. This is intensely personal: the ruler of Egypt strips away his public identity to reveal his true self to the very brothers who betrayed him.
verses.doctrinal_note: Joseph's self-revelation to his terrified brothers is a profound type of Christ's self-disclosure to Israel. Just as Joseph was unrecognized by his brothers in his exalted state, so Christ was not recognized by His own people. Just as Joseph revealed himself privately, Christ reveals Himself personally to those He calls. The moment of revelation is an act of grace, not judgment — the brothers deserved punishment but received embrace.

verses.kjv_text: And he wept aloud: and the Egyptians and the house of Pharaoh heard.
verses.original_text: וַיִּתֵּן אֶת־קֹלוֹ בִּבְכִי וַיִּשְׁמְעוּ מִצְרַיִם וַיִּשְׁמַע בֵּית פַּרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitten et-kolo bivkhi vayyishme'u Mitzrayim vayyishma beit Par'oh.
verses.study_note: Joseph 'gave his voice in weeping' (vayyitten et-kolo bivkhi) — not quiet tears but unrestrained, loud sobbing so powerful that it was heard beyond the room by the Egyptians and even reached Pharaoh's household. This is the third time Joseph weeps in the narrative (42:24, 43:30), but previously he hid it. Now he weeps openly and without restraint. The most powerful man in Egypt, second only to Pharaoh, weeps without shame. His tears are not of weakness but of overwhelming love breaking through years of separation. The Egyptians hearing signals that this family matter will soon become a state affair (vv.16-20).

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph said unto his brethren, I am Joseph; doth my father yet live? And his brethren could not answer him; for they were troubled at his presence.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל־אֶחָיו אֲנִי יוֹסֵף הַעוֹד אָבִי חָי וְלֹא־יָכְלוּ אֶחָיו לַעֲנוֹת אֹתוֹ כִּי נִבְהֲלוּ מִפָּנָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yosef el-echav ani Yosef ha'od avi chai velo-yakhlu echav la'anot oto ki nivhalu mippanav.
verses.study_note: 'Ani Yosef' — 'I am Joseph' — two of the most dramatic words in Genesis. The brothers are 'troubled' (nivhalu — terrified, dismayed, thrown into panic) at his presence (mippanav — literally 'from his face'). The word 'nivhalu' conveys the shock of sudden, overwhelming revelation. They had sold this man into slavery 22 years ago; now he stands before them as the ruler of Egypt. His question 'doth my father yet live?' is not ignorance (they had told him in 43:27-28) but the deeply personal cry of a son — he wants to hear it again, directly, in this moment of unveiled identity. The brothers' silence is the silence of guilt, fear, and astonishment.
verses.doctrinal_note: The revelation of Joseph to his brothers prefigures the future revelation of Christ to Israel. Zechariah 12:10 prophesies: 'they shall look upon me whom they have pierced, and they shall mourn.' Just as the brothers were dismayed (nivhalu) at Joseph's face, Israel will one day recognize the One they rejected — and be struck with grief and wonder. The question 'doth my father yet live?' parallels Christ's persistent concern for the Father's purposes and the Father's people.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph said unto his brethren, Come near to me, I pray you. And they came near. And he said, I am Joseph your brother, whom ye sold into Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל־אֶחָיו גְּשׁוּ־נָא אֵלַי וַיִּגָּשׁוּ וַיֹּאמֶר אֲנִי יוֹסֵף אֲחִיכֶם אֲשֶׁר־מְכַרְתֶּם אֹתִי מִצְרָיְמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yosef el-echav geshu-na elai vayyiggashu vayomer ani Yosef achikhem asher-mekhartem oti Mitzraymah.
verses.study_note: Joseph's first act after revelation is to call them close: 'Come near to me' (geshu-na elai). He does not stand at a distance in judgment but draws them in. The invitation to 'come near' (nagash) is a reconciliation verb — the same word used for approaching God in worship. Then he states it plainly: 'I am Joseph your brother, whom ye sold into Egypt.' He names the crime — 'whom ye sold' (asher-mekhartem) — not to condemn but to establish the truth that must undergird genuine reconciliation. True forgiveness does not deny what happened; it acknowledges the offense and then transcends it. Perhaps he also shows them a physical mark or speaks in Hebrew to confirm his identity.

verses.kjv_text: Now therefore be not grieved, nor angry with yourselves, that ye sold me hither: for God did send me before you to preserve life.
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה אַל־תֵּעָצְבוּ וְאַל־יִחַר בְּעֵינֵיכֶם כִּי־מְכַרְתֶּם אֹתִי הֵנָּה כִּי לְמִחְיָה שְׁלָחַנִי אֱלֹהִים לִפְנֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah al-te'atzvu ve'al-yichar be'eineikhem ki-mekhartem oti hennah ki lemichyah shelachani Elohim lifneikhem.
verses.study_note: One of the greatest theological statements in the Old Testament. Joseph addresses their grief and self-recrimination with a radical reinterpretation of history: 'God did send me before you to preserve life' (lemichyah shelachani Elohim). He does not minimize their sin ('ye sold me') but reframes it within divine providence. The key pivot is the word 'ki' (for/because) — their selling was real, but God's sending was the deeper reality. 'To preserve life' (lemichyah — literally 'for a keeping alive') elevates the purpose to salvation. Joseph moves from human causation to divine intention in a single sentence.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse establishes the biblical doctrine of divine providence over human evil: God sovereignly works through sinful human actions to accomplish His redemptive purposes without becoming the author of sin. The brothers' act was genuinely evil (Gen 50:20: 'ye thought evil'), but God's purpose was genuinely good. This same theology undergirds Acts 2:23: Christ was 'delivered by the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God' AND 'by wicked hands' crucified. Both are fully true simultaneously.

verses.kjv_text: For these two years hath the famine been in the land: and yet there are five years, in the which there shall neither be earing nor harvest.
verses.original_text: כִּי־זֶה שְׁנָתַיִם הָרָעָב בְּקֶרֶב הָאָרֶץ וְעוֹד חָמֵשׁ שָׁנִים אֲשֶׁר אֵין־חָרִישׁ וְקָצִיר׃
verses.transliteration: Ki-zeh shenatayim hara'av bekerev ha'aretz ve'od chamesh shanim asher ein-charish vekatzir.
verses.study_note: Joseph provides the practical context for his theological statement. Two of the seven famine years have passed; five remain. 'Neither earing nor harvest' (ein-charish vekatzir — no plowing and no reaping). The severity demands immediate action. Joseph's knowledge of the famine's duration confirms his prophetic gift (Gen 41:30). He places the urgency of the situation before his brothers to explain why God's timing was so precise — Joseph had to arrive in Egypt years before the famine to be positioned for the rescue.

verses.kjv_text: And God sent me before you to preserve you a posterity in the earth, and to save your lives by a great deliverance.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁלָחֵנִי אֱלֹהִים לִפְנֵיכֶם לָשׂוּם לָכֶם שְׁאֵרִית בָּאָרֶץ וּלְהַחֲיוֹת לָכֶם לִפְלֵיטָה גְדֹלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishlacheni Elohim lifneikhem lasum lakhem she'erit ba'aretz ulehachayot lakhem lifletah gedolah.
verses.study_note: The second declaration of divine sending, now deepened with two purposes: (1) 'To preserve you a posterity' (lasum lakhem she'erit — to establish for you a remnant). 'She'erit' (remnant) is a prophetic term — God preserves a faithful remnant through judgment (cf. Isaiah 10:20-22, Romans 9:27). (2) 'To save your lives by a great deliverance' (lifletah gedolah — a great escape/survival). 'Pletah' means survivors, those who escape. Joseph sees his role as preserving the covenant seed — the very line through which the Messiah will come. Without this 'great deliverance,' Jacob's family — and the covenant promises — would perish.
verses.doctrinal_note: The remnant theology: God preserves a 'she'erit' (remnant) through every judgment. This principle runs through all of Scripture — Noah's family, Lot's rescue, Joseph's provision, the remnant after exile, and the remnant of Israel who believe in Christ (Romans 11:5). God never leaves Himself without a witness or His covenant without heirs.

verses.kjv_text: So now it was not you that sent me hither, but God: and he hath made me a father to Pharaoh, and lord of all his house, and a ruler throughout all the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה לֹא־אַתֶּם שְׁלַחְתֶּם אֹתִי הֵנָּה כִּי הָאֱלֹהִים וַיְשִׂימֵנִי לְאָב לְפַרְעֹה וּלְאָדוֹן לְכָל־בֵּיתוֹ וּמֹשֵׁל בְּכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah lo-attem shelachtem oti hennah ki ha'Elohim vayesimeni le'av leFar'oh ule'adon lekhol-beto umoshel bekhol-eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: The third and climactic declaration — now stated as a direct negation: 'It was NOT you that sent me hither, but God' (lo-attem... ki ha'Elohim). Joseph does not deny their agency but subordinates it to the ultimate Agent. Three titles describe his position: (1) 'A father to Pharaoh' (le'av leFar'oh) — an advisor, a counselor, a title of highest honor in the Egyptian court. (2) 'Lord of all his house' (le'adon lekhol-beto) — administrator of the royal household. (3) 'A ruler throughout all the land' (umoshel bekhol-eretz) — governing authority over the entire nation. The dreamer whose brothers hated him for his visions of authority now holds precisely that authority — and uses it not for revenge but for salvation.
verses.doctrinal_note: Joseph's triple declaration (vv.5,7,8) of divine sovereignty over human evil is the Old Testament's clearest articulation of what theologians call 'concurrence' — God's sovereign purpose and human moral responsibility operating simultaneously. The brothers' sin was real and culpable. God's purpose was real and redemptive. Joseph holds both truths without contradiction. This is the theological foundation for understanding the cross: 'Him, being delivered by the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God, ye have taken, and by wicked hands have crucified and slain' (Acts 2:23).

verses.kjv_text: Haste ye, and go up to my father, and say unto him, Thus saith thy son Joseph, God hath made me lord of all Egypt: come down unto me, tarry not:
verses.original_text: מַהֲרוּ וַעֲלוּ אֶל־אָבִי וַאֲמַרְתֶּם אֵלָיו כֹּה אָמַר בִּנְךָ יוֹסֵף שָׂמַנִי אֱלֹהִים לְאָדוֹן לְכָל־מִצְרָיִם רְדָה אֵלַי אַל־תַּעֲמֹד׃
verses.transliteration: Maharu va'alu el-avi va'amartem elav koh amar binkha Yosef samani Elohim le'adon lekhol-Mitzrayim redah elai al-ta'amod.
verses.study_note: Joseph shifts from theology to urgent practicality: 'Haste ye' (maharu — hurry!). He speaks with the authority of Egypt's ruler but the heart of a son: 'Thus saith thy son Joseph' (koh amar binkha Yosef). The phrase 'koh amar' ('thus says') is the prophetic messenger formula, later used by the prophets: 'Thus saith the LORD.' Joseph is simultaneously ruler and son, authority and supplicant, Egyptian lord and Hebrew brother. 'Come down unto me, tarry not' (redah elai al-ta'amod) — the invitation is urgent, personal, and non-negotiable.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt dwell in the land of Goshen, and thou shalt be near unto me, thou, and thy children, and thy children’s children, and thy flocks, and thy herds, and all that thou hast:
verses.original_text: וְיָשַׁבְתָּ בְאֶרֶץ־גֹּשֶׁן וְהָיִיתָ קָרוֹב אֵלַי אַתָּה וּבָנֶיךָ וּבְנֵי בָנֶיךָ וְצֹאנְךָ וּבְקָרְךָ וְכָל־אֲשֶׁר־לָךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Veyashavta ve'eretz-Goshen vehayita karov elai attah uvanekha uvnei vanekha vetzonekha uvkareka vekhol-asher-lakh.
verses.study_note: Goshen: the northeastern Nile delta region, fertile and suitable for shepherds. Joseph has already chosen the location — he has been planning. 'Near unto me' (karov elai) — proximity is his desire. The list encompasses three generations (children, children's children) plus livestock and possessions — a complete family relocation. Goshen will provide both sustenance and separation: the Hebrews can maintain their distinct identity and pastoral culture apart from Egyptian urban centers, a providential arrangement for preserving covenant identity during the 400-year sojourn.

verses.kjv_text: And there will I nourish thee; for yet there are five years of famine; lest thou, and thy household, and all that thou hast, come to poverty.
verses.original_text: וְכִלְכַּלְתִּי אֹתְךָ שָׁם כִּי־עוֹד חָמֵשׁ שָׁנִים רָעָב פֶּן־תִּוָּרֵשׁ אַתָּה וּבֵיתְךָ וְכָל־אֲשֶׁר־לָךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhilkalti otekha sham ki-od chamesh shanim ra'av pen-tivvaresh attah uveitkha vekhol-asher-lakh.
verses.study_note: Joseph promises personal provision: 'I will nourish thee' (vekhilkalti — from 'kul,' to sustain, to provide food). The same verb used for God sustaining His people (1 Kings 17:4,9). Joseph becomes a type of the divine Provider. 'Lest thou come to poverty' (pen-tivvaresh — lest you be dispossessed/impoverished). The verb 'yarash' in its negative sense means to be driven into destitution. Joseph's concern encompasses not just survival but the preservation of the family's dignity and resources.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, your eyes see, and the eyes of my brother Benjamin, that it is my mouth that speaketh unto you.
verses.original_text: וְהִנֵּה עֵינֵיכֶם רֹאוֹת וְעֵינֵי אָחִי בִנְיָמִין כִּי־פִי הַמְדַבֵּר אֲלֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehineh eineikhem ro'ot ve'einei achi Vinyamin ki-fi hammedabber aleikhem.
verses.study_note: Joseph appeals to direct sensory evidence: 'your eyes see... it is my mouth that speaketh.' He singles out Benjamin — 'my brother Benjamin' (achi Vinyamin) — the only full brother (both sons of Rachel). The phrase 'my mouth that speaketh' (fi hammedabber) likely means he is now speaking Hebrew directly, without the interpreter used in previous meetings (cf. 42:23). This removes any doubt — the Egyptian ruler is speaking their own language, as their own brother. The appeal to eyes and mouth — sight and speech — demands they accept the reality before them.

verses.kjv_text: And ye shall tell my father of all my glory in Egypt, and of all that ye have seen; and ye shall haste and bring down my father hither.
verses.original_text: וְהִגַּדְתֶּם לְאָבִי אֶת־כָּל־כְּבוֹדִי בְּמִצְרַיִם וְאֵת כָּל־אֲשֶׁר רְאִיתֶם וּמִהַרְתֶּם וְהוֹרַדְתֶּם אֶת־אָבִי הֵנָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehiggadtem le'avi et-kol-kevodi beMitzrayim ve'et kol-asher re'item umihhartem vehoradtem et-avi hennah.
verses.study_note: 'Tell my father of all my glory' (kol-kevodi) — 'kavod' (glory/honor/weight) describes Joseph's exalted position. He wants Jacob to know not for vanity but to assure his aged father that Egypt is safe and prosperous. The repeated 'my father' (avi) — used seven times in this chapter — reveals Joseph's deepest longing. He has been separated from his father since he was seventeen (Gen 37:2); approximately 22 years have passed. The command 'haste' (mihhartem) repeats the urgency of v.9.

verses.kjv_text: And he fell upon his brother Benjamin’s neck, and wept; and Benjamin wept upon his neck.
verses.original_text: וַיִּפֹּל עַל־צַוְּארֵי בִנְיָמִן־אָחִיו וַיֵּבְךְּ וּבִנְיָמִן בָּכָה עַל־צַוָּארָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyippol al-tzavverei Vinyamin-achiv vayyevk uVinyamin bakhah al-tzavvarav.
verses.study_note: Joseph embraces Benjamin first — his only full brother, Rachel's other son. 'Fell upon his neck' (vayyippol al-tzavverei) — the same expression used for Jacob embracing Esau (Gen 33:4) and the father embracing the prodigal son (Luke 15:20). The mutual weeping is profound: Joseph weeps for the lost years; Benjamin, the youngest, weeps at finding the brother he may barely remember. Benjamin was a young child when Joseph was sold. This embrace reconnects the severed bond of Rachel's sons.

verses.kjv_text: Moreover he kissed all his brethren, and wept upon them: and after that his brethren talked with him.
verses.original_text: וַיְנַשֵּׁק לְכָל־אֶחָיו וַיֵּבְךְּ עֲלֵהֶם וְאַחֲרֵי כֵן דִּבְּרוּ אֶחָיו אִתּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayenashek lekhol-echav vayyevk alehem ve'acharei khen dibberu echav itto.
verses.study_note: Joseph kisses ALL his brothers — not just Benjamin but also the ten who sold him. The kiss (vayenashek) is the seal of forgiveness and reconciliation. He weeps 'upon them' (alehem) — his tears fall on each one. Only 'after that' (ve'acharei khen) do the brothers find their voices to speak. The sequence is crucial: forgiveness precedes dialogue. Joseph's acceptance creates the emotional space for conversation. Before his embrace, they were frozen in terror (v.3); after it, they can finally speak. Grace thaws what guilt had frozen.

verses.kjv_text: And the fame thereof was heard in Pharaoh’s house, saying, Joseph’s brethren are come: and it pleased Pharaoh well, and his servants.
verses.original_text: וְהַקֹּל נִשְׁמַע בֵּית פַּרְעֹה לֵאמֹר בָּאוּ אֲחֵי יוֹסֵף וַיִּיטַב בְּעֵינֵי פַרְעֹה וּבְעֵינֵי עֲבָדָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vehakkol nishma beit Par'oh lemor ba'u achei Yosef vayyitav be'einei Far'oh uve'einei avadav.
verses.study_note: The report (hakkol — 'the voice/report') reaches Pharaoh's court. Remarkably, it 'pleased Pharaoh well' (vayyitav be'einei — was good in the eyes of). Pharaoh is genuinely happy for Joseph. This reflects both personal affection and political wisdom — Joseph's family represents the man who saved Egypt from starvation. Pharaoh's response demonstrates that Joseph's integrity has earned deep trust. The servants' approval confirms Joseph is universally respected.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh said unto Joseph, Say unto thy brethren, This do ye; lade your beasts, and go, get you unto the land of Canaan;
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה אֶל־יוֹסֵף אֱמֹר אֶל־אַחֶיךָ זֹאת עֲשׂוּ טַעֲנוּ אֶת־בְּעִירְכֶם וּלְכוּ־בֹאוּ אַרְצָה כְּנָעַן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Par'oh el-Yosef emor el-achekha zot asu ta'anu et-be'irkhem ulkhu-vo'u artzah Kena'an.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh himself takes initiative, giving direct instructions. 'Lade your beasts' (ta'anu et-be'irkhem — load your pack animals). This is a royal command with royal resources backing it. Pharaoh essentially sponsors the family relocation. His directive parallels and amplifies Joseph's own invitation (vv.9-11). Having both Joseph's personal invitation and Pharaoh's royal endorsement gives the brothers — and Jacob — the assurance that this is genuine and authorized at the highest level.

verses.kjv_text: And take your father and your households, and come unto me: and I will give you the good of the land of Egypt, and ye shall eat the fat of the land.
verses.original_text: וּקְחוּ אֶת־אֲבִיכֶם וְאֶת־בָּתֵּיכֶם וּבֹאוּ אֵלָי וְאֶתְּנָה לָכֶם אֶת־טוּב אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם וְאִכְלוּ אֶת־חֵלֶב הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Ukchu et-avikhem ve'et-batteikhem uvo'u elai ve'ettenah lakhem et-tuv eretz Mitzrayim ve'ikhlu et-chelev ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh offers 'the good of the land of Egypt' (tuv eretz Mitzrayim) and 'the fat of the land' (chelev ha'aretz — the best, richest portion). 'Chelev' literally means fat, the choicest part offered to God in sacrifices. Pharaoh opens Egypt's finest resources to Joseph's family. This extravagant generosity from a pagan king is God's instrument of provision for the covenant family. The pattern of pagan rulers blessing God's people appears repeatedly: Pharaoh here, Cyrus in Ezra, Artaxerxes in Nehemiah.

verses.kjv_text: Now thou art commanded, this do ye; take you wagons out of the land of Egypt for your little ones, and for your wives, and bring your father, and come.
