verses.original_text: וְאַתָּה צֻוֵּיתָה זֹאת עֲשׂוּ קְחוּ־לָכֶם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם עֲגָלוֹת לְטַפְּכֶם וְלִנְשֵׁיכֶם וּנְשָׂאתֶם אֶת־אֲבִיכֶם וּבָאתֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah tzuvveitah zot asu kechu-lakhem me'eretz Mitzrayim agalot letappekhem velinisheikhem unesa'tem et-avikhem uva'tem.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh provides wagons (agalot) — an extraordinary provision. Wagons were valuable military and commercial assets in Egypt. For nomadic pastoralists, wagons were unavailable; this is royal transportation for a complete family migration. The mention of 'little ones' (tappekhem) and 'wives' (nisheikhem) makes clear this is total relocation, not a temporary visit. 'Bring your father' — even Pharaoh recognizes Jacob as the patriarch. The wagons will become the key evidence that convinces Jacob (v.27).

verses.kjv_text: Also regard not your stuff; for the good of all the land of Egypt is yours.
verses.original_text: וְעֵינְכֶם אַל־תָּחֹס עַל־כְּלֵיכֶם כִּי־טוּב כָּל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם לָכֶם הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'einekhem al-tachos al-keleikhem ki-tuv kol-eretz Mitzrayim lakhem hu.
verses.study_note: 'Regard not your stuff' (al-tachos al-keleikhem) — literally 'let your eyes not look with pity on your possessions.' 'Chus' means to spare, to have compassion on, to look with regret. Pharaoh tells them not to worry about what they leave behind — all the wealth of Egypt will replace it. This is extraordinary generosity and also practical wisdom: traveling light means traveling fast. The statement 'the good of all the land of Egypt is yours' (tuv kol-eretz Mitzrayim lakhem) is a blanket royal grant. A family of shepherds from famine-stricken Canaan is given access to the world's wealthiest civilization.

verses.kjv_text: And the children of Israel did so: and Joseph gave them wagons, according to the commandment of Pharaoh, and gave them provision for the way.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעֲשׂוּ־כֵן בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וַיִּתֵּן לָהֶם יוֹסֵף עֲגָלוֹת עַל־פִּי פַרְעֹה וַיִּתֵּן לָהֶם צֵדָה לַדָּרֶךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'asu-khen benei Yisra'el vayyitten lahem Yosef agalot al-pi Far'oh vayyitten lahem tzedah laddarekh.
verses.study_note: Note the phrase 'children of Israel' (benei Yisra'el) — the first time this term appears for the brothers as a group (they are usually 'Joseph's brothers'). This anticipates the national designation that will define them through Exodus and beyond. Joseph provides both wagons (royal transport) and provisions (tzedah — food for the journey). He acts as both brother and ruler, personal and official. 'According to the commandment of Pharaoh' (al-pi Far'oh — literally 'upon the mouth of Pharaoh') confirms the royal authority behind the provisions.

verses.kjv_text: To all of them he gave each man changes of raiment; but to Benjamin he gave three hundred pieces of silver, and five changes of raiment.
verses.original_text: לְכֻלָּם נָתַן לָאִישׁ חֲלִפוֹת שְׂמָלֹת וּלְבִנְיָמִן נָתַן שְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת כֶּסֶף וְחָמֵשׁ חֲלִפֹת שְׂמָלֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Lekullam natan la'ish chalifot semalot ulVinyamin natan shelosh me'ot kesef vechamesh chalifot semalot.
verses.study_note: Joseph gives each brother 'changes of raiment' (chalifot semalot — sets of fine clothing). The irony is exquisite: the brothers once stripped Joseph of his coat (Gen 37:23); now he clothes them. Benjamin receives significantly more: 300 pieces of silver and five sets of clothing. The disproportion recalls the original favoritism that sparked the brothers' jealousy (Gen 37:3-4). Joseph is testing whether the old jealousy has been healed — a test they pass, as no protest is recorded. The silver also recalls the 20 pieces for which Joseph was sold (Gen 37:28); 300 pieces for Benjamin is an abundant, grace-filled reversal.

verses.kjv_text: And to his father he sent after this manner; ten asses laden with the good things of Egypt, and ten she asses laden with corn and bread and meat for his father by the way.
verses.original_text: וּלְאָבִיו שָׁלַח כְּזֹאת עֲשָׂרָה חֲמֹרִים נֹשְׂאִים מִטּוּב מִצְרָיִם וְעֶשֶׂר אֲתֹנֹת נֹשְׂאֹת בָּר וָלֶחֶם וּמָזוֹן לְאָבִיו לַדָּרֶךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Ule'aviu shalach kezot asarah chamorim nos'im mittuv Mitzrayim ve'eser atonot nos'ot bar valechem umazon le'aviu laddarekh.
verses.study_note: Joseph sends a lavish caravan to his father: ten male donkeys carrying Egypt's finest goods and ten female donkeys carrying grain, bread, and provisions. Twenty donkeys in total — a substantial pack train that would visually demonstrate to Jacob the reality and wealth of Joseph's position. The detailed provisions show Joseph's characteristic planning and care. 'For his father by the way' (le'aviu laddarekh) — tenderly providing for the aged patriarch's comfort during the journey. Every detail reflects a son's love channeled through a ruler's resources.

verses.kjv_text: So he sent his brethren away, and they departed: and he said unto them, See that ye fall not out by the way.
verses.original_text: וַיְשַׁלַּח אֶת־אֶחָיו וַיֵּלֵכוּ וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם אַל־תִּרְגְּזוּ בַּדָּרֶךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayeshallach et-echav vayyeleikhu vayomer alehem al-tirgezu baddarekh.
verses.study_note: 'See that ye fall not out by the way' (al-tirgezu baddarekh) — the verb 'ragaz' means to tremble, to be agitated, to quarrel. Joseph's parting word is a warning against recrimination during the journey. He knows human nature: with the shock wearing off, the brothers might blame each other for the original crime, argue about who was most responsible, or dispute Joseph's unequal gifts. Some interpret it as 'do not be afraid on the way' — do not let anxiety about the past overwhelm you. Either way, Joseph addresses the emotional dangers of the road ahead. The path of reconciliation requires continued vigilance.

verses.kjv_text: And they went up out of Egypt, and came into the land of Canaan unto Jacob their father,
verses.original_text: וַיַּעֲלוּ מִמִּצְרָיִם וַיָּבֹאוּ אֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן אֶל־יַעֲקֹב אֲבִיהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'alu miMitzrayim vayyavo'u eretz Kena'an el-Ya'akov avihem.
verses.study_note: A brief narrative bridge. 'They went up' (vayya'alu) — the geographical ascent from Egypt to Canaan mirrors the spiritual movement from despair to hope. They come to 'Jacob' — not 'Israel.' The use of his old name may reflect his current diminished state: bereaved, aged, grieving. He will become 'Israel' again when the news revives him (v.28). The return journey must have been filled with anxiety: how do you tell your father that the son he mourned for 22 years is alive — and that you were the ones who caused the grief?

verses.kjv_text: And told him, saying, Joseph is yet alive, and he is governor over all the land of Egypt. And Jacob’s heart fainted, for he believed them not.
verses.original_text: וַיַּגִּדוּ לוֹ לֵאמֹר עוֹד יוֹסֵף חַי וְכִי־הוּא מֹשֵׁל בְּכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם וַיָּפָג לִבּוֹ כִּי לֹא־הֶאֱמִין לָהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaggidu lo lemor od Yosef chai vekhi-hu moshel bekhol-eretz Mitzrayim vayyafag libbo ki lo-he'emin lahem.
verses.study_note: 'Joseph is yet alive' (od Yosef chai) — four words that reverse 22 years of mourning. But Jacob's reaction is not joy — it is shock: 'his heart fainted' (vayyafag libbo). 'Pug' means to grow numb, to become cold, to be stunned into insensibility. His heart went cold because 'he believed them not' (lo-he'emin lahem). Jacob, the man deceived by a bloody coat (Gen 37:31-33), now cannot believe the truth. There is tragic irony: the brothers who once used a lie to devastate Jacob now struggle to make him believe the truth. Deception destroys trust so thoroughly that even truth becomes unbelievable.

verses.kjv_text: And they told him all the words of Joseph, which he had said unto them: and when he saw the wagons which Joseph had sent to carry him, the spirit of Jacob their father revived:
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבְּרוּ אֵלָיו אֵת כָּל־דִּבְרֵי יוֹסֵף אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר אֲלֵהֶם וַיַּרְא אֶת־הָעֲגָלוֹת אֲשֶׁר־שָׁלַח יוֹסֵף לָשֵׂאת אֹתוֹ וַתְּחִי רוּחַ יַעֲקֹב אֲבִיהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabberu elav et kol-divrei Yosef asher dibber alehem vayyar et-ha'agalot asher-shalach Yosef laset oto vattechi ruach Ya'akov avihem.
verses.study_note: Two things break through Jacob's disbelief: (1) 'all the words of Joseph' — the detailed message with its personal touches that only Joseph would know; (2) 'the wagons' (ha'agalot) — physical, tangible evidence that could only come from Egypt's ruler. The wagons are proof that no poor shepherd could fabricate. Some rabbinical commentators note a wordplay: 'agalot' (wagons) sounds like 'eglot' (heifers), connecting to the last Torah subject Joseph and Jacob studied together (Deut 21, the heifer ceremony) — a private sign between father and son. Whether or not this wordplay is intended, the wagons accomplish what words alone could not: 'the spirit of Jacob... revived' (vattechi ruach — his spirit came alive again). The verb 'chayah' (to live/revive) is resurrection language.

verses.kjv_text: And Israel said, It is enough; Joseph my son is yet alive: I will go and see him before I die.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יִשְׂרָאֵל רַב עוֹד־יוֹסֵף בְּנִי חָי אֵלְכָה וְאֶרְאֶנּוּ בְּטֶרֶם אָמוּת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yisra'el rav od-Yosef beni chai elekhah ve'er'ennu beterem amut.
verses.study_note: The chapter's triumphant conclusion. Note the name change: 'Jacob' (v.27) becomes 'Israel' (v.28). The news has restored not just his spirit but his identity. He is once again 'Israel' — the one who prevailed with God (Gen 32:28). 'It is enough' (rav) — literally 'much/great/abundant.' Not 'I have heard enough' but 'this is abundantly great!' Some translate it as 'Great! Joseph my son is alive!' — an exclamation of overwhelming joy. 'I will go and see him before I die' — Jacob, who had refused all comfort and expected to go down to Sheol mourning (Gen 37:35), now has a reason to live. He will travel to Egypt, embrace Joseph, bless Pharaoh, prophesy over his sons, and live seventeen more years (Gen 47:28). The God who brings life from death has done it again.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jacob's revival foreshadows the ultimate resurrection hope. A father mourning a son as dead discovers he is alive and exalted. This pattern — from death to life, from grief to glory — is the shape of the Gospel itself. 'It is enough' (rav) is the cry of every soul that discovers the One thought dead is alive and reigning: 'I will go and see him.' Paul echoes this: 'To depart and be with Christ, which is far better' (Phil 1:23). The chapter ends not with death but with the anticipation of reunion — the great biblical hope that carries from Genesis to Revelation.

meta.overview: Genesis 45 is the climactic chapter of the Joseph narrative and one of the most emotionally powerful passages in all of Scripture. After testing his brothers through the silver cup incident (ch.44), Joseph can no longer restrain himself and reveals his identity with the stunning declaration: "I am Joseph." The brothers are terrified, but Joseph — weeping loudly — comforts them with one of the Bible's greatest theological statements on divine providence: "God sent me before you to preserve life" (v.5). He repeats this truth three times (vv.5,7,8), transforming their guilt into wonder at God's sovereign plan. Joseph sends his brothers back to Canaan with wagons, provisions, and the invitation for Jacob and the entire family to relocate to Goshen in Egypt. When Jacob hears that Joseph is alive, his spirit revives. The chapter is a masterpiece of reconciliation, forgiveness, and the theology of divine sovereignty working through human sin for redemptive purposes — a supreme Old Testament type of the work of Christ.
meta.theme: Revelation, Reconciliation & the Providence of God
meta.keyWordMeaning: To send — the key verb Joseph uses three times to reinterpret his brothers' crime: "you did not send me here, but God" (v.8). Their act of selling becomes God's act of sending.
outline: Joseph Reveals His Identity (vv.1-4): 'I am Joseph' — the brothers are terrified
The Theology of Providence (vv.5-8): God sent me — three declarations of divine sovereignty
Joseph's Instructions (vv.9-15): Bring my father down to Egypt — weeping and reconciliation
Pharaoh's Generous Invitation (vv.16-20): The best of Egypt is yours
The Brothers Sent with Provisions (vv.21-24): Wagons, supplies, and Joseph's warning
Jacob Revived by the News (vv.25-28): Joseph is alive — 'it is enough'

verses.kjv_text: And Israel took his journey with all that he had, and came to Beersheba, and offered sacrifices unto the God of his father Isaac.
verses.original_text: וַיִּסַּע יִשְׂרָאֵל וְכָל־אֲשֶׁר־לוֹ וַיָּבֹא בְּאֵרָה שָׁבַע וַיִּזְבַּח זְבָחִים לֵאלֹהֵי אָבִיו יִצְחָק׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyissa Yisra'el vekhol-asher-lo vayyavo Be'erah Shava vayyizbach zevachim le'lohei aviv Yitzchak.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with the name 'Israel' — not Jacob — signifying that this journey is undertaken in his covenant identity. Beersheba ('Well of the Oath') is deeply significant: it was where Abraham planted a tamarisk tree and called on the LORD (Gen 21:33), and where God appeared to Isaac with covenant promises (Gen 26:23-25). Jacob stops at the border of the Promised Land to offer sacrifices — 'zevachim' (plural, fellowship/peace offerings), indicating both thanksgiving and a desire for divine guidance. He offers specifically to 'the God of his father Isaac' — recalling that Isaac was the one forbidden to go to Egypt (Gen 26:2). Jacob knows he is about to do what Isaac was told not to do, and he seeks confirmation.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jacob's sacrifice at Beersheba demonstrates a vital spiritual principle: major life transitions should begin with worship and seeking God. He does not presume on God's will simply because circumstances point toward Egypt. The patriarchs consistently built altars at critical junctures — Abraham at Shechem (12:7), Bethel (12:8), Hebron (13:18); Isaac at Beersheba (26:25). Worship precedes obedience.

verses.kjv_text: And God spake unto Israel in the visions of the night, and said, Jacob, Jacob. And he said, Here am I.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים לְיִשְׂרָאֵל בְּמַרְאֹת הַלַּיְלָה וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב יַעֲקֹב וַיֹּאמֶר הִנֵּנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Elohim leYisra'el bemar'ot hallaylah vayomer Ya'akov Ya'akov vayomer hineni.
verses.study_note: God responds to Jacob's sacrifice with a night vision — 'mar'ot hallaylah,' the same phrase used of Abraham's covenant vision (Gen 15:1). The double calling of the name 'Jacob, Jacob' places this alongside the most significant divine addresses in Scripture: 'Abraham, Abraham' (Gen 22:11), 'Moses, Moses' (Exod 3:4), 'Samuel, Samuel' (1 Sam 3:10). The doubled name signals intimacy and urgency. Notably, God uses 'Jacob' (the natural name) rather than 'Israel' (the covenant name) — addressing the man in his human weakness and fear, not merely in his spiritual identity. Jacob's response 'Hineni' (Here am I) echoes Abraham's identical response at the binding of Isaac (Gen 22:1) — complete availability to God.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, I am God, the God of thy father: fear not to go down into Egypt; for I will there make of thee a great nation:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אָנֹכִי הָאֵל אֱלֹהֵי אָבִיךָ אַל־תִּירָא מֵרְדָה מִצְרַיְמָה כִּי־לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל אֲשִׂימְךָ שָׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer anokhi ha'El Elohei avikha al-tira meredah Mitzraymah ki-legoy gadol asimekha sham.
verses.study_note: God identifies Himself with a triple declaration: (1) 'Anokhi' — I Myself (emphatic first person), (2) 'ha'El' — THE God (the mighty one, with the definite article), (3) 'Elohei avikha' — the God of your father. This self-identification connects to every previous patriarchal theophany. 'Fear not to go down into Egypt' — God acknowledges Jacob's fear. Jacob had reason to hesitate: Isaac was explicitly forbidden from going to Egypt (Gen 26:2), Egypt represented danger for the patriarchs (Gen 12:10-20), and leaving the Promised Land could seem like abandoning the covenant. God's command to 'fear not' (al-tira) is the most repeated command in Scripture. The promise 'I will there make of thee a great nation' (legoy gadol) echoes the original Abrahamic promise (Gen 12:2) — the covenant continues even in Egypt.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's 'fear not' addresses a legitimate spiritual concern: can the covenant survive outside the Promised Land? The answer is emphatic — God's presence, not geography, sustains the covenant. This truth becomes foundational for every exile in Scripture: Babylon (Jer 29:7), the diaspora, and the church in the world (John 17:15). God's people can flourish wherever God sends them because 'I will go down with thee' (v.4).

verses.kjv_text: I will go down with thee into Egypt; and I will also surely bring thee up again: and Joseph shall put his hand upon thine eyes.
verses.original_text: אָנֹכִי אֵרֵד עִמְּךָ מִצְרַיְמָה וְאָנֹכִי אַעַלְךָ גַם־עָלֹה וְיוֹסֵף יָשִׁית יָדוֹ עַל־עֵינֶיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Anokhi ered immekha Mitzraymah ve'anokhi a'alekha gam-aloh veYosef yashit yado al-einekha.
verses.study_note: The most intimate verse in the chapter — a fourfold divine promise: (1) 'I will go down WITH THEE' (anokhi ered immekha) — God does not merely send Jacob; He accompanies him. The emphatic 'anokhi' (I Myself) appears twice. (2) 'I will also surely bring thee up again' (a'alekha gam-aloh) — the emphatic infinitive absolute guarantees the Exodus. 'Bring thee up' (alah) is the verb of the Exodus, the opposite of 'yarad' (go down). (3) The promise reaches beyond Jacob's lifetime — 'bring thee up' refers both to Jacob's burial in Canaan (Gen 50:13) and to the nation's exodus. (4) 'Joseph shall put his hand upon thine eyes' — the tender promise that Joseph will close Jacob's eyes at death, the duty of the beloved son. After twenty-two years of believing Joseph dead, Jacob learns he will die with Joseph at his side.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Incarnational Principle: 'I will go down with thee' is the pattern fulfilled ultimately in Christ. God does not merely rescue from a distance — He enters into the suffering of His people. As God descended into Egypt with Jacob, the Son of God descended into human flesh (Phil 2:6-8), into death, and even into the grave to bring His people up. Every 'going down' in God's plan has a corresponding 'bringing up' — from Egypt, from exile, from death itself.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob rose up from Beersheba: and the sons of Israel carried Jacob their father, and their little ones, and their wives, in the wagons which Pharaoh had sent to carry him.
verses.original_text: וַיָּקָם יַעֲקֹב מִבְּאֵר שָׁבַע וַיִּשְׂאוּ בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת־יַעֲקֹב אֲבִיהֶם וְאֶת־טַפָּם וְאֶת־נְשֵׁיהֶם בָּעֲגָלוֹת אֲשֶׁר שָׁלַח פַּרְעֹה לָשֵׂאת אֹתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyakam Ya'akov miBe'er Shava vayyis'u venei-Yisra'el et-Ya'akov avihem ve'et-tappam ve'et-nesheihem ba'agalot asher shalach Par'oh laset oto.
verses.study_note: After the divine reassurance, Jacob rises with confidence. Note the subtle interplay of names: 'Jacob rose up' (the man obeys), while 'the sons of Israel carried' (the covenant family acts). Pharaoh's wagons (agalot) are the means of transport — Egyptian royal provision carrying the covenant family. The detail about 'little ones' (tappam) and 'wives' (nesheihem) confirms this is a complete family migration, not a temporary visit. The wagons sent by Pharaoh (45:19-21) become the vehicle of divine providence — a pagan king unknowingly serves God's covenant purposes.

verses.kjv_text: And they took their cattle, and their goods, which they had gotten in the land of Canaan, and came into Egypt, Jacob, and all his seed with him:
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְחוּ אֶת־מִקְנֵיהֶם וְאֶת־רְכוּשָׁם אֲשֶׁר רָכְשׁוּ בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן וַיָּבֹאוּ מִצְרָיְמָה יַעֲקֹב וְכָל־זַרְעוֹ עִמּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikchu et-mikneihem ve'et-rekhusham asher rakheshu be'eretz Kena'an vayyavo'u Mitzraymah Ya'akov vekhol-zar'o immo.
verses.study_note: The family brings their livestock (mikneh) and possessions (rekhush) — they come as a prosperous pastoral clan, not as destitute refugees. 'All his seed with him' (khol-zar'o) — the word 'zera' (seed) carries covenant weight, linking back to the Abrahamic promise (Gen 12:7, 13:15-16). The entire covenant seed enters Egypt together. This is the fulfillment of Genesis 15:13 where God told Abraham his descendants would be strangers in a land not theirs.

verses.kjv_text: His sons, and his sons’ sons with him, his daughters, and his sons’ daughters, and all his seed brought he with him into Egypt.
verses.original_text: בָּנָיו וּבְנֵי בָנָיו עִמּוֹ בְּנֹתָיו וּבְנוֹת בָּנָיו וְכָל־זַרְעוֹ הֵבִיא עִמּוֹ מִצְרָיְמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Banav uvenei vanav immo benotav uvenot banav vekhol-zar'o hevi immo Mitzraymah.
verses.study_note: A comprehensive summary before the detailed genealogy: sons, grandsons, daughters, granddaughters — every generation. 'He brought' (hevi) — Jacob, despite his age and frailty, remains the patriarch leading his family. The emphasis on totality prepares for the numbered genealogy that follows, demonstrating that the entire covenant family — without exception — enters Egypt together. This completeness fulfills the household principle: God saves families, not isolated individuals.

verses.kjv_text: And these are the names of the children of Israel, which came into Egypt, Jacob and his sons: Reuben, Jacob’s firstborn.
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה שְׁמוֹת בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל הַבָּאִים מִצְרַיְמָה יַעֲקֹב וּבָנָיו בְּכוֹר יַעֲקֹב רְאוּבֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'eleh shemot benei-Yisra'el habba'im Mitzraymah Ya'akov uvanav bekhor Ya'akov Re'uven.
verses.study_note: The genealogical register begins. 'These are the names' (ve'eleh shemot) is the same formula that opens the book of Exodus (Shemot — 'Names'). This genealogy serves as the bridge between Genesis and Exodus, connecting the patriarchal family to the nation. Reuben is listed first as the firstborn (bekhor), though he lost his birthright (Gen 49:3-4). The genealogy follows the birth order of Leah's sons first, then Zilpah's, then Rachel's, then Bilhah's — the order of the mothers, not of preference.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Reuben; Hanoch, and Phallu, and Hezron, and Carmi.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי רְאוּבֵן חֲנוֹךְ וּפַלּוּא וְחֶצְרוֹן וְכַרְמִי׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Re'uven Chanokh uPallu veChetzron veKharmi.
verses.study_note: Reuben's four sons. Hanoch ('dedicated/initiated'), Phallu ('distinguished'), Hezron ('enclosed'), and Carmi ('my vineyard'). These names become the heads of Reubenite clans (Num 26:5-9). Hezron is also a name found in Judah's line (Gen 46:12), pointing to connections across the tribal families. Despite Reuben's disgrace (Gen 35:22), his line continues — God's purposes are not thwarted by human failure.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Simeon; Jemuel, and Jamin, and Ohad, and Jachin, and Zohar, and Shaul the son of a Canaanitish woman.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי שִׁמְעוֹן יְמוּאֵל וְיָמִין וְאֹהַד וְיָכִין וְצֹחַר וְשָׁאוּל בֶּן־הַכְּנַעֲנִית׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Shim'on Yemu'el veYamin ve'Ohad veYakhin veTzochar veSha'ul ben-hakKena'anit.
verses.study_note: Simeon's six sons. The detail 'Shaul the son of a Canaanitish woman' (ben-hakKena'anit) is a significant editorial note — intermarriage with Canaanites was contrary to patriarchal practice (Gen 24:3, 28:1). This lone mention highlights the concern about mixed marriages that will become central in Israel's law. Simeon, already associated with violence at Shechem (Gen 34), now shows further departure from covenant standards. His tribe will later be the smallest (Num 26:14) and will be absorbed into Judah.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Levi; Gershon, Kohath, and Merari.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי לֵוִי גֵּרְשׁוֹן קְהָת וּמְרָרִי׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Levi Gershon Kehat uMerari.
verses.study_note: Levi's three sons become the three great divisions of the Levitical priesthood. Gershon ('exile/expulsion'), Kohath ('assembly'), and Merari ('bitter'). Kohath is the ancestor of Moses and Aaron (Exod 6:18-20) — the future deliverer and high priest. That Kohath enters Egypt as a young man means he personally experienced both the descent and the beginning of servitude. The priestly and Levitical system of Israel traces directly to these three men entering Egypt.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Judah; Er, and Onan, and Shelah, and Pharez, and Zarah: but Er and Onan died in the land of Canaan. And the sons of Pharez were Hezron and Hamul.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי יְהוּדָה עֵר וְאוֹנָן וְשֵׁלָה וָפֶרֶץ וָזֶרַח וַיָּמָת עֵר וְאוֹנָן בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן וַיִּהְיוּ בְנֵי־פֶרֶץ חֶצְרוֹן וְחָמוּל׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Yehudah Er ve'Onan veShelah vaFeretz vaZerach vayyamot Er ve'Onan be'eretz Kena'an vayyihyu venei-Feretz Chetzron veChamul.
verses.study_note: Judah's line is given special attention. Er and Onan died in Canaan under divine judgment (Gen 38:7-10) — they are counted in the genealogy but noted as deceased. Pharez (Perez, 'breach') and Zarah (Zerach, 'rising/dawn') were born from the Tamar incident (Gen 38). Most importantly, Pharez's sons Hezron and Hamul are named — Hezron is the direct ancestor of David and Christ (Ruth 4:18-22, Matt 1:3). The Messianic line runs through Judah's most scandalous chapter, demonstrating that God's redemptive purposes triumph over human sin.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Issachar; Tola, and Phuvah, and Job, and Shimron.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי יִשָּׂשכָר תּוֹלָע וּפֻוָּה וְיוֹב וְשִׁמְרוֹן׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Yissakhar Tola uFuvvah veYov veShimron.
verses.study_note: Issachar's four sons. Tola ('worm/scarlet' — the same word used for the crimson dye and prophetically of the Messiah in Psalm 22:6), Phuvah ('mouth/utterance'), Job ('persecuted' — not the same as the patriarch Job), and Shimron ('watchful/guardian'). Issachar's tribe later became known for men 'that had understanding of the times' (1 Chron 12:32).

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Zebulun; Sered, and Elon, and Jahleel.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי זְבוּלֻן סֶרֶד וְאֵלוֹן וְיַחְלְאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Zevulun Sered ve'Elon veYachle'el.
verses.study_note: Zebulun's three sons. Sered ('fear/trembling'), Elon ('oak tree'), and Jahleel ('wait for God/hope in God'). Zebulun was Jacob's tenth son, Leah's sixth. Zebulun's inheritance would later be in Galilee — the very region where Jesus would begin His ministry, fulfilling Isaiah 9:1: 'In Galilee of the nations. The people that walked in darkness have seen a great light.'

verses.kjv_text: These be the sons of Leah, which she bare unto Jacob in Padanaram, with his daughter Dinah: all the souls of his sons and his daughters were thirty and three.
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי לֵאָה אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה לְיַעֲקֹב בְּפַדַּן אֲרָם וְאֵת דִּינָה בִתּוֹ כָּל־נֶפֶשׁ בָּנָיו וּבְנוֹתָיו שְׁלֹשִׁים וְשָׁלֹשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Eleh benei Le'ah asher yaledah leYa'akov beFaddan Aram ve'et Dinah bitto kol-nefesh banav uvenotav sheloshim veshalosh.
verses.study_note: The summary for Leah's line: thirty-three souls. Leah is named first and her line is the largest — the unloved wife (Gen 29:31) bears the most fruitful family. Dinah is specifically mentioned as 'his daughter' — the only daughter named in the genealogy. 'In Padanaram' locates the births geographically, connecting back to Genesis 29-30. The word 'nefesh' (soul) rather than 'ish' (man) emphasizes each individual as a living person, not merely a number. God's favor on the unloved Leah demonstrates the principle that 'the last shall be first' (Matt 19:30).

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Gad; Ziphion, and Haggi, Shuni, and Ezbon, Eri, and Arodi, and Areli.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי גָד צִפְיוֹן וְחַגִּי שׁוּנִי וְאֶצְבֹּן עֵרִי וַאֲרוֹדִי וְאַרְאֵלִי׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Gad Tzifyon veChaggiy Shuni ve'Etzbon Eri va'Arodi ve'Ar'eli.
verses.study_note: Gad's seven sons — the largest number for any single son. Gad was Zilpah's firstborn. His name means 'fortune/troop' (Gen 30:11). Jacob's blessing prophesied: 'Gad, a troop shall overcome him: but he shall overcome at the last' (Gen 49:19). The Gadites later became renowned warriors (1 Chron 12:8).

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Asher; Jimnah, and Ishuah, and Isui, and Beriah, and Serah their sister: and the sons of Beriah; Heber, and Malchiel.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי אָשֵׁר יִמְנָה וְיִשְׁוָה וְיִשְׁוִי וּבְרִיעָה וְשֶׂרַח אֲחֹתָם וּבְנֵי בְרִיעָה חֶבֶר וּמַלְכִּיאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Asher Yimnah veYishvah veYishvi uVeri'ah veSerach achotam uvenei Veri'ah Chever uMalki'el.
verses.study_note: Asher's sons, with the notable mention of 'Serah their sister' (Serach achotam) — one of only two women named in this genealogy (along with Dinah, v.15). Serah's inclusion is extraordinary. Rabbinic tradition credits her with extraordinary longevity and with being the one who told Jacob that Joseph was alive. Her mention suggests she held a position of importance. Asher's name means 'happy/blessed' (Gen 30:13), and his tribe later occupied the fertile coastal region of northern Canaan.

verses.kjv_text: These are the sons of Zilpah, whom Laban gave to Leah his daughter, and these she bare unto Jacob, even sixteen souls.
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי זִלְפָּה אֲשֶׁר־נָתַן לָבָן לְלֵאָה בִתּוֹ וַתֵּלֶד אֶת־אֵלֶּה לְיַעֲקֹב שֵׁשׁ עֶשְׂרֵה נָפֶשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Eleh benei Zilpah asher-natan Lavan leLe'ah bitto vatteled et-eleh leYa'akov shesh esreh nafesh.
verses.study_note: Summary of Zilpah's line: sixteen souls. Zilpah was Leah's handmaid, given by Laban. Though her sons Gad and Asher were born from a secondary arrangement, they receive full standing as sons of Israel. God makes no distinction between children of wives and children of handmaids in the covenant — all twelve tribes are equally His. This speaks to the grace that elevates the lowborn.

verses.kjv_text: The sons of Rachel Jacob’s wife; Joseph, and Benjamin.
verses.original_text: בְּנֵי רָחֵל אֵשֶׁת יַעֲקֹב יוֹסֵף וּבִנְיָמִן׃
verses.transliteration: Benei Rachel eshet Ya'akov Yosef uVinyamin.
verses.study_note: Rachel alone is called 'Jacob's wife' (eshet Ya'akov) — the beloved wife. Though she bore only two sons (the fewest of any mother), they are placed in a position of prominence. Joseph, already in Egypt as vizier, is the savior of the family. Benjamin, the youngest, is the only son born in the Promised Land (near Bethlehem, Gen 35:16-19). These two sons of the beloved wife become the most favored tribes — Joseph receiving the double portion through Ephraim and Manasseh, Benjamin becoming the tribe of the first king (Saul) and of the apostle Paul.

verses.kjv_text: And unto Joseph in the land of Egypt were born Manasseh and Ephraim, which Asenath the daughter of Potipherah priest of On bare unto him.
verses.original_text: וַיִּוָּלֵד לְיוֹסֵף בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה־לּוֹ אָסְנַת בַּת־פּוֹטִי פֶרַע כֹּהֵן אוֹן אֶת־מְנַשֶּׁה וְאֶת־אֶפְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyivvaled leYosef be'eretz Mitzrayim asher yaledah-lo Asnat bat-Poti Fera kohen On et-Menasheh ve'et-Efrayim.
verses.study_note: Joseph's two sons born in Egypt to Asenath, daughter of Potipherah, priest of On (Heliopolis). Manasseh ('causing to forget' — Gen 41:51) and Ephraim ('double fruitfulness' — Gen 41:52). These two will be elevated to tribal status by Jacob's adoption blessing (Gen 48:5), giving Joseph the double portion of the firstborn. Remarkably, the covenant line passes through sons born to an Egyptian mother, a priest's daughter — demonstrating that the covenant can incorporate Gentile elements by divine election. Ephraim will later become the dominant northern tribe, often standing for the entire northern kingdom.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Benjamin were Belah, and Becher, and Ashbel, Gera, and Naaman, Ehi, and Rosh, Muppim, and Huppim, and Ard.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי בִנְיָמִן בֶּלַע וָבֶכֶר וְאַשְׁבֵּל גֵּרָא וְנַעֲמָן אֵחִי וָרֹאשׁ מֻפִּים וְחֻפִּים וָאָרְדְּ׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Vinyamin Bela vaBekher ve'Ashbel Gera veNa'aman Echi vaRosh Muppim veChuppim va'Ard.
verses.study_note: Benjamin's ten sons — the most of any grandson listed. Given that Benjamin was likely still quite young (he was the youngest brother), some of these 'sons' may include grandsons counted as sons, or the list may extend to those born later but counted as part of the household. Benjamin's tribe later produces King Saul (1 Sam 9:1-2), the apostle Paul (Phil 3:5), and the warriors of Judges 20. The name Gera later appears in the ancestry of Ehud the judge (Judg 3:15) and Shimei (2 Sam 16:5).

verses.kjv_text: These are the sons of Rachel, which were born to Jacob: all the souls were fourteen.
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי רָחֵל אֲשֶׁר יֻלַּד לְיַעֲקֹב כָּל־נֶפֶשׁ אַרְבָּעָה עָשָׂר׃
verses.transliteration: Eleh benei Rachel asher yullad leYa'akov kol-nefesh arba'ah asar.
verses.study_note: Rachel's line totals fourteen — fewer than Leah's thirty-three, but including Joseph who saves them all. God's economy is not about quantity but about purpose. Rachel, who wept 'Give me children or else I die' (Gen 30:1), produced the savior-son and the warrior tribe. The number fourteen is twice seven — a number of fullness and completion.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Dan; Hushim.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי דָן חֻשִׁים׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Dan Chushim.
verses.study_note: Dan has only one son: Hushim ('those who make haste'). Despite having only one son at this point, Dan's tribe later became one of the largest in Israel (Num 26:42-43). Dan is listed first among the sons of Bilhah. The remarkable growth from one son to a great tribe illustrates God's power to multiply the insignificant. However, Dan's tribe also became associated with idolatry (Judges 18:30-31) and is notably absent from the tribal listing in Revelation 7.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Naphtali; Jahzeel, and Guni, and Jezer, and Shillem.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי נַפְתָּלִי יַחְצְאֵל וְגוּנִי וְיֵצֶר וְשִׁלֵּם׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Naftali Yachtze'el veGuni veYetzer veShillem.
verses.study_note: Naphtali's four sons. Naphtali was Bilhah's second son, meaning 'my wrestling' (Gen 30:8). Jacob's later blessing describes Naphtali as 'a hind let loose: he giveth goodly words' (Gen 49:21) — poetic and swift. Naphtali's territory in Galilee would later be the region of Jesus' primary ministry (Matt 4:13-15), fulfilling Isaiah's prophecy about light dawning on the land of Naphtali and Zebulun.

verses.kjv_text: These are the sons of Bilhah, which Laban gave unto Rachel his daughter, and she bare these unto Jacob: all the souls were seven.
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי בִלְהָה אֲשֶׁר־נָתַן לָבָן לְרָחֵל בִּתּוֹ וַתֵּלֶד אֶת־אֵלֶּה לְיַעֲקֹב כָּל־נֶפֶשׁ שִׁבְעָה׃
verses.transliteration: Eleh benei Vilhah asher-natan Lavan leRachel bitto vatteled et-eleh leYa'akov kol-nefesh shiv'ah.
verses.study_note: Bilhah's line totals seven souls — the number of completion and divine perfection. Bilhah was Rachel's handmaid, and through her Dan and Naphtali were born. The total for all four mothers: Leah 33, Zilpah 16, Rachel 14, Bilhah 7 — equalling 70 (with Jacob himself). The symmetry is not accidental: God orders the numbers of His covenant people.

verses.kjv_text: All the souls that came with Jacob into Egypt, which came out of his loins, besides Jacob’s sons’ wives, all the souls were threescore and six;
verses.original_text: כָּל־הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַבָּאָה לְיַעֲקֹב מִצְרַיְמָה יֹצְאֵי יְרֵכוֹ מִלְּבַד נְשֵׁי בְנֵי־יַעֲקֹב כָּל־נֶפֶשׁ שִׁשִּׁים וָשֵׁשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Kol-hannefesh habba'ah leYa'akov Mitzraymah yotze'ei yerekho millevad neshei venei-Ya'akov kol-nefesh shishim vashesh.
verses.study_note: Sixty-six came 'out of his loins' (yotze'ei yerekho — literally 'coming out of his thigh,' the seat of procreative power, cf. Gen 24:2). This excludes Jacob himself, Joseph and his two sons (already in Egypt), and the wives of Jacob's sons. The number sixty-six is the base count of direct blood descendants who physically traveled to Egypt. The wives are excluded because they came from outside the covenant family.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Joseph, which were born him in Egypt, were two souls: all the souls of the house of Jacob, which came into Egypt, were threescore and ten.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי יוֹסֵף אֲשֶׁר־יֻלַּד־לוֹ בְמִצְרַיִם נֶפֶשׁ שְׁנָיִם כָּל־הַנֶּפֶשׁ לְבֵית־יַעֲקֹב הַבָּאָה מִצְרַיְמָה שִׁבְעִים׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Yosef asher-yullad-lo veMitzrayim nefesh shenayim kol-hannefesh leVeit-Ya'akov habba'ah Mitzraymah shiv'im.
verses.study_note: The grand total: seventy souls. Adding Joseph's two sons (already in Egypt) to the sixty-six travelers, plus Jacob and Joseph themselves, yields seventy. This number is profoundly significant in Scripture. Seventy nations descended from Noah (Gen 10); seventy elders accompanied Moses (Exod 24:1); Jesus sent seventy disciples (Luke 10:1). Stephen in Acts 7:14 cites seventy-five, following the LXX which includes additional grandsons of Joseph. The number seventy symbolizes the completeness of God's covenant community — from this tiny family will come 'a great nation' (v.3).
verses.doctrinal_note: The Number Seventy: From seventy souls in Egypt came the nation of Israel — millions at the Exodus (Exod 12:37). This staggering multiplication fulfills the Abrahamic covenant literally: 'I will make of thee a great nation' (Gen 12:2). Deuteronomy 10:22 later marvels: 'Thy fathers went down into Egypt with threescore and ten persons; and now the LORD thy God hath made thee as the stars of heaven for multitude.' God's faithfulness turns seventy into millions.

verses.kjv_text: And he sent Judah before him unto Joseph, to direct his face unto Goshen; and they came into the land of Goshen.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־יְהוּדָה שָׁלַח לְפָנָיו אֶל־יוֹסֵף לְהוֹרֹת לְפָנָיו גֹּשְׁנָה וַיָּבֹאוּ אַרְצָה גֹּשֶׁן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-Yehudah shalach lefanav el-Yosef lehorot lefanav Goshnah vayyavo'u artzah Goshen.
verses.study_note: Jacob sends Judah ahead — a remarkable choice. Judah, not Reuben the firstborn, is chosen as the advance envoy. This confirms Judah's rise to leadership, which has been building since his surety speech for Benjamin (Gen 43:8-10, 44:18-34). 'To direct his face' (lehorot) — from the same root as 'Torah' (instruction/direction). Judah goes to 'prepare the way' — a role that foreshadows his tribe's leadership in Israel and ultimately Christ, the Lion of Judah, who goes before His people. Goshen (in the eastern Nile Delta) was fertile grazing land, strategically separated from the centers of Egyptian civilization.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph made ready his chariot, and went up to meet Israel his father, to Goshen, and presented himself unto him; and he fell on his neck, and wept on his neck a good while.
verses.original_text: וַיֶּאְסֹר יוֹסֵף מֶרְכַּבְתּוֹ וַיַּעַל לִקְרַאת־יִשְׂרָאֵל אָבִיו גֹּשְׁנָה וַיֵּרָא אֵלָיו וַיִּפֹּל עַל־צַוָּארָיו וַיֵּבְךְּ עַל־צַוָּארָיו עוֹד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayye'esor Yosef merkavto vayya'al likrat-Yisra'el aviv Goshnah vayyera elav vayyippol al-tzavvarav vayyevk al-tzavvarav od.
verses.study_note: The climactic reunion. Joseph 'made ready his chariot' (vayye'esor merkavto) — the vizier of Egypt personally drives to meet his aged father. 'Went up' (vayya'al) — from the Nile Delta toward Canaan's direction. 'Presented himself unto him' (vayyera elav — literally 'appeared to him') — the same language used for divine theophanies. After twenty-two years of separation (Joseph was 17 when sold, now 39), father and son embrace. 'Fell on his neck and wept a good while' (vayyevk al-tzavvarav od) — literally 'wept on his neck still/yet more,' suggesting prolonged, uncontrollable weeping. The last time they saw each other, Joseph was a boy; now he is the most powerful man in Egypt. This reunion scene echoes the Prodigal Son parable (Luke 15:20) and prefigures every reunion between those long separated.
verses.doctrinal_note: The reunion of Jacob and Joseph is a type of ultimate restoration. As Joseph, who was 'dead' and is 'alive' (Gen 45:26-28), weeps over his father, so Christ, who was dead and rose again, will be reunited with His people. The joy of recognition after long separation is a foretaste of the joy of heaven, where 'God shall wipe away all tears' (Rev 21:4).

verses.kjv_text: And Israel said unto Joseph, Now let me die, since I have seen thy face, because thou art yet alive.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל־יוֹסֵף אָמוּתָה הַפָּעַם אַחֲרֵי רְאוֹתִי אֶת־פָּנֶיךָ כִּי עוֹדְךָ חָי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yisra'el el-Yosef amutah happa'am acharei re'oti et-panekha ki odekha chai.
verses.study_note: Jacob's words echo Simeon's 'Nunc Dimittis': 'Lord, now lettest thou thy servant depart in peace... for mine eyes have seen' (Luke 2:29-30). 'Now let me die' (amutah) — not despair but fulfillment. The man who wrestled with God, who mourned Joseph as dead for over two decades, has lived to see his son alive and exalted. 'Because thou art yet alive' (ki odekha chai) — these words carry the weight of twenty-two years of grief suddenly reversed. Note that Jacob speaks as 'Israel' here — in his covenant identity, the one who wrestled with God and prevailed. His life's deepest wound is healed.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph said unto his brethren, and unto his father’s house, I will go up, and shew Pharaoh, and say unto him, My brethren, and my father’s house, which were in the land of Canaan, are come unto me;
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל־אֶחָיו וְאֶל־בֵּית אָבִיו אֶעֱלֶה וְאַגִּידָה לְפַרְעֹה וְאֹמְרָה אֵלָיו אַחַי וּבֵית־אָבִי אֲשֶׁר בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן בָּאוּ אֵלָי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yosef el-echav ve'el-beit aviv e'eleh ve'aggidah leFar'oh ve'omrah elav achai uveit-avi asher be'eretz Kena'an ba'u elai.
verses.study_note: After the emotional reunion, Joseph transitions to practical wisdom. He must present his family to Pharaoh — a delicate diplomatic task. 'I will go up' (e'eleh) — from Goshen up to Pharaoh's court. Joseph mediates between two worlds: the Egyptian court and his Hebrew family. He is the bridge, the intercessor, the one who has access to the king. This mediating role is a type of Christ, who stands between God and His people, presenting them before the throne.

verses.kjv_text: And the men are shepherds, for their trade hath been to feed cattle; and they have brought their flocks, and their herds, and all that they have.
verses.original_text: וְהָאֲנָשִׁים רֹעֵי צֹאן כִּי־אַנְשֵׁי מִקְנֶה הָיוּ וְצֹאנָם וּבְקָרָם וְכָל־אֲשֶׁר לָהֶם הֵבִיאוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Veha'anashim ro'ei tzon ki-anshei mikneh hayu vetzonam ubekaram vekhol-asher lahem hevi'u.
verses.study_note: Joseph coaches his family: emphasize that they are 'shepherds' (ro'ei tzon) and 'men of cattle' (anshei mikneh). This is both truthful and strategic. Their occupation as shepherds will serve as the basis for requesting Goshen — the best grazing land, safely removed from the centers of Egyptian society. Joseph's wisdom protects the family's identity by leveraging Egyptian cultural prejudices. The irony is rich: shepherds are 'an abomination' to Egypt (v.34), yet God's chosen people are consistently identified as shepherds — Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Moses, David, and ultimately Christ, the Good Shepherd (John 10:11).

verses.kjv_text: And it shall come to pass, when Pharaoh shall call you, and shall say, What is your occupation?
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה כִּי־יִקְרָא לָכֶם פַּרְעֹה וְאָמַר מַה־מַּעֲשֵׂיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah ki-yikra lakhem Par'oh ve'amar mah-ma'aseikhem.
verses.study_note: Joseph prepares them for Pharaoh's inevitable question: 'What is your occupation?' (mah-ma'aseikhem — literally 'what are your works/deeds?'). Joseph knows the court protocol and anticipates the interview. This preparation reflects divine wisdom — Joseph ensures his family will answer in a way that serves God's covenant purposes. Their answer will secure Goshen, which provides both the best pasture and the necessary cultural separation to preserve their identity.

verses.kjv_text: That ye shall say, Thy servants’ trade hath been about cattle from our youth even until now, both we, and also our fathers: that ye may dwell in the land of Goshen; for every shepherd is an abomination unto the Egyptians.
verses.original_text: וַאֲמַרְתֶּם אַנְשֵׁי מִקְנֶה הָיוּ עֲבָדֶיךָ מִנְּעוּרֵינוּ וְעַד־עַתָּה גַּם־אֲנַחְנוּ גַּם־אֲבֹתֵינוּ בַּעֲבוּר תֵּשְׁבוּ בְּאֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן כִּי־תוֹעֲבַת מִצְרַיִם כָּל־רֹעֵה צֹאן׃
verses.transliteration: Va'amartem anshei mikneh hayu avadekha minne'ureinu ve'ad-attah gam-anachnu gam-avoteinu ba'avur teshvu be'eretz Goshen ki-to'avat Mitzrayim kol-ro'eh tzon.
verses.study_note: The final verse reveals Joseph's brilliant strategy: because 'every shepherd is an abomination unto the Egyptians' (to'avat Mitzrayim kol-ro'eh tzon), declaring themselves shepherds ensures they will be settled in Goshen — separated from Egyptian society. What seems like a social stigma becomes a divine protection. The Egyptians' prejudice against shepherds keeps Israel culturally distinct during their four centuries in Egypt, preventing total assimilation. 'From our youth even until now, both we and our fathers' — this generational identity as shepherds stretches back to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. Joseph turns Egyptian xenophobia into a shield for covenant preservation.
verses.doctrinal_note: God uses the world's prejudices to preserve His people. What the Egyptians despised — shepherding — became the very thing that kept Israel distinct and identifiable for four hundred years. This principle recurs throughout Scripture: the world's rejection of God's people serves as a boundary that preserves their identity. 'The stone which the builders rejected' becomes 'the head of the corner' (Psalm 118:22). What the world calls 'abomination,' God calls His own.

meta.overview: Genesis 46 is a pivotal chapter marking the great turning point in Israel's history — the descent of the entire covenant family into Egypt, beginning the 430-year sojourn that will culminate in the Exodus. The chapter opens at Beersheba, the southernmost point of the Promised Land, where God appears to Jacob in a night vision with a fourfold promise: (1) I am God, (2) fear not, (3) I will make thee a great nation, and (4) I will go down with thee and bring thee up again. This divine reassurance enables Jacob to leave Canaan — not as an exile fleeing famine, but as a patriarch obeying God's directive. The central section preserves the genealogy of the seventy souls who entered Egypt — a comprehensive family register that becomes the foundation of the twelve tribes of Israel. The chapter climaxes in the deeply emotional reunion between Jacob and Joseph after twenty-two years of separation, followed by Joseph's shrewd instructions to present the family as shepherds — securing their settlement in Goshen, separate from Egyptian society, and preserving their covenant identity.
meta.theme: Divine Assurance in Transition & the Preservation of the Covenant Family
meta.keyWordMeaning: To go down, descend — used of Jacob's descent into Egypt. This 'going down' becomes the defining event of Israel's pre-Exodus history, with 'going up' (alah) from Egypt as its counterpart in the Exodus.
outline: God's Vision at Beersheba (vv.1-4): Sacrifice, divine reassurance, and the fourfold promise
The Descent into Egypt (vv.5-7): The family departs Canaan with all they have
The Genealogy of Israel (vv.8-25): The seventy souls — sons of Leah (vv.8-15), sons of Zilpah (vv.16-18), sons of Rachel (vv.19-22), sons of Bilhah (vv.23-25)
The Numbering (vv.26-27): Sixty-six plus Joseph's household equals seventy
The Reunion in Goshen (vv.28-30): Judah sent ahead, Joseph meets Jacob, 'Now let me die'
Joseph's Strategy Before Pharaoh (vv.31-34): Shepherds — an abomination to Egypt — securing Goshen for Israel's preservation

verses.kjv_text: Then Joseph came and told Pharaoh, and said, My father and my brethren, and their flocks, and their herds, and all that they have, are come out of the land of Canaan; and, behold, they are in the land of Goshen.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא יוֹסֵף וַיַּגֵּד לְפַרְעֹה וַיֹּאמֶר אָבִי וְאַחַי וְצֹאנָם וּבְקָרָם וְכָל־אֲשֶׁר לָהֶם בָּאוּ מֵאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן וְהִנָּם בְּאֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayavo Yosef vayyagged leFar'oh vayomer avi ve'achai vetzonam uveqaram vekhol-asher lahem ba'u me'eretz Kena'an vehinnam be'eretz Goshen.
verses.study_note: Joseph acts as mediator between his family and Pharaoh — a role that foreshadows Christ as the mediator between God and His people. He has already positioned the family in Goshen (46:28-34), and now formally announces their arrival. The emphasis on 'flocks, herds, and all that they have' underscores that this is not a destitute refugee family but a prosperous pastoral clan. Goshen (the eastern Nile delta) was ideal for shepherds — fertile grazing land yet separate from the Egyptian population centers. Joseph's strategy protects both his family's livelihood and their distinct identity as God's covenant people.

verses.kjv_text: And he took some of his brethren, even five men, and presented them unto Pharaoh.
verses.original_text: וּמִקְצֵה אֶחָיו לָקַח חֲמִשָּׁה אֲנָשִׁים וַיַּצִּגֵם לִפְנֵי פַרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Umiktzeh echav lakach chamishah anashim vayyatzigem lifnei Far'oh.
verses.study_note: Joseph selects five brothers — 'miktzeh' can mean 'from the end/extremity' or 'some of.' Rabbinic tradition suggests he chose the least physically imposing to avoid them being conscripted into Pharaoh's army. The number five recurs throughout the Joseph narrative (45:22 — five changes of garments for Benjamin; 43:34 — Benjamin's portion five times the others). Joseph exercises wisdom in managing the encounter, ensuring the outcome serves God's purposes for the family.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh said unto his brethren, What is your occupation? And they said unto Pharaoh, Thy servants are shepherds, both we, and also our fathers.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה אֶל־אֶחָיו מַה־מַּעֲשֵׂיכֶם וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֶל־פַּרְעֹה רֹעֵה צֹאן עֲבָדֶיךָ גַּם־אֲנַחְנוּ גַּם־אֲבוֹתֵינוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Far'oh el-echav mah-ma'aseikhem vayyomeru el-Far'oh ro'eh tzon avadekha gam-anachnu gam-avoteinu.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh asks about their occupation — a practical question about how they will contribute to Egyptian society. The brothers answer exactly as Joseph had coached them (46:34): 'shepherds.' The word 'ro'eh' (shepherd) carries deep theological weight — God Himself is called the Shepherd of Israel (Psalm 23:1; 80:1). Their ancestral occupation, despised by Egyptians (46:34), is the very vocation God uses as a metaphor for His own care. 'Both we, and also our fathers' — four generations of shepherding (Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and now the twelve).

verses.kjv_text: They said moreover unto Pharaoh, For to sojourn in the land are we come; for thy servants have no pasture for their flocks; for the famine is sore in the land of Canaan: now therefore, we pray thee, let thy servants dwell in the land of Goshen.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֶל־פַּרְעֹה לָגוּר בָּאָרֶץ בָּאנוּ כִּי־אֵין מִרְעֶה לַצֹּאן אֲשֶׁר לַעֲבָדֶיךָ כִּי־כָבֵד הָרָעָב בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן וְעַתָּה יֵשְׁבוּ־נָא עֲבָדֶיךָ בְּאֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru el-Far'oh lagur ba'aretz banu ki-ein mir'eh latzon asher la'avadekha ki-khaved hara'av be'eretz Kena'an ve'attah yeshvu-na avadekha be'eretz Goshen.
verses.study_note: The key word is 'lagur' — to sojourn, to dwell as a temporary resident, not to settle permanently. The brothers define their presence in Egypt as transient from the very beginning. This is remarkable: even as they request permission to live in Goshen, they frame it as temporary. The famine in Canaan ('khaved' — heavy, severe) is the immediate cause, but behind it stands God's sovereign plan revealed to Abraham centuries earlier (15:13-16). They request Goshen specifically — maintaining the separation Joseph had strategized.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh spake unto Joseph, saying, Thy father and thy brethren are come unto thee:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה אֶל־יוֹסֵף לֵאמֹר אָבִיךָ וְאַחֶיךָ בָּאוּ אֵלֶיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Far'oh el-Yosef lemor avikha ve'achekha ba'u elekha.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh responds to Joseph rather than directly to the brothers — confirming Joseph's role as mediator. The phrase 'are come unto thee' acknowledges that Joseph is the reason for their presence and the center of this arrangement. Pharaoh's favorable response reflects both his gratitude to Joseph and God's providential favor over the covenant family. The phrasing subtly acknowledges Joseph's authority — the family has come to Joseph, and Pharaoh defers to his vizier in settling them.

verses.kjv_text: The land of Egypt is before thee; in the best of the land make thy father and brethren to dwell; in the land of Goshen let them dwell: and if thou knowest any men of activity among them, then make them rulers over my cattle.
verses.original_text: אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם לְפָנֶיךָ הִוא בְּמֵיטַב הָאָרֶץ הוֹשֵׁב אֶת־אָבִיךָ וְאֶת־אַחֶיךָ יֵשְׁבוּ בְּאֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן וְאִם־יָדַעְתָּ וְיֶשׁ־בָּם אַנְשֵׁי־חַיִל וְשַׂמְתָּם שָׂרֵי מִקְנֶה עַל־אֲשֶׁר־לִי׃
verses.transliteration: Eretz Mitzrayim lefanekha hi bemetav ha'aretz hoshev et-avikha ve'et-achekha yeshvu be'eretz Goshen ve'im-yadata veyesh-bam anshei-chayil vesamtam sarei mikneh al-asher-li.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh grants extraordinary favor: (1) 'The land of Egypt is before thee' — open access, (2) 'the best of the land' (bemetav) — the choicest territory, (3) Goshen confirmed, and (4) employment managing royal livestock. 'Anshei-chayil' (men of activity/valor) indicates capable, industrious men. This phrase is used elsewhere for military heroes (Judges 6:12) and the virtuous woman (Prov 31:10). Pharaoh recognizes the skill of Hebrew shepherds. God is fulfilling His promise: the family is provided for, protected, and positioned for future growth.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph brought in Jacob his father, and set him before Pharaoh: and Jacob blessed Pharaoh.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֵא יוֹסֵף אֶת־יַעֲקֹב אָבִיו וַיַּעֲמִדֵהוּ לִפְנֵי פַרְעֹה וַיְבָרֶךְ יַעֲקֹב אֶת־פַּרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayave Yosef et-Ya'akov aviv vayya'amidehu lifnei Far'oh vayevarekh Ya'akov et-Far'oh.
verses.study_note: One of the most remarkable moments in Genesis. Jacob — a aged, limping nomad — blesses Pharaoh, the most powerful ruler in the ancient world. 'Vayevarekh' (and he blessed) is emphatic and deliberate. The author of Hebrews draws a principle from this: 'without all contradiction the less is blessed of the better' (Heb 7:7). Jacob, despite his humble appearance, holds spiritual authority that transcends earthly power. He is the covenant bearer, the one through whom all nations will be blessed (Gen 28:14). Pharaoh has political power; Jacob has covenant authority.
verses.doctrinal_note: The greater blesses the lesser (Hebrews 7:7): Jacob's blessing of Pharaoh demonstrates that spiritual authority transcends political power. The patriarch of God's covenant people pronounces blessing upon the world's mightiest king. This principle is central to understanding the priesthood of Melchizedek (Heb 7) and ultimately Christ's authority over all earthly powers.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh said unto Jacob, How old art thou?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה אֶל־יַעֲקֹב כַּמָּה יְמֵי שְׁנֵי חַיֶּיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Far'oh el-Ya'akov kammah yemei shenei chayyekha.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh's question is literally: 'How many are the days of the years of your life?' (kammah yemei shenei chayyekha). The elaborate phrasing is not merely formal — it reflects genuine awe at Jacob's aged appearance. Jacob at 130 would have appeared ancient to the Egyptians, whose average lifespan was far shorter. The question triggers one of the most theologically profound responses in Genesis — Jacob's pilgrim testimony.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob said unto Pharaoh, The days of the years of my pilgrimage are an hundred and thirty years: few and evil have the days of the years of my life been, and have not attained unto the days of the years of the life of my fathers in the days of their pilgrimage.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב אֶל־פַּרְעֹה יְמֵי שְׁנֵי מְגוּרַי שְׁלֹשִׁים וּמְאַת שָׁנָה מְעַט וְרָעִים הָיוּ יְמֵי שְׁנֵי חַיַּי וְלֹא הִשִּׂיגוּ אֶת־יְמֵי שְׁנֵי חַיֵּי אֲבֹתַי בִּימֵי מְגוּרֵיהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Ya'akov el-Far'oh yemei shenei megurai sheloshim ume'at shanah me'at vera'im hayu yemei shenei chayyai velo hissigu et-yemei shenei chayyei avotai bimei megureihem.
verses.study_note: Jacob's answer is one of the most profound theological statements in Genesis. He does not merely state his age — he delivers a pilgrim's confession. Key elements: (1) He calls his life 'megurim' (sojournings/pilgrimage), not a settled existence. (2) 'Few' (me'at) — 130 years he considers brief. Abraham lived to 175, Isaac to 180. (3) 'Evil' (ra'im) — not morally evil but difficult, painful, full of hardship. Jacob's life included flight from Esau, Laban's deception, Dinah's violation, Joseph's 'death,' years of grief. (4) He compares himself to his fathers' pilgrimages — Abraham and Isaac were also sojourners. This verse is the theological foundation for the 'pilgrim' identity that defines God's people throughout Scripture (Heb 11:13-16; 1 Pet 2:11).
verses.doctrinal_note: The Pilgrim Theology: Jacob's confession establishes that God's people are fundamentally sojourners — temporary residents passing through a fallen world toward a promised homeland. Hebrews 11:13-16 interprets this: 'they confessed that they were strangers and pilgrims on the earth... they desire a better country, that is, an heavenly.' This pilgrim identity defines the Christian life — we are citizens of heaven (Phil 3:20), sojourning through this present age.

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob blessed Pharaoh, and went out from before Pharaoh.
verses.original_text: וַיְבָרֶךְ יַעֲקֹב אֶת־פַּרְעֹה וַיֵּצֵא מִלִּפְנֵי פַרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayevarekh Ya'akov et-Far'oh vayyetze millifnei Far'oh.
verses.study_note: Jacob blesses Pharaoh a second time — bookending the audience with blessing (vv.7,10). The repetition emphasizes that Jacob's primary role before the world's most powerful ruler is as a channel of divine blessing. This fulfills the Abrahamic promise: 'in thee shall all families of the earth be blessed' (Gen 12:3). Jacob enters and exits as the greater party — not politically, but spiritually. He who wrestled with God (32:28) now stands as God's representative before Egypt's throne.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph placed his father and his brethren, and gave them a possession in the land of Egypt, in the best of the land, in the land of Rameses, as Pharaoh had commanded.
verses.original_text: וַיּוֹשֵׁב יוֹסֵף אֶת־אָבִיו וְאֶת־אֶחָיו וַיִּתֵּן לָהֶם אֲחֻזָּה בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם בְּמֵיטַב הָאָרֶץ בְּאֶרֶץ רַעְמְסֵס כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה פַרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyoshev Yosef et-aviv ve'et-echav vayyitten lahem achuzzah be'eretz Mitzrayim bemetav ha'aretz be'eretz Ra'meses ka'asher tzivvah Far'oh.
verses.study_note: Joseph settles the family with an 'achuzzah' (possession/landed property) — a significant term. This same word describes the promised inheritance in Canaan (Gen 17:8; 48:4). They receive actual territorial possession in Egypt's finest region. 'The land of Rameses' is an editorial update of 'Goshen' — later known by the name of the dynasty that built its capital there (Exodus 1:11). Joseph fulfills his role as the providential sustainer, a type of Christ who prepares a place for His people (John 14:2-3).

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph nourished his father, and his brethren, and all his father’s household, with bread, according to their families.
verses.original_text: וַיְכַלְכֵּל יוֹסֵף אֶת־אָבִיו וְאֶת־אֶחָיו וְאֵת כָּל־בֵּית אָבִיו לֶחֶם לְפִי הַטָּף׃
verses.transliteration: Vayekhalkkel Yosef et-aviv ve'et-echav ve'et kol-beit aviv lechem lefi hattaf.
verses.study_note: Joseph 'nourished' (vayekhalkkel — from 'kul,' to sustain, provide) his entire family. The phrase 'lefi hattaf' literally means 'according to the mouth of the little ones' — provision was measured by the needs of the most vulnerable. Joseph sustains the family that once rejected him — a powerful type of Christ who sustains those who crucified Him. The verb 'kalkkel' (to nourish/sustain) appears in 1 Kings 17:4,9 of God sustaining Elijah — divine provision through a human agent.

verses.kjv_text: And there was no bread in all the land; for the famine was very sore, so that the land of Egypt and all the land of Canaan fainted by reason of the famine.
verses.original_text: וְלֶחֶם אֵין בְּכָל־הָאָרֶץ כִּי־כָבֵד הָרָעָב מְאֹד וַתֵּלַהּ אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם וְאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן מִפְּנֵי הָרָעָב׃
verses.transliteration: Velechem ein bekhol-ha'aretz ki-khaved hara'av me'od vattelah eretz Mitzrayim ve'eretz Kena'an mippenei hara'av.
verses.study_note: The narrative now shifts from family provision to national crisis. 'Fainted' (vattelah) — the land was exhausted, languished. The same word describes the spiritual exhaustion of Israel in the wilderness (Exodus 17:3). The famine affects both Egypt and Canaan — no one escapes except through Joseph's stored grain. This section (vv.13-26) shows Joseph as the sole source of survival, reinforcing his typological role as Christ — the 'Bread of Life' apart from whom there is no sustenance (John 6:35).

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph gathered up all the money that was found in the land of Egypt, and in the land of Canaan, for the corn which they bought: and Joseph brought the money into Pharaoh’s house.
verses.original_text: וַיְלַקֵּט יוֹסֵף אֶת־כָּל־הַכֶּסֶף הַנִּמְצָא בְאֶרֶץ־מִצְרַיִם וּבְאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן בַּשֶּׁבֶר אֲשֶׁר־הֵם שֹׁבְרִים וַיָּבֵא יוֹסֵף אֶת־הַכֶּסֶף בֵּיתָה פַרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayelaqqet Yosef et-kol-hakkesef hannimtza ve'eretz-Mitzrayim uve'eretz Kena'an bashshever asher-hem shoverim vayyave Yosef et-hakkesef betah Far'oh.
verses.study_note: Phase one of Joseph's economic administration: money for grain. 'Vayelaqqet' (gathered up) — the same word used for gleaning in the fields (Ruth 2:2). Joseph systematically collects all available currency. Importantly, he brings it 'into Pharaoh's house' — he does not enrich himself. Joseph's integrity in handling wealth foreshadows Christ's teaching: 'It is more blessed to give than to receive' (Acts 20:35). The money from both Egypt and Canaan flows to Pharaoh, centralizing economic power.

verses.kjv_text: And when money failed in the land of Egypt, and in the land of Canaan, all the Egyptians came unto Joseph, and said, Give us bread: for why should we die in thy presence? for the money faileth.
verses.original_text: וַיִּתֹּם הַכֶּסֶף מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם וּמֵאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן וַיָּבֹאוּ כָל־מִצְרַיִם אֶל־יוֹסֵף לֵאמֹר הָבָה־לָּנוּ לֶחֶם וְלָמָּה נָמוּת נֶגְדֶּךָ כִּי אָפֵס כָּסֶף׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyittom hakkesef me'eretz Mitzrayim ume'eretz Kena'an vayyavo'u khol-Mitzrayim el-Yosef lemor havah-lanu lechem velammah namut negdekha ki afes kasef.
verses.study_note: The money is exhausted (vayyittom — 'was finished/completed'). The people cry: 'Give us bread! Why should we die in your presence?' This desperate plea — 'havah-lanu lechem' — echoes through Scripture. Only Joseph has food; only he stands between them and death. The theological parallel is powerful: apart from Christ, humanity has nothing to offer God and stands helpless before death. The people come to Joseph not with payment but with empty hands, pleading for mercy.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph said, Give your cattle; and I will give you for your cattle, if money fail.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף הָבוּ מִקְנֵיכֶם וְאֶתְּנָה לָכֶם בְּמִקְנֵיכֶם אִם־אָפֵס כָּסֶף׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yosef havu miqneikhem ve'ettenah lakhem bemiqneikhem im-afes kasef.
verses.study_note: Phase two: livestock for grain. Joseph does not let them starve — he provides an alternative means of exchange. 'Give your cattle' (havu miqneikhem) — livestock represented a family's primary capital and means of production. Surrendering livestock meant losing the ability to sustain themselves independently. Yet Joseph's offer keeps them alive. This progressive economic restructuring has been debated by scholars — some see wise crisis management, others see exploitation. The text presents it neutrally, noting that the people themselves eventually express gratitude (v.25).

verses.kjv_text: And they brought their cattle unto Joseph: and Joseph gave them bread in exchange for horses, and for the flocks, and for the cattle of the herds, and for the asses: and he fed them with bread for all their cattle for that year.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבִיאוּ אֶת־מִקְנֵיהֶם אֶל־יוֹסֵף וַיִּתֵּן לָהֶם יוֹסֵף לֶחֶם בַּסּוּסִים וּבְמִקְנֵה הַצֹּאן וּבְמִקְנֵה הַבָּקָר וּבַחֲמֹרִים וַיְנַהֲלֵם בַּלֶּחֶם בְּכָל־מִקְנֵהֶם בַּשָּׁנָה הַהִוא׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavi'u et-miqneihem el-Yosef vayyitten lahem Yosef lechem bassusim uvemiqneh hatzon uvemiqneh habaqar uva-chamorim vayenahalem ballechem bekhol-miqnehem bashshanah hahi.
verses.study_note: Four types of livestock are listed: horses, sheep, cattle, and donkeys — representing the full range of Egyptian animal wealth. 'Vayenahalem' (and he fed/guided them) — from 'nahal,' to lead to water, to guide gently (used of a shepherd leading flocks; cf. Psalm 23:2). Even in this commercial transaction, the narrator uses a pastoral, caring verb. Joseph sustains them through an entire year. This is not predatory — it is crisis management that preserves life.

verses.kjv_text: When that year was ended, they came unto him the second year, and said unto him, We will not hide it from my lord, how that our money is spent; my lord also hath our herds of cattle; there is not ought left in the sight of my lord, but our bodies, and our lands:
verses.original_text: וַתִּתֹּם הַשָּׁנָה הַהִוא וַיָּבֹאוּ אֵלָיו בַּשָּׁנָה הַשֵּׁנִית וַיֹּאמְרוּ לוֹ לֹא־נְכַחֵד מֵאֲדֹנִי כִּי אִם־תַּם הַכֶּסֶף וּמִקְנֵה הַבְּהֵמָה אֶל־אֲדֹנִי לֹא נִשְׁאַר לִפְנֵי אֲדֹנִי בִּלְתִּי אִם־גְּוִיָּתֵנוּ וְאַדְמָתֵנוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vatittom hashshanah hahi vayyavo'u elav bashshanah hashenit vayyomeru lo lo-nekakhed me'adoni ki im-tam hakkesef umikneh habbehemah el-adoni lo nish'ar lifnei adoni bilti im-geviyyatenu ve'admatenu.
verses.study_note: The people return the following year with radical honesty: 'We will not hide it' (lo-nekakhed). They catalog what they have already surrendered (money, livestock) and state plainly what remains: 'our bodies and our lands' (geviyyatenu ve'admatenu). This unflinching self-assessment before the one who holds the food supply mirrors humanity's position before God — nothing to offer except ourselves. The progression from money to livestock to bodies and land traces the complete exhaustion of human resources before sovereign provision.

verses.kjv_text: Wherefore shall we die before thine eyes, both we and our land? buy us and our land for bread, and we and our land will be servants unto Pharaoh: and give us seed, that we may live, and not die, that the land be not desolate.
verses.original_text: לָמָּה נָמוּת לְעֵינֶיךָ גַּם־אֲנַחְנוּ גַּם אַדְמָתֵנוּ קְנֵה־אֹתָנוּ וְאֶת־אַדְמָתֵנוּ בַּלָּחֶם וְנִהְיֶה אֲנַחְנוּ וְאַדְמָתֵנוּ עֲבָדִים לְפַרְעֹה וְתֶן־זֶרַע וְנִחְיֶה וְלֹא נָמוּת וְהָאֲדָמָה לֹא תֵשָׁם׃
verses.transliteration: Lammah namut le'einekha gam-anachnu gam admatenu qeneh-otanu ve'et-admatenu ballechem venihyeh anachnu ve'admatenu avadim leFar'oh veten-zera venichyeh velo namut veha'adamah lo tesham.
verses.study_note: The people offer themselves and their land in exchange for survival. Two remarkable requests emerge: (1) 'Buy us' (qeneh-otanu) — they volunteer for servitude, and (2) 'Give us seed' (veten-zera) — beyond immediate food, they ask for the means to produce future harvests. This is wisdom within desperation: they think beyond survival to sustainability. 'That the land be not desolate' (lo tesham) shows concern for the future. The word 'zera' (seed) carries its full range of meaning — agricultural seed, offspring, and covenant promise. Even in crisis, the seed must continue.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph bought all the land of Egypt for Pharaoh; for the Egyptians sold every man his field, because the famine prevailed over them: so the land became Pharaoh’s.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקֶן יוֹסֵף אֶת־כָּל־אַדְמַת מִצְרַיִם לְפַרְעֹה כִּי־מָכְרוּ מִצְרַיִם אִישׁ שָׂדֵהוּ כִּי־חָזַק עֲלֵהֶם הָרָעָב וַתְּהִי הָאָרֶץ לְפַרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqen Yosef et-kol-admat Mitzrayim leFar'oh ki-makheru Mitzrayim ish sadehu ki-chazaq alehem hara'av vattehi ha'aretz leFar'oh.
verses.study_note: Phase three complete: Joseph acquires all the land of Egypt for Pharaoh. 'Every man his field' (ish sadehu) — individual land ownership, the foundation of personal independence, is surrendered. The famine 'prevailed over them' (chazaq — was strong against them). The entire nation is now dependent on Pharaoh. This massive centralization of power will have consequences: the same state apparatus that Joseph builds will eventually enslave his own descendants (Exod 1:8-14). God's providence works through complex, sometimes ironic, historical processes.

verses.kjv_text: And as for the people, he removed them to cities from one end of the borders of Egypt even to the other end thereof.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־הָעָם הֶעֱבִיר אֹתוֹ לֶעָרִים מִקְצֵה גְבוּל־מִצְרַיִם וְעַד־קָצֵהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-ha'am he'evir oto le'arim miktzeh gevul-Mitzrayim ve'ad-qatzehu.
verses.study_note: Joseph relocates the population to cities — a massive resettlement program. 'He'evir' (removed/transferred) — he moved them from their ancestral lands into urban centers. This served both administrative efficiency and broke the people's attachment to their former properties. The phrase 'from one end... to the other' emphasizes the totality of the reorganization. Some see this as wise consolidation during famine; others see the foundation of the totalitarian state that will later oppress Israel. The Septuagint reads differently: 'he made them servants' — either way, the transformation of Egyptian society is complete.

verses.kjv_text: Only the land of the priests bought he not; for the priests had a portion assigned them of Pharaoh, and did eat their portion which Pharaoh gave them: wherefore they sold not their lands.
verses.original_text: רַק אַדְמַת הַכֹּהֲנִים לֹא קָנָה כִּי חֹק לַכֹּהֲנִים מֵאֵת פַּרְעֹה וְאָכְלוּ אֶת־חֻקָּם אֲשֶׁר נָתַן לָהֶם פַּרְעֹה עַל־כֵּן לֹא מָכְרוּ אֶת־אַדְמָתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Raq admat hakkohanim lo qanah ki choq lakkohanim me'et Far'oh ve'okhelu et-chuqqam asher natan lahem Far'oh al-ken lo makheru et-admatam.
verses.study_note: A significant exception: the priests retain their land because they receive a 'choq' (statutory portion/allotment) from Pharaoh. The priestly class was maintained by the state and therefore did not need to sell. This detail has remarkable parallels: in Israel's later law, the Levites receive no land inheritance but are sustained by the tithe and offerings (Num 18:20-24; Deut 18:1-2). Joseph's policy, whether consciously or providentially, prefigures the Levitical arrangement. Even in pagan Egypt, the principle of priestly provision is recognized.
verses.doctrinal_note: The exemption of the priestly class establishes a principle that resonates throughout Scripture: those devoted to sacred service are provided for by the community rather than through commercial means. This is fulfilled in the Levitical system (Num 18:20-24) and in the New Testament principle that 'the labourer is worthy of his hire' (Luke 10:7; 1 Tim 5:18).

verses.kjv_text: Then Joseph said unto the people, Behold, I have bought you this day and your land for Pharaoh: lo, here is seed for you, and ye shall sow the land.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל־הָעָם הֵן קָנִיתִי אֶתְכֶם הַיּוֹם וְאֶת־אַדְמַתְכֶם לְפַרְעֹה הֵא־לָכֶם זֶרַע וּזְרַעְתֶּם אֶת־הָאֲדָמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yosef el-ha'am hen qaniti etkhem hayyom ve'et-admatkhem leFar'oh he-lakhem zera uzera'tem et-ha'adamah.
verses.study_note: Joseph formalizes the arrangement and — crucially — provides seed (zera) for planting. He does not merely take; he gives them the means to produce again. 'Here is seed for you' (he-lakhem zera) — this is the provision that transforms servitude into sustainability. The people are now tenant farmers on Pharaoh's land, but they have the tools to work and eat. Joseph's genius is that he saves both the people and the agricultural economy. The gift of seed in the midst of famine is an act of grace within the structure of obligation.

verses.kjv_text: And it shall come to pass in the increase, that ye shall give the fifth part unto Pharaoh, and four parts shall be your own, for seed of the field, and for your food, and for them of your households, and for food for your little ones.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה בַּתְּבוּאֹת וּנְתַתֶּם חֲמִישִׁית לְפַרְעֹה וְאַרְבַּע הַיָּדֹת יִהְיֶה לָכֶם לְזֶרַע הַשָּׂדֶה וּלְאָכְלְכֶם וְלַאֲשֶׁר בְּבָתֵּיכֶם וְלֶאֱכֹל לְטַפְּכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah battevu'ot unetattem chamishit leFar'oh ve'arba hayyadot yihyeh lakhem lezera hassadeh ule'okhlekhem vela'asher bevateikhem ule'ekhol letappekhem.
verses.study_note: Joseph establishes the tax rate: one-fifth (20%) of all produce goes to Pharaoh; four-fifths (80%) remains with the people. This is a remarkably moderate rate compared to other ancient Near Eastern tax systems, which could demand 50-67%. The 80% remainder is carefully allocated: seed for next year's planting, food for the household, and food for the children (tappekhem — the little ones). Joseph's policy is structured, fair, and sustainable. By comparison, when Israel later asks for a king, Samuel warns that the king will take 'the tenth' (1 Sam 8:15,17) — even half of Joseph's rate is presented as burdensome.

verses.kjv_text: And they said, Thou hast saved our lives: let us find grace in the sight of my lord, and we will be Pharaoh’s servants.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ הֶחֱיִתָנוּ נִמְצָא־חֵן בְּעֵינֵי אֲדֹנִי וְהָיִינוּ עֲבָדִים לְפַרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru hecheyitanu nimtza-chen be'einei adoni vehayinu avadim leFar'oh.
verses.study_note: The people's response is striking: gratitude, not resentment. 'Thou hast saved our lives' (hecheyitanu — 'you have caused us to live'). They seek 'grace' (chen — favor) and willingly accept servant status. This positive response suggests Joseph's administration was perceived as just and life-saving, not oppressive. The word 'hecheyitanu' connects to Joseph's own theological interpretation: 'God sent me before you to preserve life' (45:5). The people unknowingly confirm God's purpose through Joseph.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph made it a law over the land of Egypt unto this day, that Pharaoh should have the fifth part; except the land of the priests only, which became not Pharaoh’s.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשֶׂם אֹתָהּ יוֹסֵף לְחֹק עַד־הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה עַל־אַדְמַת מִצְרַיִם לְפַרְעֹה לַחֹמֶשׁ רַק אַדְמַת הַכֹּהֲנִים לְבַדָּם לֹא הָיְתָה לְפַרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyasem otah Yosef lechoq ad-hayyom hazzeh al-admat Mitzrayim leFar'oh lachomesh raq admat hakkohanim levaddam lo hayetah leFar'oh.
verses.study_note: Joseph's tax becomes permanent law (lechoq — a statute, the same word used for God's commandments). 'Unto this day' (ad-hayyom hazzeh) — a note from the narrator's perspective confirming the policy endured for centuries. The 20% royal tax and the priestly exemption became defining features of Egyptian economic life. This verse summarizes the entire economic restructuring (vv.13-26): Joseph preserved the nation through crisis and established an enduring administrative framework. The priestly exemption is restated, emphasizing its significance.

verses.kjv_text: And Israel dwelt in the land of Egypt, in the country of Goshen; and they had possessions therein, and grew, and multiplied exceedingly.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּשֶׁב יִשְׂרָאֵל בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם בְּאֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן וַיֵּאָחֲזוּ בָהּ וַיִּפְרוּ וַיִּרְבּוּ מְאֹד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyeshev Yisra'el be'eretz Mitzrayim be'eretz Goshen vayye'achezu vah vayyifru vayyirbu me'od.
verses.study_note: A pivotal transition verse. The narrator now shifts from Joseph's Egyptian administration back to the covenant family. Three key verbs describe Israel's experience: (1) 'vayye'achezu' (had possessions/took hold) — they put down roots in Goshen; (2) 'vayyifru' (were fruitful) — the creation mandate of Genesis 1:28 is being fulfilled; (3) 'vayyirbu' (multiplied) — exactly as God promised Abraham (Gen 15:5; 17:2). The name 'Israel' is used rather than 'Jacob' — emphasizing the covenant people as a growing nation, not merely a family.
verses.doctrinal_note: The fulfillment of the Abrahamic covenant: 'I will make of thee a great nation' (Gen 12:2). Despite being in Egypt — not in the promised land — God's promise of fruitfulness and multiplication is powerfully at work. This demonstrates that God's covenant purposes are not limited by geography. The same God who promised a land also promised a people, and here the 'people' promise advances even in foreign soil. This pattern — growth in adversity — becomes a hallmark of God's people (Exod 1:12; Acts 12:24).

verses.kjv_text: And Jacob lived in the land of Egypt seventeen years: so the whole age of Jacob was an hundred forty and seven years.
verses.original_text: וַיְחִי יַעֲקֹב בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם שְׁבַע עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה וַיְהִי יְמֵי־יַעֲקֹב שְׁנֵי חַיָּיו שֶׁבַע וְאַרְבָּעִים וּמְאַת שָׁנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechi Ya'akov be'eretz Mitzrayim sheva esreh shanah vayehi yemei-Ya'akov shenei chayyav sheva ve'arba'im ume'at shanah.
verses.study_note: Jacob lives seventeen years in Egypt — the same number of years he had Joseph before losing him (Joseph was sold at 17; Gen 37:2). This literary symmetry suggests God restored the lost years. Jacob's total lifespan is 147 years: less than Abraham (175) and Isaac (180), consistent with his own assessment of a 'few and evil' life (v.9). The name 'Jacob' (not 'Israel') is used here — in death, the patriarch returns to his personal identity, the man who struggled and was transformed. 'Vayechi' (and he lived) is the first word of the Torah portion that bears the same name — Parashat Vayechi, the final portion of Genesis.

verses.kjv_text: And the time drew nigh that Israel must die: and he called his son Joseph, and said unto him, If now I have found grace in thy sight, put, I pray thee, thy hand under my thigh, and deal kindly and truly with me; bury me not, I pray thee, in Egypt:
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרְבוּ יְמֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל לָמוּת וַיִּקְרָא לִבְנוֹ לְיוֹסֵף וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ אִם־נָא מָצָאתִי חֵן בְּעֵינֶיךָ שִׂים־נָא יָדְךָ תַּחַת יְרֵכִי׃
