verses.kjv_text: And when the inhabitants of the land, the Canaanites, saw the mourning in the floor of Atad, they said, This is a grievous mourning to the Egyptians: wherefore the name of it was called Abelmizraim, which is beyond Jordan.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא יוֹשֵׁב הָאָרֶץ הַכְּנַעֲנִי אֶת־הָאֵבֶל בְּגֹרֶן הָאָטָד וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֵבֶל־כָּבֵד זֶה לְמִצְרָיִם עַל־כֵּן קָרָא שְׁמָהּ אָבֵל מִצְרַיִם אֲשֶׁר בְּעֵבֶר הַיַּרְדֵּן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyar yoshev ha'aretz haKena'ani et-ha'evel begoren ha'Atad vayyomeru evel-kaved zeh leMitzrayim al-ken kara shemah Avel Mitzrayim asher be'ever haYarden.
verses.study_note: The Canaanites witness the mourning and are so struck by its intensity that they name the place 'Abel-mizraim' — 'Mourning of Egypt' (or, by wordplay, 'Meadow of Egypt'). They identify the procession as Egyptian, not Hebrew — the irony is layered: Egyptians mourn for the patriarch of Israel, and Canaanites observe from outside. The name endures as a memorial. This is one of several place-namings in Genesis that anchor the narrative in geography and memory.

verses.kjv_text: And his sons did unto him according as he commanded them:
verses.original_text: וַיַּעֲשׂוּ בָנָיו לוֹ כֵּן כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'asu vanav lo ken ka'asher tzivvam.
verses.study_note: A brief, obedient summary: Jacob's sons fulfill his dying command exactly (Gen 49:29-32). The verb 'tzivvam' (he commanded them) carries the weight of sacred obligation. After a lifetime of conflict, deception, and dysfunction, the twelve brothers unite in one act of filial obedience. This is the only recorded moment in Genesis where all twelve sons act in complete harmony — carrying their father to his chosen resting place.

verses.kjv_text: For his sons carried him into the land of Canaan, and buried him in the cave of the field of Machpelah, which Abraham bought with the field for a possession of a buryingplace of Ephron the Hittite, before Mamre.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׂאוּ אֹתוֹ בָנָיו אַרְצָה כְּנַעַן וַיִּקְבְּרוּ אֹתוֹ בִּמְעָרַת שְׂדֵה הַמַּכְפֵּלָה אֲשֶׁר קָנָה אַבְרָהָם אֶת־הַשָּׂדֶה לַאֲחֻזַּת־קֶבֶר מֵאֵת עֶפְרֹן הַחִתִּי עַל־פְּנֵי מַמְרֵא׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyis'u oto vanav artzah Kena'an vayyikberu oto bim'arat sedeh hammakhpelah asher kanah Avraham et-hassadeh la'achuzzat-kever me'et Efron haChitti al-penei Mamre.
verses.study_note: The narrative traces the legal chain of ownership: Abraham purchased the cave from Ephron the Hittite (Gen 23). Now Abraham, Sarah, Isaac, Rebekah, and Leah are already buried there (49:31), and Jacob joins them. Machpelah means 'the double cave' — a family tomb. The precise legal language ('bought... for a possession... of Ephron the Hittite') emphasizes legitimate ownership of this land in Canaan. This is the only real estate the patriarchs owned in the Promised Land — yet it represents their claim on the entire inheritance by faith.
verses.doctrinal_note: The burial at Machpelah is a supreme act of covenant faith. The patriarchs chose to be buried in Canaan rather than Egypt, claiming the Promised Land even in death. Hebrews 11:13-16 declares they 'confessed that they were strangers and pilgrims on the earth' who desired 'a better country, that is, an heavenly.' Burial in the land was their testimony that God's promise would be fulfilled.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph returned into Egypt, he, and his brethren, and all that went up with him to bury his father, after he had buried his father.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשָׁב יוֹסֵף מִצְרַיְמָה הוּא וְאֶחָיו וְכָל־הָעֹלִים אִתּוֹ לִקְבֹּר אֶת־אָבִיו אַחֲרֵי קָבְרוֹ אֶת־אָבִיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashav Yosef Mitzraymah hu ve'echav vekhol-ha'olim itto likbor et-aviv acharei kovro et-aviv.
verses.study_note: Joseph keeps his word to Pharaoh (v.5) — he returns to Egypt. But the return carries ominous overtones: they go back to a land that is not their inheritance. The repetition of 'his father' (aviv) twice underscores the finality. With Jacob gone, the family dynamic shifts dramatically — as the following verses will reveal, the brothers fear that the protective buffer of their father has been removed.

verses.kjv_text: And when Joseph’s brethren saw that their father was dead, they said, Joseph will peradventure hate us, and will certainly requite us all the evil which we did unto him.
verses.original_text: וַיִּרְאוּ אֲחֵי־יוֹסֵף כִּי־מֵת אֲבִיהֶם וַיֹּאמְרוּ לוּ יִשְׂטְמֵנוּ יוֹסֵף וְהָשֵׁב יָשִׁיב לָנוּ אֵת כָּל־הָרָעָה אֲשֶׁר גָּמַלְנוּ אֹתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyir'u achei-Yosef ki-met avihem vayyomeru lu yistemenu Yosef vehashev yashiv lanu et kol-hara'ah asher gamalnu oto.
verses.study_note: With Jacob's death, the brothers' buried guilt resurfaces. 'Joseph will peradventure hate us' (lu yistemenu) — 'yistemenu' (bear a grudge, hate) is the same word used of Esau's hatred toward Jacob (Gen 27:41). They expect the cycle of brotherly vengeance to repeat. 'Will certainly requite' (hashev yashiv) — the emphatic infinitive absolute expresses their certainty that payback is coming. Their guilt has never truly been resolved despite Joseph's earlier forgiveness (45:5-8). Unresolved guilt distorts perception — they project their own capacity for revenge onto Joseph.

verses.kjv_text: And they sent a messenger unto Joseph, saying, Thy father did command before he died, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְצַוּוּ אֶל־יוֹסֵף לֵאמֹר אָבִיךָ צִוָּה לִפְנֵי מוֹתוֹ לֵאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayetzavvu el-Yosef lemor avikha tzivvah lifnei moto lemor.
verses.study_note: The brothers 'sent a messenger' (vayetzavvu — literally 'they commanded/charged') rather than going to Joseph themselves — their fear keeps them at a distance. They claim their father left a deathbed command. Whether Jacob actually spoke these words is debated: the text does not record them in Jacob's final speeches (Gen 48-49). Some scholars believe the brothers fabricated this message; others think Jacob spoke privately to them. Either way, they invoke their father's authority as a shield.

verses.kjv_text: So shall ye say unto Joseph, Forgive, I pray thee now, the trespass of thy brethren, and their sin; for they did unto thee evil: and now, we pray thee, forgive the trespass of the servants of the God of thy father. And Joseph wept when they spake unto him.
verses.original_text: כֹּה־תֹאמְרוּ לְיוֹסֵף אָנָּא שָׂא נָא פֶּשַׁע אַחֶיךָ וְחַטָּאתָם כִּי־רָעָה גְמָלוּךָ וְעַתָּה שָׂא נָא לְפֶשַׁע עַבְדֵי אֱלֹהֵי אָבִיךָ וַיֵּבְךְּ יוֹסֵף בְּדַבְּרָם אֵלָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Koh-tomeru leYosef anna sa na pesha achekha vechattatam ki-ra'ah gemalukha ve'attah sa na lefesha avdei Elohei avikha vayyevk Yosef bedabberam elav.
verses.study_note: Three words for wrongdoing accumulate: 'trespass' (pesha — rebellion, transgression), 'sin' (chattat — missing the mark), and 'evil' (ra'ah — moral wickedness). The brothers confess fully. They also identify themselves as 'servants of the God of thy father' — appealing to the shared covenant faith. 'Forgive' (sa — literally 'lift, carry away') appears twice, the Hebrew idiom for bearing away guilt. Joseph weeps — his eighth and final weeping in Genesis. He weeps not from grief or anger but from the pain of being misunderstood after years of demonstrated love.
verses.doctrinal_note: The vocabulary of forgiveness here — pesha (rebellion), chattat (sin), sa (bear away) — is the exact vocabulary later used in the Levitical sacrificial system and in the great forgiveness passage of Exodus 34:6-7. What Joseph does for his brothers foreshadows what God does for His people: bearing away their transgression, sin, and iniquity.

verses.kjv_text: And his brethren also went and fell down before his face; and they said, Behold, we be thy servants.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּלְכוּ גַּם־אֶחָיו וַיִּפְּלוּ לְפָנָיו וַיֹּאמְרוּ הִנֶּנּוּ לְךָ לַעֲבָדִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyelekhku gam-echav vayyippelu lefanav vayyomeru hinnenu lekha la'avadim.
verses.study_note: The brothers finally come in person and prostrate themselves — 'fell down before his face' (vayyippelu lefanav). They offer themselves as slaves (la'avadim). This is the ultimate fulfillment of Joseph's dreams (Gen 37:7-9): his brothers bowing before him. But the fulfillment comes not as Joseph's triumph but as their terror. The dreams were divinely given (37:5-9), and their fulfillment has come through God's sovereign orchestration of events over decades. What began with jealousy and a pit ends with prostration and a plea.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph said unto them, Fear not: for am I in the place of God?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם יוֹסֵף אַל־תִּירָאוּ כִּי הֲתַחַת אֱלֹהִים אָנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer alehem Yosef al-tira'u ki hatachat Elohim ani.
verses.study_note: Joseph's response begins with 'Fear not' (al-tira'u) — the phrase God Himself uses to comfort His people throughout Scripture. Then the profound question: 'Am I in the place of God?' (hatachat Elohim ani — literally 'Am I under/instead of God?'). Joseph refuses to play God. Vengeance belongs to God alone (Deut 32:35). Joseph recognizes that assuming the role of judge and avenger would be to usurp God's prerogative. This is not weakness but the deepest kind of theological wisdom — Joseph subordinates his personal rights to God's sovereign authority.
verses.doctrinal_note: Joseph's question — 'Am I in the place of God?' — establishes the theological foundation of forgiveness: only God has the right to exact ultimate justice. Human vengeance usurps divine prerogative. This principle is foundational to Romans 12:19: 'Vengeance is mine; I will repay, saith the Lord.' Joseph entrusts justice to God, freeing himself to extend mercy.

verses.kjv_text: But as for you, ye thought evil against me; but God meant it unto good, to bring to pass, as it is this day, to save much people alive.
verses.original_text: וְאַתֶּם חֲשַׁבְתֶּם עָלַי רָעָה אֱלֹהִים חֲשָׁבָהּ לְטֹבָה לְמַעַן עֲשֹׂה כַּיּוֹם הַזֶּה לְהַחֲיֹת עַם־רָב׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attem chashavtem alai ra'ah Elohim chashavah letovah lema'an asoh kayyom hazzeh lehachayot am-rav.
verses.study_note: The single most important verse on divine providence in the Old Testament. The verb 'chashav' (to think, plan, reckon, account) is used for both the brothers' evil intent and God's sovereign purpose — the same word for both. This means God did not merely react to evil or work around it — He actively 'reckoned' it, weaving it into His redemptive plan. 'To save much people alive' (lehachayot am-rav) — the purpose: life, salvation, preservation. Joseph's suffering was the instrument of salvation for multitudes. The typological connection to Christ is unmistakable: sold by his brothers, humiliated, exalted, and becoming the savior of those who rejected him.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is the Old Testament's definitive statement on the compatibility of human responsibility and divine sovereignty. The brothers genuinely sinned ('ye thought evil') — they are morally responsible. Simultaneously, God sovereignly purposed the same events for good. Both are true without contradiction. This is not fatalism (blind fate) but providence (purposeful divine governance). Romans 8:28 is the New Testament echo: 'All things work together for good.' The cross of Christ is the supreme fulfillment: wicked men crucified the Son of God, and God meant it for the salvation of the world (Acts 2:23; 4:27-28).

verses.kjv_text: Now therefore fear ye not: I will nourish you, and your little ones. And he comforted them, and spake kindly unto them.
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה אַל־תִּירָאוּ אָנֹכִי אֲכַלְכֵּל אֶתְכֶם וְאֶת־טַפְּכֶם וַיְנַחֵם אוֹתָם וַיְדַבֵּר עַל־לִבָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah al-tira'u anokhi akhalkel etkhem ve'et-tappekhem vaynachem otam vaydabber al-libbam.
verses.study_note: Joseph repeats 'fear not' and adds a concrete promise: 'I will nourish you, and your little ones' (akhalkel — I will sustain, provide for). He includes the children (tappekhem) — his care extends to the next generation. 'He comforted them' (vaynachem) — from 'nacham,' the same root as 'Nehemiah' (comfort of the LORD). 'Spake kindly unto them' — literally 'spoke upon their heart' (vaydabber al-libbam), a phrase used for tender, intimate persuasion (cf. Hosea 2:14 where God speaks 'to the heart' of wayward Israel). Joseph embodies the character of God: forgiving, sustaining, comforting, and speaking tenderly to those who wronged him.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph dwelt in Egypt, he, and his father’s house: and Joseph lived an hundred and ten years.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּשֶׁב יוֹסֵף בְּמִצְרַיִם הוּא וּבֵית אָבִיו וַיְחִי יוֹסֵף מֵאָה וָעֶשֶׂר שָׁנִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyeshev Yosef beMitzrayim hu uveit aviv vayechi Yosef me'ah va'eser shanim.
verses.study_note: The narrative begins its conclusion. Joseph lives to 110 years — the ideal lifespan in Egyptian culture. To the Egyptians, 110 was the perfect life span, signifying divine blessing and a full, complete life. Joseph embodies both Hebrew covenant blessing and Egyptian cultural honor. 'He, and his father's house' — Joseph remained connected to his family, sustaining them as promised. The phrase 'vayechi' (and he lived) mirrors the 'toledot' (generations) structure that organizes Genesis.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph saw Ephraim’s children of the third generation: the children also of Machir the son of Manasseh were brought up upon Joseph’s knees.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא יוֹסֵף לְאֶפְרַיִם בְּנֵי שִׁלֵּשִׁים גַּם בְּנֵי מָכִיר בֶּן־מְנַשֶּׁה יֻלְּדוּ עַל־בִּרְכֵּי יוֹסֵף׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyar Yosef le'Efrayim benei shilleshim gam benei Makhir ben-Menasheh yulledu al-birkei Yosef.
verses.study_note: Joseph witnesses three generations of Ephraim's descendants and holds Machir's children (Manasseh's son) on his knees — a sign of adoption and blessing (cf. Gen 30:3; 48:12). 'Brought up upon Joseph's knees' (yulledu al-birkei) literally means 'born upon the knees' — an adoption formula. Machir later becomes a significant clan in the conquest of Canaan (Num 32:39-40; Josh 17:1). This scene shows Joseph enjoying the fruit of God's faithfulness — the man who was once stripped of his coat and thrown in a pit now holds great-grandchildren on his knees.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph said unto his brethren, I die: and God will surely visit you, and bring you out of this land unto the land which he sware to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל־אֶחָיו אָנֹכִי מֵת וֵאלֹהִים פָּקֹד יִפְקֹד אֶתְכֶם וְהֶעֱלָה אֶתְכֶם מִן־הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת אֶל־הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּע לְאַבְרָהָם לְיִצְחָק וּלְיַעֲקֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Yosef el-echav anokhi met vElohim pakod yifkod etkhem vehe'elah etkhem min-ha'aretz hazzo't el-ha'aretz asher nishba le'Avraham leYitzchak uleYa'akov.
verses.study_note: Joseph's dying declaration is a prophecy of the Exodus. 'God will surely visit you' (pakod yifkod) — the emphatic infinitive absolute expresses absolute certainty. This exact phrase becomes the watchword of Israel's deliverance: Moses uses it in Exodus 3:16 ('I have surely visited you') and 13:19. Joseph names the three patriarchs — Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — invoking the entire covenant chain. His death does not end the story; God's promise guarantees a future. Joseph, who saved his family by bringing them to Egypt, now prophesies that God will save them by bringing them out.
verses.doctrinal_note: Joseph's prophecy bridges Genesis and Exodus. His words 'God will surely visit you' become the code phrase of deliverance (Exod 3:16; 4:31; 13:19). This demonstrates that God's covenant with Abraham (Gen 15:13-16) — predicting 400 years of sojourning followed by liberation — was known and believed by the patriarchs. Faith looks beyond present circumstances to God's promises.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph took an oath of the children of Israel, saying, God will surely visit you, and ye shall carry up my bones from hence.
verses.original_text: וַיַּשְׁבַּע יוֹסֵף אֶת־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לֵאמֹר פָּקֹד יִפְקֹד אֱלֹהִים אֶתְכֶם וְהַעֲלִתֶם אֶת־עַצְמֹתַי מִזֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashba Yosef et-benei Yisra'el lemor pakod yifkod Elohim etkhem veha'alitem et-atzmotai mizzeh.
verses.study_note: Joseph makes them swear an oath about his bones — 'ye shall carry up my bones from hence' (veha'alitem et-atzmotai mizzeh). Unlike Jacob, who was buried immediately in Canaan, Joseph's bones remain in Egypt as a perpetual reminder and promise. Every generation that sees Joseph's coffin will remember: we are not home yet; God has sworn to bring us to the land. This oath is faithfully fulfilled centuries later: Moses carries Joseph's bones out of Egypt (Exod 13:19), and Joshua buries them at Shechem (Josh 24:32). Joseph's unburied bones become Israel's most tangible object of covenant hope. Hebrews 11:22 cites this as the supreme act of Joseph's faith.
verses.doctrinal_note: Hebrews 11:22 identifies this as Joseph's greatest act of faith: 'By faith Joseph, when he died, made mention of the departing of the children of Israel; and gave commandment concerning his bones.' Joseph's faith grasps beyond death to God's promise. His bones in Egypt are an eschatological statement: this world is not our final home; God will bring His people to their inheritance. It is a powerful Old Testament type of resurrection hope.

verses.kjv_text: So Joseph died, being an hundred and ten years old: and they embalmed him, and he was put in a coffin in Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיָּמָת יוֹסֵף בֶּן־מֵאָה וָעֶשֶׂר שָׁנִים וַיַּחַנְטוּ אֹתוֹ וַיִּישֶׂם בָּאָרוֹן בְּמִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyamot Yosef ben-me'ah va'eser shanim vayyachantu oto vayyisem ba'aron beMitzrayim.
verses.study_note: The final verse of Genesis. Joseph dies at 110, is embalmed (as was his father), and placed in a coffin (aron) — the only person in Genesis said to be placed in a coffin. The word 'aron' is the same word later used for the Ark of the Covenant (aron ha-berit). Joseph's coffin and God's Ark travel together through the wilderness (cf. Jewish tradition). The last word of Genesis is 'beMitzrayim' — 'in Egypt.' The book that began with 'In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth' ends with 'a coffin in Egypt.' This is not defeat but deliberate tension: God's story is unfinished. The coffin awaits the Exodus. The promise awaits fulfillment. Genesis ends not with an ending but with an expectation.
verses.doctrinal_note: Genesis begins with creation and ends with a coffin — the entire trajectory of the Fall is compressed into this contrast. But the coffin is not the final word. Joseph's embalmed body is a seed of faith planted in Egypt, awaiting the 'visitation' (pakod yifkod) of God. The 'aron' (coffin/ark) that holds Joseph's body prefigures the 'aron' (Ark of the Covenant) that holds God's presence. Both travel together toward the Promised Land. The book of Genesis thus ends looking forward — to Exodus, to Sinai, to Canaan, and ultimately to Christ, in whom all the promises find their 'Yes and Amen' (2 Cor 1:20).

meta.overview: Genesis 50 is the magnificent conclusion to the book of beginnings. It opens with Joseph weeping over his father Jacob's body, followed by the elaborate embalming and a grand funeral procession from Egypt to the cave of Machpelah in Canaan — fulfilling Jacob's dying wish. After the burial, Joseph's brothers fear that with their father gone, Joseph will finally exact revenge. Their terror produces one of the most theologically profound moments in all of Scripture: Joseph's declaration, "Ye thought evil against me; but God meant it unto good" (v.20) — the definitive Old Testament statement on divine providence. Joseph reassures his brothers with tears and tenderness, promising to sustain them. The chapter — and the entire book — closes with Joseph's death at 110, his embalming, and his solemn oath that God will surely visit Israel and bring them to the Promised Land. Joseph's unburied bones become a perpetual testimony to covenant faith, pointing forward to the Exodus. The book that began with "In the beginning God created" ends with "a coffin in Egypt" — the tension between promise and fulfillment that drives the rest of the biblical narrative.
meta.theme: Divine Providence, Forgiveness & Covenant Hope Beyond Death
meta.keyWordMeaning: To think, plan, reckon, account — the key verb in Joseph's theological interpretation of his suffering: "Ye thought [chashav] evil... God meant [chashav] it for good." The same word is used for both human scheming and divine sovereignty.
outline: Joseph Mourns and Embalms Jacob (vv.1-3): Egyptian burial customs and 70-day mourning
The Funeral Procession to Canaan (vv.4-11): Joseph's request to Pharaoh and the great company
Burial at Machpelah (vv.12-13): Fulfilling Jacob's command in the cave of Abraham
The Brothers' Fear After Jacob's Death (vv.14-18): Sending a message and falling before Joseph
God Meant It for Good (vv.19-21): Joseph's declaration of divine providence and forgiveness
Joseph's Final Years and Death (vv.22-26): 110 years, the oath about his bones, and a coffin in Egypt
